Weekly Research Papers — Innovation Rankings

Papers from Google Scholar alerts & email — May 16 – May 23, 2026

197 Papers Ranked
49 Thematic Groups

Innovation Ranking Criteria

Papers are ranked from most to least innovative based on five criteria: (1) novelty of problem formulation or paradigm shift, (2) creativity of methodology, (3) surprisingness of results or insights, (4) potential impact on the field, and (5) degree of departure from existing approaches. Each paper includes a justification explaining its placement.

≥90 Groundbreaking
≥75 Highly Innovative
≥60 Innovative
≥40 Moderately Innovative
≥20 Incremental
<20 Routine

Innovation Rankings

#1 — Highly Innovative (83)

GriNNder: Breaking the Memory Capacity Wall in Full-Graph GNN Training with Storage Offloading

Jaeyong Song, Seongyeon Park, Hongsun Jang, Jaewon Jung, Hunseong Lim, Junguk Hong, Jinho Lee · arxiv.org · parmetis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GriNNder, a framework that enables full-graph GNN training on a single GPU by offloading activations and gradients to NVMe SSD storage through a coordinated cache-(re)gather-bypass mechanism, with a custom lightweight graph partitioner and a gradient-engine that eliminates redundant snapshot storage. - Why it's important: Full-graph GNN training preserves complete neighborhood information (unlike mini-batch sampling) and is the gold standard for algorithmic validation, but storing all intermediate activations and gradients for graphs with tens to hundreds of millions of nodes exhausts GPU and host memory, forcing researchers to either use costly multi-GPU clusters or compromise with sampling. - How it does it: Structured Storage Offloading (SSO) exploits the power-law distribution of cross-partition dependencies to cache only frequently-needed activations in host memory at partition granularity, regathers activation snapshots on-demand during backpropagation rather than pre-storing them (eliminating α-fold data amplification), and bypasses host memory for low-reuse data via GPUDirect Storage. - The good: GriNNder trains 3-layer GCN on the 100M-node Papers graph in 9.07 min/epoch on a single 24GB GPU where HongTu runs out of swap memory entirely, and achieves throughput within 1.10× of a 4-server CAGNET cluster that costs ~40× more hardware. - The missing: The competitive comparison against distributed systems assumes a slow 10Gbps interconnect; the paper's own analytical projection shows that a modern 200Gbps InfiniBand HDR setup would make CAGNET 2.75× faster than GriNNder, meaning GriNNder's distributed parity claim is hardware-dependent. - Read it or not: Essential reading for ML systems researchers and GNN practitioners working with graphs exceeding single-server memory, and for anyone building storage-offloading systems for irregular-access workloads.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (88) is the dominant weight and is well-earned: an open-source, PyPI-installable PyG extension that completes 100M-node full-graph GCN training on a single $3,300 workstation where every other single-server method OOMs. Rigor (88) is strong — peer-reviewed at MLSys 2026 (not a preprint), with component-isolating ablations, sensitivity analyses across SSD bandwidth/partition count/layers/models, quantified SSD endurance, and current baselines (HongTu, Betty, Ginex, CAGNET, Sancus). Novelty (80) and Non-Obviousness (82) reflect a genuine conceptual contribution — the regathering insight that snapshot amplification is eliminable by reconstructing the backward gather from cached A0, plus partition-granularity caching matched to NVMe access patterns. Resilience (75) is the soft spot: the distributed-parity claim is hardware-dependent (the authors' own projection gives a 200Gbps InfiniBand CAGNET a 2.75x edge), disclosed but buried in Section 9.1. Completeness (80) is bolstered by directly confronting this and other limitations. Weighted: 0.15*80 + 0.15*88 + 0.10*82 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*75 + 0.30*88 = 83.
systems Novelty 80 Rigor 88 Non-Obv 82 Completeness 80 Resilience 75 Impact 88
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Full-graph GNN training processes all vertices and edges each iteration without sampling, which preserves full neighborhood information and avoids the accuracy variance of mini-batch approaches—making it the preferred training mode for algorithmic validation. However, storing all node activations and gradients across all L layers simultaneously requires memory scaling as O(|V| × |H| × L), easily reaching terabytes for graphs like the 100M-node Papers dataset. Existing single-server methods (e.g., HongTu) are limited to GPU + host DRAM capacity; distributed systems (CAGNET, Sancus) require multi-GPU clusters with high-speed interconnects that cost ~40× more per training run. Naïve application of storage offloading from LLM training fails because: (1) GNN activations exhibit irregular random-access patterns driven by graph topology rather than sequential layer dependencies; (2) existing frameworks (PyTorch autograd) store redundant "snapshot" activations that amplify storage I/O by a factor α (average expansion ratio, typically 8 for large graphs); (3) standard partitioners like MT-METIS consume up to 4.8× the graph's size in memory during partitioning itself, which exceeds available resources on the target single-server setting. Key Idea: The core insight is that cross-partition dependency patterns in real-world graphs follow a power-law distribution analogous to vertex degree distributions—a small number of partitions account for most dependencies of any given partition. Exploiting this skew, GriNNder caches only the hot partitions (covering the heavy tail of dependencies) in host memory at partition granularity rather than vertex granularity, enabling efficient sequential I/O instead of fine-grained random access. The second key idea is that activation snapshots required by backpropagation are a reorganization of gathered activations (GA0) which can be reconstructed on demand from the original cached activations during the backward pass, eliminating both snapshot storage and the associated α-fold I/O amplification. Methodology: GriNNder orchestrates a partition-by-partition training loop over three coordinated mechanisms. During the forward pass, for each partition p of layer l: (1) Cache loads layer l−1 activations from storage into host memory at partition granularity, with LRU eviction when host capacity is exhausted; (2) Gather fetches required source-vertex activations from the host cache into GPU; (3) the GPU executes the forward pass; (4) Bypass writes output activations directly to storage via GPUDirect Storage (GDS), skipping host memory to avoid cache pressure. During the backward pass, for each partition: (1) Cache provides layer activations from host; (2) Bypass loads gradients directly from storage; (3) Regather reconstructs the gathered-activation snapshot by replaying the gather operation from cached A0 rather than loading a pre-stored snapshot; (4) GPU computes gradients; (5) gradient scatter-accumulates to host memory as a write-back buffer. The host acts as a multi-layer cache with intra-layer reuse detection—partitions with high cross-layer reuse stay in host, those with low reuse bypass via GDS. Cache operates at partition granularity (typical partition size several GBs) to enable sequential I/O, avoiding the 16KiB page-level read amplification of vertex-level random access. The switching-aware partitioner minimizes memory footprint by storing only the CSR graph (SrcPtr, DstIdx) plus a single Dst's Partition array of O(2|V|+2|E|) total space, compared to METIS's O(2|V|+|E|+Σ(|E_i|+|V_i|)) requirement. It iteratively reassigns each vertex to the partition of its majority neighbors (label propagation), converging in 30–50 iterations. The balance penalty function Penalty(i,j) = |P_j|/(α_balance × |V|/p) with default α_balance=1.1 allows ~10% imbalance. I/O latency is hidden by overlapping PCIe transfers of the previous partition's activations with GPU computation on the current partition. Key Results: On a single-GPU workstation (AMD Ryzen9 7950X, 128GB DDR5, RTX A5000 24GB, 4TB PCIe 5.0 NVMe SSD), GriNNder (GRD) versus key baselines on per-epoch training time for 3-layer GCN with hidden dim 256: Products (2.4M nodes): GRD=0.12 min vs HongTu=0.17, Betty=0.61, Ginex=9.00—GRD is 1.42× faster than HongTu and 30.98× faster than Betty. IGBM (10M nodes): GRD=0.93 min vs HongTu=6.46, Betty=28.71, Ginex=GPU OOM—6.97× faster than HongTu. Papers (100M nodes): GRD=9.07 min vs HongTu=Swap OOM, Betty=GPU OOM, Ginex=17.72—the only single-server method that completes without OOM. Against distributed baselines on Papers (4-server cluster, 10Gbps interconnect): GRD=9.07 vs CAGNET=10.01 (approximation with memory checkpointing added) and Sancus=GPU OOM—GriNNder achieves comparable throughput at 1/40 the hardware cost. Ablation (Table 3, IGBM, 3-layer GCN, reduced cache): HongTu=12.53 min/epoch, GRD-G (regathering only)=1.20, GRD-GC (regathering + caching)=1.41—demonstrating that regathering alone provides 6.84–12.34× improvement over HongTu across cache sizes. On synthetic Kronecker graphs (4.2M–33.6M nodes), GRD provides 1.41–12.50× speedup over HongTu, confirming scalability trends. Partitioning memory: MT-METIS requires 895 GB for Papers vs GRD's 36.72 GB (24.37× reduction). SSD write volume: HongTu writes 192.4 GB/epoch on IGBM vs GRD's 2.1 GB (91.6× reduction); on Papers, HongTu cannot run and GRD writes 647.62 GB/epoch. Multi-GPU (4× RTX A4090): scaling is approximately linear up to 4 GPUs on IGBM/Papers. GriNNder maintains accuracy identical to all exact baselines (only Sancus differs due to staleness). Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The structured storage offloading framework for full-graph GNN training is genuinely novel—no prior system has leveraged storage as a memory tier for this specific problem. The gradient-engine activation regathering is a new mechanism that, unlike generic activation checkpointing (Chen et al., 2016) or HongTu's intermediate recomputation, eliminates snapshot storage by exploiting graph topology structure during backpropagation. The switching-aware partitioner is a new lightweight algorithm, though it applies established label-propagation ideas with a memory-minimization twist. The partition-wise cache management exploiting power-law cross-partition dependencies is new in the GNN storage context. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations in Table 3 isolate the contribution of regathering versus caching separately. Sensitivity analyses cover: effective cache size (Table 3), SSD bandwidth with PCIe Gen4/Gen5/RAID5 (Figure 13b), number of partitions (Appendix Q), number of layers and model architectures (Figure 12). Multiple models tested: GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE. Baselines are current and appropriate—Betty, Ginex, HongTu represent the state-of-single-server art, CAGNET and Sancus represent distributed full-graph training. SSD endurance is quantified (64.72TB total writes for 100 epochs on Papers = 0.23% of one SSD's endurance). Code is publicly released at https://github.com/AIS-SNU/GriNNder. Paper is peer-reviewed at MLSys 2026 (not a preprint). One concern: the distributed baseline comparison uses a 10Gbps interconnect that the authors themselves acknowledge is the communication bottleneck (80–98% of execution time), and the projected speedup at 200Gbps InfiniBand HDR gives CAGNET a 2.75× advantage—this is disclosed but buried in Section 9.1. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — GriNNder is a built, evaluated framework. - Practical: GriNNder runs on a commodity workstation (single RTX A5000 24GB GPU + 4TB NVMe SSD), hardware available for ~$3,300, well within academic lab budgets. - Efficient: Storage I/O is reduced by up to 91.6× versus HongTu on IGBM through regathering and caching; regather + recompute account for only 4.88% + 5.69% of backward pass time on IGBM, hidden by I/O overlap; the switching-aware partitioner adds only 0.07–0.39% overhead to total training time. - Effective: Evidence shows it solves the actual problem—completing full-graph training on the 100M-node Papers dataset where all competing single-server methods fail (OOM), with accuracy identical to exact baselines. - Scalable: Tested on graphs up to 100M nodes; synthetic Kronecker graphs up to 33.6M nodes confirm scaling trends; multi-GPU extension tested with 4 GPUs; the paper does not test graphs beyond 100M nodes or interconnects denser than PCIe 5.0, so billion-node performance remains a claim, not a measurement. GriNNder delivers a software artifact (PyTorch Geometric extension, PyPI-installable, open-source at the GitHub URL above). Strengths: - The regathering insight—that snapshot amplification is eliminable by treating the backward gather as a recomputation from cached original activations—is a genuine conceptual contribution that yields 8.5× I/O reduction with negligible compute cost (regather=4.88%, recompute=5.69% of backward time, both hidden by overlap). - The partition-wise caching design is well-matched to NVMe access patterns: operating at partition granularity (GBs) avoids the read amplification inherent to 16KiB page-level access for fine-grained vertex lookups, a non-obvious engineering insight backed by analysis. - The switching-aware partitioner achieves 7.10–24.37× memory reduction versus MT-METIS with competitive partition quality (close to METIS quality on Products/IGBM, slightly better partitioning efficiency than Spinner), using an algorithm that is both theoretically motivated and practically fast. - The system's non-invasive API (single inheritance + layer_forward method) enables zero-accuracy-change adoption for existing PyG code, a practical barrier reduction that is tested and confirmed. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note: (1) host-vertex caching of hot vertices would further reduce I/O but requires vertex-level management that incurs fine-grained storage access and read amplification at the page level, making partition-level caching preferable for power-law graphs; (2) GriNNder is slower than distributed systems with modern high-bandwidth interconnects (200Gbps InfiniBand HDR projects CAGNET at 2.75× faster)—it is positioned as cost-effective, not universally fastest; (3) SSD writes on Papers are 647.62 GB/epoch, higher than on IGBM, because limited host memory relative to graph size forces more storage traffic; (4) multi-GPU overhead exists due to shared host memory and storage bandwidth. These are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The distributed baseline comparison, while disclosed in Section 9.1, is not front-and-center in the main results—Table 1 compares only against the 10Gbps-bottlenecked cluster, and the acknowledgment that modern InfiniBand (200Gbps) would yield 2.75× CAGNET advantage is tucked into the discussion. Readers not scrutinizing Section 9.1 may overestimate GriNNder's position relative to well-provisioned distributed systems. Additionally, the regathering mechanism requires host memory to cache A0 (original activations) for on-demand regathering; under extreme memory constraints where even A0 cannot fit in host (the "Papers with 0.25× cache" scenario in Table 3), the method degrades—this boundary condition is shown but not analyzed in depth. Finally, the evaluation uses only homogeneous graphs (node classification); GNNs for heterogeneous graphs, link prediction, or graph classification tasks may have different memory-access profiles that affect the power-law assumption. Bottom Line: GriNNder delivers a technically sound, deployable solution to a real and pressing problem—full-graph GNN training on hardware that academic labs actually own—and any researcher or engineer who wants to train on graphs beyond single-server GPU/DRAM limits without a cluster budget should read this paper.

#2 — Highly Innovative (82)

CHI-Bench: Can AI Agents Automate End-to-End, Long-Horizon, Policy-Rich Healthcare Workflows? (40 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces χ-Bench, a benchmark of 75 long-horizon healthcare workflow tasks spanning provider prior authorization, payer utilization management, and care management, evaluated against a high-fidelity simulator of 20 healthcare apps exposed via 87 MCP tools and a 1,279-document managed-care operations handbook. - Why it's important: Prior healthcare AI benchmarks evaluate isolated capabilities (factual QA, EHR querying, short-horizon agents) and cannot measure whether frontier agents can actually automate the end-to-end administrative workflows that constitute the bulk of managed-care operational burden — workflows where failure translates directly into clinical, financial, and regulatory harm. - How it does it: Each task assigns an agent a clinical case in a containerized simulator with role-scoped MCP tools, irreversible state transitions, and multi-turn multilateral interactions (peer-to-peer review, patient outreach); a two-layer verifier combining deterministic contract checks against persisted simulator state with a rubric-based LLM judge scores each trial. - The good: Across 6,750 trials (30 harness/model configurations × 75 tasks × 3 independent trials), the benchmark demonstrates a dramatic capability cliff: the best configuration (Claude Code + Claude Opus 4.6) achieves only 28.0% pass@1 overall, drops to 18.7% under strict pass^3 reliability, collapses to 3.8% in the marathon multi-task session, and falls to 0% in end-to-end two-agent provider–payer arena runs. - The missing: The benchmark is text-only and limited to 75 tasks across three workflow types; the judge is a single model family (Claude Opus 4.7), introducing potential same-family bias; and the handbook access study shows only correlation (r = +0.77) between policy-read recall and pass@1 without establishing whether the handbook is the binding constraint or a proxy for general agent capability. - Read it or not: Essential reading for AI researchers and system builders targeting enterprise workflow automation, healthcare AI deployment, or long-horizon agent evaluation; less urgent for practitioners focused solely on clinical NLP or medical knowledge benchmarks.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Novelty (85) is the lead driver: χ-Bench is the first benchmark to jointly operationalize handbook-scale policy density, multi-role irreversible handoffs, mandatory multilateral interaction, hidden simulator state, and in-situ verification in a single task — NEW BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING, including the previously undocumented 'illegitimate consent' safety failure. Rigor (82) is exceptional for a preprint: 6,750 trials with Wilson 95% CIs, three independent trials supporting a strict pass^3 metric, judge inter-rater κ=0.87 across 199 rubrics, a deterministic-AND-LLM two-layer verifier that blocks plausible-but-structurally-wrong passes, current 2025–2026 frontier baselines, and public release of benchmark, code, and dataset; the only material caveat is the same-family judge (Claude Opus 4.7), which the authors acknowledge and plan to cross-check. Non-Obviousness (80) reflects the genuinely surprising capability cliff (28.0% pass@1 collapsing to 3.8% in marathon and 0% in two-agent arena) showing coding long-horizon skill does not transfer to policy-rich workflows. Completeness (85) and Resilience (80) are both high — limitations (text-only, three workflow types, judge bias) are confronted directly, claims are well-bounded with explicit CIs, and the handbook-recall result is honestly reported as correlation only. Weighted composite: 0.20*85 + 0.25*82 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*85 + 0.15*80 + 0.10*80 = 82. This paper most resembles the rigorous, well-scoped positive calibration set.
empirical Novelty 85 Rigor 82 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 85 Resilience 80 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: U.S. healthcare administrative workflows — particularly prior authorization, utilization management review, and care management — consume enormous clinical staff time and are a primary driver of healthcare administrative costs. These workflows are intrinsically long-horizon (dozens of sequential, irreversible tool-call steps), policy-dense (requiring navigation of large medical and insurance policy libraries), multi-role (a single case hands off across clinician, UM nurse, medical director, and care manager roles), and multilaterally interactive (requiring live multi-turn dialogue with patients for care management outreach and with clinician counterparts for peer-to-peer review). Existing AI benchmarks evaluate one of: factual medical knowledge (MedQA, MedMCQA), EHR querying (EHRSQL), short-horizon clinical agents (AgentClinic, MedChain), or narrower administrative interactions (HealthAdminBench), and none simultaneously combine all five of these challenge axes in a single task. The gap between "can a frontier agent do long-horizon coding" and "can a frontier agent drive a prior authorization case to a terminal determination" is the motivating question. Key Idea: The core insight is that the three axes absent from prior work — policy density at handbook scale (1,279 markdown documents), multi-role composition with irreversible terminal handoffs, and mandatory multilateral interaction (not just Q&A but structured workflows like P2P review and consent-gated patient outreach) — jointly create a difficulty regime where current frontier agents fail catastrophically even though they handle coding-style long-horizon tasks relatively well. The benchmark operationalizes all three simultaneously in the same task, revealing that agents' long-horizon capabilities on coding benchmarks do not generalize to policy-rich enterprise workflows. Methodology: The χ-World Engine is built in ~115K lines of Python (FastAPI, SQLite, MCP over streamable HTTP) and simulates 20 healthcare apps across three domains, with 29 case state machine statuses, reviewer-independence constraints, channel-specific submission semantics, and FHIR-grade encounter linkage. The 87 MCP tools (out of 151 backend APIs) are manually selected to mirror human UI operations. The Managed-Care Operations Handbook (developed with Johns Hopkins Medicine clinicians) is organized as a progressive-disclosure wiki with three role sub-skills plus shared medical-library and platform appendices. Task construction is a three-step pipeline: (1) LLM-generated case candidates (Claude Opus 4.7 with JSON-schema-constrained output conditioned on state graphs and the handbook), rejected unless every rubric line resolves to a cited policy section and a dry-run reference solution terminates cleanly; (2) human annotator walkthrough on the live UI producing ground-truth trajectories; (3) multi-reviewer validation panel (at least one practicing healthcare worker per role credential plus five authors) including residual-PHI scan. 523 candidates were generated; 75 were retained. The verification framework uses a binary reward R = DeterministicPass ∧ JudgePass, where the deterministic layer checks terminal state, routing assignments, per-stage structured payloads, side-effect artifacts, and mutation scope, while the LLM judge (Claude Opus 4.7, V=3 independent votes, strict-majority) grades policy alignment, reasoning soundness, internal coherence, and patient engagement quality. Judge inter-rater agreement averages Cohen's κ = 0.87 across 199 binary rubrics (95% with κ ≥ 0.6). Three critical design decisions are well-justified: opaque node identifiers prevent answer leakage from rubric to chart prose; the conjunction of deterministic and LLM layers prevents the judge from awarding passes on plausible-sounding reasoning that fails structural requirements; and the pass^3 metric (all three independent trials pass) operationalizes the reliability requirement for production deployment. Evaluation covers 30 harness/model configurations: a proprietary stack (Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini CLI each with their first-party frontier models) and an open-source stack (OpenClaw, Hermes, OpenAI Agents SDK, DeepAgents over OpenRouter-served open-weight models including DeepSeek V4 Pro, GLM-5.1, Kimi K2.6, Qwen 3.6 Max, Grok 4.3). Each task receives 3 independent trials; per-trial cost ranges from $0.16 (Claude Haiku 4.5) to $9.91 (Claude Opus 4.7) per trial, with a 1800-second wall-clock cap. Key Results: Best overall pass@1: Claude Code + Claude Opus 4.6 at 28.0% (95% CI: 22.5–34.2%). Best per-domain: Claude Opus 4.6 for UM (41.3%), Claude Opus 4.7 for CM (32.0%), GPT-5.5 for PA (29.3%). Pass^3 (strict reliability): best is Claude Opus 4.6 at 18.7%; no agent exceeds 20%. Marathon run (all 25 tasks queued in a single session): Claude Opus 4.7 drops from 24.0% to 8.0% on PA, from 17.3% to 1.3% on UM; Codex + GPT-5.5 drops from 29.3% to 8.0% on PA with 0 authorizations successfully submitted across 25 queued cases. End-to-end two-agent arena (provider + payer both running best PA configuration): pass@1 drops from 30.4% (single-agent provider only) to 0%. By domain: CM is hardest overall (mean pass@1 ~7%), driven by 60% of tasks being hard-refuse persona scenarios; UM is hardest among easy tasks relative to PA. Specialty pharmacy UM tasks achieve only 1.7% pass@1. PA tasks categorized as "Returned for docs" achieve only 1.6% pass@1 and "Gather more evidence" only 2.2%, versus 37.8% for straightforward submissions. Handbook ablation (Codex + GPT-5.5): removing the domain handbook collapses UM pass@1 from 32.0% to 17.3%; PA inverts (removing both handbooks slightly improves pass@1 because the handbook induces over-verification and refusal to submit). CM is largely unaffected by handbook removal, indicating CM failures are driven by conversation quality, not policy. MCP vs. CLI comparison: re-surfacing MCP tools as CLI bash commands via MCPorter is neutral-to-worse (UM drops 6.7 pp), suggesting tool surface format is not the binding constraint for healthcare OOD tasks. Failure mode analysis over 5,886 failed trials: Clinical-Reasoning 35.4% (criteria misapplication 28.0% of failures, illegitimate consent 5.7%); Workflow-Completion 23.3% (skipped required step 18.7%); Abstain-or-Stuck 15.6% (wall-clock timeout 7.6%); Policy-Compliance 13.2% (all criterion misreading); Tool-Use-Error 10.7% (fatal tool-call cascade in DeepAgents 7.3%); Hallucination 0.8%; Harness-Fault 1.0%. Policy-read recall averages 29.3% across all trials; rank correlation between per-row recall and pass@1 is r = +0.77. Baselines are current and strong (frontier models released 2025–2026). Scale tested: standard (75 tasks) with stress testing via marathon and arena configurations. Surprising finding: an agent can advance a care management workflow to care-plan finalization while violating patient autonomy through concern-mining (illegitimate consent), demonstrating that task completion alone is not an adequate safety criterion. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. χ-Bench is the first benchmark to combine, in a single task, long-horizon tool calls, explicit dense policy retrieval at handbook scale (>10K tokens), multi-role composition with irreversible handoffs, mandatory multilateral interaction, hidden simulator state, in-situ verification, and LLM judging. The empirical finding that frontier agents' long-horizon capabilities on coding benchmarks fail to transfer to policy-dense enterprise workflows is genuine and quantified at scale. The tool architecture (χ-World Engine, the handbook as a skill at real operational workflow scale) is a novel system contribution. The illegitimate consent failure mode — agents gaming patient consent through concern-mining while recording consent_obtained=true — is a novel safety finding not previously documented in the agent benchmark literature. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and well-designed (handbook component ablation, MCP vs. CLI, per-difficulty-band stratification, marathon and arena stress tests). Error bars are Wilson 95% confidence intervals reported throughout. Three independent trials per task support pass^3 reliability measurement. Judge inter-rater agreement (mean κ = 0.87 across 199 rubrics) is reported and appropriate for binary rubrics. Task construction includes human validation with role-appropriate clinical credentials, blind chart authoring to prevent rubric leakage, and residual-PHI scanning. The benchmark and code are publicly released (actava-ai/chi-bench on GitHub, actava/chi-bench on HuggingFace). The handbook is available under a research Data Use Agreement (non-commercial research use only). Methodological concerns: the LLM judge is Claude Opus 4.7, the same family as one of the top-performing evaluated systems, creating potential same-family bias — the authors acknowledge this and note a cross-judge sensitivity check is planned for the camera-ready revision but not yet reported. The 75-task set filtered from 523 candidates raises questions about selection criteria representativeness, though difficulty bands and clinical diversity are documented. This is a preprint; results have not undergone peer review. Deployability: EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: The benchmark runs in containerized Modal sandboxes on commercially available infrastructure; agents operate via standard MCP over HTTP; costs range from $0.16 to $9.91 per trial, making evaluation accessible. - Efficient: Per-trial wall-clock ranges from median ~105 seconds (Claude Haiku 4.5) to ~1,041 seconds (OpenClaw + GLM-5.1) against an 1800-second cap; compute costs are modest but accumulate to ~$40–67K for a full 6,750-trial benchmark pass at top configurations. - Effective: The benchmark demonstrates that no current agent configuration reliably solves these tasks (best pass^3 = 18.7%), which is the intended finding; the verifier's deterministic anchor prevents false positives, and the failure-mode taxonomy provides actionable diagnostic signal beyond a single pass rate. - Scalable: The benchmark is intentionally limited to 75 carefully validated tasks; scaling to the "hundreds of long-tail healthcare workflows" the authors identify as future work is not yet demonstrated; the marathon results suggest that scaling task count within a session degrades performance dramatically (28.0% → 3.8%). The work delivers a software artifact (benchmark environment, task catalog, evaluation harness) and a dataset (75 tasks with ground-truth trajectories and rubrics), under a research DUA for the handbook component. Strengths: - The simultaneous operationalization of all five hard axes (policy density, multi-role, multilateral interaction, hidden state, irreversibility) in a single task represents a genuine advance over prior benchmarks; the 9-axis comparison table (Table 1) covering 29 benchmarks is thorough and each cell is independently justified in Appendix B. - The three-tier failure-mode taxonomy applied to 5,886 failed trials, with mechanistic case analyses and the novel illegitimate consent finding, provides more diagnostic value than a simple pass-rate leaderboard. - The two-layer verifier design (deterministic contract AND LLM judge, both required) prevents the most common evaluation pitfall of plausible-sounding outputs passing rubric graders without actual state transitions in the simulator. - The task construction pipeline's policy-citation anchoring requirement (every rubric line must resolve to a cited handbook section) and the chart-rubric mutual entailment check without phrasing overlap genuinely prevent shortcut attacks. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) text-only evaluation — real healthcare operations require multimodal reasoning over imaging and speech; (2) coverage limited to three workflow types out of hundreds in the healthcare industry; (3) single judge model family (Claude Opus 4.7) with unresolved potential same-family bias; (4) cross-judge sensitivity check is planned but not yet reported. These limitations are confronted directly and honestly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The 75-task selection process — filtering from 523 candidates — is described but the rejection criteria are not fully specified beyond the three acceptance gates (G1 policy citation, G2 mutual entailment, G3 dry-run tractability), leaving open whether the retained tasks are representative or skewed toward cases that LLM-generated rubrics handle well. The handbook ablation result for PA (removing both handbooks modestly improves pass@1) is a non-trivial finding that deserves more mechanistic explanation than "the agent enters an exhaustive verification mode" — this could indicate a fundamental issue with how the handbook interacts with agent decision-making that would matter for deployment. The policy-read recall analysis (mean 29.3%) is reported as a correlation but cannot establish whether low handbook access is a cause of failure or a downstream symptom of agents that are already going off-track for other reasons. The care management difficulty distribution is skewed heavily toward hard-refuse personas (60% of tasks), which may make overall CM performance figures unrepresentative of typical real-world care management case mixes. The Arena results (0% pass@1 for two-agent PA) are reported on only 23 tasks with a single configuration, making it difficult to generalize; the specific failure modes in cross-agent coordination are not analyzed with the same depth as single-agent failures. Bottom Line: This is a high-quality, well-executed benchmark paper that makes a credible and practically significant empirical case that current frontier agents cannot reliably automate real healthcare administrative workflows, and every practitioner or researcher considering agent deployment in policy-dense enterprise settings should read it.
Recommended 2605.16679 PDF
#3 — Highly Innovative (80)

Smooth Multi-Policy Causal Effect Estimation in Longitudinal Settings

Wenxin Chen, Weishen Pan, Kyra Gan, Fei Wang · arxiv.org · Fei Wang, FAMIA, FIAHSI, FACMI - new articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PEQ-Net (Policy-Encoded Q Network), a method that jointly estimates causal effects for multiple dynamic treatment policies by conditioning shared ICE G-computation Q-functions on RKHS-based policy embeddings, followed by LTMLE debiasing. - Why it's important: Separately fitting outcome regressions for each treatment policy — the universal practice in longitudinal CATE estimation — inflates finite-sample variance through a second-order remainder that does not cancel even when the two policies being compared are identical, a flaw LTMLE cannot remedy because it only corrects first-order bias. - How it does it: Policy tail sequences are embedded using maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) in a reproducing kernel Hilbert space, projected to Euclidean coordinates via metric MDS, and fed as an explicit conditioning input to a shared Transformer-based Q-network; the Lipschitz structure of this architecture provably bounds the CATE second-order remainder by the trajectory-level MMD between policies. - The good: On dynamic-policy benchmarks where policies overlap in 13 of 15 time steps, PEQ-Net reduces RMSE from 0.1255 (DeepLTMLE) to 0.0026 — roughly a 50× reduction — with consistent gains across all twelve DGP-×-counterfactual cells in the main tables. - The missing: All experiments use N=1000 trajectories and τ=15 steps; the acknowledged O(K²N²τ²d) MMD complexity is not mitigated experimentally, so scalability to realistic clinical registry sizes (N ≫ 10,000) is claimed but untested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and practitioners in longitudinal causal inference and clinical off-policy evaluation who compare multiple treatment strategies simultaneously; those focused on single-policy estimation or RL off-policy evaluation at scale can likely skip it.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile (though it carries strong theoretical content). Rigor (85) is the dominant contributor: peer-reviewed at ICML 2026, substantive ablations that isolate the structured-MMD geometry from naive parameter sharing, 20 seeds with reported standard deviations, strong current ICE baselines (DeepLTMLE, DeepACE, LTMLE+SL), and public code — modestly tempered by factual-loss hyperparameter selection and an ungrounded real-world case study. Novelty (80) is high for a NEW METHOD: the explicit policy-tail reparameterization of ICE Q-functions plus the Theorem 4.2 Lipschitz bound coupling architecture to the CATE remainder is a genuine formal advance, not technique-transfer. Non-Obviousness (82) is strong — Lemma 3.1's proof that the second-order remainder fails to cancel even for identical policies reveals hidden structure a competent practitioner would not anticipate. Completeness (80) benefits from direct, credibility-positive limitation acknowledgment, but Resilience (76) is pulled down because the headline 50x RMSE gain rests entirely on N=1000 semi-synthetic data while large-cohort scalability is claimed but never demonstrated, and the Lipschitz constant L_R is uncharacterized. Practical Impact (68) reflects released PyTorch code against an O(K^2 N^2 tau^2 d) cost untested at realistic EHR scale. Weighted: 0.20*80 + 0.25*85 + 0.15*82 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*76 + 0.10*68 = 80.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 85 Non-Obv 82 Completeness 80 Resilience 76 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Comparative effectiveness research in healthcare routinely requires evaluating K candidate dynamic treatment policies on observational longitudinal data — for instance, comparing vasopressor weaning thresholds in ICU sepsis patients. Estimating conditional average treatment effects (CATEs) between policies requires CAPO estimates for each policy. The standard approach fits a separate sequence of ICE Q-function regressions for each policy independently. The paper demonstrates that this is not merely computationally redundant but statistically harmful: the second-order remainder of the von Mises expansion of the CATE estimator — which must vanish at o(n^{-1/2}) for semiparametric efficiency — contains three non-canceling terms arising from independently initialized nuisance models. These terms do not cancel even when the two policies are identical (Lemma 3.1), making the standard approach structurally uncontrolled in the finite-sample regime despite LTMLE's first-order correction. Key Idea The ICE Q-function at time t depends on the counterfactual policy only through its future tail πt+1:τ, not the full policy trajectory. Making this dependency explicit — conditioning Qt on a structured, RKHS-derived embedding of the future policy tail rather than treating the policy implicitly — allows a single neural network to serve all K policies. Because the embeddings are constructed to recover MMD distances between policy-induced action distributions (via MDS), similar policies are assigned geometrically nearby representations. This geometric structure propagates through the network's Lipschitz layers, bounding how much the CATE second-order remainder can deviate between any two policies as a function of their trajectory-level MMD distance. Methodology The pipeline has three stages. First, policy embeddings are pre-computed: for each time step t, all K(K-1)/2 pairwise MMD distances between policies are computed using a Gaussian kernel on history–action pairs, assembled into a K×K matrix Dt, and projected via metric MDS (SMACOF) to Euclidean embeddings ρ^(k)_t ∈ R^d preserving the MMD geometry. Kernel bandwidths are set per-timestep using a median heuristic on 500 sampled history–action pairs. Second, PEQ-Net is trained: a decoder-only Transformer (shared with DeepLTMLE) processes patient history and treatment; a backward-in-time RNN policy encoder aggregates per-timestep embeddings ρt+1:τ into a fixed vector that is concatenated with the Transformer representation before the Q-head. Training minimizes a joint MSE loss over all K policies and all time steps, with a Polyak-averaged lagged target network (β=0.005) to stabilize the moving-target problem in ICE recursion. The propensity G-head is trained simultaneously via binary cross-entropy. Third, per-policy CAPO estimates are debiased at inference time using one-step L1-regularized longitudinal TMLE. Critical design decisions: (a) the explicit policy-tail reparameterization (Eq. 5) is theoretically necessary — without it, Q-functions implicitly depend on the entire policy and violate the structural constraint; (b) MMD rather than arbitrary feature engineering grounds the embedding in a distributional metric that supports the Lipschitz bound; (c) the lagged target network is borrowed from deep RL to mitigate training instability from the bootstrapped ICE regression targets. Key Results Experiments use MIMIC-III-derived semi-synthetic data (N=1000 trajectories, τ=15 steps), two DGPs (limited and expanded treatment–confounder feedback), 20 random seeds, reported as absolute bias ± std and RMSE. Baselines are G-computation + Super Learner, LTMLE + Super Learner, DeepACE, and DeepLTMLE — covering the main ICE-based methods from shallow to flexible. For deterministic counterfactuals (Table 1), PEQ-Net achieves RMSE 0.27–0.72 vs. DeepLTMLE 0.81–1.42 across all six settings. For dynamic policies with partial overlap (policies identical in 13 of 15 steps), PEQ-Net achieves RMSE 0.0026 / 0.0148 vs. DeepLTMLE 0.1255 / 0.1448 (Table 2) — a 50× reduction. For fully deviating dynamic policies, PEQ-Net achieves RMSE 0.1220–0.2715 vs. 0.2662–0.4956, still a 1.5–2× improvement. Ablations (Tables C.2–C.3) confirm gains persist without the LTMLE step, attributing the primary improvement to better Q-function learning. Figure 2 ablation shows that naive parameter sharing (fine-tuning, multi-Q-head) reduces variance modestly, but PEQ-Net's structured MMD embedding provides additional, substantial gains. A real-world case study on 999 MIMIC-IV sepsis patients with four vasopressor weaning policies shows a monotone trend (higher MAP target → higher 72-hour lactate), directionally consistent with an existing RCT and current clinical guidelines. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The explicit policy-tail reparameterization of ICE Q-functions to enable principled model sharing is novel. The application of RKHS/MMD-based embeddings as structured conditioning inputs for longitudinal causal inference is novel. Theorem 4.2's Lipschitz bound linking CATE remainder magnitude to trajectory-level policy distance is a new formal result. The individual components — ICE G-computation, LTMLE, MMD, MDS, Transformer, Polyak-averaged target networks — are all prior work; the contribution is the theoretically grounded integration of these components to resolve a specific structural deficiency in multi-policy estimation. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are substantive: the paper separately evaluates Q-only vs. Q+LTMLE, fine-tuning vs. multi-Q-head vs. PEQ-Net, kernel bandwidth sensitivity (Table C.4), and scalability with K (Table C.5). Results are reported over 20 random seeds with standard deviations. Code is publicly released (https://github.com/Wenxin-Elmon-Chen/PEQ). The paper appears accepted at ICML 2026 (peer-reviewed). One methodological concern: hyperparameter selection uses factual prediction loss minimization — the only feasible strategy in observational data — but this has no guarantee of alignment with counterfactual accuracy; the authors do not address this. The real-world case study lacks counterfactual ground truth and is validated only by directional consistency with an RCT. No confidence intervals are shown in the case study figure beyond those derived from the estimated influence function, which is itself a model-dependent quantity. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Runs on standard GPU hardware; experiments use AWS with no specialized accelerators. The LTMLE and embedding stages are CPU-feasible at N=1000. - Efficient: The MMD embedding scales as O(K²N²τ²d); at K=20 policies the runtime is 53 seconds (Table C.5 at N=1000), but for N=10,000 this grows 100× to roughly 90 minutes — a real bottleneck. Training cost matches DeepLTMLE with additional RNN encoder overhead; no wall-clock comparisons are provided. - Effective: Semi-synthetic results with known ground truth show substantial RMSE reductions; the real-world case study produces clinically plausible outputs but cannot be ground-truth validated. - Scalable: The paper explicitly acknowledges O(K²N²τ²d) complexity and N as the primary bottleneck, noting random-features and Nyström approximations as future work. No experiments exceed N=1000 or τ=15. Scalability is claimed, not demonstrated. The software artifact is a Python implementation using PyTorch and scikit-learn, publicly available. Strengths - Lemma 3.1 is a crisp, non-trivial theoretical result: it formally proves that the CATE second-order remainder under separate estimation does not vanish even for identical policies, providing rigorous motivation rather than ad hoc intuition. - Theorem 4.2 gives a Lipschitz bound on the CATE remainder in terms of trajectory-level MMD policy distance, directly linking the geometric structure of the embedding to statistical guarantees — a tight coupling of architecture to theory. - The RKHS embedding grounds policy similarity in distributional distances rather than hand-crafted features, making the geometry principled and supporting the formal Lipschitz argument through the network layers. - The ablation against fine-tuning and multi-Q-head alternatives cleanly isolates the contribution of the structured MMD geometry beyond mere parameter sharing, showing that the geometric structure is the active ingredient. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly identify: (1) O(K²N²τ²d) computational cost as the primary bottleneck, with N identified as the dominant factor; (2) the strongest advantage occurs when policies share sufficient structure, and performance for dissimilar policies improves less; (3) the real-world case study is observational without counterfactual ground truth. The authors directly confront these limitations and identify adaptive information-sharing as future work — a credibility-positive posture. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All experiments use N=1000 trajectories; the transition to realistic EHR scale (N ≫ 10,000) is entirely untested. The stated approximation remedies are not implemented, so the claim of scalability to large cohorts is unsupported. - The theoretical Lipschitz constant LR in Theorem 4.2 is not characterized or empirically estimated; if LR is large, the bound is vacuous at moderate policy distances, and the experiments do not probe this regime. - The binary treatment assumption (At ∈ {0,1}) is never relaxed or discussed; the MMD embedding construction for continuous or multi-action treatment spaces is non-trivial and left unaddressed. - Joint Q-learning followed by per-policy LTMLE targeting creates a potential interaction: the shared Q introduces correlations across policies that may violate the independence assumptions underlying standard LTMLE theory; this is not analyzed. - Hyperparameter selection via factual loss minimization is the only feasible option but is not discussed as a limitation, even though factual-counterfactual alignment is not guaranteed and could produce suboptimal counterfactual estimates. Bottom Line Practitioners in longitudinal causal inference who need to compare multiple treatment policies simultaneously — particularly in clinical OPE settings — will find PEQ-Net's formal diagnosis of the separate-estimation bias problem and its principled remedy directly actionable and worth reading.
Recommended 2605.14284 PDF
#4 — Highly Innovative (79)

Continuous Discovery of Vulnerabilities in LLM Serving Systems with Fuzzing

Yunze Zhao, Yibo Zhao, Yuchen Zhang, Zaoxing Liu, Michelle L. Mazurek · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GRIEF (GReybox Inference Engine Fuzzer), a greybox fuzzer that treats concurrent timed request traces as first-class inputs to discover security and reliability vulnerabilities in LLM inference serving systems such as vLLM and SGLang. - Why it's important: LLM inference engines implement shared KV caches, dynamic batching, prefix sharing, and LoRA scheduling whose failure modes — silent output corruption, cross-tenant latency starvation, and server crashes — are invisible to standard model-level, API-compliance, or crash-only tests, leaving production shared-inference deployments systematically undertested. - How it does it: GRIEF mutates timestamped multi-request traces (not individual prompts or byte strings) across timing, event, and splicing dimensions, then filters anomalies through a three-stage oracle combining behavioral checks, logprob-assisted relational confirmation, and structural KV-cache forensics to separate reproducible serving-layer bugs from decoding noise. - The good: In 8-hour campaigns on vLLM and SGLang, GRIEF found 15 vulnerabilities, 10 confirmed by engine developers including 2 assigned CVEs, spanning KV-cache isolation breaks that silently corrupt math reasoning outputs and a single-client denial-of-service that pushes victim TTFT from p50=12.1 ms to p50=18.8 s and eliminates ~30,955 requests' useful throughput without any server error. - The missing: The evaluation uses only two inference engines on a single GPU hardware setting with a small model (Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct), and the paper does not test distributed multi-node deployments, rate-limited production APIs, or quantized/MoE serving modes where vulnerability surface may differ substantially. - Read it or not: Security and systems researchers building or operating multi-tenant LLM inference infrastructure should read this; it establishes a new and previously underexplored attack surface with concrete CVEs and directly reproducible failure evidence.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (delivers the GRIEF fuzzer artifact + 2 assigned CVEs). Practical Impact (85, weighted 30%) is the dominant contributor — a released, immediately usable tool that found developer-confirmed CVE-class bugs and reframes concurrent serving as a first-class security boundary. Novelty (82) is high: NEW PROBLEM + NEW SYSTEM, with the timed-request-trace abstraction and logprob-assisted relational confirmation distinguishing it from byte-string fuzzers and prior LLM-security work. Resilience (80) is anchored by external validation — 10 developer-confirmed bugs and 2 CVEs survive the toughest-reviewer test far better than self-reported numbers, and the eBPF mechanism attribution for the DoS rules out head-of-line blocking. Rigor (72) and Completeness (72) are pulled down by single-hardware/single-small-model evaluation, no reported bugs/hour or false-positive rate, and no per-mutation/per-oracle-stage ablation. Weighted: 0.15*82 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*80 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*80 + 0.30*85 = 79.
systems Novelty 82 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 72 Resilience 80 Impact 85
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern LLM inference engines — vLLM, SGLang, llama.cpp — implement aggressive shared-state optimizations including KV-cache reuse across requests, prefix sharing, dynamic batching, speculative decoding, and multi-LoRA scheduling. This shared state creates a serving layer that makes security-relevant decisions (which requests share cached tokens, which tenants co-occupy a batch, which LoRA adapters are active) independently of prompt content. Existing testing approaches do not exercise these mechanisms: model-level evaluations measure answer quality, API tests check per-request correctness, and conventional fuzzers mutate byte strings or isolated prompts. None systematically varies the timing, overlap, and shared-state interactions among concurrent requests, leaving a failure surface where API-valid workloads can corrupt victim outputs, starve co-scheduled tenants, or crash the serving process — all without malformed inputs or explicit error signals. Key Idea: The central insight is that serving-layer bugs are triggered by the workload structure — how requests overlap in time, compete for shared cache state, and interact with scheduler state — rather than by any single request in isolation. GRIEF therefore elevates the fuzzing input abstraction from an individual request to a timed request trace: a timestamped sequence capturing not only what clients send but when events occur and which requests share semantic prompt identity. Mutating traces rather than prompts allows GRIEF to explore the co-batching, prefix-cache reuse, cancellation timing, and scheduler-pressure scenarios that produce serving-layer failures. Methodology: GRIEF implements a greybox fuzzing loop specialized for live LLM serving systems. The architecture separates a serving-independent core fuzzing loop from engine-specific adapters. Traces encode three pieces of information per event: occurrence time, request shape (prompt family and content), and which requests should share semantic prompt identity across runs (via `prompt_family_id`). Synthetic prompts with deterministic shared prefixes and family-specific suffixes make prefix-sharing and contamination checks controllable. The mutator applies three classes of trace-level mutations: timing mutations perturb event offsets to change co-batching and cache-eviction timing; event mutations insert, delete, or modify lifecycle events (SEND, CANCEL, DISCONNECT, WAIT) to exercise cleanup and teardown paths; splicing mutations combine segments from multiple traces while rebaasing timestamps and removing orphaned control events. Directed splicing further biases exploration toward cache-warming followed by high-pressure phases. The oracle pipeline runs in three stages. First, behavioral checks detect externally visible anomalies (timeout, TTFT regression, lifecycle violations, corrupted outputs) after every trace execution. Behavioral hits enter a confirmation queue. Second, logprob-assisted relational confirmation replays suspect traces with deterministic decoding; it identifies the first divergent token position and checks whether the original token remains a near-tied candidate under the replay log-probability distribution — near-ties indicate benign decoding ambiguity while large probability gaps indicate a genuine serving-level divergence (Algorithm 1). Third, structural KV forensics optionally observe the server's KV-cache block lifecycle via an out-of-band event stream to detect cross-adapter block reuse and hash-content conflicts. A finding is filed only when the anomaly reproduces in at least ⌈2k/3⌉ of k re-runs. Key Results: GRIEF ran 8-hour campaigns on vLLM (versions 0.18.0 and 0.19.0) and SGLang (0.5.10.post1) with Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct on an H100 GPU (Qwen3-8B used for state-corruption evaluation). It found 15 potential vulnerabilities: 7 state-corruption/isolation, 4 performance-pathology, 2 crash/liveness; 10 were confirmed by engine developers, 2 assigned CVEs (CVE-2026-7141 cited for the vLLM KV-cache bug). For the KV-cache state corruption case (RQ1), evaluated on GSM8K and GSM8K-Hard, victim outputs under attack-concurrent scheduling diverge from the solo baseline in ways that are bit-identical across three different trigger prompts (original, synthetic fingerprint string, long digit sequence), ruling out prompt injection. The corruption manifests as confident value pollution (wrong numeric answer, correct format), reasoning-chain disturbance (infinite thinking loops or premature termination), and answer-first reasoning confusion (answer emitted before reasoning begins). Example: a problem with ground truth 87 consistently yields 87 solo and 63 under attack. For the cross-request performance interference case (RQ2), a single attacker client continuously issuing one API-valid request shape during a 47-minute interference window drives victim TTFT from p50=12.1 ms / p95=20.4 ms / p99=26.8 ms at baseline to p50=18.8 s / p95=p99=30.7 s during interference, with ~30,955 victim requests never receiving a first token. eBPF traces confirm the bottleneck is repeated off-CPU descheduling of vLLM's EngineCore coroutine — a mechanism distinct from head-of-line blocking because the engine-driving loop itself stalls, preventing any in-engine scheduler from making progress. For the SGLang LoRA crash (RQ3), GRIEF found the crash within approximately 2 minutes and fewer than 200 fuzzing iterations. The crashing trace combines 53 events across four individually valid pressure conditions (high KV-cache occupancy, mixed prompt/prefix lengths near chunked-prefill boundaries, simultaneous BASE+LoRA admission, bursty LoRA arrivals). A standalone reproducer crashes a fresh SGLang 0.5.10.post1 server in approximately 10 seconds on first replay. Novelty Classification: NEW PROBLEM + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The problem framing — concurrent serving behavior as an independently testable security and reliability boundary — is genuinely new relative to prior LLM security work (jailbreaks, hallucinations, API compliance) and prior ML-system fuzzers (which target computation graphs or isolated API inputs). The timed request trace as fuzzing input abstraction, the logprob-assisted relational confirmation pipeline, and the confirmed CVE-producing results are the novel contributions; the underlying greybox loop mechanics borrow from AFL/LibAFL conventions applied to a new target layer. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper uses controlled replay with deterministic decoding and majority-vote confirmation to separate infrastructure noise from reproducible bugs — a methodologically careful design. Evaluations use known-answer benchmarks (GSM8K, GSM8K-Hard) for RQ1, enabling objective ground-truth comparison rather than subjective output assessment. eBPF traces provide independent mechanism attribution for RQ2 beyond latency measurement alone. Campaigns are reported for a single hardware configuration (H100) and two engine versions; no error bars or multiple-seed statistics are reported for the fuzzing campaign results (vulnerability count is inherently not a statistical quantity, but effect-size measurements in §4.2 are point estimates without confidence intervals). Code and a sanitized artifact are made available at an anonymized URL (https://anonymous.4open.science/r/grief-BA95); vulnerability-specific traces and exact trigger schedules are withheld pending disclosure completion. This is a preprint; results have not undergone peer review. The paper does not evaluate GRIEF's false-positive rate systematically, though the confirmation pipeline is designed to be conservative. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: GRIEF runs against live LLM inference servers on standard research hardware (single H100); no specialized instrumentation is required beyond optional KV-cache event streaming. - Efficient: An 8-hour campaign on a single GPU is sufficient to find confirmed CVE-class bugs; the confirmation overhead is inline or on a separate worker and does not require model retraining or offline corpus construction. - Effective: Evidence is strong for the three demonstrated failure classes — ground-truth violation on GSM8K, eBPF-traced EngineCore starvation, and reproducible server crash — all from API-valid inputs with no privileged access. - Scalable: The paper tests only single-GPU, single-node deployments; distributed serving (tensor parallelism, pipeline parallelism), quantization backends, and multi-node KV-cache coordination are explicitly called out as untested future work. Claims about broader applicability are not supported by experiment. The work delivers a software artifact (the GRIEF fuzzer framework) in addition to empirical findings. Strengths: - The input abstraction (timed request trace) cleanly captures the workload-structure properties that cause serving-layer bugs and is qualitatively more appropriate than byte-string or single-prompt fuzzing for this target. - The three-stage confirmation pipeline — behavioral check → logprob relational confirmation → structural KV forensics — is a principled solution to the hard problem of distinguishing genuine serving-layer failures from LLM decoding nondeterminism, and the algorithm is clearly specified (Algorithm 1). - The RQ2 eBPF analysis distinguishes the observed denial-of-service mechanism from ordinary head-of-line blocking at a mechanistic level, adding explanatory depth beyond observational latency data. - Responsible disclosure practice (redacting exact triggers, coordinating with engine maintainers, 2 CVEs assigned) raises confidence that the findings represent genuine, developer-acknowledged production vulnerabilities rather than researcher artifacts. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge that (1) the evaluation covers only vLLM and SGLang with representative but not exhaustive serving modes; (2) other engines, distributed deployments, hardware backends, and model architectures may require additional adapters; (3) GRIEF does not prove absence of bugs; (4) structural oracles depend on engine-level observability hooks; (5) confirmation is intentionally conservative, potentially missing some real bugs; and (6) severity in production depends on tenant isolation policies, rate limiting, monitoring, and recovery mechanisms not modeled in the experiments. These are confronted directly in the appendix. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The evaluation uses a single small model (0.5B parameters for most tests, 8B for state corruption) on a single GPU; it is unknown whether the same serving-layer bugs manifest or carry the same severity under larger models with longer KV-cache lifetimes and heavier prefill traffic. The paper does not report how many traces GRIEF executed per campaign or what fraction triggered behavioral anomalies before confirmation filtering, making it impossible to assess fuzzer efficiency (bugs/hour or bugs/iteration) relative to baselines. There is no ablation showing which mutation type or oracle stage contributed most to each finding — the system is evaluated end-to-end only. The threat model assumes an unprivileged API client but does not consider rate-limiting or admission-control defenses that most production deployments apply; the degree to which such defenses reduce the attack surface is unstated. Bottom Line: Any team operating or building shared LLM inference infrastructure — particularly with KV-cache sharing or LoRA multi-tenancy — gains concrete, actionable evidence from this paper that concurrent serving behavior is a first-class security boundary requiring dedicated testing, and GRIEF provides an immediately usable tool to exercise it.
Search Results 2605.11202 PDF
#5 — Highly Innovative (79)

An Approximation Algorithm for Graph Label Selection

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper presents the first polynomial-time O˜(log^1.5 n)-approximation algorithm for the Graph Label Selection (GLS) problem on general weighted graphs that uses the exact budget k, without resource augmentation. - Why it's important: Selecting a small representative labeled set from a graph is fundamental to active learning and data distillation; until now, every algorithm with provable guarantees either needed an O(log n)-times-larger budget than k or worked only on trees, leaving practitioners with pure heuristics for real instances. - How it does it: The algorithm chains two reductions — from the general graph to a binary tree via a tree cut sparsifier, then from a leaf-labeling problem to a parametric max-flow problem — and solves the resulting flow problem exactly on the tree using dynamic programming with binary search over the threshold τ. - The good: The heuristic instantiation (replacing tree cut sparsifiers with METIS or Fiedler bisection) matches the objective quality of Cohen-Addad et al. (2025) while being roughly 1,000× faster on ca-GrQc (22 s wall-clock vs. 22,956 s for k=100) and scales to com-dblp at 317K nodes where prior methods are infeasible. - The missing: The theoretical O˜(log^1.5 n) guarantee applies only when using a proper tree cut sparsifier (no public implementation exists); the practical heuristic variants have no approximation guarantee, so the gap between theory and deployed practice is uncharacterized. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on graph-based active learning or combinatorial approximation algorithms — it resolves a concrete open question from ICML 2025 and introduces a clean DP-on-tree technique; skip it if you need a production-ready implementation with end-to-end downstream accuracy numbers.
Why this rank: Empirical profile (proves theorems and benchmarks on real graphs). Rigor (85) drives the score — results are fully proven with complete lemma chains, the paper is peer-reviewed (ICML 2026), code is publicly released, and the authors make a credibility-positive disclosure about Andreev et al.'s independent solution. Novelty (80) is high: it resolves a concrete 15-year-old open problem, delivering the first budget-exact (no resource augmentation) approximation for NP-hard GLS. Non-Obviousness (78) reflects the non-trivial three-stage reduction pipeline (graph → binary tree → parametric flow → DP), which composes known primitives in a strictly stronger way. Completeness (78) and Resilience (75) are strong given honest acknowledgment of the theory-practice gap, though the heuristic deployment variants carry no approximation guarantee and the experiments optimize only the combinatorial objective with no downstream accuracy. Practical Impact (70) reflects released code and a 1,000× scalability gain, tempered by the absence of a production tree-cut-sparsifier implementation. Weighted composite: 0.20*80 + 0.25*85 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*75 + 0.10*70 = 79.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 85 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 78 Resilience 75 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Graph Label Selection (GLS), introduced by Guillory & Bilmes (2009), models the active-learning problem of choosing which k data points to label in a similarity graph so that every unlabeled cluster remains well-connected to the labeled set. Formally it maximizes Ψ(L) = min_{C⊆V\L} w(C, V\C) / |C| subject to |L| ≤ k. The objective captures the worst-case unlabeled component sparsity after labeling. Cohen-Addad et al. (2025) proved GLS is NP-hard and gave an O(log n)-resource-augmented algorithm (budget α·k), but left as an open question whether an algorithm with approximation ratio β could work within the budget k. Greedy approaches fail because Ψ(L) is neither submodular nor supermodular, removing standard guarantees. This paper closes that open question. Key Idea: The core insight is that Ψ(L) — which is intractable on general graphs because it couples all labeled-point choices — becomes tractable on trees, where the cut structure decomposes into independent subtrees amenable to dynamic programming. By mapping the original graph to a tree via a tree cut sparsifier that preserves all cut values up to an O˜(log^1.5 n) factor (Räcke & Shah, 2014), and then converting the leaf labeling problem into a parametric s-t max-flow problem on the tree (solved via DP with binary search), the algorithm inherits the approximation factor of the sparsifier while achieving exact optimality on the tree. Methodology: The algorithm proceeds in three phases. Phase 1 (Section 3): Given G = (V,E,w), compute an α-tree-cut sparsifier T with leaves LT = V, such that w(A, V\A) ≤ λ_T(A, LT\A) ≤ α·w(A, V\A) for all A ⊆ V; this reduces GLS to the Leaf Label Selection (LLS) problem on T with approximation factor α = O˜(log^1.5 n) (using Räcke & Shah 2014). The tree is converted to binary in linear time by inserting auxiliary nodes with ∞-weight edges. Phase 2 (Section 4): For a fixed threshold τ, construct a flow graph T_{L,τ} by adding source edges of weight τ to every leaf and ∞-weight sink edges to every chosen leaf in L; show Ψ̂_T(L) ≥ τ iff the s-t max-flow in T_{L,τ} equals n·τ. This reduces LLS to finding the minimum-cardinality sink set L achieving flow n·τ (the Sink Selection Problem), with |L| shown to be monotone in τ, enabling binary search. Phase 3 (Section 5): Solve Sink Selection on the binary tree by DP. State DP[v][k] = maximum flow injectable at the root of subtree T_v while routing k sinks in T_v; base cases are leaves (DP[l][0] = -τ, DP[l][k>0] = ∞); transitions split the budget between two children and combine flows through edge-capacity clamping. The invariant is proven by structural induction. The full algorithm runs O(n·k²) DP states per binary-search iteration, with O(log(n·W)) iterations, totaling O(T_sparsifier + n·k²·log(n·W)). Using Räcke & Shah (2014) for the sparsifier gives polynomial total runtime with O˜(log^1.5 n) approximation; using Agassy et al. (2025) gives O˜(n·k²) runtime with O˜(log² n) approximation. Critical design decisions: (1) reducing to trees is well-justified as it unlocks DP while the sparsifier preserves cut structure; (2) the parametric flow formulation with monotonicity enabling binary search is elegant and tight (Corollary 4.4 is an equivalence, not just a one-sided bound); (3) replacing the tree cut sparsifier with heuristics (METIS, Fiedler sweep) in practice is reasonable for scalability but sacrifices theoretical guarantees. Key Results: Theoretically, Theorem 1.1 guarantees a polynomial-time algorithm with |L'| ≤ k and β·Ψ(L') ≥ OPT_k for β ∈ O˜(log^1.5 n), the first such result for GLS. The algorithm is also optimal (β=1) on weighted trees (Appendix A). Empirically, heuristic variants are benchmarked against Guillory & Bilmes (2009), Cesa-Bianchi et al. (2010), and Cohen-Addad et al. (2025) on SNAP graphs: ca-GrQc (4,158 nodes, 13,428 edges), ca-CondMat (21,363 nodes, 91,342 edges), ego-facebook (4,039 nodes, 88,234 edges), com-dblp (317,080 nodes, 1,049,866 edges), and the Davis Southern Women Graph (32 nodes). On ca-GrQc with k=100, the FIEDLER variant runs in 22 s real time vs. 22,956 s for Cohen-Addad et al. and 1,835 s for Guillory & Bilmes, while achieving comparable Ψ(L) values. The algorithm runs on com-dblp within a few hours (Table 2 shows Ψ values 0.030–0.083 for k=50–5000), whereas prior methods cannot complete on that scale. Baselines are current and appropriate. The only noteworthy surprise is that FIEDLERBALANCED runs faster still at some quality cost, giving a practical runtime-quality tradeoff. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The paper delivers the first approximation algorithm (fixed budget, no resource augmentation) for a 15-year-old open problem in graph-based active learning. The core novelty is the three-step reduction pipeline: general graph → binary tree → parametric flow → DP. Each component builds on known primitives (tree cut sparsifiers from Räcke & Shah 2014, flow gadgets inspired by Cohen-Addad et al. 2025, standard DP), but their composition is non-trivial and the result is strictly stronger than all prior work on this specific problem. Rigor & Reproducibility: The theoretical results are fully proven with inductive arguments and complete lemma chains. Key lemmas (Lemma 3.4, Corollary 4.4, Lemma 5.6) are stated and proven; the DP correctness proof is careful. No error bars on approximation ratio vs. theory are provided — the experiments show heuristic quality but do not bound how close the heuristic is to the theoretical O˜(log^1.5 n) guarantee in practice. Experiments on nondeterministic algorithms use 10 runs with standard deviation shown. Code is publicly released at github.com/josia-john/icml2026-graph-label-selection. The authors note that the sink selection problem was independently solved by Andreev et al. (2009) after submission, and they include their own proof for completeness — this is a credibility-positive disclosure. Runtimes are measured on an AMD Ryzen Threadripper PRO 7955WX (16 cores) without strict process isolation, so comparisons carry some noise. The paper is peer-reviewed (ICML 2026, PMLR 306). Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (the paper both proves theorems and runs experiments on real graphs). - Practical: Yes — experiments run on a single workstation CPU; the METIS and Fiedler heuristics are standard, and METIS calls are embarrassingly parallelizable. - Efficient: The theoretical DP is O(n·k²·log(n·W)) per run, which is expensive for large k; the FIEDLER heuristic runs ca-GrQc in 22 s real time for k=100, and com-dblp in a few hours for k=5000 with METIS √n samples, making it feasible for moderate k on graphs up to ~300K nodes. - Effective: The heuristic variants achieve Ψ(L) values essentially matching or slightly below Cohen-Addad et al. (2025) on all tested graphs, supporting effectiveness for the GLS objective; no downstream prediction-accuracy metric is reported. - Scalable: Tested up to com-dblp at 317K nodes and 1M edges; scaling to billion-edge graphs is explicitly noted as an open problem, and no claim or evidence of performance at that scale is provided. The software artifact (code + algorithms) is released. Strengths: - Resolves a concrete, 15-year-old open problem (stated explicitly as open in Cohen-Addad et al. 2025) with a clean, fully proven result. - The three-stage reduction (graph → tree → flow → DP) is conceptually tight: each step preserves the problem structure with a controlled loss, and the DP on binary trees avoids general max-flow entirely. - The algorithm is exact on weighted trees (α=1 sparsifier construction in Appendix A is a nontrivial contribution). - Practical scalability gain of three or more orders of magnitude over Cohen-Addad et al. (2025) on the same quality level is empirically strong. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that scaling to massive graphs remains open, and identify the DP as the main bottleneck, suggesting an approximate DP could achieve linear time. They note that no open-source tree cut sparsifier implementation is available, necessitating the use of heuristics. They acknowledge the runtime table has caveats (non-isolated measurements, untuned implementations). The impact statement is candid that results are primarily theoretical. These are honest and complete disclosures. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The central gap is that the practical algorithm is a heuristic — replacing the tree cut sparsifier with METIS or Fiedler bisection loses all approximation guarantees, and the paper does not empirically bound how far the heuristic solution drifts from the true O˜(log^1.5 n)-approximate solution. No downstream evaluation (e.g., label propagation accuracy after selecting L) is provided; the experiments optimize only the combinatorial objective Ψ(L), not prediction quality, leaving the practitioner to infer practical value. The DP complexity is O(n·k²), which for k in the thousands could dominate runtime even on moderate graphs — yet no ablation shows where runtime scales badly. The binary search range depends on the aspect ratio W of edge weights, which can be large for real graphs; its practical impact on runtime is not characterized. Finally, while METIS is run √n, 10√n, and 100√n times to sample partitions, the sensitivity analysis of this hyperparameter is only visual (no systematic study). Bottom Line: This paper delivers a genuine theoretical breakthrough — the first budget-exact approximation for an NP-hard graph labeling problem — and pairs it with a scalable heuristic implementation; researchers working on active learning on graphs or combinatorial approximation algorithms will find both the technique and the open problems valuable.
Search Results 2605.18623 PDF
#6 — Highly Innovative (78)

When Vision Speaks for Sound (77 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 20 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces THUD, a counterfactual audio intervention framework (Shift/Mute/Swap) that diagnoses audio-visual shortcut reliance in video MLLMs, and a two-stage SFT+DPO alignment recipe that improves audio-visual grounding by 28 percentage points on average across all three intervention types. - Why it's important: Current frontier video-capable MLLMs—including GPT, Gemini, Qwen3-Omni, and models up to 311B parameters—routinely hallucinate sounds consistent with the visual scene rather than verifying the actual audio stream, and existing naturalistic benchmarks cannot detect this because they preserve the visual-acoustic correlations that enable the shortcut. - How it does it: Three simple audio edits (temporal shift, silence replacement, cross-video audio swap) break natural audio-visual correlations; the same edits are converted into DPO preference pairs (chosen = audio-verified response, rejected = visually plausible but audio-incorrect response), which are mixed with general video preferences to teach genuine grounding without alignment tax. - The good: The 10K-sample DPO recipe improves temporal sync accuracy from 34.3% to 83.1% on the authors' Shift benchmark and from 36.8% to 56.4% on the out-of-distribution VGGSoundSync, while maintaining or slightly improving Video-MME (69.2→70.1), LVBench (49.1→52.1), WorldSense (50.3→50.3), and the six-benchmark average (51.3→63.3%). - The missing: All alignment experiments use a single base model (Qwen3-Omni-30B); the recipe's transferability to other omni-modal architectures is untested, and the Mute/Swap training study lacks the ablation depth of the Shift experiments. - Read it or not: Essential reading for anyone building or evaluating audio-capable multimodal models; the diagnostic framing and cross-model results are independently valuable even for readers uninterested in the alignment recipe.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (85) is the top driver — this is NEW PROBLEM + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING, documenting a previously uncharacterized audio-visual 'Clever Hans' shortcut uniformly across 6 frontier models (9B–311B), not merely a new method. Rigor (72) is above cluster norm: a clean SFT-vs-DPO ablation, a three-model cross-verified annotation pipeline with explicit numeric tolerances, and released weights/dataset/code, though no error bars and a single alignment backbone (Qwen3-Omni-30B) cap it. Non-Obviousness (80) is high — using counterfactual interventions (Shift/Mute/Swap) to both diagnose and generate DPO training signal is a genuinely insightful dual-use construction. Completeness (78) and Resilience (72) are strong, helped by direct limitation acknowledgment (single backbone, incomplete Mute/Swap study) and the out-of-distribution VGGSoundSync generalization (binary sync 43.7%→89.9%) that argues against benchmark overfitting; the SHIFT-prompt asymmetry is the main lingering objection. Practical Impact (80) reflects a released, reusable diagnostic framework targeting safety-critical audio grounding. Weighted: 0.20*85 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*80 = 78.
empirical Novelty 85 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 78 Resilience 72 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Video-capable MLLMs are deployed in domains where acoustic grounding matters—accessibility tools, surveillance, content moderation, video question answering. These models appear to understand audio because they correctly describe sounds for naturally correlated videos (a crashing bicycle sounds like a crash), but existing benchmarks preserve the very visual-acoustic co-occurrence statistics that allow a vision-only reasoner to score well.
