Critical design decisions: using filler-derived coefficients rather than fitting directly to garden path data tests surprisal theory's generalization claim; fixing ka=1000 isolates word-level multiplicity; the FORCED GARDEN PATH bound is algorithm-agnostic. Three garden path construction types are tested: MV/RR (reduced relative clause), NP/S (noun phrase vs. sentential complement), NP/Z (noun phrase vs. zero complement). Key Results Filler goodness-of-fit improves monotonically with kw (delta log-likelihood ~3000 at kw=1 to ~6000 at kw≥100 for left-corner BabyLM), plateauing around kw=25 — consistent with prior RNNG work. Predicted garden path effects in surprisal peak at kw=2–10 across all constructions and parsing strategies; effects are paradoxically smallest at kw=1 due to degraded parse quality making both conditions equally surprising. Converting to reading times: the largest predicted first-spillover effects are ~10–15ms (FORCED GARDEN PATH condition), while empirical effects are ~150–200ms for MV/RR and NP/Z and ~50–75ms for NP/S — an underprediction by a factor of 10–20× even at the theoretical upper bound. This pattern holds for both BabyLM and BLLIP models, both top-down and left-corner strategies, and across the disambiguating word and two spillover positions. The FORCED GARDEN PATH and FULL-PARALLEL conditions produce qualitatively similar underprediction magnitudes, demonstrating the gap is independent of which parses are maintained. RNNG parsing F1 on Penn Treebank: left-corner BabyLM=0.923, top-down BabyLM=0.890 — competitive with supervised parsers despite training on auto-parsed data. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The parse multiplicity mismatch hypothesis has been theorized since Jurafsky (1996) but never directly tested via controlled manipulation of parse multiplicity in a neural model. The RNNG + beam-search experimental framework is assembled from existing components; the novelty is the experimental design (systematic kw sweep, FORCED GARDEN PATH upper bound, Bayesian RT prediction) and the definitive null result. The FORCED GARDEN PATH bound is genuinely novel as a method that isolates the parse-content question from the inference-algorithm question. Rigor & Reproducibility Five seeds per condition provide variance estimates. Bayesian mixed-effects regression throughout with 95% credible intervals. The filler-derived surprisal-RT relationship is validated independently before application to garden path data, appropriately testing a key prediction of surprisal theory. Human data from 2000 participants in the SAP Benchmark. Data and analysis code are publicly available at https://osf.io/7mprn/. The manual categorization of parses by interpretation type (for FORCED GARDEN PATH and interpretation-probability analysis) lacks reported inter-rater reliability, a minor methodological gap. The paper correctly notes that its FORCED GARDEN PATH argument is robust to inference algorithm choice. The linearity assumption for the surprisal-RT mapping, borrowed from the filler context and applied to garden path sentences, is a core assumption of surprisal theory under test rather than a methodological artifact — but if the relationship is nonlinear at the scale of garden path surprisal differences, the analysis would misestimate the gap. This is a preprint; results have not yet undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — computational experiments predicting human reading times. - Practical: RNNGs with beam search run on standard CPU/GPU cluster hardware (NYU HPC); no specialized infrastructure required. - Efficient: Training 20 models and running beam search at 12 kw values is substantial one-time compute but not prohibitive; the per-sentence beam search at kw=1000 is the computational bottleneck. - Effective: The paper measures exactly what it claims — RNNG surprisals at all kw values, including the theoretical upper bound, fail to predict human garden path magnitudes, and the null result is robust. - Scalable: Not tested at scale; models are trained on 42–100M tokens. The FORCED GARDEN PATH argument is formally scale-independent (it depends on the per-parse surprisal, not on how many parses are considered), but the generalization to large-scale LMs with richer semantic knowledge is an open empirical question. The paper delivers data and analysis code (OSF); no software library or model weights. Strengths - The FORCED GARDEN PATH upper bound is the paper's strongest contribution: it demonstrates that the gap is not a consequence of which parses are on the beam but of the too-low surprisal assigned to the disambiguating word given the wrong interpretation, making the conclusion algorithm-agnostic and theoretically sharp. - Consistent replication across two training corpora, two parsing strategies, and five random seeds for each condition provides unusually strong reliability for a psycholinguistics computational study. - The dual finding precisely locates the scope of the failure: surprisal works well for naturalistic text (better at larger kw) but fails specifically for syntactic disambiguation, ruling out the hypothesis that the problem is just overall model quality. - Directly testing a theoretical claim made informally in the literature for three decades — and doing so with a controlled causal manipulation rather than correlational analysis — is a methodological advance over prior work in this area. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that their results are specific to RNNGs and may not transfer to all inference algorithms, but argue the FORCED GARDEN PATH bound is algorithm-independent. They acknowledge that a two-stage parsing model (incremental prediction + error-driven reanalysis) could potentially salvage surprisal theory as a partial account, with RNNG surprisal handling stage-one costs and a separate reanalysis mechanism handling stage-two costs. They explicitly note that their findings rule out single-stage surprisal accounts but leave two-stage accounts open. These are honest, precise scope qualifications. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The RNNG models are small (100M-word training) relative to modern LMs. The too-low surprisal of the disambiguating word given the wrong parse could reflect inadequate modeling of rare syntactic structures rather than a fundamental ceiling. This is not addressed; testing with a large-scale neural parser trained on billions of tokens would strengthen the conclusion. - The surprisal-to-reading-time conversion assumes a linear relationship estimated from unambiguous filler sentences generalizes to garden path sentences — this is exactly the hypothesis under test, creating circularity in the quantitative comparison. The ~10–20× underprediction could reflect nonlinearity at high surprisal values rather than a missing mechanism. - Only three English constructions from one experimental paradigm (self-paced reading) are used. The claim is framed generally but the evidence base is narrow. - No direct comparison between RNNG garden path surprisals and those of GPT-2 or LSTMs at the item level is reported; it is unclear whether RNNG surprisals are qualitatively similar to or different from standard LM surprisals in the garden path condition. - The manual parse categorization in the FORCED GARDEN PATH condition is described procedurally but without reliability checks; errors in categorization would affect the upper bound estimate. Bottom Line A methodologically tight preprint that delivers a strong, well-bounded null result against a specific theoretical hypothesis, and psycholinguists and NLP researchers who invoke parse multiplicity to explain LM-human mismatches will need to contend with these findings — but the extrapolation to modern large LMs remains unverified and should not be treated as settled.
Recommended 2605.15440 PDF
#23 — Innovative (74)

Measuring Individual User Fairness with User Similarity and Effectiveness Disparity

theresiavr.github.io · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PUF (Pairwise User unFairness), the first individual user fairness evaluation measure for recommender systems that jointly accounts for both user similarity and recommendation effectiveness disparity in a single score. - Why it's important: All existing individual user fairness measures in recommender systems either ignore whether compared users are actually similar (SD, Gini, envy-based) or ignore recommendation effectiveness entirely (UF), making them incomplete implementations of the canonical Dwork et al. individual fairness definition — a deficiency that leads to systematically misleading fairness rankings of recommender algorithms. - How it does it: PUF computes the mean pairwise absolute difference in per-user effectiveness scores (P@k or NDCG@k, scaled to [0,1]) across all user pairs, weighted by pairwise user similarity (cosine or Jaccard of interaction vectors), yielding PUF ∈ [0,1] with lower scores meaning fairer. - The good: In controlled experiments that artificially vary the effectiveness distribution, PUF is the only existing measure (SD partially agrees) that exhibits the theoretically expected inverted-parabola unfairness curve — peaking when ~50% of users receive all-irrelevant recommendations — while also being the only similarity-aware measure that correctly scores near 0 for a MostFair similarity assignment and separates cleanly from a MostUnfair assignment. - The missing: No statistical significance tests are applied to the Kendall's τ comparisons across only 7 models, and the paper does not analyze PUF's behavior at production scale (millions of users) or with graded relevance. - Read it or not: Researchers and practitioners responsible for auditing or comparing recommender systems on user-side fairness should read this — it delivers a computationally efficient, theoretically grounded replacement for measures that the paper demonstrates are largely measuring the wrong thing.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Novelty (75) and Non-Obviousness (75) are the top contributors: the paper diagnoses that every existing individual-user-fairness measure in RS captures only half of the Dwork definition (either similarity-blind or effectiveness-blind), then closes that gap with PUF — a conceptual contribution that is more substantive than its simple weighted-mean formula suggests. Completeness (80) is strong: four limitations are confronted directly (no graded relevance, similarity-function dependence, group-disparity blindness, untested statistical power), and the controlled §4.4-4.6 experiments build clean ground truth that isolates each factor. Rigor (70) is solid — public code, ECIR 2026 peer review, causally clean falsification tests (UF scores ≈0.8 even under MostFair) — but is held back by zero significance testing over only 7 models per dataset and an un-analyzed binarization threshold. Resilience (72) and Practical Impact (70, open-source auditing tool) round it out, with O(m²) scaling beyond ~90K users the main open question. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*70 ≈ 74.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 80 Resilience 72 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Recommender system (RS) fairness evaluation for individual users rests on the principle that similar users should receive similar treatment. In practice, individual user fairness measures in RS literature split into two camps that each capture only half of this definition. Effectiveness-disparity measures (standard deviation, Gini index, envy-based variants) measure how unevenly recommendation quality is distributed across users, but treat all user pairs as equally comparable regardless of how similar those users actually are — flagging as unfair a system that gives better results to a heavy mainstream consumer than to a sparse niche user, even though no fairness obligation exists between dissimilar users. The sole existing similarity-aware measure (UF) measures disparity in the item-representation space (item embeddings) rather than effectiveness, meaning two similar users can both receive highly relevant recommendations yet be scored as unfairly treated because their recommended item sets differ. No measure simultaneously satisfies both dimensions. This gap matters to RS researchers who need reliable metrics to compare algorithms and to auditors who must determine whether a deployed system treats comparable users comparably. Key Idea The core trick is to multiply, for each ordered user pair, the pairwise user similarity by the absolute difference in their effectiveness scores before averaging: unfairness is only charged proportionally to how similar the users are, so dissimilar users' effectiveness gaps exert near-zero influence on the overall score. This weighting makes the measure self-consistently encode the individual fairness definition — greater expected treatment parity follows from greater user similarity — without requiring a binary similarity threshold. Methodology PUF = (2 / m(m−1)) × Σ_{u∈U} Σ_{u'∈U\{u}} sim(u,u') × |S(u) − S(u')|, where sim(u,u') ∈ [0,1] is pairwise similarity (cosine or Jaccard of binarized interaction vectors, min-max normalized per dataset), and S(u) ∈ [0,1] is a per-user effectiveness measure (P@k or NDCG@k). The formula is a weighted mean over C(m,2) pairs. Three critical design decisions: (1) using all user pairs rather than thresholding above a mean similarity cutoff (as UF does), avoiding the arbitrary threshold while still naturally discounting dissimilar pairs via the weight; (2) effectiveness-based disparity rather than item-representation disparity, grounding the measure in user utility; (3) any [0,1]-compatible similarity and effectiveness functions are supported, making PUF a family of measures rather than a single fixed metric. Empirical validation runs 7 collaborative filtering models (U-KNN, I-KNN, BPR, MVAE, NGCF, NMF, NCL via RecBole) on 4 datasets (Lastfm: 1,859 users; QK-video: 4,656 users; ML-10M: 49,378 users; ML-20M: 89,917 users), with ML-* temporally split and Lastfm/QK-video randomly split at 6:2:2. Three controlled experiments isolate effectiveness sensitivity (sequential 10% replacement with all-irrelevant recommendations), similarity-distribution sensitivity (Weibull-sampled synthetic similarities with varying skewness), and extreme-case discrimination (MostFair/MostUnfair structured similarity-effectiveness assignment). Key Results Computation time: PUF runs in under 40 seconds on all four datasets (server CPU: AMD EPYC 75F3), compared to ME/MME/PEU which take 234–2,021 seconds and UF which takes 874–1,358 seconds. In effectiveness variation experiments, only PUF and SD exhibit the expected inverted-parabola unfairness trajectory; Gini and PEU monotonically worsen as effectiveness drops (conflating fairness with effectiveness), and MME and UF are nearly invariant to effectiveness changes (MME scores ≤ 5.2×10⁻³ throughout). In user similarity distribution experiments, PUF correctly decreases unfairness as the distribution becomes more right-skewed (more dissimilar pairs), while all non-similarity-based measures remain constant; at skewness > 6 (realistic for QK-video), SD-NDCG reports ≈0.2 unfairness while PUF reports ≈0, demonstrating that SD systematically underestimates fairness for datasets with dissimilar users. In MostFair/MostUnfair extreme case experiments, PUF scores near 0 for MostFair and separates clearly for MostUnfair; UF remains ≈0.8 in both conditions. Kendall's τ between PUF and UF ranges from −0.52 to 0.14 across datasets, confirming they measure different constructs; the two measures never agree on which model is fairest. NCL achieves the highest NDCG@10 overall (0.261 on Lastfm, 0.171 on ML-20M). Notably, within standard model comparisons, PUF gives different fairness rankings than all existing measures, and UF tends to overestimate unfairness in low-similarity datasets. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The formal contribution is a new evaluation measure (PUF) that is the first in the RS individual user fairness literature to satisfy both components of the Dwork individual fairness definition simultaneously. The measure itself is simple — a weighted mean — but the conceptual contribution (identifying that the two existing measure families are each measuring a half-definition) and the empirical diagnosis (demonstrating that all existing measures fail at least one of the two sensitivity requirements) are substantive. This is not a complex algorithmic invention; it is a principled gap-fill with rigorous empirical characterization. Rigor & Reproducibility Code is publicly released at github.com/theresiavr/PUF-individual-user-fairness-recsys. The paper is accepted at ECIR 2026, so peer-reviewed. The controlled experiments in §4.4–4.6 are well-designed: the effectiveness-variation procedure creates controlled ground truth for what any fairness measure should output, providing a stringent discriminating test. However, no error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests appear on any results — all Kendall's τ comparisons are point estimates over 7 models, which is a small sample for rank correlation. The binarization threshold for ML-* datasets (ratings ≥ 3 → 1) is chosen without sensitivity analysis; all effectiveness and PUF values depend on it. UF is computed with one-hot item representations (chosen for cross-model comparability) rather than learned embeddings, which may disadvantage UF. Ablations on PUF's functional form (absolute vs. squared difference, mean vs. sum) are absent. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on CPU only, requires only a precomputed binarized user-item interaction matrix; no GPU or special infrastructure needed. - Efficient: O(m²) in user count, but < 40 seconds for up to 89K users — substantially faster than ME/MME/PEU/UF despite also being pairwise; similarity matrix is computed once per dataset and reused across models. - Effective: Controlled experiments provide causal evidence that PUF correctly responds to both effectiveness disparity and user similarity changes, while all existing measures fail at least one of these; evidence is strong within the tested regime. - Scalable: Tested up to 89,917 users (ML-20M); O(m²) complexity implies quadratic cost growth, and the paper does not address behavior above ~100K users or in streaming/online settings. The work delivers a software artifact (open-source implementation). Strengths - Fills a concrete, formally stated gap: the first measure to jointly satisfy both components of Dwork individual fairness in RS evaluation, motivated by a clear taxonomy of existing measures (Tab. 1) that exposes the gap before solving it. - Controlled experiments (§4.4–4.6) are causally clean — they fix everything except the factor under study — yielding interpretable, strong evidence for the behavioral claims rather than relying solely on observational model comparisons. - The MostFair/MostUnfair extreme-case design (§4.6) provides a particularly crisp falsification test: UF scores ≈0.8 even under the maximally fair assignment, proving it measures something other than effectiveness-based fairness. - Computational efficiency is a genuine practical contribution: PUF is 30–100× faster than UF and ME/MME/PEU on large datasets despite having the same O(m²) theoretical complexity, because it avoids per-pair effectiveness recomputation. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note: (1) PUF does not support graded relevance (binary/normalized only); (2) the choice of user similarity function is not prescribed by the measure and can affect results; (3) PUF as an individual fairness measure cannot detect systematic group disparities (e.g., all niche-preference users receiving poor recommendations), because PUF only penalizes when similar users are treated differently, not when all dissimilar users are uniformly harmed; (4) statistical power of PUF is not evaluated and is listed as future work. The authors address limitations directly and clearly — a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - With only 7 recommenders per dataset, Kendall's τ estimates have high variance; at m=7, the number of distinct rank orderings is 5,040, and small changes could flip conclusions — no bootstrap or permutation tests are reported. - The binarization of ML-* ratings (threshold = 3 on a [0.5, 5] scale) converts 60% of possible ratings to "relevant"; a threshold of 4 or 4.5 would yield a substantially different interaction matrix and different effectiveness/fairness scores, but no sensitivity analysis appears. - PUF's O(m²) cost for m > 1M users (production-scale) is not analyzed; the paper's largest dataset has ~90K users, leaving scalability to industrial deployments as an open question. - The choice of one-hot encoding for UF comparison, while motivated, means UF is evaluated in a weaker-than-optimal configuration; a reader could argue UF with learned embeddings might perform differently. - The paper does not evaluate PUF's sensitivity to data sparsity effects: for highly sparse datasets (QK-video: 99.83% sparse), similarity estimates from interaction vectors are noisy, and the impact of this noise on PUF's reliability is not characterized. Bottom Line Any RS researcher who evaluates or compares recommendation algorithms on individual user fairness should adopt PUF — the paper makes a strong empirical case that the current standard tools measure the wrong things, and PUF is both faster and more aligned with the foundational definition.
Citations
#24 — Innovative (74)

Mega-ASR: Towards In-the-wild^2 Speech Recognition via Scaling up Real-world Acoustic Simulation (110 ▲ )

Zhifei Xie, Kaiyu Pang, Haobin Zhang, Deheng Ye, Xiaobin Hu, Shuicheng Yan, Chunyan Miao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 21 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MEGA-ASR, a framework combining a 2.4M-sample compound-acoustic simulation dataset (VOICES-IN-THE-WILD-2M), a three-stage WER-graded curriculum fine-tuning procedure (A2S-SFT), and a dual-granularity RL reward scheme (DG-WGPO) to achieve robust ASR under severe, compositionally-mixed real-world acoustic degradations. - Why it's important: Real-world ASR systems routinely see WERs of 10–70% under compound acoustic conditions (simultaneous noise, reverberation, echo, dropout), yet no prior dataset or training regime targets this compositional high-WER regime at scale, leaving deployed systems prone to hallucination and semantic collapse. - How it does it: It decomposes acoustic degradation into 8 primitive signal-level effects composed into 7 atomic effects and 54 compound scenarios via an anchor-modifier taxonomy, trains in three progressive SFT stages, then applies RL with a WER-gated reward that switches between token-level Levenshtein refinement and sentence-level LCS structural recovery depending on whether sample WER is above or below 30%. - The good: On extreme conditions (NOIZEUS 0dB), MEGA-ASR achieves 19.80% WER vs. 23.97% for Qwen3-ASR-1.7B (17.4% relative reduction) and 55.78% for Gemini-3-Flash (64.5% relative reduction), while a LoRA-based acoustic router preserves clean-speech parity with the Qwen3-ASR baseline. - The missing: The synthetic portion of Voices-in-the-Wild-Bench is generated by the same simulation pipeline used for training — a direct Goodhart's Law concern — and no ablation isolates dataset scale from training recipe, making it unclear whether 2.4M simulated samples alone would suffice. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ASR researchers working on robustness and practitioners deploying speech in noisy environments; the dataset, compound-scenario taxonomy, and WER-regime-aware reward design are concrete contributions that transfer to other audio-language model work.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile (dataset + training-recipe core, with a Systems-leaning deployable artifact). Novelty (82) is high: a new 2.4M-sample compound-acoustic dataset, the anchor-modifier compound-scenario taxonomy, the WER-regime-aware dual-granularity reward, and the empirical identification of a ~30% WER failure-mode boundary are genuinely new. Non-Obviousness (80) is strong — the qualitative shift from word-level confusion to sentence-level semantic collapse and the gated reward switching are not predicted by prior work. Rigor (72) is solid (fine-grained ablations Tables 5–9, a strong 12-system contemporary baseline set, public dataset+code) but capped by no error bars/seeds, the Goodhart concern on the synthetic Voices-in-the-Wild-Bench subset, the τ=0.3-vs-0.5 main-text/appendix discrepancy, and preprint status. Practical Impact (78) reflects a plug-and-play LoRA router with <1% overhead and a released dataset, though model-weight release is unconfirmed. Completeness (70) and Resilience (65) are pulled down by the absence of a dataset-vs-recipe ablation and the thin, undercharacterized 1,500-clip real-recording evaluation. Weighted: 0.20*82 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*78 ≈ 74.
empirical Novelty 82 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 78
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Current ASR systems achieve near-1% WER on clean benchmarks but degrade to 10–70% WER in real-world environments where noise, reverberation, echo, far-field attenuation, physical obstruction, recording artifacts, and transmission dropout co-occur simultaneously. Speech system developers deploying in vehicles, meeting rooms, factories, and mobile settings face this exact gap. Prior robust ASR datasets (CHiME-4, VOiCES, NOIZEUS) cover at most two isolated degradation factors and emphasize mild WER ranges (4–10%), while real compound environments routinely push WER past 30%. Existing large audio-language models trained on these mild-condition datasets hallucinate, produce empty outputs, or generate cross-lingual content when faced with severe compositional degradations — failures that are qualitatively distinct from ordinary word-level substitutions and cannot be addressed by simply scaling model size. Key Idea The core insight is that once WER exceeds approximately 30%, the dominant failure mode in ASR shifts qualitatively from word-level acoustic confusion to sentence-level semantic collapse (hallucinations, dropped utterances, cross-lingual outputs), and standard WER-based rewards become non-discriminative in precisely this regime because intra-group score variance collapses. The solution is to decouple the training signal by WER regime: use acoustic-perceptual curriculum SFT to build grounded perception at medium WER, then apply a gated RL reward that switches from token-level edit-distance refinement (when WER < 30%) to sentence-level LCS structural recovery (when WER ≥ 30%). Methodology The pipeline has four components. (1) VOICES-IN-THE-WILD-2M construction: 8 primitive signal-level transformations (additive noise, echo delay, reverberation, nonlinear distortion, resampling, spectral filtering, loudness normalization, frame-level stutter) are composed into 7 atomic acoustic effects via physically motivated chains, then into 54 compound scenarios using an anchor-modifier decomposition: 3 scene-defining anchor effects (far-field, echo&reverb, obstructed) combined with 4 portable modifier effects (noise, recording coloration, electronic distortion, transmission dropout). A single global severity variable m ~ Uniform[0,1] parameterizes all effects within a sample coherently. Samples with WER > 70% are discarded for training stability. Final dataset: 2.4M clips, 11K hours, average WER 18.42%, drawn from LibriSpeech, CommonVoice, WenetSpeech, AISHELL-1 clean speech sources. (2) A2S-SFT (three-stage LoRA fine-tuning initialized from Qwen3-ASR-1.7B): Phase I applies a WER-graded curriculum (<30% → <50% → <70%) to the acoustic encoder and aligner only; Phase II freezes the encoder and fine-tunes the LLM on the full WER<70% split for semantic recovery; Phase III jointly fine-tunes all three modules with reduced encoder/aligner learning rate (5e-7 vs 1e-6 for LLM). (3) DG-WGPO (DAPO-based RL initialized from A2S-SFT checkpoint): Static reward R_static = R_rep × R_wer (anti-repetition gate × 1-WER). Dynamic reward R_dynamic = 0.75·R_fine + 0.25·R_struc when WER(H,R) < τ, else 0.25·R_fine + 0.75·R_struc. R_fine classifies substitutions as soft (character edit similarity ≥ 0.5) or hard and discounts accordingly. R_struc = 0.5·LCS(H,R)/|R| + 0.5·max(0, 1 − ||H|−|R||/|R|). Final reward R = 0.4·R_static + 0.6·R_dynamic. Key hyperparameters: τ=0.3 (per main text), α_s=0.4, α_dyn=0.6; K=12 rollouts, 6000 RL steps. (4) Environment-aware LoRA router: A single-layer Transformer on 80-dim log-Mel features, hidden size 128, predicts clean vs. degraded; activates LoRA deltas via delta-switching with <1% inference overhead. Critical design decisions: (a) Global severity sharing ensures degradation is coherent across effects within a sample — well-justified by preventing implausible mixtures. (b) WER threshold τ=0.3 for reward switching — empirically validated via Table 9 (τ=0.3 minimizes WER; τ=0.5 causes clear degradation). (c) Discarding WER>70% samples — justified by observed training instability; the 70% cutoff is pragmatic but not ablated for alternative thresholds. Key Results On standard robust benchmarks (CHiME-4, VOiCES, NOIZEUS), MEGA-ASR achieves average WER of 6.70% vs. 7.93% for Qwen3-ASR-1.7B (best prior open-source), 10.72% for Whisper-Large-v3, and 15.59% for Gemini-3-Flash. On the hardest VOiCES subset (rm4-babb-far): 45.69% vs. 54.01% (Qwen3-ASR). On NOIZEUS 0dB: 19.80% vs. 23.97% (Qwen3-ASR) vs. 62.40% (GPT-4o-transcribe). On Voices-in-the-Wild-Bench mixed conditions (real/sim): 2.73/4.57 vs. 8.91/14.79 (Whisper-Large-v3), corresponding to 65.8%/69.1% relative WER reduction. With the acoustic router, clean-speech performance on LibriSpeech test clean/other recovers to 1.63/3.37 (matching Qwen3-ASR's 1.62/3.40). Ablation (Table 5): removing the two A2S-SFT progressive stages costs 0.72/0.67 WER on Voices/NOIZEUS; among DG-WGPO components, removing R_struc (sentence-level) causes the largest degradation (7.54/7.85 vs. full 7.35/7.64), confirming sentence-level recovery is the critical RL contribution. LLM-judge variant matches rule-based reward within 0.1 WER while taking 3.2× longer per training step. The baseline set includes 12 current systems spanning conventional ASR (Whisper-Large-v3), large open-source audio-LMs (Kimi-Audio-7B, Qwen2.5-Omni-7B, Step-Audio-2-mini), and closed-source APIs (GPT-4o, Gemini-3-Flash, Doubao-LLM ASR) — a strong and contemporary comparison. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (VOICES-IN-THE-WILD-2M and Voices-in-the-Wild-Bench), NEW METHOD (A2S-SFT curriculum + DG-WGPO reward), NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (MEGA-ASR with routing). The anchor-modifier compound scenario taxonomy and the WER-regime-aware dual-granularity reward are genuinely novel designs; the empirical identification of a ~30% WER threshold as a qualitative failure-mode boundary is a new finding. A2S-SFT applies established curriculum learning to a multimodal encoder-decoder architecture in a straightforward but well-executed way. The DG-WGPO reward is principled domain-specific reward engineering built on DAPO, not a fundamentally new RL algorithm. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and fine-grained (Tables 5–9) covering each DG-WGPO component, hyperparameter sensitivity, and reward variant. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported — standard practice in ASR but a gap. The VOICES-IN-THE-WILD-2M dataset and Voices-in-the-Wild-Bench evaluation code are publicly released (HuggingFace + GitHub). Model weights are not explicitly stated as released in the paper text. A notable methodological concern: the 3,500-clip synthetic portion of Voices-in-the-Wild-Bench is generated by the same simulation pipeline used to construct the training set, creating potential Goodhart's Law contamination for results on that subset; only the 1,500 real-recording clips provide a clean estimate of sim-to-real transfer. A second concern: the main paper states τ=0.3 (Section 4.2.2, Eq. 7) but Appendix Table 30 reports τ=0.5 for the actual training run — an unexplained discrepancy. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.19833v1, May 2026); results have not been peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL / SYSTEMS. - Practical: The system is built on Qwen3-ASR-1.7B with LoRA adapters; training requires 2–3 commodity GPUs; inference cost matches the base model with a <1% routing overhead — commercially practical. - Efficient: LoRA keeps parameter count modest; the router is a 128-hidden-dim single-layer Transformer; delta-switching avoids model reloading; total inference time on CHiME-4 is 371s vs. 374s for the baseline (Table 21). - Effective: Gains hold across both the 1,500 real-recording clips in Voices-in-the-Wild-Bench and three established community benchmarks (CHiME-4, VOiCES, NOIZEUS), providing meaningful real-world validation, though the real-recording eval set is small and sparsely characterized. - Scalable: All experiments use 1.7B parameters; scaling to larger models (7B, 70B) is not evaluated. Dataset scale vs. recipe contribution is not ablated, so it is unknown whether the approach transfers to a 10× larger training corpus or a significantly larger model. The paper delivers a public dataset (HuggingFace) and benchmark evaluation code (GitHub), but explicit model weight release is not confirmed in the paper text. Strengths - The anchor-modifier decomposition for constructing 54 compound scenarios is well-principled: it prevents physically implausible combinations (e.g., two conflicting room geometries) while systematically covering real compound environments, giving the dataset genuine ecological validity. - The empirical identification of a WER threshold (~30%) at which ASR failure shifts from word-level substitution to sentence-level semantic collapse is a concrete and actionable finding that motivates and validates the dual-granularity reward design. - The rule-based DG-WGPO reward matches an LLM-judge (Gemini-2.5-flash-lite) variant within 0.1 WER while being 3.2× faster per training step — a practically significant result that shows the reward captures genuine speech quality signal without expensive oracle calls. - The LoRA delta-switching router preserves full clean-speech capability without maintaining separate model copies, making MEGA-ASR a genuine plug-and-play upgrade rather than a specialized-domain replacement. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The paper has no dedicated Limitations section. The authors acknowledge (§4.3) that training on heavily degraded audio "partially erodes complementary capabilities such as clean-speech recognition, hotword recognition, and streaming ASR" and propose the routing mechanism as mitigation — they address this directly and provide supporting evidence. They implicitly acknowledge the simulation-to-real gap by noting that scraping real-world audio was "costly and fundamentally unscalable" (§3.2), but do not quantify the residual sim-to-real degradation. No other limitations are stated. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Benchmark-training data leakage: 3,500 of 5,000 Voices-in-the-Wild-Bench clips are synthetic using the same pipeline as training data; MEGA-ASR's benchmark-leading performance on these clips may partly reflect simulation-domain memorization rather than generalization. - τ discrepancy: Main text specifies τ=0.3 but Appendix Table 30 reports the actual run used τ=0.5; the difference is consequential given sensitivity analysis in Table 9 (τ=0.5 causes WER degradation from 7.64 to 7.70 on NOIZEUS). - No dataset-vs-recipe ablation: There is no experiment that trains A2S-SFT+DG-WGPO on an existing robust dataset (e.g., CHiME-4 + VOiCES augmentation) to isolate how much of the gain comes from VOICES-IN-THE-WILD-2M specifically vs. the training recipe. - Real-recording evaluation is small and undercharacterized: The 1,500 real clips come from internet sources and 16 participants; speaker demographics, domains, languages, and collection conditions are not reported. - No model-size scaling: All experiments use 1.7B parameters; whether A2S-SFT and DG-WGPO transfer to 7B or larger models is unevaluated. - Semantic evaluation coverage is narrow: LLM-judge metrics (Table 7) are reported only on VOICES + NOIZEUS averaged, not broken out per scenario type or extended to the full benchmark suite. Bottom Line MEGA-ASR delivers well-supported improvements in compound-acoustic ASR with a publicly released dataset that fills a real gap, and the DG-WGPO reward design is a transferable contribution; however, the sim-to-real validation is thin and the benchmark-training overlap requires caution when interpreting results on the synthetic Voices-in-the-Wild-Bench subset.
Recommended 2605.19833 PDF
#25 — Innovative (74)

Consistent PCA and Spectral Clustering

openreview.net · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces online algorithms for PCA (ConsistentPCA) and spectral clustering (PCM) that provably bound output inconsistency — the cumulative change in principal components or cluster assignments as data arrive sequentially — while maintaining approximation guarantees on solution quality. - Why it's important: In streaming or incrementally-growing datasets (social networks, recommendation systems, live monitoring), drastic reorganization of PCA projections or cluster labels upon each new data point disrupts downstream tasks and erodes user trust; this paper is the first to give provable consistency-quality tradeoff guarantees for these ubiquitous methods. - How it does it: Adding a subspace-preserving regularizer φ_SP(W, W') = ½||Π_W − Π_W'||²_F to the PCA/spectral objective forces the new subspace to stay close to the previous one in Frobenius norm, and the regularized problem reduces to a single standard eigendecomposition plus Procrustes alignment. - The good: Theorem 3.3 proves that the O(ε⁻¹) inconsistency bound achieved by ConsistentPCA is tight — any online PCA algorithm outputting a (1−ε)-approximate solution must incur Ω(ε⁻¹) inconsistency — establishing that the algorithm is optimal in this regime. - The missing: The inconsistency bound for spectral clustering (Lemma 4.3) depends on OPT^(t), the k-median optimum at each step, which is not characterized and can vary widely; the practical guarantee is therefore difficult to evaluate a priori, and graphs tested have at most 1,000 nodes. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on streaming dimensionality reduction or dynamic graph clustering and care about stability guarantees; the theory is tight and clean, the empirical improvements over GD/MEG/PCQ are consistent across datasets, and the lower bound closes the problem for the k=1 case.
Why this rank: Empirical profile with a central theoretical component. Rigor (80) and Novelty (78) anchor the score — adversarial (no i.i.d.) proofs, released code, 30/100-repeat error bars, and the first provable consistency-quality tradeoff for online PCA plus the first theoretical grounding of the 17-year-old PCM heuristic, capped by a tight matching lower bound (Theorem 3.3). The composite is held below 75 by Completeness (70) and Practical Impact (60): the optimal λ requires knowing the final-step objective ‖C^(n)‖ in advance (unavailable in a true online setting), the spectral-clustering bound depends on an uncharacterized OPT^(t), and experiments top out at ~1,000 nodes with an O(n²) Laplacian wall that the paper does not address. Weighted: 0.20*78 + 0.25*80 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*60 = 74.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 72 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: PCA and spectral clustering are cornerstone tools for data visualization, dimensionality reduction, and network analysis. In online settings — where new data points or edges arrive sequentially — standard algorithms recompute the solution from scratch and can produce dramatically different outputs at each step, even for a single added data point. Figure 1 of the paper illustrates standard PCA on MNIST where adding one class of images causes the entire 2D projection to reorganize, destroying visual continuity. This instability cascades into downstream tasks (e.g., labeling pipelines, recommendation systems), reduces user trust, and complicates longitudinal analysis. Prior work on low-recourse online algorithms (k-clustering, covering problems) cannot handle eigendecomposition-based methods. Sensitivity analysis for spectral clustering exists but yields useful bounds only when the cluster structure is very strong. The gap is: no algorithm with simultaneous provable approximation quality and provable inconsistency bounds for online PCA or spectral clustering. Key Idea: The core insight is that the subspace-preserving regularizer φ_SP(W, W') = ½||Π_W − Π_W'||²_F, which measures the squared Frobenius distance between projection matrices, is equal to 2Σsin²θᵢ where θᵢ are the principal angles between subspaces — exactly the distance used in the Davis-Kahan theorem. Adding this regularizer to the PCA objective yields a modified matrix C^(t) − λ(I − W^(t−1)⊗W^(t−1)) whose top-k eigenvectors solve the regularized problem, meaning consistency can be enforced with a single eigendecomposition at each step, with no asymptotic overhead in algorithm structure. Methodology: ConsistentPCA operates in two steps per time step t. First, compute the top-k eigenvectors of the modified covariance matrix C^(t) − λ(I − W^(t−1)⊗W^(t−1)), which maximizes variance while penalizing departure from the previous subspace. Second, apply Procrustes alignment: solve minᴿ||W̃^(t)R − W^(t−1)||²_F subject to R^T R = I_k, using the SVD of ⟨W̃^(t), W^(t−1)⟩. This handles the eigenvector sign/permutation ambiguity. For spectral clustering, the PCM algorithm (Chi et al. 2009) is analyzed: minimize tr⟨W, L^(t)W⟩ + λ·φ_SP(W, W^(t−1)) subject to orthonormality constraints, then apply D^1-sampling (k-means++ with distance weights) for the k-median step. Three critical design decisions: (1) using k-median instead of k-means improves the inconsistency bound by a factor of √k; (2) Procrustes alignment is essential to prevent artificial inconsistency from eigenvector permutation; (3) the parameter λ = Θ(ε||C^(n)||_{Fk}/k) yields a clean tradeoff between (1−ε) approximation quality and O(ε⁻¹k) total inconsistency. Proofs use a telescoping argument on the objective difference to bound cumulative subspace drift. Key Results: For PCA: with λ = Θ(ε||C^(n)||_{Fk}/k), ConsistentPCA achieves (1−ε)-approximate variance while incurring total inconsistency O(ε⁻¹k), versus the trivial bound O(kn). The matching lower bound (Theorem 3.3) for k=1 in ℝ² shows this is tight: any (1−ε)-approximate online PCA algorithm must have inconsistency Ω(ε⁻¹). Empirically, across the LFW face image dataset (d=2919, k∈{2,3,5,10}) and four OpenML classification datasets (gas-drift, har, micro-mass, mnist_784, up to 1,000 points), ConsistentPCA dominates the GD and MEG baselines from Nie et al. (2016) across all tested λ values, while GD and MEG degrade sharply when their learning rate η is off. Spherical PCA provides no meaningful improvement over vanilla PCA in the inconsistency-quality tradeoff. For spectral clustering: PCM achieves (1+ε)-approximate spectral embedding quality and sub-linear inconsistency O(k√(km/ε)) in the number of edges (vs. trivial O(km)). On a real temporal email graph (684 nodes, 6,412→26,994 edges over 20 steps) and 6 Facebook community graphs (100+ nodes), PCM dominates DSp-SC and PCQ on all four inconsistency metrics (embedding, centroid, clustering assignment). On SBM synthetic graphs up to 1,000 nodes, ARI and NMI differences between PCM and BatchSC are negligible (ARI 0.500 vs. 0.505 at most), confirming that consistency improvements do not compromise cluster quality. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The subspace-preserving regularizer itself was proposed by Chi et al. (2009) for spectral clustering under the name PCM, but without theoretical analysis. This paper's contribution is: (a) extending the regularizer to online PCA with a new ConsistentPCA algorithm; (b) providing the first provable approximation and inconsistency guarantees for both PCA and PCM; (c) proving a tight lower bound (Theorem 3.3) establishing that the O(ε⁻¹) inconsistency is optimal. The use of Procrustes alignment to handle eigenvector ambiguity and the connection to the Davis-Kahan theorem are genuine technical contributions, not routine applications of existing ideas. Rigor & Reproducibility: Theoretical claims are fully proved in the appendix with explicit assumptions stated (no i.i.d. assumption — inputs can be chosen adversarially, which is a credibility strength). Lemmas 3.1, 3.2, 4.1, 4.2, and 4.3 together yield the main approximation and consistency results; the lower bound proof (Theorem 3.3) constructs an explicit adversarial sequence. Experiments are repeated 30 times (PCA) and 100 times (SBM), with error bars shown. The paper does not report error bars on the quality metrics for spectral clustering on real data (only on synthetic SBM). Code is publicly released at github.com/sato9hara/consistent-pca-sc. No ablation on the Procrustes alignment step is provided — it is unclear how much it contributes to the empirical improvement independently of the regularizer. The lower bound (Theorem 3.3) covers only k=1 in ℝ²; the tightness for k>1 is not established. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (algorithms are implemented and evaluated on real data) with a THEORETICAL component (bounds are central). - Practical: Both algorithms reduce to standard eigendecomposition at each step and run on commodity CPUs; experiments use a 256-core AMD EPYC workstation but the per-step cost is a single eigensolver call, making it deployable on standard hardware. - Efficient: ConsistentPCA adds a rank-k perturbation to the covariance matrix and one SVD for Procrustes alignment per step — the same asymptotic cost as vanilla PCA; PCM similarly adds only the regularizer modification to the Laplacian eigendecomposition. No runtime comparison tables are reported. - Effective: Empirical results consistently show ConsistentPCA and PCM achieve better quality-consistency tradeoffs than all baselines across multiple real datasets; ARI/NMI in SBM experiments confirm cluster quality is preserved. - Scalable: Real-data experiments are limited to at most 1,000-node graphs for spectral clustering and 1,000-point datasets for PCA. Scale behavior is not tested at 10×–100× these sizes; the O(n²) Laplacian storage for spectral clustering is a hard limit for large graphs that the paper does not address. The paper delivers working open-source code; no trained model or library package is released. Strengths: - The tight lower bound (Theorem 3.3) is the paper's sharpest result: it proves ConsistentPCA is optimal in the accuracy-inconsistency tradeoff for k=1, not merely competitive. - The theoretical framework is adversarial (no i.i.d. assumption), making guarantees applicable to worst-case streaming scenarios. - Providing the first theoretical analysis of Chi et al.'s (2009) PCM regularizer — which had only empirical justification for 17 years — closes a well-known open question in the evolutionary clustering literature. - ConsistentPCA subsumes vanilla PCA as λ→0, giving a principled and practically interpretable hyperparameter with a wide effective range, unlike GD/MEG whose learning rate η must be tuned narrowly. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note two open problems: (1) establishing tight lower bounds on the approximation-consistency tradeoff (the lower bound covers k=1 only); (2) designing a consistent spectral clustering algorithm for the setting where existing point positions are perturbed (the current PCM analysis covers edge additions, not node feature updates). The authors also acknowledge that k-means clustering yields a worse inconsistency bound (by √k) than k-median. These are stated constructively, not minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The inconsistency bound for spectral clustering (Lemma 4.3) contains OPT^(t) — the optimal k-median cost — which can vary arbitrarily across time steps. The paper does not characterize or bound OPT^(t) in terms of graph properties, making the guarantee difficult to evaluate in practice without running the optimal solver. - The practical choice of λ (set optimally to Θ(ε||C^(n)||_{Fk}/k)) requires knowledge of the final-step objective ||C^(n)||_{Fk} in advance, which is unavailable in a true online setting. The paper provides no discussion of adaptive or data-free λ selection strategies. - Experimental scale for spectral clustering (max 1,000 nodes) is modest; dynamic real-world networks (social graphs, knowledge graphs) regularly exceed 10⁶ nodes, and the O(n²) Laplacian eigendecomposition cost is prohibitive there. - The lower bound (Theorem 3.3) is for k=1 in ℝ²; whether the O(ε⁻¹k) upper bound is tight for k>1 remains open and is potentially a significant gap. - No wall-clock runtime comparison between ConsistentPCA and vanilla PCA is reported; the Procrustes SVD adds a k×k computation, which matters for large k. Bottom Line: This is a clean, well-executed theory-and-experiments paper that fills a genuine gap — providing the first tight optimality result for consistent online PCA and the first theoretical grounding for a widely-used spectral clustering heuristic — and practitioners in streaming analytics or evolving graph analysis who need stability guarantees will find both the algorithms and the lower bound directly actionable.
Citations
#26 — Innovative (74)

TransitLM: A Large-Scale Dataset and Benchmark for Map-Free Transit Route Generation (83 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 22 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TransitLM, a dataset of 13.9 million transit route planning records from four Chinese cities plus a three-task benchmark (TransitBench), together with a continual pre-training + supervised fine-tuning recipe that produces a 4B-parameter model capable of end-to-end transit route generation without any map infrastructure. - Why it's important: Conventional transit routing requires structured map data and production-grade routing engines that are expensive to maintain, city-specific, and unavailable in low-resource settings; demonstrating that this knowledge can be distilled into model weights from planning logs opens a path to deploying planners without API dependencies. - How it does it: Station IDs are registered as dedicated vocabulary tokens, the model is continually pre-trained on 20B+ tokens of structured route-session text to internalize network topology, then fine-tuned on 30K task-specific examples per benchmark task. - The good: The GPS-only ablation — where all natural-language cues are stripped and only raw coordinates are provided — shows near-zero performance degradation for CPT-trained models (Route Exact Match drops by ≤0.6pp on Optimal Route Generation) while general-purpose LLMs collapse to <1% Route Exact Match, providing strong evidence that spatial grounding is genuinely encoded in the weights rather than recovered from textual hints. - The missing: All training data comes from a single commercial platform (Amap) in a single snapshot day; the evaluation protocol compares predicted routes against the routing engine's own output rather than against independently validated travel paths, so the benchmark measures alignment with one platform's preference model, not ground truth optimality. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on geospatial language models, urban AI, or dataset construction for structured sequence generation; practitioners building transit planners for non-Chinese cities should note the significant geographic and linguistic scope limitations before extrapolating.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + empirical finding). Rigor (72) is the heaviest contributor and is strong — thorough, well-controlled ablations (data scaling, single- vs. multi-city, CPT vs. SFT-only, GPS-only, tool-augmented baselines), publicly released dataset/code/models — but pulled down by a single seed with no error bars, preprint status (no peer review), and a genuinely circular evaluation where ground-truth labels are the same Amap engine's filtered output. Novelty (70) is real but bounded: the dataset scale/completeness and the empirical demonstration that spatial grounding emerges from CPT weights are the contributions, while the training recipe itself is established technique. Non-Obviousness (75) is high — the GPS-only ablation cleanly isolates weight-encoded spatial knowledge from textual lookup, a falsifiable and surprising result. Completeness (85) is excellent given the unusually candid limitations section and inclusion of the strongest tool-augmented baseline. Resilience (70) and Impact (70) are solid but capped by the closed single-platform evaluation ecosystem and Chinese-only, vocabulary-growth-bounded scaling. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*85 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*70 = 74.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 85 Resilience 70 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Urban transit route planning in production systems requires a full pipeline: map data retrieval, a topological routing algorithm (Dijkstra, RAPTOR, Transfer Patterns), and a ranking stage. This pipeline is expensive, requires continuous maintenance as networks change, and fails gracefully only when map data is available. The raw planning logs continuously generated by transit platforms implicitly encode the same routing knowledge (boarding stations, transfer logic, user preferences) but no existing dataset packages this signal in a form suitable for training language models to bypass the map dependency. Existing trajectory datasets (T-Drive, Porto Taxi, GeoLife) lack station structure; static network datasets (GTFS, CPTOND-2025) contain no user behavior. The gap is a dataset that combines complete route structures with behavioral annotations across sufficient scale to enable data-driven, map-free planning. Key Idea: Register every transit station (120,845 total) as a dedicated single token in the model vocabulary, then train the model on textual serializations of real planning sessions via next-token prediction. Because each station is an atomic token, the model cannot hallucinate station IDs through character-level composition, and the pre-training signal forces the model to associate each station-token with its spatial neighborhood and topological connections before any task supervision is applied. The result is that GPS-to-station grounding and network connectivity are represented in the weights rather than looked up at inference time. Methodology: The pipeline has two stages. In continual pre-training (CPT), all three knowledge sources (12.9M route sessions, 880K station descriptions, 148K line descriptions) are serialized to natural-language text in Chinese and packed to 4,096-token sequences; next-token prediction is applied over full sequences for ≈15,000 steps (≈3 epochs) with cosine learning-rate scheduling, AdamW, and DeepSpeed ZeRO-3 across 64 96GB PPUs. In supervised fine-tuning (SFT), the CPT checkpoint is fine-tuned for one epoch on 30K examples per task using response-only loss. Three benchmark tasks are defined: Optimal Route Generation (single best route as JSON), Preference-Aware Route Generation (route conditioned on one of four explicit preferences), and Multi-Route Generation (three diverse routes). Backbone models are Qwen3-0.6B/1.7B/4B-Base. A joint variant (4B-Joint) is fine-tuned on the union of all three SFT datasets to test multi-task generalization. Design decisions: (1) dedicated station tokens — well-justified by the hallucination problem but imposes linear vocabulary growth with geographic expansion; (2) user-selected route placed first in CPT sequences to implicitly encode preference — clever but means the model learns one platform's ranking policy, not an objective optimality criterion; (3) SFT data drawn from a separate time period to prevent leakage — appropriate. Key Results: On Optimal Route Generation (10,000 test samples across four cities), Qwen3-4B achieves 97.0% Connectivity, 98.5% Station Grounding, 91.0% Distance Plausibility, 71.0% Route Exact Match, 98.5% Estimation Accuracy, and 1.33% MAPE. The joint variant (4B-Joint) reaches 97.9% Connectivity and 73.7% Route Exact Match, with no metric regression on any of the three tasks relative to the single-task baseline. On Preference-Aware Planning, 4B-Joint reaches 90.5% Preference Compliance and 52.6% Route Exact Match. On Multi-Route Generation, 4B-Joint reaches 67.2% Route Exact Match with Route Diversity 0.547. In the comparison against six frontier general-purpose LLMs (GPT-5.4-pro, DeepSeek-V4-Pro, Gemini-3.1-Pro, Claude-Opus-4.6, Qwen3.6-Plus, Doubao-Seed-2.0-Pro) under a strictly easier evaluation (boarding/alighting only, no intermediate stations), the best LLM (Gemini-3.1-Pro) achieves only 75.5% Connectivity and 40.2% Route Exact Match; the smallest TransitLM model (Qwen3-0.6B) surpasses all six. The GPS-only ablation shows TransitLM 4B-Joint degrades by 0.8pp Route Exact Match and 0.5pp Estimation Accuracy when all text is removed, whereas general-purpose LLMs drop from ~40% to <1% Route Exact Match under the same condition. The tool-augmented LLM comparison (Appendix F.5) shows that LLMs given access to the Amap API achieve up to 74.4% Route Exact Match — approximately matching 4B-Joint — but only by selecting from routing engine outputs; TransitLM generates complete intermediate station sequences from scratch. Data scaling follows a clear hierarchy: connectivity is learned at 6.25% of CPT data (94.0%), while Route Exact Match requires the full corpus (71.0% vs. 49.9% at 6.25%). Training on all four cities costs only 3.5pp Route Exact Match on Beijing compared to a Beijing-only model. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The dataset and benchmark are the primary contributions; the training approach (continual pre-training + vocabulary expansion + SFT) applies established techniques. What is genuinely novel is the scale and completeness of the transit planning corpus (combining real sessions, station topology, and line descriptions in a form suitable for LLM training), the three-task benchmark with formally defined metrics, and the empirical demonstration that spatial grounding can emerge purely from training data without coordinate lookup modules. The vocabulary-expansion trick for structured-ID generation is previously applied in other domains but its use for transit station tokens is a practical contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are thorough and well-designed: data scaling across five CPT fractions, single-city vs. multi-city, CPT vs. SFT-only (with controlled data volume), GPS-only, and tool-augmented baselines. The SFT-only vs. CPT comparison (Table 12) is particularly informative, revealing that SFT-only achieves higher standard Route Exact Match (74.9% vs. 71.0%) but collapses under GPS-only input. No error bars or significance tests are reported; all evaluations use greedy decoding with a fixed seed (seed=1), so variance is not characterized. The dataset and evaluation code are publicly released at the URLs stated in the abstract. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.22355v1); it has not been peer-reviewed. A notable methodological concern: the ground-truth labels in all tasks are defined as the routes produced by Amap's production routing engine filtered by user selection. This means the benchmark measures alignment with one commercial platform's optimization policy rather than with independently verified optimal routes. A model that learns to mimic Amap's ranking will score well regardless of whether those routes are objectively best. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: Training requires 64 96GB PPUs for 6 days (4B model); inference uses greedy decoding and fits on a single high-end GPU, making the deployed model practically accessible on commercial hardware. - Efficient: The vocabulary expansion adds 120,845 tokens, increasing embedding table size substantially; at 4B parameters this is manageable but nationwide Chinese coverage would require ~1.8M additional tokens, imposing severe memory overhead the authors acknowledge has no current solution. - Effective: At 97.0% Connectivity and 71.0% Route Exact Match on a 10,000-sample held-out set, the model demonstrably solves the stated task for four cities, though evaluation against the same routing engine that generated training data means true effectiveness on novel OD pairs or network states is unverified. - Scalable: The paper tests models up to 4B parameters and 120,845 stations across four cities; nationwide or global expansion is blocked by linear vocabulary growth and the need to retrain on new data when the network changes — the authors state this explicitly and have no empirical evidence beyond four cities. The work delivers a publicly released dataset, benchmark with evaluation code, and trained models. Strengths: - The GPS-only ablation is a rigorous and non-obvious experiment that cleanly separates spatial knowledge (encoded in weights via CPT) from textual lookup (available only when place names appear in the query). The near-zero degradation is a strong and falsifiable claim backed by 10,000-sample evaluation. - The SFT-only ablation (Table 12) controls for total data volume, showing that CPT is not just adding data but changing the nature of the learned representations — this is the most intellectually honest experiment in the paper. - The tool-augmented LLM comparison (Appendix F.5) is appropriate inclusion of the strongest competitive baseline rather than only comparing against zero-shot LLMs; most dataset papers omit this. - The vocabulary token design is a practically sound engineering choice that prevents character-level hallucination and is well-justified empirically. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge: (1) four Chinese cities from a single platform, with no evidence the framework generalizes to different topologies or languages; (2) linear vocabulary growth as a barrier to geographic scaling (1.8M tokens for all of China); (3) static network snapshot with no ability to incorporate real-time service changes without retraining; (4) evaluation relies on structural comparison against routing engine output rather than real-trip validation or user satisfaction; (5) ground-truth routes reflect one platform's ranking strategy. This is an unusually candid limitations section. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most significant unacknowledged issue is circular evaluation: training data is generated by Amap's routing engine, ground-truth labels are filtered versions of the same engine's output, and the tool-augmented baseline also calls the same API. The entire evaluation ecosystem is closed around one platform's notion of a "good route." A model could in principle overfit to Amap's routing heuristics and score perfectly while producing routes a different engine or a real passenger would reject. Second, the comparison with general-purpose LLMs uses a simplified output format (boarding/alighting only), but the paper does not report what percentage of TransitLM's performance advantage comes from the harder output format versus genuinely better spatial knowledge — the argument that "domain knowledge is the bottleneck" would be strengthened by evaluating both systems under identical output constraints. Third, the 0.6B model evaluated under GPS-only achieves near-identical scores to the full-text condition (61.0% vs. 62.1% Route Exact Match), which is surprising — but the paper does not investigate whether this means very small models are also robust or whether the 0.6B model is simply not learning text-specific shortcuts at all. Fourth, only one calendar day of data is used; whether performance degrades for time-of-day sensitive routing or seasonal variation is unexamined. Bottom Line: A practitioner building transit planning systems for Chinese cities or researching domain-adaptive language models should read this paper — the dataset, benchmark, and GPS-only robustness finding are genuine contributions — but should treat the performance numbers as measuring alignment with one commercial routing engine rather than real-world route quality.
Recommended 2605.22355 PDF
#27 — Innovative (73)

Scratchpad Patching: Decoupling Compute from Patch Size in Byte-Level Language Models

arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Scratchpad Patching (SP), a mechanism that inserts transient within-patch computation states into patch-based byte-level language models, decoupling how often patch-level context is refreshed from the committed patch sequence length and KV-cache footprint. - Why it's important: Larger patches in byte-level models reduce compute and KV-cache cost but force non-final bytes to predict from stale previous-patch representations — a structural failure mode the authors term "patch lag" — creating a hard quality-efficiency trade-off that existing patchification strategies cannot escape. - How it does it: At high-entropy byte positions within a patch, SP recomputes an updated patch aggregate over all bytes seen so far and passes it through the main trunk, broadcasting a fresher context to subsequent bytes; because these scratchpad states are transient (never written to the persistent KV cache), they do not grow the committed patch sequence. - The good: At 16 bytes per patch, SP-augmented fixed-size patching achieves 54.2 average NLU accuracy versus 54.1 for the byte-level baseline while using a 16× smaller KV cache and 3–4× less inference compute, recovering virtually all quality lost to aggressive patching. - The missing: All experiments use ~2B-parameter models trained on ~400B bytes; there is no evidence that the quality-efficiency gains hold at larger scale, and no code or model weights are released. - Read it or not: Researchers and practitioners working on tokenizer-free or byte-level language modeling should read this — it precisely diagnoses patch lag as an architectural bottleneck, proposes a clean generic fix, and challenges the prevailing focus on patchification strategy by showing compute allocation within patches matters more.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (80) and Novelty (78) drive the score: the transient-scratchpad mechanism has no direct precedent, and the empirical finding that within-patch compute allocation matters more than patchification strategy (collapsing differentiated families in BPB-FLOPs space) is non-obvious. Rigor (72) is strong on design — shared-backbone, fixed-data, FLOPs-controlled comparisons across four patchifier families and three domains — but lacks seeds/error bars and releases no code or weights. Completeness (75) is helped by honest limitations (no hierarchical extension, no training-FLOP savings). Impact (55, weight 10) is limited by the absence of any released artifact, partly offset by post-hoc inference-time compute tuning. The main caveat is no scaling beyond ~2B params, leaving the central efficiency claim unverified at scale. Composite: 0.20*78 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*55 = 73.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 75 Resilience 72 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Patch-based tokenizer-free language models (MegaByte, SpaceByte, BLT, H-Net, and related work) aggregate contiguous bytes into patches and run an expensive main trunk once per patch, reducing trunk compute and KV-cache footprint relative to pure byte-level modeling. However, only the final byte in each patch can condition on the completed current-patch representation; every earlier byte must use the stale trunk output from the previous patch. As average patch size grows, this asymmetry — patch lag — widens: non-final bytes predict from increasingly outdated context, degrading bits-per-byte and downstream quality. This tightly couples modeling capacity to patch size, limiting the efficiency gains that larger patches can provide. Prior work addresses the boundary placement decision (where to end a patch) but not the staleness problem inside patches. Key Idea: The core insight is that within-patch intermediate representations can be treated as transient computation: a partial patch aggregate (covering bytes from the patch start to the current position) is passed through the main trunk and its output broadcast to the byte decoder, without ever being stored in the persistent KV cache. Because the KV cache only stores committed patch states, the inference memory footprint is unchanged regardless of how many scratchpads fire within a patch. Triggering these refreshes selectively at high next-byte-prediction-entropy positions allocates compute to information-dense regions rather than uniformly across all bytes, capturing most of the quality benefit at a fraction of the compute cost of dense updates. Methodology: SP augments the standard five-component patch-based architecture (encoder E, patchifier P, main trunk M, unpatchifier U, decoder D). Each patch ℓ spanning bytes [s_ℓ, e_ℓ] is associated with per-position binary indicators p_n ∈ {0,1}. When p_n=1, the partial aggregate z^t_ℓ = Aggregate(x_{s_ℓ:n}) is formed via the same cross-attention mechanism used for committed patches and forwarded through M; the resulting trunk output z̃^t_ℓ replaces the previous-patch state z̃_{ℓ−1} for subsequent byte positions until the next scratchpad or patch boundary. Training unrolls all scratchpad states concatenated into the trunk input with a specialized causal attention mask: each scratchpad attends only to itself and committed states from strictly earlier patches, not to other scratchpads within the same patch. This avoids within-trunk recurrence between scratchpads, enabling full parallel training. At inference, scratchpads are computed on the fly and immediately discarded, incurring no KV-cache growth. Scratchpad triggering uses an auxiliary 2-layer Transformer LM head on the encoder (gradient stopped) whose prediction entropy H_n exceeds a per-patchifier-family threshold τ_SP (1.5 for fixed-size and SpaceByte, 1.0 for entropy-based, 2.5 for H-Net). Critical design decisions: (1) transient-not-persistent is the enabling mechanism — justified by the memory analysis; (2) entropy-based selective triggering substantially outperforms fixed-stride and dense updates in compute-quality trade-off — confirmed by ablations across code, NL, and math domains; (3) scratchpads share their position index with the committed patch state, preventing intra-patch trunk recurrence and enabling training parallelism, at the cost of limiting the scratchpad's ability to attend to earlier scratchpads within the same patch. Key Results: All models are ~2B parameters trained on ~400B bytes (DCLM, Stack v2, OpenWebMath). On eight NLU benchmarks (ARC-E/C, BoolQ, HellaSwag, OBQA, PIQA, WinoGrande, MMLU): Fixed (p=16) baseline scores 48.0 average; Fixed (p=16)+SP scores 54.2, matching the byte-level baseline at 54.1 while using 16× smaller KV cache and 2.9× fewer FLOPs/byte at inference. Fixed (p=8)+SP scores 53.8; SpaceByte+SP scores 56.2 (best patch-based); tokenizer-based scores 55.9. On code generation (MBPP pass@1): Fixed (p=16) scores 18.2% → Fixed (p=16)+SP 27.5%, with 16× KV-cache reduction and 4.5× FLOPs/byte reduction; Fixed (p=4)+SP scores 31.9% versus byte-level baseline 26.3%. On multilingual FLORES-200 (200 languages), SP consistently improves BPB rank across languages for all patchifier families. FLOPs-matched comparison confirms SP improvements on 3/4 patchifier families arise from better-targeted compute rather than additional total compute; H-Net is the exception, where SP can slightly degrade BPB under strict FLOPs matching due to spatial coupling between learned boundaries and entropy-triggered scratchpads. Ablations on triggering strategy confirm entropy-based selection outperforms fixed-stride and dense updates; counter-intuitively, dense updates (τ_SP=0) hurt NLU BPB slightly relative to selective thresholds, because trivially predictable prose bytes dilute training signal. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The transient-scratchpad mechanism for within-patch context refresh is a genuine architectural contribution with no direct precedent in the byte-level modeling literature. The empirical finding that compute allocation across byte positions matters more than patchification strategy — causing previously differentiated patchifier families to cluster tightly in BPB-FLOPs space after SP is applied — is non-obvious and broadly impactful. The connection to adaptive compute (Mixture-of-Depths, PHD-Transformer) and universal transformers is noted, but the specific application to patch-level KV-cache decoupling is new. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations on triggering strategy are thorough, spanning entropy thresholds, fixed strides, and whitespace heuristics across three data domains, with training-FLOPs-controlled comparisons. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported — standard for large-scale pretraining but a gap. All baselines are re-implemented in a shared backbone, eliminating architecture confounders; however, labels denote re-implemented variants, not the original published architectures, so results may not reproduce exactly against those codebases. No code, model weights, or checkpoints are released (not stated in the paper). The fixed-data training regime (same raw bytes per model) controls data exposure, though total FLOPs differ across configurations. The paper is a preprint; results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile): - Practical: SP uses standard Transformer operations (cross-attention, MLP); it runs on commercially available hardware, though the paper reports experiments on TPUs. - Efficient: At inference, SP adds multiple within-patch trunk forward passes (3–4× relative to no-SP at the same patch size) but keeps KV-cache size equal to standard patching; the scratchpad frequency is a single tunable threshold that can be adjusted post-hoc without retraining to trade compute for quality on a smooth curve. - Effective: Evidence convincingly shows SP addresses patch lag on NLU (p=16+SP matches byte-level), code generation (substantial pass@1 gains), and multilingual benchmarks; proxy and downstream evaluations are consistent. - Scalable: Tested only at ~2B parameters and ~400B bytes; no scaling experiments or claims are made — a significant unverified gap given that patch lag and entropy patterns may change at larger model or data scale. The work delivers no software artifact; it stops at paper form. Strengths: - Patch lag is precisely defined (Eq. 1→2 transition), giving the problem a concrete mathematical handle rather than an intuitive complaint about patching. - SP is demonstrated across four structurally distinct patchifier families with consistent improvements on 3/4, establishing genuine architecture-agnosticism. - Post-hoc inference-time compute adjustment (vary τ_SP or patch size without retraining) exposes a continuous quality-efficiency curve unavailable in any prior patch-based approach, a practical advantage for deployment. - The shared-backbone controlled experimental design, with fixed-data training and matched parameter counts across all model families, is methodologically stronger than the ad hoc comparisons common in this literature. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: SP focuses on single-stage patching; extending to hierarchical architectures (H-Net, AU-Nets) is deferred to future work. SP does not reduce training-time FLOPs relative to standard patch-based models. The scratchpad update rule is a simple partial re-aggregation; richer recurrent-style update rules may improve expressiveness. The H-Net interaction (spatial coupling causing redundant compute near learned boundaries) is identified but not resolved. Analyst-Identified Gaps: No scaling experiments beyond 2B parameters are provided, yet the central efficiency claims (KV-cache vs. quality trade-off) may shift substantially at larger scales where longer range dependencies or different entropy profiles emerge. Training-time FLOPs are not reduced — SP is comparable to training a model with smaller patch size — which makes it a quality improvement technique at fixed patch size, not a training-efficiency technique; this cost is underemphasized relative to the inference savings narrative. The 2-layer auxiliary entropy prediction head adds parameters and training compute; its contribution to total model cost is not quantified. No code or weights are released, making reproduction dependent on replicating custom re-implementations of four distinct architectures. The paper notes that fixed-stride dense updates (S=1) match a vanilla byte-level model in compute but not in quality — the gap between byte-level and S=1 scratchpadding (which should be near-identical in compute profile) is not fully explained. Bottom Line: A practitioner building tokenizer-free language models gains a concrete, architecture-agnostic mechanism that breaks the patch-size/quality coupling, delivers inference-time compute flexibility, and substantially narrows the performance gap to byte-level baselines at large patch sizes — making this the most complete treatment of within-patch computation allocation available.
Recommended 2605.09630 PDF
#28 — Innovative (73)

Position: LLM Inference Should Be Evaluated as Energy-to-Token Production

Xiang Liu, Shimiao Yuan, Zhenheng Tang, Peijie Dong, Kaiyong Zhao, Qiang Wang, Bo Li, Xiaowen Chu · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper proposes a Token Production Function framework that formalizes LLM inference as quality-conditioned energy-to-token production, bounded jointly by a compute ceiling (K_eff/c_tok) and a delivered-power ceiling (P_IT/e_tok), and calls for J/token, active binding constraint, PUE-adjusted power, and utilization to be first-class evaluation metrics alongside accuracy and latency. - Why it's important: Global AI data center electricity consumption reached 415 TWh/yr in 2024 and is projected to hit 945 TWh by 2030, making delivered power and cooling a realized binding production constraint in many regions — yet current benchmarks report no metric that captures this ceiling, rendering published throughput and efficiency numbers incomparable across deployments. - How it does it: It models token output as a Leontief min(compute-side rate, power-side rate) production function conditioned on fixed quality target q and service target s, with a decomposable system-efficiency multiplier Φ_system that simultaneously shifts both arms of the min, and defines a ρ–ρ* diagnostic to identify which constraint is binding at a given operating point. -
