The good: The (q, s)-conditioning requirement is the paper's sharpest contribution: it explains why published J/token figures across papers are currently incomparable and provides the exact scaffolding needed to make them comparable, and the Φ_system decomposition into six ratio-form estimators (each computable from GPU performance counters) bridges the abstract framework to practical implementation. - The missing: No controlled head-to-head J/token benchmark is produced — all e_tok anchors (ranging from ~3.5 J for MHA FP16 to ~0.35 J for the MLA+INT4 projection) are compiled from heterogeneous independent sources under different workloads, batch sizes, and quality assumptions, so the claimed 10× Φ_system spread is directional, not a matched-stack measurement. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems researchers, AI infrastructure practitioners, and benchmark/leaderboard designers who need a rigorous economic framing to justify energy reporting requirements in inference papers and conferences.
Why this rank: Scored using the Theoretical profile (a framework and reporting standard; no system built). Novelty (78) and Non-Obviousness (80) are the weight-dominant drivers and are the paper's real strength — the Leontief quality-conditioned Token Production Function and the ρ–ρ* binding-constraint diagnostic are a genuine NEW FORMULATION, and the insight that published J/token figures are incomparable precisely because they lack (q*,s*) conditioning is non-obvious and durable. Resilience (78) is high: §6 seriously rebuts the obvious objections with time-constant arguments, and every quantitative anchor is labeled by source type. Completeness (75) is helped by unusually forthright limitation-framing in Appendix A. The composite is capped below the 75 band because Rigor (58) is weak by construction — no experiments are run, all e_tok anchors are heterogeneous compilations, the headline 10× spread is directional with no error estimate, and the framework admittedly cannot produce the controlled benchmark it calls for; Practical Impact (62, theoretical sense) is moderate, resting on adoptable disclosure dimensions with MLPerf Power as precedent. Weighted composite: 0.25*78 + 0.20*58 + 0.25*80 + 0.10*75 + 0.15*78 + 0.05*62 ≈ 73.
theoretical Novelty 78 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 75 Resilience 78 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM inference evaluation is dominated by accuracy, latency, throughput, and hardware Model FLOPs Utilization — metrics that answer how fast and accurate a model is but not how many quality-conditioned tokens a fixed power-and-cooling envelope can produce. As AI data centers consume roughly 47 GW globally (2024 IEA estimate) and US data centers alone may reach 325–580 TWh/yr by 2028, delivered power has become a binding production input in capacity-constrained regions. Listed API output prices diverge 3–30× between frontier Chinese and US providers (April 2026) in a pattern directionally consistent with infrastructure-level energy-efficiency differences, though the paper does not treat price as causal cost evidence. The evaluation gap is concrete: a system reporting MMLU accuracy and tokens/sec on an H100 discloses nothing about whether delivered power or theoretical peak compute is the active ceiling, what the J/token cost is at that operating point, or how KV-cache compression, quantization, or sparse attention shift the energy-to-token frontier — so optimizations that reduce e_tok without sacrificing quality are invisible to current benchmarks. Key Idea The core technical insight is that LLM inference is a Leontief (fixed-proportions) production process at a given operating point: token rate is bounded by the minimum of two independent physical ceilings — compute throughput divided by compute intensity (K_eff/c_tok) and delivered IT power divided by energy intensity (P_IT/e_tok) — and system optimizations (Φ_system) shift the production frontier by simultaneously compressing both c_tok and e_tok without proportional infrastructure expansion, making them macro-level energy levers rather than local engineering tricks. The ρ–ρ constraint-boundary diagnostic (where ρ = P_IT/K_eff is the hardware's delivered-energy-per-FLOP ratio and ρ = e_tok/c_tok is the workload's demanded ratio) identifies which ceiling is binding and converts the abstract min(·,·) into a falsifiable, per-deployment quantity. Methodology The Token Production Function is: Q̇_token(t; q, s) = min(K_eff(t)/c_tok(t;q,s), P_IT(t)/e_tok(t;q,s)) · U(t;q,s), integrated over horizon T. K_eff is effective hardware throughput (FLOPs/s) after availability and kernel-efficiency losses but before demand-side losses; P_IT = P_facility/PUE is IT-delivered power; c_tok and e_tok are compute intensity (FLOPs/token) and energy intensity (J/token) at the stated (q, s) operating point; U ∈ (0, 1] is the utilization factor capturing queuing, batching mismatch, routing, and regulatory friction, identified from real-time GPU SM-activity and queue-depth signals rather than from hardware specs. The Leontief min(·,·) is justified as a short-run binding-constraint approximation (CES nests it as σ → 0) where compute and power are co-required, not freely substitutable. Φ_system is decomposed into six measurable components (Φ_prefill, Φ_decode, Φ_mem, Φ_comp, Φ_sched, Φ_route), each admitting ratio-form estimators from hardware performance counters (e.g., Φ_mem ≈ Q_obs/Q̇^ceil_BW from HBM spec and observed token throughput; Φ_sched ≈ U_SM/U^batch_SM from SM-activity counters). Three critical design decisions: (1) conditioning all comparisons on fixed (q, s*) — well-justified as the methodological linchpin that makes token counts comparable; (2) Leontief rather than CES structure — explicitly bounded as short-run, with CES acknowledged as the generalization as substitution elasticities become measurable; (3) separating K_eff and U to avoid double-counting hardware-side and operational-side efficiency losses — well-justified by the difference in intervention type (provisioning vs. algorithmic redesign). Key Results No new experiments are run. Directional calibration is compiled from independent published sources. Table 1 anchors order-of-magnitude values: H100 BF16 K_eff ≈ 1.979×10^15 FLOPs/s; global P_facility average ~47 GW (2024); industry-average PUE 1.56; e_tok for 65B dense decode spans ~2×10^9–8×10^11 J/token across model sizes and implementations; utilization U ∈ 0.3–0.7. Table 2 (65B regime, 100 ms latency SLO on H100): MHA FP16 baseline e_tok ≈ 3.5 J (measured); MHA+INT4 ≈ 1.2 J (34% of baseline, measured); MLA with KV compression ≈ 1.1 J (31%, measured); MLA+INT4 ≈ 0.35 J (10%, projection composing independent gains — not a matched-stack measurement). Table 3 extends this to ~11 configurations and confirms a roughly 10× e_tok spread within the same nominal H100 envelope across rows A–F (measured), widening to ~30× including distillation tradeoffs (J, K). The ρ–ρ* worked example (Appendix B) shows that the same 65B H100 deployment classifies as power-bound under peak-throughput K_eff but compute-bound under realized effective throughput (5–10× lower denominator), demonstrating the convention-dependence of the diagnostic. Figure 2 plots global data center electricity (2020–2030, IEA) against an illustrative Φ_system proxy rising ~22× over the same period, consistent with tokens partially decoupling from joules historically. Novelty Classification NEW FORMULATION + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The Leontief production-function framing of LLM inference with quality-and-service conditioning is a genuine conceptual contribution — prior Green AI and carbon-accounting work (Patterson et al., Strubell et al.) accounts for training emissions without the quality-conditioned token-output formulation, and MLPerf Power measures serving power without the ρ–ρ* constraint-switching framework. The Φ_system decomposition into ratio-form estimators from serving telemetry is new as a unified decomposition, though individual components (Roofline model, memory-bandwidth analysis) are prior art. The directional e_tok compilation across heterogeneous sources is not a controlled empirical finding but provides the first organized quantitative illustration of the 10× Φ_system spread the framework predicts. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations, error bars, or significance tests — the paper runs no experiments. All quantitative anchors are explicitly labeled as directional illustrations compiled from heterogeneous independent sources under different workload configurations, batch sizes, and quality assumptions; the paper explicitly disclaims ceteris-paribus status. The ρ–ρ diagnostic is convention-dependent on the K_eff measurement choice, which the paper discloses in Appendix B and builds into the reporting standard (requiring K_eff convention to be stated). The 10× energy-reduction claim from MLA+INT4 composition is a projected upper bound, not a matched-stack measurement — the paper labels it explicitly as a projection. No code, data, or model weights are released; the contribution is a conceptual framework and reporting agenda. Self-consistency is high: every quantitative claim is labeled by source type (measured / projection / developer-report-pending-replication), and the appendices provide worked numerical examples. The main methodological concern is that the framework cannot self-validate: the paper argues J/token measurements need (q, s*) conditioning but cannot itself produce the controlled benchmark it calls for — that benchmark is designated as future work. Deployability (4-property check) THEORETICAL profile — the paper proposes a framework and reporting standard; no system is built or deployed. - Sound: The Leontief min(·,·) formulation is explicitly bounded as a short-run approximation with CES as the acknowledged generalization; the (q, s)-conditioning requirement is logically necessary and clearly motivated; the ρ–ρ* convention-dependence is disclosed and built into the proposed standard rather than papered over. - General: The framework applies to any LLM serving deployment with measurable K_eff, P_IT, PUE, c_tok, e_tok, and U; the Leontief approximation weakens when hardware substitution is fast or when power is not a binding constraint, which the paper acknowledges. - Tight: Not applicable in the formal bound sense — this is a definitional production framework; the Φ_system decomposition provides ratio-form estimators from GPU performance counters and serving telemetry that are in principle computable from existing instrumentation. - Implementable: Yes — the paper proposes six concrete disclosure dimensions (J/token, active binding constraint, PUE-adjusted delivered power, utilization-adjusted token output, K_eff convention, and (q, s) operating point) that benchmark operators could adopt immediately; MLPerf Power is cited as a partial implementation precedent. No new hardware or algorithms are required. The work stops at paper form; no software artifact, dataset, or trained model is delivered. Strengths - The (q, s)-conditioning requirement is operationally important and underappreciated: it explains precisely why published J/token numbers across papers are currently incomparable, and it provides the scaffolding to make them comparable — this is the paper's sharpest and most durable contribution. - The Φ_system decomposition into six ratio-form estimators, each computable from GPU SM-activity counters or HBM bandwidth specs, bridges abstract production economics to practical instrumentation that serving teams already have access to. - The counterarguments section (§6) engages seriously with "hardware will make energy secondary," "renewables will dissolve the Power Wall," and "demand elasticity will erase cost advantages" — each rebutted with specific time-constant arguments (hardware cycles 18–36 months vs. model release cycles 3–6 months; grid additions take 5–10 years; Jevons Paradox means per-token price falls but aggregate consumption expands). - The three-epoch historical narrative anchored to documented system-level deployments (FlashAttention/vLLM/INT4 → MLA/NSA/sparse-hybrid) provides a falsifiable timeline for when the binding constraint shifted, grounding the framework in observed industry transitions rather than hypothetical future states. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The paper is unusually forthright in Appendix A, framing each limitation as a scoping choice rather than a gap. Explicitly acknowledged: (1) Leontief is a short-run binding-constraint approximation, not a structural macro model — long-run substitution elasticities are non-zero and CES is the appropriate generalization; (2) all e_tok anchors are directional compilations conditioned on six disclosed dimensions, not ceteris-paribus benchmarks — the controlled cross-stack J/token benchmark is called for but not delivered and is explicitly designated as future work; (3) ρ–ρ* is convention-dependent on the K_eff measurement choice, which is why the reporting standard requires the convention to be stated; (4) geopolitical outcomes, capability rankings, and API price causality are out of scope by design; (5) training-time energy is out of scope except where serving-side Φ_system amortizes training cost. The paper argues each caveat tightens rather than weakens the position, because every dimension a reviewer asks to control is a dimension the proposed reporting standard already requires to be disclosed. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The Leontief structure treats U (utilization) as independent of which arm of the min is binding, but in practice demand-side batching decisions that increase U can shift the binding constraint — the feedback between U and the active ceiling is unaddressed and could make the static ρ–ρ* diagnostic misleading in dynamically loaded deployments. - The framework is defined for a single workload at a fixed (q, s) operating point, but production deployments run heterogeneous request mixes with varying context lengths, quality targets, and SLAs simultaneously — how to define or aggregate a "disclosed operating point" across a mixed-workload serving cluster is left unspecified, which is a governance problem the reporting standard inherits. - The paper notes that difficulty-adaptive reasoning achieves 22.4% token reduction, but does not quantify how often real deployments accept implicit quality tradeoffs that shift q rather than holding it fixed — if Φ_system gains in practice frequently involve shifting the operating point rather than improving efficiency at fixed (q, s*), the framework's clean separation between energy multipliers and quality tradeoffs partially collapses. - Table 3's MLA+INT4 projection (row F, e_tok ≈ 0.35 J) composes independently measured gains that come from different hardware configurations, batch sizes, and serving stacks; the paper labels this as a projection but does not quantify the uncertainty range around the composition, so the cited 10× spread has no error estimate. Bottom Line A practitioner in LLM serving, AI infrastructure, or ML benchmarking will gain a genuinely useful conceptual scaffold — the Token Production Function with (q, s)-conditioning is specific enough to be actionable and explains a real incomparability problem in current evaluation practice — but should read it as the argument for a reporting standard rather than an empirical validation of that standard, since the controlled J/token benchmark the framework calls for does not yet exist.
Search Results 2605.11733 PDF
#29 — Innovative (73)

Implicit Representations of Grammaticality in Language Models

Yingshan Susan Wang, Linlu Qiu, Zhaofeng Wu, Roger P. Levy, Yoon Kim · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper demonstrates that a linear probe trained on synthetically-perturbed text, without any human-annotated grammaticality data, extracts implicit grammaticality representations from LM hidden states that outperform string probability baselines on human-curated acceptability benchmarks. - Why it's important: LM string probabilities conflate grammaticality with semantic plausibility and corpus frequency, making them unreliable for evaluating whether LMs have learned syntactic rules—a question central to the debate over whether LMs are viable computational models of human language. - How it does it: A logistic regression probe is trained on last-token hidden states using 50,000 sentence pairs from Penn Treebank and Project Gutenberg, where ungrammatical examples are synthetically generated by random token insertion, deletion, or local shuffle, then evaluated zero-shot on human-curated acceptability benchmarks. - The good: The English-trained probe generalizes zero-shot to six typologically diverse languages (Swedish, Dutch, Italian, Russian, Japanese, Chinese), consistently outperforming LM logprob baselines—an unexpected cross-lingual transfer result that strengthens the claim that grammaticality is encoded in a language-agnostic subspace of LM representations. - The missing: The synthetic training corpus is drawn from Penn Treebank and Project Gutenberg, which may themselves contain grammatical errors, and perturbations sometimes introduce semantic implausibility rather than ungrammaticality (only 93.72% of perturbed sentences confirmed ungrammatical by LLM-as-judge), leaving the probe's behavior on subtle grammatical violations not caused by crude perturbations undercharacterized. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers in computational psycholinguistics, probing methodology, and LM interpretability—the cross-lingual transfer finding and the plausibility/grammaticality dissociation are concrete empirical contributions to the ongoing LM-as-language-model debate.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Rigor (78) anchors the score — public code, three model families at two sizes each, reported 95% CIs with mostly non-overlapping gaps, and a clean plausibility/grammaticality double dissociation. Non-Obviousness (75) is solid via the LASSO ~10-neuron localization. The composite is held below 75 by Resilience (68): the headline cross-lingual transfer claim is materially undercut by Appendix M (non-English-trained probes are far weaker), suggesting the effect is driven by English-dominant pretraining rather than a language-universal subspace — and this asymmetry is not foregrounded. Novelty (70) is a known probing + perturbation combination. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*76 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*63 = 73.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 76 Resilience 68 Impact 63
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Language models are trained to maximize corpus likelihood, which entangles grammaticality with semantic plausibility, word frequency, and discourse context. A sentence can be highly likely but ungrammatical (e.g., agreement-attracted errors in naturally occurring text) or grammatical but low-probability (unusual but well-formed constructions). Researchers studying LMs as models of human language acquisition need to know whether LMs internally distinguish grammaticality from likelihood, since this distinction is central to the competence–performance gap debated in theoretical linguistics. Prior work established that LM string probabilities perform well on minimal-pair grammaticality tests (where the grammatical and ungrammatical sentences differ only in one targeted feature) but fail on unpaired, standalone sentences. The gap between these two regimes has not been explained from the perspective of internal representations. Key Idea: The key insight is that a linear probe trained on crude, synthetically-generated grammaticality contrasts (not minimal pairs) will be forced to learn a representation that targets syntactic well-formedness rather than the semantic and frequency signals that drive string probabilities—and that this representation, learned entirely without human annotation, transfers across benchmarks and languages because LMs internally organize grammaticality information in a linearly separable, language-agnostic subspace of their hidden states. Methodology: The probe training pipeline has three stages. First, a contrastive synthetic dataset is constructed: 50,000 sentences are sampled from Penn Treebank and GenRM/Gutenberg, and each sentence is perturbed by one of three operations—random token insertion (1–5 tokens from corpus vocabulary), token deletion (1–5 alphabetic tokens), or local shuffle of a 5-token window. Original sentences are labeled grammatical; perturbed sentences are labeled ungrammatical. Second, a logistic regression probe with ℓ₂ regularization is trained on the last-token hidden state h_i := M(s_i) from a chosen layer of a frozen LM, optimizing binary cross-entropy with regularization strength α tuned on a 20% dev split. The layer is selected by maximizing dev-set AUC across all layers independently. Third, an ℓ₁-regularized LASSO variant identifies sparse neuron subsets, with sparsity targets ranging from 0.01% to 0.5% of all neurons; after neuron selection, an ℓ₂ probe is refit on those neurons. Critical design decisions: (1) using the last-token hidden state rather than sentence-level pooling—justified by the autoregressive architecture where the last token integrates full context; (2) selecting the best layer per model rather than fixing a universal layer—empirically, middle-to-late layers dominate (layer depth 0.3–0.5 relative); (3) using length-normalized cumulative logprob as the LM probability baseline rather than raw logprob—reported to be a better discriminator. Models evaluated: OLMo-2-7B, OLMo-3-7B, Llama-3.2-1B, Llama-3.1-8B, Gemma-2-2B, Gemma-2-9B (all base, non-instruction-tuned). Evaluation metrics are ACC on minimal-pair benchmarks (BLiMP, BLiMP-NL) and AUC on standalone-sentence benchmarks (CoLA, SyntaxGym, ScaLA, ItaCoLA, RuCoLA, JCoLA, SLING). Key Results: On English grammaticality benchmarks, probes substantially outperform LM logprob baselines. On BLiMP (134,000 minimal pairs), probe ACC ranges 0.77–0.91 vs. logprob ACC 0.61–0.64 across models; probe AUC ranges 0.75–0.85 vs. logprob AUC 0.58–0.62. On CoLA (10,657 sentences, standalone), probe AUC ranges 0.78–0.81 vs. logprob 0.67–0.70. On SyntaxGym (2,412 sentences), probe AUC ranges 0.59–0.71 vs. logprob 0.50–0.55. On semantic plausibility sets, the direction reverses: LM logprob AUC is approximately 0.76–0.79 while probe AUC falls to 0.56–0.62, confirming the probe is selectively tuned to syntax rather than semantic coherence. For cross-lingual transfer (English-trained probes evaluated zero-shot), probes match or exceed LM logprob on Swedish (ScaLA AUC: probe 0.68–0.83 vs. logprob 0.61–0.66), Dutch BLiMP-NL (probe ACC 0.69–0.84 vs. logprob 0.69–0.86, mixed), Italian ItaCoLA (probe AUC 0.59–0.72 vs. logprob 0.55–0.61), Russian RuCoLA (probe AUC 0.58–0.65 vs. logprob 0.43–0.47), Japanese JCoLA (probe AUC 0.55–0.65 vs. logprob 0.55–0.59), and Chinese SLING (probe ACC 0.63–0.75 vs. logprob 0.58–0.65). Spearman correlation between LM logprob and probe score is only 0.23–0.47 across models and benchmarks, confirming they capture distinct signals. LASSO sparsity results show that probes with only 0.01% of neurons (~10–14 neurons for OLMo 7B models) achieve above-chance performance on BLiMP, and LASSO-selected neurons substantially outperform random subsets of the same size, indicating grammaticality is not uniformly distributed but concentrated in specific neurons, primarily in the last layer. Adding LM logprob as an auxiliary feature to the probe produces no consistent improvement (Δ ACC/AUC near zero with overlapping confidence intervals). Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW METHOD. The core novelty is the empirical finding that LMs implicitly encode a grammaticality representation that is (a) linearly extractable from hidden states without human-annotated training data, (b) dissociated from string probability, and (c) cross-lingually transferable. The method—probing on synthetically perturbed data—is a combination of known probing techniques and perturbation-based data augmentation; the genuine novelty lies in demonstrating this specific combination achieves out-of-domain generalization and cross-lingual transfer on grammaticality. Rigor & Reproducibility: Code is publicly available at https://github.com/SusanWYS/grammaticality_probe. Multiple model families (OLMo, Llama, Gemma) across two size points each are tested, which adds credibility. 95% confidence intervals are reported for all key metrics (Tables 11–13), and the probe-vs-logprob gaps are largely non-overlapping, supporting statistical reliability. The synthetic data quality is validated using Claude Opus 4.6 as an LLM judge on 5,000 sentence pairs, finding 93.72% of perturbed sentences judged ungrammatical—a reasonable but not perfect rate. Ablations include: layer-by-layer comparison (Figure 6), supervised probe baseline (BLiMP-trained probe), LASSO neuron count sweeps, and augmented probes with logprob as a feature. A notable methodological concern: the 6.28% of perturbed sentences that remain grammatical (or become semantically implausible rather than ungrammatical) introduce noise into training labels; the paper acknowledges this but does not ablate its effect on downstream benchmark performance. No significance tests beyond confidence intervals are reported. The evaluation set overlap concern (probe hyperparameters tuned on synthetic dev set, not on the benchmarks themselves) is handled correctly—benchmarks serve as true held-out sets. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: All inference runs on H100 and A100 clusters via HuggingFace APIs; the probe itself is a logistic regression requiring only a forward pass through a frozen base LM, making it deployable on standard GPU hardware. - Efficient: Probe training completes in under 15 hours total (LASSO neuron selection dominates at ~8 hours); inference is a single forward pass plus a dot product, adding negligible cost over LM inference. - Effective: Probes outperform LM logprob on all three English grammaticality benchmarks and on five of six cross-lingual benchmarks, but the gains on SyntaxGym (AUC 0.59–0.71) are modest in absolute terms, and the probe does not approach near-perfect grammaticality discrimination—substantial room remains. - Scalable: The paper tests models from 1B to 9B parameters; no experiments are run at larger scales (30B+), and no claim about scalability beyond the tested range is made. The work delivers a software artifact (code + probe training pipeline) and a trained probe is implicitly reproducible from the released code. Strengths: - The cross-lingual zero-shot transfer result—English-trained probes outperforming language-specific LM logprobs on Russian, Japanese, and Chinese acceptability benchmarks—is a concrete, surprising finding that goes beyond what the probing-for-syntax literature has previously demonstrated at this scope. - The plausibility/grammaticality double dissociation (probes better at syntax, logprobs better at plausibility) is methodologically clean and directly answers the entanglement critique of string-probability-based grammaticality assessment. - The LASSO localization result (meaningful grammaticality signal in ~10 neurons, concentrated in late layers) is a specific, falsifiable claim about the geometry of grammaticality representation that invites follow-up mechanistic interpretability work. - Hyperparameters are tuned on a synthetic dev set and benchmarks serve as held-out evaluation, avoiding the Goodhart's Law problem common in probing work where the same dataset is used for both tuning and reporting. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state: (1) synthetic training data may include grammatically incorrect originals from PTB/Gutenberg, and perturbations may produce semantic implausibility rather than ungrammaticality; (2) the work is restricted to base LMs at final checkpoints, with no analysis of how grammaticality representations develop during training; (3) scope is limited to natural languages, with formal language syntactic acceptability outside the study's bounds; (4) the English-dominance of pretraining data likely explains the English-centric representation space underlying cross-lingual transfer, rather than any language-universal syntactic abstraction. These are confronted directly and credibly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The paper does not evaluate on instruction-tuned models, which are the variants most practitioners use; the claim that instruction tuning confounds analysis is reasonable but leaves open whether the grammaticality representation persists post-RLHF. The synthetic data quality validation (LLM-as-judge) uses Claude Opus 4.6—the same model family as the paper's institution's collaborators—without a secondary validation; this is a minor concern but worth noting. The cross-lingual transfer results for non-English-trained probes (Appendix M) are reported to be substantially weaker, which is mentioned but not shown in the main text; this asymmetry suggests the cross-lingual finding is driven by English-dominant representation spaces, not by a universal grammaticality subspace, and warrants more prominent discussion. The probe's relatively low SyntaxGym performance (AUC 0.59–0.71) on syntactically diverse constructions suggests the crude perturbation training distribution does not cover all grammatical phenomena uniformly—an analysis of which BLiMP categories are best vs. worst served (Table 10, in appendix) reveals wide variation (NPI Licensing AUC 0.75–0.91 vs. Filler Gap AUC 0.67–0.83), but this breakdown is not foregrounded in the main paper. Bottom Line: This is a well-executed empirical study with a clear, surprising cross-lingual transfer finding that advances the debate on whether LMs encode grammaticality as a concept distinct from likelihood; researchers in computational psycholinguistics and probing methodology will find concrete new results worth building on, though practitioners seeking robust grammaticality judgment tools should note that the probe's absolute performance on diverse syntactic phenomena remains moderate.
Recommended 2605.05197 PDF
#30 — Innovative (73)

DelTA: Discriminative Token Credit Assignment for Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (123 ▲ )

Kaiyi Zhang, Wei Wu, Yankai Lin · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 22 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DelTA, a token credit assignment method that reweights the RLVR training objective by computing per-token discriminative scores derived from iteratively refined contrastive gradient centroids between positive- and negative-advantage responses. - Why it's important: RLVR trains with response-level rewards but updates at token level, causing high-frequency shared tokens (formatting, common phrases) to dominate policy-gradient updates and dilute learning signals from the reasoning-critical tokens that actually distinguish correct from incorrect responses. - How it does it: DelTA formalizes the policy-gradient update as an implicit linear discriminator in token-gradient space, then iteratively refines positive/negative side-wise centroids to amplify gradient directions characteristic of their own advantage side—via an entropy-regularized soft assignment solved in closed form as a sigmoid of a squared-distance margin—while downweighting shared directions. - The good: DelTA outperforms four strong concurrent baselines by +3.26 average points on Qwen3-8B-Base and +2.62 points on Qwen3-14B-Base across seven math benchmarks, with gains replicated on a third backbone (Olmo3-7B) and on code generation. - The missing: No multi-seed training runs are reported; the theoretical discriminator analysis ignores Adam preconditioning and multi-step nonlinear dynamics; and there is no comparison against process reward models, the most direct alternative approach to the same credit-assignment problem. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners working on RLVR training improvements—the discriminator framework is analytically clear and the ablations are diagnostic—though absent multi-seed training, exact gain magnitudes should be treated as approximate.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. This is the strongest paper in the cluster on the composite. Novelty (76) is high: NEW METHOD plus a NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING — the discriminator view unifies GRPO/DAPO/Forking-Tokens/FIPO as special cases of a centroid estimator, and the result that bottom-50% λ tokens actively collapse training (not merely fail to help) is genuinely surprising, lifting Non-Obviousness (81). Rigor (71) is held back from higher by the absence of multi-seed training on a high-variance RL objective (gains are only 2–3 points on AIME-style benchmarks) and a checkpoint-selection conflict (AIME25 avg@8 is both selector and a primary eval), only partly offset by five diagnostic ablations, a random-coefficient control, and released code. Resilience (69) reflects the uncharacterized Adam-preconditioning gap and the missing direct comparison to process reward models — the natural alternative for the same granularity mismatch. Completeness (72) and Impact (71) are solid: honest Appendix-A limitations and a released training-framework wrapper, validated across three backbones and two task domains.
empirical Novelty 76 Rigor 71 Non-Obv 81 Completeness 72 Resilience 69 Impact 71
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation RLVR optimizes response-level rewards (e.g., answer correctness) without dense process annotations, creating a granularity mismatch: every token in a response receives the same scalar advantage despite contributing differently to the outcome. Recent empirical work shows RLVR induces sparse token-level distributional shifts, concentrating probability mass changes on a small fraction of tokens, yet standard sequence-level RLVR provides no mechanism to identify which tokens are responsible. The specific failure mode diagnosed here is that positive- and negative-advantage responses share substantial common structure—formatting tokens, problem-specific entities—whose high-frequency gradient directions pull both positive and negative side-wise centroids toward background noise, weakening the gradient signal for the reasoning-critical tokens that actually separate correct from incorrect responses. Key Idea The core insight is that any advantage-weighted policy-gradient update implicitly defines a linear discriminator in token-gradient space: a candidate token's log-probability increases when its gradient vector aligns more with the positive-side centroid than the negative-side centroid, and decreases otherwise. Standard RLVR constructs these centroids by advantage-weighted averaging, which minimizes within-side squared distances but is not optimized for between-side discrimination. DelTA reshapes these effective centroids by assigning each token a soft score proportional to how much more representative its gradient is of its own advantage side than of the opposite side—the score is a sigmoid of a squared-distance margin, derived as the closed-form solution to an entropy-regularized assignment problem—and then uses these scores to reweight the RLVR surrogate. Methodology DelTA operates as a wrapper around a DAPO-style RLVR surrogate. For each rollout batch of G sampled responses, the pipeline proceeds as follows. First, layer-restricted token-gradient proxies v_{i,t} are computed: for token y_t with final hidden state h_t, the proxy is (1 − p_t(y_t))h_t, a frozen-representation approximation to ∇_{W_{y_t}} log p_t(y_t). Second, positive and negative side-wise centroids μ_+ and μ_- are initialized as advantage-weighted averages of these proxies from positive- and negative-advantage responses respectively; these are provably the weighted least-squares centroids (Appendix D). Third, K=1 refinement iteration executes: for each token, a soft assignment score α_{i,t} = σ((‖v_{i,t} − μ_-‖² − ‖v_{i,t} − μ_+‖²) / γ_+) is computed (symmetric for negative-side tokens), where temperature γ_+ adapts from the empirical variance of the distance margins; centroids are then updated as score-weighted within-side averages. Fourth, final raw scores α* are linearly mapped to bounded coefficients λ_{i,t} ∈ [0.8, 1.2] and self-normalized so the average coefficient equals 1. The DAPO clipped surrogate is then optimized with these per-token multipliers as stop-gradient constants. Critical design decisions: (1) LM-head proxy rather than full gradients—justified by memory cost at LLM scale, supported by proxy ablations; (2) entropy regularization h(α) on the assignment—prevents hard 0/1 decisions and improves stability; (3) bounded range [0.8, 1.2] with K=1—prevents extreme reweighting; ablations show K=2,3 degrade performance, suggesting overfitting to batch-level gradient geometry. Key Results On seven math benchmarks (AIME24/25/26, HMMT25-Feb/Nov, HMMT26-Feb, Brumo25), trained on DeepMath-103K with 8×NVIDIA B200 GPUs: DelTA achieves 28.40 average on Qwen3-8B-Base vs. 25.14 for SAPO (best baseline, +3.26 points) and 39.91 average on Qwen3-14B-Base vs. 37.29 for FIPO (best baseline, +2.62 points). DelTA wins on every individual benchmark at both scales. On Olmo3-7B-Base, DelTA improves from 19.01 (DAPO) to 22.80 (+3.79 points). On code generation (HumanEval+, MBPP+, LiveCodeBench), DelTA raises weighted average from 47.7 to 49.5. On OOD benchmarks (GPQA-Diamond, MMLU-Pro), DelTA improves Qwen3-8B-Base from 58.87 to 62.38 and Qwen3-14B-Base from 66.77 to 68.40. Baselines (DAPO, DAPO w/ Forking Tokens, SAPO, FIPO) are strong and concurrent (2025–2026 preprints). A notable counter-intuitive finding: training DAPO on only the bottom-50% tokens by DelTA coefficient causes rapid training collapse, establishing that low-λ tokens are not merely uninformative but actively harmful to policy improvement. Checkpoint selection is based on AIME25 avg@8, applied uniformly to all methods. Statistical significance is assessed via one-sided Mann-Whitney U test across 16 evaluation runs (not multiple training seeds). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The discriminator view of RLVR updates (Section 3.1) is a new theoretical perspective that unifies GRPO, DAPO, Forking Tokens, and FIPO as special cases of a token-weighted centroid estimator. The iterative contrastive centroid refinement for token credit assignment is genuinely novel. The empirical finding that low-discriminativity tokens actively collapse training (not merely fail to contribute) is a new result. The component ideas—linear discriminant analysis, entropy-regularized assignment, policy-gradient optimization—are individually well-known; the synthesis and application in token-gradient space for RLVR is the contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough: five component ablations (Table 3) each degrade performance when removed, with the largest drop from removing centroid refinement (−3.30 avg. points). Proxy ablations (Table 4) confirm robustness to proxy choice, and a random-coefficient control (18.34 avg.) rules out the benefit being due to mere weight perturbation. Hyperparameter sensitivity (Table 11) shows modest variation across λ-range settings. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/RUCBM/DelTA. No multiple training seeds are reported; the authors explicitly acknowledge this as a cost-driven limitation and substitute evaluation-level significance testing. Checkpoint selection uses AIME25 avg@8, which is also a primary evaluation benchmark—this is a methodological conflict that could confer benchmark-specific advantage even under uniform protocol. The theoretical analysis is explicitly restricted to a local first-order approximation and ignores Adam preconditioning, gradient clipping, and multi-step dynamics. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs on 8×NVIDIA B200 GPUs, which are commercially available high-end hardware; no custom silicon required. - Efficient: Adds K+2=3 extra actor forward passes per rollout batch; measured at ~10.2% overhead of first-step wall-clock time on 8B model; overhead is expected to grow with response length since hidden states must be recomputed rather than cached due to memory constraints. - Effective: Token-selection ablation (Figures 3–4) provides strong evidence that the coefficients identify genuinely useful gradient directions; gains transfer across math, code, OOD tasks, and three backbone families. - Scalable: Tested up to 14B parameters; no experiments at 70B+ scale; the hidden-state recomputation and centroid construction cost scales quadratically in batch token count, which may become a bottleneck at larger scales or longer response lengths—not tested. The work delivers a software artifact (code at https://github.com/RUCBM/DelTA) that wraps an existing RLVR training framework. Strengths - The discriminator view (Section 3.1) is theoretically clean, with a complete derivation showing that standard RLVR centroids are within-side least-squares summaries, not between-side discriminators—a precise identification of the failure mode that directly motivates the fix. - The token-selection diagnostic (Section 5.2) is a strong independent probe: using DelTA scores for hard token selection (top-50%) outperforms full-token DAPO, while bottom-50% selection collapses training, ruling out that the benefit is a sparsification artifact. - The empirical scope is unusually broad for a preprint: 3 backbone families, 10+ benchmarks, 2 task domains, OOD evaluation, and 5 component ablations—all consistent with the claimed mechanism. - The connection between the distance-margin soft assignment and the inner-product discriminator (Appendix G) shows that the geometric criterion is internally consistent with the theoretical framework, not an ad hoc choice. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge four limitations in Appendix A: (1) token-gradient proxy is layer-restricted (LM-head only) rather than full-parameter; (2) evaluation focus is primarily on mathematical reasoning; (3) additional forward-pass overhead per training step; and (4) future work needed on multi-turn interaction, tool use, and broader verifiable-signal domains. The authors confront these directly, which is a credibility positive. They do not acknowledge the checkpoint selection conflict with AIME25 as an evaluation benchmark, nor do they discuss the gap between the local first-order analysis and actual Adam-optimized training. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No multi-seed training: Evaluation-level significance cannot distinguish training-run variance from method-level effect. Given that gains are 2–3 points on benchmarks with high stochasticity (AIME problems have pass@1 rates of 10–55%), even a single alternative training seed would substantially strengthen the claim. - Adam preconditioning gap: The discriminator view analyzes raw gradient directions, but Adam normalizes gradient coordinates element-wise by their running RMS. The effective update direction seen by the model is the preconditioned gradient, which may differ substantially from the raw gradient analyzed. This gap is uncharacterized and limits the explanatory scope of the theory. - No comparison against PRMs: Process reward models and step-level credit assignment (e.g., VinePPO) address the same granularity mismatch and are the natural alternative. The paper claims DelTA is "complementary" but provides no direct comparison or combination experiment. - Checkpoint selection bias: AIME25 avg@8 is the selection criterion and is also one of the seven primary evaluation benchmarks; this correlation—even under uniform protocol—may inflate reported scores on AIME25 specifically relative to other benchmarks. - Proxy-theory gap not quantified: Ablations show robustness across proxy choices, but no analysis quantifies how closely LM-head proxies approximate full-parameter gradient directions, or whether the discriminative structure identified by the proxy holds in full-gradient space. Bottom Line A practitioner working on RLVR training will gain a useful theoretical lens and a modestly-overhead, well-ablated method that consistently beats strong concurrent baselines across three backbones and two task domains—read it, but treat the exact gain magnitudes as preliminary until multi-seed training results are available.
Recommended 2605.21467 PDF
#31 — Innovative (73)

The Power of Anomaly Detection in Predictive Maintenance:[Experiments & Analysis]

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper presents a large-scale experimental study evaluating 14 (semi/un)-supervised time-series anomaly detection algorithms across 4 deployment flavors, 11 maintenance datasets, and up to 50 hyperparameter configurations per combination, with statistical analysis, runtime profiling, robustness assessment, and adversarial evaluation for online predictive maintenance. - Why it's important: Prior PdM benchmarks compare ≤3 algorithms on single proprietary datasets reporting only best-case results, leaving practitioners without reliable guidance for method selection under realistic edge-IoT constraints (limited compute, no labeled failure data, concept drift). - How it does it: A unified evaluation framework formalizing Predictive Horizon (PH) and Lead time (L) intervals uses AD1 AUC-PR as the primary metric and Friedman-Nemenyi statistical tests across 33 performance axes per flavor to compare worst-, typical-, and best-case performance rather than best-only. - The good: LOF (online flavor) achieves 94.8% of the best average AD1 AUC-PR across all datasets while incurring only 5.3% of the average computational cost — a specific, actionable trade-off finding absent from prior TSAD benchmarks. - The missing: Adversarial robustness is evaluated on only one dataset (EDP-WT), limiting generalizability of the structural dichotomy observed between point-based and sequence-based method vulnerability; sensitivity to the chosen PH parameterization is not analyzed. - Read it or not: Essential for IIoT practitioners and TSAD researchers needing evidence-based method-selection guidance; less relevant if the focus is supervised RUL estimation or foundation-model time-series research.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Rigor (79) is the dominant driver and exceptional for this space — worst/typical/best-case decomposition across 50 hyperparameter configs, Friedman-Nemenyi testing, threshold-free VUS-PR supplement, and open code make this the most credible PdM-TSAD comparison to date; the preprint status and single-dataset adversarial evaluation hold it just short of the next band. Non-Obviousness (75) and Impact (75) are strong: the LOF 94.8%-accuracy-at-5.3%-cost Pareto finding and the result that pre-trained LLMs fail to beat a naive preventive-maintenance baseline are surprising and directly actionable, backed by a released benchmark framework. Completeness (78) is high with honest limitations, though the incomparable XGBoost split and appendix-only LLM variants leave a gap. Novelty (60) is capped because the contribution is experimental design, not new algorithms. Weighted composite: 0.20*60 + 0.25*79 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*75 = 73.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 79 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 78 Resilience 72 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Industrial equipment failures cost approximately $1 trillion/year globally. Predictive maintenance (PdM) seeks to detect early degradation in IoT sensor time series before failures occur, with reported reductions in maintenance costs and unplanned downtime of up to 40% and 50%, respectively. Time-series anomaly detection (TSAD) is a natural approach — it requires no labeled failure data, can deploy from day zero, and generalizes across failure modes — yet the specific requirements of PdM differ from generic TSAD: anomalies must precede failures by a useful lead time, not merely co-occur; annotation reflects failure events rather than explicit anomaly boundaries; and online deployability on edge devices is a hard constraint. Prior comparative studies evaluate ≤3 algorithms, rely on single or private datasets, use best-case-only hyperparameter results, omit runtime analysis, and do not compare multiple implementation flavors concurrently. Practitioners deploying TSAD on edge IoT devices — where compute is limited, cloud-based hyperparameter tuning may be prohibited by data localization rules, and concept drift invalidates prior tuning — have no principled guidance. Key Idea: The central insight is that the interaction between deployment flavor (how a detector is trained and updated relative to incoming data) and the temporal structure of the dataset (monotonic degradation vs. concept drift) determines which method succeeds, and that evaluating only best-case performance systematically misleads practitioners facing realistic uncertainty in hyperparameter selection. Formalizing this with a worst/typical/best-case analysis across up to 50 configurations per method-flavor pair, combined with a PH+L evaluation framework and statistical testing, makes the relative rankings under operational constraints visible for the first time. Methodology: The study builds a unified API for 14 algorithms: proximity-based (KNN, LOF, NP, PB), distribution-based (OCSVM), tree-based (IF), online clustering (SAND), and deep learning (CHRONOS, CHRONOS-2, LTSF, TIME-LLM, TIMEMIXER++, TRANAD, USAD). Four deployment flavors are defined: semi-supervised historical (train on verified healthy history), semi-supervised online (reference set built at episode start), semi-supervised sliding (retrain on sliding window), and unsupervised. The evaluation metric AD1 AUC-PR is derived from precision_AD1 (fraction of alarms falling in PH) and recall_AD1 (fraction of episodes with ≥1 alarm in PH); VUS-PR integrates over varying PHs. Up to 50 hyperparameter configurations are evaluated per method-flavor-dataset combination subject to a 1-week runtime cap, with a global parameterization shared across all assets in a dataset. The 11 datasets span real-world (AZURE, BHD 2023, CNC, EDP-WT, FORMULA 1, METROPT-3, NAVARCHOS), experimental (FEMTO, IMS, XJTU-SY), and synthetic (CMAPSS) origins. Dataset difficulty is assessed via a Bradley-Terry maximum-likelihood model over pairwise AD1 AUC-PR comparisons. Three critical design decisions: (1) worst/typical/best-case analysis rather than best-only — well-justified for edge deployment; (2) global parameterization per dataset — realistic but may underestimate per-asset tuning potential; (3) PH set to 10% × L_min by default — practical but the sensitivity of rankings to this choice is unanalyzed. Key Results: KNN (sliding) achieves the highest median AD1 AUC-PR of 0.369 across datasets; PB (online) 0.288; TRANAD (online) 0.272. LOF (online) achieves 94.8% of the best average AD1 AUC-PR at 5.3% of average compute cost. ORACLE performance ranges from 0.488 (AZURE) to 1.000 (IMS), reflecting large variation in problem difficulty. BHD 2023 (hard drive SMART data) is the hardest dataset with near-absent pre-failure anomalies; IMS and FEMTO are easiest due to consistent degradation signatures. CHRONOS is the only method whose best-case performance is not statistically better than the ADD preventive maintenance baseline; pre-trained LLMs (CHRONOS, TIME-LLM) are statistically outperformed by KNN, LOF, and PB on AD1 AUC-PR. TRANAD is the most hyperparameter-robust method across flavors; LTSF and USAD exhibit the highest sensitivity. Supervised XGBoost is competitive only on CMAPSS (0.567) and IMS (0.687), both run-to-failure synthetic/experimental corpora with abundant labeled scenarios. The sliding flavor outperforms online on concept-drift datasets (NAVARCHOS); online outperforms sliding on monotonic degradation (CMAPSS). Post-hoc min-max normalization would increase KNN AD1 AUC-PR from 0.445 to 0.808 on CMAPSS — quantifying the gap attributable to threshold selection rather than detection quality. Under adversarial attack, KNN is the most robust (order-invariant decision rule) while sequence-based models (LTSF) degrade sharply; randomized smoothing improves OCSVM from 0.385 to 0.758 under high noise but degrades TRANAD. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The primary contribution is an empirical one: systematic worst/typical/best-case comparison across 14 methods × 4 flavors × 50 hyperparameter configurations × 11 datasets is unique in the PdM literature per Table 1. The Formula 1 dataset (non-constant sub-second sampling) is introduced for the first time in a PdM context. The PH+L evaluation framework and AD1 AUC-PR metric are adapted from prior work; the TSAD algorithms are existing methods. Genuine novelty lies in the experimental design and the findings it enables, not in new algorithms or architectures. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablation-equivalent analysis is present through worst/typical/best-case decomposition across 50 hyperparameter configurations and CD diagrams with 33 comparison axes per flavor. Friedman-Nemenyi statistical testing at α=0.05 is applied consistently. Box plots in Table 5 display full hyperparameter distributions. Code and data are open-sourced at https://github.com/apapadoi/tsad-for-pdm. The VUS-PR metric (threshold-free) supplements AD1 AUC-PR, reducing threshold-gaming risk. Concerns: (1) a single global parameterization per dataset may systematically disadvantage methods that benefit from per-asset tuning; (2) adversarial robustness is tested on only one dataset (EDP-WT); (3) the 1-week runtime cap may truncate hyperparameter search for DL methods more than for lightweight methods; (4) LLM variants (TIME-LLM, TIMEMIXER++) are reported only in appendix tables, reducing scrutiny. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL — all methods run on actual datasets with measured runtimes. - Practical: Proximity-based methods (KNN, LOF, IF) run on standard CPU hardware, tested on an Ubuntu server with RTX 3090; edge suitability is argued for CHRONOS via quantization but not experimentally validated on actual edge hardware. - Efficient: LOF (online) at 5.3% of average compute cost vs. the best method is the headline finding; TRANAD and CHRONOS are 1–2 orders of magnitude slower, which would be prohibitive on constrained edge devices. - Effective: LOF and PB achieve median performance statistically better than the ADD preventive maintenance baseline; SAND wins best-case on all 11 datasets; BHD 2023 remains largely unsolved (max AD1 AUC-PR 0.525), indicating the approach fails when pre-failure anomalies are absent. - Scalable: No experiments at 10×–100× the tested scale; FORMULA 1 (5.5M records, variable sub-second sampling) is the most demanding dataset but no scaling law is established. Delivers a software artifact (open-source unified benchmark framework). Strengths: 1. The worst/typical/best-case hyperparameter analysis across 50 configurations is the only study in this space to do this rigorously; the finding that proximity-based methods have the smallest average worst-case gap to ORACLE is a practically load-bearing result that best-only analyses would miss. 2. The quantitative effectiveness-runtime Pareto frontier (Figure 5) revealing LOF's 94.8%/5.3% trade-off is specific, reproducible, and directly actionable for deployment decisions. 3. The multifaceted EDP-WT case study — combining per-failure-type AD1 AUC-PR breakdown, SHAP local/global explanations, and adversarial robustness evaluation — grounds the benchmark in operationally realistic analysis. 4. The Bradley-Terry dataset difficulty ranking with explicit anomaly-type characterization (point-based vs. sequence-based) provides principled guidance for dataset selection in future studies rather than ad hoc choice. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note: results may vary with different hardware, preprocessing pipelines, and hyperparameter search spaces; thresholding and instance-wise normalization strategies remain open problems; abrupt input-scale changes in the online flavor can suppress detection for other assets; only a subset of 100+ TSAD algorithms is covered; ensemble methods for handling heterogeneous failure types warrant further research. These limitations are confronted directly and honestly, adding credibility to the conclusions. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The PH=10%×L_min parameterization is acknowledged as a practical choice but no sensitivity analysis verifies that rankings are stable across different PH values; for datasets near the performance boundary this could be consequential. - Adversarial robustness tests on a single dataset generalize the point-based vs. sequence-based dichotomy beyond the evidence; several datasets with quite different temporal characteristics (BHD 2023, NAVARCHOS) are not tested. - Log-trace evidence from EDP-WT shows generator failures correlate with human interventions not captured in sensor channels, motivating data fusion — this gap is named but not quantified, leaving unclear how much performance headroom remains from sensor-only analysis. - TIME-LLM and TIMEMIXER++ results are presented only in appendix tables; their operating assumptions (fine-tuned on training data) differ from CHRONOS (zero-shot), making the aggregate claim that "LLMs underperform" conflate architecturally distinct models. - The supervised XGBoost baseline uses an incomparable train/test split (acknowledged), but no truly comparable supervised baseline with the same evaluation protocol is included, making it impossible to quantify the cost of forgoing labels. Bottom Line: This is the most rigorous empirical study of TSAD for PdM to date, and the specific finding that proximity-based methods (KNN, LOF, PB) with an online deployment flavor dominate both accuracy and runtime trade-offs — while pre-trained LLMs fail to beat a naive preventive maintenance policy — should materially change default method choices for any IIoT practitioner deploying anomaly detection in industrial settings.
Citations
#32 — Innovative (73)

FERMI: Exploiting Relations for Membership Inference Against Tabular Diffusion Models

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces FERMI, a feature-mapping framework that distills relational membership signals from multi-table auxiliary data into the single-table feature space to mount stronger membership inference attacks against tabular diffusion models at inference time using only a single target-table row. - Why it's important: Real sensitive tabular data lives in relational databases, yet all standard MIA evaluations assume a single-table setting, causing practitioners to substantially underestimate the privacy risk of deployed tabular diffusion models. - How it does it: A neural mapper trained with MSE reconstruction loss plus CORAL covariance alignment projects single-table loss-fingerprint features toward their relational counterparts learned from shadow models, transferring the stronger relational membership signal without requiring relational access at query time. - The good: Under the Full-Relational Side Information setting, FERMI improves TPR@0.1FPR by up to 53% relative over the single-table baseline in white-box, and in black-box recovers 55% of the AUC gap (0.687→0.725 vs. merged-upper-bound 0.755) using only the released synthetic data. - The missing: The paper provides no evaluation against differentially private tabular diffusion models — the most relevant practical defense — leaving open the question of whether FERMI survives the defense practitioners would actually deploy. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers in synthetic data privacy, tabular generative models, or relational privacy auditing; the TabSyn finding alone — that latent-space bottlenecks provide near-complete structural resistance to relational augmentation — has immediate implications for architecture selection in privacy-sensitive deployments.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (75) and Non-Obviousness (78) carry the score: formalizing the training-inference asymmetry in multi-relational MIA is a genuine NEW FORMULATION, and the domain-adaptation move (harvesting relational signal at training time, projecting single-table features at inference) is non-obvious. Rigor (72) is solid for an attack paper — the identity-mapper ablation is a specific, convincing capacity control and std devs are reported — but small 200-record challenge sets make TPR@0.01 noisy, there are no significance tests, no public code, and critically no evaluation against DP-trained models (the defense practitioners actually deploy), which also caps Resilience (72). Completeness (78) is a positive: the paper pre-identifies its own failure conditions and the TabSyn negative result honestly. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*55 = 73.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 78 Resilience 72 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Sensitive tabular records in healthcare, finance, and social science reside in relational databases where an individual's information spans many linked tables connected by foreign keys. Organizations increasingly train per-table diffusion models to generate synthetic versions of these records for privacy-preserving data sharing. Membership inference attacks (MIAs) are the standard empirical tool for auditing whether a generative model leaks training data, yet every existing MIA against tabular diffusion models assumes a single-table setting — the adversary sees only the target table's attribute vector. This ignores that a realistic adversary may possess rich auxiliary relational data (from prior releases, related-institution leaks, or public databases) during the attack training phase, even though at inference time they observe only a single candidate row from the target table without access to that row's parent, child, or sibling records. No prior work formalizes or addresses this training-inference asymmetry in the multi-relational MIA setting, and the MIDST challenge (the only public benchmark for tabular diffusion MIAs with relational tracks) saw no winning submission exploit related-table information. Key Idea Querying a diffusion model on joined (merged) multi-table representations produces a substantially wider gap in per-record denoising loss between member and non-member records than querying on the single-table representation alone, because the model internalizes not just individual attribute statistics but their relational conditioning context. This richer membership signal can be harvested from shadow models during attack training even though it is unavailable at inference time: a neural mapper trained on paired (single-table features, multi-table features) examples from shadow models learns to project single-table loss fingerprints into a geometry that mimics the relational feature space, injecting an inductive bias that concentrates discriminative signal at the same small-timestep regime where relational features are most discriminative. At inference time the adversary applies the learned mapper to single-table features alone, recovering a substantial fraction of the relational signal without access to any related tables. Methodology FERMI follows the standard shadow-model paradigm and proceeds in three stages. (1) Paired Feature Extraction: for each shadow record x in target table T_j, two loss-fingerprint feature vectors are extracted — Φ_single(x) from a diffusion model trained on T_j alone, and Φ_multi(x̃) from a model trained on the merged table x̃ = x ⋈ x_p (child joined with parent attributes). Both use the loss-fingerprint extractor Φ(x_0) = [ℓ_{θ_j}(x_0, t, ε)]_{t∈T, ε∈E} ∈ ℝ^d, probing at |T| timesteps with |E| noise samples each; for TabDDPM, d = 7 × 500 = 3,500 per record. (2) Mapping: a neural mapper f_map (residual MLP, 3 blocks, hidden dim 128, layer norm) is trained on tuples (Φ_single(x), Φ_multi(x̃), y) across 10 shadow models, minimizing L_map = E[||f_map(Φ_single) − Φ_multi||²₂] + λ_coral · (1/4d²)||C_map − C_multi||²_F, combining pointwise MSE reconstruction with CORAL covariance alignment (λ_coral = 0.01) to match not just values but the distributional geometry of the relational feature space. (3) Membership Prediction: an attack classifier f_att (4-layer MLP, hidden dim 128, BN, dropout 0.2, binary cross-entropy) is trained on mapped features. The mapper and classifier are jointly fine-tuned end-to-end with L_total = λ_map L_map + λ_cls L_cls (λ_map = 0.05, 500 epochs, Adam). Two auxiliary knowledge settings are evaluated: Parent-Context Side Information (PCSI, target table plus parent tables only) and Full-Relational Side Information (FRSI, all linked tables). Critical design decisions: (a) CORAL alignment is well-justified — pointwise MSE alone may not align the covariance structure, which governs which dimensions a bounded-capacity downstream classifier can exploit; (b) using shadow auxiliary partitions for joins makes the merge tractable even on million-row datasets; (c) joint fine-tuning (rather than pipeline training) allows the mapper to specialize toward membership-informative directions rather than generic reconstruction. Key Results Single-table baselines (Table 1) establish that MIA susceptibility is architecture-dependent: TabDDPM is the most vulnerable (AUC 0.753–0.949 across three datasets); TabDiff varies considerably (AUC 0.503–0.814); TabSyn achieves near-random AUC (0.494–0.529) on all datasets and attack variants, suggesting its VAE encoder acts as a privacy bottleneck. Under PCSI (Table 2): the Merged (upper bound) attack on TabDDPM Instacart achieves AUC 0.967, TPR@0.1 = 0.996, TPR@0.01 = 0.806 — a dramatic jump over the single-table AUC 0.949. FERMI on the same setting: AUC 0.961, TPR@0.1 = 0.892 (+10.7 pp absolute over baseline 0.806), TPR@0.01 = 0.658 (+59.6 pp over baseline estimated from context). On Berka TabDiff, FERMI TPR@0.1 rises from single-table 0.258 to 0.378 (+46% relative). Under FRSI (Table 3): the Merged attack on TabDDPM Instacart reaches AUC 0.997, TPR@0.01 = 0.994 — near-perfect identification of members at 1% FPR. FERMI FRSI: AUC 0.963, TPR@0.1 = 0.910. On Berka TabDiff FRSI, FERMI TPR@0.1 = 0.394 vs. single-table baseline 0.258, a ~53% relative gain. In black-box (Table 4, TabDDPM California): single-table AUC 0.687, FERMI 0.725, Merged 0.755; FERMI recovers 55% of the AUC gap and 51% of the TPR@0.1 gap. TabSyn resists all relational attack variants across all settings, with AUC uniformly near 0.5. The ablation confirming the mapper contributes signal rather than capacity (identity-mapper variant produces AUC 0.893, TPR@0.1 0.656, TPR@0.01 0.380 — statistically indistinguishable from the single-table baseline) is the strongest piece of evidence that the framework's gains are mechanistically genuine. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW FORMULATION + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuine novelty is the formalization of the training-inference asymmetry in multi-relational MIA — no prior work distinguished what relational context is available at attack-training time from what is available at inference time — combined with the domain-adaptation approach to bridging this gap. The individual components (shadow-model paradigm, loss fingerprinting, CORAL domain adaptation) are existing ideas; their composition in the multi-relational MIA context is new. The empirical finding that TabSyn's latent encoding provides near-complete structural resistance to relational augmentation — a finding that persists even under the full-relational oracle setting — is a genuinely actionable architectural result not previously documented. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and targeted: the identity-mapper ablation (Appendix F) isolates that gains come from relational supervision signal, not added parameters or training budget — a specific and convincing control. The loss-visualization ablation (Figure 2) provides intuitive mechanistic support. Results are averaged over 5 target models with 10 shadow models each; standard deviations are reported throughout. However, challenge sets of only 200 records per model (100 member/100 non-member) produce noticeably noisy TPR@0.01 estimates, with standard deviations often exceeding 0.04–0.09. No formal significance tests are reported. The paper includes formal theoretical guarantees: a non-degradation theorem (FERMI optimal attack class ≥ single-table) and a necessary condition for useful relational supervision (conditional mutual information > 0 between the single-table features and the multi-table features given membership). Remark 1 honestly acknowledges that finite-sample training can produce a mapper that degrades performance when relational features are adversarial to child-record membership — a meaningful caveat. No code repository or model weights are mentioned in the paper; reproducibility is limited by the absence of a public release. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper designs, implements, and evaluates an attack framework experimentally. - Practical: All experiments run on a single NVIDIA L40 GPU with 16 CPU cores; no specialized hardware required, though shadow training for 10 models × 3 architectures × 3 datasets is compute-intensive. - Efficient: Feature extraction requires 500 noise samples per probing point per record (7 timesteps for TabDDPM/TabSyn, 7 for TabDiff), yielding 3,500–7,000 dimensional vectors; this is substantially more expensive than a single forward pass but tractable on moderate hardware. Training times are not reported. - Effective: FERMI demonstrably solves the stated problem for data-space diffusion architectures (TabDDPM, TabDiff); it explicitly does not improve over single-table attacks for latent-space architectures (TabSyn), and the paper is transparent about this scope condition. - Scalable: Datasets reach up to 1.69M rows in the target table but challenge sets are capped at 200 records; no experiments at 10× shadow training data scale. The claim that the framework extends to larger schemas is asserted but untested. The work delivers a paper-form attack framework with algorithm descriptions and hyperparameters sufficient for reimplementation, but no software artifact is released. Strengths - Formalizes a real and previously overlooked threat model: the training-inference asymmetry in multi-relational MIA is a practically motivated distinction that changes what privacy auditors should measure, and FERMI is the first framework built explicitly around it. - Honest and informative scope characterization: the paper pre-identifies when FERMI will fail (when the merged attack does not outperform the single-table attack), provides an empirical check (compare Merged vs. Direct Single before deploying the framework), and does not overstate transferability. - The identity-mapper ablation is a strong, specific control that rules out capacity-based explanations for the gains — a level of rigor that many attack papers omit. - The TabSyn finding is architecturally actionable: it establishes that the VAE latent bottleneck provides structural privacy resistance that persists even under full-relational oracle access, giving practitioners a concrete rationale for choosing latent-space diffusion architectures in privacy-sensitive deployments. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that FERMI cannot improve attack performance when the target architecture provides no meaningful relational membership signal (TabSyn), and leave latent-space–specific attacks to future work. They acknowledge that the Merged attack is a natural upper bound that FERMI only partially closes, that black-box experiments are limited to a single architecture-dataset pair (TabDDPM on California), and that Remark 1 identifies conditions under which the mapper can degrade finite-sample performance. These are stated with appropriate directness rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No evaluation against differentially private tabular diffusion models — DP-SGD or DP-Adam with calibrated noise budget is the standard practical defense, and whether FERMI's relational signal survives DP noise is the most policy-relevant open question, entirely absent from the paper. - Challenge sets of 200 records per model are small; TPR@0.01 estimates with standard deviations of 0.05–0.09 are unreliable for practical risk characterization, where decisions hinge on the low-FPR regime. - All three datasets are established ML benchmarks (California census microdata, Instacart market basket, Berka financial transactions) rather than proprietary or clinical records; the gap between benchmark-scale risk estimates and production deployment risk is unaddressed. - The black-box evaluation is restricted to TabDDPM on California; the claim that FERMI generalizes to black-box settings for TabDiff or multi-dataset settings is asserted from one data point. - No discussion of adaptive defenses: a model trainer who knows FERMI exists could train with randomized relational context augmentation or relational differential privacy to degrade the mapper's signal — this attack-defense equilibrium is not explored. Bottom Line Practitioners auditing privacy of relational tabular synthesis pipelines should read this paper — it exposes a concrete and previously unmeasured threat (relational context in attack training), provides a practical framework for exploiting it, and delivers the actionable architectural finding that latent-space diffusion (TabSyn) resists relational augmentation in a way that data-space models do not.
Search Results 2605.11527 PDF
#33 — Innovative (72)

An Interpretable Latency Model for Speculative Decoding in LLM Serving

arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a closed-form, interpretable latency model for speculative decoding (SD) in production LLM serving that decomposes per-request cost into load-independent and load-dependent components and uses Little's Law to infer effective batch size from request rate. - Why it's important: Practitioners deploying SD in real serving stacks (e.g., vLLM with continuous batching) cannot directly control batch size and frequently observe that SD speedups measured at batch=1 evaporate or reverse under real load — yet no analytical model previously explained when and why this happens. - How it does it: The model substitutes Little's Law (B = RPS × L) into a roofline-style linear latency decomposition (L = C1 + B × C2), then parameterizes C1 and C2 via a prefill/verify/draft cost split with acceptance-rate and draft-length dependence, yielding a single closed-form expression for latency and speedup across load. - The good: Normalized latencies from Llama, Qwen3, and MoE models spanning 0.6B–235B parameters, 16 prefill/decode length combinations, and varied acceptance rates all collapse onto the predicted y = 1/(1−x) curve with R² ≥ 0.96, validating the model's universality. - The missing: The entire evaluation uses synthetic token inputs from Pride and Prejudice with fixed prefill and decode lengths, not real user query distributions with heavy-tailed or highly variable lengths; the assumption of constant per-token acceptance probability α is made explicit but not relaxed. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or configuring production LLM serving systems with SD, particularly for understanding how to set draft length k under varying load; it is less valuable for researchers seeking algorithmic SD improvements.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (78) and Non-Obviousness (80) are the high contributors: substituting Little's Law into a roofline latency decomposition to yield a closed-form SD-aware serving model is a new formulation, and the falsifiable C2,R>1 result explaining why SD speedups erode under load — plus the MoE expert-coverage correction — reveals previously poorly-understood structure. Rigor (72) reflects unusually broad validation (0.6B–235B, R²≥0.96, leave-n-out generalization, p95/p99, H100) but is pulled down by the synthetic Pride-and-Prejudice workload, no error bars on fitted coefficients, and no quantitative comparison against any prior SD latency model. Completeness (68) and Resilience (68) are limited by the unrelaxed constant-α assumption and the proposed k-controller never being implemented. Impact (60) is moderate — no software artifact, but a directly actionable profiling framework. Weighted: 0.20*78 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*60 = 72.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 68 Resilience 68 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Operators serving LLMs in production use systems like vLLM that employ continuous batching and chunked prefill, where effective batch size is scheduler-determined and not an exposed control variable. Most SD evaluations are conducted at batch size = 1 or in fixed-batch synchronous settings, which do not reflect this dynamic. As a result, practitioners have no principled model to predict when SD helps, when it is neutral, and when it degrades latency under realistic multi-user load. Prior work (e.g., TurboSpec/Liu et al. 2024) has optimized SD goodput empirically but lacks an interpretable analytical account of why speedup erodes with utilization and how parameters like draft length k, acceptance rate α, verifier size, and drafter size jointly determine the operating regime. Key Idea The central insight is that Little's Law (B = RPS × L) lets you eliminate unobservable batch size from a roofline-style latency equation (L = C1 + B × C2), yielding L = C1 / (1 − RPS × C2). SD then reshapes C1 and C2 through a prefill/verify/draft cost decomposition in which fixed costs (C1) are reduced by SD (C1,R < 1) while load-dependent costs (C2) are typically increased (C2,R > 1), explaining why speedup is high at low load but erodes as RPS rises — the sign of load's effect on speedup is governed entirely by whether C2,R exceeds 1. Methodology The pipeline consists of: (1) measuring synchronous baselines, estimating maximum stable RPS before preemption, and sweeping 8 evenly spaced RPS values between them using GuideLLM v0.5.2 driving vLLM v0.13.0; (2) fitting the two-parameter latency model L = C1 / (1 − RPS × C2) per configuration using SciPy curve_fit; (3) reparameterizing C1 and C2 via the SD cost decomposition C1,EFF = c1,p + (g/E)(c1,v + k·c1,d) and C2,EFF = c2,p + (g/E)(c2,v + k·c2,d), where E = (1−α^(k+1))/(1−α) is expected accepted tokens per cycle; (4) fitting the unified 6-parameter speculation-aware model (eq. 3) across all α and k jointly per prefill/decode configuration; (5) extending to MoE via an expert-coverage factor φ(T) = 1 − (1 − m/M)^T derived from independent uniform routing. Critical design decisions: (a) treating the load-dependent verification coefficient c2,v as independent of k (justified empirically — no strong linear dependence observed); (b) excluding the saturation boundary (where preemption makes latency unstable) and modeling only the stable pre-saturation regime; (c) using constant per-token acceptance probability α (standard assumption from Leviathan et al. 2023). Models evaluated: Llama-3.1-8B/70B, gpt-oss-20b, Qwen3 family (0.6B, 1.7B, 8B, 14B, 32B, 30B-A3B, 235B-A22B). SD uses EAGLE-3 lightweight drafters for all except Llama-70B (vanilla SD with Llama-8B drafter) and Qwen3-14B (vanilla SD with Qwen3-1.7B).
