Key Results All normalized end-to-end latencies across all models, prefill/decode configurations, and SD settings collapse onto y = 1/(1−x), validating the Little's Law formulation; per-configuration R² is 0.96–1.00. The speculation-aware unified model (eq. 3) restores the collapsed curve across all (α, k) combinations where a single shared (C1, C2) failed. C1,R < 1 holds across all measured configurations, confirming SD reduces fixed per-request costs. C2,R > 1 for most acceptance rate and draft length combinations (the exception being acceptance rates approaching 100%), confirming that speedup erodes under load in practically relevant regimes. The draft length that minimizes C1,R (optimizing low-load speedup) is consistently larger than the draft length that minimizes C2,R (optimizing high-load speedup), quantifying the low-load vs. high-load tuning conflict. For MoE models, the expert-coverage correction improves R² from 0.902 → 0.997 (gpt-oss-20b), 0.830 → 0.976 (Qwen3-30B-A3B), and 0.906 → 0.989 (Qwen3-235B-A22B) on the lower-load half of the RPS sweep. Leave-n-out predictive R² for load-dependent coefficients c2,p and c2,v remains close to full-data R² for larger models, with held-out R² ≥ 0.97 for 8B+ models at n=4 left out. On H100, Qwen3 family fits achieve R² = 0.992–0.998. The model remains a good approximation for p95 latency and p99 latency on larger models (R² ≥ 0.93 for Qwen3-8B+ at p95; R² ≥ 0.88 for 8B+ at p99), breaking down for small models at p99. Novelty Classification NEW FORMULATION + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The application of Little's Law to derive a closed-form SD-aware latency model for continuous-batching LLM servers, and the prefill/verify/draft decomposition that connects SD algorithmic parameters (α, k) to load-dependent serving behavior, are genuinely new. The individual components — Little's Law, roofline decompositions, SD acceptance probability models — are all classical or prior work; the contribution is their combination into an interpretable, empirically validated serving model and the specific insight that C2,R > 1 explains load-driven speedup erosion. Rigor & Reproducibility The model is validated across 5 model families, 16 sequence-length configurations, 9 request rates, acceptance rates 50%–100%, and draft lengths 1–10, giving broad empirical coverage. A leave-n-out generalization test on prefill/decode configurations demonstrates predictive rather than merely descriptive accuracy, at least for larger models. Tail latency validation (p95/p99) and hardware robustness validation (H100) are included in the appendix. No ablation isolates individual terms of eq. 3 to show each contributes independently; the paper fits the full model and compares aggregate R². No error bars or confidence intervals are reported on fitted coefficients, and no significance tests are applied. Code is not released, and the measurement data is not publicly shared; the paper cites GuideLLM (open source) and vLLM (open source) as the measurement infrastructure. The use of Pride and Prejudice text as the workload generator is a notable methodological choice — real user inputs have variable length distributions that may change the load-latency relationship. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (measurements on real hardware running a real serving stack, plus an analytical model fitted to those measurements). - Practical: The measurement infrastructure (GuideLLM + vLLM) runs on standard A100/H100 GPUs and the model fitting requires only SciPy curve_fit on a small number of profiling runs — practically accessible. - Efficient: The model itself has negligible runtime overhead; profiling cost is a one-time sweep of ~9 RPS points per configuration, which the paper characterizes as lightweight. - Effective: The model accurately describes mean latency and speedup trends in the stable pre-saturation regime; it does not handle the saturation boundary, tail latency for small models, bursty arrivals, or preemption dynamics. - Scalable: Validated from 0.6B to 235B parameters and across single-GPU to 8-GPU tensor-parallel setups, but always on a single server; multi-server or disaggregated serving (prefill/decode disaggregation) is not addressed and may require additional cost terms. The paper delivers no software artifact — it is paper form only. Strengths - The empirical collapse of normalized latency onto a single curve across model families, sizes, and SD configurations is a striking validation that gives the model genuine generality rather than being a curve-fitted post-hoc description. - The C1,R / C2,R decomposition yields a sharp, falsifiable prediction about when SD speedup improves or erodes with load — the sign of (1 − C2,R) determines the direction — which is actionable for practitioners without requiring detailed internal knowledge of the serving stack. - The MoE extension via the expert-coverage factor φ is analytically motivated (independent uniform routing) and empirically validated, explaining a previously observed but poorly understood deviation (MoE underperforming SD at batch=1). - The leave-n-out generalization experiment demonstrates that the fitted coefficient scaling trends generalize to unseen sequence-length configurations, going beyond mere interpolation. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) the model covers only the stable pre-saturation regime and excludes the saturation boundary where preemption causes unstable latency; (2) fitted coefficients are system-dependent and change across GPUs, parallelism strategies, and serving engines; (3) the model targets mean latency and does not describe latency distributions; (4) SD variants with adaptive drafting or tree verification may require additional cost terms; (5) it focuses solely on vLLM. These limitations are confronted directly and framed constructively — the paper positions the model as a profiling framework rather than a universal constant set, which is an honest and appropriate scope claim. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The entire workload uses simulated constant-length inputs drawn from Jane Austen's Pride and Prejudice. Real user queries have heavy-tailed, highly variable length distributions; since both C1 and C2 depend on prefill and decode lengths, a realistic workload with length variability would produce a mixture of operating points, potentially invalidating the single-model fit per configuration. - The constant per-token acceptance probability α assumption is stated explicitly but never relaxed or stress-tested. EAGLE-3 and other speculative drafters have position-dependent acceptance rates; the paper does not evaluate whether the model degrades when α varies across decode positions. - The paper does not compare against any prior latency model for SD serving (not even the goodput optimization framework from TurboSpec/Liu et al. 2024 is used as a quantitative baseline for model accuracy), making it impossible to assess how much improvement in predictive accuracy the proposed formulation provides over alternatives. - The proposed use case of dynamically selecting k by estimating α and RPS is described but not implemented or evaluated as a controller; the paper does not show that acting on the model's predictions actually improves serving outcomes relative to a static k. - This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.15051v1, May 2026); results have not been peer reviewed. Bottom Line A practitioner deploying speculative decoding in vLLM will gain a concrete, actionable analytical framework for understanding why their SD speedups shrink under load and how to reason about draft length selection across operating regimes — this is a real and currently unmet need, and the empirical validation is unusually broad for a modeling paper.
Recommended 2605.15051 PDF
#34 — Innovative (72)

Generative Recursive Reasoning (21 ▲ )

Junyeob Baek, Mingyu Jo, Minsu Kim, Mengye Ren, Yoshua Bengio, Sungjin Ahn · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 21 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GRAM (Generative Recursive reAsoning Models), a framework that replaces the deterministic latent-state update in Recursive Reasoning Models with a learned stochastic Gaussian perturbation, enabling probabilistic multi-trajectory inference trained via amortized variational inference. - Why it's important: All existing RRMs follow a single fixed latent trajectory, making them structurally incapable of representing multiple valid solutions or escaping local minima — a fundamental failure mode on any constraint satisfaction task where the solution space is multimodal. - How it does it: At each recursion step, GRAM computes a deterministic update u_t, then samples a state-dependent Gaussian noise ε_t ~ N(μ_θ(u_t), σ²_θ(u_t)I) and adds it as a residual, inducing a distribution over reasoning trajectories that is trained with an ELBO objective and exploited at inference via parallel trajectory sampling. - The good: GRAM with N=20 parallel samples at 16 iterations achieves 97.0% on Sudoku-Extreme and outperforms TRM at 320 iterations (90.5%), reaching the same accuracy ceiling with a parallelizable rather than sequential compute budget. - The missing: All evaluation targets toy-scale synthetic benchmarks (9×9 grids, 8–10 node graphs, 28×28 images); there is no evaluation on any real-world reasoning task where GRAM's design principles would need to compete with autoregressive systems under realistic conditions. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers designing latent-space or recursive reasoning architectures for combinatorial or constraint satisfaction tasks; practitioners focused on LLM-scale reasoning will find the experimental scope too restricted to act on.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Novelty (80) and Non-Obviousness (82) are the top contributors — reinterpreting stochastic latent-state dynamics as a distribution over *reasoning* trajectories (rather than observation uncertainty) is a genuine NEW METHOD + NEW FORMULATION, and the emergent unconditional-generation result is non-obvious. Rigor (70) is strong (clean ablations isolating stochasticity vs. learned direction, error bars, baselines reproduced under identical settings) but is held back by preprint status, an unablated per-task β tuned over a 12× range, an augmentation-confounded ARC comparison, and unclear code release. Completeness (70) benefits from honestly confronted scaling/calibration limitations but lacks a Best-of-N+verifier comparison and wall-clock latency, and Resilience (68) plus the toy-only experimental scope (9×9 Sudoku, 8-node graphs, 28×28 images) cap the composite just below the Highly Innovative band despite the rigorous execution. Weighted: 0.20*80 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*82 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*55 = 72.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 82 Completeness 70 Resilience 68 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Recursive Reasoning Models (RRMs) such as HRM and TRM use iterative latent-state refinement with shared transition functions, decoupling reasoning depth from parameter count and output length. This is appealing for constraint propagation, state tracking, and multi-step inference. However, all existing RRMs are deterministic: they follow a single fixed latent trajectory and converge to one prediction. This is structurally inadequate for problems with multiple valid solutions — such as N-Queens or graph coloring — where deterministic models exhibit mode collapse and coverage rates below 36% regardless of depth. More generally, any problem where a single refinement path can become trapped in a suboptimal attractor suffers from this single-trajectory design. The gap is the absence of principled multi-hypothesis exploration within the RRM paradigm. Key Idea The core insight is to reinterpret the deterministic latent update as the mean of a learned stochastic distribution: instead of z_t = u_t (a fixed next state), GRAM adds a state-dependent Gaussian perturbation ε_t ~ N(μ_θ(u_t), σ²_θ(u_t)I) to produce z_t = u_t + ε_t. This converts repeated recursive computation from a deterministic fixed point into a distribution over trajectories — a latent-variable generative model trained with an ELBO. The key distinguishing move is applying stochasticity at the level of the abstract high-level state (not output decoding, not random initialization), and training the noise distribution with a target-conditioned variational posterior so the model learns which directions to explore, not just random noise. Methodology GRAM maintains a hierarchical latent state z = (h, l): the high-level component h carries abstract reasoning state and is updated stochastically once per outer step; the low-level component l carries fine-grained computation and is refined K times deterministically per outer step (K=6 for Sudoku, K=4 otherwise; T=3 outer steps per supervision step). The full recursion is organized into N_sup=16 supervision steps applied sequentially, with the terminal state of each step passed as input to the next. Training maximizes an ELBO: L_ELBO = E_q[log p(y|z_T, x)] − KL(q_φ(ε|u, y) || p_θ(ε|u)). Because backpropagating through the full T_total = T × N_sup trajectory is prohibitive, gradients are propagated only through the final transition of each supervision step (truncated BPTT), a standard approximation validated empirically by showing that the surrogate objective tracks the full ELBO monotonically during training. At inference, GRAM samples N parallel trajectories from the learned prior and selects the best via majority voting or a Latent Process Reward Model (LPRM) — a value head v_ψ(z_t) trained jointly to predict trajectory accuracy from the latent state. Adaptive Computation Time (ACT) via a Q-learning halt head allows early termination. Three critical design decisions: (1) stochasticity injected only at the high level (ablation confirms low-level noise provides no benefit); (2) learned directional guidance μ_θ is essential in multi-solution settings (stochasticity-only collapses on N-Queens at 50.27%); (3) truncated ELBO surrogate consistently tracks full ELBO (Figure 8 confirms monotone co-decrease on both Sudoku and N-Queens). Key Results On Sudoku-Extreme, GRAM achieves 97.0% vs. TRM 87.4%, HRM 55.0%, Looped TF 61.3% (all at 16 supervision steps; GRAM 10M params vs HRM 27M, TRM 7M). On ARC-AGI-1: GRAM 52.0% vs TRM 44.6%, HRM 40.3%. On ARC-AGI-2: GRAM 11.1% vs TRM 7.8%, HRM 5.0%. Width scaling: N=20 parallel samples at 16 iterations outperforms all deterministic baselines at 320 iterations including TRM (97.0% vs 90.5%), with the advantage of parallelizability. On N-Queens 8×8: GRAM 99.7% accuracy and 90.3% solution coverage (20 samples) vs. best generative baseline MDLM 96.1%/87.2%; deterministic recursive baselines cover at most 36.1%. On 8-vertex Graph Coloring: GRAM 2.7 conflict edges vs AR 19.0 and TRM 109.3. Unconditional Sudoku generation: 99.05% validity with 10.9M params and 16 steps vs D3PM-Uniform-Big 91.33% with 55.1M params and 1,000 steps. Binarized MNIST: IS 2.04, FID 73.34 at 256 inference steps vs D3PM 1.86/74.03 at 1,000 steps. Baselines within the recursive model class are current and reproduced under identical settings. LLM comparisons (o3-mini-high, GPT-5.2, Grok-4-thinking, Gemini 3 Pro) are reported as reference points for benchmark difficulty, not controlled baselines. The surprising finding that Deepseek-R1 (671B parameters) scores 0% on Sudoku-Extreme — matching direct prediction baselines — underscores that these benchmarks probe a reasoning axis not addressed by pretraining scale alone. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW FORMULATION. The genuinely novel contribution is framing stochastic latent-state dynamics (previously used in VRNNs, DSSMs, and Dreamer-family world models for temporal observation modeling) as computation rather than perception: latent transitions define possible reasoning trajectories, supporting conditional and unconditional generation from a single compact model. Width-based inference-time scaling via parallel trajectory sampling within the RRM paradigm is novel. The underlying components (variational inference, hierarchical latent dynamics, truncated BPTT, ACT) are individually established; the contribution lies in their coherent integration and reinterpretation as a reasoning framework. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: Table 3a shows cumulative contribution of deep supervision, hierarchical recursion, and stochastic guidance; Table 3b isolates the roles of stochasticity vs. directional guidance. Error bars (mean ± std) are reported across runs in Table 1. The surrogate ELBO approximation is empirically validated by tracking both the training objective and the full ELBO throughout training (Figure 8), showing monotone co-decrease. Code is referenced at a website (ahn-ml.github.io/gram-website) but no explicit public repository or model release URL appears in the paper; this is a preprint (arXiv:2605.19376). Methodological concern: the KL coefficient β is tuned per task over a wide range (0.04–0.5) and no β sensitivity ablation is provided, indicating possible fragility. The ARC-AGI comparison with TRM is partially confounded by augmentation: TRM's published numbers use 1,000 augmentations per problem, while the paper's stated comparison is at a fixed epoch/hyperparameter budget; Appendix D.2 addresses this partially but the main table does not make augmentation regime explicit for all methods. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL - Practical: Trained on 8× NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPUs; training times range from 1 hour (N-Queens 8×8) to 5 days (ARC-AGI). Inference with N=20 parallel samples is feasible on consumer GPU hardware. - Efficient: GRAM (10M params) is more parameter-efficient than HRM (27M) and comparable to TRM (7M). Parallel sampling multiplies inference FLOPs by N, but this is offset by the ability to reduce sequential recursion depth; N=20 at 16 iters achieves what deterministic baselines need 320 iters to reach, and the N trajectories are fully parallelizable unlike sequential depth. - Effective: Achieves best accuracy and coverage among comparable recursive reasoning models on all five task families tested; the structural advantage on multi-solution tasks (mode collapse of deterministic baselines is irreversible regardless of depth) is well-established. However, all evidence is on small-scale toy benchmarks. - Scalable: The sequential nature of deep supervision — N_sup gradient updates per example — is explicitly acknowledged as a barrier to scaling toward larger foundation models. No experiments beyond toy scale are presented; scalability to real-world problem sizes is claimed but not evidenced. The paper delivers a model and training procedure; code availability is unclear from the paper text alone. Strengths - Framing stochastic latent-state transitions as a distribution over reasoning trajectories (rather than observation uncertainty) is a conceptually clean contribution that bridges two previously separate literatures: probabilistic SSMs and recursive reasoning architectures. - Width-based inference scaling via parallel trajectories is practically useful because it is embarrassingly parallelizable, unlike sequential depth scaling, and the paper provides a concrete mechanism (LPRM) for trajectory selection without external verifiers. - The ablation precisely characterizes the roles of each component: deterministic guidance alone (variance → 0) causes catastrophic overfitting to the target (0% on both tasks); stochasticity without learned direction works for unimodal tasks but collapses on multi-solution problems (50.27% N-Queens); the full combination is required for robust performance. - The unconditional generation capability emerging from the same architecture — with validity and quality competitive with diffusion baselines at a fraction of the parameter count and step count — is a non-obvious and empirically well-supported finding. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state that the sequential nature of deep supervision limits training efficiency compared to Transformers, posing a significant barrier to scaling GRAM toward larger foundation models. They also note that multi-sample inference may increase compute and energy costs at scale, and that real-world deployment requires rigorous uncertainty calibration. These are confronted transparently rather than minimized, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All benchmarks are synthetic toy problems (9×9 Sudoku, 8–10 node graphs, 8×8/10×10 N-Queens, 28×28 images); there is no evidence the framework transfers to any real-world reasoning task, language understanding, or planning problem where practical impact is claimed. - The KL coefficient β is tuned per task across a 12× range (0.04 to 0.5) with no sensitivity analysis; this suggests significant hyperparameter fragility that is uncharacterized. - The augmentation regime is inconsistent across the TRM vs. GRAM ARC-AGI comparison in Table 8: TRM's published numbers rely on 1,000 augmentations per test instance, making the main table comparison potentially misleading until Appendix D.2 partially unpacks it. - Parallel trajectory sampling is functionally similar to Best-of-N with a learned process reward model — a well-studied technique in the LLM literature. No comparison to BoN with task-specific verifiers (which would be trivial to implement for Sudoku and graph coloring) is provided, leaving open whether the latent-trajectory structure adds anything beyond what external verification already enables. - No wall-clock latency comparison is reported; it is possible that GRAM N=20 at 16 steps is slower in wall time than TRM at 320 steps despite equivalent accuracy, depending on GPU utilization and memory overhead. Bottom Line A researcher building compact recursive or latent-space reasoning systems for constraint satisfaction or multi-solution combinatorial problems gains a concrete and well-ablated design principle from reading this paper; practitioners focused on LLM-scale reasoning or real-world applications will find the experimental scope too restricted to draw transferable conclusions.
Recommended 2605.19376 PDF
#35 — Innovative (72)

AstraFlow: Dataflow-Oriented Reinforcement Learning for Agentic LLMs

Haizhong Zheng, Yizhuo Di, Jiahui Wang, Shuowei Jin, Xueshen Liu, Yongji Wu, Z. Morley Mao, Ion Stoica, Jiawei Zhao, Beidi Chen · arxiv.org · Ion Stoica - new articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces AstraFlow, a dataflow-oriented RL training system for agentic LLMs that replaces trainer-centered control with three composable abstractions — a dataflow layer, Rollout-as-a-Service (RaaS), and substitutable trainers — enabling multi-policy collaborative training, elastic/heterogeneous rollout, and modular data algorithms without feature-specific system engineering. - Why it's important: Existing RL systems for LLMs are built around a single trainer-centered control loop, making each new capability (multi-policy coordination, elastic scaling, cross-region rollout) require a dedicated ad-hoc rewrite; this rigidity makes agentic RL — which requires all these capabilities simultaneously — prohibitively expensive to deploy. - How it does it: The system decouples rollout, data management, and training into autonomous components that interact only through narrow data and weight interfaces, so that capabilities like multi-policy coordination and elastic scaling emerge from the architecture rather than being bolted on as patches. - The good: AstraFlow achieves a 2.7× reduction in per-iteration training time versus the verl-based Dr. MAS system on multi-policy math training while matching or exceeding its accuracy (56.5% vs. 54.4% average), demonstrating that the architectural choice carries no accuracy cost. - The missing: All multi-policy experiments use only two-policy workflows on Qwen3-8B; there is no evaluation of three-or-more-policy systems, larger model scales in the multi-policy setting, or workloads with fundamentally different rollout/train ratios that would stress the dataflow layer's scheduling behavior. - Read it or not: Essential reading for ML infrastructure engineers building or evaluating RL training systems for agentic LLMs; less valuable for researchers focused purely on RL algorithms or model quality rather than systems design.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (82) is the dominant contributor under systems weights and is genuinely strong: an open-source framework (github.com/Infini-AI-Lab/astraflow), a measured 2.7x per-iteration speedup over the dominant synchronous verl/Dr. MAS baseline at accuracy parity, and a novel agentic auto-scaling use case (~13% GPU-hour savings). Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (68) reflect a real architectural insight — disaggregating control responsibility, not just compute placement — though the constituent techniques (FSDP, SGLang, delta compression, async RL) are all known. Rigor (70) benefits from unusually transparent methodology (matched hardware, verbatim prompts, full hyperparameters) and released code, but is held back by no error bars/significance tests, simulated cross-region networking (tc/netem), and preprint status. Resilience (62) is the main limiter: all multi-policy claims rest on exactly two policies, the speedup is measured against synchronous verl rather than an async multi-policy baseline, and the substitutability and by-design scalability claims are architectural assertions never empirically validated. Composite: 0.15*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*68 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*62 + 0.30*82 = 72.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 70 Resilience 62 Impact 82
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Scaling reinforcement learning post-training to agentic LLMs requires simultaneously satisfying four properties that existing systems each fail to compose: multi-policy collaborative training (where coding or reasoning agents involve a solver, verifier, or test-case generator trained jointly), elastic rollout capacity (dynamically allocating GPU compute as demand shifts), heterogeneous and cross-region rollout (exploiting preemptible or geographically distributed resources), and modular data algorithms (curriculum scheduling, replay, filtering). Existing colocated synchronous systems like verl couple all these concerns into a single trainer-centered control loop, making each extension require invasive pipeline rewrites. Disaggregated systems like AReaL and SLIME separate rollout and training compute but still embed data routing, staleness handling, and weight synchronization inside the trainer's control logic. The result is that composing multiple capabilities amplifies engineering complexity multiplicatively rather than additively. Key Idea The core insight is that disaggregating compute placement is insufficient; what must be disaggregated is control responsibility. AstraFlow introduces three components — a dataflow layer, RaaS nodes, and trainers — that each run autonomous control loops and interact exclusively through minimal data and weight interfaces. Because no component owns global control flow, new capabilities (additional policies, new rollout backends, new data algorithms) are expressed as configuration changes to the interfaces rather than rewrites of any component's internals. The pull-based sparse weight transfer mechanism (exploiting the empirical observation that ≥97% of bf16 parameters are bit-exactly unchanged per RL iteration) is a secondary but consequential insight that makes cross-region weight synchronization practical. Methodology AstraFlow's architecture consists of three abstraction layers. The dataflow layer sits between rollout services and trainers, representing RL data in natural units (prompts, trajectories, metadata, batches). It exposes a programmable interface for data algorithms — selective rollout (GRESO), post-rollout filtering (dynamic sampling), replay, curriculum scheduling — and uses data availability and routing to coordinate the autonomous components without a global control loop. RaaS (Rollout-as-a-Service) models rollout generation as a pure agent-serving service; each RaaS node exposes three operations: consume tasks, produce trajectories, and pull updated weights. This interface makes rollout backends substitutable and makes capacity elastic (adding capacity = launching more RaaS nodes). Trainers consume batches from the dataflow layer and publish updated weights through a weight-transfer interface; they have no visibility into rollout scheduling or inter-trainer coordination, making them independently replaceable. Weight transfer uses a fully asynchronous pull-based protocol with delta compression: RaaS nodes pull only the changed (non-bit-identical) weight parameters, amortizing periodic full syncs over many delta steps. The optimization algorithm used throughout evaluation is M2PO, an actor-verify policy optimization variant. Critical design decisions: (1) pull-based rather than push-based weight transfer — justified by the high-sparsity observation and network heterogeneity, well-motivated; (2) the dataflow layer as sole coordination plane rather than peer-to-peer trainer-to-trainer signaling — necessary for multi-policy composability, well-justified; (3) the RaaS interface requiring only task consumption, trajectory production, and weight refresh — deliberately minimal to maximize substitutability, well-argued. Key Results On multi-policy math (Qwen3-8B, Solver+Verifier, DAPO math set), AstraFlow achieves 56.5% average accuracy across AIME24/AIME25/MATH500/Minerva vs. 54.4% for verl-based Dr. MAS and 51.1% for a single-policy solver, while reducing per-iteration training time from 212.64s (Dr. MAS/verl) to 77.65s — a 2.7× speedup. The speedup comes from fully asynchronous execution masking long-tail multi-agent rollout latency that stalls the synchronous verl loop. On multi-policy code (Qwen3-8B, two workflows), the Solver+Test-Case-Generator workflow raises average accuracy from 30.29% to 34.55% (+4.26) across LiveCodeBench v5/v6 and Codeforces, with Solver+Selector also improving (+1.85 avg). Against AReaL on single-policy Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3-8B math, AstraFlow matches within 0.2 and 0.6 average accuracy points, and within 1–2% per-iteration throughput — confirming no performance regression from the added flexibility. Auto-scaling experiments on Qwen3-14B show that an LLM-driven agentic controller (Claude Code) reduces total GPU-hours by ~13% (312.0 vs. ~359 for fixed pools) while matching the wall-clock time of the over-allocated 11-GPU baseline (24.4h vs. 23.9h). Cross-region heterogeneous training with simulated 4 Gbit/s / 300ms links reaches 67.6 average accuracy vs. 68.0 for a homogeneous local baseline, with delta sparsity of ≥98.9% for Qwen3-14B making the payload ~28 GB → ~1.5 GB per iteration. Baselines are current (AReaL 2025, Dr. MAS 2026, verl), and the head-to-head Dr. MAS comparison is measured on matched hardware. Scale tested: Qwen3-1.7B through Qwen3-14B; all experiments are at standard research scale (8–16 GPUs), not at large-cluster production scale. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW FORMULATION. The dataflow-oriented abstraction for RL training systems — decomposing trainer-centered control into three narrow composable interfaces — is a genuine architectural contribution that has not been formalized in prior LLM RL systems. The specific combination of pull-based sparse delta weight transfer with autonomous RaaS and dataflow-layer coordination is new. The claim of being the first fully asynchronous multi-policy collaborative RL framework is plausible and not contradicted by the comparison table. The individual techniques (FSDP training, SGLang serving, delta compression, async RL) are all known; the novelty is entirely in the composable abstraction design and its demonstrated consequences. Rigor & Reproducibility The paper provides unusually complete experimental detail: verbatim prompt templates for all roles, precise hyperparameters (learning rate, KL coefficient, batch size, number of iterations, hardware topology), and careful methodology notes (e.g., disabling dynamic sampling in the Dr. MAS comparison to match baselines; using the same 8×H100 node for both the verl and AstraFlow timing measurements). Ablations are present for the auto-scaling controller (comparing fixed-6, fixed-11, and auto-scaling configurations) and for data algorithms (vanilla vs. DS+Replay vs. DS+Replay+GRESO). No error bars or significance tests are reported; training curves are shown in Figure 6 but no statistical tests are applied to the final accuracy numbers. The cross-region experiment uses simulated bandwidth throttling via tc/netem rather than actual cross-region infrastructure, which is a notable limitation. The paper is an arXiv preprint (v1, May 2026) and has not been peer-reviewed. Code is publicly released at github.com/Infini-AI-Lab/astraflow. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — AstraFlow is a built framework evaluated by deployment on real training jobs. - Practical: AstraFlow runs on standard NVIDIA GPU hardware (H100/H200/A100/L40S) and requires no custom silicon; the cross-region case uses standard TCP networking and tc/netem shaping. - Efficient: Per-iteration throughput matches AReaL within 1–2% on single-policy workloads while reducing multi-policy training time 2.7×; delta weight transfer cuts per-iteration weight payload by ≥30× at standard learning rates, making cross-region deployment practical. - Effective: The evidence shows AstraFlow enables multi-policy, elastic, and heterogeneous workflows that are impractical in existing systems without code changes, and does so with accuracy parity to specialized single-policy frameworks — this directly addresses the stated problem. - Scalable: Experiments are conducted at up to 16 GPUs and Qwen3-14B; production deployments of agentic RL can involve hundreds of GPUs and many simultaneous policies, a scale not tested. The paper claims the architecture scales by design (adding RaaS nodes is additive) but provides no empirical evidence beyond the 6–11 GPU auto-scaling range. The work delivers a software artifact (open-source framework at github.com/Infini-AI-Lab/astraflow). Strengths - The architectural abstraction is principled and well-motivated: the argument that compute disaggregation alone is insufficient and control-responsibility disaggregation is the necessary step is clearly articulated and supported by the limitations table. - The agentic maintainer use case (Claude Code as an auto-scaling controller) is a genuinely novel and practically useful application of LLM agents to infrastructure management, and the 13% GPU-hour savings with identical accuracy is concrete evidence of value. - Experimental methodology is unusually transparent: hardware matched for timing comparisons, verbatim prompts published, hyperparameters fully specified, and the reason for truncating the GRESO prompt set (multi-pass requirement) is explicitly stated. - The delta sparsity measurement across models (1.7B–14B) and tasks (math, AlfWorld, WebShop, search) is a useful empirical contribution in its own right, establishing that ≥97% sparsity holds broadly across workloads and that learning rate, not model size or task type, is the dominant driver. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) AstraFlow proposes a systems abstraction, not a new RL optimization algorithm; (2) evaluation does not cover long-horizon web agents, robotics, or safety-critical interactive systems; (3) some experiments use controlled or simulated deployment settings (cross-region bandwidth is simulated via tc/netem); (4) observed benefits depend on workload-specific factors including rollout latency, trainer throughput, network bandwidth, and weight-transfer frequency; (5) model quality, robustness, and safety still depend on underlying data, rewards, and evaluation protocol. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All multi-policy experiments use exactly two policies; the composability claim is not validated at three or more simultaneous policies, where the dataflow layer's routing and staleness management would face qualitatively different scheduling dynamics. - The 2.7× speedup versus Dr. MAS is measured against verl's synchronous execution, which is known to be bottlenecked by long-tail rollout; it is not compared against AReaL (which is asynchronous) in a multi-policy setting, leaving open whether the speedup survives against a strong async single-policy baseline extended to multi-policy. - The cross-region experiment uses simulated rather than actual network conditions; real cross-region deployments involve additional failure modes (partial failures, variable latency spikes, cloud provider preemption) not present in the simulation. - No experiment tests the claimed property that trainers and rollout engines are substitutable in practice; all experiments use the same SGLang RaaS and FSDP trainer throughout, so the plug-in flexibility is architectural but not empirically validated with a different backend. - The data algorithm flexibility experiment uses a truncated 8,000-prompt subset rather than the full dataset; the choice is justified for GRESO but means accuracy comparisons in Figure 9 are not directly comparable to the main results in Tables 2–5. Bottom Line Any team building or evaluating RL infrastructure for multi-policy or agentic LLM workloads will find AstraFlow's architectural argument and measured 2.7× training-time improvement over the dominant synchronous framework directly actionable, even though production-scale validation and substitutability with alternative backends remain undemonstrated.
Recommended 2605.15565 PDF
#36 — Innovative (71)

VeriCache: Turning Lossy KV Cache into Lossless LLM Inference

Jiayi Yao, Samuel Shen, Kuntai Du, Shaoting Feng, Dongjoo Seo, Rui Zhang, Yuyang Huang, Yuhan Liu, Shan Lu, Junchen Jiang · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces VeriCache, an inference framework that achieves output identical to full-KV-cache decoding while reaching up to 4× higher throughput by using any KV cache compression method as a speculative drafter and verifying token batches against the full KV cache kept off-GPU. - Why it's important: KV cache compression is inherently lossy — per-step distribution shift compounds linearly in output length, collapsing code-format accuracy to near zero and function-call accuracy below 10% under 4× compression, yet F1 stays above 75%, making existing evaluation metrics blind to the failure. - How it does it: Drafting (HBM-bandwidth-bound, compressed KV in GPU) and verification (PCIe/network-bound, full KV loaded from CPU/storage) consume orthogonal hardware resources, so cross-resource staggering overlaps them across requests; the same model weights are used for both, yielding 25–40 accepted tokens per verification round instead of the 2–3 typical of small-model drafters. - The good: Across 7 compression methods, 3 models (Mistral-24B, Qwen-32B, Llama-70B), and 5 task-specific datasets, VeriCache's KL divergence from full-KV stays below 0.01 nats while delivering 1.92×–2.73× throughput gain on long-context decoding and 1.33×–2.11× on remote prefix caching. - The missing: The "identical output" guarantee holds only under greedy decoding (temperature 0); the sampling-based extension via rejection sampling is mentioned but not evaluated, and all task benchmarks use small samples (50–150) with no confidence intervals. - Read it or not: Engineers deploying LLMs for code generation, agentic tool use, or structured output at long context should read this — it identifies a production-critical failure mode masked by standard metrics and provides a principled, working system fix.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Novelty (80) and Non-Obviousness (82) are the standouts: the lossless-by-construction guarantee via speculative verification of compressed KV, plus the cross-resource staggering insight that HBM-bound drafting and PCIe-bound full-KV loading are orthogonal and fully overlappable, is genuinely new and clean. Rigor (62) and Resilience (65) pull the composite down — it is an unreleased preprint with no code, the 'identical output' guarantee is restricted to greedy decoding (buried in a footnote), task benchmarks use only 50–150 samples with no confidence intervals, and the 1M-context CPU-DRAM requirement undermines the stated motivation. The KL-accumulation framing is broadly valuable but the residual <0.01 nats KL is never translated into a token-error rate.
systems Novelty 80 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 82 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM inference at long context (100K–1M tokens) is bottlenecked by KV cache size in two ways: it limits GPU HBM available for request batching (a 100K-token Qwen-32B context consumes ~15 GB, reducing batch size to 1 on an 80 GB H100), and each decode step must read the entire cache from HBM (Llama-3.1-8B decode step takes ~25 ms at 500K context vs. ~5 ms at 5K). KV cache compression (token dropping, quantization) reduces memory and bandwidth by 2–5×, but is inherently lossy: it substitutes a systematically biased token distribution p_lossy for the model's true p_full at every decode step. By the chain rule of KL divergence, sequence-level KL grows at least linearly in output length T — a ~2% per-step gap compounds to a 400× probability mismatch after 250 tokens. On SWE-bench Lite coding tasks, KVzip 4× drops syntactically valid code-format accuracy to near zero; on ComplexFuncBench tool-calling tasks, exact argument-matching accuracy drops below 10%. Token-level metrics (F1, ROUGE) remain above 75% in both cases, masking the failure entirely. Users deploying LLMs for precision-critical applications — code generation, agentic tool use, any structured output — cannot safely adopt any current compression method. Key Idea The core insight is that compressed-KV drafting and full-KV verification consume completely orthogonal hardware resources: drafting is HBM-bandwidth-bound (sequential vector-matrix multiplication over the compressed cache already in GPU memory), while verification is PCIe/network-bound (loading full KV from CPU DRAM or storage) plus GPU-compute-bound (a parallel forward pass over x token positions). Because these bottlenecks do not compete, verification can be hidden behind drafting of other requests through cross-resource staggering. Additionally, using the same model weights for both drafter and verifier means the compressed-KV drafter preserves dominant attention patterns, yielding acceptance lengths of 19–23 tokens at draft length 30 and 4× compression — an order of magnitude beyond the 1–2 tokens typical of small auxiliary-model drafters — which amortizes each expensive full-KV reload across many accepted tokens. Methodology VeriCache implements a three-phase loop: (1) Draft — autoregressively generate x tokens using KV_comp resident in GPU HBM; (2) Verify — one parallel forward pass over x drafted positions conditioned on KV_full (loaded from CPU/storage), yielding x+1 full-KV predictions; (3) Accept — keep the longest matching prefix, correct the first divergence with the verifier's token, and discard the rest. Two mechanisms control overhead. Cross-resource staggering (P1): a runtime scheduler maintains a BW ring (interconnect capacity reserved per future iteration) and an HBM ring (in-flight KV occupancy per future iteration). An Admit algorithm places each request's next verification in the earliest window satisfying both ring constraints, targeting ~B/x verifications per iteration. Drafting for B requests and verification for B/x requests then proceed simultaneously within each iteration, overlapping HBM reads and PCIe transfers. The iteration time is max(T_gpu, T_xfer) rather than their sum. High acceptance rate (P2): because compressed KV retains model weights and dominant attention patterns, γ(x,c) exceeds 0.8 at draft length 30 and 4× compression. Draft lengths of 20–50 tokens are used, amortizing each full-KV reload over many accepted tokens and keeping verification frequency low. Two deployment settings are supported: (a) long-context decoding — full KV on CPU, reloaded over PCIe per verification; (b) remote prefix caching — a small local GPU pool holds full KV (loaded from storage over fast link ~40 GB/s), a larger remote pool drafts on compressed KV (over slow link ~1.2 GB/s). A uniform compressor interface (compress/decompress/update) allows any token-dropping or quantization method to plug in without scheduler changes; 7 methods are instantiated. Critical design decisions: (i) full KV stays off GPU during drafting — this is the primary throughput lever, enabling larger compressed-KV batches vs. SparseSpec which keeps full KV in HBM; (ii) the BW+HBM ring scheduler rather than lock-step verification — lock-step would serialize all B full-KV reloads at iteration x, taking ~800 ms PCIe transfer vs. ~37 ms per staggered iteration; (iii) fixed per-workload draft length — acknowledged as suboptimal, adaptive per-request policy is future work. Key Results Long-context decoding (Pipeline 1, single serving instance): VeriCache delivers 1.92×–2.73× over full-KV across Mistral-24B (204 vs. 157 tok/s), Qwen-32B (120 vs. 81 tok/s), Llama-70B (256 vs. 102 tok/s). Composing with EAGLE3 peaks at 4.26× on Qwen-32B (317 tok/s). SparseSpec — the strongest self-speculative baseline — achieves only 1.02× speedup at small KV budgets because it keeps full KV resident in HBM. Remote prefix caching (Pipeline 2, 1 local + 4 remote instances): VeriCache achieves 1.33×–2.11× (e.g., Llama-70B: 485 vs. 240 tok/s). No prior speculative-with-compression system handles this setting. Quality: VeriCache's KL from full-KV stays below 0.01 nats across all 3 models and both pipelines, attributed to hardware floating-point nondeterminism. KVzip at compression 0.5 accumulates ~14.4 nats per request on Llama-70B, meaning the lossy model emits the full-KV output with probability ~5×10⁻⁷. Task-specific quality: VeriCache preserves full-KV function-call accuracy at 59% of KVzip's peak throughput; KVzip collapses to ~31% of full-KV accuracy on Llama-70B. VeriCache maintains 100% LongGenBench completion rate at 339 tok/s and 90% GSM8K-Long accuracy at 385 tok/s. Hardware sweep (Pipeline 1): speedup grows from 1.61× to 2.71× as KV budget shrinks from 0.74 to 0.2 of HBM, and from 1.92× to 3.01× as HBM/interconnect bandwidth ratio falls from 60 (H100 NVL) to 10 (GH200). Baselines are current and strong: EAGLE3 with pretrained RedHatAI speculators for both Llama-70B and Qwen-32B; SparseSpec (self-speculative with PillarAttn). Sample sizes for task benchmarks are small: 20 (acceptance rate), 50–150 (functional tasks), 900 (KL trace). Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The lossless-inference guarantee via speculative verification of compressed KV, combined with the cross-resource staggering scheduler that exploits orthogonal HBM/PCIe bottlenecks, is genuinely novel as a complete deployable system. The application of speculative decoding to compressed KV is not new (MagicDec, QuantSpec, SparseSpec), but all prior work keeps full KV in HBM (capping compression's batch-size benefit), does not handle remote prefix caching, and hard-wires a single compressor. VeriCache's scheduler and compressor interface are the non-obvious contributions. Rigor & Reproducibility This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.17613v1, May 2026); not peer-reviewed. No code repository or model weights are released. Ablations shown: acceptance rate vs. draft length (Figures 8–9), hardware parameter sweeps (Figure 13), and composition with EAGLE (Figure 10). No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed runs are reported on any metric. The "identical output" guarantee is precisely scoped to greedy decoding (temperature 0) and the non-zero KL (<0.01 nats) is attributed to hardware nondeterminism without quantifying how often it produces a token difference in practice. Evaluation covers 7 compression methods, 3 model sizes, 5 datasets, and 2 hardware settings — breadth is reasonable. The formal KL accumulation proof (Appendix A) is sound and closes the motivation rigorously. Per-iteration throughput models (Appendix B) are analytically derived and the paper provides concrete numerical examples validating them. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built serving system (vLLM + LMCache extension, 8K LoC Python + C++), evaluated under realistic workloads. - Practical: Runs on commercially available hardware (NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000, H100 NVL); requires substantial CPU DRAM (a 100K-token Qwen-32B context needs ~15 GB of CPU DRAM per request for the full KV) — feasible on server hardware but not on consumer or edge devices. - Efficient: Peak HBM per iteration is bounded to model weights + compressed KV (all requests) + one full KV (verifying request); the paper confirms this fits within 96 GB for tested configurations. CPU storage requirement doubles total KV memory footprint vs. lossy-only serving. - Effective: The lossless guarantee is proven by construction under greedy decoding; functional correctness evidence on code formatting, function calling, and long-form generation is direct and compelling, not proxy-metric-based. - Scalable: Demonstrated up to 2 GPUs (tensor-parallel Llama-70B) and 5-node remote prefix caching (1+4 split); no evaluation at larger clusters or under heterogeneous context-length mixes. The paper characterizes how speedup varies with hardware parameters but does not test at 10× scale. Delivers a software system; not yet publicly released. Strengths - The KL accumulation analysis (Section 3.2) provides a rigorous, quantitative explanation of why soft metrics (F1) mask functional failures in precision-critical applications — this framing is genuinely valuable and broadly applicable beyond the paper's specific system. - The cross-resource staggering insight — that HBM-bound drafting and PCIe-bound full-KV loading are orthogonal bottlenecks and can be fully overlapped — is clean, non-obvious, and the concrete scheduling analysis (BW ring + HBM ring) makes it operationally precise. - The acceptance-length advantage (25–40 vs. 2–3 tokens) is both theoretically explained and empirically demonstrated against EAGLE and small-model drafters, and directly underpins the system's performance claim. - The uniform compressor interface instantiated for 7 compression methods demonstrates genuine compression-agnosticism rather than cherry-picked tuning to a single method. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly confront four limitations: (i) doubled storage overhead (full KV on CPU + compressed KV on GPU); (ii) static per-workload draft length, which would benefit from per-request adaptive tuning; (iii) existing compressors optimize direct-serving accuracy rather than acceptance length at large draft horizons, so a co-designed compressor could push throughput further; (iv) verification beyond compression (e.g., CacheBlend-style non-prefix KV reuse) is an open extension. The limitation on the "identical output" guarantee being restricted to greedy decoding is noted in a footnote rather than the main text — this undersells a real scope boundary. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All task-specific benchmarks use 50–150 samples with no confidence intervals; the quality gap between VeriCache and full-KV (both at <0.01 nats KL) cannot be assessed for statistical significance. - The <0.01 nats residual KL from hardware nondeterminism is not translated into a token-error rate — for applications where even single-token divergence is catastrophic (e.g., shell command generation), this matters and is unquantified. - No tail latency (P99) is reported; all latency figures are averages. Production serving SLOs are typically defined on tail latency. - The remote prefix caching setting uses a fixed 1:4 local:remote GPU ratio identified as the "sweet spot" through sweeping; no deployment methodology is given for choosing this ratio in practice given real traffic distributions. - Throughput experiments are all single-model, single-context-length; real production workloads are heterogeneous. Whether the BW/HBM ring scheduler degrades gracefully under mixed workloads is not evaluated. - At 1M-token contexts (the stated motivation), full KV for Qwen-32B would occupy ~150 GB of CPU DRAM per request — a requirement that exceeds standard server memory configurations and is not addressed. Bottom Line VeriCache identifies and solves a real, underappreciated production failure mode — KV compression's silent functional correctness collapse on structured-output tasks — and the cross-resource staggering mechanism is non-obvious and cleanly engineered; practitioners running long-context LLM serving for code or agentic use cases should take this seriously, though the lack of a public release and the restriction to greedy decoding require verification before adoption.
Search Results 2605.17613 PDF
#37 — Innovative (71)

OScaR: The Occam's Razor for Extreme KV Cache Quantization in LLMs and Beyond (37 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 21 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OScaR (Omni-Scaled Canalized Rotation), a training-free INT2 KV cache quantization framework for text-only, multi-modal, and omni-modal LLMs that applies a Hadamard transform (Canalized Rotation) followed by token-wise L2 normalization (Omni-Token Scaling) to achieve near-lossless compression. - Why it's important: KV cache memory grows linearly with sequence length and exhausts GPU HBM at long contexts, forcing small batch sizes and low throughput; existing INT2 methods degrade accuracy unacceptably because they fail to handle inter-token norm variance, blocking practical long-context deployment. - How it does it: A Fast Hadamard Transform redistributes channel-wise outlier energy uniformly across all head dimensions before token-wise scaling, preventing the Scaling-Induced Outlier Artifact that makes naive norm equalization destructive to per-channel quantization. - The good: On NIAH long-context retrieval under INT2, OScaR achieves 96.5% accuracy, slightly exceeding the 16-bit baseline (96.0%) and the best competitor at 92.7%, while delivering 3.0× decoding speedup, 5.3× memory reduction, and 4.1× throughput improvement over BF16 FlashDecoding-v2 at 128K context on a single H20 GPU. - The missing: Efficiency is measured only on a single H20 GPU; multi-GPU tensor-parallel deployments (standard for 30B+ models) are not evaluated, and layer-adaptive bit allocation — known to help accuracy at very low bits — is absent. - Read it or not: Engineers deploying long-context LLMs in memory-constrained environments should read this; those focused on weight quantization, training-time compression, or multi-GPU serving infrastructure can defer.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (78) and Impact (75) lead: the Scaling-Induced Outlier Artifact analysis (why Canalized Rotation must precede Omni-Token Scaling) is a rigorously demonstrated, non-obvious mechanism, and the open-source CUDA library delivers an immediately deployable INT2 plug-in with 3× decode speedup at 128K. Rigor (65) holds the composite below 75 — no error bars or multi-seed runs anywhere, the efficiency comparison conflates PyTorch vs llama.cpp runtimes, and the TNI characterization rests on a 4-sentence toy prompt generalized to 128K. Code release and broad multimodal/omni-modal coverage are strong positives that align with the rigorous positive-calibration set.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 70 Resilience 68 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation During long-context autoregressive decoding, the KV cache grows linearly with sequence length and becomes the dominant consumer of GPU High Bandwidth Memory. At 128K tokens this exhausts available HBM on modern accelerators, forcing drastic batch size reductions and degrading inference throughput. Extreme INT2 quantization is the natural solution, but existing per-channel Key quantization methods (KIVI, KVQuant, OTT) assume tokens within a quantization block share similar magnitudes. This assumption is violated by Token Norm Imbalance (TNI): sparse but consistent low-norm tokens (attention sinks) and, in multi-modal models, inter-modality norm disparities inflate the per-channel dynamic range and force a coarse quantization step that corrupts precision for all normal tokens. Methods that address this (TurboQuant, QJL) add complex auxiliary pipelines — online randomized rotations, residual error correction, LUT-based quantization — that incur 170–260× higher prefill cost and remain slow at long contexts. No prior work characterizes TNI across omni-modal LLMs or provides a solution that is simultaneously accurate, lightweight, and hardware-efficient. Key Idea The core insight is that naive token-wise scaling to equalize norms fails because low-norm tokens (near-zero uniformly across all channels) become artificial per-channel outliers once amplified, expanding the dynamic range of channels where normal tokens are small. The fix is to apply a Hadamard transform before scaling: it redistributes channel-wise outlier energy uniformly across all head dimensions, eliminating the structural asymmetry (channel-dominated normal tokens versus uniformly-small low-norm tokens) that makes subsequent scaling destructive. Only after this Canalized Rotation can token-wise Omni-Token Scaling safely balance inter-token norms without introducing new quantization artifacts. The two components are mutually essential — either alone is insufficient. Methodology OScaR is a training-free, plug-in inference pipeline with two algorithmic components and three CUDA kernels. Canalized Rotation (CR): During inference, an online Fast Hadamard Transform (FHT) is applied to each Key and Query token (O(d log d) arithmetic). For Values, an offline Hadamard transform is pre-fused with the Value projection weight W_V and output weight W_O before inference, adding zero runtime overhead while improving Value quantization fidelity. Omni-Token Scaling (OTS): After CR, the L2 norm of each Key token across all attention heads is computed using the hardware-accelerated rsqrt instruction. Keys are normalized to unit vectors (stored as INT2 per-channel, group size G=32), and each token's norm is stored as a single FP16 scalar in the cache. During dequantization, the stored norm rescales the key before the attention dot product. This norm metadata adds negligible storage overhead. Quantization scheme: Keys use per-channel INT2 with a 128-token full-precision residual buffer that is flushed and quantized once full (carrying per-channel min/max statistics). Values use per-token INT2. CUDA kernels: Three fused kernels atop HadaCore and BitDecoding: (i) online FHT+scaling for Key, FHT-only for Query; (ii) INT2 quantization for both Key and Value; (iii) dequantization + de-scaling + attention, using WMMA (Tensor Core) tiles with Kronecker decomposition H_128 = H_8 ⊗ H_16 for head dimension 128. Critical design decisions: Ordering (CR before OTS) is verified by ablation — OTS alone hurts accuracy below KIVI baseline on WorldSense; CR alone fails to balance norms; together they recover near 16-bit accuracy. rsqrt is chosen over explicit L2 norm after ablation confirms equivalent accuracy with lower latency. Offline Value rotation avoids the runtime cost paid by methods that apply online rotation to Values. Key Results All competing INT2 methods use group size 32 (text) or 128 (multimodal) unless noted; TurboQuant+ uses 2.5-bit (mixed precision with higher-bit outlier channels). LongBench-E (text-only, INT2): OScaR achieves 41.75% avg on Llama-3.1-8B versus 41.70% 16-bit baseline, 40.74% second-best (OTT), 40.03% TurboQuant+. On Qwen3-8B: 48.74% versus 49.56% 16-bit, 48.21% OTT. NIAH (Qwen3-8B, INT2, up to 42K context): OScaR 96.5% retrieval accuracy versus 96.0% 16-bit baseline, 92.7% TurboQuant+ (2.5-bit), 90.1% OTT, 88.8% KIVI. OCRBench (multimodal, INT2): Qwen3-VL-8B: 856 versus 858 16-bit, 851 KIVI. Qwen3-VL-4B: 838 versus 852 16-bit, 831 OTT — a 2.5-point gain over the second-best quantized method. DocVQA (multimodal, INT2): Qwen3-VL-8B: 95.01 ANLS versus 94.93 16-bit (marginally exceeds full precision). MMAU-Pro (Qwen3-Omni-30B-A3B, INT2): Open-ended QA 67.4% versus 66.2% 16-bit; audio instruction following 88.5% versus 87.4% 16-bit — OScaR exceeds the full-precision baseline, likely within benchmark variance. Efficiency (single H20 GPU, Qwen3-8B): At 128K context length, decode latency 30.9 ms/tok versus 92.9 ms/tok BF16 (3.0× speedup) and 72.9 ms/tok TurboQuant+ (2.4× faster). At batch size 48, context 4K: 5.3× memory reduction, 4.1× throughput increase. Theoretical decode cost 123M units versus 81.9M (KIVI) and 247.9M (TurboQuant+). Baselines are current and representative for the INT2 regime. The absence of an official TurboQuant code release (replaced by third-party TurboQuant+) and the comparison of OScaR (PyTorch) versus TurboQuant+ (llama.cpp) introduces runtime confounding in efficiency tables. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. The identification of TNI as a structural bottleneck in per-channel quantization, with three distinct manifestation patterns in multi-modal LLMs (broader norm variance, inter-modality disparities, large-norm outlier tokens) beyond text-only attention sinks, is a genuinely new empirical finding. The Canalized Rotation + Omni-Token Scaling combination and its specific rationale — the Scaling-Induced Outlier Artifact analysis proving why CR must precede OTS — constitutes a new method, though individual components (Hadamard transforms in QuaRot/RotateKV; token scaling in normalization literature) are known. The optimized CUDA kernels translating O(d log d) FHT into practical GPU throughput gains represent engineering optimization. The extension to omni-modal LLMs is the first such benchmarking of KV quantization on this model class. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation Table 9 (WorldSense, two model sizes) isolates CR and OTS contributions and confirms mutual necessity — this is a meaningful ablation. Table 10 (LongBench-E, four model families) compares four normalization variants for OTS and identifies rsqrt as the practical optimum; the catastrophic collapse of Max normalization on Qwen2.5-7B (14.47% vs. 42.57%) validates that the choice is non-trivial. No error bars or multi-seed results are reported anywhere; all numbers appear to be single runs. The theoretical MSE lower bound (Appendix G, Eq. 11) is a standard uniform quantization analysis — it proves TNI amplifies error but does not bound residual error post-OScaR, so the theory is illustrative rather than tight. TNI visualization uses a 4-sentence toy prompt, which may not represent real long-context norm distributions. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/ZunhaiSu/OScaR-KV-Quant. This is a preprint; results have not been peer-reviewed. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS — a built inference system with custom CUDA kernels evaluated on real benchmarks. - Practical: Runs on a single H20 GPU (141GB); kernel design uses standard CUDA primitives (WMMA tiles, shared memory butterfly FHT) applicable to A100/H100-class GPUs, though only H20 is tested. - Efficient: Decode overhead over KIVI (the minimal baseline) is 1.5× in theoretical cost and adds online FHT + rsqrt per token; at long contexts the memory bandwidth savings dominate, yielding net 3× speedup over full-precision BF16. Overhead is substantially lower than TurboQuant+ (2× cheaper decode). - Effective: Achieves near-lossless INT2 accuracy on six benchmarks across three modality classes; on NIAH, DocVQA (Qwen3-VL-8B), and MMAU-Pro, OScaR marginally exceeds 16-bit baseline — likely within noise given no significance testing, but not an artifact of cherry-picking. - Scalable: Tested up to 30B parameters (Qwen3-Omni-30B-A3B) and 128K tokens on a single GPU. Multi-GPU tensor-parallel inference — standard for 30B+ models — is not evaluated; norm metadata synchronization across ranks could add overhead not accounted for in reported numbers. Delivers open-source CUDA inference library and model-agnostic plug-in (no retraining required). Strengths - The TNI characterization across X-LLMs is empirically thorough, specifically identifying three qualitatively distinct patterns in multi-modal settings (attention-sink low-norm tokens, inter-modality norm gaps, and large-norm vision outliers) that prior work conflated or ignored; the quantitative error analysis in Table 2 (140% per-channel error increase for mixed-modality groups under INT2) is concrete. - The Scaling-Induced Outlier Artifact analysis (Appendix I) is a non-obvious and rigorously demonstrated explanation for why token scaling alone fails; the algebraic condition (Eq. 12) and numerical example (Eq. 13: channel dynamic range expands 50× after naive scaling) provide genuine insight rather than post-hoc rationalization. - The efficiency result at 128K context (3.0× speedup, near-lossless accuracy) is practically meaningful — the combination of memory savings and bandwidth reduction overcomes the FHT overhead, and the latency plot (Figure 6) shows OScaR's latency remains nearly flat from 16K to 128K while BF16 grows steeply. - Coverage of omni-modal LLMs (Qwen3-Omni-30B-A3B) is the first in the KV quantization literature, and the result (exceeding 16-bit AIF accuracy) suggests OScaR generalizes to audio-visual modality mixing without architecture-specific tuning. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) online Canalized Rotation with RoPE prevents offline fusion of Query and Key Hadamard transforms with weight matrices, incurring unavoidable per-token FHT overhead absent in plain per-channel methods; (2) experiments are primarily on LLM-backbone models, and applicability to streaming vision models (StreamVGGT), visual autoregressive models, and diffusion LLMs with KV cache is hypothesized but untested. Both limitations are confronted directly rather than minimized, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The efficiency comparison between OScaR (PyTorch runtime) and TurboQuant+ (llama.cpp runtime) conflates software framework overhead with algorithmic overhead; OScaR's latency advantage at 128K may be partially explained by framework differences rather than algorithmic superiority, since TurboQuant+ achieves lower latency than OScaR at short contexts (7.8 ms/tok vs. 25.1 ms/tok at 1K), suggesting its kernel efficiency is not uniformly worse. - Evaluation uses a 4-sentence toy prompt for all TNI visualization and norm analysis (Appendix D). Long-context documents with diverse syntactic structures may exhibit different TNI severity and patterns; the claim that TNI is universally the primary bottleneck rests on short-prompt observations generalized to 128K inference. - No layer-adaptive bit allocation: uniform INT2 across all layers leaves known accuracy recovery unused (early and final layers are quantization-sensitive); the 1–1.7% accuracy gaps versus 16-bit on text benchmarks may partially close with minimal mixed-precision. - Multi-GPU tensor-parallel norm metadata synchronization is unaddressed; for 30B+ models in production (which require multiple GPUs), storing and broadcasting per-token norm scalars across tensor-parallel ranks adds an untested communication cost. - The theoretical bound (Eq. 11) proves TNI amplifies MSE but does not prove OScaR minimizes post-transformation error; there is no convergence guarantee that the specific OScaR transformation achieves the best achievable MSE for a given bit budget. Bottom Line OScaR is a practically compelling, immediately deployable contribution for long-context LLM inference — the combination of near-lossless INT2 accuracy across text, vision, and audio modalities, a 3× decoding speedup at 128K context, and open-source CUDA kernels puts it decisively ahead of current alternatives for engineers facing KV cache memory bottlenecks.
Recommended 2605.19660 PDF
#38 — Innovative (71)

Anti-Self-Distillation for Reasoning RL via Pointwise Mutual Information (60 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 20 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Anti-Self-Distillation (AntiSD), a drop-in replacement for standard on-policy self-distillation that inverts the per-token gradient direction — ascending Jensen-Shannon divergence between student and teacher rather than descending KL — and adds an entropy-triggered gate to stabilize training. - Why it's important: On-policy self-distillation is a promising self-improvement mechanism for LLM reasoning that requires no external teacher, but it consistently underperforms simple GRPO on hard math benchmarks; AntiSD explains why and fixes it, reducing training cost by 2–10× and adding up to 11.5 accuracy points. - How it does it: The per-token signal in standard self-distillation is conditional pointwise mutual information between the next token and the privileged context (verified solution), which inflates reward on shortcut tokens already implied by the solution and penalizes deliberation tokens (Wait, Let, Maybe); AntiSD negates this signal, uses JSD's softplus shape to bound the deliberation side, and disables the term via a Schmitt-trigger on teacher entropy. - The good: AntiSD improves final average accuracy over GRPO on all five tested models (4B–30B), with gains of +2.1 to +11.5 points, and the pass@k analysis on HMMT 2025 confirms the gain comes from expanded problem coverage rather than variance reduction. - The missing: The method is evaluated only on DAPO-Math-17k as training data, with 200 on-policy steps; whether the gains persist under longer training, different data mixtures, or at scales above 30B is not tested. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on LLM reasoning post-training — the PMI decomposition of the self-distillation signal is a clean and transferable diagnostic that reframes a widely observed empirical failure, and the fix is simple enough to drop into any GRPO pipeline.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (85) drives the score: identifying that the self-distillation per-token signal is conditional PMI — and that this rewards shortcut tokens while penalizing deliberation tokens — is a genuine root-cause diagnosis of a widely observed failure, and inverting the gradient direction is counter-intuitive. Novelty (75) is high for the same reason (NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING). Rigor (68) and Impact (62) are lifted by the public code release with wandb training curves and the drop-in GRPO compatibility, and gains are large (+2.1 to +11.5 across 5 models), but single-run training with no seeds, only 200 steps on one dataset, and the absence of PRM baselines (PRIME/Math-Shepherd) cap Rigor below the top band. Completeness (68) and Resilience (68) are supported by the pass@k analysis that rules out the mode-concentration objection, but the PMI diagnostic shown on one model and generalized leaves an opening. Composite: 0.20*75 + 0.25*68 + 0.15*85 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*62 = 71.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 85 Completeness 68 Resilience 68 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) provides only a sparse, trajectory-level scalar reward, leaving credit assignment to individual reasoning steps unsolved. On-policy self-distillation — where the same model is conditioned on a verified solution to produce a token-level imitation signal for itself — has been proposed as a teacher-free solution, but consistently delivers inconsistent or negative results on hard math reasoning benchmarks (AIME, HMMT) even when the same approach works on instruction-following or scientific QA. The gap is observed across model families at 4B–30B parameters: default self-distillation frequently underperforms a strong GRPO baseline. The root cause was previously undiagnosed; practitioners knew the symptom (responses shorten, accuracy drops) but not the mechanism. Key Idea The per-token signal δt = log πT(yt)/πS(yt) produced by standard self-distillation is exactly the conditional pointwise mutual information PMI(yt; c | x, y<t), where c is the privileged context (the verified solution). By Bayes' rule, this ratio equals the log-posterior ratio of c given y≤t versus y<t — i.e., how much each token increases the model's confidence in the privileged solution. This identifies a structural asymmetry: tokens that are structurally implied by the known answer ("Given", "holds", "boxed") get high positive signal, while deliberation tokens that re-examine alternatives ("Wait", "Let", "Maybe") get strongly negative signal because the teacher has already committed to a solution. The fix is to invert the sign — reward deliberation, penalize shortcuts — while bounding the inversion using JSD's softplus shape and gating it with teacher entropy. Methodology AntiSD augments the GRPO objective with a per-token advantage term Ai,t = Aseq_i + λ · δt. In standard self-distillation, δt = +ut where ut = log πT(yt | x,c,y<t) − log πS(yt | x,y<t). AntiSD replaces this with δt = −φ(ut), where φ(u) = ½(softplus(u) − log 2) is the f-divergence derivative for JSD. This inverts the per-token sign (rewarding deliberation tokens, penalizing shortcut tokens) and provides a one-sided bound: the advantage on the over-sampled deliberation side is capped at ½ log 2, absorbing extreme ut ≤ −20 spikes, while the shortcut side keeps a linear penalty. The entropy-triggered gate monitors the batch-median teacher entropy H and disables the AntiSD term (λ → 0) when H falls below τdown = 0.93 · Hwarm, re-enabling once H recovers — a Schmitt trigger preventing chatter. The gate is auto-calibrated over 5 warmup steps with no per-model tuning. Critical design decisions: (1) JSD over reverse-KL ascent — well-justified, as reverse-KL ascent collapses in ablations (Qwen3-8B Avg 30.6 vs 65.7); (2) additive over multiplicative composition — well-justified, multiplicative form zeroes out the AntiSD signal precisely when GRPO's sparse reward is uninformative; (3) the 0.93 threshold — partially justified as cross-model transferable, but its sensitivity is model-conditional (−8.3 pp on Qwen3-4B-IT-2507 when loosened to 0.90, negligible on Qwen3-8B). Training uses DAPO-Math-17k, 200 on-policy steps, 8 rollouts per problem, AdamW lr=1e-6, λmax=0.5, on 8 NVIDIA H20 GPUs via verl. Key Results Across five models (Qwen3-4B-IT-2507, Qwen3-8B, Qwen3-30B-A3B MoE, OLMo3-7B-IT, OLMo3-7B-TK) on AIME 2024/2025/2026, HMMT 2025 (avg@32), and MinervaMath (avg@4): AntiSD reaches the GRPO baseline's best accuracy in 2–10× fewer steps (10× on Qwen3-4B-IT-2507, 9.5× on OLMo3-7B-IT, 5× on Qwen3-8B, 2.9× on Qwen3-30B-A3B, 2× on OLMo3-7B-TK). Final average accuracy exceeds GRPO by +11.5 pp on Qwen3-8B (65.7 vs 57.4), +11.5 on Qwen3-4B-IT-2507 (62.8 vs 51.3), +5.3 on OLMo3-7B-IT (48.3 vs 43.0), +7.7 on Qwen3-30B-A3B (66.8 vs 59.1), +2.1 on OLMo3-7B-TK (66.2 vs 64.1). Default SD underperforms GRPO on every model, sometimes by wide margins (Qwen3-8B: 30.6 vs 57.4). On HMMT 2025 pass@k, AntiSD's lead over GRPO on Qwen3-8B is ~13 pp at k=1 and ~7–10 pp at k=32, indicating expanded problem coverage rather than variance reduction. Code reasoning (Qwen3-8B on HumanEval+ and MBPP+) shows +1.2 and +2.3 pp over GRPO, smaller but directionally consistent. Continual AntiSD applied to a saturated GRPO checkpoint on Qwen3-8B recovers 65.0 of the 65.7 from-base peak in only 30 post-resume steps. All results are from a preprint; no peer review. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The method (AntiSD) is genuinely novel in its diagnosis and fix: the PMI characterization of the self-distillation per-token signal as conditional PMI is a clean formal identity not previously stated, and inverting the gradient direction with JSD bounding plus entropy gating is a non-obvious algorithmic choice with theoretical grounding. The empirical finding that default self-distillation reliably underperforms GRPO across 5 model families on AIME/HMMT-class problems, explained through the PMI lens, is an important diagnostic contribution. The components individually (JSD divergence, entropy gating, privileged context) are standard; the contribution is their composition motivated by the PMI analysis. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover the three main design knobs (divergence type, gate threshold, composition mode) across two model families, with failure modes explicitly shown (Figure 5). The pass@k analysis (Figure 3) provides important evidence distinguishing coverage gains from variance reduction. Code is publicly released (github.com/FloyedShen/AntiSD) with training curves on wandb. Methodological concerns: (1) all models are trained for only 200 on-policy steps — short enough that long-horizon stability is not demonstrated; (2) training data is fixed to DAPO-Math-17k, limiting generalization claims; (3) the PMI diagnostic (Figure 2) is computed on a single model (Qwen3-4B-IT-2507) at one benchmark (AIME-25), then generalized to all models; (4) gate threshold sensitivity is acknowledged as model-conditional but the 0.93 value is reported as a transferable default without error bars or seeds to quantify variability; (5) no comparison to strong PRM baselines (PRIME, Math-Shepherd) that are direct competitors on the credit-assignment problem. avg@32 on AIME is expensive and meaningful, but single-run training curves without multiple seeds leave statistical uncertainty uncharacterized. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper runs training experiments on real hardware and evaluates on held-out benchmarks. - Practical: AntiSD requires one extra forward pass per token per rollout (the teacher conditioning) and a median entropy computation per batch, adding negligible overhead beyond standard GRPO; runs on 8× H20 GPUs for models up to 30B, which is commercially available. - Efficient: The authors claim "no additional cost" as a drop-in for self-distillation — the teacher forward pass is required by SD anyway; compared to GRPO alone, the cost is one additional conditional forward pass per rollout, modest relative to the 2–10× step reduction. - Effective: The evidence is strong on math reasoning across 5 models; code reasoning results are promising but thin (one model, two benchmarks, small gains); multi-turn agentic tasks are not tested. - Scalable: Tested up to 30B (Qwen3-30B-A3B MoE); gains persist at that scale (+7.7 pp), but 100B+ is not tested and claimed only qualitatively. Training is bounded at 200 steps; behavior at longer horizons is unknown. The work delivers a software artifact (code + training scripts) and a reusable proof technique (Lemmas 1–5 in appendix). Strengths - The PMI identity (Lemma 2 + Lemma 3) provides a rigorous, clean explanation for why standard self-distillation fails on hard reasoning tasks: it is potential-based reward shaping that biases toward tokens the privileged context already implies, not toward tokens that drive search.
