This is a genuine theoretical contribution that unifies several empirical observations. - The JSD ascent formulation is principled: the asymmetric bounding property (½ log 2 cap on the over-sampled deliberation side, linear on shortcut side) directly addresses the observed asymmetry in the ut distribution (Figure 2b), and this design choice is validated by ablation showing reverse-KL ascent collapses. - The entropy gate design (Schmitt trigger, auto-calibrated from 5 warmup steps, single shared threshold 0.93 across model families) is practical and minimizes hyperparameter burden, with the failure mode (no-gate collapse on Qwen) explicitly demonstrated. - The pass@k analysis (Figure 3) is an important methodological contribution to the self-improvement literature: it rules out the explanation that gains come from mode concentration at the cost of diversity, showing sustained lead out to k=32. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) the PMI account is a local per-step characterization, not a global optimum statement about the combined objective; (2) evaluation is limited to math reasoning (4B–30B) with only an initial code probe; (3) extensions to multi-turn agentic settings, broader coding benchmarks, larger scales, and richer privileged context (process-level critiques, rationale rankings) are left as future work. They also note the threshold is not a per-model optimum. The authors engage with limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The PMI diagnostic Figure 2 is shown for one model (Qwen3-4B-IT-2507) at one benchmark; the claim that this mechanism drives failure across all five model families is supported by the performance patterns but not by showing the PMI signal analysis on each model — it is assumed to generalize. - No comparison to competitive PRM methods (PRIME, Math-Shepherd, OmegaPRM) on the same benchmarks; these are the direct competitors on the credit-assignment problem, and their omission makes it impossible to assess whether AntiSD closes the gap to the best available dense-signal approaches. - All results use a single training run per condition (no seeds); given the sensitivity of RL training to initialization and the demonstrated model-conditional gate behavior, variance estimates are absent and the reported gains may be noisy. - The code reasoning evaluation is too thin to support the generalization claim: one model, two benchmarks, +1.2/+2.3 pp gains that could be within noise without error bars. - The continual AntiSD result (Table 4) is interesting but unexplained mechanistically: why does Qwen3-4B-IT-2507 end 2.3 pp short of from-base while Qwen3-8B essentially closes the gap? The two interpretations offered (basin geometry vs. conservative gate) are not tested. - Training is capped at 200 steps on a single dataset (DAPO-Math-17k); it is unknown whether the deliberation bias persists and remains beneficial under longer training or distributional shift. Bottom Line AntiSD delivers a theoretically grounded, practically cheap fix to a widely observed failure mode in LLM reasoning RL — the PMI analysis is convincing and the empirical gains across five models are large enough to warrant adoption — but the absence of multi-seed runs, PRM comparisons, and out-of-distribution evaluation makes the magnitude of the claims somewhat provisional for a practitioner deciding whether to replace their current training recipe.
Recommended 2605.11609 PDF
#39 — Innovative (71)

CiteVQA: Benchmarking Evidence Attribution for Trustworthy Document Intelligence (86 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 18 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CiteVQA, a benchmark of 1,897 questions over 711 multi-page PDFs that requires MLLMs to return element-level bounding-box citations alongside answers, evaluated jointly via a new Strict Attributed Accuracy (SAA) metric. - Why it's important: Existing Doc-VQA benchmarks score only final answers, masking a critical failure mode where models produce correct text grounded in the wrong document region — a safety risk in law, finance, and medicine where every conclusion must be traceable to a specific source. - How it does it: An automated pipeline uses MinerU document parsing, MLLM agents to extract multi-page evidence chains, template-distilled QA synthesis from real-world domain datasets, and a masking-ablation procedure to identify crucial evidence elements, with LLM judges evaluating both answer correctness and evidence relevance. - The good: The benchmark quantifies a pervasive "Attribution Hallucination" gap — GPT-5.4 achieves 87.1 answer accuracy but only 59.0 SAA, and the best open-source model (Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B) reaches only 22.5 SAA despite 72.3 answer accuracy — demonstrating that frontier models cannot reliably ground answers in correct document regions. - The missing: Crucial evidence ground truth is labeled by the same class of frontier MLLM (Gemini) used as a top test model, introducing potential circular bias; no cross-family annotation validation or IoU threshold sensitivity analysis is provided. - Read it or not: Essential for researchers building or evaluating document intelligence systems in high-stakes domains; the 20-model audit provides immediately actionable numbers about where current MLLMs fail on traceable reasoning.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile (dataset/benchmark + empirical-finding paper). Novelty (75) and Non-Obviousness (75) carry the score: element-level, jointly-scored attribution across multi-page/multi-domain/cross-lingual documents is a genuinely new benchmark configuration, the SAA metric is a new joint formalization, and the masking-ablation operationalization of 'crucial evidence' via causal necessity (rather than annotator judgment) is a principled, non-obvious move. Impact (75) is high because code and BBox metadata are open-sourced and the Table-8 result shows the pipeline data rivals human-annotated SFT data, making it usable as both eval and training source. Rigor (68) is pulled down by preprint status (no peer review) plus circular annotation — Gemini-class models label crucial evidence while Gemini-3.1-Pro is the top test model — and no IoU-threshold or cross-family sensitivity analysis; Resilience (65) reflects that this circularity and the lenient SAA OR-condition are exploitable under adversarial review. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*68 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*75 = 71.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Existing Doc-VQA benchmarks (DocVQA, MMLongBench-Doc, SlideVQA, ViDoRe V3) evaluate models solely on final answer accuracy. This creates a failure mode: models can achieve high answer scores by relying on parametric knowledge or by finding the correct answer while citing the wrong document region. In legal consultation, financial auditing, and clinical decision support, traceability of every conclusion to a specific source passage is a non-negotiable requirement — a wrong citation is itself an error even when the textual answer is correct. No prior benchmark jointly evaluates answer accuracy and spatial evidence attribution at the element level using a unified, standardized metric. Bounding-box-annotated datasets that exist (SlideVQA, ViDoRe V3) evaluate citation and answer as separate tasks rather than with a single sample-level criterion, and none operate at element granularity across multi-page, multi-domain, cross-lingual long documents. Key Idea The core technical insight is operationalizing "necessary evidence" objectively through masking ablation rather than human annotation: an element is labeled "crucial" if covering it (masking individual BBox elements one at a time before presenting to a powerful MLLM) causes the model to fail the question. This grounds the evaluation metric in causal necessity rather than annotator judgment about what looks relevant. The SAA metric then enforces a strict conjunction: credit a model only when it gets both the text answer (Ans ≥ 4/5) and the spatial citation (Rel ≥ 4/5 or Recall IoU@0.5 ≥ 0.6) right on the same sample. Methodology The construction pipeline has four stages. (1) Document collection: 100M+ Common Crawl PDFs → 250k stratified candidates → 711 final documents via two-stage MLLM coarse/fine domain classification, covering 7 macro-domains, 30 sub-categories, English (451) and Chinese (260). (2) Evidence package extraction: MinerU2.5 parses PDFs into element-level OCR + BBox structures. MLLM agents (Gemini-3.0-Flash-Preview) navigate the BBox space to assemble multi-page, multi-element evidence chains (minimum 2 pages, 2 element types per bundle). Multi-document linking uses MLLM-generated semantic profiles, dense retrieval (top-5 by cosine similarity), and chain-of-thought cross-document section matching to form linked document groups. (3) QA construction: Real questions from five domain datasets (SPIQA, MedQA, PubMedQA, ViDoRe V3, MaintNorm, PolicyBench) are distilled into four template categories (Factual Retrieval, Complex Synthesis, Quantitative Reasoning, Multimodal Parsing); MLLMs synthesize new QA pairs guided by templates and evidence packages. (4) Quality control: answerability verification (retain only pairs answerable from evidence screenshots alone), zero-document self-test to discard common-knowledge questions, and masking-ablation crucial evidence identification. Critical design decisions: (a) SAA uses an OR condition on evidence quality (Rel ≥ 4 OR Rec ≥ 0.6), which permits passage via either semantic alignment or spatial overlap alone — slightly lenient but practically motivated; (b) element-level BBox granularity (not page-level) is the evaluation target, which is more demanding but more useful for human verification; (c) automated annotation pipeline is validated via PhD-level expert audit on 200 samples. Key Results 20 MLLMs evaluated on 1,897 questions. Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview leads overall: SAA=76.0, Ans=86.1, Rec=66.0. GPT-5.4 achieves the highest answer accuracy (87.1) but SAA=59.0 — the largest absolute attribution gap among top models, 28 points. Gemini-3-Flash-Preview: Ans=84.5, SAA=65.4. Gemini-2.5-Pro: Ans=82.2, SAA=47.0 despite strong Pagerecall (90.3%), suggesting it navigates to correct pages but fails fine-grained localization. Best open-source: Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B at SAA=22.5, Ans=72.3 — a dramatic cliff versus closed-source. Small open-source models fall below SAA=10 (Qwen3-VL-8B=7.5). Multi-doc (N-Gold) is the hardest scenario: Gemini-3.1-Pro Recall drops from 68.9 (single-doc) to 55.3 (N-Gold). Resolution matters non-linearly: halving pixel budget (1024² → 724²) cuts SAA from 22.5 to 11.8 for Qwen3-VL-235B — a near-50% collapse while answer accuracy falls only 8%. Ablation restricting context to GT pages yields +2–5.3% answer accuracy; providing a single gold document in multi-doc tasks yields +5–13.4% — demonstrating that retrieval/localization is a binding bottleneck independent of reasoning capacity. Academic Tech documents yield highest SAA (Gemini-3.1-Pro at 85.0); Publishing & Media is hardest (max SAA=63.3). Automated judges validated via Friedman test (p-values 0.14–0.53) on 200 human-scored samples show no statistically significant deviation. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. Element-level, jointly-scored evidence attribution in multi-page, multi-domain, cross-lingual document VQA is genuinely novel — no prior benchmark combines all three properties. The SAA metric is a new formalization of joint answer-evidence scoring. The "Attribution Hallucination" pattern is a new empirical finding, though conceptually related to known MLLM hallucination phenomena. The automated pipeline reuses existing components (MinerU, MLLM agents, masking ablation) in a new configuration; no fundamentally new algorithm is introduced. Rigor & Reproducibility PHD-level expert audit on 200 randomly sampled instances (Table 7) rates Evidence Quality at 4.91/5, Answer Quality at 4.43/5 — credible, though 200/1,897 is a 10.5% sample. Automated judge validation via non-parametric Friedman test on 200 samples for two inference models is appropriate and yields p-values well above 0.05. Training validation (Table 8) showing CiteVQA-generated data (3k samples) achieves comparable SFT performance to 5k human-annotated samples on FinRAGBench-V and ViDoRe V3 is a strong practical validation. Resolution sensitivity experiment (Table 10) and search-space ablation (Table 4) are methodologically sound. Code and BBox metadata are open-sourced at https://github.com/opendatalab/CiteVQA; actual PDFs are distributed via download links only for copyright compliance. Key reproducibility concern: the pipeline requires access to frontier MLLMs (Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview, Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B) that are not freely accessible at scale, making large-scale replication costly. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.12882v1, May 2026); results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) Profile: EMPIRICAL — constructs a dataset and evaluates 20 deployed systems. - Practical: Benchmark evaluation runs against any model with an API or local deployment; the evaluation harness requires standard GPU infrastructure (authors used 8×H200 for open-source models). - Efficient: Processing 40.6-page average documents as image sequences is VRAM-intensive; automated annotation depends on frontier MLLM inference (Gemini, Qwen3-VL-235B) which is expensive and not self-hostable without significant compute. - Effective: SAA demonstrably captures a real failure mode — the consistent Ans-vs-SAA gap across all 20 models confirms the metric measures something invisible to answer-only evaluation; expert scores validate annotation quality. - Scalable: The pipeline architecture scales — selecting 711 documents from 100M PDFs proves feasibility at corpus scale; extending to additional domains or languages is architecturally straightforward, but frontier MLLM annotation cost scales linearly with dataset size. The paper delivers a dataset and evaluation framework; no trained model, library, or CLI is released. Strengths - The masking-ablation method for identifying crucial evidence is more principled than human annotation of "relevant regions" — it operationalizes causal necessity, eliminating annotator subjectivity about which parts of a passage actually support an answer. - The SAA metric's joint binary requirement prevents models from inflating scores by producing many candidate evidence boxes (since Relevance and Recall average over all predicted boxes, a model citing both correct and incorrect regions is still penalized). - The empirical finding that answer accuracy positively correlates with evidence quality beyond the hallucination zone (Figure 6) — suggesting attribution is a functional prerequisite for reasoning, not merely post-hoc justification — is a substantive discovery beyond benchmarking. - The training utility validation (Table 8) is a non-obvious contribution: showing that automated pipeline data achieves near-human-annotated SFT performance makes CiteVQA actionable as a training data source, not just an evaluation tool. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) domain-specific nuance in authoritative evidence for highly specialized vertical fields may not be captured; (2) frontier MLLM dependency creates a computational barrier for large-scale replication; (3) multi-dimensional evaluation has higher overhead than standard VQA; (4) risk of models overfitting to CiteVQA's specific metrics and document distribution, reducing generalizability to unseen real-world structures. The authors confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Circular annotation: Crucial evidence is labeled using Gemini-class MLLMs, and Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview is the top-performing test model. If Gemini's internal attention patterns systematically prefer certain evidence regions, its SAA scores may be artificially inflated relative to models with different grounding mechanisms. No cross-family annotation validation (e.g., using GPT as annotator and measuring Gemini vs. GPT performance differential) is presented. - IoU threshold arbitrariness: SAA's Recall branch requires IoU ≥ 0.5; a predicted box overlapping 49% of the crucial region scores 0. No sensitivity analysis on IoU threshold is provided — for large tables spanning half a page, the threshold choice may substantially change rankings. - No inter-annotator agreement on evidence completeness: For questions where multiple non-overlapping regions each independently support the answer, masking any one may not cause failure — these may all be labeled "supplemental" even if any one of them would be a legitimate citation. Models citing a valid non-crucial element are penalized. - Language performance not disaggregated: With 451 English and 260 Chinese documents, cross-lingual attribution performance is not separately reported; whether the open-source/closed-source gap is language-dependent is unknown. - No human annotation baseline: All 1,897 QA pairs are machine-generated; the 200-sample audit validates quality but does not include human-authored questions. Whether template-distilled questions capture the distribution of real professional queries in legal/financial workflows is empirically untested. Bottom Line CiteVQA fills a genuine gap in the evaluation landscape and produces the first systematic evidence that frontier MLLMs — including GPT-5.4 and Gemini-3.1-Pro — cannot reliably trace their correct answers to the right document regions; practitioners deploying these models in auditable workflows should read this before assuming high answer accuracy implies trustworthy grounding.
Recommended 2605.12882 PDF
#40 — Innovative (71)

LiteFrame: Efficient Vision Encoders Unlock Frame Scaling in Video LLMs (19 ▲ )

Jihwan Kim, Nikhil Parthasarathy, Danfeng Qin, Junhwa Hur, Deqing Sun, Bohyung Han, Ming-Hsuan Yang, Boqing Gong · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LiteFrame, a compact 87M-parameter video encoder trained via Compressed Token Distillation (CTD) that internalizes spatio-temporal token compression within the vision backbone, replacing the standard heavy ViT in Video LLMs to eliminate the latency floor imposed by post-hoc token reduction. - Why it's important: Existing post-hoc token reduction methods—the dominant efficiency strategy for Video LLMs—only shift the latency bottleneck from the LLM to the vision encoder, meaning inference cost cannot be reduced below the encoder's per-frame cost regardless of how aggressively tokens are pruned afterward. - How it does it: A ViT-Base student with interleaved depth-wise 1D temporal convolutions and progressive strided downsampling layers is trained to minimize MSE against WAP-compressed (not full-resolution) teacher features—explicitly teaching the student to output compact, information-dense representations rather than redundant dense feature maps. - The good: At matched or lower total latency, LiteFrame processes 8× more frames than InternVL3-8B and achieves higher average accuracy across four long-video benchmarks (65.7% vs. 65.3%), while simultaneously outperforming all tested post-hoc baselines—a strict Pareto improvement over both the teacher and the competing paradigm. - The missing: All experiments use InternVL3-8B as the sole teacher and LLM; no ablation demonstrates that CTD transfers to a different ViT architecture or language model backbone. - Read it or not: Engineers and researchers building efficient Video LLM inference pipelines should read this—the bottleneck diagnosis is rigorous, the CTD design choice is non-obvious and well-validated, and the latency gains are large and practically meaningful.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (efficiency-driven encoder replacement; Impact-weighted). Practical Impact (72) and Non-Obviousness (78) carry the score: the CTD insight of distilling to WAP-compressed rather than full-resolution teacher features is non-obvious and decisively validated (CTD-without-LMA beats RTD-with-LMA), and the strict Pareto improvement (8× more frames at lower latency, +0.4%p accuracy) is practically meaningful. Rigor (62) is held back by single-teacher/single-LLM lock-in (InternVL3-8B everywhere), no error bars on sub-1%p margins, and unconfirmed code/weight release — a real ding for a systems paper whose value rests on the artifact. Completeness (75) is helped by honest limitations (training instabilities below 87M, untested image tasks). Weighted: 0.15*70 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*78 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*68 + 0.30*72 = 71.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 75 Resilience 68 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Video LLMs process video frame-by-frame with a heavy vision encoder (typically ViT-Large), an alignment projector, and a transformer-based LLM. As frame count grows for long-form video understanding, both encoder and LLM cost increase. The field has converged on post-hoc token reduction—applying pruning or merging after the encoder—to reduce the LLM's quadratic attention overhead. The paper identifies an overlooked consequence: once post-hoc compression is aggressive enough that LLM cost drops, encoder cost dominates latency, setting an irreducible floor. Specifically, at 16× post-hoc compression with 64 frames, encoder latency exceeds LLM latency for InternVL3-8B, making further efficiency gains from token reduction impossible. No prior work addressed efficient video-specific encoding as the primary target for reducing end-to-end latency in Video LLMs. Key Idea The central insight is that a compact student encoder should be trained to predict the WAP-compressed output of the teacher (not the full-resolution output), so that compression is baked into the network weights rather than applied as a runtime post-processing step. This lets the student simultaneously produce fewer tokens and run faster, resolving both bottlenecks at once. The non-obvious component is the distillation target: using the WAP primitive—which preserves spatial structure via class-token-weighted pooling—as the supervision signal rather than a generic reconstruction objective produces a semantically richer compressed representation for downstream LLM reasoning. Methodology The approach has three stages. First, a lightweight spatio-temporal encoder architecture is designed: a 12-layer, 768D ViT-Base (87M params) backbone with depth-wise (DW) 1D temporal convolution layers interleaved after every spatial attention block (where temporal dimension > 1), plus DW strided convolution layers after the 4th and 8th blocks with strides [t,h,w]=[2,2,2] and [2,1,1] to progressively downsample feature maps. This reduces 256 tokens/frame to 16 tokens/frame internally. Second, Compressed Token Distillation (CTD) trains this student against WAP-compressed teacher features: L_CTD = ||S_θ(x) - P(T(x))||², where P is the WAP operator applied to the teacher's dense output at 16× compression. WAP pools non-overlapping spatio-temporal blocks weighted by class-token attention scores, preserving structure that attention-based pruning disrupts. CTD runs for 1800 epochs on 8× H100 GPUs (~21 days), batch size 512, AdamW with cosine LR 4e-5. Third, Language Model Adaptation (LMA) fine-tunes the LLM via LoRA (r=4, α=8) and the student encoder together on video-text pairs with cross-entropy loss, using {128, 256, 512} frame inputs for 25K steps. Critical design decisions: (1) DW temporal conv over full spatio-temporal attention—same FLOPs but 14% lower latency (174.84ms vs. 204.35ms at 256 frames) and higher accuracy; (2) WAP-compressed distillation target over auto-encoding (RTD)—CTD without LMA (62.8%) beats RTD with LMA (61.5%), confirming the target design is essential; (3) spatio-temporal compression (4×spatial, 4×temporal) over spatial-only 16× compression—at 128 frames, 62.8% vs. 60.5% average accuracy, with the largest gap on Video-MME w/o subtitles (61.9% vs. 57.0%). Key Results Primary evaluation uses four benchmarks: Video-MME (w/ and w/o subtitles; CVPR 2025), MLVU (CVPR 2025), LongVideoBench (NeurIPS 2024). Baseline is InternVL3-8B with InternViT-300M (304M params). At matched latency budgets: LiteFrame at 256 frames (532.3ms) achieves 65.7% average vs. InternVL3-8B at 32 frames (814.5ms, 65.3%)—35% lower latency with 0.4%p higher accuracy. At tighter budgets (8 frames for teacher): LiteFrame at 64 frames (150.1ms vs. 208.4ms) improves accuracy by 2.1%p (61.1% vs. 59.0%). FastVID (post-hoc SOTA) at 128 frames costs 794.9ms and achieves 62.9%—strictly dominated. AutoGaze at 256 frames costs 6106.5ms (682% slower than LiteFrame) with 63.1% accuracy on a different base model. Encoder-only speedup: 5.3× faster ViT, 12.7× faster LLM prefilling at 64 frames vs. teacher. On HLVid (zero-shot spatial scaling): LiteFrame achieves 54.1 at 2688px/48 frames, vs. AutoGaze 52.6 at 3584px/1024 frames, with no high-resolution training. LiteFrame is 3.3× faster than FastVLM and 1.2× faster than VideoPanda in total latency at 32 frames. On short benchmarks: 28% latency reduction on MVBench and 63% on TVBench while maintaining accuracy. All baselines are current (2025-2026) and represent the strongest available post-hoc and encoder-replacement methods. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is (1) the empirical diagnosis and quantification of the encoder-latency bottleneck shift—a field-wide oversight—and (2) CTD, which is non-obvious in its choice of WAP-compressed (rather than full-resolution or auto-encoded) features as the distillation target; this design choice is the load-bearing innovation and is rigorously validated against alternatives. The architecture (DW temporal conv + progressive strided downsampling in a ViT) is a competent but incremental combination of known components; the training framework and its specific target formulation are the genuine contributions. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation coverage is thorough: each of the four components (token-compressive architecture, DW temporal convolutions, WAP distillation target, LMA) is individually removed (Table 5); alternative distillation objectives (CTD vs. RTD) are compared across frame counts (Table 6); compression strategies (spatio-temporal vs. spatial-only vs. no compression) are compared on full benchmark suites (Table 7). No error bars, confidence intervals, or multi-seed results are reported—all numbers appear to be single runs. Code availability is not stated in the paper; the project page (jjihwan.github.io/projects/LiteFrame) is listed but no explicit code or model weight release is mentioned. Significant methodological concern: all experiments use InternVL3-8B exclusively as both teacher and LLM—generalizability to other architectures is unvalidated. Latency is measured as median over 100 iterations on a single A100-80GB, which is a reasonable methodology. The paper is an arXiv preprint (v1, 2026-05-17); results are not peer-reviewed. Training cost (21 days on 8× H100 GPUs for CTD) is stated, which is a reproducibility barrier. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Inference runs on a single A100-80GB GPU; the 87M student encoder fits on commodity accelerators and reduces total memory pressure relative to the 304M teacher. - Efficient: Student encoder is 71% smaller than teacher (87M vs. 304M params) and produces 16× fewer tokens/frame (16 vs. 256); at 64 frames this yields 5.3× ViT speedup and 12.7× LLM prefilling speedup, with overall latency well under 600ms at 256 frames on A100. - Effective: Across four standard long-video benchmarks, LiteFrame simultaneously reduces latency and improves accuracy versus its teacher baseline—this is validated at multiple latency budgets (8, 16, 32 base frames for teacher). The HLVid zero-shot spatial result (54.1 vs. 52.6 AutoGaze) with no high-resolution training is particularly credible evidence of the encoder's generalization. - Scalable: The system is tested up to 512 input frames (LVBench, Figure 9) and demonstrated at 256 frames on the main benchmarks; accuracy improvements hold through the tested range. Behavior beyond 512 frames is not evaluated, and the LLM's attention over 512×16=8192 visual tokens remains quadratic—this sets a practical ceiling that the paper does not characterize. The paper delivers a trained system; model weights are not explicitly confirmed as released. Strengths - The bottleneck diagnosis is rigorous and practically important: the paper quantifies exactly when and why post-hoc reduction creates an encoder-latency floor, correcting a widely held implicit assumption in the field. - CTD's design (distilling to compressed rather than full-resolution teacher features) is non-obvious and the ablation against RTD (Table 6) is decisive: CTD without LMA outperforms RTD with LMA, providing strong evidence that the target design—not just having a lightweight student—explains the gains. - The Pareto improvement is genuine: LiteFrame simultaneously achieves lower latency and higher accuracy versus the teacher, not just a tradeoff, which makes the practical case compelling. - The zero-shot spatial scaling result (HLVid SOTA without high-resolution training) is a non-trivial byproduct of token efficiency, adding credibility to the encoder's learned representations. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge three limitations: (1) LMA was trained on a subset of existing video data and could benefit from higher-quality long-form video datasets; (2) LiteFrame was evaluated exclusively on video benchmarks, leaving performance on static image tasks unexplored; (3) scaling down to ultra-lightweight student models (<87M params) caused training instabilities (loss explosions), limiting applicability to smaller encoders. These are honest, substantive admissions—the authors do not minimize them. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single-teacher lock-in: every experiment uses InternVL3-8B as teacher, student, and LLM. Whether CTD transfers to a different ViT family (e.g., SigLIP, EVA-CLIP) or a different LLM is completely untested. The distillation procedure is tightly coupled to InternViT-300M's class token structure (WAP uses the class token as the weighting signal), which may not transfer. - No statistical significance testing for accuracy differences that are often <1%p (e.g., 65.7% vs. 65.3% in the primary comparison)—single-run results at these margins are within plausible noise. - The 512-frame capability is claimed for LMA but Table 3's systematic latency-accuracy characterization stops at 256 frames; LVBench at 512 frames (Figure 9) is a single data point on one benchmark. - Fine-grained visual tasks beyond HLVid are not evaluated—the 16 tokens/frame output is a 4×4 spatial grid per frame after 16× compression, which may fail on tasks requiring dense spatial detail (OCR in video, counting, localization). Video-MME and MLVU partially cover this but may not stress-test spatial fidelity. - Training cost (21 days on 8× H100s for CTD alone) makes replication and adaptation to new base models non-trivial; the paper does not report whether shorter training (e.g., 400 epochs) approaches competitive performance. Bottom Line A practitioner building efficient long-form video understanding systems gains a directly applicable, well-validated encoder replacement with a rigorous training recipe; the CTD framework is the transferable intellectual contribution worth understanding even for those working with different base models.
Recommended 2605.17260 PDF
#41 — Innovative (71)

Video2GUI: Synthesizing Large-Scale Interaction Trajectories for Generalized GUI Agent Pretraining (86 ▲ )

Weimin Xiong, Shuhao Gu, Bowen Ye, Zihao Yue, Lei Li, Feifan Song, Sujian Li, Hao Tian · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 21 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Video2GUI, a fully automated three-stage pipeline that extracts grounded GUI interaction trajectories from unlabeled YouTube tutorial videos, producing WildGUI, a dataset of 12.7 million trajectories across 1,500+ applications and websites. - Why it's important: GUI agent training is bottlenecked by the high cost of manual annotation and the narrow domain coverage of existing datasets; WildGUI is an order of magnitude larger than the next biggest open-source GUI pretraining dataset, unlocking internet-scale behavioral data without human labeling. - How it does it: A coarse-to-fine funnel (LLM-fine-tuned metadata classifier → VLM-based video quality scorer → Gemini-3-Pro trajectory extraction with sliding-window context → multi-frame spatial grounding) converts raw video into structured (instruction, grounded action sequence) pairs, then models are pretrained on three complementary objectives (grounding, action prediction, trajectory modeling) before supervised post-training. - The good: Mimo-VL-7B pretrained on WildGUI achieves 67.6 average on OSWorld-G, surpassing both Seed1.5-VL (62.9) and Qwen3-VL-32B (60.6) despite 4.5× smaller parameter count; AndroidWorld success rate nearly doubles from 16.4% (base) to 31.9% (Stage 1+2), demonstrating offline-to-online transfer. - The missing: All trajectory annotation is generated by a single proprietary model (Gemini-3-Pro); no analysis of semantic or temporal error rate in extracted trajectories at scale — the 200-sample spatial grounding check validates coordinate accuracy but not whether the action sequence itself is correct. - Read it or not: Essential reading for anyone building GUI agents or large-scale video-derived data pipelines; the dataset and pipeline are the primary deliverables and will likely define the pretraining standard for 7B-scale GUI agents in the near term.
Why this rank: Scored using the Systems profile, where Practical Impact (85, 30% weight) drives the composite: WildGUI is an order-of-magnitude-larger dataset plus a released pipeline and trained checkpoints that plausibly set the 7B GUI-agent pretraining standard, and the offline-to-online transfer demonstrates genuine generalization. Rigor (70) is solid — multi-stage quantitative validation (R²≥0.845, Krippendorff α=0.84, >95% grounding) and strong current baselines — but it loses points for a single proprietary annotation oracle (Gemini-3-Pro) whose biases propagate, and for validating only coordinate accuracy, not semantic trajectory correctness at 12.7M scale. Completeness (55) is pulled down hardest by the explicit absence of any acknowledged limitations, a real credibility negative. The result is the top of the Innovative band despite being a preprint.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 55 Resilience 62 Impact 85
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation GUI agents that autonomously operate digital interfaces (web, desktop, mobile) require large, diverse trajectory datasets capturing real user behavior across many applications. Existing resources fall into two camps: manually annotated datasets (AITW at 715K images, AndroidControl at 15K images, MIND2WEB at 2.3K tasks) that are high quality but expensive and narrow in scope, and simulated environments that lack authentic interface diversity. The largest prior open-source cross-platform dataset, GUI-Net, contains 1M trajectories. The gap is the absence of a scalable, zero-annotation-cost method for converting the vast existing corpus of publicly available screen-recording tutorial videos — which encode dense, naturalistic GUI demonstrations with step-level narration — into structured training data. No prior approach successfully scaled beyond single-platform coverage or avoided brittle keyword-retrieval for video sourcing. Key Idea The core insight is that YouTube tutorial videos implicitly encode GUI interaction knowledge in their visual and audio streams, and that the mismatch between the low resolution needed for long-context trajectory parsing and the high resolution needed for pixel-level action grounding can be resolved by decoupling the two steps: extract coarse temporal annotations from compressed video, then re-ground each action to high-resolution frames via a temporal triplet (t−0.5s, t, t+0.5s). This multi-frame fallback strategy addresses the aliasing problem introduced by video compression at GUI interaction frequencies, enabling precise coordinate prediction without requiring uncompressed video throughout the pipeline. Methodology The pipeline has three stages. Stage A (Video Filtering): Starting from 500M YouTube video metadata entries, a Qwen2.5-7B text classifier (fine-tuned on 10K samples labeled by DeepSeek-V3, cross-entropy loss, 3 epochs at lr=2×10⁻⁵) reduces the set to ~20M candidates. A Qwen2.5-Omni video scorer (fine-tuned on 200h labeled by Gemini-3-Pro, MSE regression on three dimensions — topic relevance, instruction clarity, recording quality — threshold ≥4.2 on all three) further selects 4.16M videos (~300K hours). Stage B (Trajectory Extraction): Gemini-3-Pro processes 4-minute sliding windows with prior-segment history as context, outputting task instructions, dense captions, action plans, platforms, and per-step annotations (timestamp, action type, grounding instruction, rationale, parameters). A video segmentation preprocessing step pre-splits each video at atomic action boundaries before feeding to the model. Stage C (Spatial Grounding): For each extracted action timestamp t, three high-resolution frames are retrieved; Gemini-3-Pro predicts bounding box and center point coordinates in relative 0-1000 space, using the first frame where grounding is feasible. Actions failing all three frames are discarded. Agent Training: Stage 1 continual pretraining on WildGUI (1 epoch, ~200B tokens, mixed objective Lpretrain = Lground + Laction + Ltraj, 24,000 steps, lr from 2.5×10⁻⁵ to 1.0×10⁻⁵ for LM, 8.0×10⁻⁶ fixed for vision encoder, 256 NVIDIA GPUs). Stage 2 supervised fine-tuning on 9 curated open-source datasets (3 epochs, ~15B tokens, lr from 1.0×10⁻⁵ to 1.0×10⁻⁶). Critical design decisions: (1) lightweight classifier proxies for frontier-model annotation are well-justified by cost/throughput tradeoffs, (2) the multi-frame triplet is empirically motivated by GUI action frequency, (3) the separation of pretraining and post-training is validated by ablation showing catastrophic collapse without Stage 2 (AndroidWorld SR drops to 6.0%). Key Results On GUI grounding: Mimo-VL-7B+WildGUI achieves 67.6 avg on OSWorld-G (base: 54.7, +12.9pp), surpassing Seed1.5-VL at 62.9 and Qwen3-VL-32B at 60.6. On ScreenSpot-Pro, it reaches 56.9 (base: 41.2, +15.7pp), second only to Seed1.5-VL (60.9). Qwen2.5-VL-7B+WildGUI improves from 26.8 to 41.9 on ScreenSpot-Pro (+15.1pp). On offline agent evaluation: Mimo-VL-7B+WildGUI achieves 91.8 Step SR on AndroidControl-Low (base 87.9) and 71.4 on AndroidControl-High (base 65.6); on CAGUI (Chinese interfaces), type accuracy 90.3 vs. base 82.2. On online evaluation: AndroidWorld SR improves from 16.4% (base) to 23.3% (Stage2-only) to 31.9% (Stage1+2); OSWorld SR from 8.3% to 10.4% to 12.3%. Baselines include GPT-4o, UI-TARS-7B/72B, OS-Atlas-7B, Aguvis-7B, Qwen3-VL-2B/8B/32B, and GUI-Owl-7B — these are current, strong baselines. Scaling experiment (0 to 200B tokens) shows consistent improvement with no saturation, with the Stage1+Stage2 model exceeding Stage2-only at ~50B tokens. Ablation (Table 4) confirms all three pretraining objectives contribute; Ltraj removal is most damaging to online tasks (31.9→24.1 on AndroidWorld); Lground removal most damaging to grounding benchmarks (56.9→49.8 on ScreenSpot-Pro). Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The primary contributions are the WildGUI dataset (genuinely novel in scale and coverage) and the Video2GUI pipeline (novel in its combination of lightweight proxy models, frontier model annotation, and multi-frame grounding at internet scale). The two-stage training recipe is a straightforward application of established pretraining + fine-tuning practice, not a methodological advance. The coarse-to-fine filtering architecture is novel in this domain. There is no new model architecture, loss function, or learning algorithm. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough and cleanly isolate loss components and training stages. No error bars or significance tests are reported on benchmark numbers, consistent with norms in this subfield but still a weakness given the small margin on some comparisons. Data quality is validated at three checkpoints: video scorer test set (R²=0.845–0.896, MAE<0.37 across dimensions), trajectory quality user study (n=300 samples, 5 expert annotators, Krippendorff's α=0.84, WildGUI scores 4.62 vs. TongUI 3.35 and VideoAgentTrek 4.05), and spatial grounding manual check (200 samples, >95% accuracy). Dataset and pipeline are announced to be released (project page exists); model weights are not explicitly mentioned. Methodological concern: the video quality scorer and trajectory extractor both use Gemini-3-Pro as the annotation oracle, so the quality ceiling of WildGUI is fundamentally limited by Gemini-3-Pro's judgment on a 200-hour training subset. Any systematic bias in Gemini-3-Pro's grounding behavior (e.g., preferred coordinate conventions, action granularity) will be inherited by downstream models. Additionally, the 200-sample grounding check validates pixel-level precision but does not validate whether the extracted action sequences are semantically correct representations of the video content. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (pipeline for data construction + trained models evaluated on benchmarks and live environments). - Practical: The trained 7B models run on standard multi-GPU hardware; Stage 1 pretraining required 256 NVIDIA GPUs but is a one-time cost for users who consume WildGUI directly. The pipeline itself requires Gemini-3-Pro API access (~$0.0763/sample, implying ~$970K total for WildGUI construction), which is accessible but non-trivial to reproduce at full scale. - Efficient: Stage 1 pretraining covers ~200B tokens across 24,000 steps on 256 GPUs; this is substantial but standard for 7B-scale pretraining. Inference with the trained models is no more expensive than the base VLMs they extend. - Effective: Yes — gains are consistent across 6 independent benchmarks spanning grounding, offline, and online evaluation tasks, and the offline-to-online transfer (AndroidWorld 31.9% vs. 16.4% base) demonstrates genuine generalization beyond the training distribution. - Scalable: The scaling curve shows no saturation at 200B tokens, suggesting room for continued improvement. However, no experiments test models larger than 7B, and the pipeline is bounded by Gemini-3-Pro API economics at larger video corpus scales. The claim of continued improvement with more data rests on a single model family at a single scale. The work delivers: (1) WildGUI dataset, (2) Video2GUI pipeline (announced for release), (3) trained model checkpoints (Mimo-VL-7B+WildGUI and Qwen2.5-VL-7B+WildGUI). It stops at paper form for the pipeline code; availability is announced but not confirmed at submission. Strengths - Scale genuinely discontinuous from prior work: 12.7M trajectories across 1,500+ apps dwarfs the next-largest open-source dataset by 12×, and the cross-platform coverage (Windows 65.8%, Mac 13.1%, Android 12.7%, iOS 4.5%, Linux 3.9%) is the most comprehensive of any existing resource. - Quality validation is multi-stage and quantitative at each funnel step, not just an end-to-end claim — the video scorer test set metrics (R²≥0.845), inter-rater agreement (α=0.84), and grounding precision (>95%) are independently verifiable. - Cross-architecture robustness: improvements hold across both Qwen2.5-VL and Mimo-VL, confirming the dataset's value is not specific to one model family. - The offline-to-online transfer finding — that models pretrained on static tutorial video data show large gains on live interactive benchmarks (OSWorld, AndroidWorld) — is the most scientifically interesting result, as it implies tutorial videos encode generalizable GUI interaction priors rather than mere pattern memorization. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors do not acknowledge limitations in the body of the paper. The Impact Statement explicitly states "none of which we feel must be specifically highlighted here." No discussion of potential failure modes, data biases, pipeline error rates, cost barriers, or limitations of the Gemini-3-Pro oracle appears in the paper. This is a credibility negative; the absence of any author-acknowledged limitations suggests the limitations section was deliberately omitted rather than reflecting a genuinely complete system. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 200-sample grounding check tests spatial coordinate accuracy but not semantic correctness of the full trajectory — there is no estimate of the rate at which Gemini-3-Pro generates plausible but factually incorrect action sequences (wrong timestamps, hallucinated steps, incorrect task segmentation). At 12.7M trajectories, even a 5% semantic error rate would introduce 635K corrupted training examples. - No ablation on data diversity vs. volume: would 1M carefully curated diverse trajectories match or exceed 12.7M YouTube-sourced ones at the same training compute budget? - Scaling tested only for 7B models; whether the WildGUI pretraining benefit persists at 13B, 32B, or 70B is not evaluated, limiting claims of general scalability. - Windows accounts for 65.8% of platform coverage and Internet/Communication for 43.4% of software categories — if evaluation benchmarks share this distribution skew, the benchmark numbers may overstate generalization to underrepresented domains (e.g., industrial/professional Linux desktop applications). - Total annotation cost (~$970K) is reported but not compared to the cost of producing a comparable manually-annotated dataset, leaving the cost-quality frontier unquantified. - The post-training datasets (Rico, SeeClickWeb, OS-Atlas, AITW, AITZ, AndroidControl, AMEX, GUI-Odyssey) overlap with the evaluation benchmarks' source distributions; this is not necessarily Goodhart's Law violation but the degree of test-train overlap is not analyzed. Bottom Line WildGUI and Video2GUI are a genuine infrastructure contribution to the GUI agent field — practitioners building or pretraining GUI agents should read this paper and use the dataset, as it currently represents the most scalable path to broad GUI interaction coverage without manual annotation.
Recommended 2605.14747 PDF
#42 — Innovative (71)

OProver: A Unified Framework for Agentic Formal Theorem Proving (28 ▲ )

David Ma, Kaijing Ma, Shawn Guo, Yunfeng Shi, Enduo Zhao, Jiajun Shi, Zhaoxiang Zhang, Gavin Cheung, Jiaheng Liu, Zili Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OProver, a unified framework for agentic formal theorem proving in Lean 4 that trains a policy to iteratively revise failed proof attempts using retrieved compiler-verified proofs and raw Lean 4 compiler diagnostics, paired with OProofs, a corpus of 1.77M Lean statements, 6.86M compiler-verified proofs, and 1.07M serialized multi-round agentic proving trajectories. - Why it's important: Prior provers treat retrieval and compiler feedback as inference-time heuristics grafted onto a fixed model trained only on finalized proofs, creating a train–inference mismatch that leaves the richest source of supervision—failure traces—unexploited. - How it does it: The policy conditions on a compact state (theorem statement, top-k retrieved proofs, most-recent failed attempt, raw compiler diagnostics) and is trained end-to-end through continued pretraining followed by an iterative loop alternating SFT on round-level repair examples and GSPO reinforcement learning on hard unsolved cases, with newly verified proofs and repair trajectories recirculated into the corpus and retrieval index each round. - The good: OProver-32B, a 32B dense model, achieves Pass@32 of 93.3% on MiniF2F, 58.2% on ProverBench, and 11.3% on PutnamBench—outperforming or matching LongCat-Flash-Prover (560B MoE, ~17× total parameters) on three of five benchmarks, with ablations confirming that iterative compiler feedback contributes the dominant performance share (e.g., removing it drops MathOlympiad from 22.8 to 16.5 for the 32B model). - The missing: The ablation table never isolates retrieval augmentation alone (with feedback intact), so the independent contribution of RAG cannot be measured against the full system—only its marginal value on top of a feedback-free baseline is reported. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners working on LLM-based formal verification or agentic post-training pipelines; the co-evolution loop and compact-state formulation are clearly described and the corpus release (OProofs) provides a concrete artifact, but readers primarily interested in tactic-level or tree-search provers will find limited relevance.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. The strongest paper in the cluster: Novelty (76) and Impact (82) lead — the co-evolution loop trains feedback-conditioned repair as a learned policy (not an inference-time wrapper), OProofs is the first Lean corpus with all six supervision dimensions, and the artifact (corpus + 8B/32B weights + Lean server) is substantial. A 32B dense model matching or beating a 560B MoE and a 671B prover on three of five benchmarks, with ablations showing iterative compiler feedback is the dominant contributor, gives strong Rigor (68) and Resilience (66). It falls just short of the Highly-Innovative band because it is an unreviewed preprint with no error bars, baselines lifted from published reports rather than re-run under its protocol, no RAG-only ablation (so retrieval's independent value is unmeasured), and no Pass@1 numbers.
empirical Novelty 76 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 66 Resilience 66 Impact 82
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Formal theorem proving in Lean 4 requires every proof step to pass mechanical kernel verification, creating a dense, semantically rich feedback signal at each failure. Despite this, most neural provers—including recent high-performing systems like Goedel-Prover-V2 and DeepSeek-Prover-V2—are trained exclusively on finalized compiler-verified proofs. When retrieval or compiler feedback is incorporated at all, it appears only at inference time as an external wrapper, not as part of the learned policy. This train–inference mismatch means the model is never optimized to interpret diagnostics or leverage retrieved lemma patterns. Additionally, existing Lean corpora (NuminaMath-LEAN, Lean-Workbook, Goedel-Pset, etc.) record only the final verified proof, discarding the failed attempts, retrieved context, and compiler messages that actually drove proof construction—the supervision needed to learn agentic self-correction. Key Idea: The core insight is that agentic theorem proving should be formulated as a bounded multi-round Markov decision process with a compact state X_t = (s, R_t, p_{t-1}, f_{t-1}), where s is the theorem statement, R_t is the top-k retrieved compiler-verified proofs, p_{t-1} is the previous proof attempt, and f_{t-1} is the raw Lean 4 compiler diagnostic—and that this exact state formulation should be used identically during rollout collection, SFT, and RL training so the training-time interface matches proving-time interaction perfectly. Crucially, rather than projecting compiler output into a hand-designed error taxonomy, OProver passes raw textual diagnostics directly to the policy, preserving fine-grained type-mismatch, goal, and identifier information that categorical encoding would lose. Methodology: OProver has three interlocking components. (1) OProofs construction proceeds via two branches: public Lean resources (NuminaMath-LEAN, Lean-Workbook, etc.) deduplicated and then agentically proved using existing open-source provers; and raw informal math statements mined from Common Crawl and GitHub via a FastText classifier, autoformalized into Lean 4 using CriticLean, then proved. Both branches retain only Lean-verified proofs. (2) Agentic proving at inference time runs up to R refinement rounds; on failure, raw compiler feedback f_{t-1} is fed back to the policy alongside the top-k retrieved proofs from retrieval memory M (using Qwen3-8B-Embedding, selected over Goedel-Prover-V2-8B via 66.7% GPT-4o judge preference on 1,000 retrieval queries). Successful proofs are immediately indexed back into M. (3) Training proceeds in two phases: a one-time continued pretraining on a 65B-token mixture (30% Lean/OProofs, 40% Nemotron-Math-4-Plus, 20% OpenCoder, 10% ProLong-64K) yielding OProver-Base; then iterative post-training alternating SFT (cross-entropy on target proof p_t only, sequence length 40,960) and GSPO RL (reward r_t = 0.8 + 0.2·1[format correct] if Lean-verified, else 0.0; advantages normalized group-relative across all n×R rounds per theorem; n=8 rollouts, R=4 for 8B, R=2 for 32B). DAPO-style group filtering excludes fully-solved and fully-failed groups. Each iteration re-indexes newly verified proofs into M and adds repair trajectories to the SFT pool. Key design decisions: compact state (only most-recent attempt, not full history) is justified by keeping context manageable while preserving the locally-relevant correction signal; raw compiler feedback rather than taxonomy encoding preserves fine-grained repair information; round-level decomposition of trajectories provides process-level supervision without consuming full interaction histories. Key Results: OProver-32B achieves Pass@32 (n=64) of 93.3% on MiniF2F (vs. 90.4% Goedel-Prover-V2-32B with self-correction, 90.2% LongCat-Flash-Prover with TIR), 58.2% on ProverBench (vs. 57.9% LongCat), and 11.3% on PutnamBench (vs. 10.4% LongCat), and ranks second on MathOlympiadBench (22.8% vs. 27.5% LongCat TIR) and ProofNet (33.2% vs. 36.1% LongCat TIR). Even OProver-8B exceeds Goedel-Prover-V2-32B across all five benchmarks. OProver-32B surpasses DeepSeek-Prover-V2-671B on all five benchmarks despite 21× fewer parameters. Ablation (Table 3): removing iterative feedback (keeping only round 1) drops OProver-32B by 4.9 points on MiniF2F, 6.3 on MathOlympiad, 7.4 on ProofNet, 6.2 on ProverBench, and 4.3 on PutnamBench—the dominant contribution. Further removing retrieval adds 0.5–1.7 point additional drops. Iterative post-training (Figure 6): OProver-8B improves 12.3 points (79.5→91.8) over 3 post-training rounds on MiniF2F; OProver-32B improves 8.6 points (84.7→93.3) over 2 rounds, with no saturation observed. Test-time scaling (Figure 4): BestPass(B) improves consistently from B=8 to B=256 across all benchmarks, with optimal refinement depth R=16 on easier benchmarks and R=8 on PutnamBench (where low per-chain success probability penalizes narrow sampling). Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW METHOD. The genuinely novel contribution is the unified co-evolution loop: training agentic feedback-conditioned repair as a learned policy (not inference-time wrapper) and coupling it with a corpus that continuously grows from the prover's own repair traces. The compact-state formulation and GSPO pooling across n×R rounds are concrete technical choices not seen in prior provers. OProofs is the first public Lean corpus to simultaneously include retrieved proof context, compiler feedback, and multi-round repair supervision (Table 1 shows all prior datasets lack at least one of these). The broader approach—iterative self-improvement via verified proofs and failure traces—is related to STP and Seed-Prover, but those systems iterate on statement–proof pairs without serialized multi-turn trajectories. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations in Table 3 cover the two core components (feedback and retrieval) across both model sizes and all five benchmarks—this is thorough by community standards. Figure 6 tracks all intermediate checkpoints across post-training iterations, establishing monotonic improvement. No error bars or significance tests are reported (standard in this community given the computational cost). Code and model weights are publicly released at github.com/multimodal-art-projection/OProver and huggingface.co/m-a-p/oprover. The retriever comparison uses GPT-4o as a pairwise judge over 1,000 queries—an appropriate but not infallible proxy. Autoformalization quality for Branch 2 (informal→Lean) is not independently validated; the corpus trusts CriticLean's output after compiler verification, which filters syntactic correctness but not semantic faithfulness. The baselines marked with † are taken from published reports rather than re-evaluated under OProver's protocol, which may conflate differences in evaluation setup. This is a preprint; results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: OProver-8B and -32B are dense models deployable on commodity GPU clusters; the Lean 4 verification server runs locally with a 240-second timeout per request, which is within reach of standard academic hardware. - Efficient: Multi-round inference at up to R=16 rounds multiplies generation cost by up to 16× over single-pass provers; at the optimal budget B=256 with R=8, total compute per theorem is substantial but bounded and comparable to best-of-N sampling used by competing systems. - Effective: Ablations confirm the method solves the stated problem (agentic repair of failed proofs), with compiler feedback providing 4.3–7.4 point Pass@32 gains over single-round generation across benchmarks. - Scalable: Test-time scaling shows consistent improvement from B=8 to B=256, with diminishing returns; the co-evolution loop has been run for 2–3 iterations and shows no saturation, but whether additional iterations continue yielding gains at comparable cost is untested. The paper delivers trained model weights and a software system (Lean server + retrieval pipeline), not just a paper-form result. Strengths: - The co-evolution loop design is well-motivated and concretely specified (Algorithm 1): each component (corpus, retrieval memory, SFT data, RL hard cases) maps to a specific routing rule, making the system reproducible. - OProofs fills a genuine gap: it is the only public Lean corpus providing all six supervision dimensions (formal statements, verified proofs, NL rationale, compiler feedback, retrieved proof context, multi-round repair) simultaneously. - The compact-state formulation (condition only on most-recent attempt + feedback, not full history) is a principled design choice that aligns training and inference exactly, avoiding the common failure where a model trained on finalized proofs encounters out-of-distribution multi-turn inputs at deployment. - The test-time compute analysis (§4.2) is unusually rigorous for the field: fixing total budget B and sweeping (R, k) allocation reveals the benchmark-dependent optimal depth, providing actionable guidance for practitioners. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors do not explicitly enumerate limitations in a dedicated section. The paper acknowledges that on PutnamBench, all methods—including OProver-32B—remain below 12% Pass@32, and that BestPass shows diminishing returns with budget. The paper notes that OProofs statistics cover only the Lean 4 theorem-proving component; auxiliary pretraining corpora are excluded from reported counts. These are factual scope statements rather than critical limitations directly confronted. The lack of an explicit limitations section is a mild credibility concern. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The ablation table (Table 3) removes feedback first, then both feedback and RAG—but never tests RAG-only removal (with feedback intact). The marginal contribution of retrieval is thus measured only on top of an already-degraded no-feedback baseline, potentially understating retrieval's independent value. - Baseline comparisons are not apples-to-apples: OProver uses multi-round rollouts (each sample = a complete trajectory of up to R rounds) while baselines use whole-proof single-pass sampling or best-of-N. Section 4.2 provides a budget-controlled comparison, but the baselines' own BestPass curves are not shown—only OProver's scaling is characterized. - The autoformalization quality of Branch 2 (Common Crawl + GitHub → Lean 4 via CriticLean) is validated only by Lean compiler acceptance, not by semantic faithfulness to the original informal claim. Semantically incorrect but syntactically valid formalizations could degrade proof quality silently. - LLM-as-judge (GPT-4o) for retriever selection is a proxy metric; direct task performance (Pass@k with and without each retriever) would be a stronger validation. - No Pass@1 numbers are reported anywhere; the paper reports exclusively Pass@32, which masks the practical single-attempt performance relevant to many real use cases. - The GSPO hyperparameter choice (n=8 rollouts, R=4 for 8B, R=2 for 32B during RL) is stated but not ablated, so the sensitivity of RL gains to these settings is unknown. Bottom Line: This is a technically solid and well-executed system paper: the co-evolution design is coherent, the OProofs corpus fills a documented gap, the ablations are honest, and the SOTA results on three of five benchmarks at 32B dense scale are genuine—but practitioners should note it is an unreviewed preprint, the multi-round inference cost is non-trivial relative to single-pass baselines, and the ablation design leaves retrieval's independent contribution unclear.
Recommended 2605.17283 PDF
#43 — Innovative (71)

TabPFN-3: Technical Report

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TabPFN-3, a tabular foundation model that scales in-context learning to datasets with 1M training rows while simultaneously improving predictive performance, speed, and coverage (many-class, temporal, relational, and text-tabular settings). - Why it's important: Tabular data drives most high-value prediction in science and industry, and prior tabular foundation models topped out at ~100K rows and failed on many-class tasks; closing those gaps without sacrificing speed enables production deployment where AutoML ensembles were previously the only option. - How it does it: A three-stage architecture (column-wise inducing-point embedding → row-wise CLS-token aggregation → ICL transformer with multi-query cross-attention) decouples memory cost from rows×features, enabling row-chunked inference at 1M rows on a single H100 with a ~7 GB KV cache per estimator. - The good: TabPFN-3's default single-forward-pass achieves 1,677 Elo on TabArena (816 tasks, 51 datasets), outperforming all tuned and ensembled baselines, with statistical significance (Wilcoxon p<0.0001 over TabPFN-2.5 on per-dataset scores); and it achieves normalized ROC-AUC of 1.00 on a 100-class synthetic benchmark, where all GBT baselines fail or degrade sharply. - The missing: The "Thinking mode" mechanism — which accounts for the most dramatic gains (+200-420 Elo) — is entirely undescribed; no architecture, algorithm, or ablation is provided, making those results irreproducible and unverifiable. - Read it or not: Essential reading for practitioners working on tabular prediction at scale and for researchers building on the TabPFN ecosystem; the open-source model (weights + code) is immediately usable, though the Thinking-mode claims should be treated as vendor-reported until independently verified.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (90) dominates and is the highest in the cluster: open-source weights and library, an API, AWS/Azure enterprise deployments, and validated 1M-row inference on a single H100 — a genuine production alternative to AutoML. Novelty (70) credits the three-stage compression architecture and non-parametric many-class decoder, though it builds on TabICLv2. Rigor (60) is held down because the headline +200-420 Elo Thinking-mode gains rest on an entirely undescribed proprietary mechanism and the 100K-1M-row claims use an author-curated internal benchmark, and it is an un-peer-reviewed technical report. Non-Obviousness (70) reflects the row-dimension-only scaling insight. Completeness (55) is the main drag — the most impressive results have no disclosed algorithm or ablation. Resilience (60): the open-source default results would survive review, but the best numbers collapse under scrutiny as vendor self-reports. Weighted: 0.15*70 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*70 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*60 + 0.30*90 = 71.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 55 Resilience 60 Impact 90
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Tabular data underlies clinical risk scoring, credit decisioning, predictive maintenance, and scientific measurement, yet prior tabular foundation models had hard ceilings: TabPFN v2 topped at 10K rows, TabPFN-2.5 at 100K rows with up to 2K features. Beyond row count, those models failed on many-class classification (>10 classes), could not handle text-valued columns natively, and had KV caches prohibitively large for online production inference. AutoML systems like AutoGluon remained the practical choice for larger or more diverse datasets at the cost of multi-hour tuning budgets. TabPFN-3 targets all of these gaps simultaneously. Key Idea: The core insight is that a tabular in-context learner does not need to carry per-cell representations through the full depth of a transformer — it can compress each column into a fixed-size inducing-point summary (128 points) and each row into a fixed-dimensional CLS embedding (4 × 128d), after which the ICL attention operates only over rows. This row-dimension-only scaling enables (a) row-chunked inference that keeps peak activation memory constant in row count, and (b) multi-query attention (1 KV head for test→train cross-attention vs 8 for train↔train) that shrinks the KV cache ~8×, together enabling 1M-row inference on commodity hardware. Methodology: TabPFN-3 uses a three-stage pipeline. Stage 1 (feature distribution embedding) processes each column independently with a 3-block transformer using 128 inducing points via cross-attention, compressing N rows of a column into 128 vectors — cost is O(N·K) not O(N²). Stage 2 (feature aggregation) produces a single 4·128=512-dimensional row embedding by having 4 learned CLS tokens attend to the grouped feature embeddings (triplet groups of 3 cyclically-shifted features). Stage 3 (ICL transformer, 24 blocks) performs full multi-head self-attention among train rows and multi-query cross-attention (1 KV head) from test to train. An attention-based retrieval decoder replaces the fixed MLP head: for classification it computes a head-averaged attention-weighted average of one-hot label vectors, making it non-parametric in class count (up to 160, set at pre-training). Training uses an improved SCM prior with 8 enhancements over v2.5: richer graph sampling, new combiner mechanisms, reworked categorical variables, high-frequency sinusoidal activations, spatial priors, many-class priors, temporal/dynamic-SCM priors, and OOD extrapolation tasks. The model was trained on >8 trillion synthetic tokens. Inference uses row-chunking (activated when n_train + n_test > 2048) and optional KV caching; FA3 and torch.compile provide additional 1.5–1.7× speedup on H100/MI-250x. The preprocessing ensemble aggregates 8 estimators with distinct permutations, feature transformations, and for >100K rows, Gini-importance-guided feature selection. TabPFN-3-Plus (API/enterprise only) adds native text encoding. "Thinking mode" applies additional test-time compute on top of TabPFN-3-Plus; its mechanism is not disclosed. Key Results: On TabArena (51 datasets, 816 tasks, up to 100K rows): TabPFN-3 default achieves 1,677 Elo, +72 Elo over RealTabPFN-2.5 tuned+ensembled and +54 Elo over TabICLv2 default; it has >80% win rate against tuned+ensembled CatBoost/LightGBM/XGBoost and 62% against 4h AutoGluon 1.5 extreme. TabPFN-3-Plus (Thinking) reaches 1,800 Elo, +100 Elo over AutoGluon 1.5 extreme, while being 10× faster. On the 15 largest TabArena datasets (10k–100k rows), Thinking mode reaches 2,146 Elo vs 1,907 for AutoGluon extreme and 1,835 for TabPFN-3 default, with >99% win rate over tuned/ensembled LightGBM/XGBoost. On TALENT (274 datasets spanning regression, binary, multiclass): mean rank 3.75, first overall and per task type. On the authors' large-data internal benchmark (100K–1M rows, 13 datasets): TabPFN-3 outperforms 8h-tuned GBT baselines in a single forward pass (average ranks 2.67 classification, 2.25 regression; CD diagrams show significance over all defaults and tuned LightGBM for classification). On the 100-class synthetic many-class benchmark: normalized ROC-AUC 1.00 vs TabICLv2 at 0.89 and TabPFN-2.5 at 0.83. On fev-bench time series (100 tasks): TabPFN-TS-3 ranks 2nd in SQL skill (43.1%) and MASE skill (30.6%), behind only Chronos-2 (47.3%/35.5%), trained purely on synthetic data vs real-data-pretrained competitors. On RelBenchV1: first among relational foundation models on both entity classification (76.91 avg AUC, rank 4.29) and regression (0.864 normalized MAE, rank 3.56), second overall to supervised RelGNN. On TabSTAR (50 text-tabular datasets): first overall with Thinking mode. Inference: 20× faster than TabPFN-2.5 on large datasets; KV-cached predict is 1–2 orders of magnitude faster than cold fit+predict (22 ms vs 342 ms for n_train=50K, n_features=10); SHAP via KV cache is up to 120× faster. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING, ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. The three-stage compression architecture adapting TabICLv2's design to enable 1M-row ICL is genuinely novel in the tabular foundation model space. The attention-based many-class decoder is a clean non-parametric extension. The row-chunking scheme is a sound engineering contribution (two-phase precomputation of inducing states). The improved SCM prior with temporal, spatial, and OOD extensions adds meaningful breadth. What is largely standard: the multi-query attention reduction, FlashAttention integration, and the use of synthetic-data pretraining for ICL. Rigor & Reproducibility: TabArena evaluation reuses the official TabArena code and pipeline; the authors commit to contributing an official submission after public release. The Wilcoxon p<0.0001 significance test over TabPFN-2.5 on per-dataset TabArena scores is appropriate. CD diagrams (Conover-Friedman) are provided for large-data, many-class, and quantile regression benchmarks. Multiple ablations are present for inference optimization components (chunking vs. no chunking, KV cache, FA3, compile). The large-data benchmark is internal, designed by the authors, and not independently curated — this is a substantive concern for the 100K–1M results where authors claim to beat 8h-tuned GBTs. The Thinking mode results are entirely non-reproducible (API-only, mechanism undisclosed). TabPFN-3-Plus (Thinking) results are labeled "Preview" on the large-data classification benchmark. Code and weights for the open-source TabPFN-3 are released via HuggingFace under the TABPFN-3.0 License v1.0 (permissive for research, restricted for commercial production). The report is a preprint (arXiv:2605.13986v1, May 2026), not peer-reviewed. Deployability (SYSTEMS): - Practical: The open-source model runs on a single H100 (or RTX Pro 6000 Blackwell as shown in SHAP experiments) for datasets up to 1M×200; the KV cache requires ~7 GB per estimator at 1M rows, so the default 8-estimator ensemble needs ~56 GB, placing it at the edge of single-GPU viability for the largest scale. Smaller datasets run comfortably on consumer GPUs. - Efficient: The model is 20× faster than TabPFN-2.5 on large datasets and achieves 22 ms cached-predict latency at n_train=50K, n_features=10; the 53–58M parameter count is modest, but the 8-estimator ensemble multiplies cost; training on 8T synthetic tokens is not reproducible by end users. - Effective: Evidence is strong for datasets ≤100K rows (public TabArena, TALENT) and moderately strong for 100K–1M rows (internal benchmark, which also uses TALENT large-data slice as confirmation). The paper is careful to distinguish regimes where TabPFN-3 is not validated. - Scalable: The paper explicitly validates up to 1M rows × 200 features, and separately 1K rows × 20K features; it also acknowledges the trade-off when both rows and features are simultaneously large. Beyond 1M rows is unvalidated. Temporal datasets for regression are not supported by Thinking mode at time of writing. The work delivers open-source model weights (HuggingFace), a Python library (tabpfn), an API, and enterprise deployments on AWS SageMaker and Azure AI Foundry. Strengths: - The three-stage compression plus row-chunking is a technically sound solution to the quadratic memory wall, and the equivalence to unchunked computation is rigorously described. - Benchmark breadth is exceptional: public tabular (TabArena, TALENT), text-tabular (TabSTAR), time series (fev-bench, 100 tasks), relational (RelBenchV1), quantile regression, many-class, large-scale, and high-dimensional slices — all reported with appropriate metrics and significance tests. - The many-class decoder is a principled non-parametric design that correctly avoids fixing class count at model capacity rather than architecture, and orthogonal label embeddings address the gradient flow problem in the many-class regime. - Training on 8 trillion synthetic tokens with a richer SCM prior (spatial, temporal, OOD) represents a qualitative expansion of what the prior can express, and the extrapolation capability (Figure 26) demonstrates a concrete advantage over tree-based methods. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: - The large-row/large-feature regime (simultaneously many rows AND many features) induces a compression bottleneck; the 200-feature-per-estimator cap means the high-dimensional slice is addressed by ensembling rather than architecture. - Thinking mode does not yet support temporal/regression datasets at time of writing. - The architecture cap of C_max=160 classes is fixed at pre-training time. - Authors note that on the RealCause causal benchmark, TabPFN-3 slightly underperforms TabPFN-2.5 (smaller datasets where v2.5's architecture may be better suited). Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The "Thinking mode" — responsible for the most dramatic results (+200–420 Elo) — receives zero technical description in a 24-page paper plus 60-page appendix. This is a significant omission: a vendor claiming headline results via a black-box API cannot be independently verified, and the Thinking-mode results should be treated as vendor self-reports with associated uncertainty. - The large-data internal benchmark (13 datasets, 100K–1M rows) is designed and curated by the authors and has not been independently reviewed. The TALENT large-data slice (14 datasets) provides partial external confirmation, but the overlap in performance is asserted rather than fully reconciled. - TabArena results for TabPFN-3 and Thinking mode were run on a weaker GPU (RTX 6000) vs an H200 for all other foundation models — this actually strengthens the performance claims, but the paper does not explicitly flag this asymmetry, leaving readers to find it in Appendix E.2.2. - The many-class benchmark is entirely synthetic (converted from regression tasks via Dirichlet-jittered quantile binning); the authors acknowledge real-world many-class datasets are scarce, but performance on this benchmark may not transfer to naturally sparse-class distributions. - The open-source model is licensed restrictively (TABPFN-3.0 License v1.0): commercial production use requires an enterprise license, and Thinking mode is entirely API/enterprise-only — the paper presents open-source and closed-source results without always clearly distinguishing them, which may mislead practitioners. Bottom Line: TabPFN-3 is a genuine and substantial advance in tabular foundation modeling — the architecture, benchmarking, and engineering are all of high quality — but the headline Thinking-mode results rest on an undescribed proprietary mechanism, making the most impressive claims unreproducible; practitioners should adopt the open-source default model with confidence and treat Thinking-mode numbers as commercially self-reported.
Search Results 2605.13986 PDF
#44 — Innovative (71)

Different Prompts, Different Ranks: Prompt-aware Dynamic Rank Selection for SVD-based LLM Compression

Hengyi Zhu, Zhendong Mi, Grace Li Zhang, Shaoyi Huang · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PARSE, a post-training framework that trains a linear router to perform prompt-aware dynamic rank selection over SVD-compressed LLM weight matrices, consistently improving quality and inference speed across multiple SVD baselines. - Why it's important: Static rank truncation in SVD compression applies a fixed singular-component prefix to every input regardless of its complexity, causing localized perplexity spikes and cross-domain degradation that aggregate metrics conceal — a real cost for any heterogeneous inference workload. - How it does it: Each SVD-compressed weight is reformulated as a mixture of independent rank experts; a single linear router trained on a large diverse corpus against the original dense model's outputs (not calibration statistics) selects a prompt-specific expert subset, whose identity is cached by prompt embedding and reused across all decoding steps. - The good: PARSE improves average zero-shot accuracy by up to 10 percentage points at compression ratio 0.6 on LLaMA-7B and simultaneously achieves 2.5× prefill and 2.4× decode speedup over native SVD execution — gains that hold consistently across four different SVD baselines. - The missing: Evaluation is limited to decoder-only models and standard language modeling / zero-shot reasoning benchmarks; no instruction-tuned models, multimodal architectures, or 70B+ scale are tested, leaving generalization to production workloads unconfirmed. - Read it or not: ML systems researchers and practitioners already using SVD-based compression should read this — the diagnostic analysis is unusually rigorous and the plug-in design means adoption does not require replacing an existing pipeline.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile (the core contribution is a method evaluated on perplexity/zero-shot benchmarks, with systems work secondary). Non-Obviousness (78) is the top driver: the empirical discovery that optimal rank subsets are fixed by first-layer semantics and remain stable across 60+ decoding steps is genuinely surprising and is what makes the caching strategy viable. Novelty (72) is solid — MoE-style routing over SVD rank experts is a new, non-obvious formulation, though it is a new method rather than a new problem class. What pulled the composite below 75 is Rigor (68) and Impact (68): all numbers appear single-run with no error bars, no public code is released, and there is no comparison against INT4/GPTQ quantization, a strong competitor in the same deployment niche, leaving a real opening for an adversarial reviewer. Composite ≈ 0.20·72 + 0.25·68 + 0.15·78 + 0.15·72 + 0.15·70 + 0.10·68 = 71. Methodologically rigorous and well-motivated, resembling the positive calibration set.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 72 Resilience 70 Impact 68
Show full analysis
