#57 — Innovative (68)

Sloth: scaling laws for llm skills to predict multi-benchmark performance across families

proceedings.neurips.cc · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Sloth (Skills Scaling Laws), a scaling law framework that models LLM benchmark performance across multiple families as a function of low-dimensional latent skills (Reasoning, Knowledge, Instruction Following) whose acquisition is governed by translog production functions of model size and training tokens. - Why it's important: Existing scaling laws either fail to generalize across LLM families with different training configurations or require training multiple models per family, making them expensive and impractical for predicting the performance of large, unobserved models before committing training resources. - How it does it: Benchmark scores are modeled as linear combinations of d latent skill vectors (Λθ + b) where each skill follows a translog function of log-size and log-tokens with shared slopes across families but family-specific intercepts, fitted jointly on publicly available leaderboard data via Huber loss minimization. - The good: On Open LLM Leaderboard v2 with one observed family model at test time, Sloth (d=2) achieves average MAE of 2.9 percentage points versus 4.1 for the Size-and-Tokens baseline and 4.9 for FLOPs — a 30–40% error reduction — while using fewer parameters than competing baselines when more than four families are present. - The missing: The model still requires at least one observed model from the test family at training time in the primary experimental setting; true zero-shot cross-family generalization is only partially explored via the shared-intercept variant, and no evaluation covers closed-weight frontier models (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini). - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML practitioners interested in benchmark predictability, LLM evaluation infrastructure, or compute-optimal training decisions — the interpretability findings on instruction tuning and reasoning are a practical bonus; those seeking training-time scaling laws (loss vs. compute) can skip it.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. The composite is dragged down by Rigor (65) and Resilience (63): no error bars, confidence intervals, or initialization-sensitivity analysis accompany the MAE tables, so the headline edge over the Size-and-Tokens baseline (e.g., 4.3 vs 4.4 on v1) may not be statistically distinguishable, and the unacknowledged benchmark-contamination propagating from leaderboard fits weakens the central predictive claim. Novelty (72) is real — the latent-skills factor model with translog production functions plus a formal identifiability proof is more than engineering — but the downstream-task evidence rests on one or two held-out points. Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*67 + 0.15*63 + 0.10*68 = 68.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 67 Resilience 63 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Large language model practitioners need to predict the benchmark performance of large, untrained models given limited observations from smaller models of the same family. The core difficulty is that models from different families trained on different data with different recipes exhibit large benchmark score differences even at matched compute budgets, so a single universal scaling law overfits poorly across families, while family-specific scaling laws require training multiple models at varying sizes for every new model family — expensive before deployment decisions are made. Prior work (Owen 2024; Ruan et al. 2024) either discards family information entirely (worse accuracy) or assumes the large model is already trained (not useful for pre-training decisions). The paper targets the setting where a practitioner observes only the smallest model(s) of a new family and wants to extrapolate performance of 10×–100× larger models across many benchmarks simultaneously. Key Idea The central insight is that benchmark scores across the 12 Open LLM Leaderboard tasks are not 12 independent functions of compute — they share a low-dimensional latent structure (d = 2–4 skills). Rather than modeling each benchmark's scaling law independently, Sloth models that latent structure via a factor-analysis-like decomposition: each benchmark's score is a linear combination of the same d skill dimensions, and each skill dimension follows its own translog production function of log(parameters) and log(tokens) with slopes shared across families (the universal part) but family-specific intercepts (the family-specific efficiency). This shared-slope / family-specific-intercept design simultaneously achieves parameter efficiency, cross-family information sharing, and interpretable skill structure. Methodology The generative model has three stages. First, a translog production function maps compute inputs to skill levels: θ_ik(s,t) = α_ik + β_k^T [log(s), log(t), log(s)log(t)] for each family i and skill k, where α_ik is a family-specific intercept and β_k is a shared slope vector across families. The interaction term log(s)log(t) captures the fact that the marginal return to scale may depend on the number of training tokens. Second, benchmark scores are generated as η_i(s,t) = Λθ_i(s,t) + b, where Λ ∈ R^{J×d} is a shared loading matrix (J=12 benchmarks, d≪J) and b ∈ R^J is a bias. Third, a per-benchmark monotone link function σ_j maps η_ij to the [0,1] score range; in the "basic" variant σ_j is fixed as sigmoid with γ_j set to the multiple-choice random-guess asymptote, while in the full variant σ_j is a two-hidden-layer monotone neural network (non-negative weights, sigmoid output). Model fitting minimizes the summed Huber loss (δ=0.01) across all families, models, and benchmarks jointly via Adam with decaying learning rate. Since the optimization is non-convex (as with all exploratory factor analysis), multiple initializations are recommended. Post-fitting, factor rotation (Geomin oblique method via FactorAnalyzer) is applied to make Λ sparse and interpretable without changing model outputs. The paper proves identifiability of (Λ, B) up to an invertible rotation M under standard factor-analysis conditions: zero-mean, unit-covariance skills, rank(Λ)=d, rank(X)=p (Theorem A.2). Critical design decisions: (1) shared β_k across families — well-justified as the inductive bias enabling cross-family transfer; (2) family-specific α_ik to absorb data quality, post-training, and recipe differences — essential and empirically validated; (3) trainable σ_j — improves prediction accuracy but sacrifices extrapolation guarantees. Key Results Evaluation uses leave-one-out cross-validation at the family level across 30 LLM families (Bloom, Falcon, Gemma, LLaMA, OPT, Pythia, Qwen, Yi, StarCoder, and many others) on 12 benchmarks (GSM8K, MMLU, MMLU-PRO, MATH Lv5, BBH, GPQA, MUSR, TruthfulQA, HellaSwag, Winogrande, ARC, IFEval). In the one-observation-per-family setting: Sloth (d=2) achieves average MAE 4.3 on Leaderboard v1 versus 4.4 for Size-and-Tokens and 4.9 for FLOPs; on Leaderboard v2, Sloth (d=2) achieves 2.9 versus 4.1 and 3.8, respectively. With two observations per family Sloth (d=4) achieves average MAE 4.3 (v1) and 3.2 (v2). Sloth consistently matches or beats the "Size and Tokens" variant — importantly, the paper interprets this as evidence that the latent skill structure is a valid inductive bias. In the shared-intercept (family-agnostic) setting on five benchmarks, Sloth (d=3) achieves average MAE 2.2–2.5 versus 4.8 for the FLOPs shared-intercept baseline. For downstream task prediction: Sloth-predicted skills for meta-llama-3-70B accurately place the model on the HumanEval logistic curve fitted from other models; the held-out model falls near the training curve in both coding and emotional intelligence tasks. For test-time scaling: Sloth combined with Item Response Theory correctly predicts pass@k curves for LLaMA 3 Instruct, Gemma, and Pythia families on MATH as k varies from 1 to 10,000. A compute-optimal analysis yields closed-form quadratic maximization showing, e.g., at 19,360×10^19 FLOPs the optimal allocation for Reasoning is 72B parameters / 2.59T tokens. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The genuinely novel contribution is the formulation of LLM benchmark scaling as a latent-skills factor model with translog production functions for skill acquisition — specifically the identification that benchmark correlations can be exploited to share statistical strength across benchmarks and families simultaneously. The translog production function and factor rotation are borrowed from econometrics and psychometrics respectively, and the IRT combination for test-time scaling follows prior work directly; what is new is their synthesis into a coherent multi-family, multi-benchmark predictive framework fitted from leaderboard data without training new models. Rigor & Reproducibility Code is publicly available at https://github.com/felipemaiapolo/sloth. The identifiability theorem (Theorem A.2) is stated and proved in Appendix A with explicit assumptions. Leave-one-out cross-validation is the evaluation protocol, which is a credible choice that avoids leakage. Both MAE and MAPE metrics are reported (Figures 1 and 10), and results are qualitatively consistent between them. No error bars or confidence intervals are reported for the MAE tables — results are point estimates only, and it is not clear how sensitive they are to the random initialization of the optimizer. The non-convex optimization is noted as a limitation but no sensitivity analysis to initialization is shown. The model is fitted on leaderboard data (not independently collected benchmarks), so benchmark contamination in the underlying LLMs propagates into the scaling law fits — this is unacknowledged. The downstream task prediction experiments involve very few held-out observations (meta-llama-3-70B base and instruct only), making those results more illustrative than statistically conclusive. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (fits a statistical model from existing data; inference runs at prediction time). - Practical: Fits and runs in seconds on a laptop; no GPU required; uses only publicly available benchmark data from Open LLM Leaderboard. - Efficient: Far cheaper than alternatives that require training multiple models per family; d=2–4 latent dimensions yield parameter counts of ~69+3f (versus 36+12f for FLOPs baselines) — parameter-efficient for f≥4 families. - Effective: Predicts held-out family benchmark scores with 2.9–4.3 percentage-point MAE; extends to downstream tasks (coding, emotional intelligence) and test-time scaling with qualitatively accurate predictions. Evidence is most convincing for standard leaderboard benchmarks; downstream task results rest on very few test points. - Scalable: Not stress-tested — the dataset spans models up to ~70B parameters and the LLM families on the Open LLM Leaderboard; no evaluation covers >100B parameter models, frontier closed-weight APIs, or the latest instruction-tuned models (GPT-4, Claude 3, Gemini). Extrapolation accuracy at those scales is an open question. The work delivers a software artifact (GitHub repo) alongside a statistical modeling framework. Strengths - The shared-slope / family-specific-intercept decomposition is an elegant and principled inductive bias that simultaneously explains cross-family variation and enables cross-family transfer; the parameter count advantage over baselines (for f≥4) is concrete and non-trivial. - Interpretable skill discovery is robust across d=2,3,4 — Reasoning consistently loads on math/logic benchmarks, Knowledge on common-sense benchmarks, Instruction Following on IFEval — suggesting the latent structure is meaningful rather than an artifact of one particular rotation. - The compute-optimal skill allocation analysis (Section 4.6, Appendix B) is a practically useful application that existing scaling law literature has only performed for aggregate loss, not per-skill benchmarks; the closed-form quadratic solution is clean. - The formal identifiability proof (Theorem A.2) is a credibility anchor that distinguishes this from purely empirical factor analyses in the LLM literature. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) the model usually requires at least one observed model from the test family, limiting truly zero-shot generalization; (2) the trainable link function σ_j has no extrapolation guarantee when the test family lies far from training data in skill space; (3) identification is understood only for the basic (fixed σ) variant, limiting interpretability of the full neural-link version. These limitations are stated directly in the Conclusion, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No error bars or significance tests accompany the MAE tables; with 30 families and leave-one-out, variance across folds is estimable but not reported — the apparent advantage of Sloth over "Size and Tokens" (e.g., 4.3 vs 4.4 on v1) may not be statistically distinguishable. - All evaluation families are open-weight models on the Open LLM Leaderboard; frontier proprietary models (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5, Gemini 1.5) are entirely absent. The production function may not transfer to these models due to unknown training data and architecture differences. - The benchmark set is the Open LLM Leaderboard, which is well-documented to suffer from data contamination and saturation; the scaling law is implicitly contaminated and no robustness check against held-out, contamination-resistant benchmarks is provided. - The interaction term log(s)log(t) is motivated empirically (Appendix D) but no ablation isolates its contribution to predictive accuracy; it could be absorbing family-specific variation rather than capturing a genuine interaction. - The downstream task experiments (HumanEval, EQ-Bench, AgentBench) involve holding out a single model family with one or two test points — insufficient to distinguish a good fit from overfitting to those specific models. - Compute-optimal allocation assumes β_k2 > 0 (concave quadratic) but this is not verified to hold for all skill-family combinations; if β_k2 ≤ 0, the optimization degenerates to a boundary solution with different practical implications. Bottom Line A practitioner who allocates training compute across LLM families and benchmarks, or who wants to predict large-model performance from small-model observations, gains a statistically principled and practically deployable tool from this paper; the interpretability findings on instruction tuning's divergent effects on Reasoning versus Instruction Following are an additional concrete takeaway.
Recommended
#58 — Innovative (68)

Flash-GRPO: Efficient Alignment for Video Diffusion via One-Step Policy Optimization (27 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 18 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Flash-GRPO, a single-step GRPO training framework for video diffusion alignment that combines iso-temporal grouping and temporal gradient rectification to match or exceed full-trajectory training quality at ~6× lower compute cost. - Why it's important: Aligning large video diffusion models (14B parameters) with human preferences via GRPO requires hundreds of GPU-days per experiment under current full-trajectory methods, making research iteration and practical use prohibitively expensive. - How it does it: Two theoretically-grounded mechanisms: (1) enforcing that all rollouts within a GRPO prompt group share the same denoising timestep (iso-temporal grouping) to decouple advantage estimates from timestep difficulty, and (2) explicitly dividing the policy gradient by a derived time-dependent scaling factor λ(t) from the SDE discretization (temporal gradient rectification) to equalize gradient magnitudes across timesteps. - The good: On Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B with 350 GPU hours, Flash-GRPO reaches HPSv3 reward ~5.4 versus ~5.1 for full-trajectory Flow-GRPO and catastrophic collapse for the competing one-step baseline Flow-GRPO-Fast1, with ablations cleanly isolating each component's contribution. - The missing: Evaluation is confined to two model sizes from a single model family (Wan2.1); no comparison to DPO-style or offline preference methods; reward model (HPSv3) is used both during training and evaluation, raising a Goodhart's Law concern. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners working on compute-efficient RLHF for diffusion models; the theoretical derivation of λ(t) and its practical fix are the most portable takeaway.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile, the strongest paper in the cluster. Non-Obviousness (78) and Novelty (72) lead: deriving the time-dependent factor λ(t) from the Euler-Maruyama SDE discretization turns an observed empirical pathology into a closed-form diagnosis with a principled fix, and iso-temporal grouping is a clean, theoretically motivated correction. Rigor (68) benefits materially from ICML 2026 acceptance (peer review) and clean additive ablations, but is capped by no error bars/seeds, a Goodhart concern (HPSv3 used for both training and evaluation), 14B results shown only qualitatively, and DanceGRPO omitted from numeric baselines. It stays just below the Highly-Innovative threshold rather than crossing it.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 62 Resilience 65 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Video diffusion models trained with GRPO-style RL must backpropagate gradients through all T denoising timesteps for every rollout, creating memory and throughput bottlenecks that make a single experiment on a 14B model require hundreds of GPU-days. Existing one-step or sliding-window subsampling approaches (Flow-GRPO-Fast, MixGRPO) reduce cost but suffer catastrophic reward collapse and fail to reach the performance of full-trajectory training. The root cause of this failure has not been formally identified or corrected before this work. Researchers and practitioners trying to iterate quickly on video RL alignment have no practical single-step alternative. Key Idea: The core insight is that single-step GRPO fails for two analytically distinct reasons that can each be fixed precisely. First, mixing different denoising timesteps within a GRPO advantage group confounds reward variance with noise-level difficulty — solved by forcing all rollouts in a group to use the same timestep (iso-temporal grouping). Second, the Euler-Maruyama discretization of the reverse-time SDE introduces a time-dependent multiplicative factor λ(t) = (√Δt/σt) + (σt√Δt(1-t)/2t) into the policy gradient, causing gradient magnitudes to vary by orders of magnitude across the batch when different prompts train at different timesteps — solved by dividing the loss by λ(t) (temporal gradient rectification). Methodology: Flash-GRPO operates within the Flow-GRPO framework for flow-matching video models. During each training step: (1) a single timestep tk is sampled per prompt ck; (2) all G rollouts for that prompt share timestep tk but use independent Gaussian noise initializations, performing an ODE-to-SDE transition at tk only (other timesteps use deterministic ODE); (3) rewards are computed on the final generated frames (HPSv3 for visual quality, VideoAlign motion score for temporal coherence); (4) advantages are computed within each prompt group under identical timestep conditions; (5) the GRPO objective is computed with the loss scaled by 1/λ(t) per Equation 11; (6) temporal diversity is preserved globally by sampling different timesteps across prompts in the batch. The clip ratio is tightened to ε=0.001 (versus standard ~0.2) to stabilize single-step updates. A KL divergence term relative to the reference policy is optionally applied. Critical design decisions: (a) ODE for non-selected timesteps to preserve generation quality for reward computation — justified as necessary for accurate reward signals; (b) stratified timestep sampling across the batch for temporal coverage — justified empirically; (c) derivation of λ(t) from first principles via Euler-Maruyama discretization — well-justified theoretically. Key Results: On Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B with 350 GPU hours: Flash-GRPO achieves VBench Aesthetic Quality 66.43 versus 65.92 (Flow-GRPO-Fast1) and 65.79 (Flow-GRPO); Imaging Quality 68.28 versus 65.96 (Fast1) and 68.60 (Flow-GRPO); Subject Consistency 98.70 versus 98.46 (Fast1) and 97.28 (Flow-GRPO). On the held-out 300-prompt evaluation set, Flash-GRPO reaches HPSv3 reward ~5.4 versus ~5.1 for full-trajectory Flow-GRPO and ~4.9 for Fast1-with-KL. Flash-GRPO achieves 6× training acceleration relative to full-trajectory GRPO by training on a single timestep. Motion quality score reaches approximately −0.28 versus −0.34 for Fast1. Without KL regularization, Flash-GRPO maintains stable monotonic gradient norms while Fast1 exhibits catastrophic gradient spikes exceeding 0.001 L2 norm. Ablation on 1.3B: naive single-step 4.64 → +ITG 5.31 → +ITG+TGR 5.42 HPSv3 reward. Scalability to 14B Wan2.1 is demonstrated qualitatively (no numeric 14B evaluation table provided — only reward curves). Baselines are current (Flow-GRPO, DanceGRPO from 2025) and represent the direct prior art, though DanceGRPO is not numerically benchmarked in the main tables. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The combination of iso-temporal grouping and temporal gradient rectification is genuinely novel in the context of single-step video GRPO. The mathematical derivation of the λ(t) factor from SDE discretization as the root cause of gradient instability is a non-obvious analytical contribution. The application of GRPO to video diffusion is prior work (Flow-GRPO, DanceGRPO); Flash-GRPO's contribution is identifying and correcting the two specific failure modes that prevented single-step training from working. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and cleanly structured (Table 2), isolating ITG and TGR contributions. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported — a notable gap for reward curves that show high variance in baselines. The paper is accepted at ICML 2026 (43rd ICML), conferring peer-review confidence. Code is listed at a GitHub Pages URL (https://shredded-pork.github.io/Flash-GRPO.github.io/), suggesting a project page rather than a direct code repository — actual code availability is unclear. The HPSv3 reward model is used as both the training reward and the primary evaluation metric for most experiments, which conflates training optimization with evaluation (Goodhart's Law concern). The held-out 300-prompt evaluation set mitigates this somewhat. The 14B model results lack a numeric table; only qualitative figures and reward curves are shown. DanceGRPO, cited as a key prior method, is not included as a numeric baseline. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Flash-GRPO reduces a 14B model experiment from hundreds of GPU-days to a fraction of that cost; the 1.3B model completes alignment in 350 GPU hours, which is tractable on academic-scale clusters. - Efficient: Single-step training provides 6× wall-clock speedup over full-trajectory GRPO by eliminating T-1 gradient computation steps; memory reduction follows proportionally since only one timestep is backpropagated. - Effective: Flash-GRPO outperforms full-trajectory Flow-GRPO on held-out HPSv3 reward (5.4 vs 5.1) and VBench aesthetic/consistency metrics, not just on training reward — the evidence is consistent. - Scalable: The paper claims scalability to 14B and shows qualitative results and reward curves, but provides no numeric VBench or HPSv3 table at 14B scale; 14B scalability is asserted with limited quantitative support. The paper delivers a training algorithm and a trained model but does not release weights or a packaged library as of submission. Strengths: - The mathematical derivation of the time-dependent gradient scaling factor λ(t) from first principles (Euler-Maruyama discretization of the reverse SDE) is the paper's most intellectually solid contribution — it transforms an observed empirical pathology into a closed-form diagnosis with a principled fix. - Iso-temporal grouping is a clean algorithmic solution that is both easy to implement and theoretically motivated: it reduces advantage estimation variance by eliminating the timestep-induced confound, improving credit assignment quality without additional compute. - The ablation (Table 2) demonstrates additive, non-redundant contributions from each component: ITG alone provides +0.67 HPSv3 reward but without stability; TGR on top of ITG adds +0.11 reward and achieves training stability. - The paper directly falsifies the assumption of prior efficiency methods (that sliding-window subsampling is an acceptable efficiency-quality tradeoff) with both theoretical analysis and empirical gradient norm visualization. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The paper acknowledges that comparison with full-trajectory Flow-GRPO is limited to the first half of the training schedule due to compute cost. The authors note that their 14B experiments are presented primarily qualitatively. Beyond these, the authors do not explicitly flag the Goodhart's Law concern or the absence of multi-seed experiments. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The primary evaluation metric (HPSv3) is also the training reward signal; despite the held-out prompt split, this means the evaluation distribution is not fully independent. A second reward model (e.g., VideoScore or a human preference study) would strengthen the evaluation. - DanceGRPO is a key prior method cited but excluded from numerical comparisons — its absence is unexplained and potentially favorable to Flash-GRPO. - No error bars or confidence intervals appear on any reward curve or table entry; given the high variance visible in baseline curves, reporting variance matters. - The strict GRPO clip ratio of ε=0.001 (100× smaller than typical PPO) is a critical hyperparameter that the paper does not ablate; it is plausible this alone contributes substantially to training stability. - Evaluation at 14B scale is qualitative only; the paper's claim of 14B scalability is not supported by a numeric VBench table. - No comparison to offline preference optimization methods (e.g., video DPO variants), which may offer similar quality at lower cost. Bottom Line: Practitioners trying to iterate on video diffusion alignment without access to massive GPU budgets will find Flash-GRPO's theoretically-grounded single-step approach and 6× efficiency gain directly actionable, with the caveat that the 14B evaluation and multi-seed robustness claims need stronger empirical backing.
Recommended 2605.15980 PDF
#59 — Innovative (68)

OpenComputer: Verifiable Software Worlds for Computer-Use Agents (50 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 20 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OpenComputer, a verifier-grounded framework that automatically synthesizes verifiable desktop task benchmarks for computer-use agents, covering 33 applications and 1,000 machine-checkable tasks. - Why it's important: Scaling computer-use agent training and evaluation is bottlenecked by the cost of manually constructing realistic desktop environments and the unreliability of LLM-as-judge evaluation for fine-grained application state that is invisible in screenshots. - How it does it: App-specific programmatic verifiers exposing structured inspection endpoints (CDP, D-Bus, SQLite, UNO, file parsing) are generated and iteratively refined via a self-evolving loop that compares verifier outputs against LLM reference judgments on frozen calibration trajectories, then tasks are synthesized only for states those verifiers can check. - The good: Hard-coded verifiers match human adjudication on 113/120 tasks (94.1% task-level alignment) versus 95/120 (79.2%) for an agentic LLM judge, concretely validating the reliability claim on a head-to-head comparison. - The missing: The 120-task human-annotation comparison that validates verifier reliability does not report annotator expertise or inter-annotator agreement, so the gold standard itself is uncharacterized; the paper also provides no failure-mode breakdown by application or task type for frontier models. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building computer-use agent training pipelines or evaluation infrastructure who need a scalable, reproducible alternative to manual task curation with auditable reward signals.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile, so Practical Impact (80) carries the largest weight and is well-earned: a complete open-source release (verifier library, 1,000-task suite, init scripts, harness) addressing a real bottleneck, with the verifier-first synthesis principle being a non-obvious architectural inversion (Non-Obviousness 70). The open-source-OSWorld-collapse finding (>35pp drops) is a high-value diagnostic. Rigor (60) and Resilience (55) cap the composite: the 120-task human gold standard reports no annotator expertise or inter-annotator agreement, there are no error bars, and the self-evolution loop's reliance on an LLM judge to classify verifier-vs-agent errors is an unanalyzed partial circularity.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Researchers building computer-use agents face two coupled bottlenecks. First, constructing realistic, reproducible desktop task environments requires application-specific manual preparation — creating files, populating spreadsheets, configuring browser history, seeding databases — which does not scale. Second, evaluating whether an agent completed a task requires custom inspection logic per task, because success is often encoded in hidden application state (file metadata, database entries, configuration values) rather than visible pixels. The natural fallback, LLM-as-judge evaluation, is sensitive to prompt wording, produces model-specific biases, and cannot reliably detect fine-grained state errors such as a value typed into the wrong spreadsheet cell. Existing benchmarks (OSWorld, AndroidWorld, Windows Agent Arena) are largely human-curated and limited in task count and application breadth, making large-scale agent training impractical. Key Idea The core insight is that verification should be the organizing principle of environment and task construction rather than a downstream afterthought: by first building app-specific programmatic verifiers that expose inspection endpoints over real application state, and then synthesizing tasks only for states those verifiers can check, the system guarantees that every generated task has a machine-checkable reward grounded in actual application state rather than a visual proxy. A self-evolving loop further strengthens this by running strong agents on calibration tasks, comparing programmatic verdicts against LLM reference judgments, and repairing verifier-side failures before large-scale synthesis — addressing the practical problem that app schemas and APIs often diverge from documentation. Methodology OpenComputer is a four-phase pipeline operating over real desktop Docker sandboxes. Phase 1 (Verifier Generation): For each of 33 applications, an LLM agent enumerates inspectable state surfaces and implements Python verifier modules exposing CLI subcommands with JSON output. Inspection channels are application-specific: Chrome/Firefox use CDP, VS Code/Slack use CDP over Electron, LibreOffice uses UNO, darktable/Zotero/Chrome use SQLite profile databases, VLC uses D-Bus, GIMP uses PIL/Pillow, and Blender uses headless Python scripting. Each verifier undergoes a debug-fix-retry loop with unit tests covering positive/negative cases, JSON validity, and common failure modes until stable. Phase 2 (Self-Evolving Verification): Approximately 15 calibration tasks per application are run by a strong agent; the resulting trajectory is frozen and evaluated independently by an LLM judge and the programmatic verifier. Disagreements are classified as verifier-side or agent-side errors; only verifier-side failures trigger refinement. The verifier is updated (checker code, endpoint implementations, or documentation) and re-executed on the same cached final state, iterating up to 3 rounds. App-specific lessons are stored for future verifier extension. Phase 3 (Task Generation): Candidate tasks are proposed from a user-goal perspective without conditioning on available endpoints, encouraging diversity and avoiding benchmarks that overfit to easy-to-check outcomes. Candidates are filtered for multi-step complexity and data generatability (upper half of difficulty scale), matched to the verification stack, and materialized into environment artifacts (files, profiles, configs). If an outcome is inspectable but not yet exposed, the verifier is extended. Each finalized task is stored as τ=(x, e, c): instruction, initialization procedure, success criteria. Phase 4 (Evaluation Harness): Fresh sandbox per task, screenshot-action loop, partial-credit reward R = Npass/Ntotal, full trajectory logging, final save action attempt before verification. Three critical design decisions: (1) Verification-first synthesis — 17 visually-grounded tasks that could not be reduced to programmatic checks are excluded from the main benchmark; justified by reproducibility requirements, with those tasks released separately. (2) Self-evolving calibration before large-scale synthesis — justified empirically by an 89.4% checker-side repair rate and +8.9pp agreement improvement. (3) Partial-credit scoring — justified by the observation that frontier models often complete most but not all subtasks in multi-step workflows. Key Results All results are from an arXiv preprint and have not been peer-reviewed. Frontier models: GPT-5.4 achieves 68.3% task success rate and 88.4% average reward at 19.0 steps/task. Claude-Sonnet-4.6 reaches 64.4% success (76.6% reward, 31.5 steps). Kimi-K2.6 reaches 58.8% success (70.7% reward, 35.7 steps). Even the strongest model fails to complete roughly one third of tasks end-to-end. Open-source model collapse: GUI-OWL-1.5-8B drops from 52.3% on OSWorld-Verified to 5.7% on OpenComputer (−46.6pp); EvoCUA-8B drops from 46.1% to 10.9% (−35.2pp). Qwen-3.5-27B drops from 56.2% to 32.3% (−23.9pp). These gaps indicate that strong OSWorld performance reflects benchmark-specific adaptation rather than general desktop automation capability. Verifier reliability (120-task human-annotated comparison): hard-coded verifier achieves 94.1% task-level alignment and 97.3% checklist agreement with humans; the agentic LLM judge achieves 79.2% and 92.2%, respectively. The gap is largest for terminal-heavy applications (Blender, developer tools) where success evidence lives in scrollback logs and intermediate artifacts. Self-evolution ablation (450 calibration tasks): 76 checker-side errors identified; 68 repaired (89.4% repair rate). Pre/post-evolution agreement with humans: 85.2% → 94.1% (+8.9pp). GUI vs. CLI (343-task shared subset): GUI GPT-5.4 achieves 75.2% success in 288s/task; GUI Claude Sonnet 4.6 achieves 73.0% in 622s/task; CLI Claude Code achieves 67.2% in 141s/task — 2–4× faster but 6–8pp less accurate. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The verifier-first synthesis pipeline is genuinely novel; prior work (OSWorld, AndroidWorld) generates tasks manually and treats verification as a separate, manually written check. The self-evolving verification loop that uses calibration-execution disagreements to repair verifier code is a new operational mechanism not present in concurrent work (Gym-Anything, InfiniteWeb, GUI-Genesis). The empirical finding that open-source models' OSWorld scores do not transfer to OpenComputer (>35pp drops) is a concrete diagnostic result about benchmark saturation that is new and practically significant. The GUI vs. CLI comparison is a novel evaluation dimension. Rigor & Reproducibility Code, verifier modules, task specifications, and environment initialization scripts are released at https://github.com/echo0715/OpenComputer. The 120-task human-annotation comparison is the primary validation of verifier reliability; however, annotator expertise, selection criteria for the 120 tasks, and inter-annotator agreement are not reported, making it impossible to assess the quality of the gold standard. No error bars or significance tests are reported on any agent evaluation result. The self-evolution ablation (450 calibration tasks, 76 checker-side errors) is well-designed and the case study in Appendix A (darktable schema drift) provides concrete, code-level evidence for the mechanism. The self-evolution loop uses an LLM judge as ground truth for disagreement classification, creating a partial circularity: if the LLM judge systematically misclassifies agent failures as verifier failures, refinement could introduce errors; this is not analyzed. The paper correctly notes this is a preprint. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS - Practical: Runs in standard Docker-based desktop sandboxes; supports local execution and cloud deployment on AWS, Tencent Cloud, or E2B for parallel rollouts on commercially available hardware. - Efficient: No per-task wall-clock cost analysis for the synthesis pipeline is provided; agent evaluation requires 2× H100 GPUs for open-source model inference, which is accessible but not commodity; Docker sandbox overhead is standard. - Effective: Hard-coded verifiers achieve 97.3% checklist agreement with human annotations on a 120-task sample, demonstrating they measure actual task completion rather than a proxy; the darktable case study shows the verifier correctly detects a specific SQL schema bug that an LLM judge missed. - Scalable: The synthesis pipeline is extensible by design (new apps follow the same verifier-generation workflow), but no stress test beyond 33 apps and 1,000 tasks is reported; the claim that it scales to training-data generation is stated as future work, not demonstrated. Delivers a full software artifact: verifier library, task suite, environment initialization scripts, and evaluation harness at the GitHub repository above. Strengths - The verifier-first design principle cleanly decouples "what can be checked" from "what tasks to generate," which avoids Goodhart's Law dynamics where benchmarks drift toward easy-to-verify rather than realistic outcomes; this is a genuine architectural contribution over prior work. - The self-evolving calibration loop solves a practical and previously underaddressed problem — application schema drift between documentation and actual runtime state — and demonstrates it concretely with code-level examples (darktable library.db vs. data.db split). - The empirical finding that open-source model OSWorld scores collapse on OpenComputer (GUI-OWL-1.5-8B: 52.3% → 5.7%) is a high-value diagnostic result that directly informs the field about the current state of benchmark saturation and model generalization. - The GUI vs. CLI comparison (Section 5.2) is an underexplored evaluation dimension that yields a quantitatively concrete and non-obvious tradeoff: CLI is 2–4× faster per task but loses 6–8pp accuracy even on CLI-compatible tasks, suggesting visual grounding provides useful signal beyond mere execution capability. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge that some task success criteria require visual or geometric judgments that cannot be reliably expressed as programmatic checks — e.g., whether an arrow in Draw.io visually connects two specific boxes in the intended direction, or whether spatial layout and visual alignment are correct. They exclude 17 such tasks from the main benchmark, release them separately with an LLM-as-judge pipeline, and frame hybrid verification (combining executable state checks with visual judgments) as future work. This is handled directly and honestly, with a specific count rather than a vague hedge. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 120-task human-annotation comparison is the sole empirical validation of verifier reliability, but annotator qualifications, selection methodology for the 120 tasks, and inter-annotator agreement are not reported; the gold standard quality is uncharacterized. - The self-evolution loop depends on the LLM judge correctly classifying disagreements as verifier-side vs. agent-side failures; the rate at which the LLM judge misclassifies failures in either direction is not reported, leaving the calibration mechanism's precision unquantified. - Task difficulty distribution is characterized only as "upper half of difficulty scale" with no formal difficulty metric, no per-application task count breakdown, and no analysis of which applications or task categories are hardest — making it difficult to assess whether the benchmark is well-calibrated or skewed. - Frontier model failures are reported as aggregate success rates only; no breakdown by application, error type (wrong action, incomplete sequence, navigation failure), or task category is provided, limiting diagnostic value for practitioners trying to improve agents. - The CLI subset (343 tasks, 14 apps) was constructed by removing GUI-only applications; if GUI-only apps are harder, the subset may not be representative of the full benchmark difficulty, potentially understating the CLI accuracy gap. - The paper claims OpenComputer supports RL and rejection-sampling training pipelines but provides no evidence — trajectory quality, diversity, or reward signal variance are not characterized; this remains an unvalidated design goal. Bottom Line This is a well-executed systems-and-benchmark paper that delivers a practically useful infrastructure contribution — the verifier-first synthesis pipeline and self-evolving calibration loop address real bottlenecks, the open-source release is complete, and the empirical finding that open-source OSWorld scores collapse on OpenComputer is directly actionable for the computer-use agent research community.
Recommended 2605.19769 PDF
#60 — Innovative (68)

Aurora: Unified Video Editing with a Tool-Using Agent (20 ▲ )

Yongsheng Yu, Ziyun Zeng, Zhiyuan Xiao, Zhenghong Zhou, Hang Hua, Wei Xiong, Jiebo Luo · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 20 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Aurora, an agentic video editing framework that pairs a LoRA-finetuned VLM agent (Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct) with a unified video diffusion transformer to automatically fill missing reference images, spatial masks, and rewritten instructions — via web image search and grounded segmentation — before video generation. - Why it's important: Unified video editing models already accept rich conditioning (text, source video, reference images, masks) but assume the user supplies it all; real requests routinely name specific entities without providing reference images or describe regions without supplying masks, causing even strong models to hallucinate generic substitutes or edit the wrong region. - How it does it: The VLM agent maps each raw user request to a four-field structured plan (rewritten instruction, task label, image-search query, mask phrase), then executes tool calls — Serper API web search + GroundingDINO/SAM2 segmentation — to construct the complete conditioning tuple before the video DiT runs. - The good: Adding the Aurora VLM agent raises the AgentEdit-Bench overall score from 74.7 to 87.9 on the same video DiT (+13.2 points), and the same frozen LoRA agent also improves third-party models: UniVideo 67.0→76.8, Kiwi-Edit 69.7→71.7. - The missing: All quantitative evaluation relies on a single closed-source LLM judge (Gemini 2.5 Pro) across a 150-case benchmark with no human evaluation or inter-annotator agreement, raising the risk that the judge systematically favors Aurora's more explicit rewritten instructions over raw-prompt baselines. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building agentic generation pipelines or studying condition-construction for unified video editing; the model-agnostic agent transfer result and DPO boundary-case taxonomy are the most reusable takeaways.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (delivers a working agentic pipeline + released code). Practical Impact (75, weight 30) carries the score: the model-agnostic agent transfer — the same frozen LoRA agent lifting two third-party frozen models without retraining — is the most credible and reusable result, though weights are not confirmed released and no latency numbers exist. Non-Obviousness (75) and Novelty (72) are high: treating condition-construction as a separately trainable problem and the targeted DPO boundary-case taxonomy are non-obvious contributions. Resilience (58) is the main drag — all numbers on all three benchmarks come from a single closed-source judge (Gemini 2.5 Pro) with no human validation, and since Aurora's agent rewrites instructions to be more explicit, the judge may systematically favor it; the retrieval-vs-rewriting contribution is also never isolated. Rigor (60) reflects present ablations but absent error bars and unvalidated judge correlation. Weighted: 0.15*72 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*58 + 0.30*75 ≈ 68.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 58 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Unified video editing models accept text, source video, reference images, and segmentation masks as conditioning, but they do not resolve how to construct those conditions from a raw user request that omits them. The paper categorizes this incompleteness along two axes: visual underspecification (the user names a specific entity — a brand, character, artwork — but provides no reference image) and textual underspecification (the edit intent is implied rather than stated, e.g., "change the blue item to a vehicle that runs much faster"). No prior work specifically targets this condition-construction gap for unified video editing models; prior agentic visual generation work addresses image editing or multi-model video coordination, not the problem of completing a single model's conditioning interface from underspecified natural language. Key Idea The core insight is to treat condition construction as a separately trainable problem: the video model already defines the full conditioning interface it needs; the missing piece is a trained agent that reliably fills that interface from imprecise natural language, before the generator runs. Because the planning and generation stages are decoupled, a single trained agent can transfer to any video editing model that shares the same conditioning format, without retraining the generator. Methodology The system has two sequentially trained components. The Aurora video DiT (Wan2.2-TI2V-5B, 5B parameters) is trained with flow matching on ~4.67M image/video editing pairs across three conditioning regimes: text-only image edits (2.39M pairs), instruction+source-video edits (1.67M pairs), and reference-guided video edits (610K pairs). Source and reference tokens are concatenated along the token-sequence dimension with zero-timestep AdaLN modulation, so conditioning tokens are decoupled from the denoising noise schedule. A frozen Qwen3.5-4B MLLM encodes instruction + sampled source frames + reference images into cross-attention context via a zero-initialized linear projector. Masks are composited as masked images and passed as ordinary reference images, eliminating a separate mask conditioning branch. Critical design decision: the same flow-matching objective trains the model on empty-reference, user-reference, and masked-image edits simultaneously, so no architecture changes are needed at inference. After the DiT is frozen, the VLM agent (Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct + LoRA) is trained in two stages. SFT on 25K planning examples (clean instructions degraded into colloquial user requests, four-field plan as supervision) and 10K reference-image selection examples teaches valid plan construction. DPO on 1.8K chosen/rejected pairs targets five specific plan-level boundary cases that SFT leaves ambiguous: source-entity false triggers, ambiguous mask phrases, false image-search triggers, constraint-losing rewrites, and task-routing confusions. Each DPO pair varies exactly one decision axis while holding other plan fields fixed, making the preference signal highly targeted. Sampling during SFT is organized by task type rather than raw data frequency to prevent high-frequency tasks from dominating. Key Results On AgentEdit-Bench (150 cases, Gemini 2.5 Pro judge, scores as percentage of rubric maximum): Aurora with agent achieves 87.9 overall vs. 74.7 without agent — a +13.2-point gain from the agent stage alone. The largest gains are on IP replacement (73.0→89.6) and IP addition (68.0→85.3), where the raw request names an entity but provides no reference image. Baselines without Aurora's agent: UniVideo 67.0, Kiwi-Edit 69.7; both improve substantially when the Aurora agent is prepended (76.8 and 71.7 respectively), demonstrating model-agnostic transfer. Ablation: SFT alone yields 85.0; adding DPO brings it to 87.9 (+2.9 points). On EditVerse-Bench (120 cases, 0–10 scale): Aurora 7.61 — best reported, above closed-source Runway Aleph (7.17) and EditVerse (7.52) and all open-source methods. On OpenVE-Bench (358 cases, 1–5 scale): Aurora 3.38, second only to Runway Aleph (3.51, closed-source). Baselines are current and the field's strongest open-source releases; the closed-source comparisons are included as reference points only. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW METHOD. The condition-construction framing — training a lightweight VLM agent to complete a video model's conditioning tuple from underspecified user requests — is not a direct combination of prior work. Prior agentic image editing pipelines (AgentBanana, Gen-Searcher, PhotoAgent) target image generation and do not address the multi-channel conditioning interface of video editing models. The DPO boundary-case taxonomy for plan-level alignment is a non-obvious methodological contribution. AgentEdit-Bench is the first benchmark designed to measure agent contribution to video editing under structured underspecification, with an evaluation protocol that separately isolates agent transfer from video model quality. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present for the most critical design decision — VLM agent training stages — and agent transfer is evaluated on two third-party models across two benchmarks, providing some external validity. However, all quantitative evaluation relies on a single closed-source judge (Gemini 2.5 Pro, temperature=0) with no human evaluation, no inter-annotator agreement, and no validation that Gemini 2.5 Pro scores correlate with human preference on this specific task. AgentEdit-Bench has 150 cases, which is small; the 3-point margin separating Kiwi-Edit+agent (71.7) from Aurora-no-agent (74.7) is within the range that per-case Gemini variability could affect. No error bars or significance tests are reported anywhere. The paper also does not ablate the image-search retrieval quality — there is no experiment showing what happens when only instruction rewriting (no retrieval) is applied to IP tasks, which would isolate how much the improvement comes from retrieval vs. rewriting. Code is released at github.com/yeates/Aurora; model weights and training datasets are not explicitly stated as publicly available. This is an arXiv preprint (May 2026), not peer-reviewed. Deployability (SYSTEMS) - Practical: The Aurora video DiT (5B parameters) and VLM agent (8B parameters) both fit on commercially available high-memory GPUs (A100/H100 class); the additional Serper API and GroundingDINO/SAM2 for segmentation introduce external dependencies but are off-the-shelf. - Efficient: Inference requires 50 denoising steps at up to 921,600 pixels (≈708×1280) for 81 frames, plus a Serper API call and a segmentation pass for IP/removal tasks; no inference latency numbers are reported, making cost comparisons impossible, but the multi-step pipeline is clearly slower than prompt-only baselines. - Effective: On AgentEdit-Bench the system demonstrates clear, measurable gains on its stated problem (IP entity identity preservation, correct mask localization); the agent transfer results confirm the improvement is not artifact of a single model pairing. - Scalable: Not tested beyond the 150/120/358-case benchmarks; no throughput or batch-processing experiments; the Serper API introduces a commercial rate-limited external bottleneck. Scaling claims are absent. The system delivers a software artifact (code at github.com/yeates/Aurora); model weights are not confirmed released. Strengths - The model-agnostic VLM agent transfer result — the same LoRA agent improves two frozen third-party video models (UniVideo, Kiwi-Edit) without retraining — is the most credible and practically significant finding; it demonstrates that the agent learns a generalizable planning skill, not a shortcut specific to Aurora's DiT. - The DPO boundary-case taxonomy is principled: five specific failure modes are identified, each pair varies exactly one decision axis, and the ablation confirms DPO adds +2.9 points over SFT alone, with specific attributions to false-trigger suppression and mask-phrase disambiguation. - Treating masks as composited reference images (no separate mask branch) is an elegant engineering choice that simplifies the conditioning architecture and allows the existing video DiT training objective to handle mask conditioning without modification. - AgentEdit-Bench's rubric distinguishes IP Presence (entity placed in roughly the right region) from IP Identity Match (entity matches specific real-world appearance), a granularity not present in prior benchmarks and directly relevant to the paper's stated problem. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge two limitations directly: (1) the VLM agent is trained only with offline DPO; online RL (e.g., GRPO) with rewards from the actually edited clip is noted as impractical at current compute budget, and few-step DiT distillation combined with joint online RL is proposed as the path forward; (2) the 5B DiT handles limited-motion edits reliably but fails to add new subjects with large, physically plausible motion, attributed to model capacity and training data coverage. A broader impacts section acknowledges misuse risks (non-consensual identity manipulation, copyright from web-retrieved references) without proposing mitigation beyond calling for creative-tool deployment. The authors confront the RL limitation directly and propose a concrete path, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The judge reliability problem is the most serious concern: all numbers on all three benchmarks come from Gemini 2.5 Pro at temperature=0 with no human validation. Aurora's VLM agent outputs a rewritten instruction that is more explicit and grammatically complete than the original underspecified user request; if Gemini 2.5 Pro's judgment is influenced by instruction clarity (not just edit quality), the scores systematically favor Aurora's pipeline regardless of actual visual output quality. - The Serper API dependency is never ablated against retrieval-free alternatives. The question of how much improvement comes from retrieval (getting the right reference image) vs. rewriting (clarifying the instruction) is answered only indirectly via the ablation on already-specified benchmarks where tools are disabled — but those benchmarks don't test IP tasks where retrieval matters most. - The "fair baseline" comparison (baselines given Aurora's retrieved reference images, Table 2 ✓ rows) is quantitatively reported but qualitatively underemphasized; it would directly answer whether the Aurora DiT or the Aurora agent is the source of advantage on IP tasks, since Kiwi-Edit+Aurora-agent (71.7) still falls below Aurora+Aurora-agent (87.9), suggesting the DiT architecture also plays a role. - No latency, memory, or throughput numbers are reported anywhere; a practitioner cannot estimate whether the agentic pipeline is feasible for interactive use or batch processing at scale. Bottom Line Aurora offers a practically useful and well-motivated contribution — the model-agnostic agent transfer result is solid — but the exclusive reliance on a 150-case LLM-judged benchmark without human validation leaves the magnitude of the improvement uncertain; practitioners should treat the specific score gaps as directional rather than definitive.
Recommended 2605.18748 PDF
#61 — Innovative (67)

Performance -Driven Policy Optimization for Speculative Decoding with Adaptive Windowing

Jie Jiang, Xing Sun, Ruotian Chen, Jianan Su, Kaixin Shen · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PPOW, a reinforcement learning framework that trains speculative decoding draft models using window-level rewards (accepted prefix length and distributional proximity) instead of the token-level cross-entropy objectives used by all prior learned drafters. - Why it's important: A single early mismatch in a speculative window voids all remaining draft tokens, so token-level imitation accuracy does not directly optimize inference throughput — the gap between training objective and inference utility is the primary bottleneck for existing drafters including EAGLE-3 and GRIFFIN. - How it does it: PPOW applies a PPO-style clipped group-relative policy objective at the speculative-window level, using a cost-aware accepted-prefix reward plus a fallback distributional proximity reward for zero-acceptance windows, while concentrating training on high-criticality windows via confidence-weighted KL divergence prioritization (ADAW). - The good: Across LLaMA-3 (8B, 70B) and Qwen3 (8B, 32B) with a unified decoding protocol, PPOW achieves mean acceptance lengths of 6.29–6.52 and speedups of 3.39–4.36×, beating EAGLE-3 and GRIFFIN baselines, and matches the supervised baseline's τ at 4× fewer inference candidates — directly reducing verification overhead. - The missing: No variance estimates or significance tests are reported for any result, which is a material omission for an RL-based method with known training instability; additionally, gains on open-ended NL tasks (WMT14 speedup 1.64×, X-SUM speedup 2.87× vs EAGLE-3's 1.56× and 2.81×) are marginal, and the boundary conditions for PPOW's effectiveness are not characterized. - Read it or not: Researchers and engineers working on LLM inference serving should read this — the window-level RL formulation is cleanly motivated, the CST control experiment is rigorous, and the candidate-group efficiency result has direct practical implications.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (75) is solid — window-level group-relative RL with target-anchored KL is a genuinely new training objective for speculative drafters, and Non-Obviousness (78) is high given the surprising finding that supervised training degrades after saturation while RL keeps improving. The composite is pulled down by Rigor (62) and Resilience (58): for an RL method with documented training variance, the complete absence of error bars or multi-seed results makes sub-0.5τ margins over EAGLE-3 statistically unverifiable, NL-task gains are marginal (WMT14 1.64× vs 1.56×), and the longer-acceptance-yet-lower-speedup MT-Bench discrepancy is unexplained. Completeness (65) benefits from a strong CST control and honest limitations but loses ground on the unquantified extra greedy-decode cost. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*58 + 0.10*62 = 67.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 65 Resilience 58 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Speculative decoding accelerates LLM inference by having a lightweight draft model propose a window of K candidate tokens for parallel verification by a larger target model. In practice, a single early token mismatch truncates the accepted prefix and invalidates all subsequent draft tokens in the window — making inference throughput governed by acceptance bottlenecks rather than average token quality. The entire landscape of learned drafters (MEDUSA, Hydra, EAGLE 1–3, HASS, GRIFFIN, DistillSpec, OSD, FastDraft) optimizes token-level cross-entropy or distribution matching objectives. These objectives improve local next-token prediction fidelity but are structurally misaligned with the prefix-sensitive, window-level utility of speculative verification: maximizing token-level accuracy does not directly maximize the number of tokens that survive the rejection-sampling acceptance test, particularly at the early positions that function as bottlenecks. Key Idea The core technical insight is that the speculative drafter should be treated as a policy over complete speculative windows rather than a per-step predictor, and that the training signal should be concentrated on windows containing the acceptance bottlenecks most consequential for inference throughput. The enabling trick is two-fold: (1) group-relative PPO advantages computed at window granularity, with the target model used as the KL anchor (rather than a separate reference policy), align optimization directly with rejection-sampling verification; (2) confidence-weighted KL divergence between draft and target distributions identifies which windows contain hard-to-draft positions where divergence will actually hurt acceptance — low-entropy (confident) target positions where the draft diverges are far more consequential than high-entropy positions where both models are uncertain. Methodology PPOW is a two-stage pipeline: supervised initialization from an EAGLE-3 checkpoint, then RL fine-tuning. The RL stage proceeds as follows per training prefix x. First, the frozen target model produces a response y, hidden states H, and distributions {P_t}. The drafter produces distributions {Q_t}. ADAW computes a token criticality score v_t = C(P_t) · KL(P_t || Q_t), where C(P_t) = 1 − H(P_t)/log|V| is entropy-normalized confidence, then aggregates v_t over each candidate window to get window score s_j; a training window start j* is sampled proportional to {s_j} under a curriculum that increases the hard-window fraction from 0.2 to 0.6 across training. For the selected window, G_roll=8 speculative windows are sampled from the drafter and each is verified against the target (rejection sampling), yielding accepted prefix length k_g. The reward is R = k/(kγ+1) + η·1[k=0]·1[Δ<ε], where γ=0.12 is the drafter-to-target compute ratio, and Δ measures cumulative log-probability gap between the draft and target-preferred (greedy) window — the proximity term activates only when k=0. Group-relative advantages Â_i = (R_i − μ_R)/(σ_R + δ) are computed within the rollout group. The policy update uses a PPO clipped objective with KL(π_θ || π_target) regularization (β=0.03), with the target model as anchor rather than an initialization snapshot. Three critical design decisions: (1) KL anchoring to the target model is well-justified as it stabilizes training around the very distribution that governs verification; (2) activating the proximity reward only at k=0 is sound in keeping the reward sparse at high-acceptance positions but leaves partially-accepted windows (k>0 but k<K) without a soft signal; (3) the hard-window curriculum schedule is fixed at a predetermined step (44k), not adaptive to learning progress. Key Results At temperature 0.0 under a unified decoding protocol (tree decoding, K=10, branching factor 3), PPOW achieves: LLaMA-3.1-8B mean τ=6.40, speedup 3.46× (vs EAGLE-3: 6.09, 3.41×; GRIFFIN: 5.95, 3.31×); LLaMA-3.3-70B mean τ=6.29, speedup 4.36× (vs EAGLE-3: 5.94, 4.15×; GRIFFIN: 5.89, 4.12×); Qwen3-8B mean τ=6.52, speedup 3.39× (vs EAGLE-3: 6.16, 3.17×); Qwen3-32B mean τ=6.44, speedup 3.92× (vs EAGLE-3: 5.99, 3.81×). Gains are strongest on HumanEval and GSM8K (structured outputs); on open-ended MT-Bench, PPOW's gains are smaller and it underperforms EAGLE-3 on speedup for LLaMA-3.1-8B (2.72× vs 2.91×). The candidate group-size experiment (Table 2) shows PPOW at group size 4 achieves τ=6.33 while the supervised baseline requires group size 16 to reach comparable acceptance. Against continued supervised training (CST) at matched steps and learning rates, PPOW reaches τ=6.50 vs CST's 5.72 on GSM8K/LLaMA-3.1-8B; the supervised baseline degrades after ~12k steps while PPOW improves monotonically (Figure 3). On natural-language tasks, WMT14 speedup is only 1.64× (EAGLE-3: 1.56×) and X-SUM 2.87× (EAGLE-3: 2.81×) — minimal practical benefit. Against older baselines: PPOW τ=6.50 vs OSD 3.53 on Vicuna-7B/GSM8K; τ=6.50 vs Lookahead 2.26 on LLaMA-2-7B/GSM8K. This is a preprint; results are self-reported with no peer review. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The window-level RL formulation for speculative drafter training is genuinely novel — no prior work has applied group-relative policy optimization at speculative-window granularity with target-anchored KL regularization. ADAW's confidence-weighted divergence prioritization is a novel window-selection criterion with formal justification. The individual components (PPO/GRPO, KL regularization, curriculum learning, divergence-based sampling) are all known, and the EAGLE-3 backbone is reused; the genuine contribution is their specific integration and the theoretical framing of speculative utility as a window-level RL reward. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present for both key components (Table 4), isolating ADAW and R_dist individually and jointly — this is a real strength. The CST control at matched training budget is a notably rigorous comparison that rules out the "more compute" hypothesis. However, no error bars, standard deviations, or multi-seed results are reported for any experiment — a significant omission for an RL method where training variance is well-documented. Four model families provide breadth. Hardware and training costs are specified (NVIDIA H100, 50–200 GPU-hours). No code or model weights are released (not stated in paper). Ablation of confidence weighting in ADAW (C(P_t)) versus plain KL divergence is not done empirically — only analytical justification (Appendix B) is given. The train/eval data overlap is not analyzed: for Qwen models, PPOW is trained on ShareGPT + UltraChat-200k, and evaluation includes GSM8K and HumanEval which may share distributional characteristics. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs on standard NVIDIA H100 GPUs; inference requires only the improved drafter weights and is fully compatible with existing speculative decoding serving stacks without modification. - Efficient: Training requires grouped rollouts (G_roll=8) plus target-model verification passes per window, substantially increasing training compute over supervised fine-tuning — 50/100/200 H100 GPU-hours for 8B/32B/70B targets; inference cost is unchanged from any other EAGLE-family drafter. - Effective: Acceptance length and measured wall-clock speedup improvements are reported together, confirming the gains are not just proxy metrics; however, effectiveness degrades significantly on open-ended generation tasks (WMT14 speedup gap ~0.08×), and single-run reporting limits confidence. - Scalable: Tested at 8B, 32B, and 70B target scales with training cost growing roughly linearly; no evaluation beyond 70B, no analysis of behavior at longer context lengths or higher batch sizes where speculative decoding dynamics differ. The work delivers no software artifact — it stops at paper form. Strengths - The identification of the token-level vs. window-level training objective mismatch is formally grounded via a Pinsker-inequality lower bound on acceptance probability (Appendix B), not just argued intuitively. - The CST vs. PPOW comparison under identical training budget is methodologically strong and provides compelling evidence that the RL objective — not additional training — drives the gains. - The finding that supervised training degrades after saturation (Figure 3) while RL continues improving is a concrete and surprising empirical result that advances the community's understanding of drafter training dynamics. - The candidate group-size efficiency result (4× fewer candidates needed) has direct practical value for deployment under inference latency budgets. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) higher training complexity and resource cost versus continued supervised training due to grouped rollouts and target-model verification; (2) a larger hyperparameter surface than standard RL (K, γ, η, ε, curriculum proportions) and the absence of adaptive self-tuning; (3) weaker gains on open-ended tasks (X-SUM, WMT14) attributed to broader valid continuation spaces weakening the group-relative reward signal. These limitations are confronted directly and are a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No variance estimates across runs: RL training variance is substantial and all comparisons are single-run, making the margin over EAGLE-3 (which is sometimes under 0.5 τ units) statistically unverifiable. - The Distribution-Based Proximity Reward requires an additional greedy target-model decoding pass per training window (to construct the reference window y), effectively doubling target-model calls beyond what verification already requires — this additional cost is not quantified or discussed. - The ADAW curriculum proportion (0.2/0.4/0.6) is triggered at fixed training steps rather than adaptive to learning progress; the choice of 44k steps for the switch is not ablated. - No ablation of confidence weighting C(P_t) in ADAW versus plain KL divergence alone — the theoretical argument is given but empirical validation of the confidence term's contribution is absent. - The MT-Bench speedup for LLaMA-3.1-8B is actually lower for PPOW (2.72×) than EAGLE-3 (2.91×) even while τ is higher (5.47 vs 5.53) — this discrepancy (longer acceptance yet lower speedup) is unexplained, suggesting possible overhead or tree-structure interaction not accounted for. - No evaluation in batched serving scenarios where speculative decoding acceptance rates are known to differ from single-request settings. Bottom Line For anyone deploying or researching LLM inference serving, PPOW offers a principled and practically useful upgrade to EAGLE-family drafters at the cost of a one-time RL fine-tuning pass — the candidate-group efficiency result alone justifies reading it.

#62 — Innovative (67)

Combining On-Policy Optimization and Distillation for Long-Context Reasoning in Large Language Models

Miguel Moura Ramos, Duarte M. Alves, André F. T. Martins · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces dGRPO (Distilled Group Relative Policy Optimization), a post-training method that augments on-policy GRPO with dense token-level teacher guidance, plus LONGBLOCKS, a 193K-sample multilingual synthetic long-context dataset, forming a two-stage recipe for long-context alignment of LLMs. - Why it's important: Existing post-training methods for long-context fail in complementary ways — off-policy methods suffer from exposure bias over long horizons while on-policy RL with sparse rewards is unstable — leaving practitioners without a reliable recipe for long-context alignment. - How it does it: dGRPO jointly optimizes sparse outcome-based GRPO rewards and a reverse-KL on-policy distillation term toward a stronger teacher (Qwen3-32B) along the model's own sampled trajectories, providing dense token-level regularization that stabilizes reward optimization. - The good: RULER performance at 128K tokens improves from 11.0 to 44.5 (a 4× gain) while short-context benchmarks are preserved or slightly improved, and the final 1.7B model is competitive with 3B–4B baselines including a YaRN-based context-extension variant. - The missing: The entire empirical study is conducted at 1.7B parameters; no evidence is provided that the recipe scales to 7B, 14B, or 70B models, where reward-hacking dynamics and teacher-capacity gaps may differ substantially. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on post-training alignment for long-context LLMs — the recipe is concrete, reproducible, and the ablations are unusually thorough; skip it if you need scale evidence beyond 1.7B.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Novelty (70) is solid but bounded — dGRPO is a NEW METHOD plus a NEW DATASET (LONGBLOCKS), yet the core mechanism is a straightforward linear combination of established GRPO (Shao 2024) and on-policy distillation (Agarwal 2024). Rigor (70) reflects unusually thorough ablations (β sweep, teacher source, SFT-vs-KD init, data mix) with 5-run std reported and public model/data, but is materially dragged down by in-family circular validation (Qwen3 generates LONGBLOCKS, a Qwen3 judge scores rewards) and the single 1.7B scale. The two dimensions pulling the composite below 75 are Resilience (62) and Novelty — the recipe's central scale-generalization claim is asserted but untested, a gap the toughest reviewer would flag immediately. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*65 = 67.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 68 Resilience 62 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Real-world applications requiring LLMs to reason over code repositories, multi-document collections, and long conversations expose a fundamental limitation: post-training methods that work well at short context fail to generalize. Off-policy SFT and knowledge distillation suffer from exposure bias — they optimize only on expert-generated prefixes, so the model never learns to recover from its own long-horizon errors. On-policy GRPO addresses exposure bias but relies on sparse trajectory-level rewards that produce noisy gradient estimates, unstable training, and degradation of short-context skills when applied at long context. On-policy distillation (OPD) provides dense token-level guidance on model-generated rollouts but cannot directly optimize arbitrary reward signals. The gap is a principled method that combines on-policy reward optimization with dense supervision, applied to a sufficiently diverse long-context training set. Key Idea The core insight is that GRPO's sparse reward signal and OPD's dense teacher signal are complementary rather than competing: by adding a reverse-KL divergence term between the student policy and a stronger teacher model (evaluated along the student's own sampled trajectories) directly to the GRPO objective, the teacher provides per-token learning signal that stabilizes credit assignment over long sequences while the GRPO term preserves the ability to optimize non-differentiable task rewards. The teacher's KL term acts as a learned regularizer tied to the student's current distribution, avoiding the train-inference mismatch of standard KD. Methodology The training pipeline has two stages. Stage 1 is an off-policy SFT warm-up on a 10/90 mix of short-context (Nemotron-Post-Training-Dataset-v2) and long-context (LONGBLOCKS) data, trained for 2 epochs with AdamW (lr=1×10⁻⁵, batch size 4M tokens, bfloat16, sequence packing). This stage stabilizes rollout coherence before on-policy RL. Stage 2 applies dGRPO for 1 epoch on the same data mixture. The dGRPO objective is: J_dGRPO(θ) = E[GRPO_objective − β·D_KL(π_θ(·|p,o_{<t}) ∥ π_teacher(·|p,o_{<t}))], where β=0.5 (swept over {0, 0.1, 0.25, 0.4, 0.5}), and the KL is computed over student-sampled completions. Three critical design decisions: (1) setting β_ref=0 (eliminating the KL term to the SFT reference policy, following prior reasoning-model work), well-justified by evidence that reasoning models can safely diverge from their initialization; (2) using Qwen3-32B as the external teacher rather than self-distillation, justified empirically by Figure 5 showing self-distillation is consistently inferior; (3) the 10/90 short/long token ratio, justified by ablation in Appendix C.4 showing long-context performance is sensitive to this ratio while short-context is not. Rewards are verifiable (SymPy for math, unit tests for coding) or LM-judge-based (Qwen3-4B as binary judge for chat and long-context tasks). LONGBLOCKS is constructed by prompting Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Thinking to generate QA pairs from raw documents, then filtering with an LLM-as-judge step that rejects locally answerable, weakly grounded, or malformed examples. The base model is Qwen3-1.7B extended to 128K context by fixing the RoPE base frequency to 1×10⁶ without modifying positional parameterization. Key Results All evaluations use lm-eval-harness with temperature 0.6, top-p=0.95, top-k=20, 32,768 max generation tokens, averaged over 5 runs. On RULER (128K context), average score improves from 64.5 to 74.8 (Qwen3-1.7B base → ours); the 128K-specific score improves from 11.0 to 44.5. On LongBench the model improves from 34.4 to 39.6; on ∞Bench from 6.6 to 22.7. Short-context benchmarks show preservation or slight gains: GSM8K 89.8→89.8, MATH-500 60.4→65.3, HumanEval 67.7→69.5. The final model is competitive with Llama-3.2-3B, Gemma-3-4B, and Qwen3-1.7B+YaRN while using a 1.7B parameter base. The dGRPO ablation (Figure 4) shows GRPO alone produces noisy reward curves and lower long-context accuracy, OPD produces stable but smaller gains, and dGRPO achieves the highest reward and best long-context performance. The β=0 (GRPO-only) case is strictly dominated across all β>0 settings tested. One notable finding: KD cold-start provides only marginal gains over SFT (e.g., RULER 28.4 vs 28.9) at higher computational cost, making SFT the preferred initialization. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD (dGRPO) + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (LONGBLOCKS). The combination of on-policy reward optimization with on-policy distillation in a single objective is genuinely novel for long-context post-training; individual components (GRPO from Shao et al. 2024, OPD from Agarwal et al. 2024) are established, and the integration follows a straightforward linear combination, but its application to the long-context stability problem and the systematic recipe are new contributions. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough and directly address the main design choices: β sweep, teacher source (external vs. self), SFT vs. KD cold start, and short/long data ratio. Each benchmark is run 5 times and standard deviations are reported in Appendix C.6; deviations are broadly comparable between the base model and the proposed method, confirming the training procedure does not inflate variance. LONGBLOCKS data generation prompts are fully reproduced in Appendix A. The LM-judge prompt for reward computation is shown in Appendix B. Methodological concern: the LM-judge reward (Qwen3-4B) is the same model family as the base model (Qwen3), and LONGBLOCKS is generated by Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Thinking and verified by Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Thinking — creating potential in-family circular quality validation. Code and trained models are stated to be available on Hugging Face (no explicit URL provided in the paper text, but footnote 1 indicates Hugging Face). This is a preprint under review.
