Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: The full pipeline runs on consumer-accessible GPU hardware; the base model is 1.7B parameters and the teacher (Qwen3-32B) is available commercially, though RL training at 128K context requires significant GPU memory not quantified in the paper. - Efficient: dGRPO adds a teacher forward pass per training step versus plain GRPO; the relative compute overhead is not reported, which is a missing practical detail. - Effective: The evidence shows genuine long-context capability gains (RULER 128K: 11.0→44.5) on held-out benchmarks with distinct task formats, not just the training distribution; the LM-judge reward for open-ended tasks introduces some Goodhart risk that is not analyzed. - Scalable: The paper provides no evidence beyond the 1.7B scale; the claim that the recipe generalizes to larger models is asserted but untested. The paper delivers a trained model and dataset (Hugging Face), not a general-purpose library. Strengths - The dGRPO objective is well-motivated by a precise diagnosis of what GRPO and OPD each lack, and the combination is principled rather than ad hoc. - The RULER 128K result (11.0→44.5) is a dramatic capability jump on a held-out diagnostic benchmark specifically designed to test long-context retention and reasoning. - Ablations are unusually complete for a preprint: β sensitivity, teacher quality, initialization strategy, and data mix ratio are each independently evaluated with appropriate controls. - The short/long-context frontier visualization (Figure 3) cleanly isolates the contribution of each training stage and provides a compelling comparison against external baselines. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors do not include an explicit limitations section, but they acknowledge in the results that very long-context tasks (>100K tokens) remain difficult, particularly for a 1.7B model (∞Bench scores remain low despite improvement). They note the β hyperparameter is task-dependent, citing prior work. They do not acknowledge scale generalizability as a limitation, which a reviewer would flag. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The entire study is conducted at 1.7B parameters; there is no evidence the recipe transfers to 7B+ models where sparse-reward dynamics and teacher-capacity gaps may behave differently. - The LM-judge reward for long-context tasks (Qwen3-4B evaluating Qwen3-1.7B outputs) is an in-family evaluation; reward hacking or systematic bias cannot be ruled out without cross-family judge evaluation. - LONGBLOCKS is generated and quality-verified exclusively by Qwen3-family models, creating a potential distribution-narrow training set that may inflate Qwen3-family model evaluations. - Retrieve.KV on ∞Bench scores 0.0 for both the baseline and the proposed model, suggesting a systematic failure mode not discussed. - Compute cost of dGRPO relative to GRPO (teacher forward pass overhead) is not reported, which is relevant to practitioners deciding between methods. - No analysis of catastrophic forgetting dynamics during RL at long context beyond the aggregate short-context accuracy metric. Bottom Line Practitioners doing long-context post-training on sub-3B models will find a concrete, well-ablated recipe with substantial empirical support; the single-scale limitation (1.7B only) means the recipe should be validated before applying it to larger models.
Recommended 2605.12227 PDF
#63 — Innovative (67)

Hölder Policy Optimisation (15 ▲ )

Yuxiang Chen, Dingli Liang, Yihang Chen, Ziqin Gong, Chenyang Le, Zhaokai Wang, Jiachen Zhu, Lingyu Yang, Jianghao Lin, Weinan Zhang, Jun Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 18 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces HölderPO, a generalized GRPO framework that replaces the fixed arithmetic-mean aggregation of token-level importance ratios with the Hölder mean parameterized by a continuous scalar p ∈ ℝ, instantiated with a dynamic linear annealing schedule that decays p from +2 to −2 during training. - Why it's important: GRPO's fixed aggregation operator creates an unresolvable concentration-stability trade-off — sparse-signal tasks (e.g., AIME) need high p to amplify rare critical tokens, while dense-signal tasks (e.g., MATH500) need low p to prevent high-variance gradient updates that cause training collapse, and no single fixed p can satisfy both. - How it does it: The Hölder mean (power mean) parameterizes a continuous spectrum from arithmetic (p=1) through geometric (p=0) to inverse-concentration (p<0), with gradient weights W_{i,t}(p) ∝ r_{i,t}^p that concentrate on high-ratio tokens for p>0 and low-ratio tokens for p<0, and the schedule exploits early signal amplification then late-stage variance contraction. - The good: Dynamic annealing achieves 54.9% average accuracy across five mathematical benchmarks on Qwen2.5-Math-7B (versus 51.2% GRPO and 54.2% concurrent PMPO) and 93.8% on ALFWorld, with results consistent across Qwen2.5-Math, R1-Distill-Qwen, and Qwen3 model families up to 8B parameters. - The missing: No error bars or multiple random seeds are reported anywhere; AIME24 has only 30 problems, making a 3.3% accuracy difference correspond to one problem and rendering individual benchmark comparisons statistically uninformative without variance estimates. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone working on GRPO-based post-training optimization — the Hölder mean unification is clean, the theory is substantive, and the empirical gains are consistent across architectures, but treat per-benchmark margins with appropriate skepticism.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile (per the deployability tag, despite substantial supporting theory). Novelty (75) and Non-Obviousness (75) are the top contributors — the Hölder-mean generalization cleanly subsumes GRPO/GMPO/GSPO as special cases, and the identification of the p<0 inverse-concentration regime (with MATH500 peaking at p=−1) is a genuine discovery prior work missed, reinforced by the temporal scheduling that distinguishes it from concurrent PMPO. Impact (70) is good via a drop-in, zero-overhead loss replacement with released code. The composite is capped below 75 almost entirely by Rigor (58) and Resilience (58): there are no error bars, seeds, or confidence intervals anywhere, and AIME24's 30-problem test set makes the headline per-benchmark margins equal to a single problem — the core theoretical guarantee (Theorem 3) also rests on an unverified pre-saturation premise. The explicit, directly-confronted limitations section is a Completeness (68) positive. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*58 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*58 + 0.10*70 = 67.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 68 Resilience 58 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation In GRPO-style reinforcement learning post-training for LLMs, mapping trajectory-level advantages to policy updates requires aggregating token-level importance ratios r_{i,t} = π_θ(y_{i,t}|x,y_{<t}) / π_{θ_old}(y_{i,t}|x,y_{<t}) within each sequence into a scalar surrogate ρ_i. Standard GRPO uses the arithmetic mean; GMPO and GSPO use the geometric mean. These static choices impose a fundamental concentration-stability trade-off. On sparse-signal tasks such as AIME, where correct reasoning concentrates in a few rare high-magnitude tokens, the arithmetic mean dilutes those gradients across hundreds of unremarkable tokens; on dense-signal tasks such as MATH500, a high-concentration mean over-amplifies minor token-level discrepancies, producing high-variance gradient updates that destabilize training. No fixed aggregation operator resolves both failure modes simultaneously, yet all existing methods lock in a static choice. Key Idea Replace the fixed aggregation operator with the Hölder mean of order p ∈ ℝ: ρ_{i,p} = ((1/|y_i|) Σ r_{i,t}^p)^{1/p}, which recovers arithmetic mean at p=1, geometric mean at p→0, and introduces an unexplored inverse-concentration regime at p<0. The per-token gradient weights become W_{i,t}(p) = r_{i,t}^p / Σ_k r_{i,k}^p — a softmax over log-importance-ratios scaled by p — so varying p does not change gradient directions but continuously deforms the weight distribution from concentrated on maximum-ratio tokens (p→+∞) through uniform (p=0) to concentrated on minimum-ratio tokens (p→−∞). Because the optimal p is task- and stage-dependent, a monotonically decaying schedule from p_high to p_low during training captures early-phase signal amplification followed by late-phase variance contraction. Methodology The HölderPO objective replaces GRPO's arithmetic aggregation with ρ_{i,p}(θ) and applies PPO-style clipping at the sequence level (not token level): J_{Hs}(θ) = E[(1/G) Σ_i min(ρ_{i,p} Â_i, clip(ρ_{i,p}, 1−ε, 1+ε) Â_i)]. The gradient decomposes as ∇_θ ρ_{i,p} = ρ_{i,p} Σ_t W_{i,t}(p) ∇_θ log π_θ(y_{i,t}|·), a weighted sum of token-level log-gradients. Three formal results characterize the framework: Theorem 1 proves Shannon entropy of W^p is maximized at p=0 and strictly decreases as |p| increases; Theorem 2 proves the gradient variance bound E[Â_i^2 ρ_{i,p}^2] is monotonically increasing in p for all p ∈ ℝ; Theorem 3 proves a dynamic schedule [p_high → p_low] strictly outperforms any fixed p_stat on both early-phase signal amplification and late-phase variance contraction. Implementation is a single-line modification of GRPO evaluated in log-space for numerical stability. Three design decisions are well-justified: (1) sequence-level clipping is mathematically required to preserve the monotonic variance bound — token-level clipping breaks the algebraic correspondence between outer Hölder multiplier and inner weights, which Appendix D confirms empirically; (2) extending p to the full real line identifies p<0 as qualitatively distinct from prior work covering only p∈[0,1]; (3) temporal adaptation across training steps (rather than per-trajectory) enables complementary roles for the two regimes. Training follows the Dr.GRPO recipe for math (8,523 problems, 8 rollouts per prompt, 4×H100s) and GiGPO protocol for ALFWorld. Key Results On Qwen2.5-Math-7B across five benchmarks (AIME24, AMC, MATH500, Minerva, OlympiadBench): dynamic schedule (2→−2) achieves 54.9% average versus GRPO 51.2%, GMPO 52.7%, and concurrent PMPO 54.2%. Static ablation confirms AIME24 peaks at p=3 (46.7% vs. GRPO 40.0%) and MATH500 peaks at p=−1 (85.0% vs. GRPO 83.4%). On R1-Distill-Qwen-7B, HölderPO reaches 66.4% average versus GRPO 59.3%, Dr.GRPO 61.5%, GMPO 63.4%, PMPO 64.6%. On Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B, gains over the strongest baseline DAPO are +4.5 and +4.3 points respectively. On ALFWorld with Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct, the 1→−1 schedule achieves 93.8% average versus GiGPO 86.7%, GMPO 85.9%, GRPO 72.8%. The schedule-shape ablation (Table 5) tests linear, square, cube, and sinusoidal in both ascending and descending directions; descending linear wins. Baselines are current and competitive. However, AIME24 contains 30 problems, so 1 correct answer = 3.3% accuracy; no confidence intervals are reported anywhere. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The contribution is genuinely novel in two respects: (i) extending the GRPO token-aggregation operator to the full Hölder mean family with p ∈ ℝ, identifying p<0 as a qualitatively distinct regime unexplored by prior work; and (ii) adapting p along the temporal axis of training (rather than per-trajectory), enabling early-stage signal amplification followed by late-stage variance contraction. Concurrent PMPO (Zhao et al., 2026) covers p∈[0,1] with per-trajectory adaptation — both the extension to negative p and the temporal scheduling are genuinely new. Recovering GRPO, GMPO, and GSPO as special cases (p=1, p→0, p→0 with sequence-level clip) is a standard generalization move applied carefully. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are substantive: static p sweep validates the concentration-stability trade-off prediction; token-level vs. sequence-level clipping is directly compared; schedule shapes are exhaustively swept; results are replicated across five model architectures (1.5B, 4B, 7B, 8B, R1-distill). Formal proofs for all six theorems are provided in appendices. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/YihangChen9/HolderPO. The principal methodological concern is that no error bars, confidence intervals, or multi-seed averages are reported anywhere. AIME24's 30-problem test set means a 3.3% difference corresponds to one problem, making the headline comparison (HölderPO 43.3% vs. GRPO 40.0% on this benchmark, or vs. PMPO 54.2% vs. 54.9% on average) statistically uninformative without variance estimation. The approximate gradient orthogonality assumption (Assumption 1) used in Theorem 7 is motivated by dimensionality arguments but never empirically verified for the tested architectures. This is a preprint; peer review has not occurred. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Trains on 4×H100 GPUs under the same protocol as GRPO; the algorithmic change is a single-line modification to the loss computation with no additional rollouts or value model. - Efficient: Zero additional compute overhead versus GRPO; log-space implementation handles numerical stability for large |p| at no cost. - Effective: Strong consistent evidence across 5 benchmark types and 5 model families (math and agentic); the static ablation validates the theoretical concentration-stability hypothesis, not just aggregate performance. - Scalable: Tested from 1.5B to 8B parameters; no experiments beyond 8B. The method's per-step cost is identical to GRPO at any scale, but behavior of optimal [p_high, p_low] at larger scales (70B+) is uncharacterized. The work delivers a software artifact (code on GitHub) and a reusable algorithmic component (drop-in loss replacement for GRPO). Strengths - The Hölder mean formulation is a minimal, theoretically clean generalization that subsumes GRPO, GMPO, and GSPO as special cases with provable concentration and variance properties — the framework is not an ad-hoc heuristic. - Identifies the qualitatively new p<0 inverse-concentration regime and empirically demonstrates it improves dense-signal tasks (MATH500 peaks at p=−1 rather than at the geometric mean p→0), which is a nontrivial finding prior work missed. - Theorem 3 provides a quantitative formal argument for why any fixed p is suboptimal for long-horizon tasks, directly motivating the dynamic schedule — this is a stronger theoretical justification than most engineering-paper motivation sections. - Consistent gains across Qwen2.5-Math, R1-Distill-Qwen, and Qwen3 architectures (Tables 2, 6, 7) reduce the risk that results are architecture-specific. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly identify two limitations in Section 5: (1) the schedule introduces three hyperparameters (p_high, p_low, decay shape) requiring per-task empirical tuning with no theoretical guidance on optimal shape beyond the linear default; (2) the positive-p concentration regime amplifies reward hacking when the verifier provides false-positive signals, since incorrect reasoning steps with high importance ratios receive disproportionate gradient weight. Both limitations are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The absence of error bars is a serious omission. AIME24's 30-problem test set and the reported 1-problem differences between baselines make individual-benchmark comparisons uninterpretable without confidence intervals. The core claim that dynamic HölderPO (43.3% on AIME24) outperforms GRPO (40.0%) reduces to "1 more correct answer." - The pre-saturation condition required for Theorem 3's exponential amplification bound (r_{i,t*}^{p_high} ≪ n−1) is argued informally to hold in early training but never measured on actual training trajectories — the paper's key theoretical guarantee rests on an unverified empirical premise. - All experiments use Qwen-family or DeepSeek-distill base models — all strong math-pretrained models. The concentration-stability hypothesis may behave differently on general-purpose base models (Llama, Mistral) without domain pretraining, which the ALFWorld results hint at (conservative schedule needed) but do not systematically study. - The comparison to PMPO is methodologically incomplete: PMPO adapts p per-trajectory while HölderPO adapts per training step, making them complementary rather than directly competing; combining both approaches is noted as orthogonal but never evaluated. - Endpoint selection guidance reduces to "sweep and report what worked" — for practitioners, this means an additional hyperparameter search on top of the existing GRPO hyperparameter space. Bottom Line Researchers building on GRPO for LLM post-training should read this paper — the Hölder mean unification is theoretically sound, the p<0 regime is a genuine discovery, and the dynamic schedule delivers consistent gains across architectures, but the lack of confidence intervals on small test sets means the headline margins should be treated as directional rather than definitive.
Recommended 2605.12058 PDF
#64 — Innovative (67)

GIFT: Guided Fine-Tuning and Transfer for Enhancing Instruction-Tuned Language Models

Zhiwen Ruan, Yichao Du, Jianjie Zheng, Longyue Wang, Yun Chen, Peng Li, Jinsong Su, Yang Liu, Guanhua Chen · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GIFT, a two-phase framework that uses per-token confidence scores from an instruction-tuned model to reweight the cross-entropy loss during LoRA adapter training on the pretrained base model, then merges the adapter back into the instruction model. - Why it's important: Directly fine-tuning instruction-tuned LLMs risks disrupting their alignment; existing base-model-then-merge approaches treat the instruction model as a passive post-hoc target, ignoring the useful calibration signal it could provide during training. - How it does it: A single offline forward pass through the instruction model computes token-level confidence scores q_t, which multiplicatively reweight each token's contribution to the standard cross-entropy loss, concentrating gradient signal on instruction-consistent tokens and suppressing updates on uncertain ones. - The good: GIFT consistently outperforms all baselines across four model families (Llama 3.1-8B, Llama 3.2-3B, Qwen2.5-7B, DeepSeek-Math-7B) and across model scales from 0.5B to 32B, with the improvement persisting under test-time scaling (Pass@N), indicating the gains reflect genuine capability acquisition rather than single-sample artifacts. - The missing: No comparison against knowledge distillation baselines, which is the most closely related idea and the strongest methodological challenger to GIFT's confidence-weighting scheme. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners adapting instruction-tuned open-source LLMs to specialized domains, as the method is lightweight, model-agnostic, and well-ablated, though the core idea is incremental relative to the merge-and-transfer literature.
Why this rank: Empirical profile, the strongest paper in the cluster. Rigor (73) drives the score: evaluation breadth across four model families, scales from 0.5B to 32B, two domains, plus the GIFT-BaseT control that cleanly pins instruction-model confidence as the causal factor, is rarely seen in merge-and-transfer work; code and full hyperparameters are released. Completeness (67) and Impact (70) are helped by honest limitation framing, zero added inference cost, and a drop-in design. The composite is held below 75 by Novelty (63) — the contribution is an incremental integration of known ideas within the established merge-and-transfer paradigm — and by the missing knowledge-distillation baseline plus non-standard Average@16 metric, which leave the fundamental advantage over distribution-matching unestablished and cap Resilience (64).
empirical Novelty 63 Rigor 73 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 67 Resilience 64 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Instruction-tuned LLMs such as Qwen and Llama encode a carefully balanced set of capabilities—instruction following, reasoning, robustness—acquired through expensive post-training pipelines. Practitioners who want to specialize these models for tasks like mathematical reasoning or medical QA face a dilemma: direct fine-tuning on the instruction model degrades alignment under limited supervision and stochastic decoding, while the dominant alternative (fine-tune the base model, merge the adapter into the instruction model) treats the instruction model purely as a merge target, ignoring the rich per-token alignment signal it already encodes. Existing merge-based methods (Shadow-FT, Chat Vector, Re-Adapt, LoRE-Adapt) all share this passivity, and the adapter is therefore trained to minimize loss uniformly over all tokens—including stylistic fillers and uncertain predictions that are misaligned with the instruction model's representation space. Key Idea: The instruction-tuned model's per-token predictive confidence q_t = p_{θ_I}(y_t | x, y_{<t}) is a reliable proxy for which tokens carry instruction-aligned, task-critical content. Multiplying each token's cross-entropy loss by q_t before summing concentrates the adapter's gradient signal on high-confidence, instruction-consistent tokens and suppresses gradient from uncertain tokens that would push the base model into regions incompatible with the instruction model's parameter space. Methodology: GIFT operates in two phases. Phase 1 (Data Annotation and Guided Fine-Tuning): the instruction model θ_I is run once over the training set D = {(x,y)} to compute token-level scalar scores q_t ∈ [0,1] for every response token (single forward pass, no gradient). These scores annotate the dataset as (x, y, q), yielding the GIFT loss L_GIFT(φ) = E_{(x,y)~D}[Σ_t q_t · ℓ_t(φ)], where ℓ_t(φ) = -log p_{θ_B,φ}(y_t | x, y_{<t}) is the per-token cross-entropy of the base model with adapter φ. No temperature scaling, clipping, or normalization is applied to q_t. Phase 2 (Transfer and Merge): the trained adapter φ is merged into the instruction model via standard LoRA merging: θ'_I = θ_I + M(φ). Critical design decisions: (1) offline annotation is done once, keeping fine-tuning cost identical to standard SFT; (2) q_t acts as a multiplicative weight, not a distillation target—GIFT does not minimize KL divergence between student and teacher distributions; (3) the paper uses standard LoRA merging to isolate the effect of guided fine-tuning from post-hoc merging improvements (TIES, DARE are noted as orthogonal and complementary). Training uses AdamW for 1 epoch, batch size 256, learning rate 2×10⁻⁴, LoRA rank 64, α=128, dropout 0.05. Key Results: On mathematical reasoning benchmarks (Math500, Minerva Math, OlympiadBench, AIME 2024, AMC 2023), evaluated at Average@16 with temperature 1.0: GIFT improves Llama3.1-8B-Instruct by +5.2 points average (16.8→22.0), Llama3.2-3B-Instruct by +3.0 (16.5→19.5), Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct by +1.6 (41.3→42.9), and DeepSeek-Math-7B-Instruct by +2.9 (16.8→19.7), consistently surpassing Shadow-FT, Re-Adapt, LoRE-Adapt, and direct instruction-model SFT. On medical QA (MedQA, MMLU-medical, MedMCQA), GIFT improves Llama3.1-8B-Instruct from 62.6 to 68.8 (+6.2). On general capabilities (MMLU and IFEval), GIFT matches or slightly exceeds the instruction baseline, indicating no generalization regression. Pass@N curves on Llama3.1-8B show GIFT dominates at every N from 1 to 10 across all five math benchmarks, and the advantage widens on harder benchmarks (OlympiadBench, AIME 2024). Model size scaling on Qwen2.5 (0.5B to 32B) shows GIFT consistently wins; Shadow-FT degrades on smaller models (0.5B average: Shadow-FT 2.8 vs. GIFT 8.3 vs. Instruct 7.9). On Qwen3-8B (Appendix), GIFT achieves 53.0 average vs. 51.8 for the baseline and 49.8 for Shadow-FT. The ablation (Table 3, Qwen2.5-7B) shows SFT+Merge drops instruction baseline by 3.3 points, GIFT-BaseT (using base model as teacher) recovers to ~41.0 (≈baseline) but does not improve, and full GIFT achieves 42.9, confirming the instruction-aligned guidance is the active ingredient. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The core contribution is instruction-guided token-importance weighting during base-model adapter training, applied within the established merge-and-transfer paradigm. What is genuinely novel is the use of instruction-model confidence as a training-time signal rather than a post-hoc merging signal, and the empirical demonstration that this redistribution of gradient signal is the causal mechanism behind improved merge compatibility. The broader pipeline (LoRA on base model → merge into instruction model) and the per-token confidence idea (related to selective token fine-tuning and confidence-based data filtering) each have prior art; GIFT's novelty is their specific integration. Rigor & Reproducibility: The ablation in Table 3 is well-designed and isolates the specific contribution of instruction-model guidance (vs. base-model guidance and vs. unguided merging), directly addressing the central claim. Evaluation uses Average@16 with stochastic decoding, which captures test-time scaling behavior but makes direct comparison to works using greedy decoding non-trivial. No error bars or statistical significance tests are reported across any table. The learning signal redistribution analysis (Figure 3, Section 5.5) provides mechanistic evidence covering 500k tokens. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/sustech-nlp/gift. Training hyperparameters are fully specified. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.01256v1, 2 May 2026); results have not been peer-reviewed. One mild Goodhart concern: the NuminaMath-CoT training set was designed for math reasoning, and several evaluation benchmarks (Math500, AMC) overlap thematically, though they are held-out test sets. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile): - Practical: The offline annotation step runs on a single RTX 4090 24GB GPU in ~2 minutes for 2,000 training examples (Table 7); the LoRA fine-tuning itself uses standard hardware at the scale of 7–8B models, making the full pipeline accessible on commercially available hardware. - Efficient: The annotation overhead produces a single 11–12 MB augmented dataset file; fine-tuning cost is identical to baseline SFT because q_t is precomputed and stored, and LoRA rank/hyperparameters match the baselines exactly—there is no additional inference-time cost. - Effective: The evidence shows consistent improvement over strong merge-based baselines on held-out test sets across two domains and four model families; the ablation confirms that instruction-aligned guidance (not generic token reweighting) is the causal factor. - Scalable: Tested from 0.5B to 32B parameters (Qwen2.5 family) with consistent gains; the annotation cost scales linearly with dataset size, which the authors acknowledge as a limitation for very large datasets but do not quantify beyond the 2,000-sample setting. No experiments on datasets larger than 10,000 samples are reported. The paper delivers a code release but no trained model weights. Strengths: - The mechanistic analysis (Figure 3, Section 5.5) demonstrating that GIFT shifts gradient energy from low-confidence to high-confidence tokens (from 79.7% to 29.6% originating from low-confidence regions under Shadow-FT vs. GIFT) provides an empirical explanation for the method's behavior beyond performance tables. - Coverage across four model families, six benchmarks, two domains, and six model scales (0.5B–32B) gives the evaluation breadth rarely seen in merge-based fine-tuning papers. - The GIFT-BaseT ablation is a clean control that rules out generic token reweighting as the explanation and pinpoints instruction-model confidence specifically as the active signal. - Offline precomputation design is a principled engineering choice that keeps fine-tuning cost identical to the SFT baseline, removing a common objection to guidance-based methods. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note (1) the offline annotation step introduces additional preprocessing cost that scales with dataset size, and (2) the paper restricts to standard LoRA merging for clarity, leaving combinations with advanced merging techniques (TIES, Fisher-weighted averaging) as future work. These are stated directly in the Limitations section and are credibly framed. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) No comparison against knowledge distillation — specifically sequence-level or token-level KD where the student (base model + adapter) is trained to match the instruction model's output distribution. This is the most natural methodological competitor and its omission makes it impossible to know whether GIFT's confidence weighting is superior to, equivalent to, or just a computationally cheaper approximation of KD. (2) The Average@16 metric with temperature 1.0 is non-standard; most prior work reports greedy or pass@1, making cross-paper comparisons difficult and potentially inflating apparent gains by rewarding diversity as well as correctness. (3) The annotation cost profiling (Table 7) covers only 2,000 samples; the 10,000-sample MedMCQA experiment's annotation cost is not reported, and large-dataset scalability (e.g., 100K+ samples) is uncharacterized. (4) Evaluations outside math and medical are limited to a small summarization experiment (Table 5, 800 test instances across 8 tasks) and MMLU/IFEval as preservation checks; the method's effectiveness on code generation, commonsense reasoning, or open-ended generation is unknown. (5) Sensitivity to LoRA rank and the interaction between rank and guidance quality are not ablated. Bottom Line: GIFT delivers a well-motivated, cleanly executed improvement to the merge-and-transfer paradigm for instruction-tuned LLMs — practitioners who routinely adapt instruction models via Shadow-FT or Re-Adapt will find a drop-in upgrade with consistent gains and no inference overhead, though the missing KD baseline leaves the method's fundamental advantage over distribution-matching approaches unestablished.
Recommended 2605.01256 PDF
#65 — Innovative (67)

CANTANTE: Optimizing Agentic Systems via Contrastive Credit Attribution

Tom Zehle · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CANTANTE, a framework that decomposes system-level rewards in LLM-based multi-agent systems into per-agent update signals by contrasting K parallel rollouts of different joint agent configurations on the same query, instantiated here for automated prompt optimization. - Why it's important: Manual prompt tuning of multi-agent systems grows combinatorially with agent count, and existing automated optimizers (MIPROv2, GEPA) naively route global scores to individual agents without solving the structural credit-assignment mismatch between local parameters and global evaluation feedback. - How it does it: A prompted LLM attributer receives groups of trajectories from K joint configurations run on the same query, assigns scalar credits in [−1, +1] per agent per rollout based on behavioral differences and outcome correlation across the group, and forwards those credits to each agent's local optimizer. - The good: On MBPP and GSM8K, CANTANTE improves over the strongest baseline by +18.9 pp and +12.5 pp respectively while simultaneously consuming fewer evaluation-time tokens than both baselines and the unoptimized prompts, ruling out the explanation that gains come from longer reasoning traces. - The missing: All three benchmarks use topologies of at most four agents; scalability to larger systems is claimed but not tested, and HotpotQA performance (11.93%) is so low across all methods that the benchmark adds little discriminative signal. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building 2–4-agent LLM pipelines who want a principled, modular alternative to global-score propagation for automated prompt tuning; the clean identity-attributer ablation and per-agent credit correlation analysis make it worth the read even if the HotpotQA results are underwhelming.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (70) and Non-Obviousness (70) drive the score: the contrastive in-group attribution insight is genuinely new, and the equal-budget identity-attributer ablation cleanly isolates the attribution mechanism from the underlying CAPO optimizer rather than conflating them. The credit-correlation recency-bias finding and the broad ablation suite (group size, alt optimizer, alt attributer, dataset size) are above community norm. Resilience (60) is the limiter — only 3 seeds with ±14-18pp baseline variance, ≤4-agent topologies, and an unexplained HotpotQA collapse — keeping it in the Innovative rather than Highly-Innovative band.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 60 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM-based multi-agent systems are increasingly deployed for complex tasks—autonomous software engineering, ML pipeline automation, multi-hop retrieval—but configuring them requires hand-authored prompts per agent and workflow design that grows combinatorially with system size. Automated configuration is the natural solution, but it faces a structural mismatch: the evaluation signal is a single scalar at the system level (did the pipeline produce the right answer?), while the parameters governing behavior are local to each agent. Existing automated approaches either treat the joint prompt configuration as a single monolithic parameter (MIPROv2) or optimize one agent at a time while holding others fixed (GEPA round-robin), both of which propagate global scores as if each agent bore equal responsibility for the outcome—a known failure mode when agents specialize and errors propagate through the pipeline. Key Idea: The core insight is that relative attribution within a comparison group is tractable where absolute attribution is not: by executing K different joint configurations on the same query and comparing the resulting trajectories contrastively, an attribution model can identify which agent's parameterization correlated with better outcomes without requiring knowledge of what optimal agent behavior looks like in isolation. This adapts the counterfactual intuition from COMA (multi-agent RL) to the LLM prompt-optimization setting, but without requiring credits to decompose the global score additively (unlike Shapley-based methods such as HiveMind). Methodology: CANTANTE formalizes multi-agent optimization as maximizing expected system score over a query set subject to a token budget, treating workflow topology as fixed. Each optimization iteration proceeds in four stages: (1) each of N local optimizers independently proposes K candidate parameterizations; these are assembled into K joint configurations by pairing one candidate per agent. (2) Each joint configuration is executed on the task, producing a system score s_i and a trajectory ξ_i (ordered sequence of per-agent outputs). (3) The K configurations are randomly partitioned into comparison groups of size g (default g=3); for each group, a prompted LLM attributer receives the query, per-agent outputs extracted from each trajectory, and system scores, then outputs a scalar credit c_{q,a,i} ∈ [−1, +1] per agent per configuration per query; credits are averaged over the query set to produce r_{a,i}. (4) Each local optimizer receives the (parameterization, credit) pairs and proposes updated candidates. The paper instantiates CANTANTE with CAPO as the default local optimizer. The attributer is GPT-OSS-120B; task agents are Qwen3-30B-A3B (30B total, 3B active—a mixture-of-experts model). The attributer prompt includes a few-shot example covering three archetypal scenarios (correct execution, early-agent failure, late-agent formatting error). Three critical design decisions: (a) K rollouts instead of K^N cross-combinations avoids exponential evaluation cost; (b) scalar credits in a signed range rather than ranks allow the attributer to express both contribution magnitude and direction; (c) group size g=3 is empirically validated—g=2 causes a 54.4 pp accuracy collapse on GSM8K due to high per-query credit variance with only a single contrastive pair. Key Results: Results are reported as mean ± std over three seeds (7, 42, 47) on held-out test sets of 500 instances, with Bessel's correction. MBPP (code generation, 500 test instances): CANTANTE 41.89 ± 7.56%, GEPA 22.96 ± 18.30%, MIPROv2 18.42 ± 14.13%. CANTANTE beats the strongest baseline by +18.93 pp with substantially lower variance (±7.56 vs. ±14–18 pp). Notably, MIPROv2 received an extended token budget (up to 15.8M instead of 10M) on two seeds where it failed to converge—favoring the baseline in the comparison. GSM8K (math reasoning, 500 test instances): CANTANTE 82.33 ± 4.35%, GEPA 61.27 ± 2.66%, MIPROv2 69.80 ± 7.10%. +12.53 pp over MIPROv2. HotpotQA (multi-hop QA, 500 test instances): CANTANTE 11.93 ± 5.06%, GEPA 10.93 ± 3.91%, MIPROv2 14.20 ± 5.72%. Differences across all methods are within one standard deviation; all methods perform poorly in absolute terms. Identity-attributer ablation (GSM8K, seed 42): removing contrastive attribution and routing global scores directly to the local optimizer drops accuracy from 84.0% to 70.6% (equal steps, −13.4 pp) or 79.6% (equal budget, −4.4 pp). Even with the extended budget, CANTANTE retains a 4.4 pp advantage, isolating the attribution mechanism—not just the CAPO optimizer—as the primary driver. Evaluation-time token cost: on MBPP and GSM8K, CANTANTE uses 1.99k and 1.74k tokens/query, below both baselines and unoptimized prompts. On HotpotQA it uses 2.28k, above baselines and initial (1.17k), suggesting inefficient prompting in a bottlenecked pipeline. Alternative local optimizer (EvoPrompt instead of CAPO): +3.2 pp on GSM8K seed 42, confirming the framework generalizes beyond CAPO. Dataset size reduction: halving or quartering the development set costs only 2.8–3.4 pp on GSM8K, indicating robustness to low-data regimes. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The contrastive in-group attribution mechanism applied to LLM-based MAS optimization is the primary contribution. The framework is genuinely novel in that it: (a) applies contrastive trajectory comparison across joint configurations (rather than per-agent counterfactuals) to a prompt optimization setting; (b) decouples attribution from the choice of local optimizer, enabling plug-in compatibility; (c) does not require credits to reconstruct the global score additively. The use of an LLM as an attributer is not novel (LLM-MCA does this for traditional RL agents), and the prompt optimization instantiation via CAPO is a direct reuse of prior work. The insight is genuinely new; the engineering is largely standard. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are thorough: identity attributer (equal steps and equal budget), alternative attributer prompt, alternative attributer model (Qwen3-Next-80B), alternative local optimizer (EvoPrompt), group size (g=2, g=5), and dataset size (75, 150 vs. 300 samples). The credit correlation analysis (Spearman ρ per agent per benchmark, per seed) provides a secondary validation that the attributer produces differentiated per-agent signals rather than echoing the global score. Three seeds is marginal given the high variance on MBPP (±14–18 pp for baselines); the aggregate rank metric is more reliable than individual benchmark comparisons under these conditions. The paper uses Bessel's correction appropriately. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/finitearth/cantante. Models are open-weight (Qwen3, GPT-OSS-120B) served via vLLM, enabling reproduction. A concern: initial prompts are auto-generated by GPT-OSS-120B, the same model used for attribution and optimization, meaning starting-point quality is entangled with the optimizer's model family; results with human-authored initial prompts are not reported. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on commodity NVIDIA L40 GPU clusters; ~130 GPU-hours for all main experiments and ablations; open-source implementation available; requires access to a large attribution model (GPT-OSS-120B class) which represents a non-trivial compute cost. - Efficient: Attribution and optimization meta-calls consume ~25% of the token budget (versus ~24% for GEPA and ~0.5% for MIPROv2); the overhead is substantial but the resulting optimized prompts achieve lower evaluation-time token cost on MBPP and GSM8K, so the investment amortizes at inference time. - Effective: Strong causal evidence on MBPP and GSM8K via identity-attributer ablation; ineffective on HotpotQA where the paper attributes failure to topological limitations without proving this claim experimentally. - Scalable: Tested on topologies of 2–4 agents; the authors explicitly acknowledge that scalability to substantially larger systems is empirically untested, and context-limit concerns with long multi-agent trajectories are noted but not resolved. Delivers: open-source software library with full prompt sets, ablation configurations, and pseudo-code (https://github.com/finitearth/cantante). Strengths: - The identity-attributer ablation with an equal-budget condition is methodologically rigorous: it rules out the confound that CANTANTE's gains are simply due to CAPO being allocated more effective optimization steps, not the attribution mechanism itself. - The credit correlation analysis revealing recency bias in the GSM8K ensemble (Executor 1 near-zero ρ, Executor 3 highest ρ despite identical roles) is a substantive empirical finding that extends beyond prompt optimization into MAS topology diagnosis—this is a genuine secondary contribution. - Decoupling attribution from the local optimizer is an important architectural decision: existing methods couple their credit assignment to built-in mutation operators (HiveMind, MAPRO), while CANTANTE's modular design enables any prompt optimizer to serve as a local optimizer, demonstrated empirically with both CAPO and EvoPrompt. - The few-shot ablation failure (−9.2 pp with bootstrapped exemplars) is reported honestly and analyzed with credit-correlation evidence, providing a practical warning about initialization quality that practitioners need. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) fixed topology—performance depends on quality of the initial workflow graph design; (2) attribution quality is bounded by the attributer model's capability; (3) attributer overhead consumes budget that could fund additional optimization steps; (4) growing trajectory length as agent count increases may degrade attribution quality or exceed context limits; (5) experiments cover only topologies of ≤4 agents. The authors confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them, which is a credibility-positive presentation choice. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The "topological bottleneck" explanation for HotpotQA is asserted but not tested: varying the HotpotQA topology or comparing against a known-strong RAG baseline on this benchmark would distinguish a CANTANTE limitation from a MAS design limitation. The 14.20% MIPROv2 peak on HotpotQA is far below what retrieval-augmented systems routinely achieve on this benchmark, suggesting the chosen MAS architecture may be fundamentally weak regardless of prompt quality. - The attributer model (GPT-OSS-120B) is substantially larger than the task model (Qwen3-30B-A3B with 3B active parameters). This is an asymmetric attribution regime; the Qwen3-Next-80B ablation partially addresses this but still uses a much larger model than the task agents. Results with a matched-scale or smaller attributer are not reported. - Three seeds with high variance on MBPP means individual seed comparisons carry wide confidence intervals. The average rank metric is the most defensible aggregate, but the paper would benefit from more seeds on the highest-variance benchmark. - The few-shot bootstrapping failure is acknowledged but no warmup strategy (e.g., using few-shot examples only after several optimization rounds) is evaluated, leaving practitioners without actionable guidance on when to enable few-shot prompting. - Exact-match scoring on HotpotQA is known to undercount correct answers due to surface-form variation; this may disproportionately penalize methods that produce correctly-reasoned but differently-phrased answers, though this would affect all methods equally and does not change the relative ranking. Bottom Line: CANTANTE makes a credible, well-ablated case that contrastive in-group attribution is strictly better than global-score propagation for automated prompt optimization in LLM multi-agent systems, with gains large enough to be meaningful even under high variance; practitioners building multi-agent pipelines with 2–4 agents and a scored evaluation function should read this paper and try the released code.
Citations 2605.13295 PDF
#66 — Innovative (67)

Mitigating Label Bias with Interpretable Rubric Embeddings

5harad.com · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces rubric embeddings — LLM-scored, expert-defined feature vectors — as a representation-level intervention that simultaneously reduces gender bias inherited from biased training labels and improves cohort quality in algorithmic selection tasks. - Why it's important: Algorithmic decision systems trained on historically biased proxy labels (e.g., admissions scores that favored male applicants) reproduce and amplify that bias, and existing mitigations — orthogonalization, redaction, marginalization — each have disqualifying failure modes that the paper characterizes formally. - How it does it: Replace black-box text embeddings with a 396-dimensional feature vector derived by scoring applicant materials against expert-designed rubric criteria using an LLM, restricting the model's hypothesis class to semantically meaningful, non-gendered dimensions so biased label signals cannot propagate through gender-predictive features. - The good: On 1,112 real graduate applications with synthetically injected gender bias (advantage parameter b ∈ [0, 2.5]), rubric embeddings achieve near-zero measured correspondence-experiment bias across the full range while producing admitted cohorts with higher mean true scores (≈16–17 vs. ≈14 for black-box at b=2.5) and better gender balance than all four comparators. - The missing: The entire empirical evaluation rests on a single domain (one public-policy master's program) and a single protected attribute (binary gender); no evaluation against race, age, or other bias vectors, and no ablation on rubric quality or rubric item count. - Read it or not: Essential for ML fairness researchers and practitioners building screening systems — the theoretical bias decomposition is clean, the empirical results are striking, and the legal framing (disparate treatment vs. disparate impact) is immediately practical.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Novelty (73) and Non-Obviousness (78) are the strengths: the formal decomposition of bias into omitted-variable and label-perturbation components (Props 2-3) and the observation that the two are indistinguishable in audit studies is a genuine, non-obvious analytical advance, and the fairness-quality co-improvement avoids the usual fairness-accuracy tradeoff. Rigor (62) is what holds the composite below the 75 band — strong statistics (nested CV, Rubin's rules, CIs, theory-matches-empirics) but no code or data release, GPT-5 rubric scores never validated against human scorers (a potential new bias source), and no ablation on rubric count or misspecification. Practical Impact (47) is low: only a methodological framework and prompts ship, and the labor-intensive rubric construction does not transfer cheaply, while Resilience (64) is dented by the circular treatment of admissions-officer scores as ground truth and single-domain/single-attribute evidence. Weighted: 0.20*73 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*64 + 0.10*47 = 67.
empirical Novelty 73 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 70 Resilience 64 Impact 47
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Statistical decision systems in hiring, admissions, and content moderation are typically trained on historical human evaluations rather than ground-truth outcomes, because true outcomes (e.g., job success) are unobserved or observed only for a selected, non-representative subset. These proxy labels frequently encode historical discrimination. Models trained on such labels inherit the bias, and the problem is compounded because standard black-box text embeddings encode sensitive attributes with near-perfect fidelity — ridge regression predicts binary gender from OpenAI text-embedding-3-large embeddings at AUC 99.6% ± 0.1%, and even after aggressive redaction (names, pronouns, titles removed), gender remains predictable at AUC 81.2% ± 1.4%. Existing mitigation strategies have significant practical limitations: orthogonalization enforces demographic parity and can penalize a genuinely more-qualified group; redaction fails to remove all gendered signals from black-box representations; marginalization corrects apparent algorithmic bias that may itself reflect representation deficiencies rather than label bias, thereby compounding the harm. Key Idea The core insight is that bias propagates through representations, not just labels: if the feature representation encodes protected attributes, any bias in the training signal — whether injected via biased labels or arising from omitted variables correlated with gender — flows into predictions. The fix is to restrict the hypothesis class upfront by replacing generic black-box embeddings with a representation constructed from only features that domain experts deem legitimately decision-relevant, preventing the model from exploiting gendered signals that are not substantively meaningful. Proposition 3 formalizes this: for a linear predictor, bias equals the dot product of the average representation shift under the gender manipulation with the OLS coefficient vector, meaning bias is nonzero precisely when features that shift with gender are also predictive of the outcome — exactly what rubric embeddings disrupt by excluding gender-predictive but substantively irrelevant features. Methodology The pipeline has four stages. (1) Dataset: 1,112 applications to a real public-policy master's program (2025–2026 cycle), each containing resumes, essays, letters of recommendation, and transcripts; admissions officer scores (4–20 pt scale) serve as ground truth Y; synthetic gender bias is injected as Y' = Y ± Z, Z ~ N(b, 1) for b ∈ [0, 2.5], enabling controlled study across bias magnitudes. (2) Representations: black-box embeddings use OpenAI text-embedding-3-large (first 250 dimensions per document type, ~1,000 total); rubric embeddings use 396 expert-defined items across essay quality (14 items), work history (8 items), letters of recommendation (39 items), and prior academic performance (335 items, including course-level GPA), scored by GPT-5 via zero-shot prompting. (3) Correspondence experiment: gender manipulation T(x,g) is implemented via an iterative LLM-based templating pipeline that wraps pronouns, titles, kinship terms, gendered organizations, and applicant names in structured markers, then instantiates male/female/neutral/original variants; country-matched names from historical applicants address non-Western naming patterns; validation and repair loops reduce uncompleted files to <0.005%. (4) Evaluation: ridge regression (glmnet), 10×10-fold nested cross-validation with 20 repetitions and Rubin's rules; top-20% selection policy evaluated on gender composition and mean true score Y. Critical design decisions: rubric developed through iterative engagement with admissions staff (justified but labor-intensive and non-scalable); 396 items are objective and demographic-free (well-justified for legal compliance); country-matched names in the correspondence experiment close a gap that generic name-swap studies leave open. Key Results Black-box embeddings encode gender with 91.2% ± 0.6% representation shift under gender manipulation and predict self-reported gender at AUC 99.6% ± 0.1%; even redacted materials yield AUC 81.2% ± 1.4%. Rubric embeddings reduce this to near zero. Bias of black-box models grows linearly with injected advantage b, reaching approximately 4.0 bias units at b=2.5 (on the 4–20 point score scale); rubric embedding models maintain bias ≈ 0 across the full range. Under a top-20% selection policy at b=2.5, black-box models admit effectively 0% women while rubric embedding models sustain approximately 38% women, close to the unbiased optimum. Mean true score of the admitted class degrades from ~16.5 to ~14 for black-box models as b rises, while rubric embeddings stay near ~16.5. These patterns hold across alternative quality metrics (mean GPA ≈ 3.65–3.70, mean GRE Quant ≈ 163, mean GRE Verbal ≈ 163 for rubric embeddings vs. degrading values for black-box and kitchen-sink models). Theoretical predictions from Propositions 2–3 match empirical estimates closely across all figures (Figs. 1, A2). The baseline bias at b=0 for black-box models is −0.67 against men — attributed to omitted variable bias, not label bias — and rubric embeddings exhibit approximately zero baseline bias. Marginalized black-box models, while formally unbiased, admit fewer women and lower-quality cohorts than rubric embeddings because they overcorrect for an apparent bias that reflects representation deficiencies rather than actual label bias. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. Concept bottleneck and rubric representations exist in the literature (Koh et al. 2020, Demirel et al. 2026), but their application as a label-bias mitigation strategy — with formal theoretical backing via correspondence experiments — is genuinely new. The theoretical decomposition of bias into baseline (omitted variable) and label-perturbation components (Propositions 2 and 3) is a formal analytical contribution; the non-obvious result that omitted variable bias and label bias are observationally indistinguishable in audit studies, and that rubric embeddings address both simultaneously, is the central intellectual advance. The audit study pipeline for rich multi-modal text (beyond name-swapping) is a methodological contribution to the social-science literature on correspondence experiments. Rigor & Reproducibility Error bars are reported (68% and 95% pointwise CIs across 20 repetitions with Rubin's rules). Theoretical predictions match empirical results closely in Figures 1 and A2, providing internal validation. Proofs for all propositions appear in Appendix A with stated regularity conditions. Prompt templates for the audit study LLM pipeline are reproduced in full (Appendix B, Prompts A1–A3). No code or dataset is released — the real application data cannot be released due to applicant privacy, and the rubric extraction code is not mentioned. The use of GPT-5 series for rubric scoring (reference [47]) creates reproducibility barriers: GPT-5 is a frontier model with API-only access and potential version drift. The paper evaluates bias across a synthetic injection range, which is appropriate but means all results are conditional on the assumed Gaussian perturbation model. There is no ablation on rubric item count (does a 50-item subset perform comparably?), no ablation by rubric category, and the GPT-5 scores are not validated against human rubric scorers for accuracy or bias. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Ridge regression runs on commodity hardware (4-core CPU); rubric scoring requires batch LLM API access to GPT-5, which is commercially available but costly and API-dependent. - Efficient: LLM scoring at 396 items × 1,112 applicants × 4 gender-variant documents represents a large inference workload whose cost is not reported; black-box embedding extraction ran in ~12 hours on CPU for the modeling step, but the full pipeline including LLM calls is unquantified. - Effective: The evidence shows rubric embeddings solve the stated problem — reducing gender bias while improving cohort quality — within the tested domain; the result is internally consistent and theoretically explained. - Scalable: Tested on 1,112 applicants from a single program; the method has not been evaluated at 10×–100× scale, and the rubric construction process (iterative engagement with domain staff over multiple sessions) does not transfer cheaply to new domains or protected attributes. The paper delivers a methodological framework and prompt templates; no software artifact or trained model is released. Strengths - The formal bias decomposition (Propositions 2 and 3) into omitted-variable and label-perturbation components is a genuine theoretical contribution that reframes the Amazon hiring-algorithm story more precisely and provides actionable guidance: reduce gender-predictiveness of the representation, not just the labels. - The correspondence experiment framework is implemented more carefully than typical name-swap studies — country-matched names, iterative LLM validation/repair for near-complete gender signal removal — addressing a real methodological gap. - Rubric embeddings simultaneously improve fairness and cohort quality, avoiding the canonical fairness-accuracy tradeoff that haunts most debiasing approaches; this result is both empirically demonstrated and theoretically explained via the causal DAG (rubric d-separates black-box features from true label). - The explicit connection to U.S. anti-discrimination law (disparate treatment vs. disparate impact) and the structured acknowledgment of which legal protections rubric embeddings do and do not provide is unusual and practically valuable for deployment decisions. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly and honestly confront three limitations: (1) rubric construction requires substantial domain expertise and iterative engagement with practitioners that cannot be fully automated; (2) rubric embeddings protect against disparate treatment (as operationalized via correspondence experiments) but not fully against disparate impact — label bias can still distort feature weights even when features are nominally group-neutral (e.g., if biased labels underweight public-service experience that female applicants disproportionately have); (3) excluding protected group membership from the rubric precludes affirmative-action-style interventions and may be inappropriate in medical contexts where group membership is clinically relevant. This honest framing is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single domain, single protected attribute: all experiments use one public-policy master's program and binary gender; the approach is not evaluated on race, age, disability, or intersectional categories, nor on hiring contexts where the rubric-true-label relationship may differ structurally — the generalization claim is unsupported by evidence. - Circularity in "ground truth": the paper treats admissions officer scores as Y and shows rubric embeddings better predict them, but if officer scores themselves embed gender bias (plausible), the entire framing of "better quality" is undermined; the authors acknowledge scores "may contain noise or bias" but use them as fixed reference without sensitivity analysis. - No ablation on rubric quality or misspecification: the paper does not test what happens with a poorly constructed rubric (one that inadvertently includes proxies for protected attributes, or omits key dimensions); the method's brittleness to rubric misspecification — a critical practical question — is entirely unexplored. - LLM rubric scoring introduces unvalidated bias: GPT-5 may systematically score applications from certain demographic groups differently on rubric dimensions, introducing a new bias source; this is not evaluated against human rubric scorers or across demographic subgroups. - Orthogonalized models are excluded from empirical comparison "given their theoretical limitations," but practitioners use them; omitting the empirical comparison prevents readers from gauging the practical magnitude of the theoretical limitation. Bottom Line This is a well-executed, honestly scoped paper with a clean theoretical framework and striking empirical results that establish representation choice — not just label correction — as a first-class intervention for bias mitigation; fairness and ML-for-decisions researchers should read it, with the caveat that single-domain, single-attribute evidence is insufficient to justify broad deployment claims.
Search Results
#67 — Innovative (67)

BAT: Target-Instance-Free Data Preparation Synthesis via LLM-Driven Tree Search

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces BAT, an end-to-end automatic data preparation framework that synthesizes multi-step relational table transformation pipelines from heterogeneous source tables to a target schema using only target metadata (no target data instances), via LLM-driven Monte Carlo Tree Search over a five-action symbolic sandbox. - Why it's important: In Data Mesh architectures governed by privacy regulations and data residency laws, source-side data providers are legally barred from accessing target table instances, rendering all existing ADP methods — which require example pairs, distributions, or training data from the target — inapplicable. - How it does it: Pipeline synthesis is cast as MCTS over five abstract actions (SchemaMapping, OperatorDiscovery, CodeSynthesis, CodeRefinement, Termination) with transition constraints that prune infeasible sequences; LLM instantiates action parameters at each node expansion, and a grounded reward is computed by actually executing the generated pipeline on source data and measuring schema overlap with the target. - The good: BAT-32B achieves 88.57% exact execution accuracy on the 481-task Auto-Pipeline benchmark versus 79.83% for the best baseline (ReAct), and 68.57% versus 55.45% (SQLMorpher) on the 105-task Smart Building benchmark — using only open-source Qwen2.5-Coder-32B. - The missing: Both evaluation benchmarks are small (481 and 105 tasks respectively) and no statistical significance testing is reported, so the magnitude of gains over strong baselines (ReAct, PoT) cannot be confidently bounded; additionally, the execution-aware reward checks only schema column-name overlap, not data correctness, so pipelines with correct column structure but wrong aggregation logic score full reward. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and practitioners in data integration, ETL automation, or LLM-driven code synthesis — the MCTS-over-abstract-actions pattern and execution-grounded reward are reusable ideas; practitioners facing privacy-constrained data preparation scenarios will find it directly applicable.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (75) leads — a peer-reviewed SIGMOD 2026 open-source system that solves a real, underserved metadata-only constraint with meaningful accuracy gains over the closest alternatives. Rigor (65) reflects comprehensive component-wise ablations across three LLM families and honest negative results, but is capped by tiny benchmarks (105/481 tasks) with no significance testing and reliance on API rather than the claimed local deployment. Completeness (65) and Resilience (60) are pulled down by the schema-overlap-only reward (which scores wrong-aggregation pipelines as correct), the selective 4.85× framing against the weakest baseline, and the privacy story weakened by required source-instance exposure. Weighted composite lands at 67.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Enterprise data integration increasingly operates in Data Mesh architectures where source-side providers submit transformation pipelines to a central warehouse. Privacy regulations (GDPR, data residency laws, zero-trust policies) prevent these providers from ever seeing the target table's actual rows — only its schema and column descriptions are visible. This "metadata-only" constraint disqualifies all established ADP paradigms: transform-by-example requires (source, target) pairs; transform-by-target methods (e.g., Auto-Pipeline) leverage functional dependencies or distributions from target data; supervised models (Auto-Tables, Auto-Prep) require ≥50% of target training data. The remaining challenge is combinatorial: source schemas are heterogeneous and evolving, the space of valid operator sequences is large, and LLM hallucinations on schema-specific tasks can produce syntactically plausible but semantically broken pipelines. A single mis-specified GroupBy key or missing Unpivot operator causes the output schema to diverge from the target, blocking downstream data ingestion. Key Idea: Lift the pipeline generation problem from choosing among atomic transformation operators (where the combinatorial space is large and LLMs hallucinate more) to choosing among five high-level symbolic actions — SchemaMapping, OperatorDiscovery, CodeSynthesis, CodeRefinement, Termination — and run MCTS over this abstracted action space. The critical enabler is the execution-aware reward: rather than asking the LLM to judge its own output (self-reward, which is unreliable), BAT actually executes the generated pipeline on the source data and checks whether the resulting table's column names and types match the target schema. This provides a deterministic, grounded signal that sidesteps both LLM hallucination and the absence of target instances. Methodology: BAT has three components. (1) DPAS (DP Action Sandbox): Defines five abstract action types with hard transition constraints derived from real-world workflow patterns (e.g., CodeRefinement must follow CodeSynthesis; Table 2). This reduces search tree branching from an open-ended operator set to at most five choices per level and tree depth bounded at five. (2) FPG (Fundamental Pipeline Generator): Standard MCTS (Selection via UCT, Expansion via LLM parameter instantiation per action type with structured prompts, Simulation to Termination, Backpropagation of reward). At the terminal node, the LLM self-evaluates the pipeline as r ∈ {0, 0.5, 1.0}. Rollout budget N=10, exploration constant c=1. (3) EPO (Execution-aware Pipeline Optimizer): Replaces LLM self-reward with schema similarity sim(T̂_r, T_r) = |Ŝ_r ∩ S_r| / |S_r| penalizing type mismatches; adds a simulation cache that memoizes LLM calls per (node, action_type) context to avoid redundant inference; and early termination when K=2 reward-1 pipelines are found. Design decisions: (a) abstract vs. atomic action space — well-justified by empirical 5× runtime reduction with negligible quality loss; (b) execution-aware vs. self vs. hybrid reward — empirically validated, with execution-aware dominating by 23.81 pp EX on Smart Building; (c) early termination threshold K=2 — justified by sensitivity analysis showing diminishing returns beyond K=2. Key Results: On Auto-Pipeline (481 tasks, avg. 3.4 source tables, 8.3 columns, 8,446 rows): BAT-32B 88.57% EX, 89.69% CS, 1.16 min versus best baseline ReAct at 79.83% EX, 84.40% CS, 0.10 min. On Smart Building (105 tasks, single-table, irregular schemas): BAT-32B 68.57% EX, 84.67% CS, 3.24 min versus SQLMorpher at 55.45% EX, 58.54% CS, 0.42 min. BAT-7B (47.19% EX on Auto-Pipeline) falls below ReAct and PoT, showing strong model-size dependence. With GPT-OSS-120B: BAT 91.27% EX versus ReAct 90.44% on Auto-Pipeline; BAT 85.71% versus ReAct 77.14% on Smart Building — larger gap on the harder benchmark. With DeepSeek-V3.2: BAT 93.14% versus ReAct 88.77% on Auto-Pipeline. Performance declines at pipeline length 9+ where Union operations dominate, where SQLMorpher is relatively stronger. FunctionCalling achieves only 18.27–19.61% EX, the weakest baseline. The "idealized" variant with target instances available for reward shows near-identical accuracy to the metadata-only BAT, empirically validating schema similarity as a sufficient proxy. Baselines are well-chosen contemporaries adapted fairly to the same operator set. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM. The formulation of ADP pipeline synthesis as MCTS over a five-action symbolic abstraction (rather than atomic operators), combined with execution-grounded reward in a target-instance-free setting, is genuinely novel as a combination. Individual components — MCTS, LLM-driven expansion, schema similarity, simulation caching — are established ideas from MCTS literature, program synthesis, and data integration. The paper's contribution is their principled integration for the specific constraint of metadata-only ADP, with the abstract action sandbox being the most original design element. Rigor & Reproducibility: Source code and prompts are available at https://github.com/ZJU-DAILY/BAT. Ablations cover all claimed components (DPAS constraints, EPO sub-components SC and ET individually, reward strategies, early termination threshold K, exploration constant c). Generalizability is tested across three LLM families (Qwen, GPT-OSS-120B, DeepSeek-V3.2). Error analysis by operator type is provided. However, no error bars or statistical significance tests are reported despite small benchmark sizes (105 tasks on Smart Building means a 1-pp EX difference equals ~1 pipeline). The EX metric uses ground-truth target instances at evaluation time only — the generation process never sees them, which is methodologically sound. One consistency concern: BAT is designed for local LLM deployment (a stated privacy benefit), but experiments use API calls due to "limitations in our experimental environment," which somewhat undermines the privacy argument. The hybrid reward experiment (Section 3.3 discussion) appropriately reports that it performs between self-reward and execution-aware reward, showing honest negative results. Deployability: SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Runs on open-source Qwen-Coder models; tested on a CPU-only Linux server (Intel Xeon Silver 4310, 128GB RAM); no GPU requirement for the pipeline framework itself, though LLM inference requires GPU or API access. - Efficient: BAT-32B completes in 1.16 min (Auto-Pipeline) and 3.24 min (Smart Building), 3–30× slower than fastest baselines (PoT at 0.09 min), but the authors argue this is acceptable for enterprise batch ETL schedules (hourly/daily). Simulation cache measurably reduces runtime by ~0.5 min. - Effective: Achieves 88.57% EX on Auto-Pipeline and 68.57% on Smart Building; performance degrades on noisy/ambiguous schemas (Smart Building) and long pipelines (length 9+); BAT-7B is weaker than several baselines, so the method requires at least a 14–32B model to be competitive. - Scalable: Tested on tables with 2–6+ source files and varying column widths; large models show stable accuracy across schema widths. No testing at enterprise row scales (millions of rows) or on tables with hundreds of columns. Row count is claimed irrelevant to LLM reasoning (only few-shot samples and schema are used), which is plausible but not experimentally verified at stress scale. - Delivers: Open-source Python system (github.com/ZJU-DAILY/BAT) with full prompt templates. Strengths: - The abstraction from atomic operators to a five-action symbolic sandbox is principled and well-justified: Table 6 shows removing transition constraints causes 3–5× runtime inflation with negligible quality gain, confirming that the constraints are effective pruning heuristics rather than restrictive limitations. - The execution-aware reward — running the pipeline on source data and checking schema overlap — is a simple, deterministic, and highly effective substitute for target-instance feedback; the near-zero gap to the idealized instance-aware variant (Figure 10) is a strong empirical validation. - The ablation depth is genuinely comprehensive: every claimed component is individually justified with numbers, and the paper does not overstate components that show marginal individual impact (SC removes ~0.2 pp EX but saves ~0.24 min). - Peer-reviewed at SIGMOD 2026 with open-source release, providing a reproducible baseline for the metadata-only ADP problem. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: Performance degrades on Smart Building's ambiguous and schema-noisy tasks; performance declines on long (length 9+) pipelines. BAT runtime exceeds baselines. Public benchmarks for metadata-only ADP are scarce. Experiments use API-based LLM access rather than true local deployment. The authors state a future plan for more complex and large-scale datasets and for incorporating user natural-language intent. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The execution-aware reward measures schema column-name and type overlap, not data content correctness — a pipeline that produces the right output schema via the wrong aggregation logic (e.g., sum instead of mean) scores r=1 and would be returned as correct. The EX metric at evaluation does catch this (it compares full output against ground truth), but the training signal does not. This gap may explain why Smart Building EX (68.57%) lags CS (84.67%) by ~16 pp — pipelines are producing correct-looking schemas more often than correct data. Additionally, the "target-instance-free" privacy framing is weakened by the fact that BAT requires source data instances (few-shot rows from source tables are explicit MCTS inputs); in strict zero-trust environments, even source row exposure could violate data residency constraints, and this is not discussed. The 4.85× EX improvement claim is computed against FunctionCalling (the weakest baseline at 18.27%/19.61%), not against ReAct or PoT, which is selective framing — the improvement over the strongest baseline is 8.74 pp. Finally, the benchmarks are small enough (especially Smart Building at 105 tasks) that the absence of confidence intervals makes it impossible to assess whether differences between, e.g., BAT-32B (68.57%) and SQLMorpher (55.45%) on Smart Building are statistically robust. Bottom Line: BAT is a well-executed systems paper solving a real and underserved constraint in enterprise data integration; practitioners who cannot access target data instances for privacy or compliance reasons gain a working, open-source solution with meaningful accuracy gains over the closest alternatives, making it worth reading for anyone in the data integration or LLM-driven program synthesis space.
Citations
#68 — Innovative (67)

You Only Need Minimal RLVR Training: Extrapolating LLMs via Rank-1 Trajectories (41 ▲ )

Zhepei Wei, Xinyu Zhu, Wei-Lin Chen, Chengsong Huang, Jiaxin Huang, Yu Meng · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 21 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces RELEX (REinforcement Learning EXtrapolation), a training-free method that predicts full RLVR-trained model checkpoints by fitting a rank-1 SVD subspace to early training dynamics and extrapolating future weights via closed-form linear regression. - Why it's important: RLVR training requires days of GPU compute and scales linearly with training steps; cutting 80–85% of that cost without sacrificing accuracy would substantially reduce the barrier to training reasoning-capable LLMs. - How it does it: RLVR parameter deltas per weight tensor concentrate in a single dominant direction (rank-1 SVD captures most task-relevant variance), and the scalar projection coefficient onto that direction evolves near-linearly with training step (R² > 0.98), so a short observed prefix is sufficient to extrapolate the full trajectory via OLS. - The good: RELEX matches or exceeds full GRPO performance on both the in-domain MATH benchmark and 5 OOD competition benchmarks (AIME, HMMT, AMC, OlympiadBench) across all three tested models using only 15–20% of training steps. - The missing: All experiments use a single RL algorithm (GRPO) on math reasoning with three Qwen-family models; generalization to other RL algorithms (PPO), task domains (code, instruction following), or model families (Llama) is entirely untested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on RLVR efficiency or LLM training geometry; the rank-1 trajectory finding is a clean, surprising empirical result — but practitioners should wait for multi-seed validation and broader task coverage before adoption.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (80) and Novelty (76) carry the score — the rank-1-per-tensor trajectory finding with near-linear scalar dynamics (R²>0.98) is a clean, surprising NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING, and the denoising interpretation (extrapolated checkpoints beating true RLVR on 4/5 OOD benchmarks) is genuinely counter-intuitive. Rigor (63) is the main drag: no multi-seed runs or error bars despite RL's known variance, observation-window instability the paper underweights (85.6%→50.9% on wrong T_cut), and preprint status, partially offset by thorough design-choice ablations and released code. Resilience (60) and Completeness (65) are limited by untested generalization beyond GRPO/math/Qwen, the undercosted T_cut-selection problem that erodes the headline 80–85% savings, an absent LoRA baseline, and ignored per-step checkpoint storage. Weighted: 0.20*76 + 0.25*63 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*60 = 67.
empirical Novelty 76 Rigor 63 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation RLVR training (e.g., via GRPO) improves LLM reasoning but requires massive numbers of optimization steps — often days of GPU time for moderately-sized models — with cost scaling directly with step count. Prior work suggests RLVR amplifies latent pretrained behaviors rather than learning entirely new capabilities, and recent analyses indicate that RLVR updates are highly structured and may modify only sparse or low-dimensional subsets of parameters. This raises the question of whether the full training trajectory is necessary or whether a short prefix encodes enough information to predict the endpoint. No prior method exploits the joint trajectory structure (shared rank-1 basis + scalar temporal dynamics) to extrapolate checkpoints at near-zero post-observation cost. Key Idea For each weight tensor, RLVR parameter delta trajectories lie in a rank-1 subspace: stacking per-step deltas into a trajectory matrix and applying SVD reveals a single dominant direction that captures nearly all task-relevant change (rank-1 reconstruction matches full RLVR performance at every training step). The scalar projection coefficient onto this direction evolves near-linearly with training step (R² > 0.98 for most tensors). These two structural properties together reduce future checkpoint prediction to (1) estimating the rank-1 direction from a short observed prefix and (2) extrapolating the scalar coefficient via a two-parameter linear fit — both closed-form and parameter-free. Methodology RELEX operates per weight tensor W^(ℓ) in three closed-form steps. Step 1 (subspace estimation): compute parameter deltas Δθ_t = θ_t − θ_0 for t = 1…T_cut, flatten and stack into a trajectory matrix M ∈ R^{T_cut × d}, compute compact SVD via the T×T Gram matrix (since T << d), and extract the top right singular vector v_1. Step 2 (coefficient extrapolation): project each observed delta onto v_1 to obtain scalar trajectory c_t, fit c(t) = at + b via OLS (closed-form slope and intercept), and extrapolate to target step T. Step 3 (weight prediction): reconstruct Ŵ_T = W_0 + ĉ_T · v_1. Repeating across all tensors yields the full predicted checkpoint. Training uses GRPO on the MATH training split with AdamW (lr=10⁻⁶, KL=0.001, G=8 rollouts/prompt, batch=256) for 500 steps on 8×H200 GPUs.
