#79 — Innovative (66)

CutVerse: A Compositional GUI Agents Benchmark for Media Post-Production Editing (19 ▲ )

Haobo Hu, Xiangwu Guo, Zhiheng Chen, Difei Gao, Haotian Liu, Libiao Jin, Qi Mao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 21 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CutVerse, a benchmark of 186 expert-annotated long-horizon tasks across 7 professional media editing applications (Premiere Pro, After Effects, Photoshop, DaVinci Resolve, JianYing, ComfyUI, Keling) for evaluating GUI agents on realistic post-production workflows, paired with a live Windows VM execution environment and a milestone-driven VLM-as-judge evaluation protocol. - Why it's important: Existing GUI agent benchmarks are confined to short-horizon web/desktop tasks and cannot capture the pixel-level spatial grounding, cross-modal audio-visual alignment, and sustained planning that professional creative software demands — leaving a critical gap between reported benchmark performance and deployment readiness. - How it does it: Expert screen recordings are parsed into hierarchical milestone trajectories; agents execute via low-level mouse/keyboard actions in resettable Hyper-V VMs, and a dual-model VLM judge (GPT-5.4 + Claude-Opus-4.6) evaluates each milestone via multimodal QA pairs, achieving 98.3–99% human-agreement on 300 trajectories. - The good: The milestone-vs-task consistency gap is the most actionable finding: Claude-Opus-4.6 achieves 92.9% milestone success on audio/rhythm editing but only 33.3% full-task success, quantitatively proving that current evaluation paradigms using binary task success systematically overestimate GUI agent capability. - The missing: Gemini-3-flash uses a different prompt schema (compound multi-action arrays, CutVerse-custom JSON) than the other four agents (OSWorld-style), introducing an uncontrolled confound that may inflate Gemini's performance; this is not discussed. - Read it or not: Researchers building GUI agents or designing agentic benchmarks should read it — the failure taxonomy and milestone infrastructure are specific and actionable; practitioners focused on web or mobile agents will find limited direct applicability.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (benchmark evaluation study delivering infrastructure). Practical Impact (76) and Novelty (70) are the contributors: live VM execution with checkpointing plus the first benchmark combining professional-software coverage, AIGC tools, expert trajectory annotation, and milestone-driven evaluation is a substantive infrastructure contribution, and the milestone-vs-task consistency gap is a real empirical finding. The composite is pulled down by Rigor (62) and Resilience (60): only 186 tasks (as few as 8–10 per category) reported to two decimals without confidence intervals gives false precision, the Gemini prompt-schema confound is an uncontrolled variable inflating one model's comparative results, and the proprietary VLM judges plus preprint status further limit it. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*76 ≈ 66.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 76
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation GUI agents have achieved strong results on web navigation (Mind2Web, WebArena) and general desktop tasks (OSWorld, macOSWorld), but these benchmarks use simplified interfaces with structured HTML DOMs, short action horizons (typically under 15 steps), and no professional creative software. Media post-production workflows — video timeline editing, compositing, color grading, audio alignment — present qualitatively different challenges: interfaces where unstructured timelines account for 46% of all interactions, action sequences averaging 18.73 steps and peaking at 239 steps, and tasks requiring tightly coupled key-mouse coordination for which no privileged API access exists. Existing creative benchmarks (VideoGUI: 178 tasks, no live environment; ComfyBench) are output-oriented rather than trajectory-oriented. The result is that no prior benchmark measures whether an agent can actually execute a sustained professional editing task, as a human creator would, in a live software environment. Key Idea The core insight is to decompose monolithic long-horizon editing workflows into hierarchical semantic milestones — each corresponding to a verifiable audio-visual state transition — then evaluate milestone completion via multimodal QA pairs rather than deterministic program checks or binary final-output comparison. This transforms an ill-posed open-ended evaluation into a sequence of interpretable, automatable verification steps, while exposing exactly where agents fail within extended trajectories rather than only whether they ultimately succeed. Methodology The benchmark is built through three phases. First, 10 professional creators record expert demonstrations across 7 software platforms using synchronized screen capture and I/O event logging, producing 2.43 hours of 186 human-verified tasks. Second, a custom parsing pipeline synchronizes high-framerate recordings with low-level logs to extract spatiotemporally grounded action trajectories, then decomposes each trajectory into a hierarchy of semantic milestones (e.g., "locate Effects panel" → "drag Cross Dissolve transition" → "adjust Edge Feather to 71") mapped to atomic GUI capabilities. The action space is a vision-only POMDP over 12 low-level primitives (click, drag, scroll, hotkey, write, wait, done, fail — no accessibility tree or DOM). Third, agents execute in live resettable Windows 11 Pro VMs via Hyper-V; each task is initialized from a precise VM checkpoint. Evaluation uses a VLM-as-judge pipeline (GPT-5.4 and Claude-Opus-4.6 independently) over per-milestone QA pairs. Five frontier models are evaluated: Claude-Opus-4.6 and Gemini-3-flash via API; Qwen3-VL-32B-Thinking, UI-TARS-1.5-7B, and EvoCUA-32B deployed locally on 4×RTX 5090 GPUs. Critical design decisions: (1) vision-only grounding rather than accessibility tree access — well-justified by the argument that professional software lacks DOM equivalents; (2) milestone decomposition rather than binary task success — empirically justified by the large milestone-task gap revealed in experiments; (3) multi-model VLM judge — well-justified to avoid evaluator-specific leniency, though both judges are proprietary. Key Results Overall task success rates: Claude-Opus-4.6 68.3%, Gemini-3-flash 67.2%, EvoCUA-32B 51.6%, Qwen3-32B 48.4%, UI-TARS-1.5-7B 44.1%. On procedural tasks (launch, export, generative workflow), all models score above 75%, with every model achieving 100% on generative workflows. Core editing tasks collapse dramatically: masking/matting/tracking task success ranges from 9.5% (UI-TARS) to 38.1% (Gemini); effects/visual tuning from 20.7% (Qwen) to 58.6% (Claude). The milestone-task gap is stark: Claude achieves 92.9% milestone success on audio/rhythm editing but only 33.3% task success; Gemini drops from 78.6% milestones to 50.0% tasks. Incomplete task ratios: Claude/Gemini ~32%, Qwen/UI-TARS >50%. Per-software, Photoshop (avg 42.61 steps, 91.2s avg duration) and After Effects are hardest; Keling (avg 8.31 steps) is easiest. Task consistency gap (declared completion vs. verified accuracy) peaks at 0.148 for Qwen, indicating frequent execution hallucination — agents declaring success without achieving it. Baselines are current frontier models (2025–2026) and the comparison is strong. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is the combination of professional-tier software coverage, live VM execution, AIGC tool integration, human-expert trajectory annotation, and milestone-driven VLM evaluation in a single framework — no prior benchmark achieves all five simultaneously. The "Vibe Cutting" paradigm framing is a soft conceptual contribution without formal grounding. The parsing pipeline and evaluation infrastructure are engineering contributions. No new learning method, model architecture, or algorithm is proposed. Rigor & Reproducibility The human-alignment study (300 trajectories, 98.3–99% agreement with professional evaluators) is a meaningful rigor positive that most benchmark papers omit. Detailed agent configurations, vLLM deployment parameters, system prompts, and coordinate conventions are provided in the appendix, enabling reproduction of agent behavior. The dataset and code are stated to be open-sourced at https://github.com/CUC-MIPG/CutVerse (a preprint; peer-review status not established as of arXiv v1 dated May 2026). No ablations isolate the contribution of specific benchmark design choices (milestone granularity, QA question formulation, VM checkpoint fidelity). With only 186 total tasks — as few as 8 for generative workflow and 10 for masking/tracking — task success rates in individual categories have high variance, and differences of 5–10 percentage points between models cannot be considered statistically significant without confidence intervals, which are not reported. The proprietary VLM judges (GPT-5.4, Claude-Opus-4.6) introduce a reproducibility barrier for community replication of evaluation scores. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (a benchmark infrastructure evaluated by deployment of five frontier models in live VMs). - Practical: Running the benchmark requires Windows 11 Pro VMs with Hyper-V, 4×RTX 5090 GPUs (peak 127 GB VRAM for EvoCUA-32B) for open-source model serving, and API access to proprietary models — accessible to well-resourced labs but not trivially reproducible. - Efficient: The live VM execution paradigm is inherently expensive: each evaluation step requires VLM inference, pyautogui execution, screenshot capture, and a separate VLM judge API call; no per-step cost is reported, making the total evaluation budget opaque. - Effective: The benchmark discriminates clearly between models, task types, and software platforms, and the milestone decomposition reliably distinguishes local from global competence — it measures what it claims. - Scalable: At 186 tasks, this is small by benchmark standards; the authors argue the infrastructure scales via checkpointing, but no expansion beyond 186 tasks is demonstrated, and the human annotation bottleneck (10 professional creators, careful QA refinement) limits rapid scaling. The work delivers a software artifact (benchmark dataset, evaluation infrastructure, open-source code) alongside the paper. Strengths - The milestone-vs-task consistency gap is the paper's strongest empirical contribution: showing that milestone success rates of 70–93% coexist with task success rates of 17–50% on core editing categories quantitatively proves that conventional binary-success metrics are insufficient for long-horizon GUI evaluation and motivates the milestone framework on its own merits. - Human-expert annotation by 10 professional creators with iterative QA refinement produces task distributions that genuinely reflect professional workflows rather than synthetic or crowd-sourced approximations. - Live VM execution with precise state checkpointing eliminates the offline-prediction shortcut that inflates performance on static action prediction benchmarks; the closed-loop design forces agents to handle cascading errors authentically. - The failure taxonomy (component misrecognition, inaccurate fine-grained grounding, lack of global perception, repetitive action loops) is grounded in specific trajectory evidence and directly suggests concrete research directions. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors state that current models are limited in long-horizon reliability, domain-specific planning, spatial grounding, and compositional interaction. They explicitly identify four failure modes with trajectory-level evidence. They do not acknowledge: the Windows-only scope (excluding macOS creative ecosystems), the statistical power issues from small per-category task counts, the Gemini prompt-schema confound, or the reproducibility barrier posed by proprietary VLM judges. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Gemini prompt schema confound: Gemini-3-flash uses a fundamentally different prompt format (CutVerse-custom JSON with compound multi-action arrays and milestone-completion metadata) versus the OSWorld-style single-action schema used for the other four agents. Gemini achieves near-parity with Claude (67.2% vs 68.3%) despite being a "flash" model; whether this reflects model capability or prompt engineering advantage is not isolated. - Statistical power: Task success differences between models in small categories (8–14 tasks) are reported to two decimal places without error bars or significance tests, giving false precision — a 10-percentage-point difference over 10 tasks is not reliably discriminative. - No ablation on evaluation design: The paper claims milestone-driven VLM evaluation is superior to binary task success, but never ablates alternatives (e.g., final-state screenshot comparison, code-based state verification for some tasks) to quantify this claim. - macOS exclusion: Final Cut Pro, Logic Pro, and Motion cover large segments of professional media production but are absent — limiting generalizability of conclusions about "professional creative workflows." - No inter-annotator agreement on milestone design: The 98.3–99% human agreement measures VLM judge vs. human, not consistency among the 10 annotators who defined milestones — leaving milestone boundary subjectivity unmeasured. Bottom Line CutVerse is the most rigorous live-execution GUI agent benchmark for professional creative software to date, and the milestone-task consistency finding is a substantive empirical contribution that should influence how the broader GUI agent community designs evaluations; the Gemini prompt confound and small task counts limit the strength of comparative model claims, but neither undermines the benchmark's value as a research infrastructure.

#80 — Innovative (66)

InsightTok: Improving Text and Face Fidelity in Discrete Tokenization for Autoregressive Image Generation (31 ▲ )

Yang Yue, Fangyun Wei, Tianyu He, Jinjing Zhao, Zanlin Ni, Zeyu Liu, Jiayi Guo, Lei Shi, Yue Dong, Li Chen, Ji Li, Gao Huang, Dong Chen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 18 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces InsightTok, a discrete visual tokenizer training framework that augments standard VQGAN-style training with localized, area-weighted perceptual losses computed using domain-specific recognition networks on detected text and face regions. - Why it's important: Text and faces are the dominant failure modes of discrete AR image generators, and prior fixes (larger codebooks, more tokens) increase computational cost without targeting the supervision mismatch that actually causes the problem. - How it does it: Text and face regions are detected offline, cropped from both original and reconstruction, and compared in the feature space of a pretrained text recognizer and an ArcFace model respectively, with losses weighted proportional to region area to prevent small hard-to-reconstruct regions from dominating optimization. - The good: On TokBench at a 16k codebook, InsightTok achieves 53.05% T-ACCm versus 24.16% for the prior best IBQ-16k — a +28.89 percentage-point gain — while general rFID changes by only 0.09, and a controlled same-architecture AR comparison (LlamaGenTok-AR vs. InsightAR) shows text NED rising from 79.86% to 95.83%. - The missing: The face evaluation metric is ArcFace cosine similarity, the same model family used in the face perceptual loss, creating a circularity that an independent metric or human study would need to resolve; additionally, the text rendering generation benchmark uses blank-background paragraphs rather than natural scene text. - Read it or not: Researchers building discrete autoregressive image generators who need better text or face fidelity should read this; the method is low-overhead, well-ablated, and the controlled tokenizer-swap experiment makes causal attribution credible.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (68) is the strongest dimension and the main reason this ranks first in the cluster: the LlamaGenTok-AR vs. InsightAR controlled tokenizer-swap (same 7B LLM, same 150M images, only the tokenizer differs) cleanly isolates causation, the area-weighting and encoder-vs-decoder ablations are thorough, and — uniquely in this cluster — both code AND trained weights are released at negligible (~2%) overhead. Practical Impact (70) is highest because it ships a directly usable artifact. The cap below 'Highly Innovative' comes from Novelty (65, a tokenizer-training trick rather than a new formulation) and the unaddressed ArcFace circularity (face loss optimizes the same model family used as the face metric) plus the blank-background text benchmark, which hold Completeness/Resilience at 64. Composite: 0.20*65 + 0.25*68 + 0.15*62 + 0.15*64 + 0.15*64 + 0.10*70 = 66.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 64 Resilience 64 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Autoregressive image generation systems quantize images into discrete token grids using vector quantizers with aggressive spatial downsampling (typically 16×). This compression discards the fine-grained spatial detail required for legible glyphs and distinctive facial features. The problem is compounded by two structural factors: text and face regions often occupy only a small fraction of an image, so their gradients are diluted by surrounding scene content in pixel-level or LPIPS losses; and LPIPS (VGG-based) was trained on patch similarity rather than text readability or identity preservation. Prior attempts to fix this increase codebook capacity (up to 262k entries) or token count, incurring substantial computational overhead without addressing the supervision alignment problem. The gap is practically consequential because AR generators are increasingly applied to poster design, portrait synthesis, and graphic creation. Key Idea The tokenizer training objective should apply domain-specific supervision only to the regions that benefit from it. Rather than asking a generic perceptual loss to implicitly capture glyph readability across an entire image, detect text and face regions offline, crop and normalize them from both the original and reconstruction, and compute feature-space losses using recognition models already trained to be sensitive to the precise structure that matters — text legibility and facial identity. Area-based aggregation weights each region's loss contribution by its fractional image area, preventing tiny, high-error regions from overwhelming the general reconstruction signal. Methodology Training proceeds in three phases on a 426M-parameter convolutional VQGAN (5 stages, 4 residual blocks per stage, 16× downsampling, K=16,384 codebook, d=256 embeddings, EMA updates with random restart for dead codes). Phase 1 (200k steps): standard VQGAN objective — L1 reconstruction + commitment loss (β=0.02) + LPIPS (γ=0.5) + PatchGAN adversarial (η=0.5) — on 100M+ images. Phase 2 (40k steps): adds Ltext and Lface (α1=α2=1.0) on curated LAION subsets (~4M text-annotated, ~8M face-annotated images). Text loss: bounding boxes detected offline via differentiable binarization; crops from x and x̂ resized to 32×128 and passed through 5 layers of a pretrained ABINet text recognizer; MSE in feature space aggregated with area weights. Face loss: landmark-guided similarity transform warps both x and x̂ to a 112×112 canonical space via ArcFace model's coordinate system; L-layer feature MSE aggregated with area weights. Phase 3 (40k steps): encoder and quantizer frozen; decoder fine-tuned with reduced α=0.1. Three critical design decisions: (1) area-based weighting is well-justified by Table 3 — without it, rFID degrades from 0.69 to 1.11; (2) full-model training versus decoder-only is well-justified by Table 4 — decoder-only yields marginal T-ACCm improvement (24.15 vs. 23.52 baseline, versus 40.64 for full model), showing the encoder's latent representation must change; (3) offline detection keeps training overhead to ~2%, verified empirically. Key Results Tokenizer reconstruction (TokBench, 512×512 resolution): InsightTok T-ACCm 53.05% versus IBQ-16k 24.16% (next best at identical 16k codebook and 16× downsampling) and Emu3.5-IBQ 41.52% (131k codebook). T-NEDm 71.40% versus 43.66% (IBQ-16k). F-Simm 0.36 versus 0.27 (IBQ-16k) and 0.31 (O-MAGVIT2-262k). General metrics: rFID 0.69 (competitive with IBQ-16k's 0.64), PSNR 23.64 (best among 16k-codebook methods). Generation (Table 2): InsightAR text NED 95.83% versus LlamaGenTok-AR 79.86% (same 7B architecture, same 150M training images, only tokenizer differs — the cleanest comparison); MagFace score 23.33 versus 22.29; GenEval 0.82 versus 0.81; DPG-Bench 84.11 versus 84.19, indicating no degradation of general generation quality. The text generation improvement is the most striking and most credibly attributed result. The face generation improvement (23.33 vs. 22.29) is positive but small relative to the large reconstruction gain (F-Simm +0.09), and this discrepancy is not analyzed. Table 10 provides an honest comparison to continuous tokenizers: at 0.0547 BPP, InsightTok (T-ACCm 53.05) sits between DC-AE at 1.0 BPP (38.25) and SDXL-VAE at 2.0 BPP (56.86), showing content-targeted supervision partially closes the gap with 18–146× higher bitrate representations. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The contribution is a tokenizer training methodology: the specific composition of offline region detection + domain-specific recognition model perceptual losses + area-weighted aggregation applied to discrete VQGAN training is new. Individual components (perceptual losses, attention to text/faces, area weighting) are not new in isolation, but their application to the tokenizer training objective — rather than to the downstream generator — and the demonstration that this reshapes the learned latent representation (not just the decoder mapping) is a genuine methodological contribution. It is not a new quantization scheme or codebook design. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: Table 3 validates area weighting, Table 4 isolates encoder vs. decoder contribution, Table 5 compares against OCR-VQGAN loss, Table 6 shows codebook-size generalization, Tables 7–9 cover model size, individual loss contributions, and detector recall sensitivity. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported anywhere; given 32-A100, 5-day training, variance across runs is unknown. Code is available at https://github.com/LeapLabTHU/InsightTok. A methodological concern: the face reconstruction evaluation uses ArcFace cosine similarity, and InsightTok's face loss explicitly minimizes ArcFace feature distance — the method is trained against the evaluation metric, creating circular self-validation. An independent face quality metric would be required for unbiased assessment. The TokBench benchmark itself is a concurrent preprint (arXiv:2505.18142), so both the evaluation benchmark and this paper are unreviewed. This is a preprint. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL (with a SYSTEMS component — delivers trained tokenizer and AR model). - Practical: Detection is offline; at inference time InsightTok is a standard VQGAN with no added modules. Training requires 32 A100 GPUs for ~5 days, consistent with standard large tokenizer training. - Efficient: Training overhead measured at ~2% in FLOPs (2575 vs. 25.0+12.6 GFLOPs for recognition models) and ~2% in wall-clock time (2056 → 2099 ms/iter); GPU memory increases 44.1 → 44.3 GB. This is genuinely negligible. - Effective: Evidence is strong for text: both reconstruction (+28.89 pp T-ACCm) and generation (+16 pp NED) improve dramatically with a controlled comparison. Face gains are real but smaller in generation despite large reconstruction improvements; the gap is unexplained. - Scalable: Tested only at 512×512 with 1024 tokens. Higher resolutions and longer token sequences are not evaluated. Codebook scaling to 65k (Table 6) validates the method still helps, but AR model scaling beyond 7B is untested. The paper delivers trained weights via the GitHub repository. Strengths - The LlamaGenTok-AR controlled comparison is the paper's strongest contribution: same 7B LLM, same 150M training images, same pipeline — only the tokenizer swapped. This cleanly attributes the generation improvement to tokenizer quality rather than model scale or data differences. - The area-weighting ablation (Table 3) shows the authors identified and solved the interference problem: without it, the specialized losses improve text/face but increase rFID by 0.51; with it, rFID changes by only 0.09. This is a concrete and well-designed design choice. - Table 10's comparison to continuous tokenizers is honest about the absolute performance ceiling: at 145× lower BPP, InsightTok is still below SDXL-VAE, giving practitioners accurate calibration. - The offline detection design is genuinely practical: no changes to inference, no detection latency at generation time, and the 2% training overhead is empirically verified with both FLOPs and wall-clock measurements. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) the method targets only text and faces, not general reconstruction quality; (2) text support is limited to English, with multilingual extension left to future work; (3) improved facial fidelity enables deepfake misuse — mitigated by non-commercial restriction and no use of personal identity labels during training. These are stated directly and credibly. The authors do not acknowledge the ArcFace circularity in evaluation or the synthetic nature of the text rendering benchmark. Analyst-Identified Gaps - ArcFace circularity: The face perceptual loss minimizes ArcFace (ResNet50) feature distance; the face reconstruction metric is ArcFace cosine similarity. This is the same model evaluated against itself. An independent metric (e.g., FaceNet, AdaFace, or a perceptual user study) is needed to validate that the gains reflect genuine perceptual improvement rather than metric overfitting. - Text rendering evaluation is too easy: Evaluating text NED by generating paragraphs on blank backgrounds tests near-isolated typography; this likely overestimates the benefit for real poster or scene-text generation where text is embedded in complex visual context. In-the-wild text generation evaluation is absent. - Weak face generation transfer: Reconstruction F-Simm improves by 0.09 (33% relative), but generation MagFace improves by 1.04 (4.7% relative). The paper does not analyze this mismatch. Either the generation model underutilizes the improved face representation, or MagFace norm is an insensitive generation quality metric, or reconstruction improvements don't transfer through the AR model. - No multi-seed statistics: All quantitative results reflect single training runs. For a method with three training phases and stochastic codebook initialization, the variance is unknown. - Resolution limitation: All experiments are at 512×512. Poster and design use cases often require higher resolution; the method's behavior at 1024×1024 or with longer token sequences is unknown. - Detector quality as a ceiling: Table 9 shows monotonic degradation with region dropout but does not report the actual detector precision/recall on LAION. False-positive text detections (e.g., detecting textures as text) could introduce noise in the training signal; this is not analyzed. Bottom Line A practitioner building a discrete autoregressive image generator who needs legible text or faithful faces should read and use this paper: the method is low-overhead, well-ablated, and the controlled tokenizer-swap comparison credibly isolates the contribution — though the ArcFace evaluation circularity and the synthetic text rendering benchmark warrant independent verification before drawing strong conclusions about face quality and real-world text rendering.
Recommended 2605.14333 PDF
#81 — Innovative (65)

Self-Distilled Trajectory-Aware Boltzmann Modeling: Bridging the Training- Inference Discrepancy in Diffusion Language Models

arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TABOM (Trajectory-Aligned Boltzmann Optimization via Modeling), a post-training framework for Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) that replaces the uniform-masking NELBO fine-tuning objective with a pairwise ranking loss derived from a Boltzmann model of the inference-time easy-to-hard unmasking distribution. - Why it's important: DLMs fine-tuned with standard NELBO suffer a severe training-inference mismatch — training treats all masked tokens equally, while inference always unmasks the most-certain tokens first — causing catastrophic forgetting of out-of-distribution capabilities when ground-truth data is used, and only marginal in-domain gains when self-distilled data is used naively. - How it does it: TABOM formalizes the inference unmasking preference as a Boltzmann distribution over predictive entropies, then approximates the intractable KL divergence between training and inference distributions via a local pairwise hinge ranking loss over W=32 token windows within self-distilled trajectories. - The good: On Dream-7B-Instruct fine-tuned for math reasoning, TABOM achieves 84.31% on GSM8K — +2.90 over No-SFT and +2.19 over the next-best trajectory baseline (dInfer 82.33%) — while simultaneously improving out-of-distribution performance, a combination no baseline achieves. - The missing: The method is only tested at 7–8B parameters; there is no comparison against RL-based DLM post-training methods (D1, DARE) despite those being directly relevant post-training alternatives; and no code release is mentioned. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on DLM post-training or anyone thinking about training-inference alignment in non-autoregressive generative models — the Boltzmann formulation is a clean theoretical contribution and the empirical story is unusually honest about what self-distillation alone cannot do.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (75) and Novelty (72) are the strengths — the Boltzmann formulation of the inference-time unmasking distribution and its reduction to a tractable local pairwise ranking loss is a clean, non-obvious theoretical move, and the finding that naive self-distillation fails due to distribution discrepancy (not data quality) contradicts prior trajectory-distillation assumptions. The composite is held below 75 by Completeness (62) and Impact (55): there is no comparison against the directly relevant RL-based DLM post-training methods (D1, DARE) despite the paper framing itself against all post-training approaches, no code/weights release, an unreported trajectory-filtering rate, and a self-defined TDS metric that is not independently validated. Rigor (64) is otherwise sound with component-isolating ablations and 3-seed stds. Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*64 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*62 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*55 = 65.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 64 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 62 Resilience 60 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Diffusion Language Models use confidence-guided easy-to-hard decoding at inference: starting from a fully masked sequence, each decoding step unmasks the tokens with the lowest predictive entropy (highest certainty), iteratively until all positions are filled. However, the standard NELBO training objective samples a uniformly random subset of tokens to mask and reconstruct in one shot, instilling a uniform inductive bias that treats all tokens as equally difficult regardless of their inherent uncertainty. This mismatch means the model is never trained to favor the high-certainty-first unmasking order it actually uses at test time. Practitioners who try to close this gap by fine-tuning on self-distilled trajectories (the model's own decoded outputs) using the same NELBO objective observe only marginal in-domain improvements, because the distribution discrepancy between uniform training masking and the peaked inference trajectory distribution remains large. Prior trajectory-based methods (dInfer, T3D) address this for inference efficiency (step compression) but not for genuine knowledge acquisition. Key Idea The easy-to-hard decoding process at inference naturally defines a peaked distribution over unmasked token sets — sets composed of low-entropy (easy) tokens are exponentially more likely to be visited than sets containing high-entropy tokens. This can be formally modeled as a Boltzmann distribution with energy proportional to the sum of predictive entropies of the unmasked tokens. Rather than minimizing the intractable global KL divergence between this Boltzmann target and the model's predicted unmasking distribution (which requires summing over all 2^N unmasked subsets), TABOM exploits the fact that maximum likelihood estimation of a Boltzmann is equivalent to enforcing correct pairwise rankings. Any two tokens decoded at different positions in a self-distilled trajectory provide a ground-truth preference: the earlier-decoded token (lower entropy, easier) should have strictly lower predicted entropy than the later-decoded one. A local pairwise hinge loss over a fixed window of W=32 consecutively decoded tokens enforces this ordering without requiring partition function computation. Methodology TABOM operates in three stages. First, self-distilled trajectories are collected by running the base DLM's default entropy-based decoder on task queries, yielding approximately 3.8K valid trajectories for code and 5.1K for math. Second, for each sampled trajectory τ and a randomly selected local window (t, t') with |Δ_{t→t'}| = W=32 newly decoded tokens, the pairwise ordered set P_{t,t'} contains all token pairs (r,s) where r is decoded before s (step_τ(r) > step_τ(s) in reverse-time notation). The ranking loss penalizes cases where the predicted entropy of the easier token r is not sufficiently lower than the harder token s: L_rank = (1/|P|) Σ max(0, h_θ(r;τ) − h_θ(s;τ) + γ). Third, this ranking term is added to the trajectory-aware NELBO reconstruction term: L_TABOM = E[NELBO over local window tokens] + (2λ/W(W−1)) × L_rank. Critical design decisions: (1) local window W=32 rather than global — comparing tokens decoded at vastly different stages introduces noise because their difficulty is inherently incommensurable (validated in Table 5 ablation, where global window drops MATH500 from 41.10 to 40.20); (2) pairwise ranking rather than direct distribution fitting — avoids the intractable partition function over 2^N states; (3) LoRA fine-tuning (rank=16, α=16, targeting q_proj and v_proj) across 8 GPUs with AdamW and 5 epochs, separate models per domain. Hyperparameters λ ∈ {1,2} and γ ∈ {0.1, 0.2, 0.3} are tuned by grid search. Key Results Experiments run on Dream-7B-Instruct and LLaDA-8B-Instruct across five benchmarks: GSM8K and MATH500 (mathematical reasoning), HumanEval and MBPP (code generation), IFEval (instruction following). Baselines are No-SFT, SFT-GT (ground-truth data, standard NELBO), SFT-SD (self-distilled data, standard NELBO), dInfer, and T3D — all use the same LoRA configuration and same self-distilled data except SFT-GT. For Dream-7B code fine-tuning: TABOM achieves average in-domain score 60.48 vs. SFT-GT 59.78 and SFT-SD 55.76; OOD average 59.86 vs. SFT-GT 43.65 (catastrophic forgetting) and SFT-SD 60.17. For Dream-7B math fine-tuning: TABOM in-domain 62.71 vs. dInfer 61.97; OOD average 57.98 vs. SFT-SD 57.51. A particularly striking result: under 2-token parallel decoding, TABOM scores 77.79 on GSM8K vs. SFT-GT 72.33, because entropy-landscape alignment inherently makes more tokens high-certainty and thus safe to decode in parallel. Trajectory Discrimination Score (TDS), a proposed diagnostic metric measuring variance of per-token entropies at each decoding step, confirms TABOM produces the most discriminative entropy landscape — Dream base SFT-SD TDS on MBPP is 0.035, TABOM raises it to 0.929. The improvements over dInfer and T3D are consistent but moderate (1–3 percentage points), suggesting the pairwise ranking adds genuine signal beyond trajectory-aware masking alone. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The Boltzmann formulation of the DLM inference unmasking distribution and its reduction to a pairwise ranking objective is a genuinely novel theoretical contribution; the empirical finding that naive self-distillation with NELBO is insufficient despite the lower optimization barrier — and that the fundamental cause is a distribution discrepancy, not data quality — is a non-obvious insight that contradicts the implicit assumption of prior trajectory distillation work. The individual components (pairwise ranking, energy-based models, self-distillation) are established; the synthesis and application to DLM training-inference alignment is new. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations in Table 5 cleanly isolate the contribution of trajectory-aware masking vs. pairwise ranking vs. local window, providing strong evidence that the ranking loss drives the gains. Sensitivity analyses cover hyperparameters λ, γ (Table 7, 6 settings, all outperform No-SFT), and window size W (Table 9, W=32 is a clear sweet spot). Results include standard deviations across 3 seeds for most experiments. No code or model weights release is mentioned. The Trajectory Discrimination Score is self-defined and not independently validated. All baselines share the same LoRA configuration and training data, making comparisons fair. One concern: the self-distilled trajectories are filtered to valid (correct) completions only (~3.8K–5.1K from presumably larger raw sets) — the filtering criterion and rejection rate are not reported. The paper is a preprint; results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: LoRA fine-tuning on 8 GPUs (unspecified GPU type) with rank=16 is commercially feasible; self-distilled trajectory collection adds a one-time generation overhead not quantified in the paper. - Efficient: Training cost is LoRA-scale; the ranking loss computation over local windows of W=32 adds O(W²/2) pairwise comparisons per sample but this is negligible relative to the forward pass; no inference-time cost added. - Effective: Convincingly outperforms all NELBO-based and prior trajectory baselines on both in-domain and OOD metrics, and the TDS metric provides mechanistic evidence that the entropy landscape is genuinely reshaped rather than just data-fitted. - Scalable: Tested only at 7–8B parameters; no experiments at larger scales and no analysis of whether the easy-to-hard trajectory structure and Boltzmann formulation remain stable at 70B+ scale. The work delivers a training recipe and loss function, not a software artifact or library. Strengths - The Boltzmann distribution derivation (Proposition 3) cleanly formalizes why uniform NELBO wastes self-distilled trajectory structure, connecting an observed empirical failure (marginal SFT-SD gains) to a principled theoretical cause (large KL divergence between training and inference masking distributions). - The reduction from intractable global KL to local pairwise ranking (Lemma 1 + Theorem 1) is technically sound and practically efficient — avoiding partition function computation over exponentially many states is the key enabler. - The OOD preservation result is genuinely important: TABOM expands knowledge without catastrophic forgetting, which is the central obstacle to practical DLM fine-tuning and is not solved by any prior baseline. - The ablation is unusually complete for a preprint: it isolates trajectory-aware masking, pairwise ranking, and local vs. global windowing independently, and all three components are shown to matter. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors note that TABOM's primary focus is performance improvement rather than inference acceleration and do not claim to address decoding speed. They acknowledge that trajectory baselines (dInfer, T3D) may still be preferred when step compression is the priority. No other explicit limitations are stated; the authors do not discuss the cost of trajectory collection, the quality ceiling imposed by the base model's self-distilled outputs, or the lack of scaling experiments. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No comparison against RL-based DLM post-training (D1, DARE, referenced but excluded from main results) — these are direct competitors for performance-oriented post-training, and their omission is a significant gap given that the paper frames itself against all post-training approaches. - The valid trajectory filtering rate is unreported: if only a small fraction of self-distilled completions are correct (especially on harder math), the effective data size could be quite small, and the method's performance at lower trajectory quality or quantity is not analyzed. - Evaluation is limited to two DLM architectures (both masked discrete diffusion); it is unclear whether TABOM applies to continuous-time or score-based DLMs with different inference schedules. - The pairwise ranking loss enforces local entropy ordering but does not guarantee that the global Boltzmann distribution is matched — the gap between the local surrogate and the global target is acknowledged theoretically but not quantified empirically. - All tasks use short sequences (≤512 tokens, ≤256 new tokens); performance on long-form generation where the easy-to-hard trajectory structure may differ substantially is unexamined. Bottom Line A practitioner building or fine-tuning DLMs will find TABOM directly actionable — it resolves the forgetting-vs-gains dilemma that makes naive SFT impractical — and the Boltzmann formulation gives a principled vocabulary for thinking about DLM training-inference alignment that the field currently lacks.
Search Results 2605.11854 PDF
#82 — Innovative (65)

Mid-Training with Self-Generated Data Improves Reinforcement Learning in Language Models

arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a heuristic-guided mid-training framework that fine-tunes LLMs on self-generated, diverse correct solution trajectories — conditioned on Pólya's problem-solving heuristics — before RL training, improving subsequent GRPO-based RL across mathematical reasoning benchmarks. - Why it's important: RL with verifiable rewards primarily amplifies behaviors already present in the base model rather than inducing new ones, so models lacking diverse solution priors see limited gains; this approach addresses that bottleneck without distillation from a stronger teacher. - How it does it: For each training question the base model generates up to 128 heuristic-conditioned candidates, filters for correctness via Math-Verify, selects the highest-reward correct response per heuristic, then fine-tunes on the resulting multi-solution dataset before GRPO — creating N-modal next-token distributions that policy-gradient updates traverse more conservatively and productively. - The good: At pass@64, mid-trained models (n=64 heuristics) improve average accuracy from 46.30% (vanilla RL) to 48.17% across six benchmarks, with larger absolute gains on harder tasks (AIME 2025: 12.84%→18.66%, AMC 2023: 83.49%→85.18%). - The missing: Experiments are limited to Llama 3.2–3B and Qwen2.5–7B; no evidence at 70B+ scale, and it is unclear whether the benefit persists when the base model already has substantial implicit diversity. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building RL post-training pipelines for small-to-medium reasoning models, especially those where pass@k at k>1 matters; those targeting pass@1 or using large models gain less.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. The composite is pulled below 75 chiefly by Practical Impact (35) — no code, weights, or dataset are released and the data-generation GPU cost is unreported — and by Rigor (65), where the absence of any public artifact plus math-only RL data that may inflate the OOD claims offsets the otherwise commendable mean+/-SE reporting, formal Theorem 4.1 proof, and two-model-family ablations. Resilience (63) is limited because pass@1 gains over vanilla RL are marginal (STaR still wins at pass@1), the benefit appears to invert by 7B, and nothing is tested at 70B+. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (75) are real — the N-modal mechanistic account and the optimal-n-tracks-rollout-size finding are non-trivial. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*63 + 0.10*35 = 65.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 63 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation RL with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a standard post-training step for LLM reasoning, but recent evidence shows it primarily sharpens behaviors already in the base model rather than inducing new capabilities. A single reasoning problem can often be solved via analogy, backward reasoning, decomposition, or auxiliary-quantity introduction; a model that has seen only a narrow set of approaches in training concentrates its distribution on those strategies and RL cannot diversify beyond them. This is especially acute for smaller models (Llama 3.2–3B) that lack the implicit diversity of larger systems. The gap motivates mid-training — an intermediate fine-tuning step — as a principled way to enrich the model's prior before RL, without requiring distillation from a stronger teacher. Key Idea The core insight, formalized in Theorem 4.1, is that a policy-gradient update changes a sampled token's probability by ~ηAε² in the uni-modal regime (model near-certain of one token) but by ~ηA(1/N)(1−1/N) in the N-modal regime (probability mass spread across N dominant modes). In the N-modal regime, each update is conservative but non-negligible, and a negative update on one mode redistributes mass to the remaining N−1 alternatives rather than collapsing onto a single option. By mid-training on n distinct correct solution strategies per question, the model acquires N-modal next-token distributions at reasoning branching points, giving RL a richer probability landscape to optimize over and allowing composition of strategies within a single RL-generated chain. Methodology The pipeline has two phases. In mid-training, the base instruct model (not a stronger teacher) generates 128 candidate responses for each of 7,112 GSM8K training questions, conditioned on a heuristic description + four few-shot exemplars drawn from 64 Pólya-style heuristics (analogy, decomposition, carry-out, bolzano bisection, auxiliary elements, etc.). Candidates are first filtered for correctness via Math-Verify; survivors are scored by Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.2-3B and the top-scoring correct response per question–heuristic pair is retained. This produces D_Pólya containing n solution variants per question (n ∈ {1,2,4,8,16,32,64}). The mid-training loss is average negative log-likelihood over all n trajectories per question, trained for one epoch on 4×H100s with AdamW (lr=1×10⁻⁶, cosine schedule, bfloat16). In RL, GRPO is applied on DAPO-Math-17k with rollout group size g=16, batch size 32, no KL regularization, for one epoch; reward is binary answer correctness. Baselines: zero-shot base instruct, vanilla RL (GRPO on base), and STaR+RL (STaR rationalization then GRPO). Three critical design decisions: (1) bootstrapping from the same base model avoids capability-transfer confounds from distillation; (2) filtering requires both correctness and heuristic adherence, not just stylistic variation; (3) effective batch size is scaled with n to hold total optimization steps constant at 444 across all configurations, enabling fair comparison. Key Results On Llama 3.2–3B–Instruct across Math-500, AIME 2024/2025, AMC 2023, HMMT 2025, and OlympiadBench: at pass@64, D_Pólya (n=64) reaches average 48.17% vs. 46.30% for vanilla RL and 45.69% for STaR+RL. AIME 2025 pass@64: 12.84%→18.66%; OlympiadBench: 42.13%→43.57%. At pass@1, gains are modest (11.50% for n=64 vs. 11.08% zero-shot), and STaR achieves higher average pass@1 (13.02%) than all Pólya configurations — indicating STaR better sharpens single-sample accuracy while Pólya better preserves distributional breadth. The controlled ablation (§6.2) using identical training instance counts (7,408) shows multiple-approaches-per-question outperforms single-solution-per-question by ~7% relative across all k values. Mid-training with heuristic-diverse but incorrect trajectories degrades RL below the vanilla baseline on all benchmarks — correctness is necessary, diversity alone is insufficient. Vendi Score of the mid-training data scales from 1.99 (n=2) to 40.56 (n=64), and the n=16 configuration (Vendi 13.81) outperforms teacher-distilled data (Vendi 10.95) on downstream RL pass@1 and pass@64. On Qwen2.5–7B–Instruct, n=8 is optimal (consistent with the rollout-size hypothesis g=8); n=16 mid-trained model achieves 17.06%/50.50% average pass@1/pass@64 vs. 18.47%/51.39% for zero-shot, suggesting diminishing returns when the base model already has strong priors. OOD: HumanEval 51.14%→52.82% (n=64 vs. vanilla RL); MuSR Murder Mystery 53.15%→57.36%; Team Allocations 23.46% (vanilla RL) → 39.07% (n=64). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The individual components (self-play data generation, Pólya heuristics as prompts, GRPO RL) are known; the contribution is their systematic combination and the theoretical characterization of the N-modal regime that explains why approach diversity benefits RL. Genuinely novel: operationalizing Pólya's heuristics as a bootstrapped diversity engine, the proof that N-modal distributions slow mode collapse under policy-gradient updates, the empirical finding that the optimal n tracks the RL rollout group size g, and the correctness-necessity ablation. Not novel: the basic mid-training-then-RL recipe and GRPO itself. Rigor & Reproducibility Results are mean ± SE over 3 inference runs. Ablations cover n ∈ {1,2,4,8,16,32,64}, more-problems vs. more-approaches (§6.2), incorrect-trajectories condition (§6.3), distillation comparison (§6.4), and two model families. Theorem 4.1 is formally proved in Appendix A.6 via Taylor expansion of softmax probabilities; the proof is complete and assumptions are explicit. No code repository or trained model weights are mentioned. The LLM judge used to classify reasoning traces (GPT-4o-mini) is a proprietary model; inter-rater reliability with humans is discussed in §A.9 but inter-rater statistics are not reported in the main text. The mid-training seed data (GSM8K) and RL training data (DAPO-Math-17k) are both math-domain, which may inflate estimates of OOD generalization. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Runs on 4×H100 GPUs; data generation (128 samples × 64 heuristics × 7K questions through the base model + reward model scoring) is feasible for academic labs with GPU access. - Efficient: The GPU-hour cost of data generation is not reported, making the total compute budget relative to simply running more RL steps impossible to assess from the paper; mid-training itself is a single-epoch SFT at standard cost. - Effective: Evidence is solid for pass@k improvements at k≥2 on hard math benchmarks and OOD tasks; pass@1 improvement over vanilla RL is marginal and STaR still dominates at pass@1, so the approach is most justified when diverse sampling is the deployment mode. - Scalable: Tested only at 3B and 7B; the rollout-group-size sensitivity (optimal n≈g) and the observation that Qwen2.5–7B saturates at n=8 suggest that scaling behavior requires per-model tuning and is not yet characterized at 70B+. No software artifact, dataset, or trained weights are publicly released. Strengths - Theorem 4.1 and Proposition 4.2 provide a mechanistic, formally proved account of why N-modal mid-training helps RL — uncommon rigor in this space and a genuine intellectual contribution beyond empirical demonstration. - The controlled more-problems vs. more-approaches experiment (§6.2) cleanly isolates approach diversity from dataset size using identical instance counts, delivering a ~7% relative advantage that is robust across all k and all six benchmarks. - The Vendi Score analysis quantifies that self-generated Pólya data (Vendi 13.81 at n=16) is measurably more diverse than teacher-distilled data from QwQ-32B (Vendi 10.95), supporting the bootstrapping design choice over distillation without capability-transfer confounds. - The incorrect-trajectories ablation (§6.3) is a sharp negative control: it establishes that stylistic diversity without correctness actively harms RL, pinpointing what the mechanism actually requires. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The heuristics are inherently math-centric (sourced from Pólya's How to Solve It); whether a domain-general version exists is explicitly left open. Pass@1 improvement is modest, with STaR outperforming at that metric. The study uses a single STaR iteration to mitigate overfitting. The paper explicitly raises but does not resolve whether RL elicits genuinely emergent behaviors or primarily recombines pre-training knowledge. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No experiments at 70B+: the diminishing-return pattern already visible at 7B (where zero-shot outperforms all mid-trained models at pass@1) suggests the benefit may invert for large models with high intrinsic diversity, but this is untested. - GPU-hour cost of the full data-generation pipeline (128 samples × 64 heuristics × 7K questions + reward-model scoring) is not reported; without this, the cost-effectiveness comparison against more RL steps or larger RL datasets is incomplete. - The rollout-group-size hypothesis (optimal n≈g) is empirically supported but not theoretically derived; in practice, n and g are coupled hyperparameters that practitioners must jointly tune, adding non-trivial search cost. - Heuristic classification of generated traces (§6.1) uses GPT-4o-mini as judge; the fidelity of this classification — whether it reliably detects the intended Pólya strategy vs. surface text patterns — is not validated beyond the human-evaluation appendix, which reports agreement but not accuracy on known ground truth. - All RL training data (DAPO-Math-17k) is math-domain, which may overstate OOD benefit by preserving the in-domain structure; a code or science reasoning RL training set would provide a stronger test of whether N-modal distributions transfer across domains. Bottom Line A practitioner building RL post-training pipelines for 3B–7B reasoning models will find concrete, reproducible evidence that Pólya-style mid-training is worth the data generation overhead when pass@k at k>1 is the target metric; those optimizing pass@1 or working with large models with already-diverse priors will see marginal or no benefit.
Recommended 2605.08472 PDF
#83 — Innovative (65)

MMSkills: Towards Multimodal Skills for General Visual Agents (101 ▲ )

Kangning Zhang, Shuai Shao, Qingyao Li, Jianghao Lin, Lingyue Fu, Shijian Wang, Wenxiang Jiao, Yuan Lu, Weiwen Liu, Weinan Zhang, Yong Yu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 18 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MMSkills, a framework for representing, generating, and using reusable multimodal skill packages for visual agents, where each skill binds a textual procedure to runtime state cards (encoding when-to-use/not-use conditions and visual cues) and multi-view keyframes, retrieved and applied via a branch-loading mechanism at inference time. - Why it's important: Existing agent skill libraries encode reusable behavior as text or code, which fails for visual agents whose correct decisions depend on recognizing specific visual states — a text-only skill for chart creation cannot detect that the chart landed on the wrong sheet, causing zero-score task completion even when the procedural steps are known. - How it does it: A 5-phase LLM-based generator converts public, non-evaluation trajectories into skill packages through clustering, procedure induction, visual grounding, and meta-skill-guided auditing; at runtime, a 2-stage temporary branch selects relevant state cards and keyframe views, aligns them with the live environment, and returns a compact structured guidance tuple to the main agent without injecting the full package into the main context. - The good: MMSkills improve OSWorld overall success for every evaluated model family, including a +14.6pp gain for the 8B Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct (10.78% → 25.40%), with complementary behavioral evidence showing Qwen3-VL-235B exact repeated actions fall from 21.8% to 6.2%. - The missing: No direct comparison against concurrent multimodal skill systems (Mirage-1, XSkill, CUA-Skill) despite citing them as closely related; Super Mario Bros results rest on only 4 held-out evaluation cases, making those numbers anecdotal. - Read it or not: Worth reading for visual agent and GUI automation researchers — the state-card schema and branch-loading design are concrete, well-ablated contributions — but the missing head-to-head comparison with Mirage-1 and XSkill is a real gap that limits its standing as a definitive method.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Practical Impact (72) and Rigor (56) are the main drivers: code and the skill dataset are publicly released on GitHub and HuggingFace (a concrete positive most cluster peers only promise), and gains are large and consistent across six model families spanning 8B→235B with corroborating behavioral evidence (reduced repetition, shorter trajectories). Novelty (72) is solid — the state-card schema with decision-conditional visual grounding and branch-loading is genuinely new for visual agents. The composite is pulled below 75 by Rigor and Resilience (62): there are no error bars (all single-run), the generation-pipeline quality is uncharacterized, and most damaging, there is no head-to-head comparison against the closest concurrent multimodal skill systems (Mirage-1, XSkill, CUA-Skill), leaving the marginal value of state cards + branch loading unestablished.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 56 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 62 Resilience 62 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation GUI and game-playing agents must ground procedural decisions in visual state: a desktop agent may know the correct operation but fail to recognize that a dialog has not yet loaded; a game agent may know the intended goal but need visual cues to distinguish progress from completion. Prior skill systems (Voyager, SkillWeaver, CUA-Skill) represent reusable behavior as text, code, or execution graphs, which either omit visual grounding entirely or replay raw demonstrations that are instance-specific and context-heavy. The gap is not just missing images — a text skill with screenshots attached does not specify when a procedure is valid, which visual cues indicate the current state matches the skill's preconditions, or what visual evidence confirms completion versus failure. This leaves agents verbose-yet-underspecified when operating on real desktop environments where state transitions are visually driven. Key Idea The core insight is that reusable procedural knowledge for visual agents should be organized around decision-relevant runtime states, not action sequences. Each skill encodes not just what to do but when the procedure applies (when-to-use/when-not-to-use conditions), what visual evidence identifies the relevant state (visible cues), and what visual evidence verifies completion (verification cues). This is realized through state cards that make these conditions explicit and multi-view keyframe bundles (full-frame, focus-crop, before, after) that make states visually recognizable. A secondary insight — branch loading — prevents these reference images from contaminating the main agent's live observation context by reading them in a temporary branch that returns distilled structured guidance rather than raw evidence. Methodology MMSkills has three components. (1) Skill Package M = (D, P, S, K): D is a compact descriptor; P is a reusable textual procedure; S = {S_j} is a set of runtime state cards, each encoding when-to-use, when-not-to-use, visible_cues, verification_cue, and available_views; K = {K_j} is a set of multi-view keyframe bundles aligned with those cards. Views are drawn from {full_frame, focus_crop, before, after}. (2) Skill Generation Pipeline (5 phases): Phase 0 embeds and semantically clusters trajectory instructions; Phase 1 uses an LLM-based agent to propose atomic skills with workflow boundaries per cluster; Phase 2 deduplicates, merges, and generalizes across clusters while rejecting overly broad skills; Phase 3 drafts textual procedures and state-card schemas without reading images; Phase 4 grounds each state card in real trajectory keyframes, constructs multi-view bundles, and audits packages with reusable meta-skill templates. The meta-skill F acts as a factory providing reusable schemas and quality gates throughout generation. (3) Branch-loaded Agent: candidate skills are pre-recalled from the library before each episode. When the main agent decides to consult a skill, Stage 1 selects relevant state cards and view types using only text and state-card descriptions (loading images only when genuinely needed for state transitions or visual verification); Stage 2 aligns the selected evidence with the live observation and returns G_t = (applicable, subgoal, plan, do_not_do, verify). The main agent uses G_t as decision support but grounds executable actions in the live screenshot. Three critical design decisions: non-evaluation trajectory sources (well-justified, prevents contamination); branch loading rather than direct context injection (well-justified by ablation); conservative visual grounding policy that adds views only for state recognition, transition comparison, or completion verification (sensible but not deeply ablated). Key Results On OSWorld (360 test cases), MMSkills improve overall success rate across all six evaluated model families: Gemini 3.1 Pro 44.08% → 50.11% (+6.0pp), Gemini 3 Flash 36.65% → 47.97% (+11.3pp), Qwen3-VL-235B 21.34% → 39.17% (+17.8pp), GLM-5V 28.71% → 38.51% (+9.8pp), Kimi-K2.6 34.98% → 46.59% (+11.6pp), Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct 10.78% → 25.40% (+14.6pp). On macOSWorld (143 test cases), Gemini 3 Flash improves 55.94% → 65.73% and GLM-5V improves 34.97% → 51.75%. VAB-Minecraft shows consistent gains in both success rate and average score across all tested models, with Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct rising from 23.28% to 38.79% success. Baselines are current (frontier VLMs released 2025–2026) and comparisons are internally consistent, but there is no head-to-head comparison against other multimodal skill methods. Text-only skills are unstable — they help in some domains and hurt in others — while MMSkills consistently improve. Trajectory analysis reveals MMSkills shorten average step counts rather than adding overhead (Qwen3-VL-235B on OSWorld: 15.22 → 9.87 steps/task). Super Mario Bros evaluation uses 4 held-out cases; those reward numbers should be treated as illustrative, not statistical evidence. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The multimodal skill package representation (state cards with decision-conditional visual grounding) and branch-loading inference mechanism are genuinely novel — no prior skill framework for agents organizes reusable knowledge around runtime visual states with explicit applicability conditions. The trajectory-to-skill generation pipeline applies known LLM clustering and summarization in a new setting with a novel visual grounding stage. The broader idea of external skill retrieval for LLM agents is well-established; what is new is the specific form of the skill artifact and the loading mechanism. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present for both skill content (removing state cards and/or images) and branch-loading mechanism (direct vs. branch, with/without view selection), covering two models × two benchmarks. Both removals degrade performance; branch loading helps even for text-only skills. No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported anywhere in the paper; all numbers are single-run results. Code is publicly released (github.com/DeepExperience/MMSkills) and skills are released on HuggingFace (huggingface.co/datasets/zhangkangning/mmskills). This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.13527v2, dated May 14, 2026); results are not peer-reviewed. Methodological concern: the skill generation pipeline quality is not evaluated — there is no analysis of the fraction of generated skills that are correct, what generation errors look like, or how pipeline errors propagate downstream. The pre-recall retrieval mechanism is not ablated. Not all models are run on all benchmarks (Gemini 3.1 Pro and Kimi-K2.6 are run only on OSWorld due to inference cost), fragmenting cross-benchmark evidence. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (and SYSTEMS — a built framework evaluated on multiple benchmarks). - Practical: Runs on any infrastructure that hosts the base VLMs; GUI benchmarks are run on AWS using official benchmark images, and the framework wraps any model that can process screenshots and emit structured output. - Efficient: Branch loading adds two LLM calls per skill consultation; with invocation rates of 62–82% and 1–2.4 skill calls per episode, the overhead is approximately 1.5–5× additional LLM calls relative to no-skill baselines, but this is not quantified as token counts or wall-clock time, and two frontier models were excluded from auxiliary benchmarks due to cost — a tacit acknowledgment that overhead is non-trivial. - Effective: Improvements are consistent across 6 model families, 4 benchmarks, and corroborated by behavioral analysis (reduced repetition, shorter trajectories, increased task-complete signals), providing reasonably strong evidence that the gains reflect genuine procedural grounding rather than incidental factors. - Scalable: Tested at standard benchmark scale (247 OSWorld skill packages, 360 test cases). The paper does not test skill library scaling (e.g., 10× more skills), retrieval degradation at scale, or generation cost for very large trajectory pools; scalability is claimed but not measured. The work delivers a software artifact (code + skill dataset) and a reusable framework design. Strengths - The runtime state card schema (when-to-use/not-use, visible cues, verification cues) is a principled formalization that distinguishes this from simply attaching screenshots to text skills; it enables the agent to make an explicit applicability decision rather than blindly following a retrieved procedure. - Branch loading is a well-motivated design with ablation evidence that both separated evidence inspection (branch) and filtered view selection are individually necessary; the concept draws on progressive disclosure ideas and addresses a real failure mode (visual anchoring to reference screenshots). - Performance gains are consistent across model families spanning a 30× parameter range (8B to 235B+ frontier models), with larger relative gains for weaker models — suggesting the framework compensates for limited model-internal procedural priors rather than amplifying already-capable models. - The behavioral analysis (action primitive distribution, repetition statistics, DONE-rate changes) provides mechanistic evidence beyond success rates, lending credibility to the claim that MMSkills reshape agent decision-making rather than producing incidental wins. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors state three limitations in the conclusion: dependence on source-trajectory coverage (skills cannot represent procedures absent from the training trajectories); possible errors from skill generation or visual grounding; and extra inference cost from branch loading. They note that extending to embodied or safety-critical settings will require stronger verification and online skill repair. These are stated directly and honestly, though the inference cost point is underdeveloped relative to its practical significance. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No comparison against concurrent multimodal skill systems (Mirage-1, XSkill, CUA-Skill) despite all three being cited as the closest related work. This is the most significant omission: without such comparison, the relative contribution of state cards + branch loading over simpler multimodal skill designs cannot be assessed. - Super Mario Bros results are based on 4 held-out evaluation cases; the reported total performance and reward numbers are effectively case studies, not statistical evidence. - The generation pipeline quality is entirely uncharacterized: no analysis of skill generation failure rate, hallucinated state cards, misgrounded keyframes, or how generation errors affect downstream benchmark performance. - Inference overhead is acknowledged but not quantified as tokens, latency, or cost. The frontier-model coverage gap (Gemini 3.1 Pro and Kimi-K2.6 absent from macOSWorld, VAB-Minecraft, and Super Mario) suggests the overhead is a genuine deployment barrier, not a minor footnote. - The pre-recall retrieval mechanism (which selects candidate skills before each episode from task instructions) is not ablated or described in sufficient detail to reproduce; retrieval precision/recall is not reported. - None of the ablations disentangle the contribution of the generation pipeline quality from the skill format: it is possible that a simpler automated generation process producing the same format would achieve similar gains. Bottom Line Practitioners building GUI and visual game agents will find concrete, reproducible gains from the state-conditioned skill package format and branch-loading mechanism, but should run their own comparison against Mirage-1 or XSkill before committing to MMSkills as the best available multimodal skill approach, since that comparison is conspicuously absent from this preprint.
Recommended 2605.13527 PDF
#84 — Innovative (65)

BadSKP: Backdoor Attacks on Knowledge Graph-Enhanced LLMs with Soft Prompts

Xiaoting Lyu, Yufei Han, Hangwei Qian, Haoyuan Yu, Xiang Ao, Bin Wang, Chenxu Wang, Xiaobo Ma, Wei Wang · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces BadSKP, a multi-stage backdoor attack that targets the graph-to-prompt interface in KG-enhanced LLMs by poisoning graph node attributes to redirect the graph-induced soft prompt toward adversarial semantics in the LLM's latent space. - Why it's important: As soft-prompt KG-enhanced LLMs gain adoption, their apparent immunity to textual backdoor attacks (text-only ASR ≈ 0 under DoS) creates a false sense of security that BadSKP decisively breaks — the graph channel is itself the exploitable surface. - How it does it: BadSKP constructs an adversarial target embedding in the LLM's final hidden layer, optimizes poisoned node embeddings via cosine similarity to steer the GNN-projected soft prompt toward that target, approximates the result with fluent discrete tokens via BERT-guided greedy search, and (in the trojaned setting) aligns backdoor and main-task gradients to survive downstream clean fine-tuning. - The good: BadSKP achieves ASR 0.70–1.00 across 2 victim systems, 4 GNN architectures, 4 LLM backbones, and 4 trigger entities under both frozen and trojaned settings, while text-only baselines (ACPI, GCG) remain at or near 0.00 for the DoS objective. - The missing: The white-box threat model requires the attacker to know the LLM checkpoint and GNN architecture to compute gradients even in the "frozen" setting where the attacker supposedly cannot modify models — a contradiction that no black-box or transfer-attack experiment addresses. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers auditing or designing KG-augmented generation systems; less urgent for practitioners until a more realistic (black-box or partial-knowledge) threat model is demonstrated.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (75) and Non-Obviousness (78) are strong — the multi-stage graph-to-prompt attack and the semantic-anchoring hypothesis (validated with a 2×4 factorial and a t-test, plus the falsifiable negative result that GCG hits ~0.00 DoS ASR) show mechanistic depth rare for attack papers. Resilience (58) is the main drag: the white-box 'frozen' threat model is internally inconsistent (requires LLM checkpoint/gradient access yet assumes no model modification) with no black-box or transfer experiment, and the perplexity-defense comparison is undermined by benign-ACC collapse across all methods. Rigor (62) suffers from no seeds/error bars in the main attack tables, only 4 trigger entities, an un-ablated BERT-Contriever mismatch, and no code release. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*58 + 0.10*48 = 65.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 60 Resilience 58 Impact 48
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Knowledge graph-enhanced LLMs increasingly move beyond linearizing retrieved triples into text and instead encode retrieved subgraphs into continuous soft prompts via GNNs (e.g., G-Retriever, GNP). This dual-channel architecture — textual token embeddings plus a graph-conditioned soft prompt prepended at inference — was observed to be markedly more resistant to text-channel backdoor injection than purely textual KG prompting systems (StructGPT, RoG, ToG), which are trivially hijacked by adversarial instructions in node attributes. The security community lacked a characterization of whether this resilience constitutes a genuine security guarantee or merely reflects that existing attacks were designed for a different attack surface. The consequence of misreading apparent robustness as a guarantee is that practitioners deploying soft-prompt KG systems may forgo graph-level defenses entirely. Key Idea The key insight is that graph-derived soft prompts act as a "semantic anchor": they bias the LLM's generation-driving hidden state (final-layer, last-token position) toward query-consistent semantics and suppress adversarial token influence propagated through the text channel. Because this anchoring effect is itself induced by the graph-to-prompt pathway, an attacker who manipulates graph-level representations can redirect the anchor toward adversarial semantics rather than fighting it through text. The attack therefore bypasses the protective mechanism by corrupting its source rather than its target. Methodology The attack pipeline operates over a KG G = (V, E, A) where node text attributes are encoded by Contriever into node features, then processed by a GNN and projected into the LLM embedding space as soft prompt ẽ_Gq = MLP(g(Gq)). At inference, [ẽ_Gq; E_t] is jointly decoded. BadSKP proceeds in four stages: (1) Target embedding construction — for DoS, hadv is the mean final-layer hidden state of exemplar refusal prompts; for IrA, hadv = −h_clean, pushing generation away from query-consistent semantics. (2) Node-level embedding optimization — each poisoned node's Contriever embedding sv is treated as a continuous variable and optimized via L_bd = −cos(h_last^(L), hadv) through the full GNN→MLP→LLM forward pass. Nodes are injected and optimized sequentially (up to K=15) rather than jointly to avoid intractable mixed-integer optimization over graph structure and discrete tokens. (3) Input-space approximation — the optimized embedding sv is translated to a discrete adversarial suffix τ via greedy BERT-guided token search, selecting at each position the top-k fluent candidate that maximizes cosine similarity to sv under the Contriever encoder. (4) Persistence optimization (trojaned setting only) — soft prompt parameters θ are fine-tuned under a composite objective: α·L_bd + β·L_cln + γ·L_aln, where L_cln is negative log-likelihood on clean samples and L_aln = 1 − cos(∇_θ L_cln, ∇_θ L_bd) aligns backdoor and main-task gradients so that downstream clean fine-tuning does not erase the backdoor. Weights α=0.4, β=0.5, γ=0.1. Three design decisions warrant scrutiny: sequential node injection is justified by the gradient diffusion problem in mean-pooling GNNs and is well-reasoned; the gradient alignment loss is motivated by prior work on backdoor erasure and is well-justified; the use of BERT (a masked language model) as a fluency oracle for approximating Contriever embeddings (a dense retrieval encoder) introduces a cross-encoder mismatch that is not ablated. Key Results Frozen setting (Table III, LLaMA2-7B): Against G-Retriever, BadSKP achieves DoS ASR of 0.93–1.00 and IrA ASR of 0.88–1.00 across four trigger entities; ACPI achieves 0.00 on DoS and GCG achieves 0.00–0.03 on DoS. Against GNP, BadSKP achieves DoS ASR 0.70–0.88; baselines remain ≤0.13. Benign ACC degrades by at most 0.04 across all conditions. Trojaned setting (Table IV): BadSKP achieves DoS ASR 0.87–1.00 and IrA ASR 1.00 in 3/4 trigger entities; text-only baselines remain at 0.00 for DoS. GNN architecture generalization (Table V): ASR ≥0.70 across GCN, GAT, CGCNN, and Graph Transformer. LLM backbone generalization (Table VI): ASR ≥0.80 across LLaMA2-7B, LLaMA3-8B, Mistral-8B, and Qwen3-8B. Perplexity defense (Tables VII–VIII): BadSKP retains ASR 0.75–1.00; notably, benign ACC collapses to 0.20–0.45 under the defense for all methods, indicating the defense is destructively aggressive on short/structured node attributes. Ablations: ASR plateaus at suffix length M=30 (diminishing returns beyond); ASR grows monotonically with injected node count K, saturating near K=15. Baselines are appropriate text-channel representatives; no prior work specifically targets soft-prompt KG systems, so no stronger comparable attack baseline exists. Evaluation scale is standard (300 queries per trigger entity). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The multi-stage graph-to-prompt attack framework — latent-space target construction, continuous node embedding optimization through GNN+LLM, discrete approximation via cross-encoder greedy search, and gradient-aligned persistence — is genuinely novel and not a standard combination of GNN backdoors or LLM jailbreaks. The semantic anchoring hypothesis, formalized as an anchoring margin ∆(h) = S_Q(h) − S_C(h) and validated with a controlled 2×4 factorial experiment plus statistical testing, is a new empirical finding about how soft prompts shape LLM generation. The observation that text-only attacks (including GCG with gradient-based optimization) achieve near-zero DoS ASR against soft-prompt systems is a practically important negative result. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations on suffix length and poisoned node count are present. A controlled 2×4 factorial experiment (Table II) rigorously disentangles channel contributions to the anchoring effect. A Student's t-test (p = 4×10^−3) is reported for the hidden-state similarity claim — uncommon rigor for this area. However, no error bars or multiple-seed results appear in the main attack tables (Tables III–VI). Only 4 trigger entities (2 per dataset) are evaluated; entity-conditioned backdoor behavior may vary substantially with node degree and retrieval frequency, which is not explored. Code and data are not stated as publicly released. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.11996v1, May 2026) submitted to a journal; results are not yet peer-reviewed. The white-box assumption is clearly stated as a worst-case characterization, which is methodologically honest.
