Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the attack is implemented and measured on running KG-enhanced LLM systems. - Practical: The frozen setting requires only KG write access plus white-box knowledge of the LLM and GNN; the trojaned setting additionally requires control over the distributed soft-prompt module (a supply-chain scenario). The frozen setting is more plausible but the required white-box gradient access to the LLM checkpoint is a significant deployment constraint. - Efficient: Offline optimization involves gradient computation through up to 7B-parameter LLMs for each poisoned node suffix, which is compute-intensive but performed once; inference-time overhead is zero. - Effective: Attack consistently achieves ASR >0.70 in the frozen setting and near 1.00 in the trojaned setting while preserving benign ACC within 2–5 percentage points — the evidence strongly supports the stated attack objective. - Scalable: Tested on standard KGQA benchmarks with subgraphs of typical size; no evaluation on larger KGs, longer retrieval chains, or multi-hop reasoning at scale. The sequential node injection strategy scales linearly with K by design but this is not stress-tested. The work delivers a paper-form attack description; no code, model weights, or dataset artifact is stated as released. Strengths - The semantic anchoring analysis is formalized with a precise definition and validated with a controlled factorial experiment and statistical testing, providing mechanistic depth absent from most attack papers. This interpretation is independently useful for understanding soft-prompt behavior. - Generalization is unusually broad: 2 victim systems, 4 GNN encoders, 4 LLM backbones, 4 trigger entities, 2 datasets, 2 attack objectives, 2 attack settings, and one defense — making the attack's robustness across configurations convincing. - The gradient alignment loss for backdoor persistence in the trojaned setting is technically sound and directly addresses the known weakness of backdoor erasure under fine-tuning, with results showing near-perfect ASR persistence after clean fine-tuning. - The demonstration that text-only attacks (including GCG, a strong gradient-based adversarial method) achieve ≈0.00 DoS ASR against soft-prompt systems is a clear, falsifiable, and practically important result that reframes the security surface for this class of systems. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge that the white-box threat model (attacker knows retrieval protocol, GNN/projector architecture, and LLM checkpoint) represents a worst-case security analysis rather than a claim about universal real-world deployability, framing it as characterizing whether the attack surface is fundamentally exploitable. They do not acknowledge the narrow trigger entity scope, the BERT-Contriever mismatch in step 3, the absence of a transfer/black-box evaluation, or the severely degraded ACC under the perplexity defense. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The white-box frozen setting is internally inconsistent: the attacker must compute gradients through the LLM (requiring checkpoint access) yet cannot modify model parameters. No experiment tests whether the attack degrades when the attacker must transfer across LLM checkpoints, which is the most natural relaxation. - The anchoring-strength monitoring defense is described qualitatively only — "experimental results show that this defense effectively detects poisoned inputs" — with no detection rate, false positive rate, ROC curve, or table of results, making the defense claim unverifiable from the paper. - The BERT-Contriever mismatch in step 3 is never ablated: BERT (masked LM, different tokenizer and embedding space) is used to enforce fluency while the objective is alignment with Contriever embeddings. Using Contriever itself for candidate scoring, or ablating the gap, would validate the approximation quality. - Only 4 trigger entities are evaluated; high-degree hub entities (e.g., "United States") versus rare entities may behave very differently because mean pooling in G-Retriever would dilute adversarial node signals in large subgraphs. This is not addressed. - The perplexity defense comparison is undermined by the severe ACC collapse (to 0.20–0.45 from 0.55–0.73) across all attack methods, suggesting the defense threshold is miscalibrated rather than that BadSKP specifically evades it. Bottom Line A security researcher or architect auditing KG-enhanced LLM deployments will gain concrete and well-supported insight: the graph-to-prompt pathway is a real attack surface that text-level defenses cannot address, the mechanistic explanation is unusually rigorous for this area, and the generalization results across architectures are convincing — but the implausibly strong white-box threat model and the absent black-box evaluation limit how directly these findings transfer to real threat assessments.
Recommended 2605.11996 PDF
#85 — Innovative (65)

Ensembling Tabular Foundation Models-A Diversity Ceiling And A Calibration Trap

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper benchmarks six ensemble strategies applied to a pool of six tabular foundation models (TFMs) across 153 OpenML classification tasks, showing that near-redundant predictions (mean pairwise Q-statistic = 0.961) impose a hard ceiling on ensemble gains and that logistic-regression stacking trades calibration quality for accuracy. - Why it's important: Practitioners routinely ensemble TFMs expecting reliability gains, but this work shows the best ensemble buys only +0.18% accuracy at 253× the compute of the best single TFM, and three of six ensembles are statistically worse than the best base — a concrete warning against reflexive ensembling. - How it does it: It measures pairwise Q-statistics to quantify diversity, formalizes a convex-combination ceiling (bounded by the disagreement-set fraction), and evaluates all strategies with Friedman/Nemenyi/Wilcoxon tests plus a calibration/selective-prediction metric suite (ECE, Brier-REL, AURC, worst-group accuracy). - The good: The finding that three of six ensembles are significantly worse than the best single TFM (p < 0.05, Wilcoxon) while the best ensemble gains only +0.18% is a stark, actionable result backed by proper multi-dataset statistical testing. - The missing: No GBDT baselines are included, leaving open the central question of whether out-of-class diversity (TFM + GBDT) breaks through the within-class ceiling — which is arguably the most practically relevant comparison. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone making deployment or competition decisions about TFM ensembling; it provides a statistically grounded answer with a clear practical recommendation (greedy selection) and identifies the calibration trap of stacking.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (70) and Resilience (70) are the strengths: multi-level statistics (Friedman/Nemenyi/Wilcoxon), a formalized convex-combination ceiling proposition, 153 datasets, and a notably honest contamination disclosure framing accuracy deltas as upper bounds. Novelty (60) is a genuinely new diagnostic (diversity ceiling + calibration trap) for the TFM family, though all ensemble strategies are prior work. Completeness (65) is dragged down by the absence of the central GBDT baseline — the comparison that would test whether out-of-class diversity breaks the ceiling — and Impact (50) reflects no software artifact beyond a practical recommendation. Single seed and preprint status further cap Rigor. Weighted composite is 65.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 70 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Tabular foundation models have closed much of the gap with gradient-boosted trees, but no single TFM wins uniformly: across 153 datasets, the median per-dataset accuracy spread between best and worst TFM is 1.95%, exceeding 5% on 24% of tasks. Ensembling is the standard response to this instability. However, classical ensemble theory requires base classifiers to make decorrelated errors, and TFMs trained via in-context learning (ICL) on synthetic priors may violate this assumption: if two TFMs consult similar priors, they implicitly average over similar posterior families and fail on the same instances. The open question is whether TFM ensembling delivers meaningful benefit or merely multiplies compute. Key Idea TFMs trained with ICL on synthetic priors approximate Bayesian model averaging at inference time; because their priors are broadly similar, their errors concentrate on the same instances, yielding a mean pairwise Q-statistic of 0.961. The paper formalizes this as a convex-combination ceiling: for any weighted average of classifiers, the ensemble accuracy advantage over any single base is bounded above by the disagreement-set fraction (1 − |C|/|X|), which collapses toward zero as Q → 1. High Q is therefore not just a descriptive statistic but a structural bound that explains why expensive ensembles cannot recover meaningful gains. Methodology Six strategies are evaluated over a fixed pool of six base TFMs (TabPFNv2.5, TabPFNv2.6, TabICL, TabICLv2, LimiX, OrionMSPv1.5): (1) weighted averaging with performance-proportional weights; (2) greedy selection with forward selection with replacement over S=50 iterations, matching AutoGluon defaults; (3) stacking with a logistic-regression meta-learner on 5-fold OOF predictions; (4) temperature-scaled blending, fitting per-base temperature by NLL minimization then averaging uniformly; (5) two-level cascade stacking with skip connections (AutoGluon high-quality preset, 3-fold OOF, S=50 greedy final layer); and (6) deep ensemble averaging 3 seeds per TFM then combining by performance weights. The 153 datasets span CC18, TALENT, and TabZilla (128 to 581,012 samples; median 3,196; 2–10 classes), using 80/20 train/test splits with 75/25 internal train/validation. Primary metrics are accuracy, weighted F1, ROC-AUC, log-loss, and fit time; extended metrics (ECE, Brier reliability, AURC, coverage@95%, worst-group accuracy) are reported on the TabArena subset. Statistical significance uses Friedman global test (χ² = 389.95, p < 10⁻³⁰), Nemenyi post-hoc correction (CD = 1.347 at α = 0.05, K = 12, N = 153), and pairwise Wilcoxon. Three critical design decisions: using the intersection of completed tasks for fair comparison (sensible, but drops some large-dataset runs due to memory constraints); a single fixed seed per dataset (statistical power comes from N = 153 tasks, not within-task replication); and explicit exclusion of GBDTs (deliberate scope restriction, acknowledged as a limitation). Key Results The accuracy spread across the top eight methods is 0.45 percentage points (0.872–0.882). Against the strongest single TFM (TabICLv2, accuracy 0.881, fit time 0.7s), only Cascade_2level beats it significantly (+0.18%, p = 0.008) at 178.5s per dataset (253× overhead). Greedy_Selection ties (+0.01%, not significant) at 6.7s. Stacking_LR ties on accuracy (−0.03%, not significant) but ranks worst on log-loss among all ensembles (rank 8.13 vs. TabICLv2's 4.11) with ECE = 0.0272 vs. TabICLv2's 0.0236. Three ensembles — WA, Temp_Scaled, DeepEnsemble_3seed — are significantly worse than TabICLv2 on accuracy (p < 0.05). The Nemenyi equivalence group at the top contains Cascade_2level, Stacking_LR, Greedy_Selection, and TabICLv2: three ensembles and the best base are statistically indistinguishable. DeepEnsemble_3seed produces the worst AURC (0.0617, 28% above TabICLv2's 0.0483) at 75.7s — Pareto-dominated by simpler strategies. Cascade_2level achieves the only qualitative win: worst-group accuracy of 0.803 versus 0.776 for Greedy and Stacking_LR, suggesting its skip-connection architecture implicitly down-weights systematically biased bases. The Pearson correlation between ensemble gain and inter-base accuracy spread is −0.03, confirming the diversity ceiling is roughly uniform across dataset shapes. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW BENCHMARK. The diversity-ceiling diagnostic connecting ICL-on-synthetic-priors to a formal convex-combination bound and then empirically validating it with Q-statistics across a large benchmark is genuinely novel for this model family. The calibration trap (stacking improves accuracy while degrading log-loss/ECE) is a new demonstration in the TFM setting. All ensemble strategies are prior work; applying them with a full calibration/fairness metric overlay specifically to TFM pools on 153 tasks is the new empirical contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility Statistical testing is appropriate and multilevel: Friedman global test, Nemenyi CD correction, pairwise Wilcoxon signed-rank. Single seed per dataset is a genuine power limitation, though N = 153 tasks provides reasonable aggregate stability. Diversity statistics (Q-statistic, Cohen's κ) are computed transparently in Appendix E with the full formula. The convex-combination ceiling is formalized as a proposition with a proof sketch in Appendix D, adding theoretical grounding beyond pure empirics. Implementation uses the public TabTune library (arXiv:2511.02802); no standalone benchmark repo URL is provided. Contamination is honestly flagged: several base TFMs were pretrained on OpenML-overlapping data, so accuracy deltas are upper bounds on true ensemble effects — the authors state this directly rather than burying it. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.18696v1, May 2026), not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: All experiments run on a single H100 (80GB) GPU; the recommended strategy (greedy selection at ~6.7s per dataset) is feasible on standard ML hardware. - Efficient: Cascade_2level costs 253× more compute than the best single TFM for a +0.18% accuracy gain; greedy selection at ~10× cost achieves equivalent accuracy, making it the Pareto-dominant practical choice. - Effective: The evidence shows TFM-only ensembling is not reliably more effective than the best single TFM for accuracy, and actively degrades calibration when using a discriminative meta-learner; cascade is the sole exception, narrowly, on worst-group accuracy. - Scalable: Tested on datasets up to 581,012 samples; some large tasks were dropped for individual models due to memory constraints; no claims or tests at >1M samples or high-cardinality settings. The paper delivers no software artifact beyond dependence on the existing TabTune library; its output is benchmark results and a practical recommendation. Strengths - The diversity ceiling is both formally grounded (Appendix D proposition bounding ensemble gain by disagreement-set fraction) and empirically validated (Q̄ = 0.961, Cohen's κ = 0.856), producing a rare combination of theoretical justification and empirical confirmation in a benchmarking paper. - The calibration/uncertainty/group-robustness metric suite (ECE, Brier-REL, AURC, Cov@95, WGA) reveals qualitatively distinct failure modes — stacking harms calibration, deep ensemble harms selective prediction — that accuracy-only benchmarks would miss entirely. - The scale of evaluation (153 datasets, 12 methods, proper multiple-comparison correction) is sufficient to yield statistically credible conclusions rather than dataset-specific anecdotes. - Honest contamination disclosure with an explicit upward-bias interpretation of accuracy deltas is a methodological strength rarely seen in TFM benchmark papers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: (1) a single seed per dataset means power comes from across-dataset variation, not within-dataset replication; (2) some large tasks were dropped for individual TFMs due to memory constraints, so Table 1 reports the intersection; (3) no GBDT baselines — the comparison is strictly within the TFM class, leaving hybrid TFM+GBDT ensembles as open future work; (4) contamination between TFM pretraining data and OpenML means reported accuracy deltas are upper bounds. All four are confronted directly in §6.1–6.2, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The worst-group accuracy advantage of Cascade_2level (0.803 vs. 0.776) is explained as cascade "implicitly down-weighting systematically biased models" via skip connections, but this mechanism is entirely speculative — no ablation isolates it, and the margin is narrow enough to warrant caution. - The benchmark is classification-only; TFMs are also applied to regression and survival tasks where base-model error correlations may differ, so the ceiling finding does not generalize without additional study. - The proposed gating-learner alternative (a small model trained on dataset metafeatures to select the best TFM per dataset) is presented as future work without any preliminary evidence, yet it directly addresses the per-dataset best-base variance that ensembling is trying to solve — its omission makes the "greedy selection is the practical default" recommendation potentially conservative. - The paper's empirical conclusions may have limited shelf life: if continued pretraining on real-world data (e.g., RealTabPFN) diversifies TFM priors and lowers the Q-statistic, the ceiling lifts and the recommendations change; the paper does not characterize how sensitive the ceiling is to the TFM pool composition. Bottom Line Any practitioner or researcher deciding whether to ensemble TFMs in production or competition settings should read this: it delivers a statistically rigorous, compute-aware answer — greedy selection is the practical default, cascade stacking is only worth the cost if worst-group accuracy is the primary target, and logistic-regression stacking is a calibration trap to avoid.
Search Results 2605.18696 PDF
#86 — Innovative (65)

HodgeCover: Higher-Order Topological Coverage Drives Compression of Sparse Mixture-of-Experts

arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces HodgeCover, a learning-free expert-selection algorithm for compressing sparse Mixture-of-Experts layers that uses the harmonic kernel of the simplicial Laplacian on a 2-complex built from expert merge barriers as the selection criterion, and a hybrid HodgeCover+Wanda that combines it with weight-level sparsity. - Why it's important: Deploying production MoE models (Mixtral, DeepSeek-V3, Qwen) on smaller hardware without retraining requires expert compression, but every existing method scores experts on pairwise signals that are provably blind to irreducible higher-order mergeability obstructions — cyclic incompatibilities among triples of experts that no pair score detects. - How it does it: It Hodge-decomposes the edge-supported KL merge-barrier signal on a simplicial 2-complex (vertices = experts, edges = pairwise KL barriers, faces = triplet KL barriers) to isolate the harmonic component — the fraction unreducible by any vertex potential or triangle-boundary correction — then greedily covers the harmonic-critical edges and triplet-critical triangles via a monotone submodular objective. - The good: At 66% expert reduction on Qwen 3.5-35B, HodgeCover+Wanda outperforms STUN+Wanda by +12.6 pp downstream-task average (74.6% vs. 62.0%), driven by GSM8K (85.5% vs. 22.1%), and ablation confirms the Hodge decomposition itself — not merely triplet input — accounts for +5.74 pp of that gap. - The missing: Hyperparameters (p, qT, λe, λt, α) are set by qualitative inspection on a single Qwen 3.5-35B layer; no empirical sensitivity sweep across values or models is reported, and the triangle cap (|T| ≤ 500) is fixed by wall-clock budget rather than by ablation. - Read it or not: Essential reading for practitioners compressing large MoE models and for researchers in topological/spectral methods applied to deep learning; skippable for those focused on fine-tuning or distillation-based compression.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (85) and Novelty (81) are the highest in the cluster — operationalizing the harmonic kernel of the simplicial Laplacian as a parameter-selection objective (not merely a diagnostic), backed by a formal inexpressibility result, is a genuinely new and deep idea, with the +5.74pp Hodge-vs-triplet-penalty ablation showing the gain comes from how triplet information is processed. The composite is pulled down by Resilience (56) and Impact (52): the headline +12.6pp over STUN+Wanda collapses to a modest +0.3–0.8pp over the author-computed matched control REAP+Wanda at a 19× plan-time overhead, no public code is mentioned, and a tough reviewer would press the contested margin. Rigor (62) also suffers from absent seeds/error bars and hyperparameters (p, qT, λe, λt, α) fixed by qualitative inspection on a single layer with no sensitivity sweep. Weighted: 0.20*81 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*85 + 0.15*62 + 0.15*56 + 0.10*52 = 65.
empirical Novelty 81 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 85 Completeness 62 Resilience 56 Impact 52
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Sparse MoE models such as Mixtral, OLMoE, Qwen 3.5, and DeepSeek-V3 host dozens to hundreds of feed-forward experts per layer but activate only a small constant subset per token. Reducing expert count without retraining would let practitioners reuse pretrained checkpoints on smaller fleets. The dominant family of learning-free compressors (REAP, REAM, MC-SMoE, STUN) ranks experts using pairwise signals — output saliency, routing cosine, behavioral similarity. The structural gap is that three experts can each be pairwise compatible yet form an irreducible collective incompatibility: any score that factors through per-expert or per-pair quantities cannot detect this. Prior methods that do ingest triplets (hypergraph spectral cuts, triplet-loss penalties) aggregate them into sum-of-pairs surrogates or binary vetoes, discarding the topologically irreducible residue. The harmonic kernel of the simplicial Laplacian is the precise algebraic object capturing that residue, but topological deep learning has previously used it only as a diagnostic, never as a parameter-selection objective. Key Idea The core insight is that the edge-supported KL merge-barrier signal on a simplicial 2-complex over experts admits an orthogonal Hodge decomposition into gradient, curl, and harmonic components, and the harmonic component is exactly the part that no vertex potential (per-expert score) or triangle-boundary correction (per-face adjustment) can explain. This makes the harmonic component a principled, topology-based certificate of irreducible mergeability obstruction. Turning this decomposition into a selection objective — covering the top-p% harmonic-critical edges and top-qT% triplet-critical triangles with a greedy submodular set function — is the key move that elevates Hodge theory from analysis tool to engineering criterion. Methodology Given a sparse MoE layer with n experts, HodgeCover proceeds in four stages. (1) Barrier construction: compute all pairwise KL merge barriers bij (O(n²) forward passes on 2,048 C4-train tokens) and up to 500 sampled triplet barriers bijk for 3-cliques of the median-barrier subgraph. (2) Complex construction: form the simplicial 2-complex K = (V, E, T) with the complete edge set and the curated triangle set; apply the 1-Hodge Laplacian L1 = ∂₁ᵀ∂₁ + ∂₂∂₂ᵀ using the unweighted form so the harmonic projector is topologically invariant across layers. Solve two sequential least-squares systems via Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse to extract bgrad, bcurl, bharm. (3) Critical simplex extraction: designate top-p% edges by |bharm,e| as harmonic-critical and top-qT% triangles by |bijk| as triplet-critical. (4) Greedy coverage: maximize the monotone submodular objective Φ(S) = Σsal(i) + λe|CE(S)|/|E| + λt|CT(S)|/|T| with the standard (1 − 1/e) greedy guarantee. Non-survivors are redirected to the nearest survivor under a Hodge-weighted barrier that penalizes routing through high-harmonic edges. HodgeCover+Wanda then applies unstructured Wanda weight pruning on survivors at residual sparsity r2 ≈ 0.575 (at 66% total compression with r1 = 0.20 expert drop). Key design decisions: (a) unweighted Laplacian to decouple topology from barrier normalization — well-justified; (b) complete edge set rather than thresholded subgraph to avoid discontinuous spectral behavior — well-justified with analysis; (c) triangle cap at 500 fixed by compute budget rather than sensitivity study — not ablated. Key Results Evaluation covers OLMoE-1B-7B (64 experts, 16 layers), Qwen 3.5-35B-A3B (256 experts, 40 layers), and Qwen 3.5-122B-A10B (256 experts, 48 layers) at 33% and 66% expert reduction rates. Baselines: REAP, REAM, MC-SMoE, STUN+Wanda (published), plus REAP+Wanda and REAM+Wanda computed by the authors. Benchmarks: WikiText-103 perplexity, C4 perplexity, and 9 downstream tasks (ARC-c/e, BoolQ, HellaSwag, MMLU 5-shot, PIQA, TruthfulQA-MC2, WinoGrande, GSM8K 8-shot). At 66% on Qwen 3.5-35B-A3B, HodgeCover+Wanda achieves 74.6% DS-Avg vs. 62.0% for STUN+Wanda (+12.6 pp) and 74.1% for the matched-control REAP+Wanda (+0.5 pp); GSM8K 85.5% vs. 22.1% (STUN+W) and vs. 87.6% (REAP, expert-count axis only). At 66% on Qwen 3.5-122B-A10B, HodgeCover+Wanda achieves 75.9% DS-Avg vs. 70.8% for STUN+Wanda and 75.4% for REAP+Wanda. On the expert-count axis alone (no Wanda), HodgeCover matches REAP within ±0.3 pp DS-Avg on all four Qwen cells. The ablation on Qwen 3.5-35B-A3B at 66% shows the Hodge step accounts for +5.74 pp over Triplet-Penalty soft (same triplet input, no Hodge) and +5.7 pp over Greedy-Barrier; removing triangles entirely costs −11.5 pp; using a binary triangle veto (Triplet-Hypergraph) collapses by −30.5 pp. Harmonic energy fraction ρharm ranges 29–62% of per-layer barrier energy across the three models, confirming the obstruction is non-trivial. Plan-time for the full HodgeCover pipeline on Qwen 3.5-35B is ~480 seconds vs. ~25 seconds for REAP — a 19× overhead, offline and amortized. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuinely novel contribution is operationalizing the harmonic kernel of the simplicial Laplacian as a parameter-selection objective for MoE compression — prior topological deep learning work uses Hodge decomposition only as an analysis diagnostic. The inexpressibility result (Remark 1, Proposition 2) formally separates HodgeCover from all per-expert-ranking methods. The empirical finding that harmonic energy constitutes 29–62% of per-layer barrier energy in production MoE models is also new. The application of greedy submodular coverage and the composition with Wanda are standard techniques applied in a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation structure is strong: four ablations systematically isolate the Hodge decomposition (vs. soft triplet penalty), the triangle term (vs. harmonic-edge-only), and hard vs. soft triplet constraints. The Hodge-step contribution of +5.74 pp on Qwen 3.5-35B at 66% is the sharpest number in the paper and is experimentally clean. Matched-control hybrids (REAP+Wanda, REAM+Wanda) are computed by the authors to control for Stage-2 Wanda differences — a creditable methodological choice. Error bars, multiple seeds, and significance tests are absent throughout. Hyperparameters (p = qT = 20%, λe = 1.0, λt = 0.5, α = 3.0) are set by qualitative inspection on a single layer of a single model; no empirical sensitivity grid is reported. MC-SMoE is evaluated without its mandatory KD pass (training on 2,048 tokens causes overfitting), which reduces its baseline strength and is disclosed. Calibration corpus is fixed at 2,048 C4-train tokens, matched across all methods. Code and model weights are not mentioned as released; the paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.13997v1). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (compression system evaluated by downstream benchmarks and perplexity). - Practical: Runs on 2× NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell GPUs (98 GB each); the barrier sweep and Hodge projection fit within standard research hardware at n ≤ 256 experts, though 256-expert Qwen models require ~196 GB GPU memory for the uncompressed checkpoint before compression. - Efficient: Plan-time is ~480 seconds for Qwen 3.5-35B (vs. ~25 s for REAP), a 19× overhead paid once offline; inference throughput of the compressed model matches REAP+Wanda to within 6% at 66%; the dead-expert ratio at 66% is 1–3 pp higher than REAM+Wanda on Qwen scales, a real cost acknowledged by the authors. - Effective: Achieves best C4 perplexity on every (model, rate) cell for the hybrid axis and best WikiText perplexity on every 66% cell; the +12.6 pp DS-Avg gain over STUN+Wanda on Qwen 3.5-35B at 66% is large and driven by real task performance (GSM8K, MMLU), not perplexity artifacts. - Scalable: Evaluated at up to 122B total parameters (Qwen 3.5-122B-A10B); the pairwise sweep scales as O(n²|D|d²) — manageable at n = 256 but not evaluated beyond this expert count; the authors defer multimodal and RL post-trained MoE checkpoints to future work. The method delivers a compression pipeline (not a library or CLI), with algorithmic pseudocode and full hyperparameter tables provided but no public code repository mentioned. Strengths - The Hodge-decomposition framing is mathematically precise and identifies a structural failure mode of all prior pairwise/triplet compressors through a clean inexpressibility argument (Remark 1, Proposition 2) — this is genuine novelty, not a relabeling. - The ablation isolating the Hodge decomposition from the triplet input (+5.74 pp over Triplet-Penalty on Qwen 3.5-35B at 66%) is the strongest evidence in the paper: the improvement comes from how triplet information is processed, not merely from having it. - Diagnostic measurements (ρharm ∈ [0.29, 0.62] across 104 layers of three production models, Fig. 2) provide evidence that the harmonic obstruction is pervasive in real MoE structures, not a pathological corner case, which directly motivates why the method should work. - The choice of unweighted Laplacian (barriers enter as signal, not as operator weights) is a non-obvious but well-justified design decision that keeps the harmonic projector topologically invariant across layers and model families. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) HodgeCover cannot recover the last few perplexity points that fine-tuning or knowledge distillation would address; (2) plan-time on 35B-parameter models is ~480 seconds (vs. ~25 s for REAP), though amortized across compression rates; (3) evaluation is restricted to language MoE; multimodal and RL post-trained checkpoints are deferred; (4) the triangle cap |T| = 500 is set by wall-clock budget. The authors confront these limitations directly and without minimization, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The hyperparameter sensitivity gap is significant: p, qT, λe, λt, α are set by qualitative inspection on one layer of one model and applied universally. The paper provides only analytical boundary behavior (saturation at p → 100%, vanishing at p → 0%) without any empirical sweep. Given that the ablation shows +5.74 pp difference from topological structure, the question of how sensitive this is to the 20% threshold is material. - The triangle cap at |T| = 500 is set by compute budget and is not ablated across values. Appendix A.9 notes that |T|/C(n,3) → 0 at Qwen scale, and that the harmonic component vanishes as |T| → C(n,3) — but where the method degrades on the path between these regimes is not measured. - At 33% compression, STUN+Wanda beats HodgeCover+Wanda on WikiText for OLMoE and Qwen 3.5-35B (e.g., 8.86 vs. 9.18 for Qwen 3.5-35B), and the paper explains this as a C4 perplexity tradeoff, but the explanation for why HodgeCover+Wanda is worse at 33% on WikiText is not mechanistically explored. - The Triplet-Penalty ablation was not run on Qwen 3.5-122B-A10B because the 35B run "cleared the ablation gating threshold" — this is an incomplete ablation on the largest model, where results might differ given the very different ρharm profile. - The matched-control REAP+Wanda closes most of STUN+Wanda's gap; the margin of HodgeCover+Wanda over REAP+Wanda at 66% is 0.3–0.8 pp DS-Avg on the two Qwen scales — real but modest, and driven by a 19× plan-time overhead versus REAP. Bottom Line A practitioner running aggressive (≥66%) learning-free compression of large production MoE models — particularly Qwen-family models — will find HodgeCover+Wanda the current best option on the aggressive frontier, at the cost of a 19× plan-time overhead over REAP; a researcher in topological methods applied to neural network compression will find the Hodge-decomposition framing and the inexpressibility result the most durable contributions.
Recommended 2605.13997 PDF
#87 — Innovative (64)

XFP: Quality-Targeted Adaptive Codebook Quantization with Sparse Outlier Separation for LLM Inference

Thomas Witt · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces XFP, a calibration-free LLM weight quantizer that accepts per-layer cosine-similarity quality floors as input and automatically determines codebook size, outlier budget, and bit-packing per layer, integrated into vLLM via the MultiQuant engine. - Why it's important: Fitting 100B–400B parameter MoE models on workstation-class GPUs (2×96 GB) at usable throughput is blocked by conventional fixed-bit-width formats that cannot exploit the heterogeneous weight distributions across attention, shared-expert, and routed-expert components. - How it does it: Each weight matrix is decomposed into a sparse fp16 outlier residual (weights with |w−µ| > 4σ, capped at 2%) and a dense sub-byte index into a per-group Lloyd-learned codebook; a two-threshold auto-select algorithm then picks the minimum bit width (N ∈ {2,3,4}) per layer meeting the operator-specified cosine floor. - The good: On Qwen3.5-122B-A10B, XFP V2 delivers 110 tok/s single-stream decode versus Marlin INT4's 73.6 tok/s (+49%) at identical hardware (RTX PRO 6000 TP=1), with GSM8K strict-match of 94.62±0.67% versus 95.27±0.12%—a 0.65 pp gap within the Lloyd-initialization noise envelope. - The missing: The 49% throughput advantage is attributed to reading ~3.97 vs 4.0 effective bits, but a 0.03-bit difference cannot account for a 49% memory-bandwidth speedup; the actual source (fused kernel architecture and activation-row SMEM caching versus Marlin's design) is not disentangled, leaving the throughput claim mechanistically unsupported. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners deploying large MoE models on workstation-class hardware who need a calibration-free alternative to Marlin/NVFP4; the H-Process section offers practical deployment methodology unavailable elsewhere.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Novelty (72) and Non-Obviousness (72) reflect the genuinely useful quality-floor-as-input inversion plus calibration-free two-threshold MoE auto-select, and Impact (68) credits the released vLLM fork that enables otherwise-impossible 397B deployment. Resilience (53) and Rigor (56) pull the composite down sharply: the central 49% throughput claim is mechanistically unsupported (a 0.03-bit difference cannot explain it; the unablated fused-kernel SMEM caching is the likely cause), accuracy is validated only on GSM8K, the 397B result is single-seed, and it is a solo non-institutional preprint with self-reported numbers. Honesty about limitations is a credibility positive, but this is off-cluster (weight, not KV, quantization).
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 56 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 64 Resilience 53 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deploying 100B–400B MoE language models on workstation-class GPUs (RTX PRO 6000, DGX Spark; 96–120 GB VRAM) is constrained by three structural problems. First, transformer weight matrices exhibit heavy-tailed distributions with catastrophic outliers (observed up to 49σ in GLM-4.7-Flash attention layers); uniform INT-N quantization wastes representational capacity on the outlier range at the cost of bulk resolution. Second, MoE models exhibit sharp heterogeneity: routed experts tolerate 2-bit quantization with negligible accuracy loss while attention and shared-expert projections cannot, but a single global quality threshold cannot exploit this asymmetry. Third, workstation Blackwell GPUs (SM120/121) have 99 KB opt-in SMEM per CTA—less than half the 228 KB of data-center SMs—limiting activation-row caching and capping the reachable sequence length in memory-bound decode. NVFP4's fixed 16-entry codebook and its dedicated Tensor Core path (unavailable on SM120 at the time of the work) compound these problems. Key Idea: Invert the conventional quantization workflow: instead of requiring operators to specify bit width and accept the resulting quality, require them to specify a cosine-similarity reconstruction floor and let the quantizer determine the minimum bit width per layer that meets it. The two-threshold variant (one strict floor for attention/shared-expert, one lazy floor for routed-expert MoE) is the mechanism that makes this tractable on heterogeneous MoE models without any calibration data or Hessian computation. Methodology: XFP decomposes each weight matrix W as W ≈ W_outlier + Codebook[Q_bulk]. Outlier extraction: compute per-matrix µ and σ; extract weights where |w−µ| > kσ (default k=4.0, capped at 2%) into a sparse fp16 residual, replace with µ in the bulk. Codebook learning: initialize 2^N entries via CDF-uniform quantiles of the bulk; run 20 rounds of Lloyd (nearest-neighbor assignment + conditional-mean centroid update); store as fp16 per output channel (V2) or as a shared library of L=32 codebooks per layer with 5-bit per-group assignment (V2a). Auto-select (Algorithm 1): test N ∈ {2,3,4} ascending; return the first N whose median per-channel cosine similarity meets the active threshold (strict for attention/shared, lazy for routed-expert MoE). The fused decode kernel fuses (1) N-bit depack to int8, (2) codebook gather from SMEM, (3) sparse outlier scatter-add, and (4) bf16 GEMM via Tensor Cores; the activation row is cached in SMEM once per CTA. V2a replaces per-channel codebooks with a layer-level library of 32 codebooks (1 KB constant SMEM), extending the activation-row cache from K≤8,192 to K≤32,768 on SM120/121 at the cost of lower per-group codebook precision. The H-Process iterates (τ_strict, τ_lazy) from aggressive to conservative until the model clears both an OOM boundary (during quantize-on-load Lloyd spike) and a garbage-output boundary (cosine-passing but degenerate generation). Critical design decisions: (1) cosine similarity rather than MSE as the quality gate—motivated by the claim that GEMM is direction-sensitive and activations absorb magnitude scaling via norms; empirically, XFP2 shows 12× higher MSE than XFP4 with identical math accuracy; (2) median aggregation over channels for the quality gate, avoiding mean (outlier-sensitive) and min (overly conservative at N=2); (3) sampling 4 experts per MoE layer for auto-select rather than the full population—validated offline to produce ≤11.5% bit-width disagreements on Qwen-122B (with zero measured math impact attributed to the outlier path absorbing disagreement error). Key Results: On Qwen3.5-122B-A10B (RTX PRO 6000, TP=1): XFP V2 achieves 110 tok/s vs. Marlin INT4 (AutoRound) at 73.6 tok/s (+49%), with GSM8K strict-match 94.62±0.67% vs. 95.27±0.12% (lm-eval-harness 0.4.11, 5-shot, 1,319 problems, 3 seeds). At TP=2, XFP reaches 138 tok/s at 94.49±0.57% GSM8K. The ~0.65 pp accuracy gap is within the cross-seed Lloyd jitter envelope (reported ±0.67% std). On DGX Spark (SM121), XFP outperforms Marlin INT4 by 16–18% on long/medium decode. On Qwen3.5-397B-A17B under H-Process H1.5 (τ_strict=0.96, τ_lazy=0.93, ~3.4 effective bits): 100.9 tok/s long-output decode at 66.72% GSM8K strict-match (full 1,319-problem set, single seed; multi-seed evaluation in progress). Compared to AutoRound INT4 with REAP expert pruning (−24%) on the same hardware, XFP H1.5 outperforms on both GSM8K and throughput while retaining the full expert population. On GLM-4.7-Flash, auto-mode converges to ~3.0 effective bits overall. Outlier extraction ablation on GLM-4.7-Flash (XFP3): attention kv_a layers show 1.90× MSE reduction from outlier extraction; routed-expert layers show only 1.02×. The H-Process operating window for 397B is extremely narrow: τ_lazy=0.93 fits at 83 GiB/GPU steady state; τ_lazy=0.935 causes OOM during the encoding spike across three attempted loads. The evaluation benchmark suite is limited to GSM8K; MMLU and additional reasoning suites are not evaluated. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuinely novel contribution is the inversion of the quantization workflow (quality floor as input, bit width as output) combined with the two-threshold MoE-aware auto-select mechanism, all without calibration data or Hessian computation. The individual components—sparse outlier separation (SpQR), learned codebooks (AQLM), sub-byte packing—are known; the integration that enables calibration-free adaptive quantization for heterogeneous MoE models, plus the fused decode kernel and H-Process deployment methodology, represents a meaningful engineering synthesis rather than a straightforward combination. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper uses lm-eval-harness 0.4.11 with 1,319 GSM8K problems and 3 seeds for the main 122B accuracy claim—adequate sample size. The 397B H1.5 accuracy result (66.72%) is reported as single-seed at submission, with multi-seed evaluation explicitly marked as in progress; this is a meaningful caveat and the authors state it clearly. Ablations are present and informative: outlier extraction ablation (Table 5), MoE sampling validation (Tables 16–17), V2 vs. V2a probes, FP8 KV-cache confound check. Error bars are reported for lm-eval results. Lloyd non-determinism (±8pp on 50-problem bench.py probes) is documented and explained. Code is available at https://github.com/flash7777/vllm/tree/multiquant. No comparison against GPTQ, AQLM, or QuIP# in accuracy terms—only throughput versus Marlin INT4. The accuracy baseline (AutoRound INT4 via Marlin) is the strongest available 4-bit calibration-aware method; this is a credibility-positive baseline choice. The throughput gap (49% advantage) is attributed to reading ~3.97 vs 4.0 effective bits under a memory-bandwidth-bound model, but this 0.75% bit savings cannot analytically account for a 49% throughput gain; the fused kernel's activation-row SMEM caching is the more plausible dominant factor, and this decomposition is not explicitly ablated. This is the paper's primary methodological gap. Deployability (4-property check): SYSTEMS profile (built quantizer + inference kernel + vLLM integration, evaluated by deployment benchmarks). - Practical: Runs on commercially available workstation GPUs (RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell, DGX Spark GB10) with quantize-on-load in 25–55 minutes; no calibration data or Hessian required. - Efficient: 49% throughput gain over Marlin INT4 on 122B at TP=1; encoding adds ~25 minutes per load but results are disk-cached; the kernel collapses at concurrency ≥8 (single-user reference implementation only, explicitly out of scope). - Effective: For single-user single-stream decode, the system demonstrably enables deployment of models that cannot otherwise fit (397B on 2×96 GB); GSM8K accuracy is preserved within noise margins for 122B, and 397B is served at 66.72% (single seed) versus INT4+REAP-24% at 44%. - Scalable: Tested at 30B, 35B, 122B, and 397B—a meaningful range; batched serving is explicitly excluded; server-class (A100/H100) evaluation is absent by design; the K≤8192 SMEM bound under V2 on workstation hardware limits applicability to models with narrower intermediate dimensions. The work delivers a software artifact (vLLM fork with XFP quantizer and fused decode kernel) and a deployment process (H-Process), not just a paper-form result. Strengths: - The quality-floor inversion is a practically useful reframing: operators care about model behavior, not bit widths, and no existing deployed format exposes this interface. - The two-threshold MoE auto-select correctly exploits the empirically documented heterogeneity between attention and routed-expert weight distributions without operator-side tuning; the ablation tables (5, 13, 14) provide per-layer evidence for the distribution asymmetry claim. - The H-Process documentation of the operating envelope for 397B—including explicit OOM and garbage-output boundaries, and the narrow window between them (τ_lazy=0.93 fits, 0.935 does not)—is unusually honest and practically useful for reproduction. - The paper honestly decouples bench.py probes (n=50, high Lloyd noise) from lm-eval-harness paper-grade evaluation (n=1,319, 3 seeds), and flags which results are in which category. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state: (1) single-user reference implementation—throughput collapses at concurrency ≥8; (2) K≤8192 bound under V2 on workstation SMEM; (3) no Hessian feedback in Lloyd iteration (planned extension); (4) Lloyd non-determinism (±8pp on small benchmarks, mitigated by weight caching); (5) evaluation limited to GSM8K; (6) hardware limited to two workstation-class SM120/121 platforms; (7) H1.5 GSM8K for 397B is single-seed at submission. These are confronted directly and quantified—a credibility-positive presentation. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The 49% throughput advantage at TP=1 (110 vs 73.6 tok/s) is attributed to reading ~3.97 vs 4.0 effective bits under a memory-bandwidth-bound regime. A 0.75% reduction in bytes read cannot produce a 49% throughput gain; the dominant source is almost certainly the fused kernel architecture (activation-row SMEM caching eliminating global-memory re-reads) rather than the marginal compression ratio difference. The paper conflates these, and no ablation isolates kernel architecture from bit savings—this is the paper's most significant analytical gap. - GSM8K is a math-reasoning benchmark; accuracy on instruction-following, knowledge retrieval, or code tasks is uncharacterized, meaning the quality-floor indicator is validated only on one task type. - The V2a codebook library (L=32) is learned by running a second Lloyd pass over per-channel V2 codebooks; the quality degradation from this two-stage approximation versus direct V2a optimization is not analyzed. - Throughput comparisons include no batched serving baseline (even at batch size 2–4), making it impossible to assess whether the single-user optimization trades away anything non-obvious. - The paper is a solo preprint from a non-institutional affiliation and has not undergone peer review; all numbers are self-reported. Bottom Line: Practitioners deploying large MoE models on workstation-class hardware will find the H-Process and calibration-free auto-select genuinely useful, but the 49% throughput advantage claim requires an ablation separating kernel architecture from compression ratio before the attributed mechanism can be trusted.
Search Results 2605.14844 PDF
#88 — Innovative (64)

DiKGRec: Generative Recommender Model with Diffusion and Knowledge Graph--Based Reasoning

dl.acm.org · Tang Jie - new articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DiKGRec, a dual-module generative recommender that jointly trains a masked-diffusion interaction model and a relation-weighted KG preference-propagation module to produce top-k item recommendations. - Why it's important: Sparse user-item interaction data degrades recommendation quality, and prior generative recommenders (e.g., DiffRec) have no mechanism to leverage item-side relational structure, leaving them brittle in cold-start and low-interaction settings. - How it does it: The diffusion module reconstructs Bernoulli-masked interaction matrices back to their originals, while the KG module iteratively propagates user preference scores through relation-specific adjacency matrices using multi-head learnable scalar relation weights; both modules are trained jointly with a weighted sum loss. - The good: On Last-FM and Amazon-Book, DiKGRec improves recall@20 over the strongest baseline (DiffRec) by 19.72% and 17.87% respectively, and shows near-flat cold-start degradation curve where DiffRec collapses with 1000 masked items. - The missing: The KG reasoning module uses only relation-level (scalar per relation) weights rather than entity-relation-pair weights, so it cannot distinguish a user who likes films directed by person X from one who likes films acted by person X — a limitation the authors acknowledge but do not ablate against a finer-grained baseline. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on KG-augmented recommendation or diffusion-based recommendation; practitioners seeking a cold-start-robust generative recommender will find both the method and the analysis of odd-vs-even propagation layers genuinely useful.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. This is a solid NEW METHOD contribution whose composite is held in the Innovative band rather than higher by two dimensions. Rigor (62) is the main drag: only the headline table reports 5 seeds with no significance tests or confidence intervals, the KG is built from noisy Freebase title-matching, recent strong baselines (KGRec, MCCLK) are omitted, and the MovieLens/KGTORe comparison is self-reported rather than independently reproduced. Resilience (60) is the second limiter — the marketed '>10% average improvement' is driven entirely by the two non-Yelp datasets (Yelp gain is only +2.1%) and the cold-start test (masking items that had ≥10 interactions) is milder than true zero-history cold-start, both of which a tough reviewer would press on. Offsetting these, Non-Obviousness (70) is genuinely good — the closed-form relation-weighted propagation and the odd-vs-even layer insight reveal real KG structure — and Completeness (68) benefits from honest, non-minimizing limitation acknowledgment (Yelp KG noise, scalar-vs-predicate-level modeling). Weighted: 0.20*63 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*64 = 64.
empirical Novelty 63 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 68 Resilience 60 Impact 64
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Recommender systems in e-commerce, music streaming, and social platforms must personalize from user-item interaction logs that are inherently sparse: most users rate or click only a tiny fraction of the item catalog. Generative recommenders like DiffRec address complex interaction distributions but model only interaction data, so they are brittle when users have few interactions (sparse profile) or when items are new with no interaction history (cold-start). Existing KG-augmented recommenders are discriminative and embedding-based, which makes them sensitive to noise (dense embeddings overfit noisy KG triples) and unable to explain why a particular item was recommended. No prior work had integrated KG-based reasoning into a generative recommendation framework. Key Idea The core insight is to represent KG-based preference propagation as a closed-form matrix operation: a user-entity preference matrix P is iteratively updated as a weighted sum over relation-specific, degree-normalized adjacency matrices (P_{l+1}^h = Σ_r softmax(ω^h)_r · P_l · G_r). This is entirely parameter-free in the KG structure (only scalar relation weights ω^h are learned), making it inherently robust to item-level sparsity — a new item needs only KG connectivity, not interaction history. This propagation module is then combined with a standard diffusion module via a joint weighted loss, with inference predictions averaged by the same weights. Methodology DiKGRec has two modules trained jointly. The diffusion module applies a Bernoulli masking (rate p) to the binary interaction matrix M_ori to produce M_masked, then runs a standard DDPM forward process (Gaussian noise schedule) on M_masked. Crucially, the reverse process and all losses target M_ori, not M_masked, forcing the model to infer masked interactions from noisy data. The loss has three terms: L_match (KL between forward and reverse conditionals at each step t≥2), L_recon (reconstruction from M_1), and L_trans (MSE from masked M_0 back to M_ori, approximated by Monte Carlo over N masking samples). The reverse network is a two-layer MLP — deliberately lightweight. The KG reasoning module decomposes the KG into R relation-specific normalized adjacency matrices G_r, then runs L propagation layers with H heads; each head h has a softmax-normalized scalar weight ω^h_r per relation. User alignment α_u with each head is the softmax of per-head reconstruction errors ||P^h_L[u,:I] - M_ori[u,:]||^2 (inverted, so lower error → higher weight). Inference combines both modules: M̂ = λ1·M̂_diff + λ2·M̂_KG. Critical design choices: (1) masking targets original rather than masked data — well-justified for completing sparse inputs; (2) multi-head scalar relation weights rather than per-(relation, entity) weights — justified as scalable but acknowledged as less expressive; (3) joint loss rather than co-training — an alternative co-training scheme was empirically tested and shown inferior by 4–37% across datasets. Key Results Evaluated on Last-FM (23,566 users, 48,123 items, 9 KG relations), Yelp2018 (45,919 users, 45,538 items, 42 relations), and Amazon-Book (70,679 users, 24,915 items, 39 relations) using 70/10/20 train/val/test splits with 10-core filtering and full-ranking evaluation. Recall@20 / NDCG@20: Last-FM 0.1226/0.1104 vs best baseline DiffRec 0.1024/0.0947 (+19.72%/+16.58%); Yelp2018 0.0772/0.0512 vs best baseline KGCL 0.0756/0.0493 (+2.11%/+3.85%); Amazon-Book 0.1781/0.1009 vs DiffRec 0.1511/0.0882 (+17.87%/+14.40%). Results averaged over 5 runs. The improvement on Yelp2018 is considerably weaker than on the other two, attributed to noisier/less semantically relevant KG triples in that dataset. In cold-start evaluation (masking up to 1000 items entirely from training), DiffRec recall@20 drops roughly 50% while DiKGRec remains near-flat. In sparse user profile evaluation (5 groups by interaction density), DiKGRec slightly underperforms DiffRec in the densest group (G1) but outperforms in all sparser groups (G2–G5). Runtime is comparable to DiffRec (~2–6 min/epoch, ~3–6 h total training). MovieLens comparison against KGTORe shows DiKGRec recall@10=0.1845/NDCG@10=0.3427 vs KGTORe 0.1710/0.3212 (self-reported). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is the combination of a masked-diffusion generative framework with a parameter-sparse, relation-weighted KG preference propagation module and their joint training. The diffusion component is an incremental extension of DiffRec (adds Bernoulli masking and a transition loss term). The KG propagation component is a simplification of GNN-style methods, reducing to scalar relation weights and matrix multiplications. The integration and the demonstrated cold-start robustness are the primary contributions; neither component alone is novel. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: removing KG module, diffusion module, or masking each degrades performance, and the relative contribution varies interpretably by dataset. Five random seeds are reported for the main table. No significance tests or confidence intervals are shown. The hyperparameter sensitivity analysis covers module weight λ1, number of attention heads H, and propagation layers L across all three datasets. A notable structural finding — odd propagation layers produce poor performance because preferences first leave the item subgraph before returning — is empirically observed and plausibly explained. Code is released (referenced throughout as "our released code" with repository mentioned in appendix). The KG for Amazon-Book and Last-FM is constructed via Freebase title-matching, a noisy process that could affect reproducibility across different KG versions. The MovieLens comparison against KGTORe (Appendix H) is self-reported and uses KGTORe's own dataset, not independently reproduced. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The diffusion MLP is O(|I|·d) in parameters and the KG propagation uses sparse matrix multiplication (CSR format); both run on standard GPU hardware with no special requirements. - Efficient: Per-epoch training time (~2–7 min) and total training time (~3–6 h) are comparable to DiffRec and moderately higher than LightGCN, making it practical for weekly-or-nightly retraining pipelines on datasets up to ~70K users. - Effective: The evidence on Last-FM and Amazon-Book is strong for the stated problems (sparsity, cold-start); the Yelp2018 improvement (2.1% recall) is marginal and dataset-dependent, which the authors acknowledge. - Scalable: Tested on datasets up to ~70K users and ~88K entities; no stress-scale test (millions of users or items) is conducted, and the O(nnz(P)·nnz_row(G)) KG propagation cost could become a bottleneck with denser interaction graphs or larger KGs. The paper claims scalability based on sparsity arguments but does not test it empirically at larger scale. The paper delivers a software artifact (code released on GitHub per the appendix). Strengths - The KG propagation design is principled and interpretable: learned relation weights are directly readable as user archetypes (album-oriented vs. artist-oriented music listeners in the Last-FM case study), and the normalization by node degree has a clean information-theoretic justification. - The cold-start analysis is the most convincing experimental result: DiKGRec's near-flat performance curve as cold-start items increase from 0 to 1000 (while DiffRec and DiffKG degrade sharply) directly validates the central design claim. - The co-training ablation (Appendix B) strengthens the paper by ruling out an obvious alternative and explaining why joint loss outperforms iterative pseudo-labeling. - The odd-vs-even propagation layer finding is a genuine insight into KG structure that has practical implications for any GNN-based KG recommender. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note that Yelp2018 KG connections are noisier and less semantically relevant, reducing the KG module's contribution there. They acknowledge that relation-level modeling (vs. predicate+object level as in KGTORe) cannot distinguish fine-grained entity-specific preferences. They note the model is more sensitive to KG noise injection than to KG sparsification (Appendix G), meaning bad KG data is more harmful than missing KG data. The authors frame these as motivations for future work rather than minimizing them, which is credible. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The baselines omit several recent strong KG recommenders (e.g., KGRec, MCCLK) that postdate the included baselines; the comparison set represents work through ~2024 but the field has been active. - The 10-core filtering removes the most sparse users and items from evaluation, which is standard practice but means the cold-start scenario tested (masking items with interactions ≥10 from training) is considerably milder than real-world cold-start where items have zero interactions and no user data at all. - The MovieLens comparison in Appendix H is based on KGTORe's reported scores, not the authors' own reproduction of KGTORe under identical conditions — the improvement claim (recall@10: 0.1845 vs 0.1710) cannot be verified from the paper alone. - The Yelp2018 result (only +2.1% over best baseline KGCL) is glossed over in the main text; the paper's headline claim of "over 10% improvement in average" is driven entirely by the two non-Yelp datasets, and a reviewer would note that the average is sensitive to which datasets are included. - Training time per epoch for DiKGRec on Amazon-Book (~7 min) is already 40× slower than LightGCN per epoch, and total training time is 2× longer than LightGCN; at larger catalog sizes this gap would widen. Bottom Line DiKGRec is a solid, well-executed contribution to KG-augmented generative recommendation with a genuinely useful cold-start capability, but practitioners should note the uneven gains across datasets (strong on music/book, marginal on venue recommendation) and the lack of evidence at production scale before deploying it.
Recommended
#89 — Innovative (64)

A ritual-aware cognitive-adaptive knowledge graph for the Dongba pictographic script heritage

nature.com · neural symbolic - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces RACK-Dongba, a four-layer neuro-symbolic framework that constructs DPKG — the first ritual-context-aware, cognitively-adaptive knowledge graph for the Dongba pictographic writing system — covering 1,788 characters linked to 3 ritual event nodes, 17 semantic categories, and 42 scripture manuscript pages. - Why it's important: Fewer than 100 Dongba priests remain worldwide and the script's semantics are orally transmitted within living ritual practice; existing computational datasets treat characters as isolated visual entities, ignoring the ritual event context that gives characters their meaning and making heritage systems inaccessible to non-specialist audiences. - How it does it: It chains AHP-quantified user needs → a CIDOC CRM extension with a novel RitualEvent ontological class (NS-DPonto) → CLIP-ViT-B/32 cross-document matching plus GLM-4V multimodal annotation → four SWRL symbolic rules that classify all characters into HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW cognitive load tiers stored in Neo4j with dual-layer expert/novice query templates. - The good: The SWRL cognitive load classification explains 92.1% of observed novice cognitive load variance (partial η²=0.921, F(2,62)=363.298, p<0.001), with all pairwise group differences exceeding the large-effect threshold (Cohen's d ranging from 2.33 to 4.88). - The missing: The morphological complexity variable (morphComplexity) is derived from semantic category membership rather than from actual visual or stroke features, making the nominally 2×2 SWRL classification effectively a single-variable binary split on ritual affiliation, and no system-efficacy study exists yet to show DPKG access improves user comprehension vs. a no-system control. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building audience-adaptive digital heritage systems or applying neuro-symbolic architectures to culturally-embedded knowledge domains; skip if you need transferable ML methods or general-purpose knowledge graph engineering.
Why this rank: Scored using the Systems profile. This is the strongest paper in the cluster: genuine novelty (Novelty 70) — the first ritual-context-aware Dongba knowledge graph, a RitualEvent class with no prior counterpart, and a traceable AHP→ontology→SWRL derivation chain that turns user cognitive needs into a structural design input. Rigor (70) is high for the cluster — peer-reviewed (in press, npj Heritage Science), proper repeated-measures ANOVA with Bonferroni correction, reported inter-rater κ (0.81/0.83), retrieval ablations, and a fully public artifact (dataset, OWL ontology, SWRL rules on GitHub). Non-Obviousness (70) is supported by the behavioral finding that cognitive load stems from cultural-context deprivation rather than glyph complexity. The main drags are Impact (55, a niche heritage artifact) and Resilience (62), softened by the honestly acknowledged morphComplexity collinearity and the polarized stimulus set that makes the SWRL thresholds only partially validated; the absence of a no-system efficacy study caps it below the Highly-Innovative band. Weighted: 0.15*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*62 + 0.30*55 ≈ 64.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 62 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Dongba script is the world's only living pictographic writing system, used by the Naxi people in Yunnan, China exclusively for ceremonial and ritual purposes. Its semantics are inseparable from ritual performance and transmitted orally by a dwindling community of priests; the same character carries entirely different meanings in an exorcism versus a blessing ceremony, making character-level semantic representations structurally insufficient. Three gaps persist across all existing computational Dongba datasets (DBD400, Dongba1800, DB1404, DongbaMIE, DongbaMMTCorpus): no dataset organizes characters at the ritual event level; no system addresses cross-document glyph variation in handwritten manuscripts that renders exact-match retrieval ineffective; and no heritage knowledge graph addresses the cognitive asymmetry between expert users (priests with years of ritual literacy) and general audiences. This third gap is especially acute because heritage knowledge graph systems are designed for specialist retrieval while digital preservation goals demand accessibility to non-specialists — a conflict the field has not operationalized as a design constraint. Key Idea The single core technical insight is that ritual event context — not individual character appearance — is the true semantic unit of Dongba knowledge, and that AHP-quantified user needs can be translated directly into ontological class requirements and SWRL rule variable selection before any technical implementation begins, making cognitive accessibility a structural design constraint rather than a post-hoc interface feature. This AHP→ontology→SWRL derivation chain, not any individual component, is what distinguishes RACK-Dongba from prior heritage knowledge graph systems. Methodology The framework has four sequential layers. Layer 1 (Cognitive-Driven): AHP pairwise comparison matrices (Saaty 1–9 scale) were applied to domain experts (n=7, Dongba priests/scholars, CR=0.0036) and novice users (n=18, graduate students without Dongba background, CR=0.0056) across a 4-criterion, 16-sub-criterion hierarchy. The key empirical outcome — complete non-overlap between expert top-3 and novice top-3 demand indicators — empirically drives the dual-layer query design and identifies ritual context presentation (C7) and cognitive load scaffolding (C6, C10, C9) as mandatory ontological properties. Layer 2 (NS-AI Knowledge Production): NS-DPonto is implemented in OWL 2/RDF (Protégé 5.6), extending CIDOC CRM with four domain-specific classes: DongbaCharacter (←E22_Human-Made_Object), RitualEvent (←E5_Event), Scripture (←E73_Information_Object), SemanticCategory (←E55_Type), plus nine properties including dponto:hasRitualEvent, dponto:appearsIn, dponto:cognitiveLoad, dponto:ritualAffiliation (boolean), and dponto:morphComplexity (integer 1–5). HermiT reasoner detected no logical conflicts. CLIP-ViT-B/32 (OpenCLIP) computes cosine similarity between 1,340 compendium + 1,788 dictionary character images and 42 manuscript pages (top-5 candidates, similarity 0.70–0.76); manual expert verification (Fleiss' κ=0.81) confirmed 481 ritual-affiliated characters (21.5%). GLM-4V-flash annotates all 1,788 characters with four structured JSON fields (semantic category, ritual affiliation, morphological complexity, Chinese gloss); expert agreement κ=0.83. Layer 3 (Knowledge Hierarchical Storage): Four SWRL rules form a complete 2×2 condition coverage matrix (ritualAffiliation × morphComplexity threshold of 3), producing HIGH (161, 9.0%), MEDIUM (1,081, 60.5%), LOW (546, 30.5%). Critical design concern: morphComplexity is assigned by semantic category membership (ritual-specialist=4, natural=2, others=3) rather than measured from visual features, creating high collinearity with ritualAffiliation. The >8,000-edge Neo4j graph supports two differentiated Cypher query templates operating on the same node set — expert queries return cross-modal associations and ritual event context sorted by scripture relevance; novice queries return semantic category and cognitive load level sorted ascending by difficulty. Layer 4 (Experimental Verification): Experiment 1 evaluates entity retrieval (top-k=5) on 100 programmatically constructed queries (33 phonetic, 33 gloss, 34 category) using Precision, Recall, F1. Experiment 2 uses a one-factor within-subjects design with n=32 novice participants (94% aged 23–28, predominantly design/arts disciplines) rating 10 stimulus characters (4 HIGH, 3 MEDIUM, 3 LOW) on NASA-TLX six-dimension scale (7-point), analyzed by one-way repeated-measures ANOVA with Bonferroni post-hoc correction. Key Results Experiment 1: DPKG achieves Precision=70.2%, Recall=87.0%, F1=77.7%, outperforming CIDOC-KG (F1=44.0%) and BERT-KG (F1=15.8%) by 33.7 and 61.9 percentage points respectively. Expert queries: F1=84.1%; Novice queries: F1=71.1%. Task complexity stratification: Level 1 (perceptual) F1=82.3%; Level 2 (comprehension) F1=79.2%; Level 3 (ritual context inference) F1=72.0%; DPKG-CIDOC gap widens from 8.9pp at Level 1 to 36.3pp at Level 3, suggesting the RitualEvent layer contributes disproportionately to high-complexity queries. Ablation: removing knowledge graph structure (BERT-only) reduces F1 by 61.9%; removing Jieba segmentation reduces F1 by 18.3% (Recall drops from 87.0% to 49.0%); removing GLM-4V reduces Precision from 70.2% to 51.2% (ΔF1=-14.1%); removing CLIP or Scripture Relevance each yields ΔF1=-2.3%, with identical impact explained by the two ritual-dominant scriptures making category membership a proxy for CLIP-confirmed affiliation at the retrieval stage. DPKG per-category F1 ranges from 68.5% (plants) to 83.2% (humanities); BERT-KG collapses on ritual categories (sacrificial rites: 12.8%, religion: 13.5%). Experiment 2: NASA-TLX composite means — HIGH: 4.501 (SD=0.679), MEDIUM: 3.441 (SD=0.687), LOW: 2.502 (SD=0.670) — follow a monotonic pattern consistent with SWRL classification. ANOVA: F(2,62)=363.298, p<0.001, partial η²=0.921. All pairwise comparisons significant (Bonferroni-corrected): HIGH vs MEDIUM d=2.381, HIGH vs LOW d=4.878, MEDIUM vs LOW d=2.330. Six-dimension analysis: largest HIGH-LOW differences in Effort (Δ=2.30) and Frustration (Δ=2.19), smallest in Mental Demand (Δ=1.37), indicating novice cognitive pressure originates from cultural context inaccessibility rather than visual complexity. Behavioral confirmation: LOW characters achieved 97–100% retrieval accuracy; HIGH characters 56–78%. Q28 response: 53.1% of participants cited lack of cultural background as the primary barrier; only 6.2% cited glyph complexity. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The DPKG dataset is genuinely the first semantic knowledge graph for Dongba script with ritual event associations; the RitualEvent ontological class has no counterpart in any existing cultural heritage ontology. The AHP→ontology→SWRL derivation chain that makes user cognitive needs a structural design input is novel in heritage knowledge graph design. What is standard: CIDOC CRM extension as an approach is well-established; CLIP-ViT and SWRL are off-the-shelf components; AHP for design weighting is conventional methodology applied in a new domain. Rigor & Reproducibility The ablation study (Table 8) identifies component contributions at the retrieval stage but, as the paper explicitly acknowledges, does not ablate the CLIP→RitualEvent construction-stage dependency. GLM-4V annotation agreement (κ=0.83) and CLIP matching verification (κ=0.81) are reported. Experiment 2 applies proper repeated-measures ANOVA with Bonferroni correction; the extremely large effect sizes (partial η²=0.921, Cohen's d up to 4.878) are credible but partially predictable given the deliberately polarized stimulus set (ritual-specialist vs. plant characters). The morphComplexity variable is derived from category membership rather than measured from visual features, creating collinearity with ritualAffiliation — acknowledged as a limitation that renders the 2×2 SWRL system effectively single-variable. Experiment 1 test queries are programmatically constructed from the same dictionary corpus, not from real user queries. DPKG dataset, NS-DPonto OWL 2/RDF ontology, and SWRL rules are publicly released at https://github.com/weibize-commits/RACK-Dongba. This is a peer-reviewed article in press at npj Heritage Science. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built knowledge graph infrastructure with dual-layer query access, evaluated by retrieval and cognitive load experiments. - Practical: Runs on commercially available infrastructure — Neo4j, CLIP-ViT-B/32 via OpenCLIP, GLM-4V-flash — with no specialized hardware requirements; dataset construction is a one-time pipeline. - Efficient: Per-query cost is standard Neo4j Cypher execution; dataset construction required one-time CLIP matching and GLM-4V annotation of 1,788 characters; no runtime benchmark is reported. - Effective: Entity retrieval F1=77.7% is a practical improvement over both baselines; SWRL classification captures 92.1% of novice cognitive load variance — but whether DPKG access improves user comprehension vs. a no-system control remains untested, as Experiment 2 establishes classification validity, not system efficacy. - Scalable: Only 2 scriptures were used; the ritual event layer covers only 481 of 1,788 characters (21.5%); the paper identifies corpus expansion as the primary data-level priority but provides no scalability experiments or projections. The work delivers a complete software artifact: DPKG dataset in Neo4j (1,788 character nodes, >8,000 typed edges), NS-DPonto OWL 2/RDF ontology, and SWRL classification rules, all publicly released. Strengths - The complete non-overlap between expert and novice AHP top-3 demand indicators is a genuine empirical finding that quantitatively justifies the dual-layer architecture rather than asserting it — this kind of user-needs evidence grounding ontology design choices is rare in the heritage knowledge graph literature. - The traceable derivation chain (AHP weight vectors → RitualEvent class requirement → SWRL variable selection) makes every major architectural decision checkable against a prior empirical step, distinguishing RACK-Dongba from ad hoc component assembly. - Experiment 2's discovery that novice cognitive pressure on ritual characters concentrates in Effort and Frustration (Δ=2.30, 2.19) rather than Mental Demand (Δ=1.37), with 53.1% of participants citing cultural context deficiency as the barrier vs. 6.2% citing glyph complexity, provides behavioral evidence that ritualAffiliation — not morphological complexity — is the correct primary classification criterion. - The paper's honest dissection of the CLIP ablation result — explaining that identical ΔF1 for w/o CLIP and w/o Scripture Relevance is a corpus-level confound, not proof that CLIP is redundant, because CLIP's contribution lies at the knowledge-construction stage — demonstrates methodological integrity. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors confront limitations directly. morphComplexity is derived from category membership (ritual=4, natural=2, others=3), creating high collinearity with ritualAffiliation and making the 2×2 SWRL classification effectively single-variable in the current implementation; this is explicitly stated and the 2×2 form is retained only as an extension interface for future independent morphological features. Experiment 2 establishes classification validity, not system efficacy; a controlled task-performance experiment with a no-system control is identified as the primary next step. The participant sample (n=32, predominantly 23–28-year-old design/arts graduate students) may not represent museum visitors or the general public, potentially suppressing LOW/MEDIUM load scores relative to a fully representative sample. Only 2 scriptures were used for cross-document matching, limiting ritual event layer coverage. Experiment 1 test queries are programmatically constructed without external validation on real user queries. RE1 encompasses 325 of 481 ritual-affiliated characters (67.6%), introducing imbalance that may affect semantic discriminability. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The Experiment 2 stimulus set is deliberately polarized (4 HIGH characters all from Humanities/Ritual/Ruoqa categories, 3 LOW characters all from Plants), meaning the finding that "ritual characters produce more cognitive load than plant characters" may partly confirm a prior expectation rather than independently validate the SWRL classification thresholds. The HIGH-MEDIUM boundary (the genuinely novel claim) is tested with only 3 MEDIUM characters from the astronomy category — insufficient to establish that the morphComplexity threshold at 3 is correctly calibrated. - The retrieval evaluation uses a single-answer exact-match criterion (top-5 precision/recall against a designated answer_id). For a domain where semantically equivalent character variants exist, this underestimates precision — acknowledged — but the evaluation design also makes it impossible to distinguish retrieval of a semantically appropriate alternative from retrieval of an unrelated false positive. A graded relevance evaluation would better characterize system utility. - No comparison against the most methodologically proximate competing system — Lin et al.'s (2025) Xuan paper neuro-symbolic heritage framework, cited as the closest prior art — is performed on a common benchmark. CIDOC-KG and BERT-KG are methodological extremes, not the nearest competitors, which inflates the apparent performance gap. - The three RitualEvent categories (RE1: Exorcism/Spirit Banishment, RE2: Blessing/Ancestral Sacrifice, RE3: Soul-Calling/Funerary) were derived from only 2 scriptures, and RE1 alone contains 67.6% of ritual-affiliated characters. The paper does not estimate what fraction of actual Dongba ritual event types are represented, leaving the ontology's coverage of the ritual semantic space uncharacterized. - morphComplexity assignment is entirely determined by an integer lookup on category membership with no per-character visual measurement; two characters in the same semantic category receive identical morphComplexity scores regardless of actual glyph structure. This means the SWRL rules cannot meaningfully distinguish boundary cases — a point the paper concedes but does not quantify in terms of misclassification probability for characters at the morphComplexity=3 boundary. Bottom Line A practitioner building audience-adaptive digital heritage systems for pictographic or ritual-embedded cultural artifacts will find genuine methodological value in the AHP→ontology→SWRL derivation chain and the behavioral evidence that cognitive inaccessibility stems from cultural context deprivation rather than glyph visual complexity — but should treat the morphological complexity variable as currently unvalidated as an independent predictor and plan for a controlled efficacy study before claiming that DPKG access measurably improves user comprehension.
Search Results
#90 — Innovative (64)

Beyond Isolated Investor: Predicting Startup Success via Roleplay-Based Collective Agents

faculty.sist.shanghaitech.edu.cn · 10 new citations to your articles
