The SVD/subspace analysis toolkit is borrowed from prior RL interpretability work (Cai et al., 2025; AlphaRL); the paper's contribution is applying and extending this lens specifically to OPD vs. RL comparative dynamics. The extrapolation idea is analogous to AlphaRL but adapts the mechanism (adaptive vs. fixed step, validation-gated) to the OPD setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and address key design choices: learning rate sensitivity (Fig. 7a), validation set difficulty (Fig. 7b), and wall-clock training time (Fig. 7c). Cross-scale analysis covers 1.5B–32B and three RL algorithms. However, error bars or multiple-seed results are not reported; all performance curves appear as single-run traces, which is a concern for claims about stability differences between OPD and RL. Main results (Fig. 6) are shown as training-curve plots rather than final-checkpoint tables with standard deviations, making precise comparison to baselines difficult. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/caiyuchen-ustc/EffOPD. The training configuration is provided in detail (Appendix D), including the exact verl training command. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.11739v3) submitted May 2026; not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Training runs on 8× or 32× H20 96GB GPUs, which are commercially available cloud instances; the method is compatible with the verl training framework without additional infrastructure. - Efficient: EffOPD introduces only a lightweight 50-example validation pass at each extrapolation checkpoint and five candidate evaluations; the overhead is shown (Fig. 7c) to be small relative to training step savings. - Effective: The evidence shows EffOPD reduces training steps by >3× while maintaining comparable or slightly better final reasoning performance, which directly addresses the training-cost problem; the claim is backed by multi-scale multi-benchmark plots, though without per-run error bounds. - Scalable: Experiments reach 32B parameters (the largest tested scale), which is meaningful; behavior above 32B is not tested, and the extrapolation dynamics at 70B+ or mixture-of-experts models (e.g., DeepSeek-V4) remain untested. The paper includes a code release and verl integration, delivering a usable software artifact. Strengths: - The norm-scaling intervention on early OPD checkpoints (10% → 80% final performance) is a clean, controlled experiment that provides direct evidence for the early directional lock-in hypothesis, not just correlational evidence. - The comparative framework is unusually rigorous: the same analysis (sliding-window interventions, SVD metrics, subspace cosine similarity) is applied consistently across 10 model/algorithm configurations, making the reported properties credible rather than cherry-picked. - EffOPD's adaptive validation mechanism (greedy early-exit, 50-example mini-set) is a principled solution to the overextrapolation problem that fixed-step methods (AlphaOPD, ExOPD) suffer from, as the ablations demonstrate. - The code release and detailed training configurations (including the full verl command) make the work highly reproducible. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state: (1) the identified properties have not been validated in multi-turn agent tasks or multimodal reasoning, where distributional shifts and complex teacher-student residual structures may alter dynamics; (2) the theoretical analysis (in the appendix) is locally valid near the base model and does not capture global non-convex behavior of large-scale post-training. These are acknowledged directly, which is a credibility positive — the scope is clearly bounded. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Main results are presented as training curves without tabular final-performance numbers or confidence intervals across seeds; the 3× speedup claim is visually compelling but not verified with statistical testing. - The lightweight 50-example validation set is sampled from the training set, raising a mild Goodhart's Law concern: the extrapolation direction is validated on in-distribution data, and behavior on out-of-distribution prompts or adversarial inputs is not assessed. - The teacher in all OPD experiments is always an RL-finetuned version of the same-family base model; it is unknown whether the foresight properties and EffOPD gains hold when the teacher is from a different model family or trained by a different method (e.g., RLHF, DPO). - End-to-end wall-clock comparisons between OPD and RL (not just OPD vs. EffOPD) are not reported; OPD's efficiency advantage over RL includes teacher forward-pass costs that are not factored into the speedup claim for EffOPD. - The paper benchmarks on AIME26, MINERVA, and GPQA but does not include general instruction-following benchmarks (MT-Bench, AlpacaEval); whether EffOPD preserves non-reasoning capabilities is not tested. Bottom Line: A practitioner post-training reasoning-focused LLMs via OPD gains a directly deployable 3× training speedup with minimal implementation overhead, backed by analysis thorough enough to trust the mechanism rather than just the headline number.
Recommended 2605.11739 PDF
#96 — Innovative (63)

Contextual Linear Activation Steering of Language Models

Brandon Hsu, Daniel Beaglehole, Adityanarayanan Radhakrishnan, Mikhail Belkin · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CLAS (Contextual Linear Activation Steering), a method that replaces the single fixed scalar coefficient in linear activation steering with per-block, context-dependent coefficients computed by a learned sensing vector cℓ ∈ R^(k+1) trained via next-token prediction loss on as few as 10 prompt-completion pairs. - Why it's important: Fixed steering coefficients in standard LAS require expensive grid search over model generations and apply uniformly to all prompts regardless of context, causing systematic under- or over-steering that limits deployment reliability. - How it does it: At each transformer block ℓ, CLAS modifies the forward pass as h̃ℓ,t = hℓ,t + (cℓ · [hℓ,t 1])dℓ, where dℓ is an RFM-probe-derived steering direction (fixed) and cℓ is the learned sensing vector; only cℓ is trained, decoupling interpretable direction extraction from adaptive coefficient learning. - The good: CLAS achieves 86.3%, 88.4%, and 83.5% average steering accuracy on Qwen2.5-7B, Llama-3.1-70B, and Llama-3.1-8B respectively (vs. 64.0%, 75.1%, 74.6% for LAS) while reducing tuning time from 1–3 hours to under 4 minutes. - The missing: CLAS collapses to 8% on the MNMT task for Llama-3.2-1B (vs. 57–66% for LoRA/ReFT), revealing a systematic failure mode under-discussed by the authors; the evaluation also relies on 50 test prompts per task and o3-mini as judge for 6 of 11 tasks, creating potential evaluation dependency. - Read it or not: Researchers and practitioners working on lightweight, interpretable LLM specialization with limited labeled data should read this paper; those seeking large-scale or task-specific fine-tuning may find LoRA or ReFT at higher ranks more practical.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (60) is the primary limiter: code is released and the LAS-NTP ablation cleanly isolates context-dependence, but the 50-prompt test sets, absence of any error bars or significance tests, and the o3-mini judge-generator circularity on 6 of 11 tasks make the headline accuracy gains indicative rather than definitive. Completeness (58) and Resilience (55) are also pulled down by the unexplained MNMT collapse to 8% on Llama-3.2-1B and the lack of few-shot/system-prompt baselines, which a tough reviewer would press on. Novelty (68) and Non-Obviousness (70) are solid — the decoupled sensing-vector formulation and surprising JailbreakBench result give it genuine substance — keeping it above the publishable midpoint.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 58 Resilience 55 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Linear activation steering (LAS) inserts a fixed-direction, fixed-magnitude perturbation into a model's residual stream at inference time to elicit latent behaviors without modifying weights. The practical bottleneck is that the scalar steering coefficient α must be selected by grid search over generated outputs — evaluated by humans or an LLM judge — which is computationally expensive and yields a context-independent value applied uniformly to every input token and every model block. This rigidity causes under-steering on prompts that weakly activate the target concept and over-steering on prompts where the concept is already present, degrading generation quality and limiting reliable deployment across diverse real-world inputs. Key Idea: The core insight is that the scalar steering coefficient in LAS can be parameterized as a linear function of the current activation: αℓ,t = cℓ · [hℓ,t 1], where cℓ is a k+1 dimensional sensing vector learned by minimizing next-token prediction loss on a small labeled dataset. This replaces a single hand-tuned scalar with L learned vectors (one per transformer block), each of which reads the activation at that block to decide how strongly to apply the pre-extracted steering direction — requiring no generation, no evaluator calls, and no grid search. Methodology: The pipeline has two decoupled stages. First, block-specific steering vectors {dℓ} are extracted via Recursive Feature Machine (RFM) probing: a dataset of N=200 prompt-concept label pairs is passed through the frozen LLM, last-token residual stream activations are collected at each block, and an iterative kernel ridge regression (KRR) is fit using a Mahalanobis Laplace kernel whose data-dependent metric M is updated via the Average Gradient Outer Product (AGOP). The steering vector dℓ is the principal eigenvector of the final AGOP matrix, oriented by Pearson correlation with the labels. This stage is backpropagation-free and completes in minutes. Second, sensing vectors {cℓ} are learned by freezing the entire LLM, modifying the forward pass with the CLAS update, and minimizing standard next-token prediction loss on n=10 prompt-completion pairs via AdamW (up to 8 gradient steps, early stopping on validation loss). The three critical design decisions are: (1) decoupling direction extraction from coefficient learning, which preserves RFM's interpretability properties; (2) using NTP loss rather than evaluator-based scoring, enabling gradient-based optimization without generation; and (3) using a bias term in the sensing vector (the concatenated constant 1), which allows the coefficient to be nonzero even at zero activations. CLAS shares functional form with rank-1 DiReFT but differs fundamentally in how the steering direction is obtained — RFM probe vs. jointly trained — resulting in nearly orthogonal directions (unsigned cosine similarity ≈ 0.01–0.04 across all tasks and models). Key Results: On 10 steering tasks (excluding JailbreakBench), CLAS achieves average accuracy of 86.30 / 88.40 / 83.50 / 58.30% on Qwen2.5-7B / Llama-3.1-70B / Llama-3.1-8B / Llama-3.2-1B, versus LAS at 64.00 / 75.10 / 74.60 / 52.50%, LoRA at 78.80 / 77.90 / 82.90 / 62.40%, and ReFT at 76.80 / 84.90 / 73.70 / 65.10%. CLAS is best on 3 of 4 models; ReFT leads on Llama-3.2-1B. JailbreakBench is a notable outlier: CLAS achieves 88 / 84 / 84 / 65% while LoRA and ReFT collapse to near 0% due to repetition failure from partial steer dataset completions. Tuning time is 3–4 minutes for CLAS vs. 35 minutes to 3 hours for LAS grid search. Concept monitoring accuracy with RFM directions averages 96.14–97.19% (Avg over blocks) vs. LoRA 70.63–74.41% and ReFT 68.85–73.77%. Test sets are 50 prompts per task — a small scale that limits statistical confidence. Increasing LoRA/ReFT to rank-4 or rank-8 does not consistently improve results. This is a preprint; results have not been peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is context-dependent activation steering via sensing vectors trained with NTP loss, decoupled from probe-derived steering directions. The use of RFM probing for steering is drawn from prior work by two co-authors (Beaglehole et al., Science 2026); the NTP-based coefficient learning and the sensing vector formulation are the new contributions. This is not a straightforward combination of existing ideas — the decoupling is a deliberate architectural choice with interpretability consequences — but the method is a modest extension of rank-1 ReFT with a different direction source. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations test rank-4 and rank-8 variants of ReFT and LoRA (Appendix C.1), confirming that higher rank does not consistently help. A coefficient-baseline ablation (Appendix C.2) confirms that per-block LAS tuned via NTP loss underperforms grid search, demonstrating that the advantage of CLAS is the sensing vector's context-dependence rather than the NTP objective alone. No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported; the 50-prompt test sets and o3-mini judge evaluations are not replicated across seeds or evaluators. For 6 of 11 tasks, o3-mini is both the completion generator (for the steer dataset) and the evaluator, which creates a potential circular dependency: the judge may favor outputs stylistically similar to its own generations. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/bhdsc/CLAS. No model weights or fine-tuned checkpoints are released. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL — methods evaluated via inference experiments on standard GPU hardware. - Practical: Sensing vector learning requires only one forward-backward pass per example on consumer-grade GPUs (experiments use NVIDIA A40 for models under 70B and H200 for 70B), making deployment accessible. - Efficient: CLAS adds L(k+1) parameters — roughly half of rank-1 ReFT's L(2k+1) and far less than rank-1 LoRA's L(k+q) (q > k by 4×) — and trains in under 4 minutes on 10 examples, a 15–45× speedup over LAS grid search. - Effective: CLAS convincingly outperforms LAS on 10 of 11 tasks and matches or exceeds ReFT/LoRA on 3 of 4 models; it fails on MNMT for Llama-3.2-1B (8% vs. 57–66%) in a way the paper does not adequately explain. - Scalable: Tested up to Llama-3.1-70B; no experiments beyond 70B; test sets of 50 prompts do not establish scalability of evaluation robustness, and the 10-example steer dataset generalization to substantially out-of-distribution inputs is not evaluated. The work delivers a software library (code released) but no trained model weights or production-grade tooling. Strengths: - Decoupling direction extraction from coefficient learning preserves the full interpretability of RFM probe directions: RFM achieves 96–99% concept monitoring accuracy (max-block metric) versus 88–97% for LoRA/ReFT directions, meaning CLAS steering vectors remain semantically meaningful probes unlike fine-tuning directions. - Exceptional data efficiency: learning from only 10 labeled completions to adapt coefficient behavior across all blocks is a practically significant result, directly enabling low-resource deployment scenarios. - JailbreakBench result is genuinely surprising: LoRA and ReFT at rank-1 completely fail (near 0%) on anti-refusal steering due to degenerate repetition, while CLAS achieves 65–88%, demonstrating that activation-space methods have qualitative advantages for certain behavior types that gradient-based weight modifications cannot replicate. - The ablation comparing scalar LAS-NTP (per-block or per-model) versus sensing-vector CLAS-NTP (Table 13) cleanly isolates the contribution of context-dependence from the objective function, which is methodologically honest. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that CLAS underperforms ReFT on Llama-3.2-1B (the smallest tested model), that per-task accuracy varies and CLAS occasionally underperforms (Figure 1 shows a few negative Δ vs. LAS), and that JailbreakBench behavior is attributed to partial completions in the steer dataset. These are stated directly. The authors do not acknowledge evaluation scale concerns, o3-mini judge dependency, or the MNMT failure mode on small models. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - MNMT accuracy for CLAS on Llama-3.2-1B is 8% — far below LoRA (57%) and ReFT (66%) — with no investigation of why sensing vectors fail on multilingual generation for the smallest model; this is not a corner case but a systematic failure on one of 11 tasks. - The o3-mini judge evaluates outputs for 6 tasks, and o3-mini also generated the completions used as training targets for those same tasks; this shared provenance may inflate CLAS scores if outputs that resemble o3-mini's style receive higher judge scores. - No comparison to few-shot prompting or system-prompt baselines, which can often achieve comparable behavioral steering without any parameter modification; the "Non-Task Prompt" accuracy column in Table 6 shows the gap CLAS must exceed is sometimes small (e.g., Alpaca, ToxicChat). - The 50-test-prompt evaluation scale is small; per-task accuracy differences of 5–15 percentage points between methods correspond to 2.5–7.5 example differences and are not statistically tested. - Generalization to out-of-distribution prompt distributions is not evaluated — all test prompts come from the same source dataset as probe/steer data, so robustness to domain shift is unknown. Bottom Line: CLAS is a practically useful, well-motivated improvement to linear activation steering that any practitioner exploring lightweight, interpretable LLM specialization with limited labeled data should read — but the 50-prompt test sets and o3-mini evaluation dependency mean the reported accuracy gains should be treated as indicative rather than definitive.
Recommended 2604.24693 PDF
#97 — Innovative (63)

Internalizing Curriculum Judgment for LLM Reinforcement Fine-Tuning

Han Zheng, Yining Ma, Karthick Gunasekaran, Bharathan Balaji, Zheng Du, Shiv Vitaladevuni, Cathy Wu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces METIS (METacognitive Internalized Self-judgment), a framework that trains the RFT policy itself to predict prompt informativeness via in-context learning, then reinforces those predictions with a calibration reward, eliminating the need for external heuristics or auxiliary scorer models. - Why it's important: LLM rollouts are the dominant cost in reinforcement fine-tuning, and wasting them on prompts the policy already masters or consistently fails forfeits both efficiency and final performance; existing curriculum methods drift out of alignment with the policy's evolving competence because they externalize the judgment. - How it does it: The policy reads K recent (prompt, reward-variance) pairs as in-context calibration examples, predicts expected within-prompt reward variance for each candidate prompt, selects the top-B for rollout, then is jointly optimized via standard GRPO loss plus a REINFORCE-style Brier-score calibration reward against the realized variance. - The good: METIS achieves up to 67.9% wall-clock time reduction versus no-curriculum baseline across math, code, and agentic benchmarks at only 3.9% per-step throughput overhead, matching or exceeding the auxiliary-model baseline (PCL) that pays a 58% throughput penalty. - The missing: All results are single-seed; the accuracy differences between METIS and PCL are typically 0.5–1.5 average pass@1 points — comparable to run-to-run variance in RFT — making the accuracy advantage over the strongest baseline statistically unverified. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML researchers working on LLM post-training efficiency, particularly those using group-relative RFT; the closed-loop curriculum formulation is clean and the efficiency gains are practically significant, but practitioners should wait for multi-seed results before adopting the accuracy claims.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (75) and Novelty (71) are the high points — internalizing curriculum judgment as a learnable metacognitive capability, with within-prompt reward variance unifying binary and continuous settings as the mean squared group-relative advantage, is a genuinely fresh and clean formulation. The composite is pulled down by Rigor (57) and Resilience (55): every result is single-seed, the accuracy advantage over PCL is only 0.5–1.5 pass@1 points (within plausible run-to-run variance), and the ADCL comparison is partially circular since it uses the same variance signal METIS predicts. The strong, robustly-measured efficiency gains (3.9% overhead) keep Impact (60) and the overall score respectable.
empirical Novelty 71 Rigor 57 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 62 Resilience 55 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation In group-relative reinforcement fine-tuning of LLMs (GRPO and variants), every training step requires generating multiple rollouts per selected prompt, making compute allocation the critical resource decision. Prompts the policy consistently solves contribute zero gradient signal; prompts it consistently fails contribute noise without actionable updates. The productive regime — prompts near the policy's current competence frontier — shifts continuously as training progresses. Prior curriculum methods address this through category-level schedulers (SEC, DUMP), prompt-level heuristics over rollout statistics (ADCL, DAPO Dynamic Sampling, VCRL, GRESO), or auxiliary trained scorer models (PCL). All externalize the judgment of what constitutes a useful prompt, which means the curriculum module does not track the policy's current state directly and can fall out of alignment as learning progresses. Additionally, heuristics based on pass rate are undefined for continuous rewards (partial test-case credit in code generation, trajectory scoring in agentic tasks), limiting prior work to binary reward settings. Key Idea The core insight is that within-prompt reward variance — the variance of rewards across n rollouts of the same prompt under the current policy — is a unified, policy-dependent informativeness signal that equals the mean squared group-relative advantage, applies to both binary and continuous rewards without task-specific modification, and can be predicted pre-rollout by the LLM policy itself via in-context learning on a small calibration memory of recent (prompt, realized-variance) pairs. Jointly reinforcing this prediction from realized outcomes closes the loop so that the policy learns not only to solve prompts but also to identify which prompts are informative for it next — a form of metacognition. Methodology METIS augments any group-relative RFT algorithm (GRPO, DAPO, etc.) with three coupled operations per iteration. First, a candidate pool of m×B prompts is scored: the policy reads a calibration memory M_t = {(x_k, v(x_k))}_{k=1}^K of the K most recent prompt-variance pairs and produces a structured chain-of-thought response culminating in a boxed numerical prediction v̂_θ(x) ∈ {0.00, 0.02, ..., 0.25} for each candidate; the top-B by predicted variance are selected for rollout. Second, the B selected prompts each receive n full rollouts; rewards and realized variance v(x) = (1/n)Σ(r_i - r̄)² are computed. Third, the policy is optimized on L_total = L_policy + λL_judge, where L_policy is the standard clipped GRPO surrogate loss on task rollout tokens, and L_judge is a REINFORCE-style objective on the judgment tokens using calibration reward R_judge(x) = 1 − (4(v̂_θ(x) − v(x)))² (Brier score scaled to [0,1/4] variance range). The calibration memory M_t is refreshed after each iteration. Three design decisions are well-justified: (1) reward variance generalizes pass rate by recovering it as a special case for binary rewards (v_θ(x) = p(x)(1−p(x)), maximized at p=0.5) while remaining defined for continuous rewards; (2) using ICL rather than fine-tuning the selector enables adaptation to the moving competence frontier without retraining; (3) a small λ=0.01 maintains task-loss dominance — ablations show λ=1 collapses predictions to the maximum-variance shortcut (0.25 for all candidates). Key Results Experiments use Qwen3-4B/8B-Base/Instruct, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B as backbones, evaluated across three task families. On mathematical reasoning (DAPO-17k → AIME24/25, MATH500, Minerva), METIS achieves average pass@1 of 35.7 (Qwen3-8B-Base) vs. PCL's 35.4 and No Curriculum's 34.9, reaching its best checkpoint in 15.7 hours vs. PCL's 17.3 hours (−51.8% vs. No Curriculum's 32.6 hours). On code generation (CodeContests+ → LiveCodeBench v6, HumanEval, MBPP, BigCodeBench), METIS reduces training time by 67.9% for Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct while achieving average pass@1 of 40.4 vs. PCL's 39.7. On agentic function-calling (BFCL v3, Qwen3-4B-Instruct), METIS improves overall accuracy from 46.7 to 49.8 with consistent gains across all five subsplits. Per-step throughput drop is 3.9% for METIS vs. 58.0% for PCL and 5.1% for ADCL. The advantage-magnitude plots (Figure 3) confirm that METIS sustains higher per-step |A| signal throughout training. Baselines are current and representative, though PCL and ADCL required adaptation for continuous rewards (the paper documents these extensions). All results are single-seed. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The formulation of curriculum learning as a learnable metacognitive capability internalized within the policy via joint task + calibration reward optimization is genuinely novel in the LLM RFT setting. The use of within-prompt reward variance as a unified informativeness proxy extending prior binary-case heuristics to continuous rewards is a modest but clean theoretical contribution. The ICL elicitation mechanism and REINFORCE-style calibration reward are individually known; their combination for this specific purpose — and the closed-loop training that makes the judgment a durable policy capability — is the novel synthesis. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are informative and cover both key design dimensions: K ∈ {0, 3, 9} and λ ∈ {0, 0.01, 1} with clear outcome differences. The K=0 ablation produces an 87.6% parse-failure rate (the policy attempts to solve rather than assess), establishing the necessity of in-context evidence. The λ=0 and λ=1 ablations establish the necessity and correct magnitude of the calibration loss. However, the paper explicitly reports single-seed results throughout, citing compute constraints (20K H200 GPU-hours total). The accuracy differences vs. the strongest baseline (PCL) are typically 0.5–1.5 pass@1 points across 6 backbone-dataset pairs — differences that are plausibly within run-to-run variance and cannot be assessed without multiple seeds. Code will be released on GitHub under MIT license but is not yet available at submission time. Training stability is not formally quantified, and the self-judgment prediction is coarsely quantized to 11 values, with no analysis of quantization error's effect on ranking quality. Deployability EMPIRICAL profile. Practical: Runs on standard clusters of NVIDIA H200 GPUs using the VERL framework with vLLM; no specialized hardware required beyond what standard RFT already demands. Efficient: Only 3.9% per-step throughput overhead over plain GRPO; the judgment passes are short structured responses, far cheaper than the full task rollouts already produced, making METIS the only prompt-level curriculum method with negligible cost on both wall-clock and throughput axes. Effective: Evidence shows METIS selects higher-variance prompts (larger realized |A|) and self-regulates toward the p≈0.5 informativeness band from both easy and hard pools (Figure 4); however, single-seed evaluation limits confidence in the absolute accuracy gains over PCL. Scalable: Tested only on 4B–8B parameter models; the authors themselves flag that weaker backbones may lack the ICL capacity METIS requires, and behavior at 70B+ scale is untested and non-trivially extrapolatable. The paper releases a training pipeline and reproducibility scripts (MIT license) but no trained model checkpoints. Strengths - The unification of binary and continuous reward curriculum under within-prompt reward variance is mathematically clean, well-grounded (it equals mean squared group-relative advantage), and directly enables application to code generation and agentic tasks where pass-rate heuristics are undefined. - The case study in Figure 4 is a compelling qualitative validation: METIS independently converges to p≈0.5 from both a hard pool (DAPO, where it climbs from near-zero) and an easy pool (MATH, where it re-anchors after rapid mastery), while ADCL fluctuates and No Curriculum drifts to extremes. - METIS achieves per-step throughput overhead of 3.9% vs. PCL's 58.0%, demonstrating that internalizing the selector into the policy itself eliminates the cost that makes auxiliary-model approaches impractical at scale. - The ablation table (Table 2, Table 6) cleanly demonstrates complementarity of the two components: ICL alone (λ=0) keeps parse failures low but predictions undifferentiated; calibration loss alone without ICL context (K=0) fails catastrophically at parsing; both together are needed. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that METIS relies on the base policy's in-context learning capacity and may not work on weaker backbones. They note they do not formally quantify training stability despite visually observing more stable dynamics. Code is described as forthcoming. These limitations are stated directly without minimization, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single-seed results are the central methodological limitation: with 6 backbone-dataset pairs and accuracy differences vs. PCL of 0.1–0.7 average pass@1 points on math tasks, statistical significance cannot be established, and the accuracy advantage may not replicate. - The pool multiplier m=8 means 8× as many judgment passes per step; the paper does not ablate m, leaving open whether simply generating more rollouts (at equivalent total compute) without curriculum achieves similar gains. - ADCL's continuous-reward adaptation uses reward variance for difficulty ranking — the same signal METIS predicts — creating a partially circular comparison: METIS is being compared to an offline-estimated, fixed-schedule version of its own informativeness signal, which is arguably the most favorable possible framing for METIS. - The discretized prediction vocabulary (11 values from 0.00 to 0.25) introduces quantization error; no analysis of how often ties occur in the top-B selection or whether finer discretization matters. - All tested models are 4B–8B; no experiments approach frontier scale, limiting applicability claims to the mid-scale training regime. Bottom Line Practitioners running LLM RFT who need convergence speed improvements will find the 30–68% wall-clock reduction at 3.9% overhead compelling and immediately applicable, though the single-seed evaluation means the accuracy advantage over PCL should be treated as preliminary until replicated.

#98 — Innovative (63)

Harnessing LLM Agents with Skill Programs (24 ▲ )

Hongjun Liu, Yifei Ming, Shafiq Joty, Chen Zhao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 20 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces HASP (Harnessing LLM Agents with Skill Programs), a framework that converts reusable agent experience into executable Python Program Functions (PFs) that activate on failure-prone states and directly modify or augment the agent's next action, replacing passive textual skill advice. - Why it's important: LLM agents that store past experience as textual skills systematically ignore those skills at decision time because there is no mechanism forcing activation; this failure mode is directly responsible for recurring agent errors on multi-hop search, math, and coding tasks. - How it does it: Each PF exposes a deterministic `should_activate` predicate and an `intervene` method that either rewrites the next action (MODIFY_ACTION) or injects corrective context (INJECT_CONTEXT) into the agent loop; PF-corrected traces then provide structured four-signal supervision (timing, modality, correctness, outcome) for post-training, and residual failures drive validated library evolution. - The good: Inference-time PF-only intervention (no model update) on a 7B model raises web-search accuracy from 31.2% (multi-loop ReAct baseline) to 51.0%, and the full closed-loop system (HASP-Evolve+RS) reaches 60.3%, outperforming Search-R1 (29.9%) by 30.4 percentage points on the same split. - The missing: All experiments use a single random seed; the reported ±1.5 point variance estimate comes from internal smoke tests, not published multi-seed runs, making the significance of several single-digit margins hard to assess. - Read it or not: Researchers building skill-augmented or self-improving LLM agents should read this; practitioners seeking a drop-in improvement for ReAct-style agents on search or coding tasks will find the inference-time PF intervention directly actionable, though the teacher-dependent variants carry non-trivial API cost (~$0.045/question).
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Novelty (72) and Non-Obviousness (70) carry the score: the executable Program Function interface (deterministic should_activate + intervene) is a genuine reframing of skills as auditable guardrails rather than ignorable text, and the finding that unfiltered library evolution is actively harmful is non-obvious. The composite is held down by Rigor (55) and Resilience (58): single seed throughout with only an internal ±1.5pt variance estimate makes several 1–3pt margins indistinguishable from noise, the GPT-4o teacher triple-roles as selector, judge, and reviewer (circularity), and the method underperforms AgentFlow on math — a shortfall surfaced only in the appendix. Completeness (65) is helped by an unusually candid six-item limitations section.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 58 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Multi-step LLM agents that accumulate experience as textual skills suffer from a fundamental enforcement gap: textual reminders injected into the prompt can be ignored by the model, fire at unspecified times, and offer no mechanism to alter the actual executable action. The result is that recurring failure patterns—premature finalization, repeated searches, hallucinated answers unsupported by retrieved documents, over-constrained queries—persist even when relevant skills are present in the prompt. The problem affects practitioners deploying agents on web-search QA, mathematical reasoning, and code generation, where failures cluster into a small number of recognizable types that a reliable intervention mechanism could address without retraining. Key Idea The central insight is that skills should be executable state-action functions, not textual guidelines. Given the current agent state and the policy's proposed next action, a Program Function decides whether to fire based on a deterministic predicate over runtime context fields (step count, action type, argument length, search history, etc.) and then either rewrites the action or injects corrective context into the next observation. This transforms a passive recommendation into a hard guardrail that cannot be ignored by the model. The same PF interface is reused without modification for inference-time control, post-training signal generation, and library self-improvement. Methodology HASP wraps a base ReAct agent (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct throughout) with an external harness operating in three modes. At inference time, the harness retrieves candidate PFs via lexical scoring plus optional teacher-based re-ranking, evaluates each PF's `should_activate` predicate on the current state and proposed action, and applies activated PFs through an intervention operator Γ that produces a modified action ã_t or injected context c_t. Rate limits (per-PF fire caps, per-episode FINAL-override limit of 1) prevent oscillation. For post-training, each PF activation emits a structured record (triggering state, original action, corrected action, context, intervention metadata, downstream outcome); these records are scored with a four-signal aggregator weighted (0.15 timing, 0.10 modality, 0.25 correctness, 0.50 outcome) and used to train via SFT on PF-corrected actions, rejection sampling that filters by combined task success and PF-alignment score, or on-policy distillation with GPT-4o as teacher. For self-improvement, residual failures are clustered by heuristic detectors (12 rules) plus LLM summarization, the student proposes candidate PFs at temperature 0.7, and each candidate must pass four executable checks (syntax, interface, mock execution on 9 contexts, return-type) plus a five-dimensional teacher review (concept, trigger, intervention, executability, validation) with a composite threshold ≥ 0.60 before admission. The library is version-controlled and capped at 50 active skills. All post-training uses LoRA rank 16, α=32, bf16, on NVIDIA L40s GPUs. Key Results On web-search reasoning (HotpotQA, 2Wiki, MuSiQue, 200-sample test sets per dataset): PF-only inference raises average accuracy from 31.2% (multi-loop ReAct) to 51.0%; adding teacher PF selection reaches 56.2%; post-training with fixed library + OPD reaches 62.5%; the primary reported variant (HASP-Evolve+RS, closed-loop RS) reaches 60.3%, compared with Search-R1 at 29.9%, ReSearch at 37.8%, VerlTool at 36.5%, and AgentFlow at 53.2%. On mathematical reasoning (AIME24 30 problems, AMC23 40 problems, GameOf24 1362 problems, GPT-4o-judged): HASP-Evolve+RS reaches 45.4% average vs AgentFlow 51.5%, ToRL 37.0%, and SimpleRL-reason 36.6%; HASP underperforms AgentFlow on this domain. On coding (HumanEval, MBPP, BigCodeBench, pass@1): teacher-augmented inference reaches 68.7%, HASP-Evolve+RS reaches 69.9%, comparable to P-GRPO (69.8%) and slightly above GRPO (68.4%). All baselines use the same 7B-Instruct backbone. Ablations show that all four supervision signals and both filtering gates (executable + teacher) are individually necessary: removing the modality signal costs 15.5 points on web-search; unfiltered library evolution costs 24.0 points. The key surprising finding is that unfiltered evolution (36.3%) falls below the multi-loop ReAct baseline (31.2%) wait—it doesn't, but it is dramatically below the full-filtering variant—demonstrating that naive library growth is actively harmful. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The contribution is the Program Function interface as a representation for agent skills—a typed Python object with deterministic activation and executable intervention—which is genuinely novel relative to textual skill stores (Reflexion, ExpeL, Voyager) and distinct from RL-trained policies (Search-R1, AgentFlow). The four-signal post-training scoring scheme and the dual-gated self-improvement pipeline are also non-trivial extensions. What is not novel: the underlying ReAct backbone, the rejection sampling / SFT / distillation training recipes, and the multi-hop QA benchmarks are all standard; the individual failure modes targeted by PFs (premature finalization, repeated search, entity confusion) are well-known. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough: the paper presents a 2×3 training-method grid (SFT/RS/OPD × fixed/evolving library), single-signal dropout ablations for all four supervision signals, and a filtering-gate ablation with five configurations. Error bars and multi-seed results are absent; the authors acknowledge this limitation and report ±1.5 point estimated variance from internal smoke tests with group size g=4, which is insufficient to establish significance for several margins in the 1–3 point range. Math answers are judged by GPT-4o rather than deterministic string matching, introducing judge variance not quantified. The paper states that pre-trained LoRA adapters and evolved skill libraries will be released with the camera-ready under a permissive license; as a preprint (arXiv:2605.17734v1, 18 May 2026), these are not yet public. Full prompts (Boxes C.1–C.4) and hyperparameters (Tables 13, 20, 21) are provided. The paper follows AgentFlow's train/test split protocol for web-search and math, enabling direct comparison, but constructs its own coding splits, which are not independently validated. One methodological concern: the teacher GPT-4o used for PF selection, OPD training, and skill review is the same model used as a baseline comparator and as an answer judge, creating a potential circularity in performance assessment. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile) - Practical: The PF-only inference variant requires only a 7B model and a skill library running on standard GPU hardware; the teacher-augmented variants require concurrent access to GPT-4o, which adds API cost (~$0.045/question) and latency from up to 17 teacher invocation sites per episode. - Efficient: Training uses LoRA rank 16 on 1–2 NVIDIA L40s GPUs for 12–24 wall-clock hours per variant; the full closed-loop evolution adds ~30 hours for 5 web-search epochs, for a total project footprint of 2,000–2,500 GPU-hours—substantial but not extraordinary. - Effective: PFs demonstrably fix specific, identifiable failure modes (as shown in case studies and failure-recovery statistics in Table 16); behavior-correcting PFs (retrieval_failure, multi_hop_reasoning_failure) become unnecessary in 30–100% of cases after post-training, confirming genuine internalization rather than surface-level score inflation. - Scalable: Not evaluated beyond the 7B backbone; AgentFlow with larger RL-trained models outperforms HASP on mathematical reasoning (AIME24), suggesting PF-based elicitation is less effective when failures require genuinely new strategies rather than better application of known ones. Scalability to longer-horizon or open-ended environments is explicitly identified as unresolved. The paper delivers a software artifact (PF runtime, skill library, LoRA adapters) promised for camera-ready release but not yet available. Strengths - The executable PF interface is a principled and verifiable way to encode agent skills; the separation of `should_activate` (deterministic, no LLM calls allowed) from `intervene` (can use a teacher) makes individual PFs auditable, rate-limitable, and unit-testable in a way textual skills cannot be. - The four-signal scoring framework is carefully decomposed: the signal ablation shows each of timing, modality, correctness, and outcome contributes 7–15 independent points, validating the multi-dimensional design against simple final-answer reward. - The filtering ablation makes a concrete and important empirical point: unfiltered library evolution is not neutral but actively degrades performance below a static library, which directly justifies the framework's conservative admission protocol and is a non-obvious finding for the self-improvement literature. - The mechanism analysis (Table 6, Section 4.2) goes beyond headline numbers to identify which PF families trigger, how often, on which benchmarks, and what proportion of gains come from post-training internalization versus persistent online correction—a level of interpretability rare in agent papers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: (1) teacher-dependent variants increase cost and may introduce teacher bias; (2) PFs help apply existing strategies but cannot discover fundamentally new ones, as evidenced by HASP's underperformance on AIME24 relative to RL methods; (3) evaluation is limited to benchmark tasks with clear verification signals, leaving open-ended and weakly verifiable environments unaddressed; (4) the PF interface is restricted to action modification and context injection, excluding more complex intervention forms; (5) self-improving evolution may degrade if generated PFs become too narrow or teacher-biased over time; (6) no multi-seed results are reported. These are stated directly and constitute a credibility-positive presentation of limitations. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The GPT-4o teacher simultaneously plays three roles (PF selector, OPD teacher, answer judge, skill reviewer) without assessing whether teacher-model bias inflates results; a version with a different teacher for judging versus coaching would strengthen the claims. - The comparison to AgentFlow (the strongest baseline on web-search) is complicated by the fact that HASP uses teacher-snippet mode during training rollouts (READ disabled, GPT-4o summarizes), while AgentFlow uses RL with live retrieval; the training distribution differences are underacknowledged. - Math results are weaker than claimed relative to agentflow: HASP-Evolve+RS (45.4%) is notably below AgentFlow (51.5%) on math, yet the abstract's 30.4% gain over Search-R1 is prominently featured while this shortfall is discussed only in Appendix F.3. - The PF library is initialized from training-pool failures; there is no ablation showing how much of HASP's advantage comes from the PF format versus the content of skills that happened to be discovered—a baseline using the same skills but in text form (Prompt-Only Skills) exists but the skill content may differ between conditions. - Single seed throughout; ±1.5 point estimated variance means the 1.0 point gain from evolution over fixed library (59.3% → 60.3%) is not meaningfully distinguishable from noise. Bottom Line HASP makes a genuine engineering and conceptual contribution by operationalizing agent skills as typed, rate-limited, executable functions with auditable before/after action traces, and the empirical evidence that both the PF substrate and the four-signal scoring independently matter is convincing; practitioners building ReAct-style agents on web-search tasks should adopt the inference-time PF intervention, but the math results and single-seed design temper confidence in the training-based and self-improving variants.
Recommended 2605.17734 PDF
#99 — Innovative (63)

KVPO: ODE-Native GRPO for Autoregressive Video Alignment via KV Semantic Exploration (34 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces KVPO, an ODE-native online policy optimization framework that aligns streaming autoregressive (AR) video generators with human preferences by replacing noise-based exploration with stochastic routing of the historical KV cache. - Why it's important: Distilled AR video generators run as deterministic ODE systems, so injecting noise for reinforcement learning exploration breaks their native dynamics and perturbs low-level appearance rather than the high-level semantic storyline progression that governs long-horizon coherence. - How it does it: Causal History Routing (CHR) constructs semantically diverse generation branches by stochastically resampling which historical KV frames fill the local attention window, while Trajectory Velocity Energy (TVE) defines a Gibbs-form surrogate policy in velocity-field space to compute PPO importance ratios without relying on external geometric distances or SDE transitions. - The good: Replacing the TVE surrogate with a geometric latent ℓ2 surrogate (as used in NeighborGRPO) drops VQ from 8.14 to 6.02 on the long-video LongLive benchmark, providing a clean ablation that isolates the value of velocity-field-native policy modeling. - The missing: The method is evaluated on only two base models (LongLive and MemFlow) and compared against a single post-training competitor (Astrolabe); the closely related AR-CoPO, which also targets ODE-native alignment for AR video, is discussed but not included as an experimental baseline. - Read it or not: Researchers working on RLHF/preference alignment for flow-matching or ODE-based generative models should read this; it presents a coherent principled framework, but practitioners wanting off-the-shelf results will note the 32 H200 GPU training budget and limited comparison breadth.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (78) drives the score — using the historical KV cache as an on-manifold exploration locus and a velocity-field-space (TVE) surrogate that respects the ODE Jacobian is genuinely surprising and well-derived, supported by the clean TVE-vs-ℓ2 ablation (VQ 8.14→6.02). Rigor (58) and Completeness/Resilience (58) are held down by the omission of the closest competitor AR-CoPO as an experimental baseline, an underpowered N=32 human study with no significance tests, near-zero/negative TA values, and an evaluation-independence concern (LongLive/MemFlow/VideoAlign share authorship). Code is released but weights are not. Composite: 0.20*72 + 0.25*58 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*58 + 0.15*58 + 0.10*50 = 63.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 58 Resilience 58 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Streaming autoregressive video generators — obtained by distilling diffusion models into few-step causal generators that use KV caching for real-time synthesis — need preference alignment to improve visual quality, motion realism, and text fidelity beyond what supervised distillation achieves. Existing alignment approaches fall into two ill-fitting categories for this setting: (1) reward-weighted distillation (e.g., Reward Forcing) lacks active exploration; (2) SDE/noise-injection approaches (Flow-GRPO, DanceGRPO, Astrolabe) import stochastic exploration from diffusion models, but distilled AR generators are natively deterministic ODEs. Converting them to SDEs violates their native probability-flow formulation, induces off-manifold structural distortions, and causes exploration variance to conflate noise magnitude with policy preference signal. ODE-centric alternatives like NeighborGRPO/AR-CoPO approximate surrogate policies via Euclidean distances in latent space, which imposes an isotropic geometry assumption inconsistent with the nonlinear ODE flow's true Jacobian structure. The field therefore lacks a principled exploration and surrogate policy framework that is natively aligned with the deterministic ODE dynamics of few-step AR video generators. Key Idea: The core insight is that, for streaming AR video models, the historical KV cache is both the primary source of long-horizon temporal conditioning and a natural locus for inducing semantically distinct generation branches without ever leaving the data manifold. By randomly resampling which historical frames occupy the local attention window (Causal History Routing), KVPO creates G branches that differ in semantic storyline while sharing the same initial noise latent — disentangling policy diversity from noise variance. The companion insight is that the replay residual between a branch's cached velocity targets and the current model's re-predicted velocities under unperturbed context directly measures how likely the current policy is to reproduce that branch, yielding a geometrically sound surrogate policy grounded in the model's native flow-matching objective. Methodology: KVPO operates over block-wise autoregressive generation under the flow-matching framework, where each block is generated by integrating a probability-flow ODE. Training proceeds in three coupled stages per iteration. (1) Exploration via CHR: a pivot block b is sampled; the sink KV (earliest 3 frames) is left unchanged; 6 of the 9 local KV slots are stochastically refilled from the routable history (frames 4..L-3) with branch-specific index sets, while the 3 nearest frames remain shared. G=8 branch trajectories are generated from the same initial noise within a perturbed window B=[b, b*+W]. Critical design decisions: perturbing only the local KV (not the sink) preserves global anchors; applying CHR only to the first half of ODE solver steps (coarse layout phase) avoids destabilizing fine-detail steps. (2) Replay and TVE: each branch's intermediate states and rollout velocity targets are cached, then replayed under the unperturbed default context to compute TVE — the mean squared residual between replayed and cached velocities across all perturbed blocks and solver steps. Gibbs parameterization converts TVE values into a normalized branch probability distribution serving as the surrogate policy. The PPO importance ratio ρg = πθ(g)/πold(g) is computed in log space from TVE scores. (3) Optimization via clipped PPO with asymmetric clip range (ϵ_low=0.1, ϵ_high=0.2) plus a KL divergence penalty (β=5) toward the frozen reference policy; the gradient is zeroed for iterations where no branch exceeds the anchor reward. The reward is a composite of three signals: HPSv3 for Visual Quality (VQ), and VideoAlign-derived metrics for Motion Quality (MQ) and Text-Video Alignment (TA), averaged per segment for long-video settings. LoRA fine-tuning (rank 256, α=256) is used throughout. Key Results: Evaluated on two base models (LongLive and MemFlow) in both single-prompt short-video and multi-prompt long-video settings; baselines are the respective base models and Astrolabe. On LongLive short-video, KVPO improves VQ from 8.86 to 10.21 (+15.2%), MQ from 1.80 to 1.89 (+5.0%), and TA from 0.02 to 0.06 (+200%); on MemFlow short-video, VQ from 8.83 to 9.71 (+9.1%), MQ from 1.82 to 1.87 (+2.7%), TA from 0.02 to 0.03 (+50%). On long-video LongLive, VQ improves from 6.34 to 8.14 (+28.4%), MQ from 1.41 to 1.50 (+6.4%), TA from -0.19 to -0.14 (+26.3%); on MemFlow long-video, +10.5%, +3.6%, +15.0% respectively. KVPO consistently outperforms Astrolabe, with the gap widening in the multi-prompt long-video setting. VBench auxiliary metrics (Quality, Semantic, Consistency Score, CLIP Score) show consistent but more modest improvements. A human study with 32 participants confirms KVPO wins the majority preference vote over baseline and Astrolabe on all three dimensions. The ablation showing TVE vs. ℓ2 surrogate is the strongest individual result: replacing TVE drops VQ from 8.14 to 6.02, a 26% degradation. Note: TA baseline values are near zero (0.02) or negative (-0.19), so percentage-based improvement figures are misleading and absolute gains (≈0.04–0.05 for short-video) are modest in absolute terms. Training runs on 32 H200 GPUs for ~30 hours (~1000 GPU-hours) per model. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is the combination of two specifically designed components for ODE-native AR preference alignment: (1) CHR as a structured, on-manifold exploration mechanism using the KV cache, and (2) TVE as a velocity-field-space surrogate policy that respects the Riemannian geometry of the flow-matching ODE rather than imposing isotropic Euclidean assumptions. Applying PPO/GRPO-style optimization to video generation is not new (Flow-GRPO, DanceGRPO, Astrolabe), nor is LoRA fine-tuning; what is new is both the exploration source and the surrogate policy formulation being co-designed for ODE dynamics. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and well-scoped — CHR hyperparameters (perturbed blocks, slot counts, solver steps) and the KL weight β are each systematically varied, and the TVE vs. ℓ2 surrogate comparison directly tests the core policy modeling claim. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple seeds are reported for any quantitative result. The human study involves only 32 participants, which is small for statistical reliability on a 3-option forced choice. Code is listed at https://github.com/Richard-Zhang-AI/KVPO; trained model weights are not mentioned as released. Evaluation benchmarks (VBench, HPSv3, VideoAlign) are standard in the video generation community. Primary concern: AR-CoPO (the most closely related competing method, also targeting ODE-native AR alignment) is cited in related work but not included as an experimental baseline, making it impossible to assess KVPO's margin over the strongest direct prior. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (a new training algorithm applied to existing models, evaluated by generation quality metrics). - Practical: Requires 32 NVIDIA H200 GPUs for training (~1000 GPU-hours); inference is unchanged from the base AR model, so deployment-time hardware requirements are unaffected. - Efficient: Training overhead is non-trivial: each iteration takes ~960 seconds on 32 H200s; replay caching adds memory proportional to branch count × replay horizon × solver steps, though the paper notes this remains below peak backpropagation memory. - Effective: VQ and MQ improvements on both base models are consistent and reproduced across two independent architectures, providing reasonable evidence; TA absolute gains are small even where percentage improvements appear large. - Scalable: Tested only on the two base models at their existing video lengths and resolutions; no experiments at larger model scale, longer video lengths, or higher resolution are reported or claimed. The work delivers a training algorithm (with code) and LoRA-fine-tuned variants of two base models, but does not release trained weights publicly per the paper. Strengths: - The Gibbs-form TVE surrogate policy is a principled derivation that connects replay residuals to PPO importance ratios through log-probability algebra, and the gradient analysis in Section 3.3 rigorously shows the resulting update reduces to a reward-weighted contrastive flow-matching objective. - The on-manifold guarantee for CHR (Appendix C) is a clean theoretical argument: because CHR changes only the conditioning context and not the initial noise, each branch is an exact sample from the corresponding conditional model distribution, avoiding the off-manifold degradation documented in SDE-based methods. - The ablation comparing TVE vs. ℓ2 surrogate (Table 2, VQ: 8.14 vs. 6.02) is a crisp empirical test that isolates the impact of the surrogate policy formulation and directly supports the theoretical motivation. - The theoretical analysis of noise-based exploration in Appendix D, showing that Euclidean distance conflates noise magnitude with policy signal and that the ODE Jacobian induces anisotropic geometry that ℓ2 ignores, is a concrete, quantifiable critique rather than qualitative intuition. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge three limitations: (1) CHR is architecture-specific — it requires KV-cache-based memory and does not straightforwardly extend to Mamba-style state-space models; (2) training incurs additional compute and memory from replay state caching (though argued to be small relative to backpropagation); (3) optimization quality is bounded by the fidelity of the reward model, and weak reward signals for long-range consistency or subtle motion realism will limit KVPO's gains. The authors confront these honestly, particularly the architecture constraint, which meaningfully scopes the method's applicability. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) AR-CoPO, which directly extends NeighborGRPO to AR video generation and is the closest competitor by method family, is discussed in related work but omitted from Table 1 — without this comparison the claimed improvement over ODE-native baselines rests only on the NeighborGRPO ℓ2 ablation in Table 2, not a head-to-head result. (2) Human study with N=32 is underpowered for a 3-way preference judgment across three metrics; no inter-rater agreement or statistical tests are reported. (3) All TA values remain small in absolute terms (short-video: 0.02→0.06 on a scale not defined in the paper) and TA is negative for long-video even after improvement (-0.19→-0.14), suggesting either the metric is not well-calibrated for this setting or the model is genuinely failing on text alignment even post-training. (4) Evaluation uses only the VidProM prompt distribution; generalization to out-of-distribution prompts is not assessed. (5) Both base models appear to be from the same lab or collaborator ecosystem (MemFlow, LongLive, VideoAlign all share overlapping authorship with this work), raising a potential evaluation independence concern. Bottom Line: A practitioner working on post-training alignment for ODE-based or flow-matching video generators will find the TVE surrogate policy formulation and the CHR exploration mechanism genuinely useful and well-motivated, but should note that the omission of AR-CoPO as a direct experimental baseline and the small human study leave the claimed advantages over the strongest prior work incompletely established.
Recommended 2605.14278 PDF
#100 — Innovative (63)

Post-Trained MoE Can Skip Half Experts via Self-Distillation (25 ▲ )

Xingtai Lv, Li Sheng, Kaiyan Zhang, Yichen You, Siyan Gao, Xueheng Luo, Yuxin Zuo, Yuchen Fan, Junlin Yang, Ganqu Cui, Bingning Wang, Fan Yang, Youbang Sun, Ning Ding, Bowen Zhou · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ZEDA (Zero-Expert Self-Distillation Adaptation), a post-training framework that converts any fully post-trained static MoE language model into a dynamic MoE that skips over 50% of expert FLOPs by injecting parameter-free zero-output experts and recovering performance via two-stage self-distillation. - Why it's important: Deploying large MoE LLMs is expensive at inference time, and prior methods for making expert activation dynamic require either pre-training from scratch or task-specific adaptation that discards the accumulated post-training alignment — ZEDA eliminates that cost by operating entirely in the post-training regime. - How it does it: Zero experts (whose outputs are identically zero) are injected to expand the router candidate pool; the router learns to route easy tokens to these no-ops via SFT on teacher-sampled responses followed by on-policy distillation against the frozen original model, all regulated by a group-level balancing loss that controls the normal-vs-zero expert activation ratio without flattening the learned routing distribution among normal experts. - The good: On Qwen3-30B-A3B across 11 benchmarks, ZEDA achieves 74.2 average accuracy vs. the original 74.9 (–0.7 points) at 51.2% zero-expert activation, outperforming the strongest dynamic MoE baseline (Dynamic Skipping at 68.1) by 6.1 points while delivering a measured ~1.20× end-to-end throughput speedup. - The missing: The method is validated only on two models at ~30B scale; no evaluation on models above that size, and OOD results for GLM-4.7-Flash show a non-trivial 5.6-point drop on GPQA-Diamond (62.4 → 56.8) that the paper under-emphasizes. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML engineers deploying MoE models who want a practical post-training recipe for inference speedup; the group auxiliary loss and zero-vs-copy-expert analysis are the technically substantive contributions that practitioners will want to understand.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Composite = 0.20*62 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*62 = 63. Resilience (60) and Novelty (62) pull the composite down: the headline 6.1-point average gap is partly an artifact of baseline collapse (AdaMoE 25.0 on AIME, Dynamic Skipping failing on coding), so the advantage shrinks under adversarial review, and the under-analyzed 5.6-point GLM GPQA-Diamond OOD regression contradicts the 'negligible loss' framing. Novelty is bounded because ZEDA recombines established components (zero experts from MoE++, on-policy distillation, load-balancing losses); the group-level auxiliary loss and zero-vs-copy analysis are the genuinely new and well-validated pieces, lifting Non-Obviousness (70). Rigor (62) is solid on ablation breadth and variance-aware metrics (avg@32) but limited by no error bars across the full suite, proprietary NVIDIA training data, no public code/weights, validation only at ~30B scale, and preprint status. A capable, practically useful post-training recipe, but the inflated comparison and unexplained OOD drop keep it in the Innovative band rather than higher.
empirical Novelty 62 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large MoE language models (Qwen3, GLM-4.7, DeepSeek variants) are trained through extensive pipelines of pre-training, SFT, RL, and on-policy distillation. The resulting static models activate a fixed top-K experts per token regardless of token difficulty, wasting computation on easy tokens. Dynamic MoE — varying the number of active experts per token — addresses this, but existing dynamic MoE methods either require re-pretraining from scratch or are designed for base (not post-trained) models. Naively applying standard dynamic MoE techniques to a post-trained model disrupts its carefully calibrated routing distributions and capability alignment. The gap is a low-cost conversion path from a fully post-trained static MoE to an efficient dynamic one without sacrificing the post-training investment. Key Idea: The core trick is that a parameter-free zero expert — an expert whose output is always the zero vector — acts as a learned skip connection at the routing level. By expanding the router candidate pool with N_Z such experts while keeping the top-K activation budget fixed, any token that routes to a zero expert effectively uses fewer normal experts without changing the architecture. The router learns when to prefer zero experts over real ones via distillation from the original frozen model. The non-obvious design choice that makes this work is operating the balancing loss at the group level (normal experts vs. zero experts) rather than the expert level, which avoids flattening the non-uniform learned routing distribution among real experts. Methodology: ZEDA proceeds in two steps. First, N_Z zero experts are injected into every MoE layer, expanding each router from N to N+N_Z candidates while the top-K constraint is unchanged. Router parameters for zero experts are initialized from a Gaussian matching the mean and variance of existing router weights, preserving logit scale. The resulting dynamic MoE is then adapted through two stages of self-distillation against the frozen original model as teacher. Stage 1 (SFT): the student is trained on teacher-sampled responses via next-token prediction loss plus a group auxiliary loss L_GA. Stage 2 (OPD, on-policy distillation): student-sampled rollouts are scored against the teacher distribution via a sampled-token reverse KL objective cast as a reward within a policy-optimization framework. Both stages include L_GA, which imposes a group-level load balancing constraint between the N normal experts (one group) and N_Z zero experts (another group), with a weight parameter w controlling the target zero-expert activation ratio r_ZE = (N_Z · w)/(N + N_Z · w). Setting w=2 and N_Z = N/2 targets r_ZE ≈ 50%. Three critical design decisions: (1) group-level vs. expert-level balancing — well-justified by ablation showing L_A degrades Qwen average accuracy from 82.8 to 59.5 while L_GA retains 82.2; (2) zero vs. copy experts — well-justified by both theoretical analysis (copy experts introduce scale and direction mismatch in every layer) and empirical results (copy experts collapse on AIME, achieving ~1–3% vs. ~70–79% for zero experts); (3) SFT before OPD — justified by ablation showing SFT alone > OPD alone, and SFT→OPD > both, attributed to SFT first establishing stable zero-expert routing patterns. Key Results: On Qwen3-30B-A3B (N=128, K=8, N_Z=64), ZEDA achieves 74.2 average accuracy vs. 74.9 original, with rZE=51.2%, outperforming AdaMoE (54.8) and Dynamic Skipping (68.1) by large margins. On GLM-4.7-Flash (N=64, K=4, N_Z=32), ZEDA achieves 71.8 vs. 72.5 original, rZE=53.0%, vs. AdaMoE (57.1) and Dynamic Skipping (67.8). End-to-end inference throughput speedup is measured at approximately 1.18–1.26× prefill and 1.19–1.25× decode on a single H200 at sequence lengths from 2K to 8K. Notably, baselines AdaMoE and Dynamic Skipping exhibit domain-specific collapse: AdaMoE scores 25.0/24.8/36.7 on AIME 24/25/26 and Dynamic Skipping scores 59.1/70.0 on HumanEval+/MBPP+, making the 6.1-point average gap partly reflect baseline failure rather than uniform ZEDA superiority. OOD evaluation on MMLU-Redux and GPQA-Diamond shows ZEDA retains 89.2% MMLU (vs. 90.1) and 63.2 GPQA (vs. 63.3) for Qwen — effectively zero degradation — but GLM shows 89.0 MMLU (vs. 89.8, –0.8) and 56.8 GPQA (vs. 62.4, –5.6 points). Adaptation costs 30 GPU-hours (Qwen) and 62 GPU-hours (GLM) on 8×H200. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The combination of zero-expert injection (from MoE++) with self-distillation specifically for post-trained MoE adaptation is genuinely new, as is the group auxiliary loss formulation. The constituent ideas — zero-computation experts (MoE++, LongCat-Flash), on-policy distillation (MiniLLM, GKD), and load balancing losses — are individually well-established; the novelty lies in the specific combination and the group-level balancing design that enables stable adaptation of post-trained models. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are thorough and cover the key design decisions: L_GA vs. L_A, zero vs. copy experts, SFT vs. OPD vs. SFT→OPD, router renormalization, and w/α sensitivity. Evaluation uses avg@32 for AIME (reducing variance on small-sample competition benchmarks) and avg@8 for coding benchmarks, which is methodologically sound. However, no error bars or statistical significance tests appear across the full 11-benchmark suite; for benchmarks with high variance like AIME, the single-run avg@32 provides some variance reduction but cross-run variance is not reported. No code, model weights, or training data are released; the training data uses proprietary NVIDIA AceReason-1.1-SFT and Llama-Nemotron-Post-Training-Dataset, which limits reproducibility. The paper is a preprint with no peer review. The main concern is that AdaMoE shows catastrophic collapse on AIME (25.0 vs. 80.9 original), suggesting either a tuning failure or a fundamental incompatibility — the paper does not investigate whether AdaMoE could be rescued with better hyperparameters, which would affect interpretation of the 6.1-point gap. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs on 8×H200 GPUs; inference runs on a single H200 using SGLang, making the adapted model deployable on commodity high-end hardware. - Efficient: Adaptation costs 30–62 GPU-hours on 8×H200s depending on model, which is low relative to full pre-training but non-trivial for practitioners without H200 clusters; inference achieves ~1.20× throughput gain, with speedup decaying from ~1.36× at 2K tokens to ~1.26× at 8K for GLM. - Effective: The evidence shows it solves the stated problem — dynamic expert activation with <1-point accuracy drop on 11 in-distribution benchmarks — though GLM shows a 5.6-point OOD regression on GPQA-Diamond that is not adequately explained. - Scalable: Only tested at ~30B scale; the authors explicitly acknowledge no evaluation on larger MoE models due to compute constraints, so the claim of practical value for large-scale MoE systems is extrapolated, not measured. The work delivers a training recipe and trained model variant but no public software artifact or weights. Strengths: - The group auxiliary loss is a genuine algorithmic contribution: the insight that expert-level uniformity is harmful for post-trained MoE (because routing is intentionally non-uniform) and should be replaced by group-level balancing is non-obvious and well-validated. - The zero-vs-copy-expert analysis (Appendix B) is unusually rigorous, providing both empirical benchmarks and mechanistic analysis (layer-wise cosine similarity and norm difference) explaining why copy experts fail catastrophically while zero experts succeed. - The token-level rZE analysis (Section 4.1) provides interpretable insight: computation allocation tracks teacher-student distributional gap and token entropy, not task difficulty — code/math tokens get more zero-expert skipping than natural language within the same response, which is both surprising and practically informative. - Two-model evaluation across different MoE architectures (Qwen3 with 128 experts/K=8 and GLM-4.7 with 64 experts/K=4) provides some evidence of generalizability beyond a single architectural choice. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state three limitations in Appendix A: (1) only ~30B-scale MoE tested, not larger models; (2) no evaluation on long-horizon agentic tasks; (3) speedup degrades with sequence length, reaching ~1.18–1.20× at 8K tokens. These are confronted directly and honestly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most significant unacknowledged gap is the 5.6-point GPQA-Diamond regression for GLM-4.7-Flash (56.8 vs. 62.4), which the paper mentions briefly in Table 7 but does not analyze or explain — this is a 9% relative performance drop on scientific reasoning for a model variant claimed to have "negligible" capability loss. The comparison baselines (AdaMoE, Dynamic Skipping) exhibit domain-specific catastrophic failures that make the gap appear larger than it is; a fairer comparison would require properly tuning these baselines or including a variant of ZEDA without OPD (ZEDASFT) as the primary comparison point against baselines. The training data dependency on proprietary NVIDIA datasets (AceReason, Llama-Nemotron) means the method's performance under different or open training data is unknown. The paper also does not address whether the 60K prompt training set composition (math/code/chat split) is optimal or sensitive — the ablation only varies data quantity, not domain mix. Bottom Line: Practitioners deploying post-trained MoE models (Qwen3, GLM, DeepSeek variants) who want a practical inference speedup recipe without re-pretraining will find ZEDA's group auxiliary loss and two-stage distillation procedure directly usable — the 1.2× throughput gain at <1-point accuracy cost on 30B-scale models is credible, though the unexplained GLM OOD regression and lack of public code mean careful re-validation will be needed before production deployment.
Recommended 2605.18643 PDF
#101 — Innovative (62)

HqeKV: Towards Hybrid Quantization and Eviction for KV Cache in Long-Context LLM Inference

wzgcs.github.io · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces HqeKV, a KV cache compression framework that combines five compression actions (FP16, 4-bit, 2-bit, 1-bit quantization, and eviction) with a joint K–V importance metric and an offline TPE-based optimizer to allocate precision ratios automatically. - Why it's important: KV cache memory grows linearly with sequence length and can dominate GPU memory in long-context serving, forcing a hard quality-memory tradeoff that existing quantization-only or eviction-only methods handle poorly. - How it does it: KV pairs are sorted by a joint importance score (cumulative attention weights for K, and attention weights × value-vector range for V), then a Tree-structured Parzen Estimator searches offline for the optimal ratio of tokens assigned to each of the five compression tiers; bit-width-specific quantization strategies (uniform for 4/2-bit, normalized for 1-bit) are applied per tier. - The good: On LongBench with Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct at 3.25-bit average compression, HqeKV-3.25 achieves an average score of 50.33 versus 49.94 for KVTuner-3.25 and 40.53 for CAKE-3.25, with particularly large gains over eviction-only CAKE on QA tasks (120–124% relative improvement). - The missing: The TPE ratio search is conducted once on Wikitext-2 and transferred to all tasks; there is no cross-dataset or cross-model transferability study showing these fixed ratios generalize, and the evaluation is limited to two 8B-scale models. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building long-context LLM serving systems who need to understand the interaction between quantization granularity, eviction, and importance metrics; can be skipped by those seeking theoretical guarantees or very large-scale validation.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (62) and Non-Obviousness (65) credit the principled range-as-V-importance metric derived from an analytic quantization-error bound (PCC ≥ 0.91) and the five-tier hybrid action space with automated TPE ratio search; public code release is a strong positive aligning with the rigorous positive-calibration set. Rigor (62) and Resilience (58) cap the score: all results are single-run with no error bars, the TPE ratios are searched once on Wikitext-2 and transferred to all tasks/models with no transferability study, the TTFT/TPOT benchmarks use unrepresentative 161-token contexts rather than the long-context regime targeted, and reasoning coverage is a thin 30-problem AIME comparison against one baseline.
empirical Novelty 62 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 62 Resilience 58 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: In autoregressive LLM inference, the KV cache grows linearly with sequence length and can exceed model weight memory at 128k-token contexts. Inference-as-a-Service providers need to compress this cache without degrading generation quality. Existing compression methods split into two camps: quantization methods reduce precision but apply coarse or uniform strategies that ignore token-level information density variation; eviction methods discard low-importance tokens entirely but suffer severe quality degradation on QA tasks. Prior hybrid methods (EVICPRESS, MiniKV) treat quantization and eviction as separate subsystems bolted together, without co-optimizing the boundary between them or using a common importance signal for both. Additionally, all prior work either ignores value (V) vectors in the importance metric or incorporates them without principled justification. Key Idea: The core insight is twofold. First, the expected quantization error for a vector is determined by its dynamic range (max − min), which is derived analytically from the quantization error integral and validated empirically via Pearson correlation coefficients ≥ 0.91 on actual V-cache tokens. This justifies using the V-vector range as a signal in the joint importance metric. Second, given a joint importance ordering, a single offline optimizer (TPE, 200 iterations) can search over the continuous space of precision-tier ratios to find the allocation that minimizes calibration cross-entropy plus a regularization penalty discouraging extreme low-precision fractions—collapsing the manual tuning problem into a one-time ~8-minute precompute. Methodology: HqeKV operates in three phases. During prefilling, K and V are generated; importance metrics are computed per chunk (group size G=32): K-importance is cumulative attention weight over the last m=20 tokens; V-importance is the elementwise product of cumulative attention weight and the per-token value range. Chunks are partitioned into five tiers using the offline-optimized ratio vector (δ₁, δ₂, δ₃, δ₄) for 4-bit/2-bit/1-bit/eviction, with the remainder in FP16; the two highest-scoring chunks are always kept in FP16. K uses per-channel quantization; V uses per-token quantization. Bit-width-specific strategy: uniform quantization for 4-bit and 2-bit, normalized (quantile-based) quantization for 1-bit, justified by empirical comparison showing normalized is better only below 2-bit. During decoding, the KV cache is periodically re-quantized every T=32 generated tokens; tokens can only move to lower or equal precision (never upward), justified by the empirical observation that fewer than 3.4% of cache entries would transition upward. A custom Triton kernel packs quantized values densely.
