Critical design decisions: (1) using range as V-importance proxy — analytically justified and empirically validated with PCC ≥ 0.91; (2) per-bit quantization strategy differentiation — justified by Table 2/3 showing reversal in optimal strategy at 1-bit vs. 4-bit; (3) TPE-based offline ratio search — avoids exhaustive grid search over the 4-parameter constrained space in ~8 minutes. Key Results: On LongBench (21 tasks, bilingual) with Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct: HqeKV-2 scores 49.71 vs. FP16 49.76, KIVI-2 48.96, OTT-2 49.43; HqeKV-3.25 scores 50.33 vs. KVTuner-3.25 49.94 and CAKE-3.25 40.53. HqeKV achieves best score on 81.25% of individual tasks. On AIME-2025 math reasoning with Qwen3-8B, HqeKV-2 achieves 0.20 accuracy vs. KIVI-2 at 0.17. Memory: HqeKV reduces memory usage by 29.6% vs. OTT, 8.3% vs. ZipCache, and 8.6% vs. KVTuner at batch size 20, context 4096. Maximum supportable batch size is highest among compared methods in most context-length settings. TTFT overhead is at most ~2 seconds versus KIVI-2 (10.64s vs. 9.35s); HqeKV-3.2 is faster than ZipCache-3.2 (11.18s vs. 20.13s). ZipCache and KVTuner encounter OOM or CUDA errors on several long-context LongBench tasks at identical bit-widths, making their reported averages non-comparable without asterisk notation. Two baselines (KIVI, OTT) are conference-published; ZipCache is NeurIPS 2024; KVTuner and CAKE are ICML/ICLR 2025, constituting strong and current comparisons. All experiments use a single NVIDIA RTX A6000 (48GB); tests are conducted on 8B-scale models only. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The primary contribution is the algorithmic co-design of a five-tier hybrid quantization-eviction action space with a theoretically grounded joint K–V importance metric and an automated offline precision-ratio optimizer. The range-as-V-importance observation is a genuine insight derived from an analytic bound on quantization error, not a heuristic. The per-bit strategy differentiation (uniform vs. normalized) is empirically derived but not previously applied in this context. The combination of all three novelties distinguishes HqeKV from prior hybrid methods that treat quantization and eviction as loosely coupled subsystems. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations (Table 6 and Table 14) test each of the four innovations in isolation and show that removing any single component reduces average LongBench score, with the quantization strategy component (innovation II) being most critical (drop from 49.91 to 41.0 when using uniform quantization only). A range-only ablation (Table 10) confirms range alone is not a valid importance metric. Error bars and significance tests are not reported; all results are single-run point estimates. Code is released at https://github.com/skywclouds/HqeKV. Calibration dataset (Wikitext-2, 16k tokens) is disjoint from evaluation benchmarks (confirmed explicitly). However, the TPE ratio search uses a single calibration set and model; cross-model or cross-task transferability of found ratios is not evaluated. The TTFT/TPOT evaluation uses a short context (161 tokens) that is not representative of the long-context setting the paper targets. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS (proposes and evaluates a built compression system with custom CUDA/Triton kernels). - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA RTX A6000 (48GB) with batch sizes up to 50 at 4k context; the custom Triton kernel for dense packing is a prerequisite for the stated memory gains. - Efficient: TTFT adds at most ~1.3s (14%) overhead vs. KIVI-2; TPOT is higher than KIVI and KVTuner but lower than ZipCache; the 8-minute offline TPE search is a one-time amortized cost per model and bit-width target. - Effective: Evidence shows HqeKV matches near-lossless quality at 2-bit average (49.71 vs. FP16 49.76 on LongBench) and outperforms all quantization-eviction hybrids at 3.2–3.25-bit, with particularly strong gains on QA tasks; math reasoning (AIME) comparison is limited to one baseline. - Scalable: Only tested at 8B parameter scale with up to 128k context length (model maximum) but no explicit stress test at the full 128k context; batch size scaling is tested up to 50; behavior at 70B+ models or sequence lengths beyond the tested regime is not evaluated. The work delivers a software artifact (open-source code with custom Triton kernels and pretrained compression configurations). Strengths: - The derivation of V-vector range as a quantization loss proxy from first principles (Equation 2) provides a principled motivation absent from prior work using 1-norm of V heuristically. - The five-tier compression action space with automated boundary optimization is a genuine algorithmic contribution that removes manual ratio tuning and consistently improves over fixed-ratio alternatives. - The per-bit quantization strategy differentiation is demonstrated empirically on both real KV cache data and synthetic normal vectors, with convergent findings, and the cross-over point is correctly identified. - The memory efficiency results show HqeKV avoids the storage overhead bugs in OTT, ZipCache, and KVTuner (bit-packing vs. 8-bit storage), and the Triton kernel delivers practical gains rather than just algorithmic claims. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note: (1) the offline ratio search is agnostic to user-level or task-level heterogeneity; (2) significant quality loss persists at 1-bit extreme compression despite normalized quantization; (3) the fixed re-quantization interval T introduces sensitivity to context length in long-generation scenarios; (4) behavior under multimodal architectures is not studied. These are stated directly and constitute a credible limitation section. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) The TPE calibration uses 16k tokens from Wikitext-2 on one model; there is no experiment showing that the found ratios transfer across different model families (e.g., Llama vs. Qwen architectures search independently, but no cross-architecture transfer is tested). (2) The TTFT and TPOT benchmarks use very short context (161 tokens) or 4k context — not the long-context regime (32k–128k) the paper's motivation targets; throughput characteristics at high context lengths are not reported. (3) ZipCache and KVTuner comparisons are confounded by OOM/CUDA error in some tasks, and the averages marked with '*' are computed over different subsets, making direct numeric comparison imprecise. (4) The only reasoning benchmark is AIME-2025 with 30 problems comparing only HqeKV-2 vs. KIVI-2, which is thin coverage for a paper claiming production-grade quality. (5) Importance metric PCC values (0.91–0.96) are measured on one dataset and model; generalization to other token distributions is assumed but not tested. Bottom Line: Practitioners building long-context LLM serving infrastructure will find genuine value in HqeKV's principled joint K–V importance metric, the automated precision-ratio optimizer, and the memory-efficient Triton implementation, though the evaluation gaps at true long-context throughput and across larger model scales warrant independent validation before production adoption.
Search Results
#102 — Innovative (62)

CR^ 2: Cost-Aware Risk-Controlled Routing for Wireless Device-Edge LLM Inference

Nan Xue, Shengkang Chen, Zhiyong Chen, Jiangchao Yao, Yaping Sun, Zixia Hu, Meixia Tao · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CR², a two-stage device-edge LLM routing framework that decouples a lightweight on-device margin gate (deciding local vs. deferred execution) from a state-aware edge utility selector, with conformal risk control calibration to bound false-acceptance risk at each cost-weight operating point. - Why it's important: Mobile-edge LLM deployments require routing decisions under asymmetric information — the UE cannot observe edge model utilities before deferral — and under time-varying wireless costs that existing centralized-cloud routers ignore, causing suboptimal latency-energy-accuracy trade-offs. - How it does it: A frozen-encoder margin gate is distilled from a full-information teacher selector via sign, margin, and monotonicity losses, conditioned on the operator cost weight λ via FiLM; a per-λ CRC calibration then converts the gate score into a deployable acceptance threshold with a distribution-free false-acceptance risk guarantee. - The good: CR² reduces normalized deployment cost by up to 16.9% at matched accuracy versus the strongest evaluated baseline (KNN), while the deployed device-edge gate closely tracks the full-information reference policy across most cost ranges. - The missing: All routing experiments use simulated wireless states drawn from a parameterized channel model rather than a real wireless deployment, and the model pool is limited to four Qwen3 variants in a single hardware configuration — generalization to larger pools or heterogeneous UE fleets is untested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on query routing for LLM serving in resource-constrained or mobile-edge settings, particularly those interested in conformal risk control applied to routing decisions; practitioners deploying only cloud-based routers can skip it.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Novelty (72) and Non-Obviousness (70) drive the composite — the deployment-constrained, asymmetric-information two-stage routing formulation with a FiLM-conditioned margin gate and per-λ CRC false-acceptance guarantee is a genuinely new framing in LLM routing, and CRC-as-routing-safety is a distribution-free property no prior router provides. Rigor (58) is held back by fully simulated wireless states (parameterized channel model, no real links), absent error bars/significance tests, and no public code, despite solid ablations over loss terms, monotonicity weight, and α levels. Resilience (55) suffers from the simulated-cost gap and a non-trivial exchangeability assumption under non-stationary channels, and Impact (45) reflects a trained gate plus threshold table rather than a released library. Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*58 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*45 = 62.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 55 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: As LLMs move to mobile-edge platforms, serving a query requires choosing between a lightweight on-device model and stronger but costlier edge models over wireless links. The key constraint distinguishing this from centralized routing is a two-stage information structure: the UE must commit to local execution or deferral using only locally available signals (query embedding, operator cost preference), without access to edge model accuracy estimates or real-time runtime state ξ (wireless channel gains, instantaneous latency/energy). Existing query-level routers assume a centralized model pool and optimize static quality-cost proxies, failing to encode communication overhead, state-dependent energy costs, or the asymmetric consequences of false local acceptance (irreversibly forgoes a better edge model) vs. unnecessary deferral (adds latency/energy but recoverable at the edge). Key Idea: The central insight is to formulate UE local acceptance as a partial-information surrogate problem: rather than classifying query difficulty directly, the margin gate learns to estimate the sign and magnitude of the full-information utility margin Δ(x, ξ; λ) = u_{m₀}(x, ξ; λ) − max_{m ∈ M_edge} u_m(x, ξ; λ), which captures whether local execution is utility-competitive with the best available edge model. Because this margin depends on runtime state ξ (which the UE cannot observe before deferral), a teacher selector with access to ξ provides offline supervision via knowledge distillation. A FiLM-conditioned architecture allows a single gate to generalize across all cost weights λ, and CRC calibrates the gate score to a deployable threshold with a formal marginal false-acceptance risk guarantee. Methodology: The pipeline has three components trained and applied in sequence. (1) Teacher selector: one binary MLP head per model trained on correctness labels using BCE loss plus a pairwise ranking term (softplus of wrong-model logit minus right-model logit) to encourage correct models to outscore incorrect ones. The teacher uses full runtime state during training. (2) Margin gate: a two-layer MLP (hidden 256, dropout 0.1) operating on frozen all-MiniLM-L6-v2 embeddings, conditioned on log λ via a periodic FiLM embedding (sinusoidal frequencies {0.5, 1, 2, 4}). Trained with a combined loss: sign loss (BCE between gate output and teacher's local-accept label), margin loss (Huber regression toward continuous teacher margin), and monotonicity loss (penalizes predicted margin inversions across sorted λ values). Temperature scaling decouples the distillation scale from the threshold. (3) CRC calibration: for each fixed λ, a held-out calibration set identifies the most conservative threshold τ*(λ; α) such that the empirical marginal false-acceptance rate satisfies the CRC correction ≤ α, providing a distribution-free pointwise guarantee under exchangeability. At inference, the UE computes one frozen forward pass and one small MLP evaluation, then looks up the precomputed threshold table. Design decisions: (a) conditioning the gate on λ via FiLM rather than training separate gates per λ is justified by parameter efficiency but assumes smooth variation across λ; (b) the monotonicity regularizer is well-motivated by the profiled-regime assumption that local model cost is non-decreasing in λ relative to edge models; (c) CRC calibration is applied per-λ rather than over the entire λ sweep — the authors acknowledge this means the guarantee is pointwise, not simultaneous. Key Results: Evaluated on a routing dataset constructed from MMLU, BBH, GPQA, and MBPP with per-model correctness labels from lm-evaluation-harness, using Qwen3-1.7B on a Jetson AGX Orin (UE) and Qwen3-{4B, 8B, 14B} on RTX 4070 Ti / RTX 4090 / A40 (edge). Baselines: static selectors (Always-{1.7B, 4B, 8B, 14B}), query-level routers (MLP, KNN, EmbedLLM, LLMRank). At fixed accuracy targets, CR² reduces normalized deployment cost vs. KNN by 4.8–5.6% at 79–80% accuracy and 16.2–16.9% at 81–82% accuracy. At cost targets c̄ ∈ {0.35, 0.45, 0.55}, CR² achieves the highest pooled accuracy in all cases (0.760, 0.777, 0.804 at the three targets). CRC empirically holds marginal false-acceptance risk within specified α across tested λ values. The deployable device-edge gate tracks the full-information edge-only reference within ~2% accuracy across most cost ranges. Gains are not uniform: MLP outperforms CR² on GPQA at c̄ = 0.35. The device-edge gate uses 104,706 parameters and 207.1k FLOPs at inference (excluding shared encoder), ~3.8× fewer than the full CR²(edge) reference. Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION + NEW METHOD. The two-stage deployment-constrained routing formulation with asymmetric UE/edge information structure, state-dependent wireless costs, and CRC-calibrated risk control is genuinely novel in the LLM routing literature. The individual components — CRC calibration (from Angelopoulos et al. ICLR 2024), FiLM conditioning, knowledge distillation, query-level routing — are all established; the novelty lies in their principled integration under the mobile-edge deployment constraint and the formal analysis of the factorized policy family. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations cover loss composition (sign only → + margin → + monotonicity), monotonicity weight w_mono, CRC calibration level α, defer-path semantics (inclusive vs. edge-only), and FiLM cost weight J. Multiple CRC levels (α ∈ {0.002, 0.005, 0.010, 0.020, 0.050}) are reported. No error bars or significance tests are reported for routing accuracy metrics. Code and model weights are not mentioned as publicly released. The wireless runtime state ξ is sampled from a parameterized channel model (Rayleigh fading, UE-edge distance uniform on [30, 150] m) rather than measured in a real deployment — the routing performance is evaluated on a fixed test split of benchmark QA data, not on queries from actual wireless devices. The CRC guarantee requires exchangeability between calibration and test query-state distributions; whether real wireless deployments satisfy this is not discussed. Baseline methods (EmbedLLM ICLR 2025, LLMRank arXiv 2025) are current. Deployability (EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS profile): - Practical: The deployed UE gate requires only a frozen sentence encoder and a two-layer MLP with a precomputed threshold table, running on a Jetson AGX Orin — commercially available embedded hardware. - Efficient: The device-edge gate requires 207.1k FLOPs at inference (excluding shared encoder), substantially below KNN (13.8M FLOPs per query via cosine search over 18k training embeddings) and comparable to LLMRank (789.5k FLOPs). - Effective: The evidence shows improvement in the accuracy-cost Pareto frontier on benchmark routing tasks with simulated wireless costs; real deployment gains are not measured, and per-benchmark results show the improvement is not universal (MLP leads on GPQA at low cost). - Scalable: The model pool is four Qwen3 variants; behavior with larger or more heterogeneous pools, or with a larger calibration set drawn from a different query distribution, is not evaluated. The paper does not test at 10× the pool size or query volume. The system delivers a trained gate and threshold table as deployable artifacts, not a software library. Strengths: - The deployment-constrained factorized policy formulation cleanly separates the UE information constraint from the edge selection problem, and the formal analysis of the three design challenges (partial information, asymmetric error modes, state-dependent deferral) provides a principled motivation for each architectural choice. - CRC calibration provides a distribution-free, finite-sample guarantee on marginal false-acceptance risk — a concrete, verifiable safety property that no prior LLM router provides, and the empirical results confirm it holds across tested λ and α combinations. - The FiLM-conditioned margin gate allows a single model to trace the full accuracy-cost Pareto frontier without retraining per cost weight, which is operationally important for deployed systems. - The gate-error decomposition (Fig. 9) distinguishes false deferrals from false local acceptances and shows the dominant error mode transitions with cost level, providing actionable diagnostic insight. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The CRC guarantee is pointwise for a fixed λ and does not provide a simultaneous guarantee over the entire λ sweep. The calibration exchangeability assumption requires the test query-state distribution to match the calibration distribution; recalibration is needed if either changes. Accuracy gains are not uniform across benchmarks. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The wireless cost model is entirely simulated — latency, energy, and channel gains are drawn from a parameterized model, not measured on actual hardware with real wireless links, so the deployment cost advantage attributed to CR² over baselines reflects model assumptions rather than measured system behavior. The model pool of four Qwen3 variants is narrow; with a larger or more diverse pool (different architectures, quantization levels), the margin gate's distillation quality and the Pareto gains may change substantially. The CRC exchangeability assumption — that calibration query-state pairs are exchangeable with future test pairs — is non-trivial in a wireless setting where channel conditions are non-stationary; the paper does not analyze sensitivity to distribution shift. No comparison is made to adaptive compute within the local model (early exit, speculative decoding with the local model as draft) as an alternative to deferral. The 16.9% cost reduction headline figure applies at a specific (81–82%) accuracy target against KNN; average gains over the full Pareto curve are smaller and not reported as a single summary statistic. Bottom Line: CR² makes a clean and well-motivated contribution to the underexplored problem of deployment-constrained LLM routing in wireless device-edge settings; practitioners designing mobile-edge inference systems will find the formulation, risk-control calibration, and gate architecture directly useful, though the gap between simulated wireless conditions and real deployment warrants caution before expecting the reported cost reductions to transfer.
Search Results 2605.12001 PDF
#103 — Innovative (62)

STAGE: Tackling Semantic Drift in Multimodal Federated Graph Learning

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces STAGE, a protocol-first framework for multimodal federated graph learning that resolves cross-client semantic incompatibility before parameter aggregation by mapping heterogeneous multimodal features into a shared calibrated discrete anchor space and adaptively controlling GNN propagation intensity. - Why it's important: In federated settings where clients use different modality encoders (e.g., text vs. image), representations of the same concept are semantically incomparable before collaboration begins, which makes standard federated averaging and even prototype-exchange methods structurally incapable of recovering true semantic alignment. - How it does it: Node features are projected onto a frozen orthogonal anchor bank via KL-regularized softmax (producing per-node probability distributions), server-maintained Global Anchor Prototypes calibrate local anchor semantics across clients via InfoNCE, and a bilevel-trained meta-controller uses a 2-scalar structural-semantic conflict sketch per client to set propagation temperature in local GNNs. - The good: Full ablation across all 8 datasets with 10+ baselines confirms each component contributes meaningfully, and a 122× per-round communication reduction over FedAvg while improving accuracy is a concrete and substantial advantage. - The missing: Scalability is evaluated only up to 7 clients, far below realistic FL deployments; the "generalization and contraction guarantees" cited in the conclusion are not presented in the paper as submitted. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers actively working on federated graph learning or multimodal feature alignment; practitioners should wait for peer review and code release before adopting.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (63) is the strongest dimension — thorough four-component ablations across all 8 datasets, 10+ baselines, mean±std over 10 runs, and an (λ,β) sensitivity heatmap — but is held back by preprint status, no public code, and a group-internal benchmark (mild circularity). Novelty (71) and Non-Obviousness (70) are solid: the semantic-drift → pseudo-alignment → propagation-drift taxonomy and protocol-first ordering are a genuine NEW PROBLEM framing. Completeness (53) and Resilience (55) pull the composite down — there is no explicit limitations section, the 'generalization and contraction guarantees' cited in the conclusion are simply absent from the paper, and scalability is untested beyond K≤7 clients, which a tough reviewer would attack directly. Impact (50) reflects a real 122× communication win but no released artifact. Weighted: 0.20*71 + 0.25*63 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*53 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*50 = 62.
empirical Novelty 71 Rigor 63 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 53 Resilience 55 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Federated graph learning (FGL) distributes graph data across clients that train locally and aggregate via parameter averaging. When different clients encode different modalities — one using image encoders, another using text encoders for the same entities — their feature spaces are not structurally comparable. The same concept (e.g., "running shoe") occupies well-separated regions in a shared diagnostic space, a failure the paper terms semantic drift. Existing remedies are insufficient: classical FedAvg-style methods assume a common parameter space; prototype and logit-exchange methods still assume representations are roughly comparable before exchange; centralized multimodal graph methods assume a single learner with full data access. The paper argues this problem has a cascade structure: semantic drift enables pseudo-alignment (a naively shared anchor bank is colonized by inconsistent local semantics), which in turn enables propagation-induced drift (graph message passing amplifies residual inconsistency across neighborhoods, with mean pairwise centroid distance growing 29.8% after two GNN layers in the Toys dataset). No prior FGL method simultaneously addresses encoder-agnosticism, graph-topology awareness, and decentralized execution. Key Idea The core insight is that cross-client semantic alignment must happen in a modality-agnostic discrete distribution space rather than in a continuous parameter space. By projecting each node's multimodal feature onto a probability simplex over a frozen orthogonal anchor bank, STAGE converts incompatible continuous representations into comparable anchor-assignment distributions — a space where KL divergence and contrastive objectives are well-defined regardless of the originating modality. A secondary insight is that graph propagation itself is a drift amplifier, not a corrector, so regulated propagation must be layered on top of semantic calibration rather than treated as a substitute. Methodology STAGE proceeds in three ordered protocol stages. (1) Initialization: A globally shared anchor bank B ∈ R^{M×dp} is constructed once with orthogonal initialization and held frozen throughout training, preventing the reference space from drifting during aggregation. Each client uses modality-specific frozen backbones ψ_k^(c) and a lightweight trainable projector ϕ_k mapping fused features to the protocol space R^{dp}. (2) Semantic Calibration: Node representations are mapped to anchor distributions q_v via a KL-regularized projection onto the probability simplex (Eq. 4–5), implemented as softmax over cosine similarities to anchors with temperature τ_s. A max-entropy regularizer L_ent^(k) on the average client-wide anchor distribution prevents mode collapse by encouraging broad anchor utilization. To correct pseudo-alignment, each client computes per-anchor conditional means μ_{k,i}, which the server aggregates via EMA into Global Anchor Prototypes H̄. Clients align local anchor semantics to H̄ via InfoNCE (Eq. 8). Key design decisions: (a) freezing the bank avoids drift but risks the orthogonal basis not covering real semantic geometry — justified by the entropy regularization; (b) EMA aggregation of anchor prototypes is computationally cheap but introduces lag, which is not ablated; (c) InfoNCE temperature τ_c is a hyperparameter whose sensitivity is not reported. (3) Adaptive Regulation: Each client computes node-level Jensen-Shannon divergence between its semantic fingerprint q_v and the neighborhood aggregate q̃_v, summarized into a 2-scalar sketch D_k = [μ(d_v), σ(d_v)]. A server-side neural meta-controller π_θ maps D_k to a propagation temperature τ_k, which scales GNN attention weights (Eq. 10). The controller is optimized via implicit bilevel: each client uploads a scalar meta-gradient g_k = ∇_{τ_k} L_val^(k), and the server updates π by chain rule (Eq. 11). The total loss combines task cross-entropy, InfoNCE calibration, and entropy regularization with weights λ and β. Key Results STAGE is evaluated on 8 multimodal-attributed graph datasets (Toys, Grocery, Bili Music, DY, QB, Bili Cartoon, Flickr30k, SemArt) across 5 tasks: node classification (accuracy), link prediction (AUC), modal retrieval (R@5), G2Text (ROUGE-L), G2Image (CLIP-S). Client partitions use Louvain for most tasks and METIS for link prediction. Against 10 baselines spanning FL, multimodal GNNs, MM-FL, FGL-drift, FGL-heterogeneity, and prototype methods, STAGE achieves the best result on all 8 datasets for all reported tasks. Headline numbers: node classification 80.30% (Toys) vs. next-best FedIIH 79.19%; link prediction AUC 68.93% (Bili Music) vs. next-best Fed-MHGAT 67.46%; modal retrieval R@5 91.46% (QB) vs. S2FGL 91.23%. Margins over advanced FGL baselines are modest (0.23%–3.60%), while margins over uncalibrated multimodal FL reach up to 4.62%. Communication payload is 8,192 scalars per round vs. 1.0×10^6 for FedAvg (122× reduction) and memory footprint is 6.2 MB vs. 44.9 MB for FedAvg. STAGE's compute cost (7.3×10^8 FLOPs, 0.42s) is higher than FedAvg (0.089s) but substantially lower than FedSPA (1.83s). Results are averaged over 10 runs with standard deviations reported. Scalability is tested at K ∈ {3, 5, 7} clients, with mild degradation from 5→7 clients (accuracy drops 2.4% on Grocery node classification). Robustness experiments vary feature drift intensity α from 0 to 1 and modality corruption ratios from 2% to 30%, showing STAGE degrades more slowly than baselines in both scenarios. Novelty Classification NEW PROBLEM (articulates the semantic-drift failure hierarchy in MM-FGL with empirical measurement tools — centroid drift, anchor purity — that are operationally useful) + NEW METHOD (the specific combination of frozen-bank variational projection, InfoNCE-calibrated GAP, max-entropy regularization, and sketch-based bilevel meta-controller is new). The individual components — frozen codebooks, InfoNCE, entropy regularization, bilevel meta-learning for FL — are well-established; the novel contribution is recognizing that a protocol-first ordering of these components is necessary and designing the interaction between them specifically for the MM-FGL failure cascade. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough: all four components (frozen bank, contrastive GAP, meta-controller, max-entropy projection) are ablated individually across all 8 datasets (Table III), and each removal causes measurable degradation. Results are reported as mean ± std over 10 independent runs, which is adequate. Hyperparameter sensitivity is evaluated jointly over (λ, β) via a heatmap showing a broad stable region around λ ∈ [0.16, 0.30]. The paper mentions "theoretical analysis" providing "a generalization guarantee for semantic calibration and a contraction guarantee for regulated propagation" in the conclusion, but no such analysis appears in the submitted paper — this is a significant gap. No code or model weights are publicly released (not mentioned). The benchmark MM-FGL setting is defined by the companion benchmark paper arXiv:2601.22416 (also a preprint from the same group), which creates a mild circularity risk: the problem framing, datasets, and some baselines are defined by papers from the same research group. Several strong baselines (FedLap arXiv:2510.25657, S2FGL arXiv:2507.02409) are themselves recent preprints, which limits confidence in the comparison. This is an arXiv preprint (v1, May 12, 2026), not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Frozen backbones and lightweight projectors run on standard GPUs; the meta-controller is a small MLP; all operations are available in standard deep learning frameworks. - Efficient: Per-round communication is 8,192 scalars (versus up to 10^6 for parameter-sharing methods), and memory is 6.2 MB per client — both favorable; local computation adds ~0.33s per round over FedAvg, acceptable in practice. - Effective: Improvements over baselines on all 8 datasets across all 5 tasks are consistent, but margins over the best advanced FGL baselines are small (0.23%–1.47%) on individual datasets; the 4.62% headline gain is against weaker multimodal baselines, not the strongest FGL ones. - Scalable: Only tested at K ≤ 7 clients on graphs of unstated node count; real FL deployments involve hundreds to thousands of clients. The 2-scalar sketch per client scales well in communication, but whether anchor purity and GAP calibration remain effective with many more clients is untested and unaddressed. The work delivers a paper-form framework with no software artifact announced. Strengths - The three-stage failure taxonomy (semantic drift → pseudo-alignment → propagation-induced drift) is clearly motivated by empirical measurements (centroid distance, anchor purity heatmaps, Dirichlet energy analysis) before the method is introduced, making the design rationale unusually traceable. - The communication reduction (122× vs. FedAvg) achieved simultaneously with accuracy improvements is a genuine practical advantage: the low-dimensional protocol (D_k = 2 scalars + g_k = 1 scalar per client per round) is a real engineering contribution. - Ablation across 8 datasets with 4 component removals provides stronger evidence of contribution decomposition than is typical in this subfield. - The structural-semantic conflict sketch using JS divergence between node and neighborhood distributions is a compact and privacy-preserving summary that avoids transmitting raw topology. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The paper does not include an explicit limitations section. The conclusion acknowledges only that the work "hopes to encourage future research on protocol-level designs," which is a non-limitation. The scalability experiments (K ≤ 7) are presented as an evaluation rather than flagged as a limitation. No failure modes, no discussion of settings where STAGE might underperform, no mention of the missing theoretical analysis. The absence of explicit acknowledged limitations is a credibility concern. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The "generalization and contraction guarantees" referenced in the conclusion are absent from the paper — either omitted during submission or overstated in the conclusion. - The MM-FGL benchmark (datasets, protocol, several baselines) originates from the same research group; independent replication on independently defined benchmarks is needed. - K ≤ 7 clients is far below realistic FL scales; the claim of scalability is not supported by evidence beyond this narrow range. - The frozen anchor bank size M = 128 and its interaction with the number of semantic classes across datasets (up to 18 classes per dataset) is not ablated; dead anchors or anchor collision at larger class counts could break the calibration. - The meta-controller is described as a neural network but its architecture, capacity, and convergence behavior are not specified, making reproduction unclear. - No comparison to centralized upper-bound performance is provided, so the absolute gap due to federation is unknown. - The 2-modality setting (text + image) is the only evaluated configuration; whether the framework extends to >2 modalities or to missing-modality regimes is claimed but not shown. Bottom Line A practitioner working specifically on multimodal federated graph learning gains a well-motivated design framework and a reproducible empirical baseline, but should treat the results with appropriate skepticism given the preprint status, the group-internal benchmark, the missing theoretical analysis, and the absence of a large-scale (K >> 7) evaluation.
Search Results 2605.11919 PDF
#104 — Innovative (62)

Solvita: Enhancing Large Language Models for Competitive Programming via Agentic Evolution (15 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 18 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Solvita, a four-agent (Planner, Solver, Oracle, Hacker) framework for competitive programming that pairs each agent with a graph-structured, REINFORCE-trained knowledge network, enabling experience accumulation across problems without modifying the frozen LLM backbone. - Why it's important: LLM-based code agents are stateless — they discard all experience after each problem — so performance never improves with use even though algorithmic strategies are highly transferable, forcing every new problem to be solved from scratch. - How it does it: Outcome signals (pass/fail verdicts, test-certification ratios, adversarial break events) are recast as REINFORCE edge-weight updates on role-specific graphs, turning passive semantic retrieval into a learned routing policy that expands precisely where each agent currently struggles. - The good: With GPT-5.4 on CodeContests (165 problems), Solvita achieves 82.4% pass@1 versus 40.0% for single-pass and 75.8% for the full multi-agent scaffold without network training, while consuming roughly 151K tokens per problem — comparable to open-source frameworks and well below commercial CLI agents (~200–210K). - The missing: All five "frontier" backbones (GPT-5.4, Claude Opus 4.6, Qwen3.6, DeepSeek V4 Pro, Grok-4) are proprietary models accessed via API; no error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported in any main table; and reference [12] for "GPT-5.4" cites the GPT-4 technical report, a citation mismatch that undermines model traceability. - Read it or not: Researchers building stateful LLM agents or competitive programming systems should read it — the contrastive REINFORCE formulation and Oracle-Hacker complementarity are genuinely useful design patterns — but immediate reproduction is blocked by proprietary backbone dependencies.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Novelty (72) and Non-Obviousness (70) are good — the four-agent loop with role-aligned REINFORCE-trained graph knowledge networks, the contrastive counterfactual baseline (ΔR), and the Oracle-Hacker complementarity are genuine design patterns, and the live post-cutoff Codeforces evaluation adds external validity. Rigor (55) and Resilience (55) are what hold it back: no error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests appear in any table, the headline best-in-class claim rests on a 0.6-point margin over Codex CLI on only 165 problems, the GPT-5.4 citation points to the GPT-4 report (making the backbone unverifiable), and potential Codeforces train/test contamination is unanalyzed. Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*62 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*58 = 62.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 62 Resilience 55 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Competitive programming demands precise problem formalization, deliberate algorithmic strategy selection, iterative implementation repair, and adversarial test design — a structured reasoning chain where past experience should transfer readily. Existing multi-step pipelines such as AlphaCodium and MapCoder decompose the problem into phases but remain stateless: every problem starts from scratch and accumulated failure experience is discarded. RAG-based variants add retrieval but treat memory as a static semantic similarity lookup, which does not alter the underlying reasoning procedure. The result is flat performance across a problem stream, in contrast to human competitive programmers who improve by internalizing strategy patterns and recognizing failure modes. Key Idea The core insight is to replace flat document-retrieval memory with role-specific graph-structured knowledge networks whose edge weights are updated by REINFORCE using counterfactual rewards (ΔR = R_with_network − R_without_network). This turns memory from passive lookup into a learned routing policy: the graph expands where the agent fails and contracts where strategies prove unreliable, all without touching the frozen LLM backbone weights. Methodology Solvita couples four agents in a closed loop. The Planner formalizes the problem into a canonical specification and predicts algorithmic tags; its knowledge network is trained with tag-prediction reward (+1 per correct tag, −1 per wrong). The Solver implements the strategy as C++ and repairs failures via SEARCH/REPLACE patch blocks rather than full regeneration; it is backed by a three-layer heterogeneous graph (Q nodes = problem descriptions, M nodes = solution decompositions as function-block DAGs including contrastive pairs of correct/incorrect solutions, S nodes = annotated algorithmic skills with C++ templates), with edge weights updated by contrastive REINFORCE (Eq. 2). The Oracle constructs certified internal tests via testlib — generating input, validating, running a reference solution, accepting only when certification ratio ρ ≥ τ (default τ = 0.9) — backed by a strategy-family bandit (top-down DP, enumeration, brute-force) trained on certification quality reward (Eq. 3, 6). The Hacker mounts adversarial attacks (semantic corner-case construction, stress testing at maximum bounds, anti-hash collision generation) to find bugs surviving Oracle certification; successful breaks propagate to all four knowledge networks via a shared event bus (Eq. 4). All four agents share a contextual-bandit policy (contextual features: FSM position, previous failure type, problem tags; learning rate α = 0.01). Cold-start corpus: 8,017 problems from Codeforces, AtCoder, Aizu, and others after four-stage filtering (completeness, tag load balancing at C_max = 2,300, cosine-similarity deduplication at δ = 0.93, difficulty pruning at the 5th-percentile floor per tag). Critical design decisions: (1) patch-based repair is well-justified by preventing regression on previously passing tests; (2) contrastive REINFORCE baseline isolates the network's marginal contribution and dominates vanilla REINFORCE in ablations; (3) role-specific reward structures are necessary because tag-prediction, pass-rate, certification-ratio, and break-rate signals are incommensurable. Key Results On CodeContests (165 problems), APPS (1,000), and AetherCode (400), Solvita with GPT-5.4 achieves 82.4% / 67.7% / 49.3% pass@1 versus single-pass 40.0% / 37.9% / 18.0%. Solvita wins 14 of 15 backbone–benchmark cells; the single exception is AetherCode under Claude Opus 4.6, where Claude Code edges it 54.25% vs. 53.75%. The ablation in Table 2 shows that the multi-agent scaffold without any trained network already reaches 67.7% on CC (GPT-5.4), confirming the loop design contributes the majority of gains; trained networks add a further monotonic improvement through 5,318-problem training. The Solver knowledge network is the largest single-component contributor at each checkpoint. Patch repair achieves 82.4% solve rate at 3.74 mean iterations versus full regeneration's 75.8% at 5.18 iterations (GPT-5.4, CC), with token savings of 91.2% vs. 67.4% against a common reference cost. Combined Oracle+Hacker detection of wrong solutions is 92.8% (Claude Opus 4.6) at 96.0% preservation of correct solutions. On 12 post-training-cutoff Codeforces rounds (Div. 2 and 1+2), all three Solvita variants reach simulated Legendary Grandmaster (≥3000) while bare backbones plateau at 2700–2850. Baselines are current frontier systems (Codex CLI, Claude Code, AlphaCodium, MapCoder, AgentCoder); on CodeContests with GPT-5.4, Codex CLI achieves 81.8% — within 0.6 points of Solvita. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The four-agent closed-loop architecture with role-aligned REINFORCE-trained graph knowledge networks and cross-agent failure-event propagation is a genuine architectural contribution. Individual components (graph memory, contextual bandits, REINFORCE for prompt optimization, adversarial testing) are established, but their synthesis in a role-decomposed pipeline where Hacker break events update all four networks simultaneously — and where the counterfactual baseline cleanly attributes improvement to the network rather than the backbone — is new. The Oracle's certified test generation integrated as a supervision source rather than an external oracle is also non-standard. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough: Table 2 decomposes each network at three training checkpoints; Table 3 compares patch repair against full regeneration under matched budgets; Figure 6 isolates Oracle/Hacker contributions. However, no error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests appear in any main table, and the primary CodeContests benchmark has only 165 problems — a 1–2 percentage point difference could be within sampling noise. Multiple random seeds are not reported. Code is claimed to be released ("authentication details are released with the public code") but no GitHub URL appears in the preprint body. Reference [12], cited for the backbone "GPT-5.4," points to the GPT-4 technical report (arXiv:2303.08774, 2023), creating a citation mismatch that makes the backbone unverifiable. AetherCode [11] lists "Oracle-generated test suites" as part of its benchmark construction — if AetherCode's Oracle paradigm is methodologically aligned with Solvita's Oracle, the evaluation may not be fully independent. The cold-start corpus draws from Codeforces, which is also the source of the CodeContests test problems — potential knowledge-network-level data leakage is not analyzed. This is a preprint; peer-reviewed confidence is not warranted. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: The system requires concurrent API access to proprietary LLM endpoints plus testlib compilation and sandboxed C++ execution; deploying against open-source or locally served models (Qwen3.6 is locally served in the paper) is feasible, but the GPT-5.4 and Claude Opus 4.6 runs require vendor API access. - Efficient: Solvita consumes ~151K tokens/problem (GPT-5.4), within the range of open-source frameworks (~149–165K) and 25–30% below commercial CLI agents (~200–210K); however, cold-start training requires processing ~5,000 problems at full pipeline cost before amortization. - Effective: The multi-agent scaffold alone closes the majority of the gap over single-pass; trained networks add further monotonic improvement, and the live Codeforces evaluation provides external validity beyond static benchmarks. - Scalable: The knowledge graph grows dynamically (new Q/M/S nodes added on failure) with automatic deprecation of low-reward items; the paper trains on 5,318 problems but does not evaluate performance beyond that horizon or measure latency/throughput at scale. No stress-scale experiment is reported. The work delivers a software artifact (claimed release), not just a paper-form description. Strengths - The contrastive REINFORCE formulation (ΔR = R_with − R_without) is a clean counterfactual baseline that isolates the network's marginal contribution; the ablation in Table 2 confirms it is the dominant factor over naive absolute-reward REINFORCE. - Patch-based repair is rigorously compared against full regeneration under matched iteration budgets and a shared reference cost, with the mechanism (preventing regression on previously passing tests) directly identified — not just observed. - The Oracle-Hacker complementarity is non-trivial and convincingly shown: Oracle and Hacker cover different bug classes (certification vs. adversarial), and combining them yields 92.8% detection at 96.0% preservation — a genuine systems insight. - The live Codeforces evaluation across 12 post-training-cutoff contests is a methodologically strong addition; the rate-insertion protocol and Elo inversion are documented in detail (Appendix F.5) and substantially stronger external validation than offline benchmarks alone. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly name three trade-offs: (1) cold-start cost — the agentic loop is more expensive per problem than single-pass and requires ~5,000 training problems for cost amortization; (2) Hacker scope — anti-hash and lattice-based attacks are bounded by the backbone's math reasoning, leaving number-theoretic and geometric failure modes under-covered; (3) patch-repair drift — on globally flawed candidates the Solver can accumulate inconsistent edits before the iteration budget exhausts. These are confronted directly and honestly, a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All five frontier backbones are proprietary at time of preprint submission; the citation for GPT-5.4 points to the GPT-4 technical report, making the exact model version and capability unverifiable by external reviewers. - The Codeforces Legendary Grandmaster claim is a simulated standings insertion, not an actual account history — the inversion methodology is sound but excludes platform-side Elo dynamics and participation eligibility. - Cold-start corpus drawn from Codeforces may overlap with CodeContests test problems (also from Codeforces), creating potential knowledge-network-level contamination that is not analyzed. - On the most-controlled comparison (GPT-5.4, CodeContests), Codex CLI achieves 81.8% versus Solvita's 82.4% — a 0.6-point margin on 165 problems without significance testing; the claim of best-in-class rests partly on a statistically unconfirmed difference. - The comparison positions Codex CLI and Claude Code on non-home backbones as evidence that commercial tools "lose ground when moved to other models" — but this is expected behavior for tools designed for specific model APIs, not a meaningful deficit. Bottom Line A practitioner building stateful LLM agents for code synthesis or competitive programming gains real value from the contrastive REINFORCE design and Oracle-Hacker decomposition, but the evaluation's reliance on unreleased proprietary backbones and the absence of statistical rigor mean independent reproduction and confidence in the margin claims must wait for either a peer-reviewed version or open model experiments.
Recommended 2605.15301 PDF
#105 — Innovative (62)

Towards Efficient and Effective Diffusion Language Model Inference via Semantic-Aware Adaptive Denoising

wzgcs.github.io · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Ada-DLM, a training-free inference acceleration framework for diffusion language models that replaces static scalar confidence thresholds with a 3-step temporal clustering criterion to identify semantically converged tokens and skip their FFN computation. - Why it's important: Diffusion LLM inference requires expensive full-sequence FFN computation at every denoising step for all tokens, and existing threshold-based methods miss up to 43.75% of semantically stable tokens due to a fundamental mismatch between numerical confidence and semantic convergence, capping achievable speedup. - How it does it: Each token's confidence scores over 3 consecutive denoising steps are stacked into a 3D evolution-aware feature vector; a single-pass nearest-centroid classifier using rank-initialized centroids then freezes tokens whose trajectories cluster geometrically with the high-confidence distribution mode rather than checking absolute scalar magnitude. - The good: On long-context LongBench tasks with UltraLLaDA, Ada-DLM achieves 41.3× average speedup over vanilla while maintaining ROUGE-L parity, reducing per-sample latency from 341.55s to 8.26s — a result that demonstrates practical deployment viability where the vanilla baseline is simply unusable. - The missing: All throughput experiments use batch size 1, which simulates streaming inference but does not reflect multi-query serving; additionally, dLLM-Cache (a concurrent baseline shown to match Ada-DLM on MLLM tasks) is absent from the main short-context and long-context comparison tables without explanation. - Read it or not: Researchers and practitioners actively evaluating diffusion LLMs for inference efficiency should read it; those working primarily with autoregressive models can skip unless interested in the convergence dynamics analysis, which is independently useful.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Practical Impact (68) and Non-Obviousness (70) carry the score: the trajectory-geometry-over-scalar-magnitude reformulation is a non-obvious insight backed by an independently useful failure-mode analysis (Statistical Stagnation / post-peak decline), and the 41× long-context speedup addresses a regime where vanilla DLM inference is genuinely unusable. The composite is held back by Rigor (56) and Resilience (55): dLLM-Cache is benchmarked only in the MLLM appendix and conspicuously omitted from the main text tables where it might be competitive, all throughput is batch-size-1, multi-seed statistics cover only GSM8K, and the post-peak-decline mechanism claim is confounded with simply freezing tokens earlier. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*56 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*68 ≈ 62.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 56 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Diffusion language models (DLMs such as LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B) generate text by iteratively denoising all L output tokens in parallel across T steps. Unlike autoregressive generation where computation grows incrementally, each DLM step requires a full-sequence Transformer pass, with FFN operations dominating (~2/3 of FLOPs, scaling as O(L·D²)). Existing acceleration works (dKV-Cache, Fast-dLLM) attempt to skip computation for converged tokens by checking whether current confidence exceeds a fixed threshold τ (typically 0.9). This approach has two failure modes: (1) "Statistical Stagnation" — tokens that have locked onto the correct prediction for many steps but whose confidence sits below τ (e.g., plateauing at 0.7–0.85) are forced to continue expensive FFN execution that yields zero semantic gain, accounting for 15–20% of all active computation; and (2) "Post-Peak Confidence Decline" — tokens that transiently peak near τ but are not frozen at the optimal moment subsequently suffer confidence collapse, causing the model to select suboptimal tokens as final outputs. The gap prior methods leave open is the inability to detect semantic stability from the trajectory of scalar confidence rather than its instantaneous magnitude. Key Idea The core insight is that token convergence should be determined by the geometric position of a token's multi-step confidence trajectory relative to the distribution of all tokens' trajectories, not by whether its current scalar confidence exceeds an absolute threshold. A token that has been consistently near the high-confidence mode of the distribution — even at numerically unsaturated values — has semantically stabilized, and further FFN computation on it yields no meaningful semantic shift. This motivates encoding each token as a 3D vector of recent confidence scores and clustering tokens against data-driven centroids that adapt to the shifting confidence distribution across denoising steps. Methodology Ada-DLM operates as a post-hoc decision module inserted at each denoising step. The algorithm proceeds in three stages per step: Stage 1 — Rank-Aware Centroid Initialization: All currently active tokens are sorted by their confidence C_{t,i} in descending order. The top-ranked token x_{ρ(1)} is designated as the prototype for the high-confidence centroid μ_high; the token at rank K+1 (where K ≈ 15% of sequence length, tuned as the Pareto-optimal hyperparameter) is designated as the boundary centroid μ_bound. This reduces K-means initialization from random to deterministic O(L log L), eliminating iterative convergence overhead. Stage 2 — Semantics-Aware Adaptive Clustering: Each token is represented as v_{t,i} = [C_{t,i}, C_{t-1,i}, C_{t-2,i}]^T (Eq. 8), a 3-step confidence trajectory vector. The centroids are expanded into this 3D space by tracking their respective history. A token is frozen if D(i) = ||v_{t,i} − μ_high||² / ||v_{t,i} − μ_bound||² < 1, i.e., its trajectory is geometrically closer to the stable high-confidence mode. Frozen tokens are removed from all subsequent steps. Crucially, the final frozen set size is decoupled from K — it expands or contracts based on the current step's distributional geometry. A theoretical decomposition (Eq. 10) shows this distance simultaneously penalizes magnitude gap from the target and temporal fluctuation (local variance), providing the theoretical basis for its superiority over scalar thresholds. Stage 3 — Gather-GEMM-Scatter Execution: Active tokens are gathered into a contiguous tensor; FFN computation runs on this dense batch only, exploiting Tensor Core efficiency; results are scattered back to full sequence positions. This avoids the memory fragmentation of naive boolean masking. Critical design decisions: the 3-step window m=3 is the minimum to filter transient "flashing" confidence noise (Table 4 shows accuracy drops from 72.50 to 71.80 at m=1); K=15% is the Pareto optimal initialization capacity (Table 3); squared Euclidean distance outperforms cosine (64.20% accuracy) and Manhattan (70.34%) at 72.50% (Table 5), as it penalizes both magnitude gap and temporal fluctuation simultaneously. Key Results Short-context benchmarks (Table 1), NVIDIA A800, batch size 1: - Dream-7B: Ada-DLM achieves 97.7 TPS vs. Fast-dLLM's 47.9 TPS; average speedup 3.87× vs. 1.87×; GSM8K accuracy 73.5% vs. vanilla 75.4%; HumanEval Pass@1 14.9 vs. vanilla 14.7. - LLaDA-8B: 131.1 TPS vs. Fast-dLLM's 94.4 TPS; average speedup 4.65× vs. 2.71×; GSM8K 72.5% vs. vanilla 72.6%. Long-context LongBench (Table 2), UltraLLaDA backbone, L ≈ 4K–32K: - Ada-DLM: 41.34× average speedup vs. Fast-dLLM 26.65×; SAMSum 73.12× vs. Fast-dLLM 46.30×. - Absolute latency: vanilla 341.55 s/sample reduced to 8.26 s/sample. MLLM extension (Table 8): LLaDA-V 4.42× (vs. dLLM-Cache 4.07×), Dimple-7B 5.44× (vs. dLLM-Cache 4.74×), MMAda-8B 5.40× (vs. dLLM-Cache 4.99×). Robustness: 5-seed evaluation on GSM8K yields 72.50 ± 0.18, confirming stability against variance. The primary baselines (dKV-Cache, Fast-dLLM) are the current published DLM acceleration works. dLLM-Cache, a concurrent baseline that outperforms Fast-dLLM, appears only in the MLLM appendix and is absent from the main text tables — a notable omission. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuinely novel contributions are: (1) the first systematic empirical characterization of Statistical Stagnation and post-peak confidence decline as structural failure modes of scalar-threshold DLM acceleration, with quantitative analysis and theoretical decomposition; and (2) the reformulation of token convergence detection as a non-iterative 3D temporal clustering problem with rank-aware centroid initialization. The Gather-GEMM-Scatter execution and K-means clustering are known techniques applied in a new setting; the insight that trajectory geometry rather than scalar magnitude tracks semantic convergence is non-obvious and is the paper's primary intellectual contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present for all key hyperparameters: capacity K (Table 3), history window m (Table 4), and distance metric (Table 5). Multi-seed evaluation (5 seeds) is reported for GSM8K, yielding 72.50 ± 0.18, but multi-seed statistics are not reported for other benchmarks or baselines. No code or model weights are released; the paper describes a PyTorch 2.1 prototype. The 19% quality improvement claim in the abstract appears to be anchored in the CNN/DM ROUGE-L comparison on LLaDA-8B (16.88 → 21.82), not a universal finding across all tasks — the abstract framing is misleadingly broad. Methodological concern: dLLM-Cache is included in MLLM experiments where it approaches Ada-DLM's performance, yet is excluded from the main short- and long-context tables; this selective comparison is not explained. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs on standard NVIDIA A800 (80GB) GPUs with PyTorch 2.1 + CUDA 11.8; no specialized hardware required. - Efficient: Memory overhead is negligible (<0.02 MB for 7–8B models at 512-token contexts, +0.19 MB at 16K context); clustering decision overhead is <0.2% of total inference cost per profiling; the method eliminates FFN computation for a dynamically growing fraction of tokens without introducing additional training or model modification. - Effective: Achieves 3.87–4.65× speedup on 7–8B DLMs while maintaining accuracy within 1–2% of vanilla on standard benchmarks; the post-peak confidence decline prevention hypothesis is supported by CNN/DM improvements but not by a controlled ablation isolating this effect from the general "earlier token freezing" effect. - Scalable: Tested up to 16K context with UltraLLaDA achieving 41× speedup; quadratic attention complexity (O(L²)) remains unresolved and is acknowledged; batch size 1 throughout means scaling to multi-query serving conditions is untested. The work delivers a PyTorch prototype with documented hyperparameters, not a production library or CLI tool. Strengths - The convergence dynamics analysis (Statistical Stagnation, post-peak decline) is quantitatively grounded with trajectory visualizations, confidence density heatmaps, and a formal sum-of-squares decomposition (Eq. 10) — this analysis stands independently of the proposed method and is the paper's most durable contribution. - The rank-aware centroid initialization converts iterative K-means into a single deterministic sort, keeping the per-step decision overhead at O(L log L) and <0.2% of total cost — a clean and practically important engineering decision. - Long-context results (41.3× speedup with quality parity on LongBench) are substantially beyond prior work and address a practically critical regime where vanilla DLM inference is unusable. - The MLLM extension (Table 8) requires no architectural changes and demonstrates speedups of 4.4–5.4× on three multimodal backbones, suggesting the technique is not narrowly tuned to text-only settings. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge that: (1) Ada-DLM is a PyTorch prototype lacking custom CUDA kernel fusion (e.g., vLLM integration), so reported throughput is a conservative lower bound; (2) the quadratic attention complexity O(L²) is not addressed by this method, which targets only FFN redundancy; (3) future work is needed on fused CUDA kernels and integration with linear-attention backbones. These are confronted directly and credibly in the Limitations section. Analyst-Identified Gaps - dLLM-Cache is included in Table 8 (MLLMs) where it closely matches Ada-DLM (4.07–4.99× vs. 4.42–5.40×), yet is absent from the main short-context (Table 1) and long-context (Table 2) comparisons without explanation; this is the most significant gap in the evaluation. - All latency and throughput benchmarks use batch size 1; whether Gather-GEMM-Scatter retains efficiency at batch sizes 8–64 (standard for serving) is untested and unaddressed. - The 5-seed robustness test covers only GSM8K on LLaDA-8B; coding and summarization tasks, where output variance may be higher, have no multi-seed statistics for any method. - The claim that active clustering prevents post-peak decline is confounded with the baseline effect of simply freezing more tokens earlier — a controlled ablation comparing "freeze K% top-confidence tokens unconditionally" vs. Ada-DLM's adaptive clustering would isolate this claimed mechanism. - No comparison against reduced-step baselines (fewer denoising steps T via improved schedulers), which achieve throughput gains without per-step overhead and may reach similar latency on standard benchmarks. Bottom Line Practitioners deploying diffusion LLMs at long-context regimes gain a well-motivated, training-free acceleration technique with the strongest published throughput numbers in this space, but should independently verify performance against dLLM-Cache on text tasks and at realistic serving batch sizes before adopting it.
Search Results
#106 — Innovative (61)

Stabilizing LLM Supervised Fine-Tuning via Explicit Distributional Control

Xinyu Wang, Changzhi Sun, Yuanbin Wu, Xiaoling Wang · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Anchored Learning, a two-loop offline fine-tuning framework that constructs a dynamically evolving interpolated anchor distribution — a convex mixture of the current model and a frozen SFT reference — to constrain each optimization step's KL divergence and reduce catastrophic forgetting during supervised fine-tuning. - Why it's important: Catastrophic forgetting during SFT can collapse general capabilities by >50%, blocking safe domain adaptation of LLMs; existing fixes either require expensive on-policy RL or suffer persistent gradient conflicts from static regularization that cap final task performance. - How it does it: At each outer iteration the anchor is computed as (1-α)p_θ^(t) + α·p_sft in probability or logit space; the inner loop then distills the model toward this anchor, bounding per-iteration KL divergence proportionally to α and making the trust-region radius co-evolve with the optimization trajectory. - The good: On iGSM and MedCalc, standard SFT causes >53% general-capability degradation while Anchored Learning reduces this to <5% at near-identical domain accuracy (0.936 on iGSM vs. 1.000 for SFT), consistently placing on the Pareto frontier across six model-task configurations. - The missing: No LoRA/PEFT baseline is included despite being the dominant practical approach to catastrophic forgetting, and no error bars or multi-seed variance estimates are reported for any result. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML engineers and researchers working on domain-specific LLM post-training without on-policy RL infrastructure, as it provides a drop-in offline alternative with clean theoretical grounding; skip it if LoRA-based continual learning is your primary consideration.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (66) and Non-Obviousness (68) are real strengths — the dynamic moving-anchor interpolation that co-evolves with the optimization trajectory, backed by an information-geometric reading of logit-space interpolation and convergence proofs (Prop 5.4), is well-motivated and theoretically grounded, which also props up Resilience (60). The composite is capped by Impact (50): the paper releases only Algorithm 1 pseudocode — no code, weights, or library — and carries a 1.5–2× overhead. Completeness (57) suffers from the absent LoRA/PEFT baseline (the dominant practical anti-forgetting approach), missing 14B results despite being listed, no error bars, and an unexamined gap between the exact-projection theory and the K=5 inner loop.
empirical Novelty 66 Rigor 59 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 57 Resilience 60 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: When fine-tuning a pre-trained LLM on a new task via standard SFT, the model's general capabilities — mathematical reasoning, code generation, instruction following — can collapse dramatically: on iGSM, Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct's general benchmark average falls from 0.515 to 0.157 after SFT despite perfect domain accuracy. This catastrophic forgetting is driven by excessive distributional drift: the model's output distribution moves far from its pretrained state. On-policy RL implicitly constrains drift but requires costly data collection and complex pipelines. Static KL regularization toward a fixed reference creates persistent gradient conflicts — gradients from the task loss and the KL penalty point in conflicting directions — and converges to a fixed compromise distribution rather than the task optimum. Reducing the learning rate (Low-SFT) limits adaptation. The gap is the absence of an explicit, locally adaptive drift-control mechanism that works in a fully offline, fixed-dataset setting. Key Idea: The core insight is that the optimization target should track the current model rather than remain fixed. Constructing the distillation target as a convex interpolation between the current model p_θ^(t) and the frozen SFT reference p_sft at every outer iteration creates a dynamic anchor that co-evolves with the optimization trajectory: the anchor is always nearby the current model (KL bounded by α·KL(p_sft || p_θ^(t))), so successive distillation steps are local trust-region moves rather than a global pull toward a potentially misaligned fixed point. As training progresses the anchor progressively incorporates task knowledge from the current model, eliminating the persistent gradient conflict that plagues static KL regularization. Methodology: Anchored Learning is a two-level iterative procedure running T outer iterations, each with K inner epochs. Outer loop: construct anchor q^(t)(·|x) = (1-α)p_θ^(t)(·|x) + α·p_sft(·|x) in either probability space (arithmetic mean) or logit space (linear interpolation of pre-softmax logits, yielding a geometric mean after normalization). Detach the anchor from the computation graph. Inner loop: minimize the distillation objective KL(q^(t) || p_θ) via AdamW over the fixed dataset D, initialized from θ^(t). The updated model becomes θ^(t+1). Default hyperparameters: α=0.5, T=5, K=5. Three critical design decisions: (1) Dynamic vs. static reference — using the current model in the interpolation is essential; a static reference degenerates to conventional distillation converging to a fixed barycenter. Justified by Proposition 5.4, which proves geometric convergence to p_sft under the dynamic scheme and shows the static barycenter remains at strictly positive KL distance from p_sft. (2) Probability vs. logit interpolation — probability space is an arithmetic mean (mode-covering, higher plasticity, more forgetting); logit space is an exponential geodesic / geometric mean (mode-seeking, higher stability, lower forgetting). The tradeoff is explicit but requires manual selection. (3) Offline-only scope — no on-policy data generation, making Anchored Learning a drop-in replacement for standard SFT pipelines; on-policy distillation extensions are left to future work. Theoretical results: Lemma 5.1 bounds KL(q^(t) || p_θ^(t)) ≤ α·KL(p_sft || p_θ^(t)) under probability interpolation via Jensen's inequality applied to the convex KL functional. Lemma 5.3 bounds KL(q^(t) || p_θ^(t)) ≤ α/(1-α)·KL(p_θ^(t) || p_sft) under logit interpolation via the barycenter optimality of q^(t). Proposition 5.4 proves geometric convergence to p_sft under exact inner-loop projection, with KL(p_θ^(t) || p_sft) ≤ (1-α)^t·KL(p_base || p_sft). Key Results: Evaluated on Qwen2.5-Instruct (1.5B, 3B, 7B) and Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct across iGSM (synthetic grade-school math), MedCalc (medical calculation), and IFEval (instruction following). General capabilities measured on MMLU-Pro, Countdown, MBPP, MBPP+, HumanEval, HumanEval+. Baselines: standard SFT, Low-SFT (lr=1e-6), KL-SFT (λ=0.2), Self-SFT, Iter-SFT, STM, DFT. Qwen2.5-3B + iGSM: SFT achieves domain 1.000 but general collapses to 0.157 (overall avg) from base 0.515 — 69% degradation. Anchored Learning: domain 0.936, overall 0.591. Low-SFT (strongest offline baseline): domain 0.930, overall 0.558. Anchored Learning matches domain performance (+0.006) while recovering an additional 0.033 of general capability vs. Low-SFT. KL-SFT underperforms both (domain 0.606, general 0.189). On MedCalc: SFT domain 0.574 but overall avg 0.104 (base 0.508); Anchored Learning domain 0.563, overall 0.518 — the only method to exceed base overall performance. At 7B scale: Anchored Learning general 0.616 vs. Low-SFT 0.592 with identical domain (0.968 vs. 0.970). Incremental 3-stage adaptation (iGSM→MedCalc→IFEval): at Stage 3, Anchored Learning Domain Avg 0.505 vs. Low-SFT 0.331, Overall Avg 0.492 vs. 0.459, showing the advantage compounds across stages. Training overhead: ~1.5×–2× vs. standard SFT. No 14B results appear in any table despite 14B being listed as an evaluation model. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The moving-anchor interpolation framework for offline SFT is the primary contribution. Knowledge distillation, KL divergence bounds, and trust-region optimization are all well-established; the novel element is specifically the dynamic anchor that co-evolves with the optimization trajectory, enabling locally adaptive drift control in a fully offline setting without on-policy data. The information-geometric framing of logit interpolation as movement along an exponential geodesic is a secondary but genuine conceptual contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations cover α sensitivity (Figure 3), outer×inner iteration schedule sensitivity (Figure 4), and logit vs. probability interpolation (Figure 5) — these are informative and support the method's design choices. However, no error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported anywhere; all results are single-run point estimates. Given that some key differences are small (e.g., general average 0.591 vs. 0.558 for Anchored Learning vs. Low-SFT on iGSM), the absence of variance estimates is a material gap. No code, model weights, or data release is mentioned; no repository URL appears in the paper. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.04468v1, May 2026); results have not been peer-reviewed. The inner-loop distillation is off-policy on the fixed dataset D, which is straightforward to implement, but the exact computational graph (forward pass through both current model and frozen SFT simultaneously) has memory implications the paper does not quantify. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on commercially available GPUs but requires holding both the trainable model and a frozen SFT reference in memory simultaneously, which approximately doubles peak GPU memory compared to standard SFT; this cost is not quantified in the paper. - Efficient: Wall-clock overhead is 1.5×–2× vs. standard SFT (measured), which is substantially below on-policy RL, but the full memory cost of two concurrent model copies at larger scales is uncharacterized. - Effective: Consistent evidence across 3 tasks and 2 model families shows near-SFT domain accuracy with dramatically reduced general capability degradation; the method works as claimed at tested scales on the stated benchmarks. - Scalable: Tested at 1.5B, 3B, and 7B; 14B is listed as an evaluation model but no 14B results appear in the tables. No experiments test behavior with larger datasets, longer sequences, or more outer iterations beyond T=5. The work delivers a method described in Algorithm 1 pseudocode but no software library, CLI, dataset, or released model weights. Strengths: - The insight that dynamic anchor tracking eliminates persistent gradient conflict (vs. static regularization) is both well-motivated and formally justified: Proposition 5.4 cleanly separates the convergence behavior of dynamic vs. static barycenters, showing the latter cannot reach p_sft. - The exponential geodesic / information-geometric framing of logit-space interpolation (Lemma 5.2) is a principled explanation for the empirically observed mode-seeking stability of logit vs. probability interpolation. - The gain–stability Pareto frontier visualization across six model-task settings is a more honest evaluation framing than reporting either metric alone, and Anchored Learning's consistent upper-right placement is credible given the diversity of settings. - The incremental 3-stage adaptation experiment (Table 9) is a stronger test than any single-task comparison, and the compounding advantage over Low-SFT (Domain Avg 0.505 vs. 0.331 at Stage 3) is the paper's most compelling empirical result. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge one limitation: the 1.5×–2× training overhead from the extra forward pass for anchor construction and distillation may limit scalability in resource-constrained settings. They state this directly, which is a credibility positive. They do not acknowledge the absence of LoRA baselines, the missing 14B results, the lack of error bars, or the gap between the exact-projection assumption in Proposition 5.4 and the approximate K=5-step inner loop used in practice. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Missing LoRA/PEFT baseline: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA, DoRA, etc.) is the dominant practical approach to catastrophic forgetting in LLM post-training and is entirely absent from the baseline set. This is the most significant omission — Anchored Learning's practical advantage over a LoRA-regularized fine-tuning approach is unknown. - No statistical significance: All point estimates are single-run; key general-capability improvements (e.g., +0.033 vs. Low-SFT on iGSM) are in a range where run-to-run variance could plausibly account for the gap. - 14B results absent from tables: The paper lists Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct as an evaluation model but no 14B results appear anywhere in the appendix tables. - Exact-projection assumption in theory: Proposition 5.4 assumes p_θ^(t+1) = q^(t) exactly; in practice K=5 gradient steps on an LLM approximate this loosely, and the paper provides no empirical or theoretical characterization of this gap's effect on the KL bounds. - Memory cost unquantified: The practical requirement of holding two model copies in GPU memory simultaneously is not analyzed, which is a meaningful omission for anyone considering deployment at 70B+ scale. - Fixed hyperparameters across tasks: α=0.5, T=5, K=5 are used as defaults across all tasks without demonstrating that these transfer robustly; Figure 4 shows general performance is highly sensitive to (T, K), raising the question of whether different tasks require different tuning. Bottom Line: Anchored Learning is a simple, well-motivated method that consistently outperforms static KL regularization and matches or slightly exceeds low-LR fine-tuning on general capability retention — ML practitioners doing offline domain adaptation of LLMs without RL infrastructure will find it worth reading, but the absent LoRA comparison and lack of statistical rigor prevent confident claims about practical advantage over the strongest practical baseline.
Recommended 2605.04468 PDF
#107 — Innovative (61)

LongLive-2.0: An NVFP4 Parallel Infrastructure for Long Video Generation (90 ▲ )

Yukang Chen, Luozhou Wang, Wei Huang, Shuai Yang, Bohan Zhang, Yicheng Xiao, Ruihang Chu, Weian Mao, Qixin Hu, Shaoteng Liu, Yuyang Zhao, Huizi Mao, Ying-Cong Chen, Enze Xie, Xiaojuan Qi, Song Han · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LongLive-2.0, an end-to-end NVFP4 training and inference infrastructure for long autoregressive video generation that co-designs sequence parallelism, 4-bit quantization, KV-cache compression, and asynchronous VAE decoding into a unified system. - Why it's important: Long video generation at real-time framerates (≥24 FPS) is blocked by GPU memory and latency constraints that algorithmic work alone cannot solve, and existing systems leave a train/inference precision mismatch that degrades quality under low-bit deployment. - How it does it: The core move is treating NVFP4 as a training-aligned precision (not just a post-training compression), combined with a Balanced SP layout that assigns each GPU both the clean context and noisy target latents for the same temporal chunk, making load, VAE encoding, and attention masking all consistent with sequence-parallel execution. - The good: At 64-second video length, NVFP4+Balanced SP achieves 2.15× training speedup over BF16+SP (639.5 ms vs. 1372.9 ms per iteration) and 45.7 FPS end-to-end inference at 1280×720, surpassing all listed baselines in throughput. - The missing: All inference speed results are on NVIDIA GB200 (Blackwell) GPUs that are not yet commercially available at scale; the 5B model is compared against 1.3B–2B baselines at lower resolution, making quality comparisons on VBench structurally unequal. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on video generation infrastructure or quantization-aware training for diffusion models; skip it if your primary interest is algorithmic video quality, as the contribution is nearly entirely systems-level.
Why this rank: Scored using the Systems profile. Practical Impact (65, 30% weight) reflects concrete, deployable infrastructure achieving real-time 45.7 FPS at 720p, but is gated by Blackwell-only hardware and code/dataset not yet public.
