Rigor (55) and Resilience (55) are limited by structurally unequal VBench comparisons (a 5B/720p model against 1.3B–2B/480p baselines), some explicitly estimated rather than measured latency numbers, and self-reported NVIDIA results with no seeds. Non-Obviousness (68) is the strongest dimension — the training-aligned-precision insight and the clean/noisy chunk co-ownership in Balanced SP are non-trivial — landing the paper firmly in the Innovative band.
systems Novelty 62 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Training and deploying long autoregressive video diffusion models faces two compounding bottlenecks. First, memory: a 64-second video DiT sequence exceeds single-GPU capacity, and the clean-history + noisy-target teacher-forcing layout creates an asymmetric load that naive sequence parallelism cannot balance. Second, latency: interactive or real-time applications require sub-40 ms per frame, but BF16 models at 5B parameters with full VAE decoding cannot meet this. Prior work (Self-Forcing, Causal-Forcing, LongLive) addressed algorithmic aspects (rollout alignment, distillation) while relying on BF16 precision and post-training quantization at inference, leaving a precision mismatch that degrades quality. No prior system had applied NVFP4 end-to-end through training, distillation, and deployment for video generation. Key Idea: The central insight is that NVFP4 should be a training-aligned precision, not a deployment afterthought. This requires co-designing the sequence-parallel AR training layout (Balanced SP) so that clean and noisy latents for the same temporal chunk co-reside on the same GPU rank, which in turn allows VAE encoding, attention masking, loss computation, and KV quantization to all operate on locally consistent shards. The resulting system eliminates the precision mismatch between training and W4A4 inference that makes post-training quantization lossy. Methodology: The pipeline has four interlocking components. (1) Balanced SP: Built on DeepSpeed-Ulysses, it partitions the video temporally so rank p owns both z_clean^(p) and z_noisy^(p) from the same temporal chunk. Each rank VAE-encodes only its local chunk X^(p) plus a left halo of size h, reducing per-rank VAE cost from O(F) to O(F/P + h). The teacher-forcing AR mask is constructed directly on the post-All-to-All interleaved token order via a flex_attention predicate, avoiding expensive permutations. An SP-aware error-recycling buffer injects stochastic rollout errors into the clean prefix to reduce exposure bias. (2) NVFP4 Training: 2D block scaling for weights, 1D for activations and gradients; Random Hadamard Transform (RHT) on the weight-gradient GEMM operands for stability; FP32 for optimizer states and normalization statistics. Few-step DMD distillation runs entirely in NVFP4, with only LoRA adapters (rank 128) trainable and the quantized backbone frozen. Adaptive block scale selection (Four Over Six) evaluates both magnitude-6 and magnitude-4 FP8 block scales and picks the lower-MSE encoding per block. (3) Inference: W4A4 NVFP4 on Blackwell GPUs; KV cache quantized at the frame-chunk level with K-smoothing before block scaling, achieving ~3.6× compression (from 4T_cHd bytes to 9/8·T_cHd bytes); a custom parallel CUDA dequantization kernel keeps KV overhead below 2%; asynchronous streaming VAE decoding dedicates a separate GPU and overlaps VAE decoding of chunk c with DiT denoising of chunk c+1, reducing end-to-end latency from C(t_DiT + t_VAE) to ~C·t_DiT + t_VAE. (4) Multi-Shot Attention Sink: Sliding-window self-attention is augmented with two anchor sets — a global sink (first S_g frames, permanent) and a shot-level sink (first S_s frames of the current shot, re-bound at scene cuts). The shot-level sink incurs zero memory overhead (tracked by two scalar pointers) and integrates with chunk-wise prompting. Critical design decisions: (a) training/inference precision alignment is well-justified by the PTQ ablation showing a 1.02-point VBench Total score drop under PTQ vs. pre-trained NVFP4; (b) Balanced SP's natural mask construction on the post-All-to-All order avoids per-layer permutation overhead, which is well-justified by complexity analysis; (c) standalone LoRA injection for DMD (vs. full AR fine-tuning) is justified empirically by visual quality and operationally by parallelism with AR training, though the justification is partly subjective. Key Results: Training efficiency (Table 1, 4× GB200): at 64s video, NVFP4+Balanced SP reduces iteration time from 1372.9s (BF16+SP) to 639.5s (2.15×); at 16s/32s, speedups are 1.3× and 1.4× respectively. DMD memory (Table 2): progressive NVFP4 conversion reduces peak per-GPU memory from 70.5 GB to 49.0 GB (0.69×). Inference efficiency (Table 3, GB200): 4-step BF16 achieves 24.8 FPS for 16s video; 4-step NVFP4+KV quantization+async decoding achieves 29.7 FPS; 2-step reaches 45.7 FPS with 19.4 GB peak memory (down from 36.4 GB for BF16). VBench quality (Table 4): LongLive-2.0 BF16 scores 85.06 Total at 1280×720 vs. Self-Forcing at 84.31 (1.3B, 480p) — the strongest short-video result, though at 4× more parameters and higher resolution. VBench-Long (Table 5, 60s): LongLive-2.0 achieves best average rank 3.67, with subject consistency 97.48 and background consistency 97.00. SP vs. TP vs. DP comparison (Figure 8, 4× GB200): SP is fastest at all sequence lengths and most memory-efficient at long contexts (51.2 GB vs. 70.3 GB for TP vs. 97.8 GB for DP at 512 frames). The pre-trained NVFP4 model outperforms direct PTQ on VBench Total by 0.47 points (84.51 vs. 84.04), validating the training-alignment hypothesis. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. The paper claims to be the first end-to-end NVFP4 training and inference system for long video generation. Balanced SP's key novelty is the clean/noisy chunk co-ownership design that resolves an AR-specific load imbalance not present in generic sequence parallelism. The multi-shot attention sink is a targeted algorithmic contribution. The standalone LoRA DMD strategy is a practical simplification of prior multi-stage pipelines rather than a fundamental algorithmic advance. The broader NVFP4 quantization methodology draws heavily on established techniques (Four Over Six, RHT, block scaling) applied to a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are provided for: each inference optimization component (Table 3, progressive ablation), training parallelism strategies (Table 1), DMD quantization stages (Table 2), PTQ vs. pre-trained NVFP4 (Table 7), multi-shot attention sink (Figure 10/Appendix E), and DMD fine-tuning strategies (Appendix H). No error bars or multiple-seed results are reported for any experiment. The 64s SP inference latency numbers in Table 6 are explicitly stated to be estimated from shorter-length runs, not measured. Benchmark evaluation uses VBench and VBench-Long, which are standard in this area. Code is promised at github.com/NVlabs/LongLive but the repository was not yet public at paper submission. The training dataset (120K videos) is not released. The paper is an arXiv preprint; results and baselines are self-reported by NVIDIA with no independent verification. Hardware access (GB200) required for NVFP4 inference is not yet broadly available to the research community. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — a built infrastructure evaluated by measured training and inference benchmarks. - Practical: NVFP4 inference requires NVIDIA Blackwell GPUs (GB200/B200); for non-Blackwell GPUs (A100, H100), only SP inference is available, achieving 29.7–54.8s E2E latency for 64s video on H100 at SP=4, which is real-time only at larger SP sizes. - Efficient: Training memory drops from 35.4 GB to 19.4 GB; inference memory drops similarly; at 64s, the combined system uses 2× fewer GPU-hours than BF16 baselines (1920 GB200 GPU hours for AR training + 60 for DMD); KV-cache compression is 3.6×; total DMD memory 0.69× of BF16. - Effective: The 45.7 FPS at 1280×720 demonstrates real-time generation is achieved; VBench scores remain within 0.55 Total points of BF16 under NVFP4 (84.51 vs. 85.06), validating that the quality cost of quantization is small; however, quality comparisons against competitor methods are confounded by the model scale and resolution difference. - Scalable: Tested up to 64s video generation on 32 GB200 GPUs (training) and 4 GPUs (inference); the paper demonstrates that efficiency gains grow with video length (2.15× speedup at 64s vs. 1.3× at 16s), which is the correct scaling direction; no test beyond 64s for training or inference at scale is reported. The work delivers a software infrastructure (training + inference stack), a curated dataset (not released), and trained model weights (not yet released at submission). Strengths: - The training/inference precision alignment argument is well-executed: the PTQ ablation (Table 7) concretely demonstrates that the 0.47-point VBench drop from BF16→PTQ is largely recovered by training in NVFP4 (84.04 PTQ vs. 84.51 pre-trained), providing direct empirical justification for the design choice rather than just claiming it. - Balanced SP solves a genuine technical problem — clean/noisy load imbalance and VAE replication — that is specific to teacher-forced AR video DiT training and not addressed by any prior SP system. The mathematical derivation of the natural mask on the post-All-to-All interleaved order is a non-trivial engineering contribution. - The pipeline simplification relative to Self-Forcing / LongLive is meaningful: eliminating ODE initialization and multi-stage long-tuning reduces training complexity from 4–5 stages to 2 (AR training + standalone LoRA DMD), and this simplification is enabled by the infrastructure, not just by algorithmic choice. - The ablation table for inference optimizations (Table 3) is unusually thorough, progressively isolating the contribution of each component (NVFP4, KV quantization, async decoding, step reduction) with both latency and memory numbers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that NVFP4 inference acceleration is hardware-dependent and delivers speedups only on Blackwell GPUs, with A100 and H100 lacking native hardware support. They propose SP inference as a mitigation for non-Blackwell platforms. They note that VBench scores are resolution-dependent and that 720p models do not consistently outperform 480p models under VBench's evaluation protocol, partially excusing lower scores vs. some 480p baselines. No acknowledgment is made of the model scale disparity in comparisons. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most significant unacknowledged issue is that quality comparisons (Table 4) pit a 5B model at 1280×720 against 1.3B–2B models at 832×480 — the only fair 5B/720p baseline is Wan2.2-TI2V-5B, against which LongLive-2.0 improves by 1.74 VBench Total points but at 4-step vs. 50-step denoising, which is not a same-budget comparison. Second, the 64s inference latency numbers on H100 in Table 6 are estimated, not measured — for a paper centered on infrastructure benchmarking, this is a notable gap. Third, no perceptual user study is conducted to assess whether the 45.7 FPS 2-step generation is perceptually competitive with 4-step or BF16 generation; VBench is an automated metric. Fourth, GB200 GPUs are not commercially available at scale as of the paper's publication date, limiting near-term reproducibility for most researchers. Fifth, the 120K-video training dataset is not released and its construction methodology (MANIQA filtering threshold, caption model) is described qualitatively, making replication difficult. Sixth, the error-recycling mechanism (Section C) is described in detail but not ablated — its contribution to final model quality is unmeasured. Bottom Line: Practitioners building production long video generation infrastructure on Blackwell-class hardware will find concrete, immediately deployable techniques here; for the broader research community, the hardware dependency and scale-confounded comparisons limit the generalizability of the efficiency claims.
Recommended 2605.18739 PDF
#108 — Innovative (61)

$π$-Bench: Evaluating Proactive Personal Assistant Agents in Long-Horizon Workflows (79 ▲ )

Haoran Zhang, Luxin Xu, Zhilin Wang, Runquan Gui, Shunkai Zhang, Haodi Lei, Zihao He, Bingsu He, Chicheng Qin, Tong Zhu, Xiaoye Qu, Yang Yang, Yu Cheng, Yafu Li · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 22 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces π-BENCH, a benchmark of 100 multi-turn tasks across 5 domain-specific user personas that jointly evaluates proactivity (hidden intent resolution) and task completeness in long-horizon personal assistant workflows. - Why it's important: Existing benchmarks assume explicit user goals or treat memory retrieval as the primary challenge, leaving unmeasured whether agents can proactively surface and act on unstated preferences, constraints, and cross-session dependencies that determine whether an assistant is genuinely useful versus merely reactive. - How it does it: Each task is annotated with hidden intents absent from the initial request; a GPT-5.4-powered simulated user tracks per-turn whether each intent is agent-completed (acted on without prompting), agent-inferred (elicited via targeted clarification), or user-provided (the agent waited), yielding separate PROC and COMP scores over the full trajectory. - The good: The ablation removing prior session history from dependency groups reduces average PROC by 9.5 points but COMP by only 2.5 points, directly confirming that cross-session context is specifically valuable for proactive intent resolution rather than final task execution. - The missing: GPT-5.4 simultaneously serves as the user simulator, rubric judge, and one of the nine evaluated models — the self-consistency audit shows low disagreement rates but does not rule out systematic scoring bias in its own favor, and GPT-5.4 achieves the best PROC score. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building or evaluating long-horizon personal assistant agents — the hidden-intent framework and PROC/COMP decomposition are directly applicable to agent system design, though the 100-task scale and single-scaffold setup limit confidence in the specific leaderboard numbers.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile (benchmark contribution). Novelty (68) reflects a clean conceptual contribution — the three-way completed/inferred/provided terminal-status decomposition and the PROC/COMP separation fill a real gap. Rigor (63) is reasonable (3 runs with low std, judge reliability audited against humans and frontier models) but no explicit code URL is printed and results are an unreviewed preprint. The composite is pulled down by Resilience (53) and Non-Obviousness (60): GPT-5.4 simultaneously serves as user simulator, judge, AND a top-scoring evaluated model — an unresolved conflict of interest — while the uniformly low inferred rate (8–12%) collapses the three-way framework to a near-binary measure in practice, and the entirely Chinese-context, single-scaffold, 100-task scope limits generalizability of the leaderboard numbers. Weighted: 0.20*68 + 0.25*63 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*53 + 0.10*55 = 61.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 63 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 60 Resilience 53 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Personal assistant agents increasingly support users across multi-session workflows — trip planning, deck preparation, legal handovers — where initial requests are deliberately underspecified. Users omit constraints, preferences, and downstream dependencies that a capable assistant should infer or elicit rather than wait to be told. Prior benchmarks fail on this axis in three distinct ways: general agent benchmarks (AgentBench, GAIA) assume explicit goals at interaction time; memory benchmarks (MemoryArena, Mem2ActBench) treat memory as evidence for completing a known task rather than a signal for detecting missing requirements; and proactive agent benchmarks (ProActiveMobile, PIRA-Bench) focus on mobile or GUI settings with short consumer tasks and device context, not the artifact-centric professional workflows where proactivity has the highest cost when absent. No prior benchmark evaluates whether agents can anticipate hidden requirements across cross-session dependencies in persistent file-and-workspace environments. Key Idea The key insight is decomposing hidden-intent resolution into three mutually exclusive terminal states — completed (agent acts on an unstated requirement independently), inferred (agent asks a targeted question that directly elicits the requirement, then acts on it), and provided (user must volunteer the information without agent prompting) — and computing proactivity as PROC = (|I_completed| + |I_inferred|) / |I|, separate from checklist-based completeness COMP. This decomposition makes visible a specific failure mode of reactive agents: because the simulated user eventually provides any intent the agent misses, COMP can remain high even when PROC is near zero, revealing that task quality and proactive burden-reduction are genuinely distinct capabilities. Methodology The benchmark constructs 5 user personas (researcher, marketer, law trainee, pharmacist, financier) each with a 20-session episode organized into 6 strong dependency groups (2–3 tasks sharing essential carry-over information) and 5 largely independent tasks. Each session starts with a natural but underspecified initial request and is annotated with hidden intents I and a completeness checklist C. A simulated user agent (GPT-5.4, temperature 0) runs a two-stage intent-assignment protocol per agent turn: Stage 1 checks whether the latest agent response or workspace artifact satisfies any unresolved intent (assigns completed); Stage 2 checks whether the agent asked a targeted clarification question matching any unresolved intent (assigns inferred and answers in the next user message). If neither stage fires, the user selects one unresolved intent to provide, keeping the task moving. Sessions terminate when all intents reach terminal status. Checklist items are scored by LLM rubric evaluation (GPT-5.4 judge, strict YES/NO) for open-ended semantic criteria and by task-specific Python scripts inspecting tool call payloads for structured facts such as order IDs and exact string fields. Three critical design decisions: (1) equal credit for completed and inferred intents in PROC — justified on the grounds that both reflect agent initiative, but this conflates autonomous inference with reactive clarification and inflates scores for models that frequently ask focused questions; (2) session continues until all intents are resolved, ensuring COMP measures final artifact quality regardless of who surfaced requirements and allowing the PROC/COMP separation to be cleanly analyzed; (3) single Nanobot-adapted ReAct scaffold for all models, controlling infrastructure confounds at the cost of ecological validity across diverse agent architectures. Key Results Nine frontier models evaluated: GPT-5.4, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Claude Opus 4.6, DeepSeek V3.2, MiniMax M2.7, Kimi K2.5, Seed2.0 Pro, GLM-5.1, Qwen3.6 Plus. Best PROC: GPT-5.4 (67.0); best COMP: Claude Opus 4.6 (67.6). PROC range across models: 43.1–67.0; COMP range: 52.1–67.6. Standard deviations mostly below 2.0 across 3 repeated independent runs. The most diagnostic divergences: Kimi K2.5 achieves 61.6 COMP but only 43.1 PROC, demonstrating that reactive agents can recover task quality through user disclosure while contributing nothing to requirement discovery; Seed2.0 Pro achieves 58.4 PROC but only 52.1 COMP, showing that early intent identification does not guarantee correct artifact production. Cross-session ablation: removing prior session history from dependency groups reduces average PROC by 9.5 points (e.g., GPT-5.4 drops from 78.5 to 64.9, DeepSeek V3.2 from 61.9 to 52.1) while COMP decreases only 2.5 points on average. Domain variation is substantial: Pharmacist tasks are easiest overall (concrete file-grounded intents, highest average PROC and COMP); Law Trainee and Financier are hardest for COMP, requiring legal and financial judgment. Task-type extremes: Legal matter operations and handoffs average 84.1% COMP vs. 38.1% PROC (agents draft documents but miss handoff-readiness requirements); Drug design and formulation tasks show the inverse pattern at 84.9% PROC vs. 68.0% COMP. Judge reliability audit on 120 trajectories: human expert disagreement 2.66% (checklist), 1.48% (hidden intents); frontier model audits within 3.6% and 2.1% respectively. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The paper contributes a runnable evaluation framework with domain-expert-reviewed tasks, annotated hidden intents, cross-session dependency structure, and hybrid grading. The three-way intent terminal-status decomposition (completed / inferred / provided) as a formalization of proactivity is the primary conceptual contribution. The component ideas — simulated user agents, LLM-as-judge, rule-based checklist verification, multi-session agent evaluation — are individually established; their integration into a coherent proactivity benchmark with cross-session dependencies is what is new. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: the dependency group ablation is well-controlled and the failure analysis by task type provides mechanistic interpretation of aggregate results. Three repeated runs with independent trajectories and reported standard deviations support stable aggregate conclusions. Judge reliability is audited quantitatively against both human experts and independent frontier models with consistently low disagreement rates (<4%). A benchmark code/project link is referenced on the first page but no explicit URL is printed in the body text, making independent replication contingent on locating the linked repository. All models are accessed through hosted APIs. The benchmark is a preprint (arXiv:2605.14678v3, May 2026); results are not peer-reviewed. The task workspace assets are derived from domain expert workflows and sanitized to remove private data, but the sanitization process is described procedurally rather than verified by an external audit. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — an agent evaluation benchmark with experimental runs across 9 frontier models. - Practical: The benchmark infrastructure runs on commodity server hardware (2-socket Linux server, <8 GB RAM per task run, Docker containerized); access requires hosted API credentials for 9 model providers and the AppWorld environment. - Efficient: Three runs of 100 multi-turn tasks across 9 models represents substantial API cost (not quantified in the paper); no inference occurs locally, so GPU hardware is not required, but the cost of large-scale replication is non-trivial. - Effective: The benchmark discriminates meaningfully across models (24-point PROC spread, stable across runs) and reveals behavioral variation not captured by single-session benchmarks, as the PROC/COMP divergence demonstrates — this is validated by the dependency ablation confirming the design rationale. - Scalable: The current scope is small — 100 tasks, 5 personas, 1 agentic scaffold — and all personas reflect Chinese professional contexts (Jiangzhou court, Shanghai campus, RMB pricing, Chinese tax law, iFinD finance data). Generalization to broader domains, languages, and scaffold architectures is not demonstrated. The paper delivers a dataset and evaluation protocol (software artifact + benchmark tasks); no trained model, pretrained weights, or reusable ML algorithm is produced. Strengths - The three-way terminal-status decomposition (completed / inferred / provided) is a clean and informative formalization that makes the agent's contribution to requirement discovery measurable independently of final outcome quality. This framing is directly applicable beyond this specific benchmark. - The empirical divergence between PROC and COMP is a concrete, reproducible finding backed by low variance across 3 runs: the Kimi K2.5 and Seed2.0 Pro off-diagonal cases are not artifacts of a single run and reveal a genuine decoupling between reactive task execution and proactive burden reduction. - The cross-session dependency ablation is well-designed and confirms the benchmark's core design rationale with a controlled comparison: the 9.5-point PROC drop without prior sessions vs. 2.5-point COMP drop quantifies exactly how much cross-session context matters for proactivity versus execution. - The hybrid grading scheme (LLM rubric for semantic criteria + rule-based Python scripts for structured tool evidence) is methodologically more trustworthy than pure LLM-as-judge evaluation and is audited against human experts with good agreement. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state two limitations: (1) users are simulated rather than real, because live-user long-horizon evaluation is costly, hard to reproduce, and difficult to scale; (2) all experiments use a single Nanobot-adapted scaffold, which may not capture variation introduced by alternative scaffolds. The authors confront these directly and honestly, framing π-BENCH as an evaluation resource rather than a deployment recommendation. Analyst-Identified Gaps - GPT-5.4 simultaneously functions as user simulator, rubric judge, and one of the nine evaluated models. GPT-5.4 achieves the highest PROC (67.0) and second-highest COMP (65.6). The self-consistency audit shows low within-judge disagreement but cannot detect systematic bias favoring GPT-5.4-style outputs; this is a methodologically unresolved conflict of interest. - The inferred rate (targeted clarification) is uniformly low across all models — 8.36% to 12.29% — meaning PROC is effectively a binary completed-vs-provided distinction in practice, not the richer three-way measurement the framework defines. Tasks or evaluation criteria that would more specifically stress targeted elicitation are missing. - All 100 tasks are set in Chinese professional or consumer contexts (Chinese legal system, RMB pricing, Shanghai campus, Chinese tax law, iFinD finance data), which may confer an implicit advantage on models with stronger Chinese-language training and limits generalization to English-language workflows — the paper does not discuss this. - With only 20 tasks per persona, domain-level and task-type-level statistics (e.g., "Pharmacist PROC is highest") are based on small samples where a few individual tasks can substantially move averages; per-domain standard deviations and significance tests are not reported. - The benchmark uses a single scaffold, but the paper itself notes alternative scaffolds "can add substantial adaptation effort and scaffold-specific confounds" — no cross-scaffold pilot is reported even for one model, so it is unknown whether PROC rankings are scaffold-invariant. Bottom Line π-BENCH delivers a conceptually clean and practically useful formalization of proactive agent evaluation that fills a real gap in the benchmark landscape; practitioners building long-horizon personal assistant agents should engage with the PROC/COMP framework, but the 100-task scale, single scaffold, Chinese-centric task corpus, and GPT-5.4 judge/competitor overlap mean the specific leaderboard numbers should be treated as indicative rather than definitive.
Recommended 2605.14678 PDF
#109 — Innovative (61)

ParamSpMM: Adaptive and Efficient Sparse Matrix-Matrix Multiplication on GPUs for GNNs

Lixing Zhang, Guanhua Ye, Hongzheng Li, Shigang Li, Yingxia Shao · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ParamSpMM, a GPU SpMM kernel framework that unifies vectorized blocking, workload balancing, and thread coarsening under a single parameterized sparse data structure (PCSR) with an ML-based random-forest configuration predictor for GNN workloads. - Why it's important: SpMM dominates GNN training time and existing GPU kernels apply fixed optimization strategies that degrade on graphs with diverse degree distributions, data locality, and embedding dimensions — the exact conditions encountered in real-world GNN deployment. - How it does it: A new PCSR (Parameterized Compressed Sparse Row) format encodes four tunable parameters ⟨V, S, F, W⟩ controlling blocking granularity, workload redistribution, thread coarsening factor, and thread block size; a random forest predicts the best combination per input from 16 hand-crafted graph features. - The good: ParamSpMM achieves 1.92× average speedup over Nvidia cuSPARSE across 3232 diverse SpMM inputs (202 graphs × 16 embedding dimensions), with the ML predictor reaching ≥98.24% of oracle-configuration performance at every tested dimension. - The missing: Evaluation covers only a single GPU architecture (NVIDIA A6000/Ampere); no test on A100 or H100 where different memory hierarchies may shift the optimal ⟨V, S, F, W⟩ substantially, and PCSR generation overhead is never timed in isolation. - Read it or not: Worth reading for GPU systems researchers and GNN framework developers who need a drop-in SpMM kernel; the PCSR abstraction and MAC-job gap (gapF) analysis are clean, reusable contributions.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (72) is solid — a public PyTorch extension delivering a measured 1.92x cuSPARSE speedup across a genuinely diverse 3232-input benchmark, with end-to-end GNN gains on 6 OGB datasets. Rigor (55) and Completeness (52) pull it down: no per-component ablation (so it is impossible to tell whether blocking, balancing, or coarsening is load-bearing), ASpT — the direct blocking competitor named in the motivation — is absent from the comparison table, the random-forest choice is asserted not measured, evaluation is single-architecture (A6000 only), and there is no explicit limitations section. Workshop venue (GDMA 2025) further lowers peer-review weight. Weighted: 0.15*62 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*60 + 0.15*52 + 0.15*55 + 0.30*72 = 61.
systems Novelty 62 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 52 Resilience 55 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation GNN message-passing reduces to SpMM (sparse adjacency matrix × dense node-embedding matrix), which routinely dominates end-to-end training time. Three dimensions of input diversity make a single fixed kernel inadequate: (1) data locality — community structure in real-world graphs creates highly variable cache-hit ratios for the dense embedding matrix; (2) degree distribution — social graphs follow power-law distributions that cause severe warp workload imbalance, while road networks follow Poisson distributions where balancing overhead is unnecessary; (3) embedding dimension — GNN layer widths vary widely, and mismatch between embedding dimension and thread-coarsening factor F creates MAC-job gaps (idle compute in residual warps). Existing static kernels (DGL, PyG) fix all parameters; dynamic designs like GNNAdvisor, GE-SpMM, and DA-SpMM each address one or two of these dimensions but apply their chosen optimization unconditionally, degrading on inputs where it is counterproductive. Key Idea The core insight is that the three GPU optimizations — vectorized blocking, nonzero-split workload balancing, and thread coarsening — address orthogonal aspects of SpMM diversity and can be composed without conflict inside a single parameterized data structure. Once PCSR encodes these parameters, optimal configuration becomes a standard ML classification problem over measurable graph statistics, making the entire system self-tuning per input. Methodology ParamSpMM operates in three phases. Phase 1: an ML-based SpMM-decider (random forest) predicts ⟨W, F, V, S⟩ from 16 features in three categories — size (n, nnz, average/max degree), degree distribution (coefficient of variation, split ratio SR), and data locality (bandwidth, padding ratio PRV). Two new features are defined: PRV = 1 − nnz/(nnzV × V) quantifies zero padding introduced by vectorized blocking at block size V; SR = split_size/row_size quantifies the write-overhead multiplier from workload redistribution. Phase 2: PCSR generation partitions sparse matrix A into V×1 nonzero vectors; if S=True, rowPtr is reassigned to redistribute workload across warps (split granularity SG = ⌈d̂V/ω⌉×ω) and TRow is populated with target output positions for atomic accumulation. Phase 3: a parameterized CUDA kernel (Algorithm 2) combines blocking (B values reused V times per nonzero vector), thread coarsening (F MAC operations per nonzero fetch, boundary-handled via variable t), and atomic writes when S=True. Rabbit Reordering is applied as default preprocessing. Critical design decisions: (a) restricting V ∈ {1,2} — empirically justified by showing V=3 introduces >50% zero padding on 97.5% of 202 test graphs; (b) using random forests over deeper models — asserted to reduce overfitting risk but not ablated against gradient boosting; (c) setting SG proportional to the mean nonzero vector count per row — a fixed heuristic without sensitivity analysis. Key Results Hardware: NVIDIA A6000 (Ampere, 84 SMs, compute capability 8.6). SpMM benchmark: 202 matrices from SNAP and DIMACS10 (n ∈ [1005, 7,733,822]; density ρ ∈ [2.73×10⁻⁷, 0.025]; CV ∈ [0.006, 58.1]), 16 embedding dimensions (16–256), yielding 3232 inputs. ParamSpMM achieves average speedups of 1.92× over cuSPARSE, 2.41× over GE-SpMM, 1.55× over GNNAdvisor, and 1.64× over DA-SpMM (Table 4). The SpMM-decider achieves ≥98.24% of oracle-configuration throughput for all tested dim values vs. 68–83% for random configuration (Table 5). Graph reordering contributes 1.14× to cuSPARSE but 1.26× to ParamSpMM, indicating ParamSpMM exploits locality improvements more effectively. GNN training on 6 OGB graphs: 1.60× average (up to 2.19×) for GCN and 1.61× average (up to 2.59×) for GIN over DGL. Baselines are current and appropriate; notably, ASpT — a direct blocking-based competitor cited in the motivation — does not appear in the main comparison table. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The PCSR data structure that orthogonally composes all three GPU optimization axes is genuinely novel; the MAC-job gap metric gapF and the PR/SR feature definitions are new analytical contributions. The ML-guided configuration selection over a richer joint space than prior work (DA-SpMM covers only workload balancing) is incrementally novel. The overall approach assembles known techniques (vectorized blocking, nonzero-split balancing, thread coarsening, random forests) but the composition mechanism and the feature engineering are original. Rigor & Reproducibility Code is publicly released at https://github.com/zzzlxhhh/ParamSpMM_AE. The 202-matrix × 16-dim benchmark provides meaningful diversity. OGB graphs are held out from SpMM-decider training, providing a clean generalization test for the GNN application results. No error bars or repeated-run statistics are reported for GPU throughput measurements, which can vary by 1–5% across runs. No ablation isolates individual contributions of the three optimization components (V, S, F) — it is unclear whether blocking, balancing, or thread coarsening contributes most of the 1.92× gain. Random forest model choice is not ablated against XGBoost or gradient boosting. ASpT [11] is cited as a key prior work in the motivation but absent from quantitative comparisons. This is a workshop paper (GDMA 2025 at DASFAA), not a main-track venue; results carry lower peer-review weight than a systems conference (SC, PPoPP, OSDI) paper. Deployability (4-property check) SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Integrates as a PyTorch extension compiled with GCC 9.4.0 and NVCC 11.6; runs on a commercially available A6000 GPU with no special hardware requirements. - Efficient: PCSR generation and ML inference overhead are amortizable in iterative GNN training (the authors state the reordering and feature cost is paid once), but neither cost is timed or quantified individually, leaving actual per-invocation overhead unknown. - Effective: Consistent speedups over all four baselines across 3232 diverse inputs, with GNN end-to-end speedups on 6 OGB datasets confirming that SpMM acceleration translates to real training gains. - Scalable: Largest tested graph has n=7.7M; graphs larger than ~8M nodes are excluded due to out-of-memory on A6000; no test at 100M+ node scale, and behavior at embedding dimensions beyond 256 is not evaluated. The work delivers a software artifact (PyTorch extension + source code) rather than stopping at paper form. Strengths - The PCSR data structure is a clean abstraction: by encoding ⟨V, S, F, W⟩ into the sparse matrix representation itself, the compute kernel remains a single unified implementation rather than a set of dispatch branches, making the composition principled. - The MAC-job gap analysis (gapF formulation in Eq. 1) identifies a concrete, previously unacknowledged source of wasted computation in thread coarsening that prior work ignores; Table 2 quantifies its frequency empirically across 202 graphs. - The benchmark setup (202 graphs × 16 dimensions, boxplot distribution in Figure 4) demonstrates robustness across the input distribution rather than reporting only mean or peak speedups, giving a credible picture of worst-case behavior. - SpMM-decider's ≥98.24% oracle-matching performance across all dimensions indicates the 16 engineered features are sufficient to capture the configuration-determining signal without complex models. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The paper has no explicit limitations section. The authors acknowledge: (1) V is restricted to {1,2} to limit zero padding (Section 3.1); (2) graphs with n > ~8M are excluded due to GPU memory constraints (Section 6.1). No other limitations are explicitly stated. The authors do not acknowledge the single-architecture constraint, the unquantified PCSR generation overhead, or the narrow GNN model coverage (GCN, GIN only). Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single GPU architecture (A6000/Ampere only): H100 (Hopper) and A100 have different L2 cache sizes, memory bandwidth ratios, and SM counts; optimal ⟨V, S, F, W⟩ may differ, and the SpMM-decider's random forest may need retraining per GPU, but this is not discussed. - PCSR generation time is never reported separately; for non-iterative or low-epoch workloads the amortization argument may not hold, making the effective speedup unclear. - ASpT [11] is cited as a key motivation target but never appears in Table 4; its omission from direct comparison leaves a gap given it is specifically designed to address blocking adaptability. - No per-component ablation: the relative contribution of V, S, and F optimizations to the overall 1.92× is not broken out, making it impossible to determine which design element is load-bearing. - GNN evaluation covers only GCN and GIN (both using plain SpMM); attention-based models (GAT) with softmax-normalized sparse patterns, or models with heterogeneous graph types, are not tested. - The random forest model selection over alternative ML models (XGBoost, gradient boosting) is asserted rather than measured, leaving open whether a simpler lookup table over SR and PR thresholds would achieve comparable accuracy. Bottom Line Practitioners building GPU-accelerated GNN systems will find ParamSpMM directly adoptable — the PCSR abstraction is clean, the code is public, and the 1.92× cuSPARSE speedup is measured on a realistic and diverse benchmark — but the single-architecture scope and missing ablations mean further validation is needed before deploying across GPU generations.
Search Results 2605.15695 PDF
#110 — Innovative (61)

Artifact-Bench: Evaluating MLLMs on Detecting and Assessing the Artifacts of AI-Generated Videos (21 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 20 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Artifact-Bench, a 1,100-sample benchmark with a three-level, 30-type artifact taxonomy and three progressively challenging evaluation tasks for assessing MLLMs on AI-generated video artifact detection and diagnosis. - Why it's important: MLLMs are increasingly proposed as automated judges and reward providers for video generative models, but no prior work rigorously tests whether they actually perceive the fine-grained realism artifacts that make videos look synthetic — a gap that matters directly for whether RLHF-style video optimization can rely on MLLM feedback. - How it does it: A hybrid data-construction pipeline (real video + captioning-driven AIGC generation + targeted artifact synthesis) paired with triple-annotator consensus annotation and difficulty stratification yields three tasks — binary authenticity classification, pairwise realism comparison, and multi-label artifact identification — evaluated against a human expert ceiling. - The good: Human experts score 87.7 overall against the best MLLM (Gemini 3.1 Pro) at 47.5, with all 19 models scoring below 10% on the fine-grained Artifact Identification task versus 80.3% for humans — a stark and consistent gap across diverse model families. - The missing: Answer extraction for all 19 evaluated models is performed by Gemini 3.1 Pro itself (the top-ranked model), introducing an unacknowledged evaluation bias; inter-annotator agreement is not reported; and the pairwise comparison task has only 38 hard-level (L3) samples, making model rankings at that difficulty level statistically unreliable. - Read it or not: Essential reading for anyone building MLLM-based video evaluation or reward pipelines, as the counter-intuitive finding that thinking-enhanced (chain-of-thought) variants do not consistently outperform instruction-tuned counterparts is directly actionable for system design.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. The headline finding — all 19 models below 10% on fine-grained artifact identification versus 80.3% for humans, consistent across families — is a credible, practically relevant result, supporting Novelty (68) and Impact (65). Rigor (55) and Resilience (55) are what hold the composite down: using Gemini 3.1 Pro as the answer extractor for all 19 models including itself is an unaddressed conflict of interest that could distort rankings, inter-annotator agreement is never reported, the human ceiling rests on only 4 experts, and the PVRC L3 split has just 38 samples (a 5-point gap = 2 samples) with no confidence intervals — combined with preprint status, the comparative claims are noise-vulnerable. Weighted: 0.20*68 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*62 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*65 ≈ 61.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 62 Resilience 55 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: AI-generated video models (Veo 3, Kling, WAN, LTX-2) produce increasingly realistic content but retain characteristic failure modes: temporal inconsistencies, structural distortions, unnatural motion, and semantic incoherence. As MLLMs are proposed to automate quality assessment and reward signal generation for these models, a critical unanswered question is whether MLLMs actually perceive these artifacts or exploit superficial semantic shortcuts. Prior benchmarks address only a single evaluation paradigm at a time — authenticity classification (AEGIS, GenBuster-Bench), preference scoring (UVE-Bench), or artifact grounding (ViF-Bench) — and almost exclusively cover photorealistic AI-generated content, leaving animated and CG-style video unaddressed. No benchmark jointly evaluates coarse detection, relative realism ranking, and diagnostic artifact identification, preventing a principled assessment of whether model failure is perceptual, comparative, or diagnostic. Key Idea: Artifact perception in AI-generated video is a compositional, hierarchical capability: a model might succeed at coarse authenticity detection via semantic heuristics while completely failing at fine-grained artifact diagnosis. The key design insight is to probe these three levels — detection, comparison, identification — as separate tasks on shared content with calibrated difficulty, so that performance profiles distinguish models with genuine artifact-aware perception from those relying on spurious correlations. Methodology: The benchmark is built on a hand-designed three-level artifact taxonomy: three top-level domains (Surface Artifacts, Structural Defects, Temporal-Semantic Violations) → mid-level failure families → 30 fine-grained artifact types (e.g., Feature Instability, Refraction Distortion, Irreversibility Violation, Consequence w/o Valid Action). Data construction uses a hybrid pipeline: real videos are captioned by Gemini 3.1 Pro; captions drive generation via multiple video models. Task 1 (RVAC, 500 QA pairs): real video paired with semantically aligned AIGC counterpart. Task 2 (PVRC, 250 QA pairs): pairs of AIGC videos with comparable semantics but different realism achieved via re-generation and prompt-consistent sampling. Task 3 (AID, 350 QA pairs): combination of online-collected AIGC videos and targeted generation from prompts designed to expose specific rare artifact types; each example is a multi-answer multiple-choice with 6 candidates drawn from the taxonomy, with distractors selected from semantically adjacent failure families. Each sample is independently annotated by 3 expert annotators; consensus is required for acceptance; non-consensus samples proceed to 2 additional reviewers plus expert verification. Samples are stratified into difficulty levels L1 (clear artifacts/easy distinction) through L3 (hard to distinguish). 19 MLLMs are evaluated at 5 fps; free-form model outputs are parsed by Gemini 3.1 Pro using a structured answer-extraction prompt. The three critical design decisions are: (1) requiring multi-label selection in AID to prevent single-answer shortcutting — well-justified; (2) restricting distractors to semantically related artifact types to prevent coarse category elimination — reasonable but the selection procedure is not fully described; (3) using Gemini 3.1 Pro for answer extraction — not well-justified given that Gemini is also the top-evaluated model. Key Results: Human experts score 93.6% on RVAC, 86.4% on PVRC, and 80.3% on AID, for an 87.7 overall average. Gemini 3.1 Pro leads all models at 47.5 overall (RVAC 74.0%, PVRC 48.6%, AID 9.8%). VideoVeritas 8B reaches 46.0 overall with the strongest PVRC at 53.1%. All 19 models achieve below 10% average accuracy on AID; Gemini 3.1 Pro and VideoVeritas 8B both achieve 7.8% AID average. On the binary tasks RVAC and PVRC (50% random baseline), most models barely surpass chance, particularly at difficulty levels L2 and L3. Specialized models designed for AIGC detection (Skyra, BusterX++) do not consistently outperform general-purpose models. Thinking-enhanced variants (Qwen3-VL 32B-Thinking, MiMo-VL 7B-RL, Skyra 7B-RL) do not consistently outperform their instruction-tuned counterparts and in several cases underperform them. Model accuracy does not decrease monotonically from L1 to L3 as human performance does, and in some cases models score comparably or higher on harder subsets, indicating reliance on shortcuts rather than genuine artifact-aware perception. Experiments were conducted on 4 machines each with 8 NVIDIA H800 GPUs. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The three-task progressive evaluation framework spanning detection, comparison, and diagnostic identification, combined with the 30-type artifact taxonomy applied to non-photorealistic content (anime, CG), is the genuine addition over prior work. The finding that chain-of-thought reasoning does not improve artifact detection is a new empirical result with direct practical implications. Data construction and annotation methodology are competent applications of established benchmark-building practice. Rigor & Reproducibility: Annotation uses triple-annotator consensus plus expert verification, which is above average for a benchmark paper, but inter-annotator agreement statistics (Cohen's κ or Fleiss' κ) are not reported — an omission that leaves ground-truth reliability unverified, particularly for fine-grained AID labels. No significance tests or confidence intervals are reported on accuracy figures; with only 38 L3 samples in PVRC, observed accuracy differences between models at that difficulty level are statistically unreliable. The human baseline draws on only 4 expert annotators — thin for establishing a robust ceiling. Answer extraction via Gemini 3.1 Pro introduces a potential circularity (the extraction model is also the top-ranked evaluated model) that is neither acknowledged nor ablated; a neutral parser or a model outside the evaluation set would have been preferable. Code and benchmark data are indicated as available at https://github.com/FrankYang-17/Artifact-Bench. This is a preprint; results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (benchmark evaluation study). - Practical: All 19 models run on commercially available H800 GPUs; the benchmark itself requires only video inference, so any lab with GPU access can replicate. - Efficient: Inference cost is not reported; frame resizing was applied to some models to handle context limits, which may reduce accuracy for high-resolution artifacts. - Effective: The benchmark detects a real and large MLLM capability gap (up to 70+ percentage points on AID vs. humans), establishing it measures a meaningful skill rather than a proxy; whether improving benchmark scores translates to better real-world video judges is not tested. - Scalable: The dataset has 1,100 QA pairs — adequate for initial characterization but small for fine-grained subgroup analysis; the authors acknowledge this and propose future expansion. The paper delivers a benchmark artifact (dataset, task definitions, prompt templates, evaluation code) rather than a deployable model or library. Strengths: - The MLLM-human gap on AID (all models below 10% vs. 80.3% for humans) is large, consistent across 19 diverse models, and directly challenges the use of current MLLMs as video quality judges — a finding with immediate practical implications for RLHF-style video optimization pipelines. - The counter-intuitive finding that thinking-enhanced (chain-of-thought) model variants do not consistently improve over instruction-tuned counterparts is well-supported across multiple model families and is not a trivial result. - Broader domain coverage (photorealistic + animated + CG) than all five prior benchmarks in the comparison table is a genuine differentiator, particularly since animated and CG videos are common targets for AIGC detection in real applications. - The difficulty stratification (L1–L3) with monotonic human performance degradation provides a rare calibration signal that exposes model shortcut behavior (non-monotonic MLLM accuracy). Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note that the number of human experts (4) and the dataset scale are limited due to resource constraints, and they commit to expanding both in future work. They acknowledge models may rely on semantic priors rather than perceptual artifact reasoning, but they do not propose mechanisms to diagnose or address this. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Gemini 3.1 Pro is used as the answer-extraction parser for all 19 evaluated models, including itself, which is a methodological conflict of interest. If Gemini's parsing is biased toward extracting answers consistent with its own generation priors, rankings could be distorted. This is not acknowledged. - PVRC L3 contains only 38 samples; a 5-percentage-point difference between models at this level corresponds to just 2 samples, making within-task model rankings at L3 noise-dominated without confidence intervals. - No inter-annotator agreement statistics for the 30 fine-grained AID labels — some artifact types (e.g., Commonsense Failure vs. Action-Context Mismatch) are semantically overlapping; without agreement data, ground-truth reliability is unclear. - No fine-tuning experiments: it is unknown whether exposing any of the 19 models to even a small number of AID examples closes the gap, which would distinguish a fundamental perceptual limitation from a task-format unfamiliarity problem. - The benchmark construction for Task 1 (RVAC) uses Gemini 3.1 Pro to caption real videos before generating AIGC counterparts — if Gemini-generated captions bias the AIGC content toward distributions Gemini recognizes, RVAC accuracy for Gemini is inflated. - No error analysis characterizing whether model failures on AID stem from perceptual blindness to the artifact versus correct perception but wrong category label — a distinction critical for understanding what capability needs improvement. Bottom Line: A practitioner designing MLLM-based video evaluation or reward pipelines should read this paper — the near-zero fine-grained AID accuracy across all 19 models, including the best proprietary systems with chain-of-thought reasoning, is a specific and credible warning that current MLLMs cannot reliably serve as diagnostic judges for AI-generated video realism.
Recommended 2605.18984 PDF
#111 — Innovative (60)

Seg-Agent: Test-Time Multimodal Reasoning for Training-Free Language-Guided Segmentation

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Seg-Agent, a training-free language-guided segmentation framework that uses an explicit multimodal generate-select-refine loop — rendering candidate bounding boxes visually on the image via Set-of-Mark prompting — to guide SAM2 without any parameter updates. - Why it's important: Competing methods (LISA, Seg-Zero) require expensive supervised fine-tuning or GRPO training on large datasets, are tightly coupled to their training distribution, and cannot instantly absorb newer, stronger foundation models; Seg-Agent eliminates all of these costs. - How it does it: The MLLM proposes bounding boxes over augmented image views, NMS filters candidates, those candidates are rendered as numbered overlays (Set-of-Mark) on the original image, the MLLM selects the best one visually, then refines its boundaries against another SoM-overlaid image before passing the result to SAM2. - The good: Seg-Agent-7B matches the best training-based competitor (Seg-Zero-7B) essentially exactly on ReasonSeg Test gIoU (57.6 vs 57.5) and edges it on Various-LangSeg overall gIoU (70.6 vs 70.0), with zero training. - The missing: No concrete inference-latency measurements are reported despite the paper's claim that total token cost is comparable to Seg-Zero, and the Various-LangSeg benchmark (244 samples, 40 of which are reasoning-guided) is too small to draw statistically reliable conclusions. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building training-free segmentation pipelines on top of MLLMs, particularly those seeking a plug-in upgrade path as foundation models improve; less essential for researchers focused on pushing raw segmentation accuracy with supervised methods.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. The visual generate-select-refine loop is a non-obvious (70) and principled exploitation of what MLLMs do well (visual comparison) over what they do poorly (coordinate regression), and matching GRPO-trained Seg-Zero at zero training cost gives it real practical appeal (Impact 65). Rigor (50) is the limiter: no latency/FLOPs measurements despite an explicit cost-parity claim, the 244-sample Various-LangSeg benchmark makes 1–2 gIoU differences indistinguishable from noise, code is promised but unreleased, and N augmentations are unspecified, preventing replication. Resilience (55) suffers because the headline multi-scenario advantage rests on ~40 reasoning samples with no significance testing. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*50 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*62 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*65 = 60.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 62 Resilience 55 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Language-guided segmentation allows arbitrary target regions to be segmented from natural language queries, overcoming the fixed-category constraint of classical semantic segmentation. The dominant two-stage paradigm — MLLM for bounding-box generation, SAM for mask production — suffers because off-the-shelf MLLMs have weak spatial grounding, producing low-quality visual prompts when queried directly. Two remedies exist: (1) fine-tune the MLLM on task-specific data (LISA, SAM4MLLM), or (2) post-train the MLLM with reinforcement learning to reason in the text domain before emitting coordinates (Seg-Zero with GRPO). Both are expensive, require large curated datasets, and cannot leverage newer base models without retraining. The open gap is achieving high-quality visual prompts at test time without any parameter updates. Key Idea The core insight is that spatial grounding errors — the MLLM predicting coordinates that miss the target — can be caught and corrected by showing the MLLM its own candidate boxes rendered visually on the image, because visual comparison of candidate regions against the target description is a task MLLMs handle well, whereas pure text-coordinate reasoning lacks this grounding signal. Feeding candidates back as Set-of-Mark image overlays transforms an open-loop coordinate-prediction problem into a closed-loop visual verification-and-refinement problem, without any training. Methodology The pipeline has four sequential stages. (1) Generation: N augmented views of the input image (flip, scale, and others not fully enumerated) are each paired with the language instruction and fed to Qwen2.5-VL, which predicts one bounding box per view. (2) Selection: Augmentation-inverse transforms bring all boxes into the original image coordinate frame; NMS with IoU threshold 0.8 filters duplicates; remaining candidates are rendered as numbered overlays using Set-of-Mark prompting; the MLLM receives the SoM image plus a selection prompt and identifies the best candidate by index. (3) Refinement: The selected box is again overlaid on the image via SoM; the MLLM receives a refinement prompt and outputs adjusted coordinates (expand, shrink, translate) for better boundary alignment. (4) Segmentation: The refined box is passed as a spatial prompt to SAM2-Large, which produces the final pixel-level binary mask. Critical design decisions: (a) SoM visual feedback — well-justified by prior work showing SoM improves MLLM spatial grounding; (b) augmentation-based multi-hypothesis generation — plausible for robustness but the number N and exact augmentation set are not specified in the main text; (c) three-stage decomposition over single-shot prompting — directly validated by the ablation in Table 4. Key Results On referring segmentation (cIoU, explicit semantic), Seg-Agent-7B scores 79.9 / 76.0 / 72.2 on RefCOCO testA / RefCOCO+ testA / RefCOCOg test, versus Seg-Zero-7B (training-based SOTA) at 80.3 / 76.2 / 72.6 — a gap of under 0.5 cIoU points across all splits. On ReasonSeg (reasoning-guided), Seg-Agent-7B achieves 61.7 / 61.2 gIoU/cIoU on Val and 57.6 / 51.8 on Test, versus Seg-Zero-7B's 62.6 / 62.0 and 57.5 / 52.0 — statistically indistinguishable on Test gIoU. On Various-LangSeg (244-sample multi-scenario benchmark), Seg-Agent-7B scores 70.6 gIoU overall versus Seg-Zero-7B's 70.0, with Reasoning-Guided at 75.2 vs 74.5. Ablation on Various-LangSeg shows the full three-stage pipeline gains +2.4 gIoU overall and +5.1 gIoU on Reasoning-Guided tasks relative to the single-step Qwen2.5-VL-7B + SAM2-L baseline. Cross-model ablation (Table 5) confirms the method improves over direct inference for both InternVL3-8B and Qwen2.5-VL-7B backbones. Baselines include current and strong methods (Seg-Zero 2025, GRPO-trained); all results are reported as preprint-quality numbers without peer review. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The generate-select-refine visual feedback loop applied at test time to language-guided segmentation is a novel combination, though each individual component — Set-of-Mark prompting, augmentation ensembling, NMS, SAM2 prompting — is drawn from prior work. The key novelty is the closed-loop visual verification paradigm for spatial grounding without training. Various-LangSeg contributes a new (small) multi-scenario benchmark unifying ESS, GOS, and RGS evaluation. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations at the module level (Table 4) and cross-backbone level (Table 5) are present and informative. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed runs are reported. The benchmark Various-LangSeg has 244 samples (40 reasoning, 64 generic, 140 explicit); differences of 1–2 gIoU points at this scale are likely within noise, yet the paper draws conclusions from them. Code is promised at github.com/Fanye12/Seg-Agent but was not available at preprint submission. The number of augmentations N and the exact augmentation set are not specified, preventing exact replication. No concrete latency or throughput measurements are provided despite a claim of comparable inference cost to Seg-Zero. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.12953v1); results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (method evaluated via segmentation experiments). - Practical: Runs end-to-end on a single NVIDIA RTX 4090 24 GB using commercially available Qwen2.5-VL (via local deployment or API) and SAM2-Large; no specialized hardware required. - Efficient: Requires 3–5 MLLM forward passes per image versus one for naive prompting; the paper asserts total token count is comparable to Seg-Zero's text CoT but provides no wall-clock or FLOPs measurements to support this claim. - Effective: Ablations confirm each stage contributes measurably, and the method matches GRPO-trained Seg-Zero on all reported benchmarks; the evidence is consistent but comes from a single small benchmark (Various-LangSeg) for the multi-scenario claim. - Scalable: Tested only on standard benchmark scales (images up to standard COCO resolution, single objects per query); performance on high-resolution images, dense multi-object scenes, or video is not evaluated or claimed. The work delivers a software pipeline (code promised, not yet released) rather than a trained model artifact. Strengths - The visual feedback loop is a principled and practical solution to the MLLM spatial grounding problem: it exploits what MLLMs are good at (visual comparison) rather than what they are bad at (direct coordinate regression), and the ablations confirm this mechanistically. - Cross-model generalization (Table 5) demonstrates the framework is genuinely model-agnostic: it improves InternVL3 and two versions of SAM alongside Qwen2.5-VL, validating the modularity claim. - The paper is unusually candid about the latency tradeoff (more MLLM calls) and directly compares token count against Seg-Zero's text CoT — even if the claim needs quantification, the acknowledgment itself is a credibility positive. - Various-LangSeg, despite being small, is the first unified benchmark covering explicit, generic, and reasoning-guided segmentation in a single coherent testbed, addressing a real evaluation gap in the field. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) multiple MLLM calls introduce additional latency versus single-step approaches; (2) Various-LangSeg is modest in size (244 samples), framed as a deliberate coverage-over-scale choice with a V2 planned; (3) performance is inherently dependent on the underlying foundation model quality, though this is reframed as a feature rather than a flaw. The authors confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No wall-clock latency or FLOPs measurements are reported; the claim that inference cost is comparable to Seg-Zero's text CoT is a token-count argument that ignores prefill cost, image encoding overhead, and the multiple forward passes — this must be measured to be credible. - The number of augmentations N and their specific types are not stated in the main text, making exact replication impossible; this is a reproducibility gap the authors do not acknowledge. - Various-LangSeg's 40 reasoning-guided samples are too few to support the +0.7 gIoU advantage over Seg-Zero-7B as a reliable finding; no significance tests are reported. - Several reasoning-guided prompts (e.g., "the first lady of US") test world-knowledge retrieval as much as visual grounding; this conflates two distinct capabilities and is not acknowledged as a benchmark design limitation. - All ablations and comparisons use Qwen2.5-VL as both the generator and the selector/refiner; a cross-MLLM ablation (different model for generation vs. selection) is not explored, leaving open whether the gains derive from the visual feedback mechanism or simply from asking the same strong model twice. - Evaluation is limited to single-object, single-query scenarios; performance on ambiguous multi-object queries (where multiple objects could satisfy the instruction) is entirely unaddressed. Bottom Line Practitioners building on top of MLLMs who need language-guided segmentation without training overhead will find Seg-Agent's generate-select-refine loop directly adoptable and immediately beneficial, as it consistently matches GRPO-trained competitors at zero training cost on every benchmark tested.
Search Results 2605.12953 PDF
#112 — Innovative (60)

F-GRPO: Factorized Group-Relative Policy Optimization for Unified Candidate Generation and Ranking

Rohan Surana, Gagan Mundada, Junda Wu, Xintong Li, Yizhu Jiao, Bowen Jin, Sizhe Zhou, Tong Yu, Ritwik Sinha, Jiawei Han, Jingbo Shang, Julian McAuley · arxiv.org · Julian McAuley - new articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces F-GRPO, a training framework that factorizes a single LLM's autoregressive rollout into a candidate-slate generation phase and a reranking phase, each receiving its own group-relative policy gradient signal rather than a shared combined reward. - Why it's important: Standard GRPO and decoupled pipeline approaches both suffer from credit assignment failures — the former conflates coverage and ordering errors, the latter introduces train/test distribution mismatch — making it hard to improve both precision and recall together in list-to-rank tasks. - How it does it: Within one autoregressive pass (delimited by `<SLATE>...</SLATE><RANK>...</RANK>` tags), the method computes two independent Dr. GRPO advantages — one from an order-invariant recall reward on the slate, one from NDCG@k on the final ranked list — and applies each only to its corresponding token subsequence. - The good: A formal gradient decomposition (Proposition A.1) proves that the two loss terms share no cross-phase reward contamination at the expansion point, and training dynamics (Figure 2b) empirically confirm the predicted phase ordering: the slate generator matures ~50 steps before the ranker, exactly as the conditional policy structure implies. - The missing: All experiments are confined to 20-candidate pools and 2B–4B models, so neither the behavior at realistic retrieval pool sizes (hundreds to thousands of candidates) nor the generalization to 7B+ models is evaluated; gains over plain GRPO are also inconsistent for the 2B model on MovieLens at @3. - Read it or not: IR and recommendation researchers working on RL-based LLM training should read it; the credit-assignment decomposition and factorized-advantage idea apply broadly to any multi-stage LLM output structure, but practitioners needing evidence at production scale should treat results as preliminary.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. The composite is pulled down by Rigor (55) and Practical Impact (45): no code or checkpoints are released, the key ablations run on only one dataset/model (LastFM, Qwen3.5-2B), there are no error bars or multiple seeds, the MuSiQue test split is non-standard, and the reranker comparison is in-domain RL versus zero-shot baselines. Resilience (55) and Completeness (58) also suffer because the 2B-scale regression on MovieLens is left unexplained and the fixed 20-candidate pool leaves production-scale behavior untested. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (70) are credited for the genuinely new factorized group-relative advantage with a formal first-order gradient-separability proof. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*58 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*45 = 60.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 58 Resilience 55 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Retrieval and recommendation systems face a coupled list-to-rank problem: identify a set of relevant items and order them so the best appear first. Traditional multi-stage pipelines (retriever → reranker) fix this by training separate components, but each optimizes a proxy objective in isolation, creating distribution mismatch at inference: the reranker sees gold-generated slates during training but model-generated slates at test time. When an LLM is used end-to-end — either as a black-box reranker or as a direct generator+ranker in one pass — a different problem emerges: a single scalar reward applied to the full output can't distinguish whether poor performance came from the model failing to surface the right candidates or from ranking an adequate slate incorrectly. This credit assignment gap makes training unstable and sample-inefficient because the coverage objective (order-invariant) and the ranking objective (position-sensitive) pull the gradient in structurally different directions. Key Idea The core insight is that a single autoregressive sequence can be structurally partitioned into a slate phase and a rank phase, and GRPO's group-relative advantage can be computed independently per partition using phase-specific rewards. Rather than one combined scalar advantage applied uniformly to all tokens, separate mean-subtracted advantages (Â_slate = R_slate − R̄_slate; Â_rank = R_rank − R̄_rank) are applied only to their respective token spans. This gives the slate generator a pure coverage gradient and the ranker a pure ordering gradient, even though both update the same shared LLM parameters. Methodology The joint policy factorizes as π(τ,σ|x) = π_slate(τ|x) · π_rank(σ|x,τ), with both sub-policies realized by a single autoregressive LLM. During a rollout, the model emits a candidate slate inside `<SLATE>` tags and then a permutation of that slate inside `<RANK>` tags. Delimiter tokens participate in the forward pass (for correct autoregressive conditioning) but are masked from the loss. The slate reward is recall over gold items (an order-invariant count normalized to [0,1]); the rank reward is NDCG@k over the reordered list. G=8 rollouts per prompt are sampled; Dr. GRPO normalization (no standard-deviation denominator) is used. Three malformed-output cases are handled via format penalties. A rank subset validation step penalizes out-of-slate hallucination. Combined loss: L = L_slate + λ·L_rank (λ=1.0), with βKL=0. A formal first-order gradient decomposition (Proposition A.1) proves that at θ=θ_old the gradients decouple completely, with no cross-phase reward information in either term — this is the formal justification for the design. Key design decisions: (1) recall vs. F1 as slate reward — well-justified empirically (Figure 2a shows F1 saturates at k=3 due to precision penalty, recall improves monotonically); (2) slate size n=10 and λ=1.0 — validated by ablation (Figure 4); (3) SFT warm-start for all RL runs — justified by format sparsity without it, though one condition (Qwen3.5-2B on QA) starts from pretrained directly. Key Results On sequential recommendation (MovieLens, LastFM), Qwen3-4B F-GRPO achieves LastFM Recall@5=81.7% vs. GRPO 73.9% (+10.6% relative), and LastFM Recall@3=72.4% vs. SFT 47.1% (+53.7% relative). MovieLens Recall@3: F-GRPO 53.6% vs. GRPO 44.4% (+20.7% relative). For Qwen3-4B on multi-hop QA, HotpotQA NDCG@3: F-GRPO 93.3% vs. GRPO 93.0% (negligible difference); MuSiQue Recall@3: F-GRPO 71.3% vs. GRPO 63.0% (+13.2% relative). The F-GRPO Qwen3-4B model outperforms all four dedicated rerankers (RankZephyr 7B, MonoT5 3B, DuoT5 3B, LiT5 3B) on HotpotQA across all cutoffs and beats MonoT5 on MuSiQue at @1 and @3. A counter-intuitive finding: Qwen3.5-2B F-GRPO underperforms GRPO on MovieLens Recall@3 (45.8% vs. 50.7%) and Recall@1 (21.5% vs. 24.4%), suggesting factorized credit assignment does not uniformly help at 2B scale. The decoupled SFT (full) baseline consistently underperforms single-model SFT on all tasks, directly confirming the train/test distribution mismatch hypothesis. Error attribution (Table 9, LastFM) shows slate miss 8.9% and rank drop 9.4% — errors balanced across phases, validating that both need independent signals. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The genuinely novel contribution is applying separate group-relative advantages to different token subsequences within the same autoregressive rollout and proving first-order gradient separability. The two-phase autoregressive formulation itself (generate a slate, then rank it) is a natural extension of existing zero-shot prompting strategies; the credit assignment decomposition and its formal justification are what distinguish this from straightforward GRPO with a combined reward. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present for the two most important hyperparameters (λ and slate size n) and for the slate reward formulation (recall vs. F1), though these are run only on LastFM with Qwen3.5-2B. Error attribution (Table 9) provides principled validation of the factorization hypothesis. Phase separation dynamics (Figure 2b) and precision-recall redistribution (Figure 3) support the design narrative with training-curve evidence. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported for the main results; greedy decoding (T=0, single prediction) is used for evaluation, which removes decoding variance but also limits stochasticity analysis. Code is not released in the preprint; no model weights or dataset preprocessing scripts are mentioned as publicly available. The MuSiQue test set is constructed from the official validation split (200 val, 2000 test from a 2,417-sample validation partition) — a nonstandard construction that could inflate comparisons to published baselines using the standard split. The reranker baselines (MonoT5, RankZephyr) are MS MARCO-trained and applied zero-shot, while F-GRPO is trained in-domain on the same task distribution; the paper acknowledges this but frames it as showing F-GRPO's in-domain RL is competitive, not as a limitation of the comparison. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Runs on standard GPU hardware at 2B–4B parameter scale with BF16 precision; inference requires no architectural changes relative to standard GRPO — the only addition is the two-tag structured output format. - Efficient: Training cost is equivalent to standard GRPO (same G=8 rollouts, same backward pass); the factorization adds no computational overhead, only a change in how advantages are assigned. The two-pass rerankers (DuoT5) at O(N²) complexity are more expensive at inference. - Effective: Evidence is credible for the 4B model on the tested tasks: gains over GRPO are consistent at k≥3 cutoffs where coverage compounds with ordering. The 2B model shows mixed results (MovieLens @3 regresses), indicating the method's effectiveness depends on backbone capacity. - Scalable: Not evaluated beyond 4B parameters or candidate pools larger than 20. The paper claims the approach scales to larger models but provides no evidence. Whether factorized credit assignment remains necessary as model size increases (larger models may more naturally separate coverage from ordering) is not tested. The work delivers a training recipe (algorithm + loss formulation) and empirical results; no software library, dataset, or trained model checkpoint is released in the preprint. Strengths - The formal gradient decomposition (Proposition A.1) is a genuine intellectual contribution: it shows the factorization is not just an engineering heuristic but achieves first-order gradient separability, providing theoretical grounding the related work lacks. - The experimental design isolates exactly the right variable: the GRPO baseline uses an identical setup in every respect except that it collapses the two advantages into one, making the credit assignment effect the sole measured difference. - Phase separation dynamics (slate generator reaching 90% peak by step 150, ranker by step 200) provide independent, interpretable evidence that the conditional policy structure is being learned in the expected order. - The decoupled SFT ablation — showing that separately trained selector+ranker underperforms a single jointly-trained model — directly validates the train/test distribution mismatch hypothesis that motivates the work. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The paper restricts experiments to 2B–4B models explicitly citing compute constraints, noting this limits extrapolation to larger scales. The authors acknowledge that the QA reranker comparison is not apples-to-apples (in-domain RL vs. out-of-domain rerankers), framing it as a favorable signal rather than a fair benchmark. LLM assistance in drafting and verifying theoretical proofs is disclosed. The authors do not discuss the non-uniform results for Qwen3.5-2B on MovieLens where GRPO outperforms F-GRPO at @1 and @3. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The candidate pool is fixed at 20 throughout; practical recommendation and retrieval systems operate over hundreds to thousands of candidates. Whether factorized credit assignment helps or hurts (e.g., due to longer slate tokens) at pool sizes of 100+ is not studied. - The QA reranker comparison is structurally unfair in a way that understates the gap: MonoT5 and RankZephyr are domain-general models applied zero-shot to a task they weren't tuned for, while F-GRPO is fine-tuned on 10,000 in-domain examples — the comparison does not show F-GRPO is a better reranking method, only that it benefits more from in-domain supervision. - The MuSiQue test partition is carved from the official validation set rather than using a separate held-out set, making it impossible to compare against published results using the standard split. - The 2B model regression on MovieLens (@3: GRPO 50.7% vs. F-GRPO 45.8%) is not discussed or analyzed. If factorized credit assignment actually hurts at small scale, this constrains the method's applicability range. - No comparison to DPO-based listwise alternatives (e.g., IRPO, which trains on the same task domain with a different objective) at matched model scale, which would help position F-GRPO relative to offline preference methods. - The SFT warm-start uses gold slates with randomly sampled distractors; the sensitivity of RL training to this warm-start distribution is not ablated, yet the decoupled SFT failure is partly attributed to gold-slate conditioning. Bottom Line A practitioner working on RL training of LLMs for list-to-rank tasks gains a concrete, theoretically justified technique — factorized group-relative advantages for structured two-phase rollouts — that is cheap to implement and shows reliable gains at the 4B scale where coverage is the bottleneck, but should treat the 2B scale and large candidate pool regimes as open questions before adopting it.
Recommended 2605.12995 PDF
#113 — Innovative (60)

From Context to Skills: Can Language Models Learn from Context Skillfully?

Shuzheng Si, Haozhe Zhao, Yu Lei, Qingyi Wang, Dingwei Chen, Zhitong Wang, Zhenhailong Wang, Kangyang Luo, Zheng Wang, Gang Chen, Fanchao Qi, Minjia Zhang, Maosong Sun · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
