At individual pipeline stages, the fraction addressing both edge and multilingual concerns is low: 11% at data collection, 23% at post-training, 22% at inference, 38% at pretraining, 44% at evaluation. Only at the full-stack level does joint coverage reach 43%. Edge-only papers are overwhelmingly monolingual: 25/29 single-language papers target edge deployment alone; no edge-only paper covers more than 10 languages. In contrast, 15 multilingual-focused papers cover 50+ languages. Model families have shifted toward smaller variants: from 2023 onward, most families (Qwen2.5, Gemma 3, Phi series) offer ≤8B variants. Among 36 deployed systems: academia is most represented (52 author affiliations), government participation is limited (10); deployments cluster near model compression and dialog dataset clusters in embedding space; domains with broadest method coverage are healthcare and social; legal and agriculture rely on fewer, more generic techniques (synthetic data, SFT, LoRA). A key counter-intuitive finding: the methods most prevalent in real deployments (quantization, synthetic data generation, SFT) are not the methods receiving the most research attention (reasoning performance, prompt compression). Novelty Classification NEW FORMULATION + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The joint framing of edge and multilingual constraints as structurally competing along a shared pipeline is a new synthesis, not merely two existing surveys stapled together. The empirical finding that the fields remain siloed at individual pipeline stages, with the extent of siloization quantified per stage, is original. The survey dataset (232 annotated papers with deployment classification) is a reusable resource. What is not novel: the individual challenges at each stage are well-documented in prior surveys (Treviso et al., 2023 for edge; Liu et al., 2025a for multilingual); the paper's contribution is the joint framing and cross-analysis, not new techniques. Rigor & Reproducibility Annotation methodology is substantially reproducible: search keywords (Figure 9), LM annotation prompt (Figure 10), and the full survey dataset are publicly released (ljvmiranda921/multilinguality-at-the-edge). Human validation is described but inter-annotator agreement is not reported — a gap for a survey whose core claims depend on the reliability of paper classification. Citation-threshold filtering is a defensible heuristic but may systematically exclude community-led or grey literature directly relevant to the Global South. The LM-assisted annotation step is partially validated by human correction but the error rate before correction is not quantified, leaving uncertainty about annotation quality for borderline papers. No statistical significance tests are applied to the distributional findings (e.g., the claim that full-stack papers show more joint coverage than stage-specific papers is reported as a percentage observation, not tested). The preprint is not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) This is a SURVEY paper; the primary artifact is a curated, annotated corpus rather than a deployable system. Applying the EMPIRICAL profile to the survey methodology itself: Practical — the pipeline (Semantic Scholar API + GPT 4.1 Mini annotation + MiniLM embedding) runs on standard hardware with modest API costs. Efficient — annotation at scale via LM bootstrapping with human correction is a practical approach; the total computational footprint is low. Effective — the survey methodology produces a coherent, internally consistent corpus, though completeness cannot be fully verified without an independent gold-standard corpus. Scalable — the pipeline could be rerun on expanded corpora as the literature grows, but citation thresholds would need recalibration and the human validation bottleneck limits scalability. The paper delivers a public dataset and GitHub repository; no trained model or software tool is released. Strengths - The dual-constraint framing (edge vs. multilingual requirements competing per pipeline stage) is a clean, non-obvious conceptual contribution that organizes an otherwise fragmented literature into a usable taxonomy. - The empirical analysis of 36 real-world deployments — sector collaboration patterns, domain-method networks — goes substantively beyond a literature review and yields actionable findings: government is underrepresented, deployments cluster around a narrow method set, healthcare/social show the most methodological diversity. - Transparent methodology with public dataset release and full annotation prompts in the appendix, enabling both replication and extension. - The observation that real-world deployments cluster away from active research clusters (e.g., no deployments near "prompt compression" or "reasoning performance") is a concrete, testable signal about where translation from research to practice is failing. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) socioeconomic constraints — the third factor in community AI readiness alongside data resources and digital infrastructure — are unaddressed; (2) the survey assumes a "develop centrally, deploy at edge" model, whereas communities may prefer to be active developers rather than recipients, and data sovereignty concerns are only briefly noted; (3) the scope does not fully resolve the last-mile challenge but maps its contours. The authors confront these boundaries directly in §6.1 and §6.2, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Citation-threshold filtering biases the corpus toward well-cited (often Global North academic) work, potentially underrepresenting community-led NLP efforts, preprints from practitioner organizations, and non-ACL/NeurIPS venues where Global South researchers more often publish. This is especially ironic given the paper's stated motivation. - The analysis documents which methods exist at each pipeline stage but provides no effectiveness comparison — a practitioner cannot determine from this survey whether, say, language-specific quantization calibration outperforms standard calibration on low-resource languages, because the survey does not aggregate experimental results. - Speech and multimodal modalities are mentioned but not deeply analyzed despite being critical for communities with oral traditions or no standardized written form — arguably the communities most underserved by current NLP. - The "last mile" framing implicitly treats Global South communities as deployment targets; the paper acknowledges this in the discussion but does not structurally integrate community-centered NLP work (participatory design, community annotation projects) into the main taxonomy. - No timeline analysis: the survey covers 2021–2026 but does not examine whether the edge-multilingual gap is narrowing or widening over time, which would be directly relevant to the paper's thesis. Bottom Line A well-structured survey that makes a credible empirical case for studying edge and multilingual LM development jointly, with enough original analysis of real-world deployment patterns to be more than a literature catalog — NLP researchers and system builders targeting hardware-constrained, linguistically diverse communities will find the challenge taxonomy and deployment pattern analysis directly actionable.
Recommended 2604.21637 PDF
#130 — Moderately Innovative (57)

GoLongRL: Capability-Oriented Long Context Reinforcement Learning with Multitask Alignment (51 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 20 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GoLongRL, an open-source post-training recipe combining a 23K-sample capability-oriented RLVR dataset spanning 9 task types with heterogeneous reward functions, and TMN-Reweight, a multitask advantage estimation algorithm, for improving long-context reasoning in LLMs via reinforcement learning. - Why it's important: Existing long-context RL methods construct training data almost exclusively around retrieval patterns with uniform binary rewards, leaving capabilities like summarization, graded ranking, and aggregation systematically undertrained during post-training. - How it does it: A capability taxonomy drives data construction so each of 9 task types gets its natural evaluation metric as a reward signal; TMN-Reweight decouples cross-task gradient scale correction (via task-level RMS normalization) from within-task difficulty correction (via smoothed pass-rate exponential reweighting applied asymmetrically to positive and negative advantages). - The good: With vanilla GRPO alone, the GoLongRL dataset raises the 30B model's long-context average from 60.1 to 69.8 and drives MRCR from 41.6 to 81.6—outperforming QwenLong-L1.5 with GRPO and approaching DeepSeek-R1-0528 and Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking-2507 on the same benchmarks. - The missing: TMN-Reweight is ablated only at 4B; the paper explicitly acknowledges less settled evidence at 30B, meaning the headline 30B results rely entirely on vanilla GRPO, and the +0.8 average gain attributed to TMN-Reweight at 4B has no seed-level variance estimate to confirm it exceeds subsampling noise. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building long-context post-training pipelines, particularly for the diagnosis-driven iterative dataset construction methodology, which is the paper's most transferable contribution.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile (the EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS tag is hybrid, but the central claims are empirical and the rigor gaps are decisive, so empirical weighting is the honest choice). Rigor (50) and Resilience (50) pull the composite down hardest: it is a preprint with no error bars or multiple seeds anywhere, the headline TMN-Reweight +0.8 gain comes from a single 8K subsample with individual benchmarks swinging the opposite direction, the proposed algorithm is never validated at 30B (the flagship 30B numbers use vanilla GRPO), and unexplained Table 9 discrepancies up to -10.9 could flip comparative rankings. Completeness (55) is hurt by this scope mismatch despite honest limitation acknowledgment, and Novelty (60) is moderate since TMN-Reweight recombines existing reweighting and normalization ideas and the 'data beats algorithm' finding is intuitive in hindsight; the strong open dataset/code release lifts Impact (77) but cannot offset the rigor and resilience weaknesses under empirical weighting. Weighted: 0.20*60 + 0.25*50 + 0.15*58 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*77 = 57.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 58 Completeness 55 Resilience 50 Impact 77
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Long-context reasoning over tens to hundreds of thousands of tokens is critical for multi-document analysis, RAG, and agentic workflows, yet post-training via RL remains a bottleneck even after pretraining expands context windows. Existing RL-based long-context methods (LoongRL, LongRLVR, QwenLong-L1.5) construct training data primarily around retrieval path injection—UUID chain tracking, chunk-based QA, needle-in-a-haystack—yielding narrow task coverage, artificially uniform difficulty, and reward signals collapsed to binary EM or accuracy. Capabilities such as summarization, graded ranking, structured extraction, and exhaustive retrieval consequently receive little explicit supervision. On the algorithm side, standard GRPO's per-prompt advantage normalization introduces two interacting defects when applied to heterogeneous multitask data: per-prompt σ_u distorts difficulty signals, and removing it (as in Dr. GRPO) exposes gradients to uncorrected cross-task reward scale differences, since EM, F1, NDCG, and ROUGE-L have substantially different variance profiles. Key Idea The paper's central insight is that capability breadth and reward diversity are the primary bottlenecks in long-context RL—not algorithmic sophistication. Starting from a taxonomy of long-context capabilities and assigning each task its natural evaluation metric as the reward signal (rather than forcing all tasks into binary EM) produces richer training signals that generalize better. The complementary algorithmic insight is that per-prompt normalization and cross-task scale correction are distinct problems requiring decoupled interventions: task-level normalization handles scale, and difficulty-adaptive asymmetric reweighting handles within-task difficulty bias without conflating the two. Methodology The dataset is built via a 4-phase pipeline. P1 collects two pools: ~14K samples from annotated open-source corpora (CLongEval, WikiHop, LongBench Pro, FinancialQA, etc.) and unannotated real-world documents (Project Gutenberg, arXiv CC0, PMC Open Access, multi-turn dialogues from BEAM and Oolong) for synthesis. P2 assigns each sample to exactly one of 9 task types via task-specific selection criteria. P3 constructs RLVR-ready samples through two tracks: the open-source track applies compatibility filtering and reward format standardization; the synthetic track generates QA pairs from real documents using DeepSeek-V3.2 for ≤context-window lengths and Gemini-2.5-Pro for longer sequences, then applies two-stage quality filtering (QA-pair verification by Gemini-2.5-Pro, followed by multi-model pass-rate calibration using Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-30B-A3B with G=8 rollouts, discarding samples with pass rate below 0.25 on the stronger model). P4 applies 13-gram overlap decontamination against all evaluation benchmarks and runs diagnosis-driven iterative supplementation guided by per-task benchmark analysis. The resulting 22,965 samples span T1 (EM, 7,908), T2 (Accuracy, 6,808), T3 (F1, 3,478), T4 (math_verify, 3,054), T5 (IoU, 937), T6 (SubEM, 360), T7 (NDCG, 120), T8 (Pairwise, 180), T9 (ROUGE-L, 120), covering 0.1K–256K context lengths. TMN-Reweight modifies advantage estimation in two steps. Step 1 (Task-level Mean Normalization) replaces GRPO's per-prompt denominator σ_u with σ_task(i) = √(1/|U_task| Σ σ²_u), the RMS of per-prompt standard deviations within the task—theoretically motivated by a gradient norm bound showing per-task gradient magnitude scales with √E[σ²_u]. This reduces cross-task coefficient of variation from 0.54 (Dr. GRPO) and 0.34 (GRPO) to 0.18 (TMN), placing all 9 tasks within ±15% of uniform gradient magnitude. Step 2 (difficulty-adaptive reweighting) estimates prompt difficulty via smoothed pass rate p̂ = fraction of rollouts above a convex combination μ̃_u = α·μ_u + (1-α)·μ_task (α=0.8), computes weight w = exp(0.5 − p̂), and applies it asymmetrically: positive advantages scaled by w (amplifying rare hard-positive successes, attenuating easy ones) and negative advantages scaled by 1/w (damping destabilizing hard-negative gradients, amplifying unexpected easy failures). Training uses the verl framework, batch size 128, G=16 rollouts, lr=2e-6, 10 epochs, max context 160K. Key Results At 4B scale (Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507, 8K subset): GoLongRL+GRPO achieves 62.2 average vs. 56.1 for QwenLong-L1.5+GRPO and 53.0 for the untrained base—a +6.1 point gap attributable purely to data. GoLongRL+TMN-Reweight reaches 63.0, with CorpusQA +4.5 over GRPO (69.6 vs. 65.1) and LBV2 +1.6, though MRCR slightly favors GRPO (67.5 vs. 65.5). At 30B scale (full 23K): GoLongRL+GRPO achieves 69.8 average with MRCR at 81.6, outperforming QwenLong-L1.5+GRPO (67.2, MRCR 66.9) and approaching QwenLong-L1.5+AEPO (71.2, MRCR 82.5). The 30B model matches DeepSeek-R1-0528 (68.67) and Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking-2507 (68.45) on the aggregate. General capability retention: TMN-Reweight at 4B improves MMLU-Pro (+0.7), AIME24 (+1.6), AIME25 (+0.6), GPQA-Diamond (+2.3); LongMemEval improves +13.6 at both scales. Length extrapolation: 30B MRCR 128K–512K improves from 24.91 to 37.52, and MRCR 512K–1M from 3.69 to 9.14, despite training only to 160K. Dataset iteration shows V2→V3 (+5.2K targeted samples) yielded +7.2 average improvement vs. +2.2 from the undifferentiated V1→V2 expansion of 8.1K samples. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET: A 23K RLVR dataset with 9 heterogeneous task types and reward functions, openly released. NEW METHOD: TMN-Reweight for multitask advantage estimation in heterogeneous RLVR. NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING: Data capability coverage and reward diversity dominate algorithmic choice in long-context RL—vanilla GRPO on GoLongRL data outperforms QwenLong-L1.5's specialized AEPO algorithm at 4B. The TMN-Reweight algorithm recombines task-level normalization (related to QwenLong-L1.5's approach) with difficulty-adaptive reweighting (related to HA-DW and F-GRPO) but addresses their specific interaction in the heterogeneous multitask setting, which is genuinely new. Rigor & Reproducibility The data contribution is cleanly isolated by holding the algorithm fixed (vanilla GRPO) across comparison groups, and the algorithm contribution is isolated by holding the dataset fixed. The four-stage dataset construction pipeline is documented in detail including generation prompts (Appendix B) and QA-pair verification criteria. Iterative refinement is reported version-by-version (Table 4), with clear causal attribution of which data additions fixed which benchmark deficits. TMN-Reweight's hyperparameter α is ablated over four values (Table 6). Decontamination via 13-gram overlap is applied. The full dataset and training code are open-sourced. Critical gap: no error bars or multiple random seeds are reported for any experiment; all 4B experiments use a single 8K random subsample, so the TMN-Reweight +0.8 gain has no variance estimate. The 30B TMN-Reweight condition is not evaluated—only GRPO at 30B. Some baseline numbers are reproduced from the QwenLong-L1.5 paper rather than self-evaluated; Table 9 reveals discrepancies up to −9.7 (MRCR for 30B base) and −10.9 (Memory-Rec_Sum), which are not explained and could affect the comparative claims. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS (a built training recipe with open-released artifacts, evaluated on downstream benchmarks). - Practical: The pipeline runs on commercially available GPU clusters; the open-source codebase uses verl and standard GRPO infrastructure. Training at 30B MoE scale requires significant compute but is within reach of research institutions. - Efficient: Training compute is not reported (no GPU hours or wall-clock times given). Using GRPO avoids a separate value network, reducing memory relative to PPO, but 160K context with 16 rollouts per prompt at 30B scale is expensive. The paper is silent on cost. - Effective: Evidence is strong—clear gains on 6 diverse long-context benchmarks, plus out-of-distribution transfer to agentic and dialogue memory tasks not seen during training, and length extrapolation beyond the 160K training context to 512K–1M sequences. - Scalable: Tested at 4B and 30B; gains are consistent across scales on the data contribution. TMN-Reweight scalability beyond 4B is explicitly left as future work. No experiments beyond 30B. - Software artifact: Full dataset, training code, and construction pipeline released at https://github.com/xiaoxuanNLP/GoLongRL. Strengths - The finding that capability-oriented data diversity dominates algorithmic sophistication is well-evidenced and practically impactful: vanilla GRPO on GoLongRL data beats QwenLong-L1.5's specialized AEPO at 4B, suggesting that prior work was data-bottlenecked more than algorithm-bottlenecked. - The diagnosis-driven iterative dataset refinement is methodologically sound: MRCR stagnated at 40.7 through undifferentiated data expansion but jumped to 67.5 with 5.2K targeted multi-hop and context-memory samples, demonstrating a principled approach to capability gap closure. - TMN-Reweight's gradient norm analysis (Appendix A) provides a principled derivation of σ_task as the natural cross-task normalization constant, distinguishing within-prompt variance (which drives gradient magnitude) from total pooled variance (which conflates difficulty and scale)—a non-obvious but correct insight. - Full open release with multi-stage quality control (Gemini-2.5-Pro QA verification + two-model pass-rate calibration) and 13-gram decontamination is an unusually thorough community contribution from an industrial lab. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge that TMN-Reweight effectiveness may vary across model scales, with clearer gains at 4B and less settled evidence at 30B, motivating future study of scale-dependent optimization dynamics. A remaining CorpusQA gap suggests further targeted supplementation for multi-document reasoning would be beneficial. MoE-specific integration of TMN-Reweight (requiring stabilization mechanisms like R3) is deferred to future work. Combining context-aware token weighting with RLVR is identified as a promising but unexplored direction. These limitations are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The TMN-Reweight +0.8 point average gain at 4B is measured from a single 8K random subsample; without multiple subsample runs, it is impossible to determine whether this exceeds sampling variance, especially given that individual benchmark swings (MRCR: −2.0) go in the opposite direction. - The paper's headline 30B result uses vanilla GRPO, not TMN-Reweight; the more principled algorithm has no large-scale validation, yet Section 5.2's framing implies TMN-Reweight is the recommended method. - Unexplained evaluation protocol discrepancies in Table 9 (MRCR Δ = −9.7 for 30B base, Memory-Rec_Sum Δ = −10.9) are large enough to affect the ranking between GoLongRL (self-evaluated) and QwenLong-L1.5 (paper-reported for several numbers). The authors note these in a table but do not explain them. - Tasks T7, T8, and T9 have only 120–180 samples each (<4% combined). Whether such thin coverage genuinely trains those capabilities or merely prevents catastrophic forgetting is not tested with capability-specific held-out evaluations. - ROUGE-L as the reward signal for summarization (T9) rewards lexical overlap rather than semantic quality; this limitation is standard in the field but goes unacknowledged. - Reward hacking and answer ambiguity are identified as reasons for removing samples during P4 iteration, but no statistics are provided on rejection rates by task—a useful indicator of data quality challenges that future users of the construction pipeline would need. Bottom Line A practitioner building long-context post-training pipelines gains real, actionable insight from this work—particularly the capability taxonomy-driven data construction methodology and the evidence that broad reward diversity outperforms retrieval-centric homogeneity—but should treat the TMN-Reweight algorithmic contribution as a suggestive preliminary result pending seed-level variance analysis and 30B validation.
Recommended 2605.19577 PDF
#131 — Moderately Innovative (56)

GoodServe: Towards High-Goodput Serving of Agentic LLM Inferences over Heterogeneous Resources

arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GoodServe, a proxy-layer routing system for agentic LLM inference over heterogeneous GPU pools that maximizes goodput—the count of requests completing within their end-to-end SLO deadline—via a predict-and-rectify architecture. - Why it's important: Agentic applications require fully-formed outputs before downstream tasks can proceed, making TTLT (not TTFT or TPOT) the relevant SLO metric; existing routing methods are SLO-agnostic and leave substantial goodput on the table in the heterogeneous GPU pools that production providers actually operate. - How it does it: A MoE-style output-length predictor and EMA-smoothed black-box GPU profiler jointly estimate per-request completion time on each candidate GPU; a "just-enough" heuristic then routes each request to the slowest GPU that can still meet its deadline, reserving fast GPUs for more urgent requests, with periodic SLO-risk re-evaluation triggering token-ID-based live migration. - The good: GoodServe achieves 27.4% higher goodput than the second-best baseline (Preble) at SLO scale 2× on a 4-GPU heterogeneous testbed, with ablations showing both the MoE predictor (32.8% goodput drop when replaced with a history-based predictor) and migration (18.0% drop when disabled) are essential contributors. - The missing: The testbed contains only 4 GPUs (H800, A800, A40, V100); the MoE predictor is trained and evaluated on the same benchmark suite, leaving its accuracy on out-of-distribution agentic task types unvalidated; and the interaction between concurrent migrations and system load at scale is measured only in simulation, not on real hardware. - Read it or not: Worth reading for systems researchers and engineers building LLM serving infrastructure over heterogeneous GPU pools; the just-enough heuristic and token-ID migration design are concise, implementable ideas with solid empirical support at small scale.
Why this rank: Scored using the Systems profile. Impact (58) and a strong Non-Obviousness (65) for the counter-intuitive just-enough heuristic (route to the weakest sufficient GPU) carry the composite, supported by a real 2.5k-LoC artifact and a credible 27.4% goodput gain. Rigor (50) is the main drag — the MoE predictor is trained on benchmarks that overlap the evaluation set (in-distribution, OOD unvalidated), the testbed is only 4 GPUs with scale shown solely in simulation, error bars are weak max/min, and code is supplementary-only; the 10 Gbps network assumption favoring token-ID migration would plausibly reverse on InfiniBand. Weighted: 0.15*58 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*65 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*48 + 0.30*58 = 56.
systems Novelty 58 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 55 Resilience 48 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM service providers aggregate heterogeneous GPU generations—NVIDIA H100, A100, A40, V100, AMD MI300X—into unified resource pools, and route incoming inference requests across them. For agentic workloads (code generation, database management, mathematical reasoning), the downstream executor requires a complete output before it can act, making end-to-end inference latency (TTLT) the binding SLO metric rather than time-to-first-token or per-token latency. This distinguishes agentic serving from chatbot serving and invalidates the standard optimization objectives of existing routing methods. Existing approaches—random, round-robin, least-request (load balancing) and prefix-cache, Preble (local efficiency maximization)—treat all requests identically and cannot make globally-optimal but locally-suboptimal decisions, such as routing a low-urgency request to a weaker GPU in order to free a faster GPU for a deadline-critical request. Figure 2 quantifies the gap: Llumnix achieves 5.04 RPS against an oracle's 7.51 RPS on a 4-GPU heterogeneous testbed with a 6-second E2E-SLO, a 49% shortfall. Key Idea The central insight is the "just-enough" instance selection principle: among all GPU instances estimated to complete a request within its SLO, deliberately route to the one with the worst compute capability rather than the best. Over-serving a request (completing it faster than required) wastes high-end GPU capacity without improving goodput. Implementing this principle requires accurate per-request estimates of both output length (demand) and GPU serving speed (supply), which GoodServe provides with two lightweight estimators, correcting prediction errors through periodic monitoring and live migration. Methodology GoodServe formulates routing as maximizing Σ_r I[T(r,g*) ≤ D_r] subject to single-GPU assignment and per-GPU memory capacity constraints, where T(r,g) = q_g + p_g·(L^in_r − H_{r,g}) + d_g·L^out_r. This is NP-hard (reduces to integer linear programming), so GoodServe uses a greedy heuristic with three components. (1) MoE-style output-length predictor: Prompts are TF-IDF vectorized; a two-layer MLP gating router computes a softmax mixture weight over K=9 four-layer MLP experts (45.1M parameters total); final prediction is the weighted sum of expert outputs. Training is two-phase: first, experts are each trained on distinct data subsets formed by discretizing input/output length into √K tiers; second, experts frozen, the router is trained on the held-out half. Critical design decision: TF-IDF + small MLPs rather than a DistilBERT-style predictor, trading semantic depth for sub-3ms inference latency. This is reasonable for throughput but potentially brittle on novel task types not in training data. (2) EMA-smoothed black-box GPU profiling: Estimates q_g, p_g, d_g directly from recent observed values via exponential moving average, without querying engine internals (batch size, GPU topology). Justified by the Law of Large Numbers argument that a GPU's per-iteration time is locally stable. This is a pragmatic approximation that ignores request-specific batch interference; it suffices as long as the instance-preference ordering is correct, which Figure 5 confirms empirically on an A40 replaying the Mooncake trace. (3) Just-enough heuristic + periodic migration: Per incoming request, compute T(r,g) for all M GPUs in O(M); select the feasible GPU with the largest d_g (slowest decoder, i.e., weakest sufficient GPU). If no feasible GPU exists, route to the GPU minimizing deadline overshoot. Every τ=50 decode iterations, re-estimate SLO risk; migrate at-risk requests via token-ID transfer rather than KV-cache transfer, accepting a small prefill re-cost in exchange for 7.1–15.3× lower network transfer latency on 10 Gbps Ethernet. Key Results Testbed: 4 heterogeneous NVIDIA GPUs (H800 80GB, A800 80GB, A40 48GB, V100 32GB×2-way TP), 10 Gbps Ethernet, two models (Llama3.1-8B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct). Workload: mixed BIRD-bench, SWE-bench, LiveCodeBench prompts replayed with Mooncake production arrival traces. SLOs set at 1×–3× median solo-run latency on A800. Baselines: random, round-robin, least-request (AIBrix), lowest-TPM (LiteLLM), Preble, Llumnix. Experiments repeated 5 times with max/min error bars. GoodServe achieves the best goodput across all SLO scales and both models. Peak improvement: 27.4% over Preble (second-best) for 8B model at SLO scale 2×. Improvement grows with looser SLOs: 3.1% to 16.6% for the 14B model as SLO scale goes from 1× to 3×, consistent with the theory that looser deadlines allow more locally-suboptimal routing decisions. MoE predictor achieves 1.4× lower normalized MAE than LLM-based (DistilBERT) and 3.8× lower than history-based, with ~2.5 ms per-request prediction latency. Replacing the predictor with LLM-based, single-MLP, and history-based alternatives reduces goodput by 13.8%, 17.0%, and 18.1%, respectively. Token-ID migration achieves 7.1–15.3× lower latency than KV-cache migration across sequence lengths 500–3000 tokens. Large-scale simulation of a 512-instance cluster at 10,000 RPS yields 5.0 ms average routing overhead, which the paper claims is negligible. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The TTLT-centric SLO framing for agentic inference routing is a genuine problem reframing (versus TTFT/TPOT-optimized chatbot serving); the just-enough heuristic is a non-obvious algorithmic choice within that frame. Individual components—MoE architecture, EMA smoothing, token-ID migration—all draw on established techniques applied to a new configuration. No single component is algorithmically novel in isolation, but their integration and the routing problem formulation are a meaningful contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative, isolating the predictor and migration components with clear goodput impact numbers. Error bars (5 runs, max/min) are shown but weak; standard deviations or confidence intervals would be more informative. Code is described as attached as a supplementary file to the submission; no public GitHub URL is provided, so reproducibility currently depends on peer-review release. The predictor is trained on 8,680 samples from BIRD-bench and LiveCodeBench, which substantially overlaps the evaluation suite—generalization to out-of-distribution agentic task types is unvalidated. The SLO methodology (scaling solo A800 median latency) is standard in the LLM serving literature but produces artificial SLOs not validated against real operator requirements. References [39] and [40] are duplicate entries for the same JITServe paper, suggesting some bibliography sloppiness. The paper is a preprint; results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built proxy router evaluated by testbed deployment alongside simulation. - Practical: Runs on standard NVIDIA GPUs via vLLM; the proxy adds ~2.5k LoC and is co-located on one of the GPU instances (the A40 in experiments), which is a deployment constraint not analyzed for contention impact. - Efficient: Per-request routing overhead is ~5 ms at 10,000 RPS over 512 simulated nodes; predictor inference is ~2.5 ms; migration via token-ID transfer adds a one-time prefill cost that the paper argues is negligible for long agentic contexts. EMA profiling is constant-time per GPU. - Effective: Goodput improvement of 27.4% over the strongest comparable baseline on the 4-GPU testbed is credible evidence of effectiveness; ablations confirm each component contributes. The absolute goodput scale (1–3 RPS) is small and reflects a 4-GPU cluster, not a production-scale deployment. - Scalable: Routing complexity is O(M) per request; scalability is tested only in simulation (512 nodes), not on real hardware at scale. Concurrent migration behavior under load spikes, network contention among simultaneous migrations, and predictor accuracy drift over long deployments are untested. The work delivers a software artifact (2.5k LoC implementation, code attached as supplementary). Strengths - The just-enough heuristic is the paper's strongest intellectual contribution: counter-intuitively routing to the weakest sufficient GPU is a correct and non-obvious consequence of optimizing a global goodput objective rather than per-request latency. The oracle comparison (Fig. 2) cleanly motivates why greedy best-GPU routing is suboptimal. - The predict-and-rectify architecture correctly treats prediction uncertainty as a first-class system property rather than an obstacle to route around, embedding migration as a designed mechanism with its own ablation contribution (18% goodput impact). - The token-ID vs. KV-cache migration design decision is well-reasoned and empirically validated: the 7.1–15.3× latency speedup over 10 Gbps Ethernet is a concrete, actionable engineering finding backed by a clear cost model. - Comparison against six baselines across five SLO scales and two model sizes is thorough given the 4-GPU testbed constraint, and the result that GoodServe's advantage grows with SLO looseness directly corroborates the theoretical reasoning. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge one limitation: the absence of an admission control mechanism. When all GPUs are predicted to miss a request's SLO, GoodServe falls back to best-effort routing to the fastest GPU rather than rejecting the request. The authors correctly identify this as theoretically wasteful but practically necessary because prediction errors make aggressive admission control risky. They commit to future investigation. This is a genuine, non-trivial limitation that the authors confront directly rather than minimize. Analyst-Identified Gaps - In-distribution predictor evaluation: The MoE predictor is trained on BIRD-bench + LiveCodeBench samples and evaluated on a workload that includes those same benchmarks plus SWE-bench. No out-of-distribution predictor accuracy is reported. In production, novel agentic task types will appear; the predictor's degradation behavior in this case is unknown and could significantly erode GoodServe's advantage over simpler baselines. - 4-GPU testbed insufficient to validate heterogeneity claims: The paper motivates the work with production clusters containing dozens of GPU types (H100, A100, MI300X, etc.) but demonstrates on 4 GPUs. How goodput improvement scales with the number and diversity of GPU types is a missing experiment—the 512-instance simulation uses only routing overhead, not migration or prediction accuracy. - Network assumption not discussed: Token-ID migration's 7.1–15.3× advantage over KV-cache migration is measured on 10 Gbps Ethernet. On InfiniBand (200+ Gbps), KV-cache transfer cost collapses by two orders of magnitude and the design choice could reverse. This is not acknowledged. - Concurrent migration storms: The periodic SLO-risk check (every τ=50 iterations) could trigger many simultaneous migrations under a load spike, creating queuing cascades and prefill contention on destination GPUs. This is not analyzed. - Prefix cache accuracy: H_{r,g} (prefix cache hit length per GPU) is included in the latency model but the empirical evaluation never reports how accurately it is estimated or what fraction of the goodput benefit derives from it versus pure routing decisions. - Duplicate bibliography entry: References [39] and [40] are identical (JITServe), a minor credibility flag for a paper submitted to a venue with rigorous review standards. Bottom Line A practitioner building LLM serving infrastructure over heterogeneous GPU pools will find GoodServe's just-enough heuristic immediately actionable and the 27.4% goodput improvement credible for small heterogeneous clusters, but should expect to revalidate predictor accuracy and migration behavior before deploying in production environments with diverse task types or high-bandwidth interconnects.
Search Results 2605.16867 PDF
#132 — Moderately Innovative (56)

ReactiveGWM: Steering NPC in Reactive Game World Models (24 ▲ )

Zeqing Wang, Danze Chen, Zhaohu Xing, Zizhao Tong, Yinhan Zhang, Xingyi Yang, Yeying Jin · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 18 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ReactiveGWM, a video diffusion-based game world model that decouples player action control from NPC behavior so that a separate high-level strategy prompt (Offense / Control / Defense) governs the generated NPC independently of what the player does. - Why it's important: Existing game world models treat NPCs as background pixels implicitly tied to player-centric prompts, making them passive renderers rather than true simulation engines — a critical gap for generating competitive or strategy-rich gameplay experiences. - How it does it: Player actions are injected via lightweight per-block additive bias into a DiT backbone, while NPC strategy is grounded through cross-attention modules trained on Gemini-annotated clips; crucially, the cross-attention modules can be transplanted into an off-the-shelf vanilla world model of a different game without retraining. - The good: Human-judged NPC strategy recognition accuracy on SF2 jumps from 43.9% (vanilla) to 86.0% (ReactiveGWMbase) with no degradation in player action control (100% Move-Acc and Att-Acc on SF3), and the training-free transfer achieves 84.2% / 61.4% on SF2/SF3 without any new strategy annotations. - The missing: The evaluation uses Gemini both as the training-data annotator and as a primary referee, creating a circular self-reinforcing signal that inflates reported NPC accuracy; additionally, "game-agnostic" transfer is only demonstrated within the same Street Fighter franchise, not across genuinely different game genres. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers in controllable video generation or game AI who want a concrete example of modular strategy injection into diffusion world models; can be skipped by those seeking a deployable real-time system or evaluation on diverse game genres.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Novelty (68) and Non-Obviousness (72) are high — a genuine NEW PROBLEM (explicit NPC strategy conditioning) plus the energy-share insight (cross-attention carries ~0.71% of residual energy) that enables training-free plug-in transfer is mechanistically grounded and surprising. But Rigor (45) and Resilience (48) pull the composite down: no released code/data, thin ablations, circular Gemini annotation-and-referee evaluation on only 99 clips, training data from a random agent rather than strategic play, and a 'game-agnostic' claim supported only by same-franchise SF2→SF3 transfer. The acknowledged lack of real-time capability also caps Impact (42).
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 62 Resilience 48 Impact 42
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Game developers and AI researchers building interactive game simulations need world models that can simulate not just the player's immediate environment but also the autonomous behavior of adversarial or cooperative NPCs. The dominant paradigm trains diffusion-based world models purely from a player-centric perspective, passing a single prompt that lumps NPC behavior together with background scene dynamics. This design forces NPCs to be statistically predictable byproducts of the player's action stream rather than independent agents with tactical intent — fine for a solo-player environment renderer, but fundamentally insufficient for generating competitive or strategy-rich interactions. No prior work provides an explicit conditioning channel that lets a designer specify and steer NPC behavior independently of what the player does. Key Idea: The core insight is that the cross-attention layers in a DiT-based video backbone can serve as a low-bandwidth, high-directionality channel: they carry only ~0.71% of the total residual energy per block, so substituting them with modules trained on NPC strategy leaves the dominant visual and physical dynamics (encoded in self-attention and FFN layers) intact. This makes the cross-attention module a portable "strategy plug-in" — train it once with NPC-annotated data on a source game, then swap it into any vanilla world model of another game without touching the backbone. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. (1) Data construction: gameplay episodes from Street Fighter II (SF2) and Street Fighter Alpha 3 (SF3) are recorded via stable-retro with a random agent sampling 13 discrete behaviors; Gemini analyzes each 5-second clip in a two-stage process — first answering 12 closed-set factual questions about NPC actions (e.g., `guile_sonic_boom_count`, `guile_advances`), then a deterministic rule engine maps those facts to one of three mutually exclusive strategy labels (Offense, Control, Defense), producing ~10,000 annotated triplets (video, action sequence, NPC prompt) per game. (2) Architecture: built on the Wan2.2-TI2V-5B DiT backbone; player actions (10-bit binary button vectors) are temporally pooled via adaptive max-pooling to the latent frame rate and injected as an additive bias into each DiT block's residual stream before self-attention; NPC strategy text is encoded and grounded through cross-attention in the same blocks. (3) Training and transfer: full-parameter fine-tuning for the base model on SF2 (ReactiveGWMbase); transfer to SF3 reuses SF3's pre-trained backbone (action module, self-attention, FFN) and replaces only the cross-attention layers with those from ReactiveGWMbase — no SF3 strategy annotation needed. Critical design decisions: (a) additive bias rather than cross-attention for player actions avoids entangling NPC strategy with the dominant player conditioning pathway — justified by ablation showing equivalent action accuracy; (b) two-stage VLM annotation separates factual observation from strategy inference to limit hallucination cascades into the final label; (c) deterministic rule classification ensures mutually exclusive, reproducible labels. Key Results: On SF2, VLM-judged NPC strategy accuracy rises from 43.4% (vanilla) to 75.8% (Gemini) / 76.8% (Qwen) for ReactiveGWMbase, and to 64.6% for the training-free transfer. On SF3, accuracy goes from 41.8% / 49.5% (vanilla) to 79.8% / 78.8% (base) and 63.6% / 73.7% (transfer). Player action control is preserved at 95–100% Move-Acc and 93–100% Att-Acc across both games. Visual quality (SSIM / LPIPS) remains on par with vanilla: SF2 SSIM 0.428 vs. 0.427 vanilla; SF3 SSIM 0.394 vs. 0.392 vanilla. A 19-participant human study corroborates VLM scores: human strategy recognition accuracy reaches 86.0% (base) and 84.2% (transfer) on SF2, and 77.2% / 61.4% on SF3, versus 43.9% / 17.5% for vanilla. A notable counter-intuitive finding: the Control strategy transfers poorly to SF3 (16% human accuracy for transfer), because Control depends on game-specific ranged attacks (Sonic Boom) whose animation and timing differ substantially between titles. Baselines Matrix-Game-3.0 and LingBot-World-Base are acknowledged to be untailored for Street Fighter and serve only as broad reference points — their NPC accuracy on SF2 is 3.0% (Gemini) and 30.3% respectively, making them too weak to be meaningful competitors. Novelty Classification: NEW PROBLEM (explicit NPC strategy conditioning in game world models), NEW METHOD (decoupled additive-bias / cross-attention injection with training-free module transfer), NEW DATASET (strategy-annotated Street Fighter gameplay clips). The novelty is genuine in the problem framing and the plug-and-play cross-attention transfer mechanism; the individual components (DiT fine-tuning, VLM annotation, cross-attention conditioning) are standard building blocks applied to a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: No code, data, or model weights are released (a project page URL is listed but no repo mentioned in the paper). Ablations are limited — there is no systematic comparison of additive bias vs. cross-attention for player actions, no ablation of annotation quality or the number of training examples, and no error bars on the main VLM accuracy metrics. The user study (n=19) reports mean ± SEM and provides per-class breakdowns, which is appropriate for a small human study. A significant methodological concern: Gemini is used both to generate the training labels (Stage 1 factual questions → strategy annotation) and to serve as the primary VLM referee at evaluation time. This creates a circular dynamic — if Gemini's visual representation of "Sonic Boom = Control" is consistent across annotation and evaluation, it will inflate Gemini-judged accuracy regardless of whether the generated video would satisfy a human with a different definition of Control. Qwen3-VL-8B serves as an independent second referee, partially mitigating this, and the human study provides a further independent check. Evaluation is conducted on only 99 curated clips (33 per class), which is narrow for a 3-way classification task with a model generating 101-frame videos. This is preprint research, not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL / SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Runs on standard GPU hardware using the Wan2.2-TI2V-5B backbone; no unusual infrastructure required beyond what is needed to run a 5B-parameter video diffusion model. - Efficient: The paper explicitly acknowledges that the diffusion-based backbone introduces high inference latency and cannot support real-time interaction; no inference speed numbers are reported. - Effective: The evidence shows strategy control works for a 3-class taxonomy in 2D fighting games; whether this transfers to games with more complex NPC behavior spaces is not tested. - Scalable: Transfer is demonstrated only from SF2 to SF3 (same genre, very similar character sets and screen layouts); generalization to other game genres is claimed as future work and not tested. The work delivers a method and datasets; no reusable software artifact or pretrained model is released. Strengths: - The two-stage VLM annotation pipeline (factual questions → deterministic rule engine) is a pragmatic and reproducible solution to the hard problem of labeling high-level strategy from video without relying solely on VLM judgment. - The energy-share analysis (ρ_cross ≈ 0.71%) and cosine-similarity directional analysis (0.55) provide a mechanistic explanation for why plug-and-play cross-attention transfer preserves visual quality while steering behavior — this is genuinely useful insight for the controllable video generation community. - The human study corroborates VLM scores and reveals per-class transfer failure (Control = 16% on SF3) that the authors analyze rather than suppress, providing honest coverage of failure modes. - The problem itself — decoupled NPC modeling in game world models — is well-motivated and fills a real gap not addressed by prior work. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state explicitly that (1) evaluation is restricted to 2D fighting games and that extension to 2D FPS or multi-agent strategy games is needed to validate game-agnostic generality; (2) the diffusion backbone's high inference latency prevents real-time interactive use, and they point to autoregressive generation and model distillation as future directions. These are the two most serious practical limitations and the authors confront them directly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Beyond what the authors acknowledge: (1) Circular evaluation — Gemini annotates training data and serves as primary referee, which is a Goodhart's Law concern the authors do not flag; (2) The random agent used to generate training data produces unrealistic NPC behavior patterns (uniform sampling over action buttons, not human or scripted AI strategies), so the model learns to associate strategy labels with visually observable consequences of random play rather than strategic intent — the generalization to realistic human vs. human gameplay is untested; (3) Transfer is only cross-title within the same franchise (SF2→SF3), which share character archetypes, screen layout, and visual style — calling this "game-agnostic" overreaches the evidence; (4) Only 3 strategy classes — any real game AI taxonomy is far richer and the granularity question is not addressed; (5) No ablation on which of the structured prompt components (active behaviors, passive behaviors, strategy label) drives the accuracy gain — the strategy label alone may suffice. Bottom Line: Practitioners building controllable video generation for interactive game content will find the decoupled injection architecture and the plug-and-play cross-attention transfer to be useful, concrete ideas, but should treat the "game-agnostic" claims with skepticism until tested beyond the Street Fighter franchise.
Recommended 2605.15256 PDF
#133 — Moderately Innovative (56)

MHGraphBench: Knowledge Graph-Grounded Benchmarking of Mental Health Knowledge in Large Language Models

Weixin Liu, Congning Ni, Shelagh A. Mulvaney, Susannah L. Rose, Murat Kantarcioglu, Bradley A. Malin, Zhijun Yin · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MHGraphBench, a knowledge-graph-grounded multiple-choice benchmark for evaluating LLM mental-health biomedical knowledge across nine task families derived from a curated PrimeKG mental-health subgraph. - Why it's important: LLMs are being deployed in mental health contexts where confusing indication, contraindication, and off-label use has direct patient-safety consequences, yet no existing benchmark tests these clinically specific relational distinctions with machine-verifiable ground truth. - How it does it: A 1-hop subgraph anchored by 42 psychiatric seed diseases is extracted from PrimeKG (4,621 triples, 1,847 entities, 7 relation types), auto-converted to nine multiple-choice task families with type-matched negative sampling, and evaluated on 15 LLMs with per-task accuracy plus graph-wide coverage metrics. - The good: Even the strongest GPT models achieve only 58.6% accuracy on relation prediction and 35.8% mean accuracy on contraindication — a concretely quantified, clinically significant capability gap against a near-ceiling (~98%) entity-typing baseline. - The missing: The benchmark evaluates only closed-world KG-consistency over a single-hop subgraph with explicitly provided 2-hop scaffolds; no expert validation of generated items or negatives is performed, and response-format failures are acknowledged but not quantified per model. - Read it or not: Worth reading for NLP and biomedical AI researchers who need a reproducible diagnostic for structured mental-health knowledge in LLMs; less relevant for those seeking evidence about real-world clinical safety, which the paper explicitly disclaims assessing.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile (benchmark construction + 15-model evaluation). Rigor (50) pulls the composite down most: a single fixed seed with no error bars, no significance tests, and no design ablations, a degenerate 7-item RT task left in the reported averages, no expert clinical validation, no public repository URL, and unreported per-model response-invalidity rates that confound accuracy with instruction-following — all under a preprint discount. Novelty (63) is moderate — a useful new mental-health KG benchmark and the recognition-to-judgment gap finding, but the KG-to-QA methodology is established and ported to a new domain. Completeness (58) and Resilience (55) are limited by the closed-world KG-conformity confound and 'two-hop' tasks that supply the scaffold (verification, not true multi-step reasoning), though direct, unminimized limitation acknowledgment is a credibility positive. Weighted: 0.20*63 + 0.25*50 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*58 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*55 ≈ 56.
empirical Novelty 63 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 58 Resilience 55 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Clinicians and researchers deploying LLMs in mental health must trust that models can correctly navigate safety-critical relational distinctions — most critically, whether a drug is indicated, contraindicated, or used off-label for a given psychiatric condition. Existing biomedical benchmarks (MedPaLM-style, HealthBench) report aggregate accuracy across heterogeneous clinical tasks, providing no granular insight into mental-health-specific knowledge coverage or failure modes at the relation level. More recent mental-health-specific benchmarks (MentalBench, CounselBench, TrustMH-Bench) target diagnostic reasoning, counseling quality, or trustworthiness but not verifiable structured biomedical knowledge. Knowledge graphs are well-suited to fill this gap because they provide machine-verifiable, semantically typed facts that support systematic QA generation and explicit coverage analysis. Key Idea Anchor the benchmark entirely in a curated knowledge graph subgraph rather than in human-authored questions: every item is derived from a KG triple, every negative is constrained to be KG-unsupported under type-matched perturbation, and correctness is defined as agreement with the graph. This makes evaluation scalable, reproducible, and interpretable — performance can be mapped back to specific entities, relations, and graph regions rather than summarized by a single scalar. Methodology The pipeline has four stages. Stage 1 extracts a mental-health subgraph from PrimeKG: 42 psychiatric seed disease nodes (manually curated, 2 post-curation exclusions) define the domain boundary; all 1-hop seed-touching edges are retained for 7 clinically salient relation families (indication, contraindication, off-label use, disease_disease, disease_protein, disease_phenotype_positive, exposure_disease), yielding 4,621 canonical, deduplicated triples over 1,847 entities. Stage 2 generates nine multiple-choice task families: Entity Typing (ET, 1,847 items, 5-way), Entity Clustering (EC, 2,000, odd-one-out), Fact Checking (FC, 4,000, binary, balanced per relation), Relation Typing (RT, 7 items — effectively degenerate), Relation Prediction (RP, 1,634, 4-way: indication / contraindication / off-label use / none), Two-hop Verification (R1, 1,200, binary ≈50% yes), Two-hop Selection (R2, 1,200, 4-way), and evidence-augmented variants (R1+E, R2+E, 1,200 each with PrimeKG feature-table snippets sanitized to redact relation-label lexical forms). Stage 3 evaluates 15 models: five frontier GPT variants (GPT-4.1, GPT-4o, GPT-5-mini, GPT-5.1-chat, GPT-5.2-chat), two general-purpose open-source models (Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct), and five biomedical fine-tunes (BioMistral-7B, Med42-8B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen 7B/32B, Llama3.1-8B, Meditron-7B, OpenBioLLM-8B). API models use temperature 0, max 120 tokens; local models use forced-choice log-probability scoring over multiple surface forms of answer letters. Stage 4 reports per-task accuracy, grouped averages (AvgE, Avg*_R, AvgS, etc.), and graph-coverage metrics (mean and degree-weighted entity/relation correctness, triple coverage as average of entity/relation correctness per triple). Critical design decisions: (1) letter-only interface with A/B labels for binary tasks to reduce lexical bias — well-justified; (2) relation-balanced FC negatives — reduces relation-frequency shortcuts; (3) 1-hop subgraph only — practically tractable but limits reasoning depth. Key Results GPT-4.1 ranks first on overall accuracy (Avg*_All = 70.28%), followed by GPT-5.2-chat (69.32%) and GPT-4o (69.10%). Best open-source model is Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct at 56.09% — more than 12 percentage points below the top GPT models. Most remaining open-source models cluster in the low-to-mid 30s. Recognition tasks are near-saturated for top GPT models: ET = 97.40–98.48%, EC = 90.10–92.35%. Relation prediction is dramatically weaker: best RP is GPT-5.2-chat at 58.63%. Two-hop reasoning: GPT-4.1 AvgS = 60.79%, Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct at 54.75%, most others in the high-30s to mid-40s — notable because the 2-hop scaffold is explicitly provided in the prompt. Evidence augmentation is inconsistent: GPT-4.1 R1 improves 57.58% → 71.42%, but Qwen2.5-32B R2 degrades 59.00% → 50.08%. Fine-grained relation analysis: contraindication has a mean accuracy of 35.8% across all 15 models and is the hardest relation, while indication averages 59.1%. Biomedical-specialized models do not outperform general-purpose instruction-tuned models; Med42-8B achieves only 24.76% AvgE. Coverage ranking diverges from accuracy ranking: GPT-5-mini leads triple coverage (Cov(T) = 65.27%) despite GPT-4.1 leading per-task accuracy. All results are from an arXiv preprint; not peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The KG-to-QA framework applied to a curated psychiatric subgraph with multi-level coverage metrics is genuinely new for the mental health domain; the quantified recognition-to-judgment gap (entity typing ~98% vs. contraindication ~36% mean) and the finding that domain-specialized open-source models do not outperform general-purpose alternatives are substantive empirical contributions. The underlying methodology (KG-grounded QA generation, controlled negative sampling) applies established techniques from biomedical KG benchmarking to a new domain and evaluation scope. Rigor & Reproducibility Benchmark generation uses a fixed random seed (42); local model evaluation is deterministic. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported for any accuracy comparison. No ablations on benchmark design choices (alternative negative sampling strategies, different subgraph extraction depths, prompt format sensitivity). The RT task family contains only 7 items — an essentially degenerate subset that the authors themselves recommend interpreting cautiously, yet it remains in all reported averages. No expert clinical validation of generated items, negatives, or evidence snippets is performed; ground truth is KG-consistency only, not clinical correctness. The paper describes implementation files (kg.csv, mental_health_seed_diseases_FINAL.csv) and code details but does not provide a public repository URL, making full reproduction contingent on access to PrimeKG and the evaluation code. Response invalidity rates per model are not reported, despite the paper acknowledging that format compliance is entangled with measured accuracy. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (constructs and evaluates a benchmark across 15 LLMs). - Practical: Benchmark evaluation runs on commercially available hardware — local models require GPU with bfloat16 support; API models require API access. - Efficient: Compute cost is not reported; at batch size 1 and 4,096 max sequence length across ~14,000 items and 15 models, total inference volume is substantial but tractable. - Effective: The benchmark effectively differentiates models on structured knowledge tasks and produces interpretable per-relation and per-entity failure maps; whether high RP or R1 scores predict real-world clinical reliability is explicitly out of scope. - Scalable: The benchmark is fixed-size (4,621 KG triples); extension to more psychiatric conditions, deeper reasoning paths, or updated PrimeKG versions is possible by re-running the extraction pipeline, though no such scaling experiment is demonstrated. The work delivers a benchmark dataset (items generated from PrimeKG) and evaluation pipeline; no trained model, library, or inference tool is released. Strengths - KG-grounded construction provides machine-verifiable ground truth and principled negative sampling, eliminating the annotation artifacts common in human-authored clinical QA datasets. - The quantified recognition-to-judgment gap — near-ceiling entity typing vs. 35.8% contraindication accuracy even for frontier models — is a specific, actionable finding for practitioners assessing LLM readiness in mental health. - Complementary coverage metrics (triple coverage, degree-weighted entity/relation correctness) reveal model-specific blind spots invisible to per-task averages: GPT-5.2-chat ranks second on accuracy but has substantially lower coverage than other GPT models, indicating unevenly distributed correctness. - Evaluating 15 models spanning frontier, general open-source, and biomedical fine-tuned families directly evidences the finding that domain adaptation alone does not guarantee structured-knowledge improvement. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state: (1) the benchmark inherits PrimeKG's coverage limits and does not capture full psychiatric breadth, longitudinal context, or individualized treatment decisions; (2) KG-based labels may lag current clinical guidelines — the benchmark measures KG-consistency, not absolute clinical truth; (3) no expert validation of generated items, negatives, or evidence snippets beyond the KG construction pipeline; (4) an absent KG edge is not evidence the corresponding claim is clinically false (open-world assumption); (5) output-format reliability is entangled with measured accuracy. These are confronted directly and without minimization, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The RT task (7 items, perfect scores for top models) should be removed entirely from the evaluation; reporting it even with caveats inflates AvgAll and AvgR for models with good instruction-following, creating misleading composite scores. - Response invalidity rates per model are never reported quantitatively — the paper acknowledges format compliance matters but provides no data on how many responses were unparseable per model, making it impossible to determine whether accuracy differences reflect knowledge or instruction-following ability. - The "two-hop" reasoning tasks provide the full 2-hop scaffold explicitly in the prompt (Drug A → Disease B → Disease C is given), making these tasks closer to reading-comprehension-style verification than true multi-step graph reasoning; the claimed difficulty level should be interpreted in that context. - Evidence augmentation degrades some models, but no analysis is provided of whether this reflects distraction, residual answer leakage despite sanitization, or hallucinated KG-inconsistent reasoning — the characterization of evidence augmentation as a "diagnostic probe" is asserted without mechanistic support. - The 42 seed disease node selection and the two exclusion decisions (one as "out of scope," one as "outdated terminology") are made without inter-rater reliability checks or expert consensus documentation, leaving the domain boundary partially arbitrary. - The benchmark tests only closed-world KG-consistency: a model that answers "no" correctly to a KG-absent triple may hold a factually incorrect belief that simply happens to conflict with an incomplete graph — the evaluation cannot distinguish knowledge accuracy from KG-conformity. Bottom Line MHGraphBench is a well-scoped diagnostic tool that delivers a concrete, reproducible finding — frontier LLMs fail on the most safety-critical mental-health relation (contraindication, 35.8% mean accuracy) despite near-perfect entity recognition — making it a useful probing instrument for biomedical NLP researchers, though its single-hop graph scope, absence of expert validation, and entangled format-compliance confound limit its claim as a measure of clinically meaningful reasoning.
Search Results 2605.15589 PDF
#134 — Moderately Innovative (56)

Ablating Safety: Mechanisms for Removing Alignment in Language Models for Security Applications

arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ABLATING SAFETY, an evaluation protocol and empirical study that compares four families of alignment-removal interventions — authorized-context prompting, activation projection, representation-control projection, and LoRA fine-tuning — on small open-weight models for authorized cybersecurity tasks, measuring each on a joint utility-risk frontier of security score, general-capability retention, and out-of-scope unsafe compliance. - Why it's important: Security practitioners need to evaluate whether aligned LLMs fail security benchmarks due to missing capability or refusal-policy over-reach, and existing evaluations conflate these two failure modes, making it impossible to attribute a failed answer to incompetence versus policy. - How it does it: The core move is treating refusal as a separable, measurable dimension — evaluated alongside task utility and spillover — so that each transformation is scored on a Pareto frontier rather than by refusal rate alone. - The good: Task-only LoRA raises mean authorized-security score from 0.46 to 0.87 while keeping unsafe spillover at 0.13 and general score at 0.83, demonstrating that selective task adaptation can recover security utility without broad de-alignment. - The missing: The entire study is conducted on compact models (0.5B–3B parameters) with synthetically generated training data and a lexical-match scoring pipeline evaluated on only 60 prompts, all of which limit confidence that the utility-risk frontier observed here holds for production-scale models or real security workloads. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers studying LLM safety measurement methodology, alignment interpretability, or security benchmark design; practitioners expecting transfer to frontier models or operational security workflows will find the scale and evaluation fidelity insufficient.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. The utility-risk frontier framing earns reasonable Novelty (65) and the random-projection control yields a useful empirical finding, and Completeness (70) is a positive because the authors confront every scope boundary directly. But Rigor (48) is the dominant limiter: 60-prompt lexical keyword-match scoring (security score = keyword presence, not correctness), a self-developed benchmark with no external comparator, synthetic training data, and a 10-probe spillover set whose ±30pp CI makes most reported spillover differences statistically uninformative. Resilience (50) and sub-3B-only scope mean this reads as a motivating pilot rather than actionable guidance. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*48 + 0.15*58 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*45 = 56.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 48 Non-Obv 58 Completeness 70 Resilience 50 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Safety-aligned LLMs are routinely used in defensive security workflows — vulnerability triage, patch analysis, secure code repair, red-team exercises — that lexically overlap with prompts the models are trained to refuse. This creates a measurement confound: a model that fails a security benchmark may lack capability or may be enforcing its refusal policy on a legitimately authorized task. Existing evaluations treat compliance or non-compliance as a model property to report, not as a variable to isolate and control. Prior mechanistic work on refusal directions (Arditi et al., 2024) and practitioner abliteration workflows provide candidate interventions, but no principled framework exists for evaluating whether suppressing refusal actually recovers useful security capability versus merely introducing unsafe spillover or incoherence. Key Idea The central insight is that refusal suppression and task competence are empirically separable outcomes that must be measured jointly across three dimensions: authorized-task utility (security score), general-capability retention (general score), and unsafe out-of-scope compliance (spillover). The protocol enforces this by running every intervention — prompt, activation hook, representation projection, and LoRA adapter — through the same evaluation harness and reporting a utility-risk frontier rather than a single refusal-rate number. This reframing distinguishes surface refusal removal (compliance without competence) from useful alignment removal (competence gains with bounded spillover). Methodology The pipeline applies five transformation families to open-weight instruction-tuned models: (1) authorized-context prompting as a prompt-level baseline; (2) activation-projection hooks that suppress the residual-stream refusal direction using h' = h − α(h⊤r̂)r̂ at a selected layer; (3) representation-control projections including random, harmless, topic-matched, and rank-4 refusal-subspace variants as controls; (4) LoRA adapters (rank r=16, α=32, AdamW lr=5×10⁻⁵, 3 epochs, dropout 0.05) trained on 240 authorized-security examples and/or 480 benign-retention examples; and (5) an aligned baseline. The refusal direction is extracted from a 24-row contrast set of refusal-triggering vs. non-refusal prompts via average activation difference at a selected layer. Scoring uses a lexical refusal classifier, a literal concept-match rubric for security and general task scores (fraction of required concept groups present), and an unsafe-compliance indicator that fires when the model does not refuse and produces procedural language on out-of-scope probes. Three critical design decisions: (a) treating intervention site (input, activation, weights) as first-class variable — well-justified given the distinct reversibility and spillover profiles of each; (b) using rule-based scorers without human annotation — acknowledged as a limitation; (c) withholding raw non-refusal spillover completions from public artifacts — appropriate for responsible release but limits independent replication of the spillover metric. Key Results All results are averages over Qwen2.5-0.5B/1.5B/3B-Instruct unless noted. The aligned baseline scores 0.46 security, 0.74 general, 0.10 unsafe spillover. Single-vector refusal projection reaches security 0.50 but raises spillover to 0.47 — a 4.7× increase in unsafe compliance for a 0.04 security gain. Rank-4 refusal-subspace projection reaches security 0.51 with spillover matching the aligned baseline (0.10), showing that subspace rank matters. Task-only LoRA achieves security 0.87, general 0.83, spillover 0.13. Refusal-suppression LoRA matches task LoRA on security (0.87) with spillover 0.13; adding retention data raises general score to 0.86 but increases spillover to 0.27. In the projection pilot, single-vector projection lowers security scores for the three Qwen models relative to aligned (0.51→0.39 for 0.5B, 0.60→0.50 for 1.5B, 0.61→0.53 for 3B), and random projection sometimes matches the refusal-direction projection, indicating the extracted direction is not reliably mechanism-specific. Model dependence is substantial: Qwen2.5-3B shows zero unsafe compliance under LoRA variants while 0.5B and 1.5B show increases. The executable secure-repair validator subset (4 tasks, Qwen2.5-1.5B) gives unit-pass rates of 0.25 for aligned and single-vector projection, 0.50 for rank-4 projection and task LoRA. A three-seed sampled-decoding repeat preserves the ordering with low standard deviation (task LoRA: 0.88±0.01 security, 0.07±0.05 spillover). Benchmarks used: SECURITY-AR (authors' own, 60 prompts); no external security benchmark (CyberSecEval, Cybench) is used as a comparator. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The framing of alignment removal as a utility-risk frontier with explicit spillover controls is a genuine conceptual contribution distinct from prior jailbreak or abliteration work. SECURITY-AR, while small, is the first benchmark that jointly measures refusal, task utility, general retention, and spillover under controlled transformations for authorized security tasks. The finding that single-vector projection reliably increases unsafe spillover while often failing to improve security score — and that random projection sometimes matches it — is a concrete empirical result that complicates the practitioner abliteration narrative. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present across intervention families, projection strengths (α ∈ {0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0}), subspace ranks (k ∈ {1,2,4,8}), and transformer layers. A three-seed decoding repeat is provided for Qwen2.5-1.5B, with standard deviations reported. Code and sanitized prompts are released in a supplemental package; LoRA adapters and raw spillover completions are withheld. Methodological concerns: (a) the scoring pipeline is entirely lexical (concept-match rubrics, refusal phrase detection), with no human annotation or model-graded evaluation — the authors acknowledge this but the implication is that security score = keyword presence, not correctness; (b) SECURITY-AR is both developed and evaluated by the same team, with no external validation; (c) only 10 out-of-scope probes, making the spillover estimates high-variance; (d) LoRA training data is synthetically generated rather than drawn from real authorized security workflows; (e) the first author is listed only as "Isaac" (no surname), which is unusual and complicates attribution. This is a preprint; the results have not been peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: All experiments run on consumer/workstation hardware (MPS fallback flag suggests Apple Silicon); models are 0.5B–3B parameters, so the method is accessible on commercially available hardware. - Efficient: LoRA training at rank 16 on 720 examples with batch size 1 and gradient accumulation 8 is lightweight, but inference requires loading base model weights plus adapter for each condition; activation projection adds a per-layer forward hook with negligible overhead. - Effective: Task-only LoRA demonstrably raises authorized-security performance from 0.46 to 0.87 on the authors' rubric, but the rubric is a keyword-match proxy, not human-validated correctness; the executable validator subset (4 tasks, one model) provides partial ground-truth but is too small to draw strong conclusions. - Scalable: The study is bounded to 0.5B–3B models; the authors explicitly disclaim generalization to frontier-scale systems, and the findings for projection (which worsens performance on larger Qwen models) suggest the utility-risk frontier is model-size-dependent in ways not yet characterized. The work delivers source code and sanitized prompts as software artifacts, but not trained adapters or transformed checkpoints. Strengths - The utility-risk frontier framing — requiring simultaneous reporting of security score, general retention, and spillover — is a meaningful methodological advance over single-metric refusal-rate evaluations and directly addresses the capability-vs-policy confound in security benchmarking. - The random-projection and harmless-projection controls are non-trivial: the finding that random projection sometimes matches refusal-direction projection in security score, and that topic-matched refusal projection raises spillover to 0.30, strengthens the claim that prior abliteration evaluations may have over-attributed results to mechanism. - The projection-strength and projection-layer sweeps provide empirical evidence that refusal suppression is not monotone in intervention strength — a practically important observation for anyone attempting to tune these methods. - The responsible-release design (redacting spillover completions, withholding adapted checkpoints) is handled transparently and with explicit decision rules, which is a credibility positive. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state: the study is bounded to compact pilot models (0.5B–3B); LoRA training uses synthetic generated data; deterministic decoding is used for most conditions; the evaluation suite is 60 prompts; one contrast set per model/layer is used for direction estimation; scoring is rule-based. They explicitly disclaim generalization to frontier-scale systems. Many de-alignment methods are out of scope (weight-space abliteration, affine editing, sparse-autoencoder steering, full fine-tuning, model merging, decoding-time controls). The authors confront these limitations directly and frame them as scope boundaries rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The security task score is a literal rubric keyword-match, not validated against human expert judgment or functional correctness beyond the 4-task executable subset; a model that produces plausible-sounding but technically incorrect security advice could score highly. - The 10-probe spillover set is far too small to estimate unsafe compliance reliably — a 95% confidence interval over 10 binary outcomes is approximately ±30 percentage points, making many of the spillover differences reported (e.g., 0.10 vs. 0.13) statistically uninformative. - No external security benchmark (CyberSecEval, Cybench, HarmBench) is used, so it is impossible to determine whether SECURITY-AR scores correlate with performance on independently validated security benchmarks. - The LoRA training data is synthetically generated by an unspecified process; the paper does not describe what model generated it or how it was validated, raising concerns about train-test contamination in concept coverage. - The finding that task-only LoRA and refusal-suppression LoRA produce identical aggregate security scores (both 0.87) but via different mechanisms is not analyzed: the paper does not report whether they make different errors on specific task types. - The study evaluates only open-weight models ≤3B; this range is where single-vector refusal directions are most likely to behave as simple linear features — the results almost certainly do not transfer to instruction-tuned models at 70B+ scale where alignment is more distributed. Bottom Line The utility-risk frontier framing and the empirical result that activation projection reliably increases unsafe spillover without proportional security gains are genuine contributions to safety measurement methodology, but the combination of 60-prompt evaluation, keyword-match scoring, synthetic training data, and sub-3B models means a practitioner should treat this as a pilot study that motivates further work rather than actionable guidance for deploying de-aligned models in security workflows.
Recommended 2605.17413 PDF
#135 — Moderately Innovative (55)

AlignedServe: Orchestrating Prefix-aware Batching to Build a High- throughput and Computing-efficient LLM Serving System
