Open-source baselines (DreamOmni2 7B+5B+12B, FluxKontext 5B+12B, Qwen Image 7B+20B, BAGEL 7B+7B): INSET achieves overall image consistency 0.93 vs. next-best Qwen Image at 0.69; overall text consistency 0.75 vs. next-best DreamOmni2 at 0.57. Performance advantage grows with input count: at five objects INSET leads Qwen Image by 0.29 (image) and 0.51 (text). Closed-source comparison (GPT-4o, Nano Banana, Seedream 4.0): INSET matches or slightly exceeds on image consistency (0.93 vs. 0.91 Seedream, 0.91 GPT-4o) but trails on text consistency (0.75 vs. 0.81 GPT-4o, 0.85 Seedream). Ablation baseline (BAGEL fine-tuned on same data without interleaved formulation): 0.61/0.45 overall — INSET improves this by 0.32/0.30. The scale tested is standard (2–5 reference objects, single-image output); no stress-scale experiments at 10+ objects or higher resolutions are reported. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The native interleaved token placement for generation (as opposed to understanding) is a meaningful architectural choice not previously demonstrated at this scale; it is novel in the generation context, though the locality hypothesis for transformers is well established in NLP. The ViT-only (no VAE) reference encoding for multi-image generation is a clear design contribution validated by ablation. InterleaveBench is a new benchmark, though it is author-constructed and judge-evaluated by an in-house model. The data engine is an engineering contribution assembling known components (VLMs, SAM, DAM, LLMs) into a new pipeline — competent execution of known ideas rather than a conceptual breakthrough. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations in Table 2 isolate four components (baseline, Image First, w/o video data, w/ VAE feature) and are reported on InterleaveBench with the same metrics as the main table — this is methodologically sound. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported. Code, model weights, and InterleaveBench data are not released at time of submission (no repository URL mentioned). The benchmark and the LLM judge are both ByteDance internal products (Doubao-Seed-1.6), creating a Goodhart's Law risk: the model may be implicitly optimized toward the judge's preferences. The training mixture ratio is stated but never ablated. Baselines are reformatted to use Image First inputs for compatibility with InterleaveBench, introducing a potential confound — weaker baselines may be partially penalized by the format conversion rather than by a genuine modeling gap. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (system trained and evaluated experimentally). - Practical: INSET is a 7B+7B parameter Mixture-of-Transformers system fine-tuned from BAGEL; it runs on commercially available multi-GPU hardware at 1024px resolution but requires substantial VRAM (two 7B models), making single-consumer-GPU deployment impractical. - Efficient: Compute cost is not reported (no training GPU-hours, no inference latency numbers); two-stage CFG at inference adds overhead relative to single-pass models, but the magnitude is unstated. - Effective: Evidence on InterleaveBench shows clear improvement in multi-object identity binding over open-source baselines on a ByteDance-constructed benchmark; independent validation on third-party benchmarks is absent. - Scalable: Tested on 2–5 reference objects; behavior at 10+ references or with larger base models is not evaluated. The authors claim advantages grow with input complexity, but this is demonstrated only within the 2–5 object range. No software artifact, trained model checkpoint, or dataset is released at preprint time. Strengths: - The native interleaved formulation is a concrete, testable architectural hypothesis — not just a data recipe — and the ablation directly isolates its contribution (+0.07 image, +0.09 text overall vs. Image First on identical data). - Dropping VAE latents for reference images is a counter-intuitive but empirically validated decision: it improves both image consistency (+0.11) and text consistency (+0.19) by eliminating pixel copying and token bloat. - The video-based data track addresses a real failure mode (copy-paste without state adaptation) and is verified to add meaningful capability: removing it drops text consistency from 0.75 to 0.60 overall. - The benchmark evaluation protocol (LLM-as-Judge with identity-preserving vs. instruction-driven variation distinction, plus VQA-based text consistency) is more nuanced than CLIP/DINO-only holistic embeddings. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that INSET slightly trails closed-source models in text consistency and attribute this to limitations of the underlying text-to-image generation base model (BAGEL). No other limitations are explicitly stated. The preprint does not acknowledge the benchmark/judge conflict of interest, the absence of code/data release, or the narrow 2–5 object evaluation range. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most serious issue is that InterleaveBench is authored by ByteDance and judged by Doubao-Seed-1.6 (also ByteDance), and INSET is also a ByteDance product — this trifecta makes the quantitative claims unverifiable without independent replication. No results on established third-party benchmarks (DreamBench++, OmniContext) are included in the main evaluation, even though both are mentioned in related work. The training mixture ratio (0.2:0.2:0.1:0.5) is stated without any sensitivity analysis — it is plausible that this ratio was tuned to maximize InterleaveBench scores. The inference guidance scales (s₁=4.0, s₂=1.5) are stated without ablation. The paper does not report parameter-matched comparisons: INSET (7B+7B) is smaller than DreamOmni2 (7B+5B+12B) and Qwen Image (7B+20B) by total parameters, but the gap is not analyzed to determine how much architectural choice vs. scale drives results. Finally, the "emerging" multimodal editing capability in Section 4.4 is demonstrated only qualitatively — no quantitative editing benchmark is included. Bottom Line: INSET demonstrates a well-motivated and ablation-supported formulation for multi-image generation, but the reliance on author-owned benchmark infrastructure and an in-house judge model means the quantitative margins cannot be accepted at face value until independently replicated on third-party benchmarks.
Search Results 2605.12305 PDF
#146 — Moderately Innovative (53)

Grounded Explainable Recommendation with Faithfulness Evaluation on Amazon Reviews

ejournal.ubhara.ac.id · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces RGXRec, a hybrid recommender that linearly combines SVD collaborative filtering with TF-IDF review similarity, paired with an end-to-end evaluation framework that jointly measures ranking quality and exact model-level explanation faithfulness via term-attribution decomposition. - Why it's important: Post-hoc review quotes attached to interaction-only recommenders routinely score as high on coverage and ROUGE as genuinely grounded explanations, masking the fact that the quoted text had zero causal influence on the model's ranking decision. - How it does it: TF-IDF cosine similarity decomposes additively across terms, so the score contribution of any extracted aspect set can be computed exactly — no perturbation or retraining — giving a closed-form faithfulness ratio at negligible cost. - The good: On Luxury Beauty, RGXRec achieves NDCG@10 of 0.3606, outperforming both ReviewSim (0.2962) and SVD-MF (0.2766), while maintaining non-zero textual faithfulness (0.0929) that interaction-only baselines cannot provide by definition. - The missing: No neural review-aware baseline (DeepCoNN, NARRE, IReGNN) is included despite the paper positioning itself against exactly that literature, making it impossible to assess where RGXRec stands relative to the current field. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers seeking a clean, fully specified reproducible baseline and evaluation framework for faithful explainable recommendation; likely insufficient for practitioners who need comparison against modern neural methods.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (65) and Novelty (62) carry the score: exploiting TF-IDF's additive decomposability to get exact, perturbation-free term-attribution faithfulness is a genuinely clean move, and the finding that Coverage/ROUGE/sentiment are numerically identical across faithful and unfaithful explanations is a concrete, well-evidenced indictment of common practice. Rigor (50) and Completeness (50) pull the composite down hard: no neural review-aware baseline (DeepCoNN/NARRE/IReGNN) despite the paper framing itself against exactly those, the α=0.75 default tuned on the same eval datasets (mild Goodhart), no released code, and faithfulness defined only over the 25% TF-IDF component while 75% of the score comes from the un-attributed SVD branch. Resilience (50) suffers from the missing baselines that make the ranking numbers uninterpretable relative to current practice. Composite ≈ 53.
empirical Novelty 62 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 50 Resilience 50 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Recommender systems in review-rich e-commerce platforms routinely attach review quotes to explain their outputs, but in nearly all practical systems these explanations are post-hoc: the quotes are selected after ranking and have no formal connection to the scoring function. This means a model can score high on human-readable explanation metrics (ROUGE, coverage, sentiment agreement) while the quoted text played no role whatsoever in producing the recommendation. The field further lacks a reproducible evaluation setting that simultaneously reports ranking quality and whether the highlighted evidence actually accounts for the model's score — the two are almost never measured together in a single pipeline. The gap is practically consequential because users and auditors who rely on such explanations for trust or scrutability receive misleading signals. Key Idea The core technical insight is that TF-IDF cosine similarity is an inner product over a non-negative sparse vector space, so the contribution of any subset of terms to the total similarity score is exactly the sum of their individual term products — an additive decomposition that requires no approximation, no gradient computation, and no model perturbation. By constructing the recommender's text component around TF-IDF, the paper inherits closed-form faithfulness for free: the faithfulness ratio is simply the attributed mass of extracted aspect terms divided by the total s_text score. This is the trick that makes the faithfulness metric lightweight and exact. Methodology The pipeline has six stages. (1) Data: Amazon Review Data (2018 release), two categories — Luxury Beauty (2,382 users, 1,047 items, 21,911 interactions) and Video Games (36,869 users, 12,851 items, 321,753 interactions) — after binarizing ratings ≥ 4 and applying iterative 5-core pruning. (2) Splitting: chronological leave-one-out per user (last interaction = test, second-last = val, rest = train). (3) Five models: Pop (popularity counts), MetaGraphKNN (graph neighbor counting on also-buy/also-view edges), SVD-MF (TruncatedSVD, 64 components, interaction matrix), ReviewSim (TF-IDF cosine on concatenated review documents), and RGXRec (linear hybrid s(u,i) = α·s_cf(u,i) + (1-α)·s_text(u,i), α=0.75). (4) Evidence extraction: top-5 shared TF-IDF terms between user and item vectors; 1 user sentence + 2 item sentences scored by aspect-term substring count. (5) Faithfulness metric: Faithfulness_RGXRec(u,i) = (1-α)·Σ_{t∈A(u,i)} t_u(t)·t_i(t) / s_text(u,i). (6) Evaluation: sampled ranking with 99 negatives per user, reporting NDCG@{5,10,20}, Recall@{5,10,20}, MRR; explanation quality via evidence coverage (top-20 term overlap with held-out review), ROUGE-1 F1, Hu-Liu sentiment agreement; faithfulness via the term-attribution ratio plus comprehensiveness drop and sufficiency similarity. Three design decisions are well-justified: the α=0.75 default is selected from an explicit sweep over {0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0}; sampled evaluation with a fixed seed is standard in the implicit top-N literature; and the document truncation limits (20-30 reviews per entity) balance coverage against scalability. Key Results On Luxury Beauty, RGXRec achieves NDCG@10 = 0.3606, Recall@10 = 0.4790, MRR = 0.3381 — the best among all five methods, surpassing ReviewSim (0.2962) and SVD-MF (0.2766). On Video Games, RGXRec achieves NDCG@10 = 0.2341, below MetaGraphKNN (0.2991) and SVD-MF (0.2711) but above Pop (0.1926), indicating that adding TF-IDF signal does not collapse ranking when the text signal is weak. The most striking finding is that SVD-MF post-hoc, ReviewSim, and RGXRec all produce identical explanation quality values on both datasets (e.g., Luxury Beauty: Coverage = 0.2369, ROUGE-1 = 0.2347, SentAgree = 0.8984 for all three) — demonstrating that plausibility metrics alone cannot distinguish a faithful from an unfaithful explanation. Faithfulness diverges sharply: ReviewSim = 0.3715 (LB) / 0.2993 (VG); RGXRec = 0.0929 (LB) / 0.0748 (VG); SVD-MF = 0.0 by definition. The dominant failure mode in Video Games is NoSharedAspects (35.3%), consistent with diverse user vocabulary. Baselines are all shallow classical methods; no modern neural review-aware model is included. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The hybrid model itself (TruncatedSVD + TF-IDF linear interpolation) is a standard combination of known techniques applied to a familiar dataset. The genuinely novel contribution is the exact closed-form term-attribution faithfulness metric and the joint evaluation framework that operationalizes the distinction between post-hoc plausibility and model-grounded faithfulness. The empirical demonstration that coverage and ROUGE are agnostic to faithfulness — that SVD-MF post-hoc and ReviewSim are numerically indistinguishable on plausibility metrics despite one having zero faithfulness — is a concrete, well-evidenced finding. Rigor & Reproducibility All hyperparameters and random seeds are fully disclosed (Table 5), and the deterministic evaluation protocol means results are replicable without code. The α sensitivity sweep (Table 11) and component ablation (Table 10) adequately isolate contributions. No error bars or significance tests are reported; with 36,869 test users in Video Games this is not a serious concern for point estimates, but statistical testing would strengthen Luxury Beauty comparisons (2,382 users). Code and model weights are not publicly released — no repository URL is mentioned. Two methodological concerns warrant flagging: (1) sampled top-N evaluation with 99 negatives is known to be biased relative to full-catalog ranking and can reorder methods; (2) the Video Games sensitivity sweep (Table 11) uses a 5,000-user subset while Luxury Beauty uses the full test set, introducing an inconsistency in cross-domain comparisons of the α trade-off. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs entirely on CPU with commodity hardware; SVD fitting on Video Games (321K interactions) completes in ~40 seconds; TF-IDF construction in under 10 seconds. - Efficient: Memory and compute are dominated by the sparse TF-IDF vectors and a 64-component SVD; the faithfulness computation scales linearly with test users and requires no model re-runs, making it negligible overhead. - Effective: Solves the stated joint ranking+faithfulness problem on Luxury Beauty; on Video Games, ranking is below the collaborative baseline, so the explainability benefit comes at a measurable accuracy cost that the sensitivity analysis quantifies. - Scalable: Tested up to ~321K interactions and ~37K users; no experiments beyond these scales; the paper claims linear scaling for faithfulness computation but provides no empirical validation at larger scale. No software artifact (library, CLI, trained model, or dataset) is released. Strengths - The closed-form faithfulness metric is the most technically creative element: exploiting TF-IDF additive decomposability to get exact attribution without perturbation is a clean, non-obvious move that distinguishes this from prior comprehensiveness/sufficiency approaches. - The empirical demonstration that post-hoc and grounded explanations are indistinguishable by Coverage, ROUGE-1, and sentiment agreement (Tables 8-9) is the paper's strongest and most practically relevant finding — it directly indicts a common evaluation practice. - The failure analysis (Figure 10 distribution, Figures 11-12 examples) is specific and honest, correctly identifying NoSharedAspects as the dominant failure mode and connecting it to the domain-specific vocabulary hypothesis. - The full hyperparameter table and fixed-seed protocol make the work replicable from the paper alone, without released code. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge two limitations: (1) the extractive explanation module relies on surface lexical overlap and misses paraphrases and multi-word aspects; (2) sampled ranking with one positive item per user is a known limitation of the evaluation protocol. Both are stated directly in the Discussion section, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No neural review-aware baseline is included (HFT, DeepCoNN, NARRE, IReGNN are all cited but none compared against), despite the paper's introduction framing the problem in terms of these methods; this omission makes the ranking numbers uninterpretable relative to current practice. - The faithfulness metric covers only the TF-IDF component; at α=0.75, 75% of RGXRec's score derives from the SVD collaborative component for which no faithfulness notion is defined — the paper does not address how to interpret an explanation grounded in 25% of the score. - The identical Coverage/ROUGE/SentAgree scores across SVD-MF post-hoc, ReviewSim, and RGXRec (Tables 8-9) reveal that the explanation quality metrics are measuring the same extracted sentences regardless of model, undermining their usefulness as discriminative evaluation signals in this setting. - The α=0.75 default is selected to maximize NDCG on the validation/test sets of the same two datasets used for evaluation — a mild form of Goodhart's Law; the generalizability of this tuned default to other domains is not assessed. - No user study is conducted; all explanation quality metrics are automated proxies against held-out reviews, which are from the same corpus as training reviews and share vocabulary by construction. Bottom Line The paper's core contribution — a reproducible joint evaluation framework that exposes the gap between post-hoc explanation plausibility and model-grounded faithfulness — is valuable and clearly demonstrated, but the absence of any neural review-aware baseline means a practitioner cannot determine whether the lightweight hybrid is competitive with, or simply outclassed by, the methods it implicitly targets.
Search Results
#147 — Moderately Innovative (53)

IndusAgent: Reinforcing Open-Vocabulary Industrial Anomaly Detection with Agentic Tools (42 ▲ )

Rongbin Tan, Fangfang Lin, Zhenlong Yuan, Min Qiu, Kejin Cui, Mengmeng Wang, Yi Wang, Zijian Song, Zhiyuan Wang, Jiyuan Wang, Yue Wang, Shuhan Song§, Huawei Cao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 21 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces IndusAgent, an agentic post-training framework that fine-tunes an 8B MLLM (Qwen3-VL-8B) with domain-aligned SFT and a GRPO-based reinforcement learning objective to perform open-vocabulary industrial anomaly detection by dynamically calling four external diagnostic tools. - Why it's important: Passive MLLMs fail at industrial inspection because fine-grained defects (microscopic scratches, spacing deviations, low-contrast stains) are diluted by global visual encoding and because generic conversational training misaligns reasoning with strict diagnostic protocols, causing hallucinations that are costly in manufacturing. - How it does it: The core mechanism is an accuracy-gated reward — binary diagnostic correctness gates all subsidiary rewards (localization IoU, defect-type semantics, tool utility) multiplicatively — so the agent learns to invoke external tools (high-res cropping, texture enhancement, geometric measurement, normalcy-prior retrieval) only when doing so provably improves the final binary decision. - The good: An 8B model achieves 83.4% average balanced accuracy across five benchmarks, outperforming both GPT-4.1 (77.5%) and the nearest open-source MLLM (Anomaly-OV 7B at 79.6%), with ablations confirming that removing SFT, RL, or the tool library each degrades performance substantially (e.g., dropping from 76.8% to 55.5% on VisA when SFT is removed). - The missing: The Tprior normalcy-prior tool appears to call Qwen3-VL-Max (a frontier model orders of magnitude larger) at inference time based on the first-round routing prompt; this is not disclosed clearly in the main paper and renders size-based comparisons against 8B baselines misleading. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building tool-augmented inspection agents and researchers studying GRPO reward design for agentic tasks, but the opaque Tprior implementation and absence of pixel-level evaluation metrics require scrutiny before adopting reported numbers as benchmark scores.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. The accuracy-gated multiplicative reward is a principled idea (Novelty 68, Non-Obviousness 64) with a careful category-disjoint training protocol. But Rigor (47) and Resilience (42) collapse the composite: the Tprior tool appears to call Qwen3-VL-Max (a frontier model) at inference, undisclosed in the main results, which makes the headline claim that an 8B model beats GPT-4.1 misleading and would not survive adversarial review; the Δconf reward is computed from the model's own log-probabilities (a circularity/reward-gaming risk), there are no error bars, code/data are unreleased, and ablations cover only 3 of 5 benchmarks. Completeness (50) suffers because the four acknowledged limitations notably omit the very Tprior transparency issue that undermines the central comparison. Weighted: 0.20*68 + 0.25*47 + 0.15*64 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*42 + 0.10*45 = 53.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 47 Non-Obv 64 Completeness 50 Resilience 42 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Open-vocabulary industrial anomaly detection requires identifying defects in unseen product categories without paired normal reference images, defect-vocabulary constraints, or category-specific training data. Traditional non-LLM approaches (reconstruction networks, memory banks, normalizing flows) assume closed-set categories and require extensive normal-sample collections per category, making them brittle when novel products or defect morphologies appear. MLLMs offer zero-shot generalization but fail in industrial settings for two reasons: their conversational training does not align with the rigorous, structured diagnostic protocols inspection demands, and their global visual encoders lose fine-grained defect signals (sub-millimeter scratches, component spacing deviations) to perceptual dilution — a phenomenon where the defect region constitutes a small fraction of a high-resolution image and is compressed away during tokenization. Key Idea: The core insight is that a binary multiplicative gate on the final diagnostic correctness should control all subsidiary rewards during RL, rather than allowing additive rewards for localization, defect-type prediction, and tool invocation to accumulate independently of whether the agent diagnosed correctly. This prevents the policy from gaming rewards by hallucinating plausible defect regions or invoking tools indiscriminately: the agent earns tool-use credit only when tool feedback demonstrably shifts its confidence toward the correct binary answer and that answer is correct. Concretely, R(τ) = R_acc(τ) · (1 + αR_loc + βR_type + γR_tool) + R_format, where R_acc ∈ {0,1} acts as the multiplicative gate. Methodology: The pipeline has three sequential stages. First, Indus-CoT construction: Qwen3-VL-Max (the teacher) processes ~3,000 images sampled from Real-IAD after removing categories overlapping with all five test benchmarks (toothbrush, zipper, pcb, transistor1 removed); the teacher generates structured reasoning trajectories covering global perception, tool routing, tool observation, and final verification, validated with LLM-as-judge scoring. Each trajectory contains <think>, optional <call_tool>/<observation> blocks, <location>, <type>, and <answer> tags. Second, supervised fine-tuning: Qwen3-VL-Instruct-8B is fine-tuned for one epoch on Indus-CoT using a selective masking strategy that computes loss only over reasoning tokens, not over the input image and instruction context. Third, GRPO-based tool-augmented RL: G=4 trajectories per query are sampled; per-trajectory rewards are computed with the gated formulation; GRPO normalizes advantages within the group and applies KL regularization against a frozen reference policy. The tool suite contains four instruments: Tcrop (unsupervised foreground extraction + center-crop fallback, no ground-truth boxes), Tenhance (CLAHE, Canny edge detection), Tmeasure (geometric distances and angles from agent-specified coordinates), and Tprior (normalcy priors from an external retriever described as "domain-knowledge retriever or API," implemented in training data with calls to Qwen3-VL-Max). Confidence improvement Δconf for the tool-utility reward is estimated from normalized log-probability margin differences before and after tool observations. Evaluation uses balanced accuracy as the primary metric across MVTec-AD, VisA, MPDD, DTD, and SDD. Key Results: IndusAgent (8B) achieves 83.4% average balanced accuracy: MVTec-AD 83.6%, VisA 76.8%, MPDD 72.7%, DTD 95.6%, SDD 88.9%. Strong open-source baselines include Anomaly-OV (7B, 79.6%), Qwen2.5-VL (72B, 72.2%), and AnomalyR1 (3B, 60.7%). Commercial baselines include GPT-4.1 (77.5%), Claude-Sonnet-4 (73.4%), GPT-4.1-mini (72.3%). The claimed 9.3% improvement over "SOTA" refers specifically to MVTec-AD vs. Anomaly-OV's 74.3%; against GPT-4.1's 81.9% on MVTec, the margin narrows to 1.7%. Anomaly recall averages 86.3% vs. 79.1% for IAD-R1 and 72.5% for Claude-Sonnet-4. Ablation on three benchmarks shows: removing SFT drops VisA from 76.8% to 55.5%; removing RL drops VisA from 76.8% to 57.6%; removing tools drops VisA from 76.8% to 67.5%. Per-tool ablation shows Tcrop is the most critical for VisA (68.6% without it), Tenhance is most critical for DTD (88.8% without it). No error bars or multiple seeds are reported. Training runs on 4×A100-80GB; SFT takes 21.6 minutes, RL takes 23.4 hours. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (training corpus). The combination of category-disjoint SFT-then-GRPO post-training for tool-augmented IAD is the primary contribution; Indus-CoT is a training corpus (not an evaluation benchmark). The individual components — GRPO, tool-augmented MLLMs, SFT cold-starting RL — are all established techniques; novelty lies in their composition for open-vocabulary IAD and specifically in the accuracy-gated multiplicative reward design that ties tool-invocation credit to diagnostic correctness. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative but conducted on only 3 of 5 benchmarks and do not include error bars or multiple seeds; the NeurIPS checklist explicitly acknowledges no error bars are reported. The category-disjoint training protocol is carefully executed with semantic normalization for naming variants. However, several concerns arise. First, the Indus-CoT training data is generated by Qwen3-VL-Max using ground-truth supervision constraints (anomaly label, location, type) injected into the teacher prompt — the trajectories are conditioned on correct answers, raising the risk of teacher-biased supervision that does not faithfully represent zero-shot inference. Second, the Tprior tool appears to invoke Qwen3-VL-Max at inference time (the first-round tool routing prompt in the appendix names "qwen3vlmax-api" as a tool option), which means the 8B model effectively has access to a frontier model during inference; this is not disclosed in the main results table and makes parameter-count comparisons misleading. Third, code and Indus-CoT data are not yet released (promised upon publication). Fourth, the confidence improvement Δconf used as reward relies on log-probabilities of the fine-tuned model itself, creating a circularity risk where the model can game the reward by inflating confidence in its own (potentially wrong) answers through tool feedback. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Training runs on 4×A100-80GB GPUs; the 8B backbone runs on a single A100 at inference, though if Tprior calls Qwen3-VL-Max the inference pipeline requires access to a frontier API, increasing cost and latency. - Efficient: Average tool invocations per query are 1.12 (MVTec), 1.06 (VisA), 0.72 (DTD), which is economical; RL training at 23.4 hours on 4×A100s is feasible for practitioners. - Effective: Balanced accuracy on held-out category-disjoint benchmarks shows genuine generalization beyond the SFT training distribution, and recall improvements on hard datasets (MPDD +17.4%) suggest the RL objective addresses the false-negative problem; however, evaluation remains at image-level binary classification — no pixel-level segmentation metrics are reported despite the framework producing anomaly location predictions. - Scalable: Tested only at standard benchmark scale (hundreds to low thousands of images per dataset); no stress-test at production volumes (millions of inspection images, real-time throughput requirements) is reported or discussed. The work delivers a trained model checkpoint (unreleased at time of submission) and a training corpus; no public library or CLI is provided yet. Strengths: - The accuracy-gated reward is a genuinely principled design choice that addresses a real pathology (tool-use reward hacking) with a clean formulation; the ablation row "w/. Base" vs. the full reward confirms the gating matters (+7.6% on MVTec over the non-gated RL baseline). - Category-disjoint training protocol with semantic normalization (pcb vs. pcb1/2/3/4) is methodologically careful and substantially strengthens generalization claims compared to much prior IAD work. - Tool-selection statistics (Table 7) showing dataset-adaptive behavior (Tcrop dominates for object datasets, Tenhance for texture datasets) provide mechanistic evidence that the agent learned meaningful tool-routing policies rather than defaulting to fixed pipelines. - The SFT cold-start before RL, motivated by observed reward hacking and format collapse in direct RL (inspired by R1-Zero findings), is empirically validated and practically important for reproducibility. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state four limitations in Appendix H: (1) active inspection introduces inference overhead versus single-pass MLLMs; (2) inaccurate tool outputs (bad crops, noisy enhancement maps, incomplete priors) can mislead the agent; (3) evaluation covers only image-level binary classification, not pixel-level localization; (4) Indus-CoT generated by a strong teacher may carry prompt-template or teacher bias. The authors confront these candidly, which is a credibility positive. They do not acknowledge the Tprior/Qwen3-VL-Max inference-time issue. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The Tprior tool's implementation is not transparently disclosed: the first-round routing prompt in the appendix lists "qwen3vlmax-api" as a callable tool, strongly suggesting Qwen3-VL-Max is queried at inference time. If so, IndusAgent is not an 8B system for purposes of the Table 1 comparison — it is an 8B orchestrator with access to a frontier model, and comparing it against standalone 7B/8B baselines is unfair. The paper should either disclose this clearly and add baselines with the same frontier access, or demonstrate results without Tprior to isolate the 8B model's capability. - The training set size of ~3,000 samples is very small; the paper provides no learning curve or data efficiency analysis to show whether the gains are robust to this particular size or would saturate/improve with more data. - Balanced accuracy as the sole primary metric obscures precision-recall tradeoffs that matter for manufacturing deployment (false alarms shut down production lines; missed defects cause field failures). F1 is reported in appendix for only 4 of 5 datasets and is not the primary claimed metric. - The hyperparameter sensitivity table (Table 5) is reported without confidence intervals; the optimal (α=0.8, β=0.6, γ=0.5) configuration appears tuned on the same benchmarks used for evaluation, raising overfitting risk in reward weighting. - The concurrent work AgentIAD is acknowledged but not included as a baseline, with a setting-difference justification; readers cannot verify whether the setting difference fully explains any performance gap. Bottom Line: The accuracy-gated reward formulation and category-disjoint SFT-then-RL pipeline are genuine contributions that yield strong results, but the undisclosed use of Qwen3-VL-Max as Tprior during inference is a methodological transparency failure that must be resolved before the benchmark numbers can be taken at face value.
Recommended 2605.20682 PDF
#148 — Moderately Innovative (53)

Retrieval First: LLM -Assisted Type Inference for Automatic Test Case Generation in JavaScript

repository.tudelft.nl · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline that builds a vector database of semantically categorized code snippets (ASTs, call sites, JSDoc, control-flow hints, etc.) from a JavaScript project and uses them as selective context for LLM-based type inference to improve automatic test case generation. - Why it's important: Search-based test generation for dynamically typed languages like JavaScript is severely hampered by the absence of explicit type information, forcing search algorithms to explore a much larger input space; more accurate type inference directly constrains that space and improves test effectiveness. - How it does it: Source files are parsed into ASTs from which nine categories of code snippets are extracted, embedded via a local vector model, stored in Qdrant, and retrieved at inference time using cosine similarity against the function under test, with the top-N snippets supplied to an LLM alongside a Chain-of-Thought prompt—replacing large monolithic source file or AST context with compact, semantically targeted evidence. - The good: The RAG approach achieves a statistically significant Top-1 accuracy of 39.6% and MRR@5 of 0.57 across 50 benchmark functions, a 29% relative accuracy improvement over non-RAG LLM approaches and 85% fewer tokens consumed, with the advantage largest precisely where it matters most—complex, user-defined, and package-dependent types. - The missing: The benchmark is 50 functions from 3 open-source TypeScript projects; absolute accuracy numbers remain low (best Top-1 = 49% on a single model-approach pair), and the comparison to SynTest's probabilistic baseline uses only one model configuration, leaving open whether the finding generalizes across models. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers or practitioners building LLM-assisted tooling for dynamically typed test generation, particularly those interested in RAG-based context construction for code; skip if you need peer-reviewed evidence or results at meaningful scale.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (56) is solid for an MSc thesis — Friedman+Nemenyi with effect sizes, 10 repeated runs, Zenodo replication package — but a 50-function/3-project benchmark, no per-snippet-category ablation, and a single-model SynTest comparison cap it. Completeness (50) and Resilience (45) pull the composite down: the load-bearing motivating claim (better types improve SBST coverage) is assumed, never demonstrated end-to-end, and absolute ~40% Top-1 accuracy leaves most inferences wrong. The counterintuitive AST-underperforms-Source finding lifts Non-Obviousness (60), but this is a NEW METHOD from standard components, not a new formulation.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 56 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 50 Resilience 45 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Search-based software testing (SBST) tools such as EvoSuite and Testful rely on type information to constrain the search space of possible inputs. In statically typed languages this is available directly; in JavaScript it is absent, so search algorithms must explore a substantially larger input space, degrading coverage and requiring more iterations. Prior probabilistic approaches (JSNice, SynTest) handle simple primitive types well but fail on complex user-defined objects, higher-order functions, and types that depend on external packages—precisely the cases most common in large real-world codebases. Neural approaches (DeepTyper, NL2Type) require domain-specific training data (JSDoc comments) present in only ~0.1% of JavaScript projects. LLM-based approaches address this generalization problem but current implementations pass entire ASTs or source files to the model, inflating token counts, degrading attention quality on long contexts, and increasing latency and cost. Key Idea: The core insight is that type-relevant information is distributed across a codebase rather than concentrated in the function under test, and that selective, semantics-guided retrieval of small, categorized code snippets produces better type inference than providing larger but monolithic or structurally noisy context. Decoupling context selection from prompt construction via a vector store lets even small local models access project-wide evidence that they could not otherwise fit in a context window. Methodology: The system has two phases. In the offline setup phase, all JavaScript source files in a project are parsed using the Babel parser into ASTs, and nine categories of snippets are extracted: function definitions (parameter usage and body semantics), call sites (argument shapes and return value usage), object literal shapes, variable declarations (initializer-based type hints), class declarations and inheritance relationships, JSDoc annotations, imports/exports, literal values, and control-flow hints (typeof checks, switch statements). Each snippet is embedded using a configurable model (llama-embed-nemotron 8B, Qwen3-Embedding 4B, or bert-base-uncased 110M) and stored in a Qdrant vector database with metadata. At inference time, the function under test is embedded as a query, the top-N most similar snippets are retrieved (exact-function-under-test hits are excluded), and these snippets plus the raw source of the function-under-test are provided to the LLM via a structured Chain-of-Thought prompt requesting five ranked type predictions per identifier in TOON/JSON-compact format. The LLM is instructed to decompose complex types into their structural attributes rather than returning nominal class names, enabling finer-grained search space restriction for SBST tools. Two baselines are compared: Source File inference (original source + transitive import declarations, ~5.6K tokens) and AST inference (Babel-extracted AST summaries, ~13.1K tokens). Evaluation metrics are Top-1 through Top-5 accuracy and MRR@5; statistical significance is assessed via the Friedman test (non-parametric repeated measures) and Nemenyi post-hoc test with α = 0.05. Eight LLM configurations are tested (5 local, 3 cloud), and an efficiency score of MRR@5 / inference_time is computed to account for the iterative use pattern of SBST. Key Results: Averaged across all 8 model configurations, RAG achieves Top-1 accuracy of 39.6% versus Source File 30.3% and AST 20.1%; MRR@5 of 0.57, 0.47, and 0.34 respectively. RAG uses ~3.9K tokens on average versus ~5.6K for Source File and ~13.1K for AST—an 85% computation-time reduction over AST. The Friedman test confirms statistically significant differences (chi2 = 97.48, p ≈ 0) and the Nemenyi post-hoc shows a large effect between RAG and AST, medium effect between RAG and Source File, and no significant difference between Source File and AST. The best local model is Qwen 3 Coder (MRR@5 = 0.66 on RAG, 34.71s inference); the best cloud model is GPT-5 mini (MRR@5 = 0.61, 12.23s, efficiency ≈ 0.050). A surprising finding is that the AST approach—despite providing more structured context—underperforms Source File by 10.2% on average, attributed to attention bias from inflated token counts and representation unfamiliarity. Against SynTest's probabilistic proportional sampling baseline (evaluated only with Qwen 3 Coder), RAG achieves 0.32 versus 0.16 average accuracy, a ~100% relative gain; Source File (0.20) and Proportional Sampling (0.16) are not statistically distinguishable. For primitive types, probabilistic inference remains competitive due to speed advantages in iterative search. Absolute numbers are low: even the best per-file RAG result (workbench.ts with GPT-4) reaches only 0.74 Top-1; several files score 0.00 across all approaches. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The RAG-based type inference pipeline for JavaScript test generation is a genuine methodological contribution; the nine-category snippet extraction schema tailored to type-relevant code evidence is specific to this problem. The empirical finding that selective retrieval outperforms both larger structured (AST) and direct (source file) context—and that retrieval quality matters more than context representation quality—is a substantive insight. The components individually (RAG, LLM type inference, AST parsing) are standard; the contribution is their composition and the domain-specific snippet taxonomy. Rigor & Reproducibility: Each experiment is run 10 times to capture LLM stochasticity, with temperature fixed at 0.1. Statistical tests are appropriate (non-parametric Friedman + Nemenyi post-hoc with correct effect size reporting). Ablations across embedding models (Table 5) reveal how retrieval distribution varies by architecture, providing some insight into which components matter. However, there is no ablation isolating the effect of individual snippet categories—the paper reports retrieval frequency distributions (Table 5) but not a drop-in-accuracy experiment from removing each category. The benchmark is small (50 functions, 3 projects), and the comparison against SynTest uses only one model, which limits the generalizability of RQ3 claims. A replication package is publicly released (Zenodo DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19496755). Data leakage risk is partially addressed: training corpora were inspected, and only StarCoder 2 has confirmed exposure to Stack v2 which does not contain the benchmark projects; other models were not verifiably checked. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile): - Practical: All local models run on an Apple M1 Pro with 32 GB unified memory, and the Qdrant vector database is locally deployed, making the full pipeline usable on commodity developer hardware. - Efficient: RAG consumes ~3.9K tokens per inference versus ~13.1K for AST, and inference times range from 12–43 seconds per function; at 34s for the best local model, this is viable for offline pre-analysis but too slow for interactive use. - Effective: RAG demonstrably improves Top-1 accuracy over both alternative LLM approaches and probabilistic inference for complex types on the tested benchmark, though absolute accuracy of ~40% Top-1 means the majority of inferences are wrong, and the downstream impact on actual test coverage improvement is not measured. - Scalable: The paper argues RAG scales better to larger codebases than source file or AST approaches because prompt size does not grow with project size; however, this claim is not validated experimentally—the three benchmark projects are large (VSCode, TypeScript compiler) but only 50 functions are tested, and no stress-test at 10× function count is performed. The work delivers a replication package (code + data) but not a production-ready library or CLI tool. Strengths: - The insight that selective retrieval beats structured-but-larger context (AST underperforms Source File despite richer representation) is counterintuitive and well-evidenced, contradicting the common assumption that more structure always helps LLMs. - The nine-category snippet taxonomy is thoughtfully motivated by JavaScript's type-propagation patterns, not arbitrary—each category is tied to a specific mechanism by which type information flows through JavaScript programs. - The efficiency metric (MRR@5 / inference_time) is well-conceived for the SBST use case, recognizing that partial accuracy is still useful if it eliminates portions of the search space. - Statistical rigor is above typical for an MSc thesis: Friedman + Nemenyi with effect size, 10 repeated runs, concordance analysis. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge external validity concerns (only three projects), conclusion validity concerns from LLM non-determinism (mitigated by 10 runs), and prompt design bias (slightly different prompts across approaches). They note that RAG introduces stochastic variability (highest MAD = 0.19) due to the retrieval component and that AST-based heuristics for binary operations and array/object properties may produce coarse type hints. They also acknowledge that for primitive types, probabilistic methods remain competitive—recommending a hybrid approach as future work. These are direct, credible acknowledgments. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most significant gap is the absence of end-to-end integration with an actual SBST tool (SynTest or EvoSuite-style): the paper infers types and evaluates accuracy against TypeScript ground truth, but never measures whether more accurate type inference actually improves branch coverage or bug detection in generated tests. The paper's motivating claim—that better types reduce search space and improve test generation—is assumed, not demonstrated. Additionally, the comparison with SynTest uses only Qwen 3 Coder; a full cross-model comparison would substantially strengthen RQ3. The paper does not investigate the effect of top-N retrieval count (only the default top-10 is used for the main experiments), leaving a key RAG hyperparameter uncharacterized. The evaluation uses TypeScript-transpiled-to-JavaScript as ground truth, which may penalize the LLMs for producing valid but structurally different types (e.g., union types the TypeScript annotation doesn't enumerate) that would still constrain SBST search effectively. Bottom Line: A practitioner building LLM-assisted type inference for JavaScript test generation will find the RAG approach and snippet taxonomy directly actionable, but should be aware that this is an MSc thesis with a 50-function benchmark and no downstream test coverage validation, so adoption requires independent evaluation on their own codebase and SBST integration.
Search Results
#149 — Moderately Innovative (53)

Vector RAG vs LLM-Compiled Wiki: A Preregistered Comparison on a Small Multi-Domain Research

Theodore O. Cochran · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper reports a preregistered, blinded, two-LLM-judge comparison of single-round Vector RAG against an LLM-compiled markdown wiki on 13 questions over a 24-paper research corpus, decomposing "grounded synthesis" into three separable capabilities: synthesis structure, per-claim citation alignment, and query cost. - Why it's important: Practitioners choosing between RAG and wiki-style compiled-memory architectures have had no quantitative head-to-head comparison to rely on — only Karpathy-style informal intuition — and this study shows the common cost-amortization rationale for wiki is empirically wrong under the tested setup. - How it does it: It preregisters three directional hypotheses (wiki better at synthesis, RAG better at point-source grounding, wiki cheaper per query) with magnitude thresholds and IRR-triggered judge-averaging, then adds post-hoc claim-level citation checking and a decomposition-RAG ablation to decompose what drives the differences. - The good: The post-hoc claim-level grounding analysis is the most surprising result: on the same tier where rubric groundedness favors RAG by −2.0, per-citation supported rates favor wiki 51.9% vs 5.3% — holistic and claim-level metrics disagree in direction, not just magnitude, which is a transferable methodological finding. - The missing: No human evaluation of any kind — all rubric scoring, claim atomization, and citation verification are LLM-based, and the IRR analysis itself shows judge calibration drift large enough to flip direction on the primary grounding hypothesis. - Read it or not: Read it if you are designing or evaluating RAG vs compiled-memory pipelines for research synthesis tasks, particularly for the methodological point about separating synthesis structure, citation alignment, and cost; skip it if you need production-scale generalization, as n=4 and n=3 for the two primary hypotheses, and all evidence is LLM-judged.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Preregistration (OSF, SHA-256-hashed artifacts), Bayesian + bootstrap robustness, and unusually honest IRR disclosure make this credible, and the directional disagreement between holistic and claim-level grounding is a genuinely non-obvious methodological finding (Non-Obviousness 64). What pulls the composite down is Resilience (42) and Impact (40): n=4/n=3 on the primary hypotheses with a fully LLM-judged pipeline whose own calibration drift flips the H2 verdict by direction means the findings are well-motivated hypotheses, not settled results, and the paper ships only reproducer scripts. Preprint with no human evaluation anywhere reduces Rigor (54).
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 54 Non-Obv 64 Completeness 58 Resilience 42 Impact 40
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Practitioners assembling LLM-backed research-reading tools face a concrete architectural choice: retrieve raw chunks from source PDFs at query time (Vector RAG), or compile the corpus into a cross-linked wiki at ingest time and browse that wiki at query time (LLM Wiki). The wiki architecture was popularized by Andrej Karpathy's "agentic markdown wiki" framing, which posits three intuitions: wiki helps with multi-paper synthesis, wiki may lose source fidelity through lossy summarization, and wiki front-loads cost at ingest so per-query costs are lower. As of the authors' literature search (2026-05-08), no preregistered quantitative comparison had been published. Related work — RAPTOR, GraphRAG, OpenScholar, PaperQA2 — applies compiled abstractive memory at scale but does not directly compare against chunk-vector RAG under a controlled evaluation protocol. Key Idea: The core insight is that "grounded research synthesis" is not a single capability but at least three separable ones — synthesis structure (cross-paper narrative coherence), citation alignment (does each cited source support the specific claim it is cited for?), and query cost — and that no single architecture dominates all three. The secondary methodological insight is that rubric-style holistic grounding and claim-level citation precision can disagree in direction, not merely magnitude, because they reward different things: holistic rubrics reward verbatim chunk anchoring (favoring RAG's short, citation-dense answers), while claim-level checking rewards per-citation semantic match (favoring wiki's claim-shaped page structure). Methodology: The study uses a fully crossed, within-question-paired design: the same 24 peer-reviewed papers (8 each from AI ethics & law, climate science, precision medicine, 2017–2026) and the same 13 evaluation questions are answered by both systems using the same query model (Claude Opus 4.7 at `xhigh`). The six question tiers are chronological, conflict, multi-hop, emergence (n=2 each, preregistered wiki-favoring), policy (n=2), and bias-check (n=3, preregistered RAG-favoring). Vector RAG uses document-aware markdown chunking (512 tokens, 50-token overlap), per-chunk contextual enrichment, multi-query expansion, dense+sparse hybrid retrieval, Cohere reranking to top-5, CRAG-inspired corrective validation, and single-round generation. The LLM Wiki compiles papers offline into a cross-linked markdown wiki; at query time a tool-using agent uses `list_pages`, `read_page`, `submit_answer` (MAX_TURNS=30, no query-side prompt caching). Primary judge is GPT-5.4 (cross-family from the answer model); IRR judge is Gemini 2.5 Pro. Both score four rubric criteria (groundedness, structural_integrity, conflict_awareness, inter_paper_mapping) on 1–10 scales with explicit anchors. Three preregistered hypotheses use directional thresholds (not frequentist tests), with IRR-triggered averaging when max judge delta exceeds 2 on any criterion. Two preregistered robustness checks run: bootstrap 95% CI (10,000 resamples) and a Bayesian model (µ∼N(0,4), σ∼HalfNormal(4), NUTS, 4 chains×2000 iter). Post-hoc claim-level grounding atomizes all 466 claims via Claude Opus 4.7, then has GPT-5.4 score each cited claim as supported/partial/contradicted/unsupported against the cited evidence artifact. A post-hoc decomp-RAG ablation decomposes each question into 2–5 sub-questions, runs the same single-round RAG pipeline per sub-question, deduplicates cited chunks, and generates a merged answer. Critical design decision: H3 cost accounting follows preregistered token sums (prompt+completion+thinking) and explicitly excludes embedding/reranker spend; the wiki ingest cost is not adjudicated due to a post-hoc discovery that the telemetry aggregated uncached+cache_creation+cache_read into a single field, making billable cost reconstruction impossible. Key Results: H1 (synthesis): IRR-adjusted wiki advantage on inter_paper_mapping is +6.625 (strongly corroborated, Bayesian P(µ≥+2)=0.967); on structural_integrity it is +1.625, missing the preregistered +2.0 threshold by 0.375 — weakly supported. H2 (point-source groundedness): IRR-adjusted RAG advantage is +0.667 — supported on the registered rule but the secondary judge alone would refute it (primary +2.0, secondary −0.667). H3 (cost): refuted in the opposite direction — wiki uses 1,651,357 query tokens vs RAG's 78,093, a 21× premium rather than a discount, mathematically ruling out any positive-query-count crossover. IRR finding: Gemini 2.5 Pro saturates near 10/10 for wiki on three of four holistic criteria; the judges agree most closely on inter_paper_mapping (the one criterion with a concrete operational definition, RAG means 5.38 vs 5.23 across judges). Post-hoc claim-level grounding: conditional on cited, wiki claims are supported 40.2% vs RAG's 18.9% and unsupported 6.2% vs RAG's 34.1% — the opposite direction from the rubric groundedness verdict on the bias-check tier (5.3% wiki-cited-supported vs 51.9%). Decomp-RAG ablation: closes ~88% of wiki's inter_paper_mapping advantage (gap shrinks from +6.25 to +0.75) and ~87.5% of structural_integrity advantage (+2.00 to +0.25) at 3.4× lower per-query LLM-token cost than wiki; does not close citation precision (19.2% vs 40.2% supported). Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The preregistered head-to-head comparison of vector RAG vs LLM-compiled wiki is claimed to be the first of its kind. Genuinely novel: (1) the empirical refutation of the wiki cost-amortization intuition, (2) the directional disagreement between holistic rubric grounding and claim-level citation precision as a methodological finding, and (3) the three-way decomposition of synthesis advantage into retrieval-coverage vs representation-alignment components via the decomp-RAG ablation. The experimental apparatus — RAG pipeline, wiki compiler, LLM judging — applies known techniques to a new evaluation question; the contribution is the finding, not the method. Rigor & Reproducibility: Preregistration on OSF with SHA-256-hashed artifacts before any judge run is a genuine credibility positive. Bayesian robustness checks (Rhat ≤ 1.003, min ESS ≥ 1,907) and bootstrap CIs are reported alongside point estimates. Code and reproducer scripts are on GitHub; five primary and four post-hoc artifacts are on OSF. Key methodological concerns: n is extremely small (n=4 for H1, n=3 for H2), making the Bayesian prior dominate; no human evaluation anywhere in the pipeline; the IRR analysis reveals judge calibration drift large enough to flip the groundedness verdict by direction (primary supports H2, secondary alone would refute); the decomp ablation is single-judge with no IRR coverage; wiki source-PDF fidelity is not measured, only evidence-artifact alignment; the style confound (wiki prose vs RAG bullet-and-cite) is acknowledged but not controlled. The claim-level grounding analysis is unregistered and correctly labeled exploratory. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (two QA pipelines evaluated via LLM judging on a fixed corpus). - Practical: Both systems run on commercially available APIs (Claude Opus 4.7, GPT-5.4, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Cohere reranker); no specialized hardware required. - Efficient: Wiki uses 21× more query tokens than RAG under the tested setup; RAG totals 3.3 minutes vs wiki's 22.0 minutes wall-clock; decomp-RAG is 3.4× cheaper than wiki in LLM tokens but slower than wiki in wall-clock due to sequential sub-question retrieval. - Effective: Each architecture excels on a different capability: wiki on inter-paper synthesis and citation precision, RAG on query cost, decomp-RAG on synthesis structure at intermediate cost — no single architecture dominates; effectiveness is task-shape-dependent. - Scalable: Tested on 24 papers and 13 questions only; the authors explicitly note transfer to large-corpus or different model families is unverified; the 21× per-query wiki premium rules it out at scale on cost grounds regardless of corpus size. The paper delivers no software artifact beyond reproducer scripts; the contribution is the empirical finding and evaluation methodology. Strengths: - Preregistration with locked hypotheses, thresholds, decision rules, and robustness checks before any judge run — the single most important credibility move in an LLM-judged study. - The directional disagreement between holistic rubric groundedness and claim-level citation precision is a concrete, replicable methodological finding that should inform how any RAG/wiki evaluation is designed. - The decomp-RAG ablation cleanly separates the wiki synthesis advantage into a retrieval-coverage component (closable by sub-question decomposition) and a representation-alignment component (not closable by broader retrieval), which is mechanistically informative beyond the binary RAG-vs-wiki framing. - Honest IRR reporting: publishing that the secondary judge alone would flip the H2 verdict, and that ceiling-judge calibration drift fires on all four criteria, is a credibility-increasing disclosure that most papers in this space omit. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge: no human evaluation anywhere in the pipeline; small n (n=4, n=3) with large standard errors; single corpus and single query model limiting generalizability; uncontrolled style confound between wiki prose and RAG bullet-and-cite; decomp ablation is single-judge with no IRR; one decomposer prompt fixed before the run; wiki ingest cost unadjudicable due to cache-token aggregation; wiki evidence-artifact alignment ≠ source-PDF fidelity (not measured). The authors confront these directly rather than minimizing them, which is credibility-positive in this framework. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most consequential unresolved question — whether wiki compilation actually preserves source-PDF fidelity better or worse than RAG retrieval — is flagged as future work but is the load-bearing assumption behind the H2 rubric-vs-claim-level divergence; the current analysis cannot adjudicate it. The study uses only Claude Opus 4.7 as the answer model, and given that the wiki is compiled by the same model family, there may be a self-enhancement bias (LLM judges preferring text from the same model family) that the cross-family judging setup partially but not completely controls. The corpus is hand-curated with 8 papers per domain — selection bias in which papers and questions were chosen could systematically favor one architecture; the OSF deposit of corpus and questions partially mitigates this but the choice of tiers (pre-labeled wiki-favoring vs RAG-favoring) risks question-selection circularity. The decomp ablation uses only one decomposition prompt without prompt sensitivity analysis; decomposition quality likely varies by question type, and the ablation result (88% gap closure) could be sensitive to this choice. Bottom Line: This preprint delivers a genuine empirical contribution — the cost-amortization intuition for wiki is wrong, holistic and claim-level grounding metrics can disagree in direction, and decomp-RAG partially bridges the synthesis gap — but the tiny n, fully LLM-judged pipeline with demonstrated large calibration drift, and single-corpus scope mean these findings should be treated as well-motivated hypotheses requiring human-evaluated replication before guiding production architecture decisions.
Citations 2605.18490 PDF
#150 — Moderately Innovative (53)

VIP-COP: Context Optimization for Tabular Foundation Models

Yilong Chen, Xueying Ding, Leman Akoglu · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces VIP-COP, a black-box anytime algorithm that estimates Shapley-value-based importance scores for training examples and features to select optimal context subsets for tabular foundation models (TFMs) operating under fixed context-window constraints. - Why it's important: TFMs like TabPFN are limited to fixed context sizes (e.g., 1,024 samples × 100 features for TabPFN-v1); feeding larger datasets triggers out-of-distribution degradation, and existing heuristics select context without accounting for TFM prediction dynamics, wasting a large fraction of the available context budget. - How it does it: Context selection is cast as a cooperative game where validation performance is the coalition value; Shapley values are approximated via an extended KernelSHAP with importance-sampled iterative refinement, Hyperband-inspired multi-temperature parallel runs, and mini-batch SGD updates — all without touching model internals. - The good: Under feature noise, VIP-COP degrades only 6.1% from the clean baseline while all five baselines degrade 46–54%, and achieves 154–241% improvement over those baselines — a qualitative, not merely marginal, advantage. - The missing: All experiments use only TabPFN-v1 as the backbone; no experiments test VIP-COP with newer TFMs (TabPFN-v2, TabICL) that have different context limits and stronger pretraining robustness, limiting generalizability claims. - Read it or not: Researchers working on in-context learning for structured data, test-time compute, or black-box prompt optimization should read this; practitioners deploying TabPFN-v1 on medium-to-large datasets will find the anytime algorithm directly applicable.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. The composite is pulled down by Rigor (45) and Completeness (50): there are no ablations at all, so it is unknown which of the three algorithmic components (importance sampling, multi-temperature scheduling, iterative SGD) is load-bearing; evaluation is restricted to TabPFN-v1, the oldest and most constrained backbone; degenerate 0.500-accuracy tasks are left in the aggregate statistics, inflating improvement percentages; and the code lives at a non-persistent anonymous URL. Novelty (60) and the compelling feature-noise robustness (Non-Obviousness 65) keep it from scoring lower, but the missing ablations and single old backbone leave the contribution preliminary. Weighted composite is 53.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 50 Resilience 50 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Tabular foundation models perform prediction via in-context learning: the labeled training set is fed as context, and a single forward pass produces predictions for test queries — no task-specific training required. This paradigm concentrates the optimization burden on context design. Two concrete problems arise. First, TFMs are pretrained on datasets up to fixed sizes, so feeding larger inputs at inference time creates out-of-distribution behavior and degrades generalization — TabPFN-v1 is limited to 1,024 training examples and 100 features. Second, even within the window, noisy or irrelevant examples and features degrade the signal-to-noise ratio and waste capacity. Existing heuristics (random sampling, k-means clustering, decision-tree routing) optimize model-agnostic objectives and are oblivious to TFM inference dynamics. Soft-prompt optimization methods (TuneTables, ICD) learn abstract pseudo-samples that maximize performance but require gradient access to model internals, produce uninterpretable context tokens, and cannot distinguish informative from noisy inputs — preventing them from handling data augmentation or noisy settings. Key Idea The single core insight is to model context selection as a cooperative game: the validation accuracy when a given subset of training examples and features forms the context is the coalition value, and individual examples and features are the players. Shapley values assign each player a unique fair credit that accounts for all interaction effects with other coalition members. The trick that makes this tractable is extending KernelSHAP — which reduces Shapley estimation to a weighted linear regression over binary inclusion vectors — to work iteratively: rather than sampling coalitions uniformly upfront, VIP-COP alternates between updating Shapley estimates via mini-batch SGD on observed (context, performance) pairs and importance-sampling new contexts biased toward currently high-estimated-value items. Multiple parallel runs with geometrically spaced temperatures (Hyperband-inspired) handle the temperature sensitivity of importance sampling without manual tuning. Methodology VIP-COP takes as input a labeled training set D_train (n × d), a small validation set D_val, and a frozen TFM checkpoint with context limits (n_C, d_C). It jointly optimizes over S = n·𝟙[n>n_C] + d·𝟙[d>d_C] binary inclusion variables via the following loop: (1) Initialization: all S Shapley estimates φ initialized uniformly to 1/S. (2) Multi-temperature parallel runs: ⌊log_η(R)⌋+1 independent runs with temperatures τ = η^(r−r_max), geometrically spaced, run in parallel for R rounds each. (3) Per-round update: sample B contexts via softmax importance sampling P(i|φ,τ); evaluate each via one TFM forward pass (AUROC on D_val); update φ via mini-batch SGD to minimize squared prediction error c·φ ≈ p_val, where c ∈ {0,1}^S is the inclusion indicator. (4) Context selection: take the top-n_C samples and top-d_C features with the largest positive φ values; select the run whose sum of selected φ values is highest. The objective being optimized is validation AUROC. Key design decisions: (a) unweighted least-squares rather than KernelSHAP's original weighted regression (justified by fixing context size), though the approximation quality is not analyzed; (b) global rather than per-query context (preserves batched inference); (c) multi-temperature scheduling rather than fixed temperature (avoids manual tuning at the cost of parallelism). Experiments use TabPFN-v1 (n_C=1,024, d_C=100) on 4 × Tesla V100 GPUs. Key Results Evaluated on 38 multi-class classification datasets from the TALENT benchmark (covering small-d n<50K, small-d n>50K, and large-d subsets), compared against 5 baselines: Random (H1), Ensemble (H2), XLContext (H3), KMeansReps (O1), DT+TFM (O2). In the original HardCOp setting, VIP-COP improves 1.54–14.68% over baselines on average balanced accuracy; statistically significantly better than 4/5 baselines (p<0.001 to p=0.012), not significantly better than DT+TFM (p=0.207). Under sample augmentation (HardCOp4DA), VIP-COP improves 4.80–15.05% over baselines, benefiting +3.43% vs. no-augmentation while baselines show near-zero gain (0.40–5.88%). Under sample noise (HardCOp4DN), VIP-COP degrades only ~1% from clean while baselines degrade 11–16%; improvements of 1.92–27.38% over baselines. Under feature noise, VIP-COP degrades 6.11% while all baselines degrade 46–54%; improvements of 154–241% over baselines. In the critical difference diagram across all settings, VIP-COP achieves average rank 2.20 vs. DT+TFM's 2.80 vs. all others at 3.4–4.5. At R=100 (≈2.5 minutes), VIP-COP occupies the Pareto frontier in performance-vs-runtime. Notable per-dataset anomalies: DT+TFM outperforms VIP-COP on several large-n small-d datasets (shuttle: 0.929 vs. 0.673; accelerometer: 0.729 vs. 0.699); Amazon_employee_access and Click_prediction score 0.500 across all methods, suggesting near-random or degenerate tasks. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. VIP-COP is a genuinely new algorithm for black-box test-time context optimization of TFMs. The framing of context selection as Shapley credit allocation to capture inter-example and inter-feature interactions is novel in the tabular ICL setting. Individual components — KernelSHAP, Hyperband-inspired scheduling, importance sampling — are prior work; the combination, adaptation (fixed-size unweighted regression, joint sample-feature optimization), and application to TFM context are the new contributions. Rigor & Reproducibility Permutation tests across 38 datasets and critical difference diagrams are appropriate statistical tools, stronger than paired t-tests. All methods share identical splits, seed (42), and backbone (TabPFN-v1), ensuring fair comparison. However, no ablations are provided — the paper does not isolate the contribution of importance-sampled exploration vs. uniform sampling, multi-temperature scheduling vs. single-temperature, or iterative SGD refinement vs. a one-shot regression. It is therefore unknown which innovations in the algorithm actually drive the gains. No error bars or confidence intervals appear on individual dataset results. The anonymous code repository (https://anonymous.4open.science/r/VIP-COp) may not persist after review. No model weights, augmented/noised datasets, or benchmark splits are independently released. The paper evaluates exclusively on multi-class classification; regression tasks (which TFMs also support) are untested. The backbone is restricted to TabPFN-v1 despite TabPFN-v2 (Nature, 2025) and TabICL being available and widely used. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Runs on standard V100 GPUs and requires only forward-pass API access to the TFM; applicable to proprietary TFMs accessed via API. - Efficient: Approximately 1.4 seconds per round with B parallel TFM forward passes; 100 rounds (≈2.5 minutes) achieves near-peak performance on most datasets; linear scaling with rounds and sub-linear with S (samples + features). Faster than soft-prompt methods that require backpropagation. - Effective: Strong evidence of effectiveness in noisy and augmented regimes (qualitative superiority over all baselines in feature-noise setting); gains are more modest in the clean original setting where DT+TFM is competitive, and some datasets show no benefit over baselines. - Scalable: Tested up to n=122,403 samples and d=5,000 features; the Shapley estimation over S=n+d variables with R×B TFM forward passes is feasible at evaluated scales. Not tested at millions of samples; the paper claims scalability but does not experiment beyond the TALENT benchmark, and the quadratic TFM attention over n_C context items remains the bottleneck for larger n_C. Software artifact: code released at anonymous URL; stops at paper form for the theoretical Shapley approximation analysis. Strengths - The robustness under feature noise is the clearest and most compelling result: VIP-COP maintains near-clean performance (−6%) while all baselines collapse (−46% to −54%), attributable directly to the explicit subset selection that can identify and exclude noisy features — a structural advantage over heuristics that optimize over all features. - The anytime property with monotonically improving performance (verified across 12 datasets in Appendix Figure 7) provides a principled latency–accuracy trade-off that is practically valuable for variable-budget deployment settings. - Black-box compatibility without gradient access is a genuine differentiator from the closest white-box alternative (Rundel et al., 2024), enabling application to proprietary TFMs. - The data augmentation results demonstrate a useful emergent capability: VIP-COP can selectively retain high-value augmented samples (+3.43% gain vs. baselines near 0%), suggesting the Shapley-based credit assignment correctly identifies informative augmentations without augmentation-specific design. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note that (1) lacking model-internal signals may limit gains for open-source TFMs compared to white-box soft-prompt methods; (2) test-time compute overhead may be prohibitive under strict latency constraints; and (3) DT+TFM may be preferable in latency-critical settings. These acknowledgments are direct and not minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No ablation study: the paper provides no evidence for which of the three algorithmic innovations (importance-sampled exploration, multi-temperature scheduling, iterative SGD vs. batch regression) contributes to the gains. The algorithm could be simpler without performance loss, or one component might account for most of the benefit. - Single TFM backbone: restricting evaluation to TabPFN-v1 — the oldest and most constrained version — means the method is not validated against the models practitioners actually deploy (TabPFN-v2 with stronger pretraining robustness, TabICL for large-scale settings). It is unclear whether VIP-COP's gains diminish when the underlying TFM is already more robust. - Degenerate tasks not excluded: Amazon_employee_access and Click_prediction score 0.500 across all methods, indicating near-random performance, yet these are included in aggregate statistics without comment, artificially inflating improvement percentages. - Unweighted vs. weighted KernelSHAP approximation: the departure from the original weighted formulation is noted in a footnote but not empirically analyzed; the approximation quality and its effect on VIP score accuracy is unknown. - No regression evaluation despite TFMs being used for regression in practice. - Anonymous code URL may not persist; reproducibility depends on URL stability. Bottom Line For practitioners deploying TFMs on real-world datasets with more samples or features than the model's pretraining window, VIP-COP offers a principled, fast, and demonstrably robust improvement over existing heuristics — especially in noisy settings — and is worth adopting, but the missing ablations mean one cannot yet know which parts of the algorithm are load-bearing. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.12904v1, 13 May 2026); results have not been peer reviewed.
Citations 2605.12904 PDF
#151 — Moderately Innovative (53)

Sensor2Sensor: Cross-Embodiment Sensor Conversion for Autonomous Driving (22 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 22 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Sensor2Sensor, a generative system that converts in-the-wild monocular driving videos (dashcam, phone, internet footage) into synchronized multi-view camera streams and LiDAR point clouds matching a target AV platform's sensor suite. - Why it's important: AV fleets lack diverse, long-tail training data (rare crashes, extreme weather, novel environments) that dashcam/internet videos naturally capture, but those videos are incompatible with ADS sensor pipelines — this system bridges that gap without requiring real paired data. - How it does it: A 4DGS reconstruction pipeline renders synthetic dashcam views from existing AV logs to create paired training data, then a conditional latent diffusion model with cross-sensor attention jointly denoises multi-view images and LiDAR, conditioned on the dashcam frame; DAgger fine-tuning stabilizes auto-regressive video rollouts. - The good: Human evaluators prefer Sensor2Sensor over the nearest adapted baseline (X-Drive) in over 94% of image cases and 85% of LiDAR cases on in-the-wild dashcam and internet data, and perception models trained on real Waymo data run without fine-tuning on the generated sensor data. - The missing: The entire evaluation pipeline — training set (100K scenes), quantitative test set (1K sequences), and baseline adaptation — relies on proprietary Waymo data with no public release, making reproducibility impossible and the numeric comparisons unverifiable by the community. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers working on AV data augmentation, generative sensor simulation, or cross-modal diffusion; practitioners at companies without large proprietary AV fleets should note the method's dependence on high-quality 4DGS reconstruction data as a prerequisite.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile (the released contribution is methodological, and a Systems profile would penalize the unreleased artifact even harder via its 30% impact weight). Non-Obviousness (75) and Novelty (67) are real — repurposing 4DGS as a paired-data synthesis engine to convert an intractable unpaired translation problem into a supervised one is genuinely clever. The composite is dragged down by Rigor (45), Resilience (43), and Impact (35): the entire evaluation rests on proprietary Waymo data with no code/weights/benchmark released, so the numbers are unverifiable, all baselines are adapted-from-other-tasks rather than purpose-built, no error bars or inter-rater agreement are reported, and the central downstream-utility claim (improving ADS training) is shown only qualitatively. It is a polished vendor-internal result, not a reusable artifact.
empirical Novelty 67 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 50 Resilience 43 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Autonomous driving systems require large, diverse datasets to validate safety-critical behavior, especially for long-tail scenarios such as accidents, erratic road users, and extreme weather. Proprietary AV fleets (e.g., Waymo) collect high-fidelity multi-modal data (multi-camera + LiDAR) but are geographically and temporally limited, making organic long-tail collection prohibitively expensive. In-the-wild consumer footage (dashcams, phones, internet videos) captures long-tail diversity at scale but is sensorially incompatible with ADS pipelines: typically single-camera, uncalibrated, missing LiDAR, and lacking 360° coverage. Classical unpaired domain translation methods (e.g., CycleGAN) fail to bridge this gap because they cannot synthesize coherent multi-modal, multi-view outputs from a monocular input. The result is an "embodiment gap": data exists but cannot be consumed by the target platform. Key Idea: The central insight is to use existing AV logs as a geometric oracle: reconstruct scenes with 4D Gaussian Splatting, then render synthetic dashcam views from external novel viewpoints.
