Critical design decisions: (1) ARX for Chronos-2 only — not justified relative to other TSFMs, limiting comparative fairness; (2) best-of-context-length reporting — introduces optimization advantage for TSFMs not present for baselines; (3) simple mean ensemble — standard and reasonable. Key Results: On DAM, Chronos-2 ARX achieves MAE 12.43 EUR/MWh and RMSE 19.36 EUR/MWh versus ML ensemble MAE 13.22 and RMSE 19.72 — approximately 6% MAE improvement, average DAM price 73.38 EUR/MWh in 2024. Adding Chronos-2 ARX to the ML ensemble gives MAE 12.30 / RMSE 18.91, but the Diebold-Mariano test finds this improvement non-significant versus the ML ensemble alone. On IMB, TSFMs are generally inferior to trained models: at QH1 (15-min ahead), Chronos-2 ARX MAE is 70.1 vs. ML ensemble 67.19; at QH8 (2-hour ahead), Chronos-2 ARX MAE 93.72 marginally beats ML ensemble 93.96 — a 0.3% difference. Under extreme conditions (1st/10th price deciles), TSFMs consistently underperform trained models on IMB, with the DNN achieving MAE 78.7 at the 10th decile QH1 versus Chronos-2 ARX's 101.7. Baselines are current and appropriate; the LEAR and DNN implementations follow established benchmarks from Lago et al. (2021). Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The contribution is an application benchmark, not a new method or architecture. The genuine novelty is (a) first systematic TSFM evaluation on imbalance price forecasting and (b) documenting that ARX-mode zero-shot TSFM can match trained baselines on DAM but fails on the more volatile IMB market. No new model, dataset, or theoretical result is introduced. Rigor & Reproducibility: No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported beyond a single Diebold-Mariano test on one comparison pair. MAE/RMSE over a single test year is the sole evidence base. The context-length tuning for TSFMs (best of multiple configurations reported) is an undisclosed selection procedure that favors TSFMs without equivalent tuning for ML baselines. No code or data is released. The paper contains a visible copy-paste artifact: the MAE/RMSE equations appear twice in Section III.E with duplicated surrounding text, suggesting rushed preparation. The data-leakage check is qualitative (cross-referencing disclosed training sets) and cannot account for undisclosed pre-training corpora. Single-country, single-year evaluation limits generalizability claims. Deployability: EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: TSFMs with 120–200M parameters run on commodity GPU hardware; inference is straightforward using the `chronos-forecasting` and `timesfm` libraries. - Efficient: TSFMs require no retraining or recalibration, a stated operational advantage; Chronos-Bolt is the fast-inference variant. Relative inference latency versus LEAR/DNN is not quantified. - Effective: Chronos-2 ARX solves DAM forecasting at competitive accuracy zero-shot, but does not solve IMB forecasting — it fails on the harder, more volatile target that arguably matters more for real-time operations. - Scalable: The paper claims multi-zone scalability as an advantage but presents no evidence at scale; no additional bidding zones or markets are tested beyond Belgium. No software artifact beyond off-the-shelf library usage is released. Strengths: - Provides the first documented TSFM evaluation on imbalance price forecasting, filling a real literature gap. - The decile-stratified error analysis (Tables IV–V) surfaces the tail-performance failure of TSFMs in a way that aggregate MAE hides — methodologically useful for practitioners who care about extreme prices. - Diebold-Mariano testing on the combined ensemble is a credibility-positive practice that correctly shows the Chronos-2 + ML gain is not statistically significant. - Careful attention to data-leakage risk by cross-checking test periods against known TSFM training data. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that TSFMs struggle under extreme market conditions and that further investigation of model architecture and training data is needed to understand the ARX covariate mechanism and rule out data leakage. They flag that the improvement from combining TSFMs with ML models, while consistent, is not statistically significant. These are directly confronted, not minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) ARX mode is tested only for Chronos-2 — the comparison is structurally unfair; TimesFM and Chronos-Bolt run univariate while Chronos-2 runs multivariate. The dominant performance driver is mode, not model. (2) Context-length selection for TSFMs is optimized post-hoc and reported at best configuration, an advantage not afforded to baselines. (3) Single test year per market — no multi-year stability analysis or confidence intervals make it impossible to assess whether results reflect a stable ordering or a lucky year. (4) Belgium only — the claimed multi-zone scalability advantage is asserted but untested. (5) No intraday or probabilistic forecasting is evaluated; point forecasts alone underrepresent operational needs. (6) The paper is five pages in a short workshop format, which limits depth on all of the above. Bottom Line: A useful, honest existence proof that Chronos-2 ARX can match trained ML ensembles on DAM zero-shot and that TSFMs are not yet competitive on IMB — but the structural inequality in experimental setup (ARX only for Chronos-2, context-length tuning unreported) means the 6% DAM improvement should be treated as a lower bound on what ARX mode adds, not a fair head-to-head model comparison; practitioners should replicate before relying on these numbers.
Search Results 2605.17045 PDF
#181 — Incremental (37)

Rethinking LLMOps for Fraud and AML: Building a Compliance-Grade LLM Serving Stack

Prathamesh Vasudeo Naik, Naresh Dintakurthi, Yue Wang · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a workload-aware LLMOps serving stack for fraud and anti-money-laundering (AML) compliance workloads, combining vLLM runtime tuning, Automatic Prefix Caching, multi-adapter batching, speculative decoding, and sleep/wake lifecycle management, benchmarked on public synthetic AML datasets. - Why it's important: Generic LLM serving configurations applied to compliance workloads leave throughput severely underutilized (12% GPU utilization in the reported baseline) and produce P99 latencies of 30+ seconds, which violate the SLA and operational requirements of regulated financial deployments. - How it does it: The core move is recognizing fraud/AML prompts as prefix-dominated with short structured outputs, then systematically tuning the full vLLM serving stack — max_num_seqs, max_num_batched_tokens, stop tokens, APC — to match that specific workload shape rather than treating compliance inference as generic chat. - The good: Workload-aware tuning raised throughput from 612–650 to 3,600 req/hr (5.5–5.9×), cut P99 latency from 31–38 s to 6.4–8.7 s, lifted GPU utilization from 12% to 78%, and reduced projected capacity from ~10 GPUs to ~3–4 on a public synthetic benchmark. - The missing: All benchmarks use public synthetic AML data and controlled microbenchmarks rather than real institutional workloads, and model quality comparisons rest on 4-sample pilots with no statistical validity. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML infrastructure engineers building self-hosted LLM serving pipelines for regulated financial domains; not useful for researchers seeking novel model or algorithmic contributions.
Why this rank: Scored using the Systems profile. Rigor (25) is the dominant problem — no ablation isolates which intervention drives the 5.9× gain, the primary Case A baseline is an obviously misconfigured factory default, the speculative-decoding comparison confounds GPU count, engine, and decoding strategy, model-quality 'percentages' rest on 4-sample pilots, schema-valid goodput is named as the primary metric but never tabulated, and no code or artifact is released. Impact (40) is limited because the work stops at a benchmark report on synthetic data with no released stack, and Novelty (40) reflects a domain-specific reassembly of existing vLLM/SGLang features rather than a new mechanism; honest limitation acknowledgment lifts Completeness (45) somewhat. Weighted: 0.15*40 + 0.15*25 + 0.10*35 + 0.15*45 + 0.15*30 + 0.30*40 = 37.
systems Novelty 40 Rigor 25 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 45 Resilience 30 Impact 40
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Financial institutions deploying LLMs for fraud detection and AML compliance face a workload shape fundamentally different from general chat: prompts contain large reusable policy blocks, risk taxonomies, JSON schemas, and case-specific transaction evidence, while outputs are short structured JSON or label lists. Multiple task variants (predicate-offense classification, risk-factor extraction, narrative generation) share a base model but require different LoRA adapters. Data governance in regulated environments mandates self-hosted open-weight models rather than cloud APIs, since external services may trigger policy refusals, introduce latency variance, or expose sensitive investigation content. Prior LLM serving systems optimize for chat-style diverse-history long-output workloads; applying their default configurations to compliance inference wastes most available GPU capacity and produces latencies incompatible with operational SLAs. Key Idea The key insight is workload fingerprinting before serving configuration: by characterizing fraud/AML prompts as exhibiting high prefix-sharing ratios (same policy prefix across thousands of requests), short decode lengths (50–75 tokens for JSON labels), and multi-adapter tenancy — properties that do not co-occur in chat workloads — the paper shows that standard vLLM knobs tuned to this shape deliver order-of-magnitude improvements without any new algorithms. The secondary insight is that adapter locality and prompt-length locality must both be exploited jointly in multi-adapter batching: grouping by adapter-id first, then bucketing by input length, reduces prefill imbalance that would otherwise arise from mixing 400-token classification prompts with 3,000-token extraction prompts. Methodology The system follows a four-plane architecture: (1) workload construction — IBM AML and SAML-D transaction records are rendered into compliance prompts containing a reusable policy prefix, typology definitions, transaction evidence, and a required JSON output schema; (2) control plane — a serving router selects model family, adapter, runtime config, and caching policy per workload type; (3) serving plane — vLLM or SGLang with PagedAttention, Automatic Prefix Caching, EAGLE-style speculative decoding, and optional prefill/decode disaggregation; (4) output assurance — deterministic schema validation, reference-metric scoring (BLEU/ROUGE/cosine similarity), and multi-judge LLM rubric scoring gate releases. Benchmark protocol: 50–100 request warm-up or 5-minute warm-up; 3 runs; median and range reported over a 10–15 minute steady-state measurement window. Primary sweep axes are max_num_seqs, max_num_batched_tokens, gpu_memory_utilization, swap_space, stop tokens, and adapter grouping policy. Both closed-loop concurrency sweeps and open-loop arrival tests are run. Three critical design decisions: (1) Schema-valid goodput as the primary metric rather than raw tokens/s — justified because malformed JSON is operationally equivalent to failure in compliance settings. (2) Two-level batching (adapter-first, then length-aware bucketing) — justified to eliminate prefill imbalance in heterogeneous multi-task requests. (3) Public synthetic data only for reproducibility — justified by data governance constraints. Whether the chosen operating point of max_num_seqs=24 (vs. 16 or 32) generalizes beyond the specific workload tested is not validated. Key Results Case A (A100-class long-context extraction, public synthetic IBM AML): throughput 612–650 → 3,600 req/hr; P99 latency 31–38 s → 6.4–8.7 s; GPU utilization 12% → 78%; normalized cost (A100-hours per 1k requests) 3.08–3.27 → 0.56; projected capacity 10 GPUs → 3–4 GPUs. Case B (guided-JSON H100 speculative decoding): 1×H100 vLLM + EAGLE-style achieves 3.19 req/s at 2,292 ms vs. 2×H100 NIM baseline at 3.01 req/s and 2,397 ms; normalized cost reduced ~53%. Case C1 (multi-adapter batching, 12-prompt mixed request across 4 adapters): PEFT loading baseline 1.35 req/s → vLLM multi-adapter + length buckets 5.26 req/s (3.90× gain). Case C2 (sleep/wake lifecycle): cold start 36–126 s across Llama-8B/Qwen-14B/Qwen-32B reduced to 0.98–4.15 s (30–37× reduction). Model quality observations: Llama-3.1-8B ~85% recall and precision on predicate-offense extraction (4-sample pilot); Qwen2.5 7B–32B ~50% recall / 85% precision at budget tier. These numbers carry no statistical weight. Baselines for serving benchmarks are appropriate in kind (NIM for speculative decoding, PEFT for multi-adapter) but the primary Case A baseline appears to be an untuned factory-default configuration, not a carefully-optimized competitor. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The individual components — PagedAttention, Automatic Prefix Caching, EAGLE speculative decoding, LoRA multi-adapter serving, vLLM sleep/wake — are all existing features of vLLM and SGLang. The genuine contribution is the domain-specific serving playbook: the systematic mapping of AML workload phenotypes to serving interventions, the two-level adapter+length batching strategy, the compliance-specific quality gate architecture, and the public reproducibility track using IBM AML and SAML-D to construct prefix-heavy serving benchmarks. The combination and framing for regulated financial deployment is new; the underlying mechanisms are not. Rigor & Reproducibility Significant concerns. No ablation study: the paper reports an optimized configuration versus a poorly-tuned baseline, but does not isolate which individual intervention (max_num_seqs tuning, APC, stop tokens, CUDA graphs) contributes what fraction of the 5.9× gain. The model quality pilots are based on 4 samples — reporting "85% recall and precision" on 4 examples is statistically meaningless. Tables show point estimates without error bars; the methodology states median and range are reported, but tables in results sections omit ranges. The speculative decoding comparison mixes hardware configurations (1×H100 vs. 2×H100) and software stacks (vLLM+EAGLE vs. NIM), making it impossible to isolate the speculative decoding contribution from the hardware and software differences. No code, dataset rendering scripts, or model weights are released; no repository is mentioned. The paper explicitly states results should be read as workload-shape evidence on synthetic data, which is an honest but significant caveat. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built serving architecture evaluated by benchmark. - Practical: Runs on standard commercially available A100/H100 hardware using publicly available open-weight models (Llama 3, Qwen2.5) and vLLM/SGLang serving engines. - Efficient: 83% reduction in normalized GPU-hours per 1k requests after tuning; 1×H100 with speculative decoding matches 2×H100 NIM throughput at ~53% lower cost. - Effective: Serving performance evidence is strong on synthetic benchmarks; end-to-end compliance quality is validated only on 4-sample pilots with no significance testing, so the claim that the stack produces production-quality outputs is unsupported. - Scalable: The paper claims a path from 250k to multi-million daily prompts via APC and LMCache but does not run scale experiments — this is an assertion, not a measurement. No software artifact is released; the paper stops at the system description and benchmark report. Strengths - The domain framing — identifying fraud/AML as a distinct "prefix-dominated, short-output, multi-tenant" workload class — is concise and actionable; it gives infrastructure engineers a clear optimization priority ordering that generic LLM serving guides do not provide. - The two-level batching pattern (adapter-id grouping followed by prompt-length bucketing) is a concrete, immediately implementable engineering pattern demonstrated to yield 3.90× over naive PEFT loading on a controlled benchmark. - The sleep/wake lifecycle benchmark directly quantifies the cold-start vs. wake-path tradeoff (30–37× reduction) for sequential multi-model compliance pipelines, which is practically useful data not documented in general vLLM guides. - The LLM-as-judge quality gate design — layering deterministic schema checks, reference metrics, and multi-judge rubric scoring into a promotion gate — appropriately frames compliance serving as a quality-and-governance problem, not just a throughput problem. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge all four primary limitations directly: (1) synthetic data cannot capture real institutional nuances; (2) model quality observations are small operational pilots, not state-of-the-art task quality claims; (3) LLM-as-judge evaluation suffers from position bias, verbosity bias, family preference, and policy drift, requiring periodic human recalibration; (4) LMCache cross-worker reuse and mature prefill/decode disaggregation are described as high-confidence next steps rather than validated production defaults. This directness is a credibility positive in this framework. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The primary Case A baseline (12% GPU utilization, 31–38 s P99 latency) is an obviously misconfigured factory default, not a representative optimized competitor; the 5.9× throughput gain is partly an artifact of this choice and does not isolate the contribution of any specific technique. - No ablation table: readers cannot determine whether APC alone, max_num_seqs tuning alone, or the combination drives the improvement. - The speculative decoding comparison conflates hardware count (1 vs. 2 GPUs), inference engine (vLLM vs. NIM), and decoding strategy — three confounds in one table. - Model quality numbers on 4-sample pilots should not appear as quantitative claims; they introduce false precision. - The paper describes schema-valid goodput as the primary metric but no results table actually reports schema validity rates — only latency and throughput are tabulated. - The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.11232v1, May 2026) and has not undergone peer review; the results warrant peer-review scrutiny before relying on specific numbers for infrastructure decisions. Bottom Line A practitioner building a self-hosted LLM serving stack for regulated financial workflows will find the operational playbook, workload taxonomy, and adapter-batching pattern immediately useful, but the absence of ablations, statistically vacuous quality pilots, no code release, and synthetic-only evaluation mean the paper is an engineering experience report rather than a rigorously validated systems contribution.
Search Results 2605.11232 PDF
#182 — Incremental (37)

Personalized Course Promotion in MOOCs: A Hybrid Recommender with LLM-Structured Persuasive Explanation for Educational Advertising

ejournal.abcollab.id · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LGPRec, a hybrid MOOC course recommender coupled with a deterministic template-based explanation planner that jointly optimizes course ranking and generates personalized promotional text for educational advertising. - Why it's important: MOOC platforms must both surface the right course and frame it persuasively, yet these two tasks are studied separately — leaving a gap between ranking-only recommenders and LLM text-generation work that lacks rigorous recommendation grounding and reproducibility. - How it does it: LGPRec fuses ItemCF, SVD, TF-IDF content, and completion-sensitive signals via a fixed linear combination, while a slot-based generator fills a deterministic string template with learner history anchors and course tags drawn from the same shared user profile. - The good: The explanation planner raises explicit history mention from 0.0012 to 1.0000 and unique ratio from 0.0027 to 0.1912 while maintaining item faithfulness at 1.0000 — demonstrating that personalization and faithfulness are jointly achievable in a fully reproducible, non-stochastic system. - The missing: No neural baselines (NCF, SASRec, BERT4Rec) appear in the main comparison tables, and the IBM catalog contains only 124 courses — making ranking performance claims on a near-trivial item space of limited practical relevance. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building reproducible MOOC recommendation-plus-explanation pipelines who care about faithfulness auditing; not useful for practitioners seeking competitive ranking performance, since gains over ItemCF are marginal (~0.93% Recall@10 on a 124-item catalog) and the "LLM-structured" framing overstates actual generative capability.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Rigor (40) and Resilience (32) are the dimensions that pull this down: the IBM benchmark has only 124 courses (Recall@10≈0.73 is a near-ceiling, low-discrimination task), gains over ItemCF are marginal (+0.0018 NDCG@10), no neural baselines appear, no significance tests, and the completion branch contributes exactly 0.0000 to NDCG yet is retained. The 'LLM-structured' framing is materially misleading — no model runs at inference, it is a deterministic string template — and the 0.1912 unique ratio (≈81% duplicate explanations) is reported without analysis, undercutting the 'personalized' claim. Completeness (45) is the relative bright spot given six directly-acknowledged limitations, but the toy-scale benchmark and overstated framing keep Novelty (40) and Impact (28, no artifact) low. Composite ≈ 37.
empirical Novelty 40 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 30 Completeness 45 Resilience 32 Impact 28
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation MOOC platforms must decide which course to surface to a learner and how to frame it persuasively enough to drive enrollment. These decisions are currently handled by separate research communities: recommender systems optimize ranking accuracy, and LLM-based text generation produces persuasive content but typically relies on stochastic API calls that are difficult to audit for faithfulness or reproduce exactly. Educational practitioners lack a reproducible, jointly evaluated pipeline that measures both ranking quality and the faithfulness and personalization of learner-facing promotional text — and MOOC-specific datasets have matured enough to support such joint evaluation. Key Idea The core insight is that a single shared learner profile — constructed from enrollment history, topic tags, and completion signals — can simultaneously drive a hybrid ranking score and fill deterministic template slots to produce individualized, content-faithful promotional explanations. The "LLM-structured" contribution is architectural rather than generative: the slot schema was designed following LLM prompting conventions (learner evidence, promoted item, persuasive angle, action phrasing), but no LLM is invoked at inference time. Reproducibility is achieved by replacing stochastic decoding with a frozen string template that always produces the same output for the same learner-course pair. Methodology The pipeline has two stages. In the ranking stage, LGPRec computes a deterministic score fusion: s = s_ItemCF + 0.5·s_SVD + 0.02·s_Content + 0.01·s_Completion. ItemCF uses cosine similarity over a binarized item-user matrix weighted by normalized ratings at scoring time. SVD applies TruncatedSVD (n_components=32) to a rating-weighted user-item matrix (weight = r/3). The content branch represents each course via TF-IDF (ngram=(1,2), max_features=5000) over title, description, and 14 binary genre indicators. The completion branch reuses the same TF-IDF representation but constructs the user profile exclusively from certification-level interactions (rating=3). Weights were hand-tuned on the IBM validation split. In the explanation stage, a deterministic slot generator fills a two-sentence template with: the learner's anchor course, dominant topic tags, the recommended course title, top genres, and a description snippet — producing generic (course+skills) and personalized (history anchor + learner tags) variants. For the sequential XuetangX benchmark, HoTrans = 0.78·s_Markov + 0.22·s_RecencyCooc, where Markov uses row-normalized first-order transition probabilities and RecencyCooc applies exponential decay (decay=0.7) over prefix co-occurrences. Three critical design decisions: (1) fixed linear weights rather than learned fusion — justified by reproducibility and CPU parsimony but not empirically compared against learned alternatives; (2) exclusion of neural baselines — explicitly justified by the reproducibility/CPU-only goal, but limits claims; (3) template-based rather than live-LLM generation — well-justified given the reproducibility constraint, though it limits explanation diversity. Key Results IBM benchmark (124 active courses, 25,062 users): LGPRec achieves Recall@10=0.7264, NDCG@10=0.4750, MRR@10=0.3962, versus the strongest baseline ItemCF at 0.7197/0.4732/0.3961 — absolute deltas of +0.0067/+0.0018/+0.0001. On long-tail items (1,231 test cases), LGPRec NDCG@10 improves from 0.1413 to 0.1489 (+5.35% relative), the most meaningful gain. Content-only and SVD-only baselines are weak (Recall@10≈0.30 and 0.24 respectively), confirming that the IBM benchmark is dominated by co-enrollment signals. Explanation evaluation: personalized variant raises explicit history mention from 0.0012 → 1.0000, dominant-tag coverage 0.7789 → 1.0000, unique ratio 0.0027 → 0.1912, item faithfulness maintained at 1.0000, distinct explanations per course 1.00 → 68.49. XuetangX (1,302 courses, 82,535 sequences): HoTrans achieves 0.4848/0.3333/0.2869 vs. Markov 0.4634/0.3191/0.2745 — relative improvements of +4.64%, +4.46%, +4.53%. The paper explicitly acknowledges that a recent SASRec-style method on MOOCCubeX achieves HR@10=0.818, NDCG@10=0.524, substantially higher than both LGPRec and HoTrans. No significance tests are reported; all comparisons are deterministic single-run results. Novelty Classification NEW FORMULATION + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The joint recommendation-and-explanation framing for MOOC course promotion with reproducible, auditability-focused explanation metrics is the genuine contribution. The individual components (ItemCF, SVD, TF-IDF, first-order Markov, exponential-decay co-occurrence) are textbook methods with no algorithmic novelty; the "LLM-structured" slot design is string-template filling, not language modeling. The paper's value is the benchmark framing and the operationalization of explanation faithfulness metrics at scale, not any algorithmic advance. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation study is present (Table 8) and decomposable: each branch's marginal contribution is measured, and the completion branch is shown to contribute 0.0000 improvement in NDCG@10 yet is retained in the proposed model without comment. Subgroup analyses by history length and item popularity (Tables 6–7) are thorough and informative. No statistical significance tests are reported; the authors justify this by noting deterministic evaluation on fixed public splits. No code repository URL is provided, though the algorithm is fully specified and reproducible from the description. Data is publicly available (IBM capstone, XuetangX via SPMF). Explanation metrics are study-specific operationalizations, not drawn from a standard benchmark. The unique ratio of 0.1912 means ~81% of generated explanations are duplicates — the deterministic template produces substantial repetition that is not flagged as a concern. The paper disclosed use of ChatGPT for language editing. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs on commodity CPU hardware using standard Python scientific libraries (pandas, NumPy, scikit-learn); no GPU, cloud API, or specialized hardware required. - Efficient: Score matrix computation over 25,062 users × 124 items is computationally trivial; the template generator is O(1) per user; the full pipeline is reported as executable locally. - Effective: LGPRec marginally outperforms ItemCF on IBM (Δ NDCG@10 = +0.0018 globally, +0.0076 on long-tail); HoTrans outperforms Markov more meaningfully on XuetangX; both are substantially below neural methods on larger benchmarks per the authors' own citation. - Scalable: Untested beyond IBM (124 items, 25K users) and XuetangX (1,302 items, 82K sequences); real MOOC platforms have tens of thousands of courses; the full cosine similarity computation over large catalogs would require approximate search not present in this system. No software artifact (library, CLI, dataset, or trained model weights) is released. Strengths - Full reproducibility commitment is genuine and unusual: deterministic preprocessing, fixed random seed, public-data-only, version-pinned dependencies, and all tables generated from the same executable pipeline — a higher standard than most LLM-explanation papers in this area. - Explanation faithfulness metrics are measured over all 25,062 test users rather than illustrated with cherry-picked examples, giving an honest aggregate picture of what the template actually produces. - Long-tail subgroup analysis (Table 7) provides a genuine interpretive insight: content and completion signals contribute most precisely where collaborative filtering is weakest, which coherently justifies the hybrid design even when global gains are small. - The paper is honest about its own limitations and explicitly resists overclaiming SOTA accuracy, framing itself as a reproducible benchmark rather than a performance record. Author-Acknowledged Limitations Authors directly acknowledge: (1) the explanation planner is template-based, not live LLM generation, and this is a faithfulness-reproducibility tradeoff; (2) IBM catalog is too small to represent large MOOC platforms; (3) no online click-through or enrollment uplift is measured — only offline proxies; (4) explanation generation was not evaluated on XuetangX because that dataset lacks course text; (5) the template can inherit surface quality issues from noisy course metadata; (6) neural baselines are absent and extending to at least one is flagged as an important next step. These disclosures are direct and credible. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The title phrase "LLM-Structured" is materially misleading: no language model is invoked at runtime; the slot schema was inspired by prompting conventions, but the delivered artifact is a Python string template. A reader triaging the paper for LLM-based generation will be misled. - The unique ratio of 0.1912 means roughly 4,800 distinct explanations are produced across 25,062 users — i.e., most users receive the same explanation text. The paper reports this metric without analyzing its distribution or discussing whether this level of repetition is acceptable for a "personalized" system. - The completion branch contributes identically to NDCG@10 and MRR@10 as the content-only variant (Table 8, rows 3 vs. 4 are 0.4750/0.3962 for both), meaning the completion signal adds zero measured ranking value — yet it is included in the final model. - Manual weight tuning (0.5, 0.02, 0.01) on the validation split is undocumented in terms of search range or sensitivity, making it impossible to judge whether the weights are near-optimal or whether small perturbations would change conclusions. - Human evaluation of explanation quality is entirely absent; all explanation metrics measure surface-level string properties (does the explanation mention X?), not whether learners find the messages useful, persuasive, or trustworthy. - The IBM catalog of 124 items makes Recall@10 ≈ 0.73 equivalent to retrieving roughly 9 of 124 courses — an extremely easy retrieval task where most reasonable methods would cluster near ceiling, limiting the discriminative power of the benchmark. Bottom Line This paper delivers a disciplined, fully reproducible baseline for joint MOOC course recommendation and explanation auditing, but its algorithmic substance is thin — hand-weighted classical methods, a string template falsely branded as LLM-structured generation, and a toy-scale 124-item benchmark — making it useful mainly as a methodological reference for reproducibility-focused educational recommendation research rather than as a performance or technical contribution.
Search Results
#183 — Incremental (34)

NeuSymMS: A Hybrid Neuro- Symbolic Memory System for Persistent, Self-Curating LLM Agents

Mujahid Sultan, Sri Thuraisamy, Daya Rajaratnam · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces NeuSymMS, a production-deployed memory subsystem for LLM agents that combines LLM-based fact extraction with a CLIPS expert-system rules engine to classify, deduplicate, and lifecycle-manage user knowledge stored as subject–relation–value triples in PostgreSQL. - Why it's important: LLM agents currently handle persistent memory via conversation-log retrieval, summarization, or key-value stores, all of which fail to handle contradictions, temporal change, or cross-entity contamination deterministically and cheaply. - How it does it: LLMs act purely as parsers that extract atomic triples from dialogue; all reasoning — classification, contradiction detection, promotion, and pruning — is delegated to CLIPS forward-chaining rules, avoiding LLM calls for structured decisions. - The good: The architecture cleanly separates neural perception (LLM extraction) from symbolic control (CLIPS lifecycle), which yields deterministic, auditable, zero-marginal-cost memory management with explicit rule traces. - The missing: The paper contains zero quantitative evaluation; no benchmark numbers, latency measurements, or retrieval quality metrics are reported — evaluation is explicitly deferred to future work, making all performance claims unsubstantiated. - Read it or not: Worth a skim for engineers building production agent memory infrastructure who want a concrete architecture combining CLIPS-style rules with LLM extraction; skip if you need empirical evidence of benefit over Mem0 or MemGPT, since none exists in the paper.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Rigor (18) collapses the composite: the paper contains zero quantitative evaluation — no benchmarks, latency, or retrieval metrics — and the reference list contains self-identified placeholder and apparently fabricated entries ('placeholder entry', 'J. Doe'/'A. Smith'/'J. Smith'), which is a serious diligence and integrity concern. Resilience (25) is correspondingly low because every performance claim ('microsecond classification', 'graceful degradation') is an architectural assertion, not a measurement, and would not survive any reviewer demand for evidence. Impact (40) gives partial credit for a deployed proprietary component and a sensible LLM-as-parser / CLIPS-as-reasoner separation, but with effectiveness entirely undemonstrated this is a design sketch, not validated work. Weighted: 0.15*45 + 0.15*18 + 0.10*40 + 0.15*35 + 0.15*25 + 0.30*40 = 34.
systems Novelty 45 Rigor 18 Non-Obv 40 Completeness 35 Resilience 25 Impact 40
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-session LLM agents need persistent user models — facts about preferences, job, location, constraints — that evolve over time. The three dominant approaches each fail on a key dimension: retrieval-augmented logs treat transcripts as immutable and cannot handle belief revision (e.g., a user changing jobs); LLM summarization loses atomicity and cannot retract individual facts; key-value or embedding stores lack deterministic contradiction handling and are opaque about why a fact was stored or discarded. Production multi-tenant platforms additionally face cross-entity contamination (agent-scoped facts leaking into user context) and auditability requirements that none of these approaches address well. Key Idea: Confine LLMs to the role of a natural-language parser (extracting atomic triples from dialogue), then hand all reasoning — contradiction detection via Levenshtein-distance matching, category classification via pattern rules, access-based promotion, and time-based pruning — to a CLIPS forward-chaining rules engine running in-process. This yields a deterministic, zero-API-cost control plane over a structured fact store while keeping the neural component minimal and cheap. Methodology: The system sits between a user conversation and an LLM gateway, implementing a read path and a write path per agent turn. On the write path: (1) a lightweight LLM (gpt-4.1-mini, temperature 0.1, max 10 facts/turn) receives a specialized extraction prompt and outputs a JSON array of candidate triples with subject, relation, value, confidence, and scope; (2) candidate facts and existing active facts are loaded into a per-request CLIPS working memory (three core templates: memory-fact, candidate-fact, engine-decision) to prevent cross-user contamination; (3) CLIPS rules classify each candidate into one of nine semantic categories (personal, preference, task, relationship, skill, context, instruction, temporal, other) via pattern matching over relation and value fields; (4) contradiction detection uses entity normalization (lowercasing, Unicode normalization, punctuation trim) followed by Levenshtein similarity (threshold 0.85) to detect value conflicts on the same subject–relation pair — single-valued relations apply latest-value-wins; multi-valued relations (e.g., speaks_language) treat each value independently; negation relations (no_longer_has, lost, stopped) trigger retraction of the matched positive fact without persisting the negation; (5) storage policy: facts below confidence 0.3 are discarded; personal, preference, instruction, and skill categories auto-promote to long_term; other categories initialize as short_term; short_term facts with access_count ≥ 3 promote to long_term; a background job deactivates short_term facts older than 24 hours with access_count = 0. On the read path: active user-scoped facts are ordered by memory_type (long-term first), then access_count, then recency; at most 30 facts are injected as a formatted text block into the LLM system prompt. No embedding or vector search is used. The implementation uses Django REST Framework, PostgreSQL, CLIPSPy (CLIPS 6.4), and LiteLLM. Key Results: No quantitative results are reported. The paper presents illustrative examples (e.g., a user utterance "I just moved to Toronto and started a new job at iVedha" producing two triples with confidence 0.95 and 0.9) and a walk-through of the write path for a job-change scenario. Section VII.A describes a future evaluation roadmap against LoCoMo, LongMemEval, AMA-Bench, and MEMORYARENA benchmarks with baselines including Mem0, MemGPT/AMEM, LiCoMemory, TELEMEM, GraphRAG, and AMAC — but none of this has been run. All performance claims ("microsecond-level classification", "zero API cost", "graceful degradation") are architectural assertions, not measurements. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. The application of CLIPS-based forward-chaining rules to LLM agent memory management is a reasonable and underexplored engineering choice, but the individual components — triple stores, truth maintenance systems, dual-horizon memory inspired by cognitive psychology, access-count promotion — are all established ideas. The genuine contribution is the specific integration design for multi-tenant production use, particularly the three-tier scoping model and the principled separation of neural extraction from symbolic lifecycle control. Nothing here is a new algorithm or formal result. Rigor & Reproducibility: There are no ablations, no error bars, no significance tests, and no benchmark comparisons — the paper is entirely design description without empirical validation. No code or model weights are released; the system lives inside the proprietary Nexa platform (asknexa.ai). Several reference quality concerns undermine credibility: reference [9] is explicitly flagged "placeholder entry" in the bibliography; reference [6] lists co-authors "J. Doe" and "A. Smith," which are placeholder names; "J. Smith" appears as a co-author on three unrelated references [7], [8], [9], suggesting fabricated or incomplete citation data. The confidence threshold (0.85 for Levenshtein similarity), promotion threshold (access_count ≥ 3), and pruning TTL (24 hours) are stated as production values with no ablation or sensitivity analysis. The negation extraction design (relying on the LLM to emit a died relation from "My cat died") appears fragile and is not stress-tested. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — NeuSymMS is described as a deployed component of the Nexa commercial platform. - Practical: Runs entirely on commodity server hardware; CLIPS executes in-process via CLIPSPy with no GPU dependency; LLM extraction calls any LiteLLM-supported provider. - Efficient: CLIPS classification and contradiction detection are described as microsecond-latency; the primary cost is one LLM extraction call per agent turn using a lightweight model (gpt-4.1-mini class); context injection is string concatenation with no vector search overhead. - Effective: Not demonstrated — no task-performance or retrieval-quality numbers exist; whether the system actually improves agent responses or reduces errors relative to simpler approaches is unknown. - Scalable: Multi-tenant isolation is claimed via per-request CLIPS working memory instantiation; PostgreSQL indexes are described; but no throughput, latency, or load test results are reported, and behavior under concurrent multi-user load is unverified. The system exists as a deployed software artifact within a proprietary platform; it does not ship as an open library or tool. Strengths: - The architectural separation of LLM-as-parser from CLIPS-as-reasoner is a principled design that reduces hallucination risk, eliminates per-classification API cost, and produces auditable rule traces — a meaningful advance over systems that use LLMs for all memory decisions. - Three-tier scoping (user / agent / flow) with explicit contamination prevention is a practically important design for multi-tenant agentic platforms that existing systems (Mem0, MemGPT) do not address cleanly. - Access-count-based promotion as an organic importance signal avoids the overhead and opacity of a separate importance-scoring model, and aligns with well-established cognitive rehearsal theory. - Graceful degradation (memory failures silently no-op rather than blocking agent requests) is a correct production engineering choice that generic research memory systems often ignore. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) no quantitative evaluation exists and all benchmarking is future work; (2) temporal validity is represented only as timestamps, not explicit validity intervals — richer interval reasoning is deferred; (3) negation handling and uncertainty propagation need improvement; (4) classification and lifecycle rules are hand-written and need automated rule-learning or suggestion mechanisms for new domains. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive, though deferring all quantitative validation is an unusually large gap for a systems paper. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The reference list contains what appear to be placeholder or fabricated entries (ref [9] self-identified as "placeholder entry"; refs [6]–[8] with implausible generic author names), raising concerns about the diligence of the related-work survey. The Levenshtein similarity threshold (0.85) is critical to contradiction correctness but is stated without any empirical justification — small changes here could cause either false retractions (over-aggressive) or fact accumulation (under-aggressive). The extraction prompt design (max 10 facts/turn, temperature 0.1) is stated without evidence that these choices yield good recall or precision on realistic dialogues. The negation extraction design relies on the LLM emitting specific relation tokens (died, lost, stopped) from free-form text, which is brittle and likely fails on implicit negations or paraphrases not in the training distribution. The cap of 30 injected facts is arbitrary and untested — for users with rich long-term profiles, this may silently drop relevant facts. The paper is produced by a commercial entity (iVedha/MLSoft) and describes their proprietary platform product; no independent replication is possible. Bottom Line: NeuSymMS presents a sensible production engineering architecture for LLM agent memory that practitioners building multi-tenant agentic platforms will find concretely useful, but the complete absence of quantitative evaluation — combined with suspicious placeholder references — means it cannot yet be cited as evidence that this approach outperforms simpler alternatives.
Search Results 2605.17596 PDF
#184 — Incremental (34)

A Multimodal Approach to Knowledge Tracing for Personalized Learning based on Cognitive and Affective Data in Resource-Constrained Environments

researchsquare.com · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CAHF-LG, a multimodal deep knowledge tracing framework that fuses five student interaction modalities (math correctness, code events, help-seeking text, affective states, behavioral indicators) via a three-level context-aware hierarchical fusion architecture with learned adaptive gating. - Why it's important: Existing knowledge tracing models rely almost exclusively on correctness sequences, potentially missing the affective and behavioral signals that educational psychology research identifies as significant contributors to learning outcomes, especially in under-resourced educational contexts with high instructor-to-student ratios. - How it does it: The core mechanism is a three-level hierarchical fusion: per-modality context-gated self-attention, cross-modal adaptive gating conditioned on a learner context vector, and a causally-masked Transformer encoder over time to model knowledge state evolution. - The good: 5-fold cross-validation yields mean AUC-ROC of 0.9933 ± 0.0006, and evaluation spans three structurally distinct datasets (math tutoring, programming, large-scale math benchmark), providing some evidence of cross-domain generalization. - The missing: The paper compares only against BKT and DKT — baselines from 2010 and 2015 — while omitting a decade of transformer-based KT models (AKT, SAINT, SAINT+) that routinely achieve 0.82–0.88 AUC on these same benchmarks, making the reported gains uninterpretable. - Read it or not: Skip unless you are specifically working on multimodal fusion for educational data mining and need an architecture reference — the evaluation design has critical flaws that prevent trusting the headline numbers.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (Novelty 50, Rigor 22, Non-Obv 45, Completeness 35, Resilience 25, Impact 30). Rigor and Resilience are the dimensions that pull the composite down: the headline AUC of 0.9933 is benchmarked only against BKT (2010) and DKT (2015) while omitting AKT/SAINT/SAINT+ (which sit at 0.82–0.88 on these same datasets), there are no ablations, the 5-fold CV runs on just 1,065 sequences out of hundreds of thousands available, and the provenance of the 6-dim affective features is never explained — risking circularity in the central 43.7% affect-weight finding. Novelty is moderate (context-conditioned hierarchical fusion is a coherent integration, NEW METHOD) but every component is borrowed from other domains, and the 'resource-constrained' framing is contradicted by the affective-sensor instrumentation it requires. Composite: 0.20*50 + 0.25*22 + 0.15*45 + 0.15*35 + 0.15*25 + 0.10*30 ≈ 34.
empirical Novelty 50 Rigor 22 Non-Obv 45 Completeness 35 Resilience 25 Impact 30
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Knowledge tracing — predicting whether a student will answer the next question correctly given their interaction history — is the foundation of intelligent tutoring systems. Traditional BKT and DKT models use binary correctness sequences as the sole input signal. Educational psychology argues that affective states (boredom, frustration, confusion) and behavioral patterns (help-seeking persistence, time-on-task) substantially influence learning outcomes. In under-resourced educational systems like Zambia's, where one instructor may serve dozens of students, automated adaptive systems need to capture these richer signals to substitute for individualized teacher attention. Prior multimodal fusion approaches in education either use static/early fusion poorly suited to heterogeneous data, or lack context-sensitive gating that adjusts modality importance dynamically. Key Idea: The key idea is to condition all fusion gates at every processing level on a single learned context vector encoding the learner's current problem type, proficiency, learning phase, task difficulty, and mean affective state. This creates a unified context-adaptive pathway: the same architecture can down-weight absent modalities (when, e.g., affective sensors are unavailable) and up-weight informative ones based on the learner's current state, rather than using fixed or input-agnostic attention weights. Methodology: The CAHF-LG model takes five modality inputs — math (1-dim BKT estimate or running accuracy), code (1-dim programming event score), text (1-dim hint/scaffold usage), affect (6-dim: Bored, Concentrating, Confused, Frustrated, Off-task, Gaming), and behavioral (3-dim: help-seeking, attempt persistence, time management) — and projects each through a two-layer MLP with GELU activations and layer normalization into a shared 128-dimensional latent space. A context encoder combines four scalars (problem type, proficiency, learning phase, task difficulty) with a mean affective embedding into a 128-dim context vector. Level 1 fusion applies per-modality context-gated self-attention (4 heads) plus a feedforward network. Level 2 fusion applies cross-modal attention (4 heads) followed by adaptive gating: a 3-layer MLP maps the concatenated modality pool and context vector to 5 softmax weights (with learnable base weights and temperature), producing a weighted modality sum. Level 3 temporal fusion applies a 4-layer, 8-head causally-masked Transformer encoder over sequences up to 200 timesteps. A binary cross-entropy loss over next-response prediction is implied (not explicitly stated). Critical design decisions: (1) learnable base weights as regularization toward prior modality preferences — well-motivated; (2) causal masking to enforce proper autoregressive structure — necessary and correct; (3) collapsing all three non-affective cognitive modalities to 1-dim each — poorly justified given their conceptual richness. Key Results: On a combined validation set of 36,374 interactions across three datasets, the model achieves AUC-ROC 0.9944, accuracy 95.66%, F1 0.9619, RMSE 0.1732. Per-dataset AUCs: XES3G5M 0.9986, ASSISTments2015 0.9878, CSEDM19 0.8957 (only 68 students, 533 samples). 5-fold cross-validation on 1,065 sequences yields mean AUC 0.9933 ± 0.0006. Modality importance weights from adaptive gates: affect 43.7%, behavioral 26.3%, text 15.4%, math 7.6%, code 7.0%. The only baselines reported are BKT (AUC 0.65–0.75) and DKT (AUC 0.80–0.86); no comparisons against SAKT, AKT, SAINT, SAINT+, or other recent transformer KT models are included. The XES3G5M dataset alone — which has 16,134 of the 36,374 validation samples and very consistent response patterns — accounts for the largest share of the aggregate AUC, inflating the overall figure. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The combination of context-conditioned hierarchical fusion with adaptive gating in a knowledge tracing framework is a genuine architectural contribution. However, each individual component (hierarchical attention, gated fusion, context-aware KT, causal transformers) is a known technique applied in other domains; the novelty lies in their integration and the specific context-encoding strategy rather than any fundamentally new mechanism. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablation studies are present — the paper does not isolate the contribution of each fusion level, the adaptive gating versus fixed attention, or any single modality. No error bars beyond cross-validation standard deviations are reported. Code and trained models are not publicly released; data is available "on reasonable request" only. The most serious methodological concern is the evaluation scale: the 5-fold CV is run on 1,065 sequences, yet ASSISTments2015 alone contains interactions from 19,917 students. The paper does not explain how 1,065 sequences were selected, raising questions about representativeness and selection bias. The origin of the 6-dimensional affective state features is never explained — ASSISTments2015 does not natively contain real-time affective sensor data; if these values are inferred or synthesized, the 43.7% modality weight finding becomes circular (the affect feature may encode information already derivable from correctness sequences). The comparison baselines (BKT, DKT) are a decade or more old; the field has advanced substantially and excluding AKT, SAINT, and similar models makes the claimed improvements unverifiable. This is an unreviewed Research Square preprint. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The architecture is small (128-dim latent space, 4-layer transformer) and runs on standard hardware; the computational burden is not a bottleneck. - Efficient: Inference cost is not quantified, but given the model scale, per-sequence inference is likely negligible. Training on 1,065 sequences converges in 30 epochs — fast. - Effective: The claimed AUC of 0.9933 is implausibly high relative to the broader KT literature on these same benchmarks (state-of-the-art is ~0.82–0.88 on ASSISTments); the absence of strong baselines prevents confirming the model actually solves the problem rather than exploiting evaluation artifacts such as a non-representative 1,065-sequence subsample. - Scalable: Not tested at scale; XES3G5M contains 5.5 million interactions but the paper uses only 16,134 of them — no explanation is given for this subsampling, and scaling to the full dataset is not reported. No software artifact is released. Strengths: - The three-level hierarchical fusion design with a single shared context vector conditioning all levels is architecturally coherent and provides a clean mechanism for modality-dropout robustness, which is a genuine practical requirement. - Evaluation spans three structurally distinct dataset types (math tutoring, programming, large-scale math benchmark) rather than a single domain, providing at least some cross-domain signal. - The cross-validation is stratified and reports standard deviations, which is better practice than many papers in this space that report only single-split numbers. - The modality importance analysis via learned gate weights is an interpretable output that could genuinely inform educational system designers about which sensor investments matter most. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge that CSEDM19's small size (68 students) limits performance on that dataset. They note data availability inconsistency across datasets as a structural challenge. They do not acknowledge the missing-baseline problem, the unexplained affective data provenance, or the evaluation-scale discrepancy. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) No comparison against any KT model post-2019; AKT (Ghosh et al. 2020), SAINT (Choi et al. 2020), and SAINT+ routinely achieve 0.82–0.88 AUC on ASSISTments — including these is essential. (2) The provenance of the 6-dim affective state features is never explained; if they are inferred from interaction patterns rather than independently collected, the main empirical finding (affect contributes 43.7%) is potentially a tautology. (3) The 1,065-sequence CV evaluation vs. hundreds of thousands of available interactions is unexplained and undermines generalization claims. (4) No ablation shows what each fusion level contributes or whether adaptive gating outperforms fixed equal-weight fusion. (5) The "resource-constrained environments" framing is contradicted by the system requirements: collecting 6-dim real-time affective states requires instrumentation far beyond what Zambian higher-education institutions can typically provide. (6) The math, code, and text modalities are each collapsed to a single scalar before encoding — it is unclear whether the 128-dim projection of a 1-dim input actually captures meaningful variation or is dominated by the encoder's bias terms. Bottom Line: The architecture is interesting and the context-aware gating idea is worth engaging with, but the evaluation — tiny CV sample, decade-old baselines, unexplained affective data, no ablations — leaves the headline AUC of 0.9933 untrustworthy; researchers in knowledge tracing should not cite this work as evidence of a new performance frontier until it is reproduced on full datasets against current baselines.
Search Results
#185 — Incremental (33)

QuPort: Topology-, Port-, and Congestion-Aware Compilation for Modular Multi-QPU Quantum Systems

Soumyadip Sarkar, Subhasree Bhattacharjee · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces QuPort, a Python/Qiskit compilation framework that partitions quantum circuits across modular multi-QPU architectures by jointly optimizing weighted cut distance, communication-port overflow, and routed link-load congestion via a composite objective called TPCCAP. - Why it's important: Modular quantum processors make inter-QPU communication a scarce, topology-dependent resource rather than a free extension of local connectivity, but existing compilers model only local coupling maps and have no mechanism to reason about port limits or interconnect congestion. - How it does it: QuPort represents the compilation problem as three coupled graphs (logical interaction graph, physical coupling map, QPU interconnect graph), partitions qubits using TPCCAP local search or simulated annealing over a composite objective, then extracts an intermediate representation separating per-QPU local circuits from an ordered remote-event list. - The good: The framework is internally consistent, provides two correctness proofs (capacity preservation across all partitioning algorithms, traffic conservation under equal-cost multi-path routing), and explicitly separates the compiler abstraction from hardware-specific remote-gate protocols — a useful design boundary. - The missing: The paper contains no experimental evaluation whatsoever — no circuit benchmarks, no comparison against any existing distributed quantum compiler (including Ferrari et al. which it cites directly), and no quantitative report of TPCCAP objective values, remote-gate counts, or makespan estimates on any circuit. - Read it or not: Worth reading only for quantum compilation researchers who need a reference implementation for multi-QPU partitioning heuristics; practitioners seeking evidence that the approach improves on baselines should look elsewhere, as no such evidence is provided.
Why this rank: Scored using the Systems profile (weight 30 on Practical Impact). The decisive limiter is the complete absence of any experimental evaluation: no benchmark circuit is compiled, no comparison against the Ferrari et al. compiler it cites, no ablation isolating the port-overflow and congestion terms — so Impact (30) and Rigor (20) are both low despite the public GitHub release, since an unvalidated prototype is not a demonstrated tool. Resilience (25) is low because every performance claim would collapse under review for lack of evidence, and the hardcoded 0.1 coefficient and unstudied hyperparameters (α,β,η,γ) are unbounded. Novelty (60) is the one bright spot — the three-level graph model and TPCCAP composite objective are a genuine new formulation, though built from standard partitioning/annealing components — and author candor about heuristic limits is a partial Completeness positive (30) undercut by their failure to acknowledge the missing evaluation. Composite: 0.15*60 + 0.15*20 + 0.10*40 + 0.15*30 + 0.15*25 + 0.30*30 ≈ 33.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 20 Non-Obv 40 Completeness 30 Resilience 25 Impact 30
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Quantum circuit compilation for modular multi-QPU systems requires simultaneously minimizing cross-device communication, respecting the limited number of inter-QPU communication ports per device, and avoiding congestion on specific interconnect links. Standard compilers such as Qiskit's transpiler expose only a flat physical coupling map; a routing pass on this map may silently route what are actually cross-QPU operations as if they were local, hiding the distinction between cheap local gates and expensive remote operations. The gap is that no openly available, Qiskit-integrated framework models port scarcity and interconnect congestion at the partitioning stage before physical routing begins. Key Idea: The key insight is that modular compilation requires three distinct graph representations kept explicit and separate — a weighted logical interaction graph capturing circuit interaction frequencies, a directed physical coupling map for per-QPU Qiskit routing, and an undirected QPU-level interconnect graph for hop distance, congestion, and scheduling. By maintaining this separation, a compiler can optimize a composite objective (TPCCAP) that penalizes cross-QPU traffic weighted by hop distance, communication-port overflow beyond a per-QPU limit P, and quadratic link-load congestion — and then extract an intermediate representation (local circuits + remote-event list) that defers the choice of physical remote-gate protocol to a backend. Methodology: The pipeline proceeds in four main stages. (1) Interaction extraction: a weighted undirected logical graph G_L is built from the input circuit by incrementing edge (i,j) for each two-qubit gate on qubits i,j; an optional temporal discount factor γ^t down-weights earlier interactions. (2) Partitioning: four algorithms are provided — heavy-edge clustering (merge components along highest-weight edges under capacity K, then first-fit-decreasing bin packing), balanced greedy partitioning (assign qubits in descending weighted-degree order with a balance penalty), TPCCAP local search (start from greedy partition, iteratively accept best-improving capacity-preserving moves on the composite objective J(π) = α·Σ w_ij·d(π(i),π(j)) + β·Σ max(0, b_q − P)² + η·Σ L_e²), and TPCCAP-SA (add simulated annealing to escape local minima). (3) Layout: boundary qubits with high external interaction scores are assigned to physical communication-qubit slots; a "diverse" mode distributes them across distinct remote QPU neighbors. (4) Distributed extraction: the mapped circuit is scanned instruction-by-instruction; same-QPU two-qubit gates go to local circuits, cross-QPU gates are recorded as remote events with synchronization barriers inserted. A topology-aware estimator then models makespan using abstract latency parameters (τ_1, τ_2, τ_swap, τ_E, τ_C, τ_R) and greedily packs remote operations into rounds subject to port and link-capacity constraints. The hyperparameters α, β, η, λ, γ are present in the formulation but no guidance on their setting appears in the paper. Key Results: The paper reports no experimental results. No circuit benchmark (QFT, QAOA, Grover, VQE, random) is compiled and evaluated. No TPCCAP objective value, remote-gate count, makespan estimate, or partition quality metric is reported for any circuit or architecture configuration. No comparison against any baseline compiler — including SABRE, tket, or the Ferrari et al. distributed compiler explicitly cited — is presented. The two propositions with proofs establish correctness properties (capacity invariant preservation and traffic conservation under multi-path routing) but are not performance claims. The paper's conclusion is entirely qualitative. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW FORMULATION. The three-level graph model and the TPCCAP composite objective are genuinely new formulations for modular quantum compilation. The individual algorithmic components — heavy-edge clustering, greedy partitioning with local refinement, simulated annealing, shortest-path routing, and abstract schedule estimation — are standard classical techniques applied to a new problem domain. The intermediate representation separating local circuits from a remote-event list is a useful design contribution but conceptually straightforward. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations are present. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests appear because there are no experiments. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/neuralsorcerer/quport, which is the primary empirical artifact. Two correctness propositions are proved, but both are straightforward structural arguments (capacity invariant by induction over algorithm transitions; traffic conservation by flow-splitting on a DAG). The paper's evaluation is entirely absent, which is a fundamental methodological concern. The framework is developed and described without any demonstration on real circuits or hardware-derived parameters. Deployability: Profile: SYSTEMS — QuPort is a built tool evaluated by description, not by deployment. - Practical: The framework runs on standard hardware via Python and Qiskit with no specialized dependencies beyond curl_cffi is not present here; the standard library requirements (networkx, Qiskit) run on commodity hardware. - Efficient: Not evaluated; no runtime measurements, memory profiles, or scalability data are reported for any circuit size or QPU count. - Effective: Not demonstrated; the paper contains no experiment showing that TPCCAP-derived partitions produce fewer remote operations, lower makespan, or lower objective values than naive baselines on any circuit. - Scalable: Not tested; the paper makes no claim about behavior at scale, and no circuit larger than a conceptual example is mentioned. The deliverable is an open-source Python library plus an intermediate representation; no trained model or dataset is produced. Strengths: - The explicit three-level graph separation (logical interaction graph / physical coupling map / QPU interconnect graph) is a clean formalization that prior Qiskit-based tools lack; it correctly isolates the concerns of circuit-level partitioning from device-level routing. - The TPCCAP objective is well-motivated: each of its three terms addresses a distinct, real failure mode of naive cut-minimization (hop-distance blindness, port overflow, link congestion), and the quadratic congestion term is a reasonable proxy for bottleneck load. - The intermediate representation D(C) = ({C_q}, R) that separates local circuits from remote events is a sound abstraction boundary that enables backend-agnostic compilation studies without committing to a specific physical protocol. - The authors are unusually candid about what the framework does not do: no hardware fidelity model, no physical remote-gate implementation, no optimality guarantee — this transparency prevents the artifact from being misread as a validated hardware tool. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that TPCCAP and TPCCAP-SA are heuristic and do not guarantee optimal partitions; the schedule estimator is a layer-based greedy model and not a verified network scheduler; the framework provides no calibrated backend for any physical platform (trapped-ion, superconducting, photonic, neutral-atom); and the heuristics should not be interpreted as dominating specialized graph or hypergraph partitioning packages. These are stated in Sections 2, 9, and the abstract. The authors do not acknowledge the absence of experimental evaluation as a limitation. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The complete absence of any experimental evaluation is the dominant unacknowledged gap — a systems paper with a GitHub artifact but zero benchmarks is not a validated tool, it is an unvalidated prototype. Specifically: (1) No comparison against Ferrari et al. [4,5], the closest prior work the paper itself cites, on any circuit. (2) No ablation showing that TPCCAP's port-overflow and congestion terms improve over simple min-cut partitioning. (3) No sensitivity analysis on the four hyperparameters (α, β, η, λ) whose values are unspecified. (4) The abstract cost model (Eqs. 22–24) includes the term 0.1·d_C·τ_2 with a hardcoded coefficient 0.1 that is unexplained and not derived from any calibration. (5) The temporal weighting factor γ is introduced formally but never studied; its effect on partition quality is unknown. (6) No evaluation at any circuit scale — neither toy (5-qubit) nor moderate (50-qubit) examples appear. (7) One author is listed as "Independent Researcher" with no institutional affiliation, and the paper is a preprint with no peer review signal. Bottom Line: QuPort provides a well-structured compiler abstraction and a reasonable composite optimization objective for modular multi-QPU compilation, but the complete absence of experimental evaluation means a practitioner cannot determine whether the framework actually reduces remote operations or makespan relative to naive approaches, making it a design document rather than a validated tool at this stage.
Search Results 2605.12583 PDF
#186 — Incremental (33)

Benchmarking small language models in a React Native environment: Qwen2. 5 and Llama 3.2 inference performance on mobile

theseus.fi · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper benchmarks Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct and Llama 3.2-3B-Instruct inference performance on an iPhone 15 Pro Max via the React Native llama.rn library, measuring tokens per second, time-to-first-token, and thermal stability across 125 runs per model. - Why it's important: Developers building private, on-device AI features in React Native lack published performance data to guide model selection at the 3B parameter scale on high-end mobile hardware. - How it does it: Both models are quantized to Q4_K_M GGUF and run through llama.cpp with full Metal GPU offloading (n_gpu_layers=99) via the JSI-based llama.rn binding inside a custom React Native 0.84 benchmarking app. - The good: The GPU saturation finding — that a statistically significant 3.7% TPS gap (22.19 vs 21.40) persists across all five prompt categories yet translates to only 0.4 s per 256-token response — is a practically actionable null result for deployment decisions. - The missing: No output quality evaluation is performed; the recommendation to select models by "task-specific quality" is the central conclusion yet is entirely unsupported by any quality measurement in the study. - Read it or not: Skip for research purposes; skim if you are a developer considering llama.rn integration and want a quick data point on 3B model throughput on A17 Pro hardware.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Novelty (25) and Non-Obviousness (22) are the dimensions that cap the composite: the central finding (GPU saturation equalizes 3B architectures on A17 Pro) is an expected engineering result any competent practitioner would anticipate, explicitly framed by the summary as 'not a research contribution.' Completeness (38) is undermined because the headline recommendation — select models by 'task-specific quality' — is entirely unsupported by any quality measurement in the study. Rigor (40) gets partial credit for the appropriate Wilcoxon test and thermally-validated cooldown, but no code release, no CPU baseline, single-device scope, and undergraduate-thesis preprint status hold it down; Impact (28) reflects no released artifact. Honest five-point limitation acknowledgment is a credibility positive but cannot lift a narrow, single-device data point.
empirical Novelty 25 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 22 Completeness 38 Resilience 40 Impact 28
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Mobile app developers who need private, low-latency, on-device AI inference — particularly in React Native — face two practical problems: (1) whether 3B-parameter quantized SLMs achieve usable generation speeds on consumer smartphones, and (2) which of the two dominant open-source SLM families performs better on that hardware. The study is grounded in a concrete React Native activity-tracking application that cannot use cloud LLMs due to privacy requirements, where generation times exceeding several minutes with unoptimized models made on-device deployment impractical without systematic model selection. Key Idea The central insight the study arrives at — though it is an empirical finding rather than a designed mechanism — is that at the 3B parameter scale with Q4_K_M quantization and full Metal GPU offload on a high-end mobile SoC, the GPU becomes the throughput bottleneck rather than the model architecture. This collapses architectural differences (attention head count, embedding dimension, vocabulary size) into a statistically significant but practically imperceptible speed gap, making inference speed a non-differentiating factor for 3B model selection on A17 Pro-class hardware. Methodology Two models — Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct (1.93 GB) and Llama 3.2-3B-Instruct (2.02 GB) — are obtained as Q4_K_M GGUF files from the same HuggingFace provider (bartowski) to eliminate quantization toolchain as a variable. Both run on an iPhone 15 Pro Max (A17 Pro, 8 GB RAM, iOS 26.3.1) via llama.rn 0.11.5 wrapping llama.cpp, configured with n_gpu_layers=99 (full Metal offload), n_ctx=2048, n_batch=512, and use_mmap=true. The benchmark app runs five standardized prompts (math, factual Q&A, code generation, reasoning, creative writing) × 5 runs per prompt × 5 sessions = 125 total runs per model, with a 2-minute automated cooldown between runs and model unload between sessions. Metrics — TPS, TTFT, and token counts — are read from llama.cpp's built-in timing output. Wilcoxon signed-rank tests assess per-prompt TPS significance. Three critical design decisions: (1) same quantization provider — well-justified for variable control; (2) full GPU offload with no CPU-only baseline — unjustified omission that prevents quantifying Metal's contribution; (3) 256-token generation cap — truncates code generation responses and makes TPS measurements for that category incomplete. Key Results Qwen2.5 achieves 22.19 TPS (SD 0.15, range 21.23–22.39); Llama 3.2 achieves 21.40 TPS (SD 0.28, range 19.26–21.63). The 0.79 TPS difference (3.7%) is statistically significant at p<0.001 across all five prompt categories, with per-prompt deltas ranging from 0.73 to 0.83 TPS. TTFT is near-identical when both models evaluate the same token count; the largest difference (Llama 312 ms vs Qwen 269 ms on the math prompt) is explained by Llama's smaller vocabulary (128,256 tokens) tokenizing the prompt into 57 tokens vs Qwen's 38, while Llama actually evaluates tokens faster (189 tokens/s vs 142 tokens/s). Both models ran without memory warnings or crashes across all 250 combined runs. A GPU warm-up effect is observed on the first run of each session (Llama math: 20.36 TPS on run 0 vs 21.46 on run 1); no thermal throttling was detected thereafter. Output verbosity differs substantially — Qwen2.5 generates ~249 tokens for the math prompt vs Llama's ~95, though both arrive at the correct answer. No baseline other than the two models is included; no quality benchmarks are run. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The contribution is a concrete measurement on a specific hardware/software stack (A17 Pro + llama.rn + React Native JSI). The finding that GPU saturation equalizes 3B architectures to within 3.7% on this SoC is an expected engineering result, not a scientific advance. What is genuinely novel is the React Native JSI + llama.cpp integration characterization, the tokenization-normalized TTFT decomposition, and the GPU warm-up effect observation as practical developer guidance — all useful practitioner data, none constituting a research contribution to the field. Rigor & Reproducibility The Wilcoxon signed-rank test is an appropriate choice given the sample sizes (n=25 per prompt per model). The authors correctly acknowledge that TTFT has only n=5 valid observations per prompt (KV cache reuse invalidates subsequent measurements), which precludes significance testing for that metric. No ablations are performed; no CPU-only baseline is collected; no output quality evaluation is conducted. The benchmarking application's source code is not released. The 2-minute cooldown is empirically validated via TPS-over-run-index plots showing no downward trend — a methodological positive. Results are single-device, single-OS version (iOS 26.3.1), and not reproducible on other hardware without replication. This is an unpublished undergraduate thesis, not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Both models run on a commercially available consumer device (iPhone 15 Pro Max) requiring no special hardware beyond iOS and sufficient storage for ~2 GB model files. - Efficient: Q4_K_M quantization reduces models to ~2 GB; mmap avoids full RAM load, allowing both to coexist within the 8 GB shared memory budget without crashes across 250 runs. - Effective: The study demonstrates >21 TPS generation but does not measure whether model outputs are accurate or useful for the target activity-tracking application; the claim that "20+ TPS is sufficient for real-time conversational use" is asserted without citation or user-perception validation. - Scalable: Not tested; the 256-token cap, single device, and absence of multi-turn conversation tests prevent any conclusions about behavior at longer contexts, other parameter sizes, or mid-range SoCs. No software artifact is released; the study stops at paper form. Strengths - The controlled experimental design — same quantization provider, same hardware, built-in llama.cpp timing metrics, 2-minute cooldown validated by thermal plots — eliminates several common confounders in mobile benchmarking. - The tokenization-normalized TTFT analysis (decomposing TTFT into token count × per-token evaluation rate) is an insightful decomposition that reveals Llama evaluates tokens faster but produces more of them, which would be missed by a raw TTFT comparison. - The GPU warm-up finding and the practical recommendation to run a dummy inference at model load are concrete, actionable engineering insights absent from most SLM benchmarking papers. - The paper correctly identifies and states the practical null result: statistical significance does not equal practical significance when 0.79 TPS translates to 0.4 s per response. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) single-device generalizability failure; (2) TTFT n=5 is too small for significance testing; (3) no output quality evaluation; (4) 256-token cap truncates code generation responses; (5) no CPU-only baseline, leaving Metal GPU contribution unquantified. All five are confronted directly and honestly — a credibility positive for an undergraduate thesis.
